In analogy with graphs of morphisms, the following diagram of a graph of a rational map π can be useful (cf. Figure 11.2).

11.3.F. EXERCISE (THE BLOW-UP OF THE AFFINE PLANE AS THE GRAPH OF A RATIONAL MAP). Consider the rational map $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 }$ given by $( x , y ) \mapsto [ x , y ]$ . Show that this rational map cannot be extended over the origin. (A similar argument arises in Exercise 7.5.J on the Cremona transformation.) Show that the graph of the rational map is the morphism (the blow-up) described in Exercise 10.3.F. (When we define blow-ups in general, we will see that they are often graphs of rational maps; see Exercise 22.4.M.)
11.3.5. Variations.
Variations of the short proof of Theorem 11.3.2 yield other useful results. Exercise 11.3.B is one example. Here is another.

11.3.G. EXERCISE (MAPS TO A SEPARATED SCHEME CAN BE EXTENDED OVER AN EFFEC-TIVE CARTIER DIVISOR IN AT MOST ONE WAY). Suppose X is a Z-scheme (not necessarily reduced!), and Y is a separated Z-scheme. Suppose further that D is an effective Cartier divisor on X. Show that any Z-morphism $\cal { X } \backslash \mathrm { D  Y }$ can be extended in at most one way to a Z-morphism $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ . (Hint: Reduce to the case where $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } =$ ${ \mathsf { X } } { = } { \mathsf { S p e c } } { \mathsf { A } } ,$ and D is the vanishing scheme of $\mathfrak { t } \in \mathsf { A }$ . Reduce to showing that the scheme-theoretic image of D(t) in X is all of X. Show this by showing that $\lambda \to \lambda _ { \mathrm { t } }$ is an inclusion.)
As noted in ${ \ S 7 . 5 . 2 , }$ rational maps can be defined from any X that has associated points to any Y. The Reduced-to-Separated Theorem 11.3.2 can be extended to this setting, as follows.

11.3.H. EXERCISE (THE “ASSOCIATED-TO-SEPARATED THEOREM”). Prove that two S-morphisms $\pi$ and $\pi ^ { \prime }$ from a locally Noetherian scheme U to a separated S-scheme $Z ,$ agreeing on an open subset containing the associated points of U, are the same.
11.4 Proper Morphisms

A map of topological spaces (also known as a continuous map!) is said to be proper if the preimage of any compact set is compact. Properness of morphisms is an analogous property. For example, a variety over $\mathbb { C }$ will be proper if it is compact in the classical topology (see [Se3, §7]). Alternatively, we will see that projective A-schemes are proper over A—so this is a nice property satisfied by projective schemes, which also is convenient to work with.

Recall (§8.3.5) that a (continuous) map of topological spaces π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is closed if for each closed subset S X, $\pi ( S )$ is also closed. A morphism of schemes is closed if the underlying continuous map is closed. We say that a morphism of schemes π $: X \to \Upsilon$ is universally closed if for every morphism $Z \to \mathsf { Y } ,$ , the induced morphism $Z \times \gamma X  Z$ is closed. In other words, a morphism is universally closed if it remains closed under any base change. By now you will automatically expect the following exercise.
11.4.A. EASY EXERCISE. Show that the class of universally closed morphisms is “reasonable” in the sense of $\ S 8 . 1$ .

To motivate the definition of properness for schemes, we remark that a continuous map $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ of locally compact Hausdorff spaces that have countable bases for their topologies is universally closed if and only if it is proper (i.e., preimages of compact subsets are compact). You are welcome to prove this as an exercise.
11.4 Proper Morphisms

11.4.1. Definition. A morphism π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is proper if it is separated, finite type, and universally closed. A scheme X is often said to be proper if some implicit structure morphism is proper. For example, a k-scheme X is often described as proper if $X {  } \mathrm { S p e c k }$ is proper. (A k-scheme is often said to be complete if it is proper. We will not use this terminology.) If A is a ring, one often says that an A-scheme is proper over A if it is proper over Spec A.

Let’s try this idea out in practice. We expect that $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } \longrightarrow \mathbf { S p e c } \mathbb { C }$ is not proper, because the complex manifold corresponding to $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ is not compact. However, note that this map is separated (it is a map of affine schemes), finite type, and (trivially) closed. So the “universally” is what matters here.

11.4.B. EASY EXERCISE. Show that $\pi : \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \mathbb { C }$ is a closed map, but not universally closed. Hence $\pi$ is not proper. Possible hint: Consider a well-chosen map such as $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } \times _ { \mathbb { C } } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ . (See Figure 19.1 for another finite type, separated, closed morphism that is not proper. Showing that morphism is not proper requires more creativity.)

11.4.2. Example. As a first example: Closed embeddings are proper. They are clearly separated, as affine morphisms are separated, Proposition 11.2.4. They are finite type. After base change, they remain closed embeddings (§10.2.2), and closed embeddings are always closed. This easily extends further as follows.
11.4.3. Proposition — Finite morphisms are proper.

Proof. Finite morphisms are separated (as they are affine by definition, and affine morphisms are separated, Proposition 11.2.4), and finite type (basically because finite modules over a ring are automatically finitely generated). To show that finite morphisms are closed after any base change, we note that they remain finite after any base change (finiteness is preserved by base change, Exercise 10.4.D(d)), and finite morphisms are closed (Exercise 8.3.L). □
11.4.4. Proposition —

list_items: (a) The notion of “proper morphism” is stable under base change.

(b) The notion of “proper morphism” is local on the target (i.e., $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is proper if and only if for any affine open cover $\mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \to \mathsf { Y } ,$ , $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } ) \to \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ is proper). Note that the “only if” direction follows from (a)—consider base change by ${ \mathrm { U } } _ { \mathrm { i } } \hookrightarrow \mathsf { Y }$ .
(c) The notion of “proper morphism” is preserved by composition.

(d) The product of two proper morphisms is proper: if π : $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ and $\pi ^ { \prime } \colon X ^ { \prime } \to Y ^ { \prime }$ are proper, where all morphisms are morphisms of Z-schemes, then $\pi \times \pi ^ { \prime } \colon X \times _ { Z } X ^ { \prime } \to \Upsilon \times _ { Z } \Upsilon ^ { \prime }$ is proper.
(e) Suppose

text: is a commutative diagram, τ is proper, and ρ is separated. Then π is proper.
A sample application of (e): A morphism (over Spec k) from a proper k-scheme to a separated k-scheme is always proper.

Proof. Parts (a) through (c) say that proper morphisms form a “reasonable” class of morphisms in the sense of §8.1. So they follow because separated morphisms, finite type morphisms, and universal closed morphisms are all reasonable classes. Then (d) follows from (a) and (c) by Exercise 8.1.A. Closed embeddings are proper (Example 11.4.2), so (e) follows from the Cancellation Theorem 11.1.1 for proper morphisms. □
Chapter 11 Separated and Proper Morphisms and Varieties
11.4.C. EXERCISE (“IMAGES OF PROPER SCHEMES ARE PROPER”). Suppose in the diagram

that $\tau$ is proper and ρ is separated and finite type. Show that the scheme-theoretic image of X under $\pi$ is a proper Z-scheme. (Thus properness in algebraic geometry behaves like properness in topology.)
We now come to the most important example of proper morphisms.
11.4.5. Theorem — Projective A-schemes are proper over A.
(As finite morphisms to Spec A are projective A-schemes, Exercise 8.3.I, Theorem 11.4.5 can be used to give a second proof that finite morphisms are proper, Proposition 11.4.3.)

Proof. The structure morphism of a projective A-scheme $x $ Spec A factors as a closed embedding followed by $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda$ . Closed embeddings are proper (Example 11.4.2), and compositions of proper morphisms are proper (Proposition 11.4.4), so it suffices to show that $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda$ is proper. We have already seen that this morphism is finite type (Easy Exercise 5.3.D) and separated (Proposition 11.2.8), so it suffices to show that $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda$ is universally closed. As $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } \times _ { \mathbb { Z } } { \sf S p e c } { \sf A } ,$ it suffices to show that $\mathbb { P } _ { X } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } : = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } \times _ { \mathbb { Z } } X \to X$ is closed for any scheme X. But the property of being closed is local on the target on $\Sigma ,$ so by covering X with affine open subsets, it suffices to show that $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c \ : B }$ is closed for all rings B. This is the Fundamental Theorem of Elimination Theory (Theorem 8.4.10). □

11.4.6. Remark: “Reasonable” proper schemes are projective. It is not easy to come up with an example of an A-scheme that is proper but not projective! Over a field, all proper curves are projective (see Remark 19.2.I), and all smooth surfaces are projective (see [Ba, Thm. 1.28] for a proof of this theorem of Zariski; smoothness of course is not yet defined). We will meet a first example of a proper but not projective variety (a singular threefold) in §15.2.11. We will later see an example of a proper nonprojective surface in §19.11.11, and a simpler one in Exercise 20.2.G. Once we know about flatness, we will see Hironaka’s example of a proper nonprojective irreducible smooth threefold over $\mathbb { C }$ (§24.7.6).
11.4.7. Functions on connected reduced proper k-schemes must be constant.

As an enlightening application of these ideas, we show that if X is a connected reduced proper k-scheme where $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } , } \end{array}$ then $\Gamma ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) = \mathbf { k }$ . The analogous fact for connected compact complex manifolds uses the maximum principle. We saw this in the special case $X = \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ in Exercise 4.4.E; it will be vastly generalized by Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1.

Suppose $\mathsf { f } \in \Gamma ( \mathsf { X } , { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { X } } )$ (f is a function on X). This is the same as a map π: $\Chi \to \mathbb { A } _ { \sf k } ^ { 1 }$ (Exercise 7.3.G, discussed further in $\ S 7 . 6 . 1 $ ). Let $\pi ^ { \prime }$ be the composition of $\pi$ →with the open embedding $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . By Proposition 11.4.4(e), $\pi ^ { \prime }$ is proper, and, in particular, closed. As X is connected, the image of $\pi ^ { \prime }$ is as well. Thus the set-theoretic image of $\pi ^ { \prime }$ must be either a closed point, or all of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . But the set-theoretic image of $\pi ^ { \prime }$ lies in $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ , so it must be a closed point p (which we identify with an element of k).

By Corollary 9.4.5, the support of the scheme-theoretic image of $\pi$ is the closed point p. By Exercise 9.4.A, the scheme-theoretic image is precisely p (with the reduced structure). Thus $\pi$ can be interpreted as the structure map to Spec k, followed by a closed embedding to $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ identifying Spec k with p. You should be able to verify that this is the map to $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ corresponding to the constant function ${ \mathsf { f } } = { \mathsf { p } }$ .
11.4 Proper Morphisms

(What are counterexamples if different hypotheses are relaxed? The condition $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ cannot be ignored—if ${ \mathrm { k } } = \mathbb { R } ,$ then $X { = } S p e c \mathbb { C }$ is a connected reduced proper projective $\mathrm { k }$ -scheme, with $\Gamma ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) = \mathbf { k } ^ { 2 }$ . Interesting fact: $\ x ^ { 2 } = 0$ in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { 2 }$ shows that even nonreduced proper $\mathrm { k }$ -schemes can have only constants as functions; see $\ S 5 . 2 . 3$ and Exercise 18.6.U.)
11.4.8. Facts (not yet proved) that may help you correctly think about finiteness.
The following facts may shed some light on the notion of finiteness. We will prove them later.

A morphism of locally Noetherian schemes is finite if and only if it is proper and affine, if and only if it is proper and quasifinite (Theorem 28.5.2). We have proved parts of this statement, but we will only finish the proof once we know Zariski’s Main Theorem; cf. §8.3.12).

As an application: quasifinite morphisms from proper schemes to separated schemes are finite. Here is why: Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a quasifinite morphism over $Z ,$ where $x$ is proper over Z. Then by the Cancellation Theorem 11.1.1 for proper morphisms, $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is proper. Hence as $\pi$ is quasifinite and proper, $\pi$ is finite.

As an explicit example, consider the map π: $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ given by $[ x , y ] \mapsto [ f ( x , y ) ]$ , ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } , { \mathfrak { y } } ) ] ,$ where f and g are homogeneous polynomials of the same degree with no common roots in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . The fibers are finite, and $\pi$ is proper (from the Cancellation Theorem 11.1.1 for proper morphisms, as discussed after the statement of Theorem 11.4.4), so $\pi$ is finite. This could be checked directly as well, but now we can save ourselves the annoyance.

11.4.9.** Group varieties. The rest of §11.4 is double-starred, and we will also throughout work in the category of varieties over a field k. We briefly discuss group varieties, mainly because we can, not because we have anything profound to say. We discuss them right now only because properness gives an unexpected consequence, that proper group varieties are abelian (thanks to the surprisingly simple Rigidity Lemma 11.4.12).

As discussed in $\ S 1 1 . 2 . 9 ,$ now that we have the category of varieties over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ we immediately have the notion of a group variety (thanks to $\ S 7 . 6 . 4$ on group objects in any category). An algebraic group (over k) is a smooth group variety. Examples include ${ \mathrm { G L } } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ (Exercise 7.6.N(b)), which includes $\mathbb { G } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ as a special case, and $S \mathrm { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ (Exercise 7.6.Q).

11.4.10. Side Remarks (that we won’t prove or use). Group varieties are automatically smooth (hence algebraic groups) in characteristic 0. Group varieties are not necessarily smooth in positive characteristic. (Let k be an imperfect field of characteristic p, and choose $\mathrm { t } \in \mathrm { k }$ without a pth root in k. Consider the “closed sub-group scheme” $\mathsf { x } ^ { \mathrm { p } } = \mathsf { t y } ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ of the “additive group” $\mathbb { G } _ { \mathtt { a } } ^ { 2 } = \operatorname { S p e c } \mathtt { k } [ \mathtt { x } , \mathtt { y } ]$ . This group scheme is reduced, but not geometrically reduced; and not smooth, or even regular.) Algebraic groups are automatically quasiprojective. An algebraic group G is affine (as a scheme) if and only if it admits a closed immersion into ${ \mathrm { G L } } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ for some ${ \mathfrak { n } } \geq 0 ,$ such that ${ \mathrm { G } } \to { \mathrm { G L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is a homomorphism of group schemes (Exercise 7.6.N(a)). For further discussion on these and other issues, see [P2, §5].

11.4.11. Definition: Abelian varieties. We can now define one of the most important classes of varieties: abelian varieties. The bad news is that we have no real example yet. We will later see elliptic curves as an example (§19.10), but we will not meet anything more exotic than that. Still, it is pleasant to know that we can make the definition this early.

An abelian variety over a field k is an algebraic group that is geometrically integral and projective (over k). It turns out that the analytification of any abelian variety over $\mathbb { C }$ is a complex torus, i.e., $\mathbb { C } ^ { \mathrm { n } } / \Lambda ,$ where $\wedge$ is a lattice (of rank $2 \mathfrak { n }$ ). (The key idea: Connected compact complex Lie groups are commutative; see Remark 11.4.14; the universal cover is a simply connected commutative complex Lie group, and thus $\mathbb { C } ^ { \mathrm { { n } } }$ . See [BL, Lem. 1.1.1].)

We now show that abelian varieties are abelian, i.e., abelian varieties are commutative algebraic group varieties. (This is less tautological than it sounds! The adjective “abelian” is used in two different senses; what they have in common is that they are derived from the name Niels Henrik
Chapter 11 Separated and Proper Morphisms and Varieties
Abel.) As algebraic groups need not be commutative (witness ${ \mathrm { G L } } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ ), it is somehow surprising that commutativity could be forced by “compactness.”
The key result is the following fact, beautiful in its own right.

11.4.12. Rigidity Lemma — Let X, Y, and Z be varieties, where X is proper and geometrically integral, with a k-valued point p, and Y is irreducible. Let pr : ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } } \times { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { Y } } } \to { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { Y } } }$ be the projection, and let $\alpha \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be any morphism. Suppose $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ is such that $\propto$ is constant on $\chi \times \{ \mathbf { q } \} ;$ say $\alpha ( X \times \{ { \bf q } \} ) = { \bf r } \in Z$ .

list_items: (a) Then there is a morphism ψ : $\Upsilon  Z$ such that $\alpha = \Psi \circ \mathsf { p r } .$ . (In particular, for every $\mathbf { q } ^ { \prime } \in \mathsf { Y } ,$ , α is constant on $\mathsf { X } \times \{ \mathsf { q } ^ { \prime } \} .$ )
(b) If $\propto$ is also constant on $\{ { \mathfrak { p } } \} \times \Upsilon ,$ , then α is a constant function on all of $x \times \Upsilon$ .

text: The hypotheses can be relaxed in a number of ways, but this version suffices for our purposes. If you have not read $\ S 1 0 . 5 . 1 ,$ , where geometric integrality is discussed, you need not worry too much; this is automatic if $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ and X is integral (Hard Fact 10.5.3). Even if you have read §10.5.1, it is helpful to first read this proof under the assumption that $\operatorname { k } = { \overline { { \operatorname { k } } } } ,$ to avoid being distracted from the main idea by geometric points. But of course all of §11.4.9 is double-starred, so you shouldn’t be reading this anyway.
11.4.D. EXERCISE. Show that the properness hypothesis on X is necessary. (Can you make this accord with your intuition?)

Proof of the Rigidity Lemma 11.4.12. Define $\beta \colon X \times Y  Z$ by $\beta ( x , y ) = \alpha ( \mathtt { p } , \mathtt { y } )$ . (More precisely, if $\sigma _ { \mathrm { p } }$ is the section of ${ \boldsymbol { X } } \times { \boldsymbol { Y } } \to { \boldsymbol { Y } }$ pulled back from Spec $\mathsf { k } \mapsto \mathsf { p }$ , then $\beta = \alpha \circ \sigma _ { \mathrm { p } } \circ \mathrm { p r } .$ .) We will show that $\beta ( x , y ) = \alpha ( x , y )$ . This will imply (a) (take $\psi = \alpha \circ \sigma _ { \mathrm { p } }$ ), which in turn immediately implies (b).

Proposition 10.5.19 (geometrically reduced times reduced is reduced) implies that $\boldsymbol { x } \times \boldsymbol { Y }$ is reduced. Exercise 10.5.O (geometrically irreducible times irreducible is irreducible) implies that $\boldsymbol { x } \times \boldsymbol { Y }$ is irreducible. As Z is separated, by Exercise 11.3.A, it suffices to show that $\alpha = \beta$ on a nonempty (hence dense) open subset of $\boldsymbol { x } \times \boldsymbol { Y }$ .

Let ${ \mathrm { u } } \subset Z$ be an affine open neighborhood of r. Then $\propto ^ { - 1 } ( Z \backslash \sqcup )$ is a closed subset of $\boldsymbol { x } \times \boldsymbol { Y }$ . As the projection pr is proper (using properness of $\Sigma ,$ and preservation of properness under base change, Proposition 11.4.4(a)), we have $\mathfrak { p r } ( \alpha ^ { - 1 } ( Z \backslash \mathfrak { U } ) )$ ) is closed. Its complement is open, and contains q; let ${ \mathsf { V } } \subset { \mathsf { Y } }$ be an open neighborhood of $\mathbf { q }$ disjoint from $\mathfrak { p r } ( \alpha ^ { - 1 } ( Z \backslash \mathfrak { U } ) )$ .

11.4.E. EXERCISE. Suppose $\gamma$ : Spec $\overline { { \mathsf { K } } } \to \mathsf { V }$ is a geometric point of V. Show that the fiber $x _ { \overline { { \mathsf { K } } } }$ over Spec K (or, more precisely, over $\gamma$ ) is mapped to a point in $Z$ by the restriction $\alpha _ { \overline { { \mathsf { K } } } }$ of $\propto$ to $x _ { \overline { { \mathsf { K } } } }$ . Hint: Review §11.4.7.
11.4 Proper Morphisms

By Exercise 11.3.B (maps of varieties over an algebraically closed field are determined by the map of closed points), $\alpha _ { \overline { { { \sf K } } } } = \beta _ { \overline { { { \sf K } } } }$ .

11.4.F. EXERCISE. Use Exercise 11.3.A (the fact that the locus where $\alpha = \beta$ is a closed subscheme— and represents a certain functor) to show that $\alpha = \beta$ on $\mathsf { p r } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { V } )$ .

list_items: 11.4.13. Corollary — Suppose A is an abelian variety, G is a group variety, and $\Phi \colon A \to \mathsf { G }$ is any morphism of varieties. Then $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ is the composition of a translation and a homomorphism.

Proof. Let $e _ { A }$ and $e _ { \mathrm { G } }$ be the identity points of A and G, respectively. Composing $\Phi$ with a translation, we may assume that $\Phi ( e _ { \mathrm { A } } ) { = } e _ { \mathrm { G } }$ . Consider the morphism $\alpha \colon A \times A \to { \mathsf { G } }$ given by $\alpha ( { \mathfrak a } _ { 1 } , { \mathfrak a } _ { 2 } ) = \phi ( { \mathfrak a } _ { 1 } { \mathfrak a } _ { 2 } ) \phi ( { \mathfrak a } _ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 } \phi ( { \mathfrak a } _ { 1 } ) ^ { - 1 }$ . Then $\alpha ( \{ e _ { A } \} \times A ) = \alpha ( A \times \{ e _ { A } \} ) = \{ e _ { \mathrm G } \} ,$ so by the Rigidity Lemma 11.4.12, $\propto$ is a constant, and sends $A \times A$ to eG. Thus $\phi ( { \bf a } _ { 1 } { \bf a } _ { 2 } ) = \phi ( { \bf a } _ { 1 } { \bf \phi } ) \phi ( { \bf a } _ { 2 } ) ,$ so $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ is a homomorphism. □
11.4.G. EXERCISE (ABELIAN VARIETIES ARE ABELIAN). Show that an abelian variety is an abelian group variety. Hint: Apply Corollary 11.4.13 to the inversion morphism i: $\lambda \to { \lambda }$ .
11.4.14. Remark. A similar idea is used to show that connected compact complex Lie groups are abelian; see, for example, $[ \mathrm { B L } ,$ Lem. 1.1.1].

text: 248 Chapter 11 Separated and Proper Morphisms and Varieties
PART IV
“Geometric” Properties of Schemes
Chapter 12
Dimension
Everyone knows what a curve is, until he has studied enough mathematics to become confused.
—F. Klein, [RS, p. 90]

At this point, you know a fair bit about schemes, but there are some fundamental notions you cannot yet define. In particular, you cannot use the phrase “smooth surface,” as it involves the notions of dimension and of smoothness. You may be surprised that we have gotten so far without using these ideas. You may also be disturbed to find that these notions can be subtle, but you should keep in mind that they are subtle in all parts of mathematics.

In this chapter, we will address the first notion, that of dimension of schemes. This should agree with, and generalize, our geometric intuition. Although we think of dimension as a basic notion in geometry, it is a slippery concept, as it is throughout mathematics. Even in linear algebra, the definition of dimension of a vector space is surprising the first time you see it, even though it quickly becomes second nature. The definition of dimension for manifolds is equally nontrivial. For example, how do we know that there isn’t an isomorphism between some twodimensional manifold and some three-dimensional manifold? Your answer will likely use topology, and hence you should not be surprised that the notion of dimension is often quite topological in nature.

A caution for those thinking over the complex numbers: our dimensions will be algebraic, and hence half that of the “real” picture. For example, we will see very shortly that $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } ,$ which you may picture as the complex numbers (plus one generic point), has dimension 1.
12.1 Dimension and Codimension

12.1.1. Definition(s): Dimension. Surprisingly, the right definition is purely topological—it depends just on the topological space, and not on the structure sheaf. We define the dimension of a topological space X (denoted by dim X) as the supremum of the lengths of the chains of closed irreducible sets, starting the indexing of the closed irreducible sets with 0. (The dimension may be infinite.) Scholars of the empty set can take the dimension of the empty set to be $- \infty$ . (An analogy from linear algebra: The dimension of a vector space is the supremum of the lengths of the chains of subspaces.) Define the dimension of a ring as the supremum of the lengths of the chains of nested prime ideals (where indexing starts at zero). These two definitions of dimension are sometimes called Krull dimension. (You might think a Noetherian ring has finite dimension because all chains of prime ideals are finite, but this isn’t necessarily true—see Exercise 12.1.M.)
12.1.A. EASY EXERCISE. Show that dim Spec A = dim A. (Hint: Exercise 3.7.F gives a bijection between irreducible closed subsets of Spec A and prime ideals of A. It is “inclusion-reversing.”)
The homeomorphism between Spec A and Spec A/ $\mathfrak { N } ( A )$ (§3.4.7: the Zariski topology disregards nilpotents) implies that dim Spec A = dim Spec A/N(A).

12.1.2. Examples. We have identified all the prime ideals of k[t] (they are 0, and (f(t)) for irreducible polynomials f(t)), Z ((0) and (p)), k (only (0)), and $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { k } } [ \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } ] / ( \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } ^ { 2 } )$ (only $( x )$ ), so we can quickly check that dim $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { 1 } = \dim \mathsf { S p e c } \mathbb { Z } = 1 .$ , dim Spec k = 0, dim Spec $\ k [ x ] / ( x ^ { 2 } ) = 0$ .

12.1.3. We must be careful with the notion of dimension for reducible spaces. If Z is the union of two closed subsets X and Y, then dim $Z = \operatorname* { m a x } ( \dim X , \dim Y )$ ). Thus dimension is not a “local” characteristic of a space. This sometimes bothers us, so we try to only talk about dimensions of irreducible topological spaces. We say a topological space is pure dimensional or equidimensional (resp., pure dimension n or equidimensional of dimension n) if each of its irreducible components has the same dimension (resp., they are all of dimension n). A pure dimension 1 (resp., 2, n) topological space is said to be a curve (resp., surface, n-fold).

12.1.B. USEFUL EXERCISE. Show that a scheme has dimension n if and only if it admits an open cover by affine open subsets of dimension at most n, where equality is achieved for some affine open subset. Hint: You may find it helpful, here and later, to show the following. For any topological space X and open subset $\mathrm { u } \subset \mathrm { X } ,$ there is a bijection between irreducible closed subsets of U, and irreducible closed subsets of X that meet U.
12.1.C. EASY EXERCISE. Show that a Noetherian scheme of dimension 0 has a finite number of points.
12.1.D. EASY EXERCISE. Show that a surjection of integral domains of the same finite dimension must be an isomorphism.

12.1.E. EXERCISE (FIBERS OF INTEGRAL MORPHISMS, PROMISED IN §8.3.8). Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is an integral morphism. Show that every (nonempty) fiber of π has dimension 0. Hint: As integral morphisms are preserved by base change (Exercise 10.4.D(d)), we assume that Y = Spec k. Hence we must show that if $\Phi \colon \ker  \lambda$ is an integral extension, then dim $A = 0$ . Outline of proof: Suppose ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset { \mathfrak { m } }$ are two prime ideals of A. Mod out by ${ \mathfrak { p } } ,$ so we can assume that A is an integral domain. I claim that any nonzero element is invertible: Say $\qquad x \in A ,$ and $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } \neq \boldsymbol { 0 }$ . Then the minimal monic polynomial for $x$ has nonzero constant term. But then $x$ is invertible—recall the coefficients are in a field.

12.1.F. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Show that if $\pi$ : Spec A Spec B corresponds to an integral extension of rings, then dim Spec A = dim Spec B. Hint: Show that a chain of prime ideals downstairs gives a chain upstairs of the same length, by the Going-Up Theorem (Exercise 8.2.F). Conversely, a chain upstairs gives a chain downstairs. Use Exercise 12.1.E to show that no two elements of the chain upstairs go to the same element $[ \mathsf { q } ] \in { \mathsf { S p e c } } \mathrm { B }$ of the chain downstairs.

12.1.G. EXERCISE. Show that if ν: $\widetilde { x } \to x$ is the normalization of a scheme (possibly in a finite extension of the function field of $x$ e →), then $\operatorname { l i m } { \widetilde { X } } = \dim X$ . Feel free to assume that X is integral for convenience.
12.1.H. USEFUL EXERCISE. Suppose X is an affine $\mathrm { k }$ -scheme, and $\mathsf { K } / \mathsf { k }$ is an algebraic field extension.

(a) Suppose X has pure dimension n. Show that $x _ { \kappa } { : = } x \times _ { \kappa } { \sf K }$ has pure dimension n. (See Exercise 24.5.F for a generalization, which, for example, removes the affine hypothesis. Also, see Exercise 12.2.M and Remark 12.2.17 for the fate of possible generalizations to arbitrary field extensions.) Hint: If ${ \cal X } = { \cal S } \mathrm { p e c } { \cal A } ,$ reduce to the case where A is an integral domain. An irreducible component of ${ \tt X } _ { \sf { K } }$ corresponds to a minimal prime $\mathfrak { p }$ of $\lambda _ { \mathsf { K } } : = A \otimes _ { \mathsf { k } } { \mathsf { K } }$ . Suppose $\mathfrak { a } \in \ker ( \mathsf { A } \longrightarrow \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { K } } / \mathfrak { p } )$ . Show that ${ \bf { a } } = 0$ , using the fact that a lies in a minimal prime $\mathfrak { p }$ of $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { K } }$ (and is hence a zerodivisor, by §6.6.23), and $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { K } }$ is a free A-module (so multiplication in $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { K } }$ by $\mathbf { a } \in \mathcal { A }$ is injective if a is nonzero). Thus $\lambda \to \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { K } } / \mathsf { p }$ is injective. Then use Exercise 12.1.F.
(b) Prove the converse to (a): Show that if ${ \tt X } _ { \sf { K } }$ →has pure dimension n, then X has pure dimension n.
12.1.I. EXERCISE. Show that dim $\mathbb { Z } [ x ] = 2$ . (Hint: The prime ideals of $\mathbb { Z } [ x ]$ were implicitly determined in Exercise 3.2.Q.)
12.1.4. Codimension. Because dimension behaves oddly for disjoint unions, we need some care when defining codimension, and in using the phrase. For example, if Y is a closed subset of $\Sigma ,$ we
Chapter 12 Dimension

might define the codimension to be dim X − dim Y, but this behaves badly. For example, if X is the disjoint union of a point Y and a curve Z, then d $\operatorname* { l i m } X - \dim Y = 1 .$ , but this has nothing to do with the local behavior of X near Y.

A better definition is as follows. In order to avoid excessive pathology, we define the codimension of Y in X only when Y is irreducible. (Use extreme caution when using this word in any other setting.) Define the codimension of an irreducible subset $\Upsilon \subset X$ of a topological space as the supremum of the lengths of increasing chains of irreducible closed subsets starting with Y (where indexing starts at 0—recall that the closure of an irreducible set is irreducible, Exercise 3.6.B(b)). In particular, the codimension of a point is the codimension of its closure. The codimension of Y in X is denoted by codimX Y.

We say that a prime ideal p in a ring has codimension equal to the supremum of the lengths of the chains of decreasing prime ideals starting at p, with indexing starting at 0. Thus in an integral domain, the ideal (0) has codimension 0; and in $\mathbb { Z } ,$ the ideal (23) has codimension 1. Note that the codimension of the prime ideal $\mathfrak { p }$ in A is dim $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ (see §3.2.9). (This notion is often called height.) Thus the codimension of $\mathfrak { p }$ in A is the codimension of [p] in Spec A.

(Continuing an analogy with linear algebra: the codimension of a vector subspace $\Upsilon \subset \Upsilon$ is the supremum of the lengths of increasing chains of subspaces starting with Y. This is a better definition than dim X − dim Y, because it works even when $\dim X = \infty$ . You might prefer to define codimX Y as $\dim ( X / Y )$ ; that is analogous to defining the codimension of $\mathfrak { p }$ in A as the dimension of $\lambda _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ —see the previous paragraph.)

12.1.J. EXERCISE. Show that if Y is an irreducible closed subset of a scheme $\Sigma ,$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { n } }$ is the generic point of Y, then the codimension of Y is the dimension of the local ring $\mathcal { O } _ { { X , \mathfrak { n } } }$ .

image_caption: Figure 12.1 Behavior of codimension.

text: 12.1.5. Notice that Y is codimension 0 in X if it is an irreducible component of X. Similarly, Y is codimension 1 if it is not an irreducible component, and for every irreducible component ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ it is contained in, there is no irreducible closed subset strictly between Y and ${ \mathsf { Y } } ^ { \prime }$ . (See Figure 12.1 for examples.) A closed subset all of whose irreducible components are codimension 1 in some ambient space X is said to be a hypersurface in X. (Be careful: The word “hypersurface” can be used to mean slightly different things in algebraic geometry, for entirely reasonable reasons; see, for example, Definition 9.3.2.)
12.1.K. EASY EXERCISE. If Y is an irreducible closed subset of a scheme X, show that
We will soon see that equality always holds if X and Y are irreducible varieties (Theorem 12.2.9), but equality doesn’t hold in general (§12.3.13).

Warning. The notion of codimension still can behave slightly oddly. For example, consider Figure 12.1. (You should think of this as an intuitive sketch.) Here the total space X has dimension 2, but point p is dimension 0, and codimension 1. We also have an example of a codimension 2 subset q contained in a codimension 0 subset C with no codimension 1 subset“in between.”

Worse things can happen; we will soon see an example of a closed point in an irreducible surface that is nonetheless codimension 1, not 2, in §12.3.13. However, for irreducible varieties this can’t happen, and inequality (12.1.5.1) must be an equality (Theorem 12.2.9).
12.1.6. In unique factorization domains, codimension 1 prime ideals are principal. We make a short observation, for future reference.
12.1.7. Lemma — In a unique factorization domain A, all codimension 1 prime ideals are principal.
12.1 Dimension and Codimension

This is a first glimpse of the fact that codimension 1 is rather special—this theme will continue in $\ S 1 2 . 3$ . We will see that the converse of Lemma 12.1.7 holds as well (when A is a Noetherian integral domain, Proposition 12.3.7).

Proof. Suppose $\mathfrak { p }$ is a codimension 1 prime. Choose any $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } \neq \boldsymbol { 0 }$ in ${ \mathfrak { p } } ,$ and let g be any irreducible/ prime factor of f that is in p (there is at least one). Then (g) is a nonzero prime ideal contained in p, so $( 0 ) \subset ( { \mathfrak { g } } ) \subset { \mathfrak { p } }$ . As p is codimension 1, we must have ${ \mathfrak { p } } = ( { \mathfrak { g } } )$ , and thus p is principal. □
12.1.8. Local rings of dimension 0.
Dimension 0 objects aren’t just points; they have further information, which might be thought of as some sort of infinitesimal “shape.” We caught a glimpse of this earlier in §6.5 and Exercise 8.3.G.
12.1.9. Useful proposition — Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring. Then the following are equivalent.

list_items: (i) $\dim { \cal A } = 0 ;$
(ii) ${ \mathfrak { m } } = { \mathfrak { N } } ( A ) _ { \cdot }$
(iii) for some $\mathsf n > 1$ , $\mathfrak { m } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0 ,$ ;
(iv) A has finite length;
(v) every descending sequence of ideals of A is eventually constant.

text: Such a ring is called an Artinian or finite length local ring (Definition 6.5.7).
12.1.L. EXERCISE. Prove Proposition 12.1.9.

12.1.10. A fun but unimportant counterexample. We end this introductory section with a fun pathology. As a Noetherian ring has no infinite chain of prime ideals, you may think that Noetherian rings must have finite dimension. Nagata, the master of counterexamples, shows you otherwise with the following example.

12.1.M.** EXERCISE: AN INFINITE-DIMENSIONAL NOETHERIAN RING. Let $\lambda = \operatorname { k } [ x _ { 1 }$ , $x _ { 2 } , \ldots ]$ . Choose an increasing sequence of positive integers $\mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } , \mathfrak { m } _ { 2 } , \dots$ whose differences are also increasing $\left( \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } - \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } } > \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } } - \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 } \right)$ ). Let ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } } = ( { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } } + 1 } , \dots , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } } )$ and $S = A \setminus \cup _ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ .

list_items: (a) Show that S is a multiplicative set.

(b) Show that $\mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 } \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ in ${ \mathsf { S } } ^ { - 1 } { \mathsf { A } }$ is the largest prime ideal in a chain of prime ideals of length $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } -$ $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . Hence conclude that $\mathrm { d i m } S ^ { - 1 } A = \infty$ .

(c) Suppose B is a ring such that (i) for every maximal ideal m, $\mathrm { B } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ is Noetherian, and (ii) every nonzero $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } \in \mathbb { B }$ is contained in finitely many maximal ideals. Show that B is Noetherian. (One possible approach: Show that for any $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ is finitely generated.)
(d) Use (c) to show that $S ^ { - 1 } A$ is Noetherian.

text: 12.1.11. Remark: Noetherian local rings have finite dimension. However, we shall see in Exercise 12.3.K(a) that Noetherian local rings always have finite dimension. (This requires a surprisingly hard fact, Krull’s Height Theorem 12.3.10.) Thus points of locally Noetherian schemes always have finite codimension.
12.2 Dimension, Transcendence Degree, and Noether Normalization

We now give a powerful alternative interpretation for dimension for irreducible varieties, in terms of transcendence degree. The proof will involve a classical construction, Noether normalization, which will be useful in other ways as well. In case you haven’t seen transcendence theory, here is a lightning introduction.
12.2.A. EXERCISE/DEFINITION. Recall that an element of a field extension E/F is algebraic over F if it is integral over F. Recall also that a field extension E/F is an algebraic extension if it is an
Chapter 12 Dimension

integral extension (if all elements of E are algebraic over F; cf. §8.2). The composition of two algebraic extensions is algebraic, by Exercise 8.2.C. If E/F is a field extension, and $\mathsf { F ^ { \prime } }$ and $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ are two intermediate field extensions, then we write $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime } \sim \mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ if $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime } \mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ is algebraic over both $\mathsf { F ^ { \prime } }$ and $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ . Here $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime } \mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ is the compositum of $\mathsf { F ^ { \prime } }$ and $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ , the smallest field extension in E containing $\mathsf { F ^ { \prime } }$ and $\mathsf { F } ^ { \prime \prime }$ . ]

list_items: (a) Show that $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on subextensions of E/F. A transcendence basis of E/F is a set of elements $\{ { \tt x } _ { \mathrm { i } } \}$ that are algebraically independent over F (there is no nontrivial polynomial relation among the $x _ { \mathrm { i } }$ with coefficients in F) such that $\mathsf { F } ( \{ \mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} ) \sim \mathsf { E }$ .

(b) Show that if E/F has two transcendence bases, and one has cardinality ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ then both have cardinality n. (Hint: Show that you can substitute elements from the one basis into the other one at a time.) The size of any transcendence basis is called the transcendence degree (which may be $\infty$ ), and is denoted by trdeg. Any finitely generated field extension necessarily has finite transcendence degree. (Remark: See Theorem 10.5.17 for a related result.)

text: 12.2.1. Theorem (dimension $=$ transcendence degree) — Suppose A is a finitely generated domain over a field k (i.e., a finitely generated k-algebra that is an integral domain). Then dim Spec $A =$ trdeg K(A)/k. Hence if X is an irreducible k-variety, then dim X = trdeg K(X)/k.

We will prove Theorem 12.2.1 shortly (§12.2.7). We first show that it is useful by giving some immediate consequences. We seem to have immediately $\dim \mathbb { A } _ { \boldsymbol { \mathbf { k } } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } = \mathfrak { n }$ . However, our proof of Theorem 12.2.1 will go through this fact, so it isn’t really a consequence.

A more substantive consequence is the following. If X is an irreducible k-variety, then dim X is the transcendence degree of the function field (the stalk at the generic point) $\mathcal { O } _ { X , \mathfrak { n } }$ over k. Thus (as the generic point lies in all nonempty open sets) the dimension can be computed in any open set of X. (Warning: This is false without the finite type hypothesis, even in quite reasonable circumstances—let X be the two-point space Spec $\operatorname { k } [ \mathbf { x } ] _ { ( \mathbf { x } ) } ,$ , and let U consist of only the generic point; see Exercise 3.4.K.)
Another consequence is a second proof of the Nullstellensatz 3.2.6.

12.2.B. EXERCISE: THE NULLSTELLENSATZ FROM DIMENSION THEORY. Suppose $A =$ $\mathsf { k } [ { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { \mathrm { n } } ] / \mathrm { I }$ . Show that the residue field of any maximal ideal of A is a finite extension of $\mathtt { k }$ . (Hint: The maximal ideals correspond to dimension 0 points, which correspond to transcendence degree 0 finitely generated extensions of ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ i.e., finite extensions of k.)
Yet another consequence is geometrically believable.

12.2.D. EXERCISE. Suppose $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } \left( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } \right) , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { m } } ( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } ) \in \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } ]$ are m polynomials in n variables over a field, with $\mathsf { m } > \mathsf { n }$ . Show that the polynomials $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { m } }$ are algebraically dependent. (Remark: If ${ \mathfrak { m } } = { \mathfrak { n } } ,$ how can you tell if the $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are algebraically dependent? There is a remarkably simple answer in characteristic 0: they are algebraically dependent if and only if the determinant of the Jacobian matrix is the zero polynomial; see Exercise 21.6.C! This is false in characteristic p: if $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } ( { \mathsf { x } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \mathsf { x } } _ { \mathrm { n } } ) = { \mathsf { x } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ , then the $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are algebraically independent, yet the Jacobian matrix is the zero matrix.)
12.2.E. EXERCISE (PRACTICE WITH THE CONCEPT). Show that the three equations

text:
$$
w z - x y = 0, \quad w y - x ^ {2} = 0, \quad x z - y ^ {2} = 0
$$

cut out an integral surface S in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 4 }$ . (You may recognize these equations from Exercises 3.6.F and 9.3.A.) You might expect S to be a curve, because it is cut out by three equations in four-space. One of many ways to proceed: cut S into pieces. For example, show that $\mathsf { D } ( w ) \cong S p e c \mathsf { k } [ x , w ] _ { w }$ . (You may recognize S as the affine cone over the twisted cubic. The twisted cubic was defined in
12.2 Dimension, Transcendence Degree, and Noether Normalization

Exercise 9.3.A.) It turns out that you need three equations to cut out this surface. The first equation cuts out a threefold in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 4 }$ (by Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem 12.3.3, which we will meet soon). The second equation cuts out a surface: our surface, along with another surface. The third equation cuts out our surface, and removes the “extraneous component.” One last aside: Notice once again that the cone over the quadric surface $\ k [ w , x , y , z ] / ( w z - x y )$ makes an appearance.

12.2.2. Definition: Degree of a rational map of irreducible varieties. If $\pi \colon X \mathrm { ~ -- \gamma > ~ } \Upsilon$ is a dominant rational map of irreducible (hence integral) k-varieties of the same dimension, the degree of the field extension is called the degree of the rational map. This readily extends if X is reducible: we add up the degrees on each of the components of X. If $\pi$ is a rational map of integral affine k-varieties of the same dimension that is not dominant, we say the degree is 0. We will interpret this degree in terms of counting preimages of general points of Y in §24.5.14. Note that degree is multiplicative under composition: If $\rho \colon { \mathsf { Y } } \mathrel { - } \to Z$ is a rational map of integral k-varieties of the same dimension, then $\deg ( \rho \circ \pi ) = \deg ( \rho ) \deg ( \pi ) .$ , as degrees of field extensions are multiplicative in towers.
12.2.3. Noether normalization.
Our proof of Theorem 12.2.1 will use another important classical notion, Noether normalization.

12.2.4. Noether Normalization Lemma — Suppose A is an integral domain, finitely generated over a field k. If trdeg ${ \sf K } ( { \sf A } ) / { \sf k } = { \sf n } ,$ , then there are elements $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \in A ,$ algebraically independent over k, such that A is a finite (hence integral by Corollary 8.2.2) extension of $\mathbf { k } [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ]$ .

The geometric content behind this result is that given any integral finite type affine k-scheme $\Sigma ,$ we can find a surjective finite morphism $\Chi \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ where n is the transcendence degree of the function field of X (over k). Surjectivity follows from the Lying Over Theorem 8.2.5, in particular, Exercise 12.1.F. This interpretation is sometimes called geometric Noether normalization. (See Remark 29.3.7 for a projective version of Noether normalization.)

12.2.5. Nagata’s proof of the Noether Normalization Lemma 12.2.4. Suppose we can write $A =$ $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { y } _ { 1 } , \hdots , \mathsf { y } _ { \mathrm { m } } ] / \mathsf { p } ,$ i.e., that A can be chosen to have m generators. Note that $\mathsf { m } \geq \mathsf { n }$ . We show the result by induction on m. The base case ${ \mathfrak { m } } = { \mathfrak { n } }$ is immediate.

Assume now that ${ \mathfrak { m } } > { \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and that we have proved the result for smaller m. We will find $\mathfrak { m } - 1$ elements $z _ { 1 } , \dotsc , z _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 }$ of A such that A is finite over $\mathsf { k } [ z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { \mathsf { m } - 1 } ] / \mathsf { q }$ (i.e., the subring of A generated by $z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { \mathrm { m - 1 } } ,$ where the $z _ { \mathrm { i } }$ satisfy the relations given by the ideal q). Then by the inductive hypothesis, $\mathsf { k } [ z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { \mathsf { m } - 1 } ] / \mathsf { q }$ is finite over some $\mathbb { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ] ,$ and A is finite over $\mathsf { k } [ z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 } ] ,$ , so by Exercise 8.3.H, A is finite over $\mathbf k [ \pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \dots , \pmb { x } _ { \mathrm n } ]$ .

As $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { m }$ are algebraically dependent in A, there is some nonzero algebraic relation $\mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { y } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathsf { y } _ { \mathsf { m } } ) = 0$ among them (where f is a polynomial in m variables over k).

$z _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } - y _ { m } ^ { \mathrm { r } _ { 1 } }$ $z _ { 2 } = y _ { 2 } - y _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \mathrm { r } _ { 2 } } , \ldots , z _ { \mathrm { m - 1 } } = y _ { \mathrm { m - 1 } } - y _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \mathrm { r _ { m } - 1 } }$ , where $\Upsilon _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \Upsilon _ { { \mathrm { m } } - 1 }$ are positive

Then upon expanding this out, each monomial in f (as a polynomial in m variables) will yield a single term that is a constant times a power of ${ \mathfrak { y } } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ (with no $z _ { \mathrm { i } }$ factors). By choosing the ${ \mathrm { \Delta } } { { \mathrm { r } } _ { \mathrm { i } } }$ so that
Chapter 12 Dimension

$0 \ll \mathfrak { r } _ { 1 } \ll \mathfrak { r } _ { 2 } \ll \cdots \ll \mathfrak { r } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 } ,$ , we can ensure that the powers of ${ \mathfrak { y } } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ appearing are all distinct, so that, in particular, there is a leading term $\mathfrak { y } _ { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { \mathsf { N } } .$ , and all other terms (including those with factors of $z _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ) are of smaller degree in $y _ { \mathrm { m } }$ . Thus we have described an integral dependence of ${ \mathfrak { y } } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ on $z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { \mathrm { m - 1 } } ,$ as desired. □

12.2.6. The geometry behind Nagata’s proof. Here is the geometric intuition behind Nagata’s argument. Suppose we have an n-dimensional variety in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { \mathrm { m } }$ with $\mathsf { n } < \mathsf { m } ,$ for example, $x y = 1$ in A2. One approach is to hope the projection to a hyperplane is a finite morphism. In the case of $x y = 1$ , if we projected to the $x$ -axis, it wouldn’t be finite, roughly speaking, because the asymptote $\boldsymbol { x } = 0$ prevents the map from being closed (cf. Exercise 8.3.K). If we instead projected to a random line, we might hope that we would get rid of this problem, and indeed we usually can: this problem arises for only a finite number of directions. But we might have a problem if the field were finite: perhaps the finite number of directions in which to project each has a problem. (You can show that if $\mathrm { k }$ is an infinite field, then the substitution in the above proof $z _ { \mathrm { i } } = y _ { \mathrm { i } } - y _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \mathrm { r _ { i } } }$ can be replaced by the linear substitution $z _ { \mathrm { i } } = y _ { \mathrm { i } } - { \tt a } _ { \mathrm { i } } { \tt y } _ { \mathrm { m } } ,$ where ${ \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathbb { k } ,$ and that for a nonempty Zariski-open choice of $\mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ we indeed obtain a finite morphism.) Nagata’s trick in general is to “jiggle” the variables in a nonlinear way, and this jiggling kills the nonfiniteness of the map.

12.2.F. EXERCISE (DIMENSION IS ADDITIVE FOR PRODUCTS OF FINITE TYPE K-SCHEMES). If X and Y are irreducible k-varieties, show that $\dim X \times _ { \mathbb { k } } \ Y = \dim X + \dim \ Y$ . Hint: If we had surjective finite morphisms $\begin{array} { r } { X \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \dim X } } \end{array}$ and $\Upsilon \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \dim \Upsilon }$ × , we could construct a surjective finite morphism $\mathsf X \times _ { \mathsf k } \mathsf Y \to \bar { \mathsf { A } } _ { \mathsf k } ^ { \dim \mathsf X + \dim \mathsf Y }$ → . (Warning: This can be false without the finite type assumption; see Remark 12.2.17.)

In particular, we have dim $\operatorname { A } [ x ] = \dim \operatorname { A } + 1$ when A is finitely generated over a field. (This is true more generally when A is Noetherian, although we will not prove this.)
12.2.G. LESS IMPORTANT (BUT FUN) EXERCISE.

list_items: (a) Suppose $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is an irreducible projective k-variety. Show that the affine cone and projective cone over X both have dimension $\dim X + 1$ .
(b) Show that a prime ideal of $\mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ that is minimal over a homogeneous ideal is also homogeneous.

(c) Suppose ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset { \mathfrak { q } }$ are two homogeneous prime ideals of $\mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ such that there is no homogeneous prime ideal strictly between the two. Show that there is no prime ideal (not necessarily homogeneous) strictly between the two.

text: 12.2.7. Proof of Theorem 12.2.1 on dimension and transcendence degree. Suppose X is an integral affine k-scheme of finite type. We show that dim X equals the transcendence degree n of its function field, by induction on n. (The idea is that we reduce from X to $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ to a hypersurface in $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ to $\mathbb { A } ^ { \mathrm { n - 1 } }$ .) Assume the result is known for all transcendence degrees less than n.

By the Noether Normalization Lemma 12.2.4, there exists a surjective finite morphism ${ X \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { n } }$ By Exercise 12.1.F, $\dim X = \dim \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ . If $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ , we are done, as dim $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 0 } = 0$ .

We now show that dim $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } = \mathrm { n }$ for $\mathfrak { n } > 0$ , by induction. Clearly, dim $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { { \mathrm { n } } } \geq { \mathrm { n } } ,$ , as we can describe a chain of irreducible subsets of length n: if $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are coordinates on $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ , consider the chain of ideals

$$
(0) \subset (x _ {1}) \subset \dots \subset (x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n})
$$
in $\mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ . Suppose we have a chain of prime ideals of length at least $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ :

$$
(0) = \mathfrak {p} _ {0} \subset \dots \subset \mathfrak {p} _ {m}.
$$

Choose any nonzero element g of ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ , and let f be any irreducible factor of g lying in ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ . Then replace ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ by (f). (Of course, ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ may have been (f) to begin with.) Then $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } ] / ( \mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } ) )$ ) has
12.2 Dimension, Transcendence Degree, and Noether Normalization
transcendence degree $\mathsf { n } - \mathsf { 1 }$ , so by induction,

$$
\dim k \left[ x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n} \right] / (f) = n - 1,
$$
so dim $\mathsf { k } [ { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { \mathrm { n } } ] \leq { \boldsymbol { n } } ,$ completing the proof.
12.2.8. Codimension is the difference of dimensions for irreducible varieties.
Noether normalization will help us show that codimension is the difference of dimensions for irreducible varieties, i.e., that the inequality (12.1.5.1) is always an equality.

12.2.9. Theorem — Suppose X is a pure-dimensional k-scheme locally of finite type (for example, an irreducible k-variety), Y is an irreducible closed subset, and η is the generic point of Y. Then $\dim \Upsilon +$ dim ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X , \eta } = \dim X$ . Hence by Exercise 12.1.J, dim Y + codimX Y $=$ dim X—inequality (12.1.5.1) is always an equality.

image_caption: Figure 12.2 Key step in proof of Theorem 12.2.9.

text: Proving this will give us an excuse to introduce some useful notions, such as the Going-Down Theorem for finite extensions of integrally closed domains (Theorem 12.2.12). Before we begin the proof, we give an algebraic translation.

12.2.H. EXERCISE. A ring A is called catenary if for every nested pair of prime ideals ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset { \mathfrak { q } } \subset A ,$ every strictly increasing chain of prime ideals “from $\mathfrak { p }$ to ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ is contained in a maximal such chain, and all maximal chains have the same (finite) length. (We will not use this term in any serious way later.) Show that if A is a localization of a finitely generated ring over a field ${ \sf k } ,$ then A is catenary.

12.2.10. Remark. Most rings arising naturally in algebraic geometry are catenary. Important examples include: localizations of finitely generated $\mathbb { Z }$ -algebras; complete Noetherian local rings; Dedekind domains (§13.5.11); and Cohen–Macaulay rings (see §26.2.13). It is hard to give an example of a noncatenary ring; see, for example, [Stacks, tag 02JE] or [He].
12.2.11. Proof of Theorem 12.2.9.

12.2.I. EXERCISE. Reduce the proof of Theorem 12.2.9 to the following problem. If X is an irreducible affine $\mathrm { k }$ -variety and Z is a closed irreducible subset maximal among those smaller than X (the only larger closed irreducible subset is X), then dim $Z = \dim X - 1$ .

Let $\mathbf { d } = \mathrm { d i m } \boldsymbol { x }$ for convenience. By Noether normalization 12.2.4, we have a finite morphism $\pi \colon X \to \mathbb { A } ^ { \mathrm { d } }$ corresponding to a finite extension of rings. Then $\pi ( Z )$ is an irreducible closed subset of $\mathbb { A } ^ { \mathrm { d } }$ (finite morphisms are closed, Exercise 8.3.L).

12.2.J. EXERCISE. Show that it suffices to show that $\pi ( Z )$ is a hypersurface. (Hint: The dimension of any hypersurface is $\mathrm { d } - 1$ by Theorem 12.2.1 on dimension and transcendence degree. Exercise 12.1.F implies that $\dim \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \pi ( Z ) ) = \dim \pi ( Z )$ . But be careful: Z is not $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \pi ( Z ) )$ in general.)

Now if $\pi ( Z )$ is not a hypersurface, then it is properly contained in an irreducible hypersurface $\mathsf { H } ,$ so by the Going-Down Theorem 12.2.12 for finite extensions of integrally closed domains (which we shall now prove), there is some closed irreducible subset $Z ^ { \prime }$ of X properly containing $Z ,$ contradicting the maximality of Z. □

12.2.12. Theorem (Going-Down Theorem for finite extensions of integrally closed domains) — Suppose ϕ: $\mathbb { B } \longrightarrow \mathbb { A }$ is a finite extension of rings (so A is a finite B-module), B is an integrally closed domain, and A is an integral domain. Then, given nested prime ideals ${ \mathfrak { q } } \subset { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ of B, and a prime $\mathfrak { p ^ { \prime } }$ of A lying over ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ (i.e., ${ \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \prime } \cap { \mathrm { B } } = { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime } ,$ ), there exists a prime p of A contained in $\mathfrak { p ^ { \prime } }$ , lying over q.
Chapter 12 Dimension

As usual, you should sketch a geometric picture of the statement. This theorem is usually stated in the context of extending a chain of ideals, in the same way as the Going-Up Theorem (Exercise 8.2.F), and you may want to think this through. (Another Going-Down Theorem, for flat morphisms, will be given in Exercise 24.5.E.)

This theorem is true more generally with “finite” replaced by “integral”; see [E, p. 291] (“Completion of the proof of $1 3 . 9 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ) for the extension of Theorem 12.2.12, or else see [AtM, Thm. 5.16] or [Mat1, Thm. 5(v)] for completely different proofs. See [E, Fig. 10.4] for an example (in the form of a picture) of why the “integrally closed” hypothesis on B cannot be removed.
In the course of the proof, we will use the following easy fact. We could prove it now, but we leave it until Exercise 12.3.E, because the proof uses a trick arising in Exercise 12.3.D.

12.2.13. Proposition (prime avoidance) — Suppose p1, . . . , pn are prime ideals of a ring A, and I is another ideal of A not contained in any ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . Then I is not contained in $\cup { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ : there is an element f  I not in any of the ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ .
(Can you give a geometric interpretation of this result? Can you figure out why it is called “prime avoidance”?)

12.2.14. Proof of Theorem 12.2.12 (Going-Down Theorem for finite extensions of integrally closed domains). The proof uses Galois theory. Let L be the normal closure of K(A)/K(B) (the smallest subfield of $\overline { { \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) } }$ containing K(A) that is mapped to itself by any automorphism over $\overline { { \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) } } / \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) .$ ). Let C be the integral closure of B in L (discussed in Exercise 10.7.I). Because $\lambda \hookrightarrow C$ is an integral extension, there is a prime Q of C lying over ${ \mathfrak { q } } \subset { \mathfrak { B } }$ (by the Lying Over Theorem 8.2.5), and a prime $\mathrm { \Delta } Q ^ { \prime }$ of C containing Q lying over ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ (by the Going-Up Theorem, Exercise 8.2.F). Similarly, there is a prime $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime }$ of C lying over ${ \mathfrak { p ^ { \prime } } } \subset A$ (and thus over ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime } \subset { \mathfrak { B } }$ ). We would be done if $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime } = \mathsf { Q } ^ { \prime }$ (just take ${ \mathfrak { p } } = \mathbf { Q } \cap { \mathfrak { A } }$ ), but this needn’t be the case. However, Lemma 12.2.15 below shows there is an automorphism $\sigma$ of C over B that sends $\mathrm { \Delta } Q ^ { \prime }$ to $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime }$ , and then the image of $\sigma ( Q )$ in A does the trick, completing the proof. The following diagram, in geometric terms, may help.

12.2.15. Lemma — Suppose B is an integrally closed domain, L/K(B) is a finite normal field extension, and C is the integral closure of B in L. If ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ is a prime ideal of B, then automorphisms of L/K(B) act transitively on the prime ideals of C lying over ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ .
This result is often first seen in number theory, with $\mathrm { B } = \mathbb { Z }$ and L a Galois extension of $\mathbb { Q }$ .

Proof. Let P and $Q _ { 1 }$ be two prime ideals of C lying over ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ , and let $\mathbf { Q } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathbf { Q } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ be the prime ideals of C conjugate to $Q 1$ (the image of $Q 1$ under $\operatorname { A u t } ( \mathrm { L } / \mathsf { K } ( \mathrm { B } ) )$ ). If P is not one of the $\mathrm { Q _ { i } } ,$ then P is not contained in any of the $\mathrm { Q } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . (Do you see why? Hint: Exercise 12.1.E or 12.1.F.) Hence by prime avoidance (Proposition 12.2.13), P is not contained in their union, so there is some ${ \mathfrak { a } } \in { \mathfrak { P } }$ not contained in any $\mathrm { Q } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . Thus no conjugate of a can be contained in $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { Q } } _ { 1 }$ , so the norm $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathrm { L } / \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) } ( \mathbf { a } ) \in \mathsf { B }$ is not contained in $\mathbf { Q } _ { 1 } \cap \mathbf { B } = \mathbf { q } ^ { \prime }$ . (Recall: If L/K(B) is separable, the norm is just the product of the conjugates. But even if L/K(B) is not separable, the norm is the product of conjugates to the appropriate
12.2 Dimension, Transcendence Degree, and Noether Normalization

power, because we can factor L/K(B) as a separable extension followed by a purely inseparable extension, or because we can take an explicit basis for the extension and calculate the norm of a. See [Lan, §VI.5] for more on norms.) But since ${ \mathfrak { a } } \in { \mathfrak { P } } .$ , its norm lies in P, but also in B, and hence in $\mathsf { P } \cap \mathsf { B } = \mathsf { q } ^ { \prime }$ , yielding a contradiction. □
We end with some exercises for your practice and enlightenment.

12.2.K. EXERCISE/DEFINITION. Suppose X is a finite-dimensional Noetherian scheme. Define a function $\dim _ { x } ( . ) : x \to \mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ from the underlying set of X, by taking $\mathrm { d i m } _ { \mathsf { X } } { \mathsf { p } }$ to be the dimension of the largest irreducible component of X containing p. We call this the dimension of X at p. Caution: This definition differs from [Stacks, tag 0055]. Show that $\mathrm { d i m } _ { X } ( \cdot )$ is an upper semicontinuous function. (Recall that a function f from a topological space $x$ to $\mathbb { R }$ is upper semicontinuous if for each $\boldsymbol { x } \in \mathbb { R } , \ : \mathsf { f } ^ { - 1 } ( ( - \infty , x ) )$ is open. Informally: the function can jump $^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { u p } ^ { \prime \prime }$ upon taking limits. Our upper semicontinuous functions will map to $\mathbb { Z } ,$ so informally functions jump $^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { u p } ^ { \prime \prime }$ on closed subsets. Similarly, f is lower semicontinuous if for each $\boldsymbol { x } \in \mathbb { R } , \boldsymbol { \mathrm { f } } ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( \boldsymbol { \mathrm { x } } , \infty \right) \right)$ is open.)
12.2.16. Recurring Theme: Semicontinuities. Semicontinuity is an important notion in algebraic geometry. Here are some examples.

list_items: (i) The rank of a matrix of functions (because rank drops on closed subsets, where various discriminants vanish),
(ii) the dimension of a variety at a point (Exercise 12.2.K above),
(iii) fiber dimension (Theorem 12.4.3),
(iv) the rank of a finite type quasicoherent sheaf (Exercise 14.3.J),
(v) the degree of a finite morphism, as a function of the target (§14.3.4),
(vi) the dimension of tangent space at closed points of a variety over an algebraically closed field (Exercise 21.2.G), and
(vii) the rank of cohomology groups of coherent sheaves, in proper flat families (the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1).

text: All but (i) are upper semicontinuous; (i) is a lower semicontinuous function.

12.2.L. EXERCISE. Suppose p is a closed point of a locally finite type k-scheme X. Show that the following three integers are the same: (i) $\mathrm { d i m } _ { \mathsf { X } } { \mathsf { p } }$ (Exercise 12.2.K); (ii) dim $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { p } } ;$ ; and (iii) codimX p. (We define the Zariski topology on the closed points of a finite type k-scheme in the obvious way: Closed subsets are cut out by equations, as in §8.4.8.)

12.2.M. EXERCISE (THE DIMENSION OF A LOCALLY FINITE TYPE K-SCHEME IS PRE-SERVED BY any FIELD EXTENSION; CF. EXERCISE 12.1.H(A)). Suppose X is a locally finite type k-scheme of pure dimension ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and K/k is a field extension (not necessarily algebraic). Show that $\mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { K } }$ has pure dimension n. Hint: Reduce to the case where X is affine, so, say, $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } =$ Spec A. Reduce to the case where A is an integral domain. Show (using the axiom of choice) that $\mathsf { K } / \mathsf { k }$ can be written as an algebraic extension of a purely transcendental extension. Hence by Exercise 12.1.H(a), it suffices to deal with the case where $\mathsf { K } / \mathsf { k }$ is purely transcendental, say, with transcendence basis $\{ e _ { \mathrm { i } } \} _ { \mathrm { i \in I } }$ (possibly infinite). Show that $\begin{array} { r } { A _ { \mathrm { K } } : = A \otimes _ { \mathrm { k } } { \sf K } } \end{array}$ is an integral domain, by interpreting it as a certain localization of the domain $A [ \{ e _ { \mathrm { i } } \} ]$ . If $\mathrm { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { d } }$ is a transcendence basis for ${ \sf K } ( { \sf A } ) / { \sf k } ,$ show that $\{ e _ { \mathrm { i } } \} \cup \{ \mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { j } } \}$ is a transcendence basis for ${ \sf K } ( \sf A _ { \sf K } ) / { \sf k }$ . Show that $\{ \mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { j } } \}$ is a transcendence basis for ${ \sf K } ( \sf A _ { \sf K } ) / { \sf K }$ .

Exercise 12.2.M is conceptually important. For example, if X is described by some equations with $\mathbb { Q }$ -coefficients, the dimension of X doesn’t depend on whether we consider it as a $\mathbb { Q }$ -scheme or as a $\mathbb { C }$ -scheme.

12.2.17. Remark. Unlike Exercise 12.1.H, Exercise 12.2.M has finite type hypotheses on X. It is not true that if X is an arbitrary k-scheme of pure dimension ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and $\mathsf { K } / \mathsf { k }$ is an arbitrary extension, then

$\mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { K } }$ necessarily has pure dimension n. For example, Exercise 10.2.L shows that if k is a field, then dim $\mathbf { k } ( x ) \otimes _ { \mathbf { k } } \mathbf { k } ( y ) = 1$ .
12.2.18.* Lines on hypersurfaces, part 1. Notice: Although dimension theory is not central to the following statement, it is essential to the proof.

(Possible hint for this: How many degree d hypersurfaces con- $\mathbb { P } ^ { \binom { \mathrm { d } + 3 } { 3 } - 1 - ( \mathrm { d } + 1 ) }$ $\mathbb { G } ( 1 , 3 )$ tain the line $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { x } = \mathsf { y } = 0 ? } \end{array}$ ) Show that dim $\mathbb { G } ( 1 , 3 ) = 4$ (see §7.7: $\mathbb { G } ( 1 , 3 )$ has an open cover by $\mathbb { A } ^ { 4 \prime } \mathbf { s } ^ { \cdot }$ ). The following diagram may help.

(The argument readily generalizes to show that if $d > 2 n - 3 ,$ , then “most” degree d hypersurfaces in $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ have no lines. The case $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ and ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 2$ are trivial but worth thinking through.)

12.2.19. Side remark. If you do the previous exercise, your dimension count will suggest the true facts that degree 1 hypersurfaces—i.e., hyperplanes—have two-dimensional families of lines, and that most degree 2 hypersurfaces have one-dimensional families (rulings) of lines, as shown in Exercise 9.3.L. They will also suggest that most degree 3 hypersurfaces contain a finite number of lines, which reflects the celebrated fact that regular cubic surfaces over an algebraically closed field always contain 27 lines (Theorem 27.0.1), and we will use this “incidence correspondence” or “incidence variety” to prove it (§27.3). The statement about quartic surfaces generalizes to the Noether–Lefschetz Theorem, implying that a very general surface of degree d at least 4 contains no curves that are not the intersection of the surface with a hypersurface; see [Lef, GH2]. The term “very general” means that in the parameter space (in this case, the projective space parametrizing surfaces of degree d), the statement is true away from a countable union of proper Zariski-closed subsets. Like “general” (which was defined in §10.3.7), “very general” is a weaker version of the phrase “almost every.”
12.3 Krull’s Theorems

We next introduce a number of results that we will repeatedly use, relating functions and the codimension 1 points where they vanish. The key result is Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem, which will be geometrically believable, and whose proof is short yet somehow nonintuitive and algebraic. This section will require Noetherian hypotheses.
12.3 Krull’s Theorems

12.3.1. Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem. In a vector space, a single linear equation always cuts out a subspace of codimension 0 or 1 (and codimension 0 occurs only when the equation is 0). The Principal Ideal Theorem, due to W. Krull, generalizes this linear algebra fact.
12.3.2. Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem (geometric version) — Suppose X is a locally Noetherian scheme, and f is a function. The irreducible components of V(f) are codimension 0 or 1.
This is clearly a consequence of the following algebraic statement.

12.3.3. Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem (algebraic version) — Suppose A is a Noetherian ring, and f A. Then every prime p minimal among those containing f has codimension at most 1. If furthermore f is not a zerodivisor, then every such prime p containing f has codimension precisely 1.

For example, locally principal closed subschemes have “codimension 0 or $1 , \prime \prime$ and effective Cartier divisors have “pure codimension 1.” Here is another example (which you could certainly prove directly, without the Principal Ideal Theorem).

12.3.A. EXERCISE. Show that an irreducible homogeneous polynomial in $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ variables over a field k describes an integral scheme of dimension $\mathfrak { n } - 1$ in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ .
You should just note how short the proof of Theorem 12.3.3 is, and skip ahead to §12.3.5 until you have used the theorem enough that you very much appreciate its value.

12.3.4.** Proof of Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem 12.3.3. We dispose of the last sentence first: if $\mathfrak { p }$ is codimension 0, then it is an associated prime (Exercise 6.6.M), and hence any element of $\mathfrak { p }$ (including f) is a zerodivisor (Proposition 6.6.13).

We next wish to show that codim $\mathfrak { p } \le 1$ . We reduce to the case of a local ring: replacing A by $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ (and p by ${ \mathfrak { p } } A _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ ) does not change the problem, so we may assume that $\mathfrak { p }$ is a maximal ideal.

Suppose otherwise that $\mathfrak { q }$ is a strictly smaller prime ideal of A (so $\textsf { f } \not \in { \mathfrak { q } }$ by the minimality of p among prime ideals containing f). We wish to show that $\mathfrak { q }$ has codimension 0.
The key idea of the proof is the following clever construction.

12.3.B. EXERCISE. Let $\Phi : \lambda  \lambda _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ be the localization, and define ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) } { : = } { \Phi } ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } { \ A } _ { \mathfrak { q } } )$ . (This is called the nth symbolic power of $\mathfrak { q } ,$ → but we will not use this terminology.) Note that ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( 1 ) } \supset { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( 2 ) } \supset \cdots$ is a descending sequence of ideals of A. Thus in the local ring A/(f), the nilradical ideal is the maximal ideal, so by Proposition 12.1.9 (on dimension 0 Noetherian local rings), any descending chain of ideals of $\mathsf { A } / ( \mathsf { f } )$ eventually stabilizes. We next claim that in fact ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) } = { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } + 1 ) } + { \mathfrak { f } } { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) }$ for ${ \mathfrak { n } } \geq { \mathfrak { n } } _ { 0 }$ . Reason: Suppose ${ \mathfrak { b } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \in { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) }$ . Then by (12.3.4.1), $\mathsf { b } _ { \mathsf { n } } = \mathsf { b } _ { \mathsf { n } + 1 } + \mathsf { a f }$ for $\mathsf { b } _ { \mathsf { n } + 1 } \in \mathsf { q } ^ { ( \mathsf { n } + 1 ) } \subset \mathsf { q } ^ { ( \mathsf { n } ) }$ and ${ \mathfrak { a } } \in { \mathcal { A } }$ . Hence af $\in \mathfrak { q } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) }$ ; but $\textsf { f } \not \in { \mathfrak { q } }$ . By Exercise 12.3.B, $\mathbf { a } \in \mathfrak { q } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) }$ , so ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) } = { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } + 1 ) } + { \mathfrak { f } } { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) }$ , as claimed.

Thus by Nakayama’s Lemma 8.2.9 (using A, M = q(n)/q(n+1), $\operatorname { I } = ( \mathsf { f } )$ ), ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } ) } = { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } + 1 ) }$ , from which ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } { \mathfrak { A } } _ { \mathfrak { q } } = { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \mathrm { n } + 1 } { \mathfrak { A } } _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ . By Nakayama’s Lemma 8.2.9 (using $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { A } _ { \mathfrak { q } } . } \end{array}$ , $M = \mathfrak { q } ^ { \mathrm { n } } A _ { \mathfrak { q } } , \mathrm { I } = \mathfrak { q } A _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ ), ${ \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } { \mathfrak { A } } _ { \mathfrak { q } } = 0 ,$ , so by Proposition 12.1.9, $\lambda _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ has dimension 0, so $\mathfrak { q }$ has codimension 0 in A, as desired. □

12.3.5. We now use Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem to prove some geometric facts. We start with a fun fact in preparation for Exercise 12.3.D(a), which will also be very useful in other circumstances.
