12.3.H. USEFUL EXERCISE. Suppose f is an element of a Noetherian ring A, contained in no codimension 0 or 1 prime ideal. Show that f is invertible. (Hint: If a function vanishes nowhere, it is invertible, by Exercise 4.3.F(b).)

text: 12.3.6. A useful characterization of unique factorization domains.
We can use Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem to prove one of the four useful criteria for unique factorization domains, promised in §5.4.5.
12.3 Krull’s Theorems
12.3.7. Proposition — Suppose that A is a Noetherian integral domain. Then A is a unique factorization domain if and only if all codimension 1 prime ideals are principal.
This contains Lemma 12.1.7 and (in some sense) its converse.

Proof. One direction is Lemma 12.1.7: if A is a unique factorization domain, then all codimension 1 prime ideals are principal. So we assume conversely that all codimension 1 prime ideals of A are principal, and we will show that A is a unique factorization domain. Now each codimension 1 prime ideal, being principal, is generated by a prime element, which is unique up to multiplication by a unit. All prime elements of an integral domain are irreducible, by the usual argument. (Suppose ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in A$ is prime, and ${ \mathrm { p } } = { \mathrm { a b } } ,$ with ${ \mathfrak { a } } , { \mathfrak { b } } \in A \setminus \{ 0 \}$ . Then by primality of the ideal (p), either a or b is in (p), say, without loss of generality ${ \mathrm { \mathbf { a } } } = { \mathrm { k } } { \mathrm { \mathbf { p } } }$ . Then ${ \mathrm { p } } = { \mathrm { k p } } { \mathrm { b } } $ , from which $1 = \mathrm { b k } ,$ from which b is unit. Thus p is irreducible.) Conversely, if ${ \mathfrak { a } } \in { \mathcal { A } }$ is irreducible, then by Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem 12.3.3, V(a) contains some codimension 1 point [(p)], so ${ \mathfrak { a } } \in ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ , say, ${ \mathrm { a } } = { \mathrm { k p } }$ . But by irreducibility of $\mathbf { a } ,$ as p is not a unit, k must be a unit, from which $\mathbf { \rho } ( \mathbf { a } ) = ( \mathfrak { p } )$ , so a is prime. Thus we have shown that the prime elements are the irreducible elements (and, incidentally, that up to multiplicative units, they correspond to the codimension 1 prime ideals).

Finally, A is a unique factorization domain by the usual argument (e.g., [DF, §8.3]): Each nonunit nonzero element can be factored into a finite number of irreducibles, and the uniqueness of such a factorization can be shown using the fact that those irreducibles are also prime. □
12.3.8. Using Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem inductively. The following result will allow us to use Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem inductively.

12.3.9. Lemma — Suppose ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 0 } \subsetneq { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is a strict chain of prime ideals in a Noetherian ring A, and $\mathsf { f } \in \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \setminus \mathfrak { p } _ { 0 }$ . Then there exists a strict chain of prime ideals
in A, with $f \in { \mathfrak { q } } _ { 1 } \setminus { \mathfrak { q } } _ { 0 }$ .

You should feel compelled to immediately sketch a picture of this lemma. We are given Spec A, and a nested sequence of irreducible closed subsets, as well as a “hypersurface” containing the smallest closed subset. We are going to create a new sequence of irreducible closed subsets, with the same start and finish, which are all (after the first) contained in the hypersurface.
Proof. Our plan is to create the ideals as follows:

Unsurprisingly, we do this by descending induction. We prove the following. Suppose ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 2 } \subset$ ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 } \subset { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ with $\mathsf { f } \in \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { j } } \setminus \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { j } - 2 }$ . We seek a prime ideal $\mathfrak { q } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 }$ containing ${ \sf f } ,$ with ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 2 } \subsetneq { \mathfrak { q } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 } \subsetneq { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ . Take $\mathfrak { q } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 }$ to be a prime containing $( { \mathsf { f } } , { \mathsf { p } } _ { { \mathsf { j } } - 2 } ) ,$ , and minimal among those contained in ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ . Now $\mathfrak { q } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 }$ is codimension 1 in $\mathsf { A } / \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { j } - 2 }$ by Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem, and ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ is codimension at least 2 (we have a chain ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 2 } \subset { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 } \subset { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ of length 2). Thus ${ \mathfrak { q } } _ { \mathrm { j } - 1 } \subsetneq { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ . □
12.3.I. EXERCISE. Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring, and $\mathsf { f } \in \mathfrak { m }$ . Show that $\dim { A / ( \mathsf { f } ) } \geq$ dim A − 1.
The following generalization of Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem looks like it might follow by induction from Krull, but it is more subtle.

12.3.10. Krull’s Height Theorem — Suppose $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } =$ Spec A, where A is Noetherian, and Z is an irreducible component of $\mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { r } } )$ , where $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \hdots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } \in \boldsymbol { A }$ . Then the codimension of Z is at most r.
(As mentioned in §12.1.4, height is another common word for codimension.)
Chapter 12 Dimension

Prove Krull’s Height Theorem 12.3.10 by reducing it to the case of a Noetherian local ring $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ , where ${ \cal Z } = \mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { m } )$ , and then using Lemma 12.3.9 for the $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ in order.
12.3.K. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring.

Hence show that A has finite dimension. (For comparison, Noetherian rings in general may have infinite dimension; see Exercise 12.1.M.)

Show that there exist ${ \mathfrak { g } } _ { 1 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { g } } _ { \mathrm { d } } \in { \bar { A } }$ such that $\mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { g } _ { 1 } , \hdots , \mathsf { g } _ { \mathrm { d } } ) = \{ [ \mathsf { m } ] \}$ .

text: 12.3.11. Definition. The geometric translation of the above exercise is the following. Given a ddimensional “germ of a reasonable space” around a point p, p can be cut out set-theoretically by d equations, and we always need at least d equations. These d elements of A are called a system of parameters at p. Essentially equivalently, if $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension $\mathrm { d } ,$ then $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { 1 } , \dots , \boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { \mathrm { d } }$ is a system of parameters for $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ if $\mathfrak { m } { = } \sqrt { ( \mathfrak { f } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathfrak { f } _ { \mathrm { d } } ) }$ .
The following result will be central in the next chapter.
12.3.12. Theorem — For any Noetherian local ring (A, m, k), dim A ≤ dimk m/m2.

Proof. Now m is finitely generated, so ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ is a finitely generated $( A / \mathfrak { m } = \mathfrak { k } )$ -module, hence finite-dimensional. Say $\dim _ { \mathrm { k } } { \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 } = { \mathfrak { n } }$ . Choose a basis of ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ , and lift it to elements $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ of m. By Nakayama’s Lemma (version 4, Exercise 8.2.H), $( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { n } } ) = \mathsf { m }$ . By Exercise 12.3.K, $\dim A \leq n$ . □
We conclude with two starred sections.

12.3.13.* Pathologies of the notion of “codimension”. We can use Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem to produce the example of pathology in the notion of codimension promised earlier in this chapter. Let $\mathsf { A } = \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } ] _ { ( \mathsf { x } ) } [ \mathsf { t } ]$ . In other words, elements of A are polynomials in $\mathrm { t , }$ whose coefficients are quotients of polynomials in $x ,$ where no factors of $x$ appear in the denominator. (Warning: A is not $\mathbb { k } [ \mathbf { x } , \mathbf { t } ] _ { ( \mathbf { x } ) }$ .) Clearly, A is an integral domain, so $\boldsymbol { x } \mathrm { t } - 1$ is not a zerodivisor. You can verify that $\mathsf { A } / ( \mathsf { x t } - 1 ) \cong \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } ] _ { ( \mathsf { x } ) } [ 1 / \mathsf { x } ] \cong \mathsf { k } ( \mathsf { x } ) \mathrm { - } \mathrm { } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { i n ~ } \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } ] _ { ( \mathsf { x } ) } ,$ $\operatorname { k } [ \mathbf { x } ] _ { ( \mathbf { x } ) } ,$ we may divide by everything but $x ,$ and now we are allowed to divide by $x$ as well”—so $ { \lambda } / (  { \boldsymbol { { x } } } \mathrm { t } - 1 )$ is a field. Thus $( x \mathrm { t } - 1 )$ is not just prime but also maximal. By Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem, $( \mathsf { x t } - 1 )$ is codimension 1. Thus $( 0 ) \subset ( { \mathfrak { x } } \mathbf { t } - 1 )$ is a maximal chain. However, A has dimension at least 2: $( 0 ) \subset ( { \mathrm { t } } ) \subset ( { \mathrm { x } } , { \mathrm { t } } )$ is a chain of prime ideals of length 2. (In fact, A has dimension precisely 2, although we don’t need this fact in order to observe the pathology.) Thus we have a codimension 1 prime in a dimension 2 ring whose quotient is dimension 0, not 1. Here is a picture of this partially ordered set of ideals.

This example comes from geometry, and it is enlightening to draw a picture; see Figure 12.3. Spec $\operatorname { k } [ x ] _ { ( \mathbf { x } ) }$ corresponds to a $\prime \prime \mathrm { g e r m } ^ { \prime \prime }$ of $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ near the origin, and Spec $\operatorname { k } [ x ] _ { ( \mathbf { x } ) }$ [t] corresponds to “this $\times$ the affine line.” You may be able to see from the picture some motivation for this pathology— ${ \mathsf { V } } ( { \mathsf { x t } } - 1 )$ doesn’t meet $\mathsf { V } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ , so it can’t have any specialization on ${ \mathsf { V } } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ) .$ , and there is nowhere else for ${ \mathsf { V } } ( { \mathsf { x t } } - 1 )$ to specialize. It is disturbing that this misbehavior turns up even in a relatively benign-looking ring.

text: 12.3.15. Remark. A projective (or proper) $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ -variety X is uniruled if every point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ is contained in some $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \subset X$ . (We won’t use this word beyond this remark.) Part (b) shows that all hypersurfaces of degree at most $\mathfrak { n } - 1$ are uniruled. One can show (using methods beyond what we know now; see [Ko1, Cor. IV.1.11]) that if char $\overline { { \mathsf { k } } } = 0$ , then every smooth hypersurface of degree at least $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ in $\mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is not uniruled (thus making the open set in (c) explicit). Furthermore, smooth hypersurfaces of degree n are uniruled, but covered by conics rather than lines. Thus there is a strong difference in how hypersurfaces behave depending on how the degree relates to $\mathsf { n } + \mathsf { 1 } .$ . This is true in many other ways as well. Smooth hypersurfaces of degree less than $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ are examples of Fano varieties; smooth hypersurfaces of degree $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ are examples of Calabi-Yau varieties (with the possible exception of $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ , depending on the definition); and smooth hypersurfaces of degree greater than $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ are examples of general type varieties. We define these terms in §21.5.5.
12.4 Dimensions of Fibers of Morphisms of Varieties

In this section, we show that the dimensions of fibers of morphisms of varieties behave in a way you might expect from our geometric intuition. The reason we have waited until now to discuss this is because we will use Theorem 12.2.9 (for varieties, codimension is the difference of dimensions).

Before we begin, let’s make sure we are on the same page with respect to our intuition. Elimination theory (Theorem 8.4.10) tells us that the projection $\pi \colon { \mathbb { P } } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda$ is closed. We can interpret this as follows. A closed subset X of $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is cut out by a bunch of homogeneous equations in $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ variables (over A). The image of X is the subset of Spec A where these equations have a common nontrivial solution. If we try hard enough, we can describe this by saying that the existence of a nontrivial solution (or the existence of a preimage of a point under $\pi \colon X \to S \mathrm { { p e c } } A )$ is an “upper semicontinuous” fact. More generally, your intuition might tell you that the locus where a number of homogeneous polynomials in $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ variables over A have a solution space (in $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ ) of dimension
Chapter 12 Dimension

at least d should be a closed subset of Spec A. (As a special case, consider linear equations. The condition for m linear equations in $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ variables to have a solution space of dimension at least $\mathrm { d } + \rceil$ is a closed condition on the coefficients—do you see why, using linear algebra?) This intuition will be correct, and will use properness in a fundamental way (Theorem 12.4.3(b)). We will also make sense of upper semicontinuity in fiber dimension on the source (Theorem 12.4.3(a)). A useful example to think through is the map from the xy-plane to the $_ { x z }$ -plane (Spec k[x, y] Spec k[x, z]), given by $( x , z ) \mapsto ( x , x y )$ . (This example also came up in §8.4.1.)
We begin our substantive discussion with an inequality that holds more generally in the locally Noetherian setting.

12.4.1. Theorem — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a finite type dominant morphism of integral schemes such that the finitely generated field extension ${ \sf K } ( { \sf X } ) / { \sf K } ( { \sf Y } )$ has transcendence degree r. Then there exists a nonempty open subset U  Y such that for all $\mathbf { q } \in \mathrm { U } ,$ the fiber over q is nonempty and has pure dimension r.

An interesting fact: We will use the Noether Normalization Lemma 12.2.4 in the proof, even though the statement doesn’t involve varieties! If the generality of the statement bothers you, we have the following special case for varieties.
12.4.2. Corollary — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a (necessarily finite type) morphism of irreducible k-varieties, with $\dim X = m$ and

dim Y = n. Then there exists a nonempty open subset U Y such that for all $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { U } ,$ , the fiber over q has pure dimension ${ \mathfrak { m } } - { \mathfrak { n } } ,$ , or is empty.
(By convention, the empty set technically has pure dimension r—every irreducible component of the empty set has dimension r. This makes the statement of Corollary 12.4.2 a bit cleaner.)
12.4.B. EXERCISE. Verify that Theorem 12.4.1 implies Corollary 12.4.2.

Proof of Theorem 12.4.1. We begin with two quick reductions. (i) By shrinking Y if necessary, we may assume that Y is affine, say Spec B. (ii) We may also assume that X is affine, say Spec A. (Reason: Cover X with a finite number of affine open subsets $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { \mathrm { a } } ,$ , and take the intersection of the U’s for each of the $\pi | _ { X _ { \mathrm { i } } }$ .)

In order to motivate the rest of the argument, we describe our goal. We will produce a nonempty distinguished open subset U of Spec B so that $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } ) \to \mathsf { U }$ factors through $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { U } } ^ { \mathrm { r } }$ via a finite surjective morphism:

This will show that the dimension of any component of $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ has dimension at most r. To show equality, apply the Going-Down Theorem 12.2.12 appropriately to Spec $A $ Spec $\mathbb { B } [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { r } ]$ .

So we now work to build (12.4.2.1). We begin by noting that we have inclusions of B into both A and K(B), and from both A and K(B) into K(A). The maps from A and K(B) into K(A) both factor through $\lambda \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } { \sf K } ( { \sf B } )$ (whose Spec is the generic fiber of $\pi$ ), so the maps from both A and K(B) to $\lambda \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } { \sf K } ( { \sf B } )$ must be inclusions.

Clearly, $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { A } ) \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) = \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { A } )$ (as $\lambda \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } { \sf K } ( { \sf B } )$ can be interpreted as taking A and inverting the elements that are nonzero elements of B), and $\lambda \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } { \sf K } ( { \sf B } )$ is a finitely generated algebra over the field K(B).

By Noether normalization 12.2.4, we can find elements $\mathrm { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { t } _ { r } \in \mathsf { A } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) ,$ algebraically independent over K(B), such that $\lambda \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } { \sf K } ( { \sf B } )$ is integral over $\mathsf { K } ( \mathrm { B } ) [ \mathsf { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { t } _ { \mathrm { r } } ]$ .

Now, we can think of the elements $\mathfrak { t } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \ A \otimes _ { \mathtt { B } } \mathsf { K } ( \mathtt { B } )$ as fractions, with numerators in A and (nonzero) denominators in B. If f is the product of the denominators appearing for each $\mathrm { t _ { i } } ,$ then by replacing B by $\mathrm { B _ { f } }$ (replacing Spec B by its distinguished open subset D(f)), we may assume that the $\mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are all in A. Thus (after sloppily renaming $\mathrm { { B _ { f } } }$ as B, and $A _ { \mathrm { f } }$ as A) we can trim and extend (12.4.2.2) to the following.

Now A is finitely generated over B, and hence over $\mathbb { B } [ \mathrm { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { t } _ { r } ]$ . But we cannot yet be sure that A is finite over $\mathrm { B } [ \mathrm { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { t } _ { r } ] ,$ so we are not done. We will have to localize B further.

Suppose A is generated over B by $\mathfrak { u } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathfrak { u } _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ . Our Noether normalization argument implies that each $\mathfrak { u } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ satisfies some monic equation $\mathrm { f _ { i } } ( \mathrm { u _ { i } } ) { = } 0 $ , where $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) [ \mathsf { t } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { \ldots } , \mathsf { t } _ { r } ] [ \mathsf { t } ]$ (i.e., monic
Chapter 12 Dimension

considered as a polynomial in t with coefficients in $\mathsf { K } ( \mathrm { B } ) [ \mathsf { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { t } _ { r } ] )$ . The coefficients of $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (considered as polynomials in $\mathrm { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { t } _ { r } ,$ t) are a priori fractions in B, but by multiplying by all those denominators, we can assume each $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathsf { B } [ \mathsf { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { t } _ { r } ] [ \mathsf { t }$ ], at the cost of losing monicity of the fi (this time considered as polynomials in t). Let $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } \in \mathbb { B }$ be the product of the leading coefficients (considered as polynomials in t) of all the $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . If $\mathsf { U } = \mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { b } )$ (the locus where b is invertible), then, over U, the $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (can be taken to) have leading coefficient 1, so the $\mathfrak { u } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (in Ab) are integral over $\mathrm { B _ { b } [ t _ { 1 } , \ldots , t _ { r } ] }$ . Thus Spec $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathrm { b } } \longrightarrow \mathsf { S p e c B } _ { \mathrm { b } } \left[ \mathsf { t } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { t } _ { \mathrm { r } } \right]$ is finite and surjective (the latter by the Lying Over Theorem 8.2.5).
We have now constructed (12.4.2.1), as desired.

There are a couple of things worth pointing out about the proof. First, although the result was originally motivated by classical varieties over a field k, the proof uses the theory of varieties over another field, the function field K(B). This is an example of how the introduction of generic points to algebraic geometry is useful even for considering more “classical” questions.

Second, the idea of the main part of the argument is that we have a result over the generic point (Spec A ⊗B K(B) is finite and surjective over affine space over K(B)), and we want to “spread it out” to an open neighborhood of the generic point of Spec B. We do this by realizing that “finitely many denominators” appear when correctly describing the problem, and inverting those denominators. This “spreading out” idea is a recurring theme.

12.4.3. Theorem (upper semicontinuity of fiber dimension) — Suppose π: X Y is a morphism of finite type k-schemes.

list_items: (a) (upper semicontinuity on the source) The dimension of the fiber of π at ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ (the dimension of the largest component of $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) )$ containing p) is an upper semicontinuous function in p (i.e., on X).

(b) (upper semicontinuity on the target) If furthermore π is closed (e.g., if π is proper), then the dimension of the fiber of π over $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ is an upper semicontinuous function in q (i.e., on Y).

text: You should be able to immediately construct a counterexample to part (b) if the closedness hypothesis is dropped. (We also remark that Theorem 12.4.3(b) for projective morphisms is done, in a simple way, in Exercise 18.1.B.)

Proof. (a) Let $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ be the subset of X consisting of points where the fiber dimension is at least n. We wish to show that $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ is a closed subset for all n. We argue by induction on dim Y. The base case $\dim \Upsilon = 0$ is trivial. So we fix Y, and assume the result for all smaller-dimensional targets.

Let r = dim X − dim Y be the “relative dimension” of π. If ${ \mathfrak { n } } \leq { \mathfrak { r } } $ , then $\digamma _ { \mathfrak { n } } = X$ by Exercise 12.4.A (combined with Theorem 12.2.9, that codimension is the difference of dimensions for varieties).

If ${ \mathfrak { n } } > { \mathfrak { r } } ,$ , then let $\mathsf { U } \subset \mathsf { Y }$ be the dense open subset of Theorem 12.4.1, where “the fiber dimension is exactly r.” Then $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ does not meet the preimage of U. By replacing Y with Y \ U (and X by $x \setminus \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } ) ,$ , we are done by the inductive hypothesis.

Chapter 12 Dimension
Chapter 13
Regularity and Smoothness

One natural notion we expect to see for geometric spaces is the notion of when an object is “smooth.” In algebraic geometry, this notion, called regularity, is easy to define (Definition 13.2.1) but a bit subtle in practice. This will lead us to a different related notion of when a variety is smooth (Definition 13.2.4).

This chapter has many moving parts, of which §13.1–13.6 are the important ones. In §13.1, the Zariski tangent space is motivated and defined. In §13.2, we define regularity and smoothness over a field, the central topics of this chapter, and discuss some of their important properties. In §13.3, we give a number of important examples, mostly in the form of exercises. In §13.4, we discuss Bertini’s Theorem, a fundamental classical result. In $\ S 1 3 . 5 ,$ we give many characterizations of discrete valuation rings, which play a central role in algebraic geometry. Having seen clues that “smoothness” is a “relative” notion rather than an “absolute” one, in $\ S 1 3 . 6$ we define smooth morphisms (and in particular, étale morphisms), and give some of their properties. (We will revisit this definition in §21.2.28, once we know more.)

The remaining sections are less central. In $\ S 1 3 . 7$ , we discuss the valuative criteria for separatedness and properness. In $\ S 1 3 . 8 ,$ we mention some more sophisticated facts about regular local rings (the local rings at regular points). In $\ S 1 3 . 9 ,$ we prove the Artin-Rees Lemma, because it will have been invoked in §13.5 (and will be used later as well).
13.1 The Zariski Tangent Space

We begin by defining the tangent space of a scheme at a point. It behaves like the tangent space you know and love at “smooth” points, but also makes sense at other points. In other words, geometric intuition at the “smooth” points guides the definition, and then the definition guides the algebra at all points, which in turn lets us refine our geometric intuition.

The definition is short but surprising. The main difficulty is convincing yourself that it deserves to be called the tangent space. This is tricky to explain, because we want to show that it agrees with our intuition, but our intuition is worse than we realize. So you should just accept this definition for now, and later convince yourself that it is reasonable.

13.1.1. Definition. The Zariski cotangent space of a local ring $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is defined to be ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ ; it is a vector space over the residue field $A / { \mathfrak { m } }$ . The dual vector space is the Zariski tangent space. If X is a scheme, the Zariski cotangent space $\mathsf { T } _ { X , \mathsf { p } } ^ { \vee }$ at a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ is defined to be the Zariski cotangent space of the local ring ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X , { \mathfrak { p } } } ,$ and similarly for the Zariski tangent space ${ \sf T } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } }$ . (Note that for any maximal ideal m of any ring A, we have canonically $\mathfrak { m } / \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 } = \mathfrak { m }  { \mathrm { A } _ { \mathfrak { m } } } / \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 }  { \mathrm { A } _ { \mathfrak { m } } }$ —make sure you see why.) Elements of the Zariski cotangent space are called cotangent vectors or differentials; elements of the tangent space are called tangent vectors.

The cotangent space is more algebraically natural than the tangent space, in that the definition is shorter. There is a moral reason for this: the cotangent space is more naturally determined in terms of functions on a space, and we are very much thinking about schemes in terms of “functions on them.” This will come up later.
Here are two plausibility arguments that this is a reasonable definition. Hopefully one will catch your fancy.

In differential geometry, the tangent space at a point is sometimes defined as the vector space of derivations at that point. A derivation at a point p of a manifold is an $\mathbb { R }$ -linear operation that takes in functions f near p (i.e., elements of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ ), and outputs elements $\mathsf { f ^ { \prime } } ( \mathsf { p } )$ of $\mathbb { R } ,$ and satisfies the
Leibniz rule

$$
(f g) ^ {\prime} = f ^ {\prime} g + g ^ {\prime} f.
$$

(We will later define derivations in a more general setting, §21.2.17.) A derivation is the same as a map ${ \mathfrak { m } } \to \mathbb { R } ,$ where m is the maximal ideal of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ . (The map $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { p } } \to \mathbb { R }$ extends this, via the map $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { p } } \to \mathfrak { m }$ given by $\mathsf { f } - \mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { p } )$ .) But ${ \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ maps to 0, as if ${ \sf f } ( { \sf p } ) = { \sf g } ( { \sf p } ) = 0$ , then

$$
(f g) ^ {\prime} (p) = f ^ {\prime} (p) g (p) + g ^ {\prime} (p) f (p) = 0.
$$
Thus a derivation induces a map ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { R } ,$ i.e., an element of $( \mathfrak { m } / \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \vee }$

13.1.A. EXERCISE. Check that this is reversible, i.e., that any linear map $\mathfrak { m } / \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { R }$ gives a derivation. In other words, verify that the Leibniz rule holds.

Here is a second, vaguer, motivation that this definition is plausible for the cotangent space of the origin of An. (I prefer this one, as it is more primitive and elementary.) Functions on $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ should restrict to a linear function on the tangent space. What (linear) function does $\ x ^ { 2 } + x y + x + y$ restrict to “near the origin”? You will naturally answer: $x + y$ . Thus we “pick off the linear terms.” Hence ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ are the linear functionals on the tangent space, so ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ is the cotangent space. In particular, you should picture functions vanishing at a point (i.e., lying in m) as giving functions on the tangent space in this obvious way.

13.1.2. Old-fashioned example. Computing the Zariski tangent space is actually quite hands-on, because you can compute it just as you did when you learned multivariable calculus. In $\mathbb { A } ^ { 3 }$ , we have a curve cut out by $x + y + z ^ { 2 } + x y z = 0$ and $x - 2 y + z + x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } z ^ { 3 } = 0$ . (You can use Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem 12.3.3 to check that this is a curve, but it is not important to do so.) What is the tangent line near the origin? (Is it even smooth there?) Answer: The first surface looks like $\ x + y = 0$ and the second surface looks like $\ x - 2 y + z = 0$ . The curve has tangent line cut out by $\ x + y = 0$ and $\ x - 2 y + z = 0$ . It is smooth (in the traditional sense). In multivariable calculus, the students work hard to get the answer, because we aren’t allowed to tell them to just pick out the linear terms.

Let’s make explicit the fact that we are using. If A is a ring, m is a maximal ideal, and $\mathsf { f } \in \mathfrak { m }$ is a function vanishing at the point $[ { \mathfrak { m } } ] \in S { \mathrm { p e c } } A ,$ , then the Zariski tangent space of Spec A/(f) at m is cut out in the Zariski tangent space of Spec A (at m) by the single linear equation f (mod ${ \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ ). The next exercise will force you to think this through.

13.1.B. IMPORTANT EXERCISE (“KRULL’S PRINCIPAL IDEAL THEOREM FOR TANGENT SPACES”—BUT MUCH EASIER THAN KRULL’S PRINCIPAL IDEAL THEOREM 12.3.3!). Suppose A is a ring, and m a maximal ideal. If $\mathsf { f } \in \mathfrak { m }$ , show that the Zariski tangent space of $A / \mathsfit { f }$ is cut out in the Zariski tangent space of A by f (mod ${ \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ ). (Note: We can quotient by f and localize at m in either order, as quotient and localization commute, (4.3.7.1).) Hence the dimension of the Zariski tangent space of Spec A/(f) at [m] is the dimension of the Zariski tangent space of Spec A at [m], or one less. (That last sentence should be suitably interpreted if the dimension is infinite, although it is less interesting in this case.)

Here is another example to see this principle in action, extending Example 13.1.2: $x + y + z ^ { 2 } = 0$ and $x + y + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 4 } + z ^ { 5 } = 0$ cut out a curve, which obviously passes through the origin. If I asked my multivariable calculus students to calculate the tangent line to the curve at the origin, they would do calculations which would boil down (without their realizing it) to picking off the linear terms. They would end up with the equations $\ x + y = 0$ and $\ x + y = 0$ , which cut out a plane, not a line. They would be disturbed, and I would explain that this is because the curve isn’t smooth at a point, and their techniques don’t work. We, on the other hand, bravely declare that the tangent space is cut out by $\ x + y = 0 .$ , and (will soon) use this pathology as definition of what makes a point singular (or nonregular). (Intuitively, the curve near the origin is very close to lying in the plane $\ x + y = 0$ .) Notice: The cotangent space jumped up in dimension from what it was “supposed to be,” not down. (This is not a coincidence: see Theorem 12.3.12.)

text: Here is a pleasant consequence of the notion of Zariski tangent space.
13.1.3. Problem. Consider the ring $\mathsf { A } { = } \mathsf { k } [ x , y , z ] / ( \mathsf { x y } - z ^ { 2 } )$ . Show that $( x , z )$ is not a principal ideal.

As $\dim { \cal A } = 2$ (why?), and $A / ( x , z ) \cong \mathbf { k } [ y ]$ has dimension 1, we see that this ideal is codimension 1 (as codimension is the difference of dimensions for irreducible varieties, Theorem 12.2.9). Our geometric picture is that Spec A is a cone (we can diagonalize the quadric as $x y - z ^ { 2 } =$ $( ( x + y ) / 2 ) ^ { 2 } - ( ( x - y ) / 2 ) ^ { 2 } - z ^ { 2 } ,$ at least if char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ —see Exercise 5.4.J), and that $( x , z )$ is a line on the cone. (See Figure 13.1 for a sketch.) This suggests that we look at the cone point.

13.1.4. Solution. Let ${ \mathfrak { m } } = ( { \mathfrak { x } } , { \mathfrak { y } } , z )$ be the maximal ideal corresponding to the origin. Then Spec A has Zariski tangent space of dimension 3 at the origin, and Spec A/(x, z) has Zariski tangent space of dimension 1 at the origin. But Spec A/(f) must have Zariski tangent space of dimension at least 2 at the origin by Exercise 13.1.B. (As an added bonus, we have shown that A is not a unique factorization domain, by Lemma 12.1.7.))

13.1.5.* Remark. Another approach to solving the problem, not requiring the definition of the Zariski tangent space, is to use the fact that the ring is graded (where $x , \ y ,$ and z each have degree 1), and the ideal $( x , z )$ is a homogeneous ideal. (You may

image_caption: Figure 13.1 ${ \mathsf { V } } ( { \mathsf { x } } , z ) \subset$ Spec $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } , z ] / ( \mathsf { x y } - z ^ { 2 } )$ is a line on a cone.

text: enjoy thinking this through.) The advantage of using the tangent space is that it applies to more general situations where there is no grading. For example, (a) $( x , z )$ is not a principal ideal of $\ k [ x , y , z ] / ( x y - z ^ { 2 } - z ^ { 3 } )$ . As a different example, (b) $( x , z )$ is not a principal ideal of the local ring $( \mathsf { k } [ x , y , z ] / ( x y - z ^ { 2 } ) ) _ { ( x , y , z ) }$ (the “germ of the cone”). However, we remark that the graded case is still very useful. The construction of replacing a filtered ring by its “associated graded” ring can turn more general rings into graded rings (and can be used to turn example (a) into the graded case). The construction of completion can turn local rings into graded local rings (and can be used to turn example (b) into, essentially, the graded case). Filtered rings will come up in §13.9, the associated graded construction will implicitly come up in our discussions of the blow-up in $\ S 2 2 . 3 ,$ and many aspects of completions will be described in Chapter 28.

text: 13.1.6. Morphisms and tangent spaces. Suppose π $: X \to \Upsilon ,$ , and $\pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } ) = { \mathfrak { q } }$ . Then if we were in the category of differentiable manifolds, we would expect a tangent map, from the tangent space of p to the tangent space at q. Indeed that is the case; we have a map of stalks $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , { \mathfrak { q } } } \longrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } , { \mathfrak { p } } } ,$ which sends the maximal ideal of the former n to the maximal ideal of the latter m (we checked that this is a “local morphism” when we briefly discussed locally ringed spaces; see $\ S 7 . 3 . 1 \ r ,$ ). Thus ${ \mathfrak { n } } ^ { 2 }$ maps to ${ \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ , from which we see that n/n2 maps to ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ . If $( \mathcal { O } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } } , \mathfrak { m } )$ and $( \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { Y } , \mathsf { q } } , \mathsf { n } )$ have the same residue field $\mathsf { K } ,$ so that $\mathfrak { n } / \mathfrak { n } ^ { 2 } \to \mathfrak { m } / \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 }$ is a linear map of $\boldsymbol { \kappa }$ -vector spaces, then we have a natural map $( { \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \vee } $ $( { \mathfrak { n } } / { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \vee }$ → →. This is the map from the tangent space of p to the tangent space at q that we sought. (Aside: Note that the cotangent map always exists, without requiring p and q to have the same residue field— a sign that cotangent spaces are more natural than tangent spaces in algebraic geometry.)

text: Thus every finite type k-scheme X comes with an intrinsic “Jacobian corank function” from (the underlying set of) $x$ to $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ , whose value at k-valued points p of X is $\dim _ { \mathbb { k } } \operatorname { T } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } }$ . We temporarily call this function $\mathrm { J C } : \mathbf { X } \to \mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 } ,$ , although we will later discover that it is really the rank of the coherent sheaf of differentials $\Omega _ { X / \kappa }$ (Exercise 21.2.E).

13.2 Regularity, and Smoothness over a Field
The key idea in the definition of regularity is contained in Theorem 12.3.12 (the dimension of the Zariski tangent space is at least the dimension of the local ring).
13.2 Regularity, and Smoothness over a Field

13.2.1. Definition. If equality holds in Theorem 12.3.12, we say that A is a regular local ring. In particular, regular local rings are Noetherian by definition. (If a Noetherian ring A is regular at all of its prime ideals, i.e., if $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a regular local ring for all prime ideals $\mathfrak { p }$ of $A$ , then A is said to be a regular ring. We basically won’t use this terminology. Fact 13.8.2 reassuringly implies that regular local rings are indeed regular rings.) A locally Noetherian scheme X is regular at a point p if the local ring $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { p } }$ is regular. (The word nonsingular is often used as well, notably in the case when X is finite type over a field, but for the sake of consistency we will use “regular” throughout.) It is singular at the point otherwise, and we say that the point is a singularity. (The word nonregular is also used, but for the sake of consistency, we will use “singular,” despite the fact that this choice is inconsistent with our choice of “regular” over “nonsingular.” A possible motivation for the inconsistency: “regular local ring” and “singularity” are both standard terminology, so at some point we are forced to make a choice.) A scheme is regular (or nonsingular) if it is regular at all points. It is singular otherwise (i.e., if it is singular at at least one point—if it has a singularity).
13.2.A. EXERCISE. Show that a dimension 0 Noetherian local ring is regular if and only if it is a field.

You will hopefully gradually become convinced that this is the right notion of “smoothness” of schemes. Remarkably, Krull introduced the notion of a regular local ring for purely algebraic reasons, some time before Zariski realized that it was a fundamental notion in geometry in 1947.

13.2.B. EXERCISE. Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a regular local ring of dimension $\mathfrak { n } > 0$ , and $\mathsf { f } \in \boldsymbol { A }$ . Show that A/(f) is a regular local ring of dimension $n - 1$ if and only if $\mathsf { f } \in \mathsf { m } \setminus \mathsf { m } ^ { 2 }$ . (Hint: Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem for tangent spaces, Exercise 13.1.B.)

13.2.C. EXERCISE (THE SLICING CRITERION FOR REGULARITY). Suppose X is a Noetherian scheme (such as a variety), D is an effective Cartier divisor on X (Definition 9.5.1), and $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathrm { D }$ . Show that if p is a regular point of D then p is a regular point of X. (Hint: Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem for tangent spaces, Exercise 13.1.B.)

13.2.D. EASY EXERCISE (PRACTICE WITH THE CONCEPT). Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a regular local ring of dimension d, and $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \hdots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { d } } \in \mathfrak { m }$ are such that their images in the vector space ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ form a basis. Show that they are a system of parameters (Definition 12.3.11) for $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ . (They are “particularly good” parameters.)
13.2.2. The Jacobian criterion for regularity.

A finite type k-scheme is locally of the form Spec $\operatorname { k } [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ] / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } )$ . The Jacobian criterion for regularity (Exercise 13.2.E) gives a hands-on method for checking for singularity at closed points, using the equations $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } _ { \mathrm { r } } ,$ if $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ .

13.2.E. IMPORTANT EXERCISE (THE JACOBIAN CRITERION FOR REGULARITY—EASY, GIVEN EXERCISE 13.1.I). Suppose $X { = } S \mathrm { p e c k } [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { \mathrm { n } } ] / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } )$ has pure dimension d. Show that a k-valued point $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ is regular if and only if the Jacobian corank function at p is d.

13.2.F. EASY EXERCISE. Suppose $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ . Show that the singular closed points of the hypersurface $\mathsf { f } ( { \mathsf { x } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \mathsf { x } } _ { \mathsf { n } } ) = 0$ in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ are given by the equations

$$
f = \frac {\partial f}{\partial x _ {1}} = \dots = \frac {\partial f}{\partial x _ {n}} = 0.
$$
(Translation: The singular points of $\mathsf { f } = 0$ are where the gradient of f vanishes. This is not shocking.)
13.2.3. Smoothness over a field.

There seem to be two serious drawbacks with the Jacobian criterion. For finite type schemes over ${ \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ,$ the criterion gives a necessary condition for regularity, but it is not obviously sufficient, as we need
Chapter 13 Regularity and Smoothness

to check regularity at non-closed points as well. We can prove sufficiency by working hard to show Fact 13.8.2, which implies that the non-closed points must be regular as well. A second failing is that the criterion requires k to be algebraically closed. These problems suggest that old-fashioned ideas of using derivatives and Jacobians are ill-suited to the fancy modern notion of regularity. But that is wrong—the fault is with the concept of regularity. There is a better notion of smoothness over a field. Better yet, this idea generalizes to the notion of a smooth morphism of schemes (to be discussed in §13.6, and again in Chapter 24.8), which behaves well in all possible ways (including in the sense of §8.1). This is another sign that some properties we think of as of objects (“absolute notions”) should really be thought of as properties of morphisms (“relative notions”). We know enough to imperfectly (but correctly) define what it means for a scheme to be k-smooth, or smooth over k.

13.2.4. Definition. A k-scheme is k-smooth of dimension d, or smooth of dimension d over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ if it is of pure dimension d, and there exists a cover by affine open sets Spec $\operatorname { k } [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ] / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { r } )$ where the Jacobian matrix has corank d at all points (not just closed points). (In particular, smooth schemes are implicitly locally of finite type.) A k-scheme is smooth over k if it is smooth of some dimension. The k is often omitted when it is clear from context. By the theory of the Jacobian corank function developed in §13.1.7, for any pure dimension d variety, this can be checked on any affine open cover.

(b) Suppose $\mathsf { f } \in \mathbf { k } [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ is a polynomial such that the system of equations

text:
$$
f = \frac {\partial f}{\partial x _ {1}} = \dots = \frac {\partial f}{\partial x _ {n}} = 0
$$

Suppose X is a locally finite type $\mathrm { k }$ -scheme, and $\operatorname { k } \subset \ell$ is a field extension. Show that X is smooth over k if and only if $\mathsf { X } \times _ { \mathsf { S p e c k } } \mathsf { S p e c } \ell$ is smooth over ℓ.
The next exercise shows that we need only check closed points, thereby making a connection to classical geometry.

13.2.I. EXERCISE. Show that if the Jacobian matrix for $X { = } S p e c \ k [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ] / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { r } } )$ has corank d at all closed points, then it has corank d at all points. (Hint: The locus where the Jacobian matrix has corank d can be described in terms of the vanishing and nonvanishing of determinants of certain explicit matrices.)

13.2.5. In defense of regularity. Having made a spirited case for smoothness, we should be clear that regularity is still very useful. For example, it is the only concept that makes sense in mixed characteristic. In particular, $\mathbb { Z }$ is regular at its points (it is a regular ring), and more generally, discrete valuation rings are incredibly useful examples of regular rings.
13.2.6. Regularity vs. smoothness.

13.2.J. EXERCISE. Suppose X is a locally finite type scheme of pure dimension d over an algebraically closed field $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ . Show that $x$ is regular at its closed points if and only if it is smooth. (We will soon learn that for locally finite type $\bar { \mathsf k }$ -schemes, regularity at closed points is the same as regularity everywhere, Theorem 13.8.3.) Hint to show regularity implies smoothness: Use the Jacobian criterion to show that the corank of the Jacobian is d at the closed points of X. Then use Exercise 13.2.I.
13.2 Regularity, and Smoothness over a Field

More generally, if $\mathrm { k }$ is perfect (e.g., if char ${ \mathrm { k } } = 0$ or k is a finite field), then smoothness is the same as regularity at closed points (see Exercise 13.2.R). More generally still, we will later prove the following fact. We mention it now because it will make a number of statements cleaner long before we finally prove it. (There will be no circularity.)
13.2.7. Smoothness-Regularity Comparison Theorem —

list_items: (a) If k is perfect, every regular finite type k-scheme is smooth over k.
(b) Every smooth k-scheme is regular (with no hypotheses on perfection).

text: Part (a) will be proved in Exercise 13.2.R. Part (b) will be proved in §24.8.9. The fact that Theorem 13.2.7(b) will be proved so far in the future does not mean that we truly need to wait that long. But we deliberately postpone the proof until we have machinery that will do much of the work for us. If you wish for some insight right away, here is the outline of the argument for (b), which you can even try to implement after reading this chapter. We will soon show (in Exercise 13.3.P) that $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is regular for all fields k. Once we know the definition of étale morphism, we will realize that every smooth variety locally admits an étale morphism to $\mathbb { A } _ { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (Observation 13.6.4). You can then show that if π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is an étale morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, and ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ then p is regular if $\pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ is regular. This will be shown in Exercise 24.8.E, but you could reasonably do this after reading the definition of étaleness.

list_items: 13.2.8. Caution: Regularity does not imply smoothness. If $\mathrm { k }$ is not perfect, then regularity does not imply smoothness, as demonstrated by the following example. Let $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { k } = \mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } } ( \mathfrak { u } ) , } \end{array}$ and consider the hypersurface $X { = } \mathrm { S p e c k } [ \mathsf { x } ] / ( \mathsf { x } ^ { \flat } - \mathsf { u } )$ . Now $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } ] / ( \mathsf { x } ^ { \mathsf { p } } - \mathsf { u } )$ is a field, hence regular. But if $\operatorname { f } ( x ) =$ ${ \tt x } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } - { \tt u } ,$ then $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { u } ^ { 1 / \mathsf { p } } ) = \frac { \mathrm { d f } } { \mathrm { d } \mathsf { x } } ( \mathsf { u } ^ { 1 / \mathsf { p } } ) = 0 , } \end{array}$ , so the Jacobian criterion fails—X is not smooth over k. (Never forget that smoothness requires a choice of field—it is a “relative” notion, and we will later define smoothness over an arbitrary scheme, in §13.6.)

13.2.9. In case the previous example is too “small” to be enlightening (because the scheme in question is smooth over a different field, namely, $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } ] / ( \mathsf { x } ^ { \mathsf { p } } - \mathsf { u } ) ,$ ), here is another. Let $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \mathbb { F } _ { \mathrm { p } } \left( \mathrm { u } \right)$ as before, with $\mathfrak { p } > 2 ,$ and consider the curve Sp $\operatorname { \mathfrak { x c k } } [ \mathbf { \mathfrak { x } } , \mathbf { \mathfrak { y } } ] / ( \mathbf { \mathfrak { y } } ^ { 2 } - \mathbf { \mathfrak { x } } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } + \mathbf { \mathfrak { u } } )$ . Then the closed point $\left( \mathsf { y } , \mathsf { x } ^ { \mathsf { p } } - \mathsf { u } \right)$ is regular but not smooth.

text: Thus you should not use “regular” and “smooth” interchangeably.
13.2.10. Regular local rings are integral domains.
You might expect from geometric intuition that a scheme is “locally irreducible” at a “smooth” point. Put algebraically:
13.2.11. Theorem — Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } , { \mathfrak { k } } )$ is a regular local ring of dimension n. Then A is an integral domain.
Before proving it (in §13.2.14), we give some consequences.

list_items: 13.2.K. EXERCISE. Suppose p is a regular point of a locally Noetherian scheme X. Show that only one irreducible component of X passes through p.
13.2.L. EASY EXERCISE. Show that a nonempty regular Noetherian scheme is irreducible if and only if it is connected.
13.2.M. IMPORTANT EXERCISE (REGULAR SCHEMES IN REGULAR SCHEMES ARE REGU-LAR EMBEDDINGS).

(a) Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } , { \mathfrak { k } } )$ is a regular local ring of dimension n, and $\mathrm { I } \subset A$ is an ideal of A cutting out a regular local ring of dimension d. Let $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { r } } = \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } - \boldsymbol { \mathrm { d } }$ . Show that Spec A/I is a regular embedding in Spec A. Hint: Show that there are elements f1, . . . , fr of I giving a basis of the $\mathtt { k }$ -vector space $\mathrm { I } / ( \mathrm { I } \cap \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 } )$ . Show that the quotient of A by both $\left( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } \right)$ and I yields dimension d regular local rings. By Exercise 12.1.D, a surjection of integral domains of the same dimension must be an isomorphism.

list_items: (a) Suppose W is a smooth variety of pure dimension d over an algebraically closed field ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ and X and Y are pure dimensional subvarieties (possibly singular) of W codimension m and ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ respectively. Show that every component of X  Y has codimension at most $\mathsf { m } + \mathsf { n }$ in W, as follows. Show that the diagonal $W \cong \Delta \subset W \times _ { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } W$ is a regular embedding of codimension d. Then follow the rest of the hint to Exercise 12.3.F. (Why is there a hypothesis of smoothness rather than of regularity on W?)

text: 13.2.12. Remark. The following example shows that the smoothness hypotheses in Exercise 13.2.N cannot be (completely) dropped. Let $W { = } S p e c { \mathrm { k } } [ w , x , y , z ] / ( w z - x y )$ be the cone over the smooth quadric surface, which is an integral threefold. Let X be the surface $w = { \boldsymbol { x } } = 0$ and Y the surface $\mathsf { y } = \boldsymbol { z } = 0 .$ ; both lie in W. Then X ∩ Y is just the origin, so we have two codimension 1 subvarieties meeting in a codimension 3 subvariety. (It is no coincidence that X and Y are the affine cones over two lines in the same ruling; see Exercise 9.3.L. This example will arise again in Exercise 22.4.N.)
13.2.13. Proposition — Smooth k-schemes are reduced.
This argument is surprisingly serpentine!

Proof. Suppose X is a smooth k-scheme, and let $\mathsf X _ { \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } : = \mathsf X \times _ { \mathsf { S p e c k } }$ Spec k as usual. Then $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ is smooth (Exercise 13.2.H), so $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ is regular at its closed points (Exercise 13.2.J), so the local rings of $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ at closed points are integral domains (Theorem 13.2.11) and thus reduced, so $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ is reduced at its closed points and hence reduced (Exercise 5.2.E or 6.6.N), so X is reduced (do you see why?).
13.2.O. EXERCISE. Suppose p is a closed point of a smooth k-scheme X of dimension n. Show that ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } }$ is an integral domain (necessarily of dimension n).

13.2.14. Proof of Theorem 13.2.11 (following [Liu, Prop. 2.11]). We prove the result by induction on n. The case $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ is clear (dimension 0 regular local rings are fields, Exercise 13.2.A). So assume $\mathfrak { n } > 0 ,$ , and that the result holds for smaller dimension.

Suppose p is prime, and $\dim A / { \mathfrak { p } } = { \mathfrak { n } }$ . We wish to show that ${ \mathfrak { p } } = ( 0 )$ . Now $\mathsf { A } / \mathsf { p }$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension n, and its Zariski cotangent space is a quotient of that of A cut out by the “equations of $\mathfrak { p } ^ { \prime \prime }$ (Exercise 13.1.B). But $\dim { \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 } = { \mathfrak { n } } ,$ so ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ . Hence $\mathsf { A } / \mathfrak { p }$ is a regular local ring of dimension n.

Choose any $\mathsf { f } \in \mathsf { m } \setminus \mathsf { m } ^ { 2 }$ (so ${ \textsf { f } } \notin { \mathfrak { p } }$ ). By Exercise 12.3.I (applied to both A/(f) and $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { A } } / ( \mathfrak { p } , \mathsf { f } ) )$ , $\dim { \cal A } / ( \mathsf { f } ) \geq \mathsf { n } - 1 .$ , and $\dim \bar { A } / ( \mathsf { f } , \mathsf { p } ) \geq \mathsf { n } - 1 ,$ , but both rings have Zariski tangent space of dimension $n - 1$ , so both are regular local rings of dimension ${ \mathfrak { n } } - 1 ,$ and by the inductive hypothesis both are integral domains. The only way one integral domain can be the quotient of another integral domain of the same dimension is if the quotient is an isomorphism (Exercise 12.1.D). Thus $( { \mathfrak { p } } , { \mathfrak { f } } ) = ( { \mathfrak { f } } ) .$ , from which ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset ( { \mathfrak { f } } ) ,$ , from which ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset$ fp, from which ${ \mathfrak { p } } = { \mathfrak { f p } }$ . Then by Nakayama’s Lemma 8.2.9, ${ \mathfrak { p } } = ( 0 )$ , as desired. □

13.2.15.** Checking regularity of k-schemes at closed points by base changing to k. (We revisit these ideas using a different approach in 21.2.Y, so you should read this only if you are particularly curious.) The Jacobian criterion is a great criterion for checking regularity of finite type k-schemes at k-valued points. The following result extends its applicability to more general closed points.
13.2 Regularity, and Smoothness over a Field

Suppose $x$ is a finite type k-scheme of pure dimension n, and ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ is a closed point with residue field $\mathrm { k ^ { \prime } }$ . By the Nullstellensatz 3.2.6, $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k ^ { \prime } / k } }$ is a finite extension of fields. Suppose that it is separable. Define $\pi \colon X _ { \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } : = X \times _ { \mathtt { k } } \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } \to X$ by base change from Spec $\bar { \kappa } $ Spec k.

You can show this by following up on Exercise 13.2.Q; first deal with the case when $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k ^ { \prime } / k } }$ is Galois, and obtain some transitive group action of $\mathrm { G a l } ( \mathrm { k } ^ { \prime } / \mathrm { k } )$ on $\{ \mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { d } } \}$ . 13.2.R. EXERCISE. Suppose k is perfect, and X is a finite type k-scheme. Show that X is smooth if and only if X is regular at all closed points. In particular, Theorem 13.2.7(a) holds. Explain why X is smooth if and only if $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ is smooth if and only if $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ is regular at all closed points if and only if $x$ is regular at all closed points. See Exercise 13.2.Q for the last step.
13.3 Examples
We now explore regularity and smoothness in practice through a series of examples and exercises.
13.3.1. Geometric examples.

Show that $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ and $\mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { 2 }$ are regular.
Chapter 13 Regularity and Smoothness

The generalization to arbitrary dimension is harder, so we leave it to Exercise 13.3.P. By contrast, it is basically immediate that $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ and $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ are smooth.

text: 13.3.2. Remark. There are different possible definitions of node, cusp, and tacnode. One reasonable definition in the case $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ is the following. Suppose C is pure dimension 1, and ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in C$ is a closed point. Let $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ be the local ring $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } , \mathsf { p } } , \mathsf { s o A } / \mathsf { m } = \mathsf { k } .$ We say p is a node of C if ${ \mathrm { d i m } } _ { \mathrm { k } } { \mathrm { m } } / { \mathrm { m } } ^ { 2 } = 2 ,$ the kernel of

has dimension 1, and the kernel is not spanned by the square of an element in ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ . We won’t need this definition, so you needn’t memorize it. But can you figure out why it is reasonable? Can you make a reasonable guess for a definition of a cusp? Alternate definitions of node, cusp, and more will be given in $\ S 2 8 . 2$ .

13.3.3. Tangent planes and tangent lines.

text: Suppose a scheme $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { A } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is cut out by equations $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , . . . , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } ,$ and X is smooth of (pure) dimension d at the k-valued point $\mathbf { a } = ( \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathbf { a } _ { n } )$ . Then the tangent d-plane to X at a (sometimes denoted by

$\mathsf { T } _ { \mathrm { a } } \boldsymbol { X } )$ is given by the r equations

$$
\left(\frac {\partial f _ {i}}{\partial x _ {1}} (a)\right) \left(x _ {1} - a _ {1}\right) + \dots + \left(\frac {\partial f _ {i}}{\partial x _ {n}} (a)\right) \left(x _ {n} - a _ {n}\right) = 0,
$$

where (as in (13.1.7.1)) $\frac { \partial \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { ~ i ~ } } } { \partial \mathbf { x } _ { 1 } } \left( \mathbf { a } \right)$ ∂x is the evaluation of ∂fi at a = (a1, . . . , an). $\frac { \partial \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { ~ i ~ } } } { \partial \mathbf { x } _ { 1 } }$ ∂x1 $\mathbf { a } = ( \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathbf { a } _ { n } )$
13.3.H. EXERCISE. Why is this independent of the choice of defining equations f1, . . . , fr of X?

The Jacobian criterion for regularity (Exercise 13.2.E) ensures that these r equations indeed cut out a d-plane. (If $\mathrm { d } = 1$ , this is called the tangent line.) This can be readily shown to be notion of tangent plane that we see in multivariable calculus, but note that here this is the definition, and thus we don’t have to worry about $\delta ^ { \prime } \boldsymbol { \mathrm { s } }$ and $\epsilon ^ { \prime } \mathbf { s } .$ . Instead we will have to just be careful that it behaves the way we want it to.
13.3.I. EXERCISE. Compute the tangent line to the curve of Exercise 13.3.F(b) at $( 1 , 1 )$
13.3.J. EXERCISE. Compute the (projective) tangent line to the twisted cubic curve of Exercise 13.3.G at [1, 1, 1, 1].

13.3.K. EXERCISE. Suppose $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ (k, as usual, a field) is cut out by homogeneous equations $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } _ { \mathrm { r } } ,$ and $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ is a k-valued point that is smooth of (pure) dimension d. Define the (projective) tangent d-plane to X at p. (Definition 9.3.3 gives the definition of a d-plane in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , but you shouldn’t need to refer to it.)

13.3.4. Side remark to help you think cleanly. We would want the definition of tangent k-plane to be natural in the sense that for any automorphism σ of $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (or, in the case of the previous Exercise, $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { \mathtt { n } }$ ), $\sigma ( \mathsf { T } _ { \mathsf { p } } X ) = \mathsf { T } _ { \sigma ( \ p ) } \sigma ( X )$ . You could verify this by hand, but you can also say this in a cleaner way, by interpreting the equations cutting out the tangent space in a coordinate free manner. Informally speaking, we are using the canonical identification of n-space with the tangent space to n-space at p, and using the fact that the Jacobian “linear transformation” cuts out $\mathsf { T } _ { \mathsf { p } } X$ in $\mathsf { T } _ { \mathsf { p } } \mathbb { A } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ in a way independent of the choice of coordinates on $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ or our defining equations of X. Your solution to Exercise 13.3.H will help you start to think in this way.

13.3.L. EXERCISE. Suppose $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is a degree d hypersurface cut out by $\mathsf { f } = 0$ , and L is a line not contained in X. Exercise 9.3.D (a case of Bézout’s Theorem) showed that X and L meet at d points, counted “with multiplicity.” Suppose L meets X “with multiplicity at least $2 ^ { \prime \prime }$ at a k-valued point $\mathsf { p } \in \mathsf { L } \cap \mathsf { X } ,$ , and that p is a regular point of X. Show that L is contained in the tangent plane to X at p. (Do you have a picture of this in your mind?)
13.3.5. Arithmetic examples.
13.3.M. EASY EXERCISE. Show that Spec $\mathbb { Z }$ is a regular curve.

13.3.N. EXERCISE. (This tricky exercise is for those who know about the primes of the Gaussian integers Z[i].) There are several ways of showing that $\mathbb { Z } [ { \mathrm { i } } ]$ is dimension 1. (For example: (i) It is a principal ideal domain; (ii) it is the normalization of $\mathbb { Z }$ in the field extension $\mathbb { Q } ( \mathrm { i } ) / \mathbb { Q } )$ ; (iii) using Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem 12.3.3 and the fact that $\operatorname { i m } \mathbb { Z } [ x ] = 2$ by Exercise 12.1.I.) Show that Spec Z[i] is a regular curve. (There are several ways to proceed. You could use Exercise 13.1.B. As an example to work through first, consider the prime $( 1 + \mathrm { i } )$ , which is cut out by the equation $1 + x$ in Spec $\mathbb { Z } [ x ] / ( x ^ { 2 } + 1 )$ .) We will later (§13.5.9) have a simpler approach once we discuss discrete valuation rings.

13.3.O. EXERCISE. Show that [(5, 5i)] is the unique singular point of Spec Z[5i]. (Hint: $\mathbb { Z } [ \mathrm { i } ] _ { 5 } \cong$ $\mathbb { Z } [ 5 \mathrm { i } ] _ { 5 }$ . Use the previous exercise.)

13.3.6. Back to geometry: $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is regular.
The key step to showing that $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is regular (where $\mathrm { k }$ is a field) is the following.

13.3.7. Proposition — Suppose $( \mathbb { B } , \mathfrak { n } , \mathbb { k } )$ is a regular local ring of dimension d. Let $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ $\Phi \colon \mathrm { B }  \mathrm { B } [ \boldsymbol { x } ]$ . Suppose p is a prime ideal of $\mathbf { \dot { A } } : = \mathbf { B } \left[ \mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } \right]$ such that ${ \mathfrak { n } } \mathrm { { B } } [ { \mathfrak { x } } ] \subset { \mathfrak { p } }$ . Then $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ is a regular local ring.

Proof. Geometrically: we have a morphism $\pi \colon X { = } \mathrm { S p e c }$ $\mathsf { B } [ \mathsf { x } ] \to \mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { S p e c B } _ { \boldsymbol { \cdot } }$ , and $\pi ( \left[ { \mathfrak { p } } \right] ) = [ { \mathfrak { n } } ]$ . The fiber $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( [ { \mathfrak { n } } ] )$ is $\operatorname { S p e c } ( \mathrm { B } [ \mathrm { x } ] / \mathfrak { n B } [ \mathrm { x } ] ) = \operatorname { S p e c } \mathrm { k } [ \mathrm { x } ]$ . Thus either (i) $\overline { { [ { \mathfrak { p } } ] } }$ is the fiber $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ above [n], or (ii) $\overline { { [ { \mathfrak { p } } ] } }$ is a closed point of the fiber $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . (See Figure 13.4.)

text: Before considering these two cases, we make two remarks. As $( \mathtt { B } , \mathfrak { n } )$ is a regular local ring of dimension d, n is generated by d elements of B, say, $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { 1 } , \dots , \boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { \mathrm { d } }$ (Exercise 13.2.D), and there is a chain of prime ideals

Case (i): [p] is the fiber $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . In this case, ${ \mathfrak { p } } = { \mathfrak { n B } } [ x ]$ . Thus by Krull’s Height Theorem 12.3.10, the codimension of ${ \mathfrak { p } } = { \mathfrak { n B } } [ x ]$ is at most $\mathrm { d } ,$ because $\mathfrak { p }$ is generated by the d elements $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { 1 } , \dots , \boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { \mathrm { d } }$ . But p has codimension at least d: given the chain (13.3.7.1) ending with ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ we have a corresponding chain of prime ideals of $\mathtt { B } [ x ]$ ending with nB[x]. Hence the codimension of $\mathfrak { p }$ is precisely $\mathrm { d } ,$ and ${ \mathfrak { p } } A _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is generated by f1, . . . , $\mathrm { f _ { d } , }$ implying that it is a regular local ring.

Case (ii): [p] is a closed point of the fiber $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . The closed point $\mathfrak { p }$ of $\mathbb { k } [ x ]$ corresponds to some monic irreducible polynomial ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( x ) \in { \mathfrak { k } } [ x ]$ . Arbitrarily lift the coefficients of $9$ to B; we sloppily denote the resulting polynomial in $\mathtt { B } [ x ]$ by ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( x )$ as well. Then $\mathfrak { p } = ( \mathfrak { f } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathfrak { f } _ { \mathrm { d } } , \mathfrak { g } ) .$ , so by Krull’s Height Theorem 12.3.10, the codimension of $\mathfrak { p }$ is at most $\mathrm { d } + 1$ . But $\mathfrak { p }$ has codimension at least $\mathrm { d } + 1$ : given the chain (13.3.7.1) ending with ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ we have a corresponding chain in $\mathtt { B } [ x ]$ ending with ${ \mathfrak { n } } \mathrm { B } [ \times ] ,$ , and we can extend it by appending p. Hence the codimension of $\mathfrak { p }$ is precisely $\mathrm { d } + 1 ,$ and ${ \mathfrak { p } } A _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is generated by $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { 1 } , \dots , \boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { \mathrm { d } } , \boldsymbol { \mathfrak { g } } ,$ implying that it is a regular local ring. □

13.3.P. EXERCISE. Use Proposition 13.3.7 to show that if X is a regular (locally Noetherian) scheme, then so is $\mathsf X \times \mathbb A ^ { 1 } .$ . In particular, show that $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is regular. (Hint: Think about fibers.)
13.4 Bertini’s Theorem
We now discuss Bertini’s Theorem, a fundamental classical result.

13.4.1. Definition: Dual projective space. The dual (or dual projective space) to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (with projective coordinates $x _ { 0 } , \ldots , x _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ ) is informally the space of hyperplanes in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . Somewhat more precisely, it is a projective space $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ with projective coordinates $\mathbf { a } _ { 0 } , \ldots , { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ (which we denote by $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ in the futile hope of preventing confusion), along with the data of the “incidence variety” or “incidence correspondence” $\mathrm { I } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ cut out by the equation $\mathbf { a } _ { 0 } { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \mathbf { a } _ { \mathfrak { n } } { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0$ . (This is often called the universal hyperplane.) Note that the k-valued points of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n v } }$ indeed correspond to hyperplanes in $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ defined over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ and this is also clearly a duality relation (there is a symmetry in the definition between the $x$ -variables and the a-variables). This definition is concrete enough to use in practice, and extends over an arbitrary base (notably Spec Z). (But if you have a delicate and refined sensibility, you may want to come up with a coordinate-free definition.)

13.4.2. Bertini’s Theorem — Suppose X is a smooth closed subvariety of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ of (pure) dimension d. Then there is a nonempty $( =$ dense) open subset U of dual projective space $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ such that for every point $\mathfrak { p } = \left[ \mathsf { H } \right] \in \mathsf { U } ,$ , the scheme H  X is smooth over κ(p) of (pure) dimension $\mathrm { d } - 1$ .
13.4 Bertini’s Theorem

(You should perhaps think first of the case where $\mathsf { \kappa } ( \mathsf { p } ) = \mathsf { k } ,$ i.e., H is a hyperplane cut out by a linear form with coefficients in k.) Any theorem of this flavor is often called a “Bertini Theorem.” One example is the Kleiman–Bertini Theorem 21.6.10, which was not proved jointly by Kleiman and Bertini.

As an application of Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2, a general degree $\mathrm { d } > 0$ hypersurface in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ intersects X in a smooth subvariety of codimension 1 in X: replace $\mathbf { \chi } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ with the composition

$$
X \longrightarrow \mathbb {P} ^ {n \leftarrow} \stackrel {\gamma_ {d}} {\longrightarrow} \mathbb {P} ^ {N},
$$

where $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { v } } _ { \mathrm { d } }$ is the dth Veronese embedding (9.3.8). Here “general” has its usual meaning in algebraic geometry; see $\ S 1 0 . 3 . 7 .$ (See Exercise 13.4.B for a generalization.) A useful consequence of this, taking $X = \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ : we immediately see that there exists a smooth degree d hypersurface in $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (over any infinite field $\mathrm { k }$ ), without any messing around with specific equations (as in the special case Exercise 13.3.E).
Exercise 21.6.I gives a useful improvement of Bertini’s Theorem in characteristic 0 (see Exercise 21.6.J).

Proof. The central idea of the proof is quite naive. We will describe the hyperplanes that are “bad,” and show that they form a closed subset of dimension at most $\mathfrak { n } - 1$ of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ , and hence that the complement is a dense open subset. Somewhat more precisely, we will define a projective variety $Z \subset X \times \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ that can informally be described as:
and either p is a singular point of $\mathsf { H } \cap \mathsf { X } ,$ or ${ \mathrm { ~ \boldsymbol ~ { ~ X ~ } ~ } } ( { \mathrm { ~ \boldsymbol ~ { ~ H ~ } ~ } } )$ .

We will see that the projection $\pi \colon Z \to X$ has fibers at closed points that are projective spaces of dimension $\mathrm { n } - 1 - \mathrm { d i m } X ,$ → and hence that $\mathrm { d i m } Z \le \mathsf { n } - 1$ . Thus the image of Z in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ will be a closed subset (Theorem 8.4.10), of dimension of at most $\mathsf n - 1$ , so its complement will be open and nonempty. We now put this strategy into action.

13.4.3. We first define Z more precisely, in terms of equations on $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ , where the coordinates on $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ are $x _ { 0 } , \ldots x _ { n } ,$ and the dual coordinates on $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ are $\mathbf { a } _ { 0 } , \ldots , { \mathrm { ~ } } \mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ . Suppose X is cut out by $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ . Then we take these equations as the first of the defining equations of Z. (So far we have defined the subscheme $\ b X \times \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ .) We also add the equation $\mathbf { a } _ { 0 } { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \mathbf { a } _ { \mathfrak { n } } { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0$ . (So far we have described the subscheme of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n V } }$ corresponding to points $( \mathfrak { p } , \mathsf { H } )$ , where ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ and $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { H }$ .) Note that the projective Jacobian matrix (13.1.9.1)

$$
\left( \begin{array}{c c c} \frac {\partial f _ {1}}{\partial x _ {0}} (p) & \dots & \frac {\partial f _ {r}}{\partial x _ {0}} (p) \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac {\partial f _ {1}}{\partial x _ {n}} (p) & \dots & \frac {\partial f _ {r}}{\partial x _ {n}} (p) \end{array} \right)
$$
has corank precisely $\mathrm { d } + 1$ at all points of $x$ by the projective Jacobian corank criterion (Exercise 13.1.N). We then require that the projective Jacobian matrix with a new column

$$
\left( \begin{array}{c} a _ {0} \\ \vdots \\ a _ {n} \end{array} \right)
$$

appended has corank $\geq \mathrm { d } + 1$ (hence $= \mathrm { d } + 1$ ). This is precisely the notion that we wished to capture—do you see why? This is cut out by equations (the determinants of certain minors). (Remark 13.4.4 works through an example, which may help clarify how this works.)

We next show that dim $Z \leq { \mathfrak { n } } - 1$ . For each closed point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ let $W _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ be the locus of hyperplanes containing p, such that H X is singular at p, or else contains all of X; what is the dimension of $W _ { \mathrm { p } } ?$ Suppose dim ${ \boldsymbol { x } } = { \mathrm { d } }$ . Then the restrictions on the hyperplanes in the definition of $W _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ correspond to $\mathrm { d } + 1$ linear conditions. (Do you see why?) This means that $W _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a codimension $\mathrm { d } + 1$ ,
