14.2 Locally Free Sheaves on Schemes in Particular

We now discuss aspects of locally free sheaves more specific to schemes. Based on your intuition for line bundles on manifolds, you might hope that every point has a “small” open neighborhood on which all invertible sheaves (or locally free sheaves) are trivial. Sadly, this is not the case. We will eventually see (§19.11.1) that for the curve $y ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 3 } - x = 0$ in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 2 } ,$ every nonempty open set has nontrivial invertible sheaves. (This will use the fact that it is an open subset of an elliptic curve.) This is a persistent problem in algebraic geometry: the Zariski topology is too blunt.
14.2.A. EASY EXERCISE. Prove that locally free sheaves on a scheme are quasicoherent.

Similarly, finite rank locally free sheaves are always finite type, and if ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ is coherent, finite rank locally free sheaves on X are coherent. (Remember: If ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ is not coherent, then coherence is a pretty useless notion on X.)
14.2.B. UNIMPORTANT EXERCISE. Use the example of Exercise 6.1.B(b) to show that not every quasicoherent sheaf is locally free.
14.2 Locally Free Sheaves on Schemes in Particular

14.2.C. EXERCISE. Show that every (finite) rank n vector bundle on $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ is trivial of rank n. Hint: Finitely generated modules over a principal ideal domain are finite direct sums of cyclic modules, as mentioned in Remark 6.4.4. (Be careful: Before invoking this, you need to be sure that the module in question is finitely generated!) See the aside in §15.4.12 for the difficult generalization to $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ .
14.2.1. Definition. We define rational (and regular) sections of a locally free sheaf on a scheme X just as we did rational (and regular) functions (see, for example, §6.6 and §7.5).

14.2.D. EXERCISE. Suppose s is a section of a locally free sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on a scheme X. Define the notion of the closed subscheme cut out by $s = 0$ , denoted (for obvious reasons) by V(s). Be sure to check that your definition is independent of choices! (This exercise gives a new solution to Exercise 4.5.S.) Hint: Given a trivialization over an open set U, s corresponds to a number of functions $\ f _ { 1 } , \ldots$ on U; on U, take the scheme cut out by these functions. Alternate hint that avoids coordinates: Figure out how to define it as the largest closed subscheme on which s restricts to 0.

14.2.E. EASY EXERCISE. Show that locally free sheaves on locally Noetherian normal schemes satisfy “Algebraic Hartogs’s Lemma”: sections defined away from a set of codimension at least 2 extend over that set. (Algebraic Hartogs’s Lemma for Noetherian normal schemes is Theorem 13.5.19.)
14.2.F. EASY EXERCISE. Suppose s is a nonzero rational section of an invertible sheaf on a locally Noetherian normal scheme. Show that if s has no poles, then s is regular. (Hint: Exercise 13.5.I.)
14.2.2. Locally free sheaves in short exact sequences.

14.2.3. Vector bundle maps.

Maps of vector bundles (in the more conventional sense) are more restrictive than morphisms of quasicoherent sheaves that happen to be locally free sheaves. A locally free subsheaf of a locally free sheaf does not always yield a “subvector bundle” of a vector bundle. The archetypal example is the exact sequence of quasicoherent sheaves on $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 } =$ Spec k[t] corresponding to the following exact sequence of k[t]-modules:
The locally free sheaf tk[t] is a subsheaf of k[t], but it does not correspond to a “subvector bundle”; the cokernel is not a vector bundle. (This example was mentioned in §14.1.8.)

Because maps of locally free sheaves that are actually “maps of vector bundles” have some particularly good and useful behavior, it seems worthwhile to give them a name. There is no accepted name, so we will just choose one.

14.2.4. Definition. Let X be a scheme (or, indeed, more generally, a ringed space), and let $\mathcal { E } ^ { \mathcal { C } }$ and $\mathcal { F }$ be two finite rank locally free sheaves on X, of rank $\mathrm { a } + \mathrm { b }$ and ${ \mathrm { \bf ~ a } } + { \mathrm { \bf c } } ,$ respectively. We say that $\phi : \mathcal { E } \longrightarrow \mathcal { F }$ is a rank a map of vector bundles if for every point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ there is an open neighborhood U of p with a commutative diagram

where the map $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } } ^ { \oplus ( \mathbf { a } + \mathbf { b } ) } \to \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } } ^ { \oplus \mathbf { a } }$ is projection to the first a summands, and the map $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } } ^ { \oplus \mathbf { a } } \hookrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } } ^ { \oplus ( \mathbf { a } + \mathbf { c } ) }$ is inclusion as the first a summands. We say that $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ is a map of vector bundles if near every point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in { \mathsf { X } } , \Phi$ is a rank a map of vector bundles for some a. If $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } = 0$ , we say $\Phi$ is an injection of vector bundles (as in Exercise 14.2.H(b)); we use this terminology sparingly.

14.2.5. Important but straightforward observations. (i) The notion “rank a map of vector bundles” commutes with any base change $\Upsilon  X$ . (ii) The quasicoherent sheaves ker ϕ, $\mathrm { i m } \Phi ,$ and

coker $\Phi$ are locally free (of finite rank b, a, and c, respectively), and their construction commutes with any base change $\Upsilon  X$ .

(iii) If $\phi \colon { \mathcal { E } } \to { \mathcal { F } }$ is a rank a map of vector bundles, then at any point $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X } ,$ , the rank of $\Phi | _ { \mathfrak { p } } : \mathcal { E } | _ { \mathfrak { p } } \to$ ${ \mathcal { F } } | _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a.
In the next exercises we give three criteria for when a morphism $\Phi$ of finite rank locally free sheaves is a map of vector bundles.

14.2.6.* Tensor algebra constructions.

(These important ideas require more work, so you may wish to leave them for later.) For the next exercises, recall the following. If $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ is an A-module, then the tensor algebra $\mathbf { \mathscr { T } } ^ { \bullet } ( M )$ is a noncommutative algebra, graded by $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ , defined as follows. $\mathsf { T } ^ { 0 } ( M ) = \mathsf { A } , \mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( M ) = \mathsf { M } \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } M$ (where n terms appear in the product), and multiplication is what you expect.

The symmetric algebra $\mathrm { S y m } ^ { \bullet } M$ is a symmetric algebra, graded by $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ , defined as the quotient of $\mathbf { \mathscr { T } } ^ { \bullet } ( M )$ by the (two-sided) ideal generated by all elements of the form ${ \tt x } \otimes { \tt y } - { \tt y } \otimes { \tt x }$ for all $\mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } \in M$ . Thus Symn M is the quotient of $M \otimes \cdots \otimes M$ by the relations of the form $\mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } \otimes \cdot \cdot \cdot \otimes \mathfrak { m } _ { n } -$ $\mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \otimes \cdots \otimes \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \prime } ,$ where $\left( \mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \prime } \right)$ is a rearrangement of $\left( \mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right)$ .

The exterior algebra $\wedge ^ { \bullet } M$ is defined to be the quotient of $\mathsf { T } ^ { \bullet } M$ by the (two-sided) ideal generated by all elements of the form $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } \otimes \mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } }$ for all $\boldsymbol { x } \in M$ . Expanding $( { \bf a } + { \bf b } ) \otimes ( { \bf a } + { \bf b } ) ,$ we see that $\mathbf { a } \otimes \mathbf { b } = - \mathbf { b } \otimes \mathbf { a }$ in $\wedge ^ { 2 } M$ . This implies that if 2 is invertible in A (e.g., if A is a field of characteristic not 2), $\wedge ^ { n } M$ is the quotient of $M \otimes \cdots \otimes M$ by the relations of the form $\mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } \otimes \cdot \cdot \cdot \otimes \mathfrak { m } _ { n } -$ $( - 1 ) ^ { \mathrm { s g n } ( \sigma ) } { \mathfrak { m } } _ { \sigma ( 1 ) } \otimes \cdots \otimes { \mathfrak { m } } _ { \sigma ( { \mathfrak { n } } ) } ,$ where $\sigma$ is a permutation of $\{ 1 , \ldots , \mathsf { n } \}$ . The exterior algebra is a “skew-commutative” A-algebra.

Better: both Sym and $\wedge$ can be defined by universal properties. For example, any map of A-modules $M ^ { \otimes \mathbf { n } } \to \mathsf { N }$ that is symmetric in the n entries factors uniquely through $\mathsf { S y m } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( M )$ .

It is most correct to write T •A(M), $\mathrm { S y m } _ { A } ^ { \bullet } ( { \cal M } )$ , and $\textstyle \bigwedge _ { A } ^ { \bullet } ( M )$ , but the “base $\mathrm { { r i n g ^ { \prime \prime } } } A$ is usually omitted for convenience.

We note that in this case, $\textstyle \bigwedge ^ { \mathrm { r a n k } \mathcal { F } } \mathcal { F }$ is denoted by det $\mathcal { F } _ { \mathcal { \epsilon } }$ , and is called the determinant (line) bundle or (both better and worse) the determinant locally free sheaf.
(Remark: These constructions can be defined for $\mathcal { O }$ -modules on an arbitrary ringed space.)

You can also define the sheaf of noncommutative algebras $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { T } ^ { \bullet } } \mathcal { F }$ , the sheaf of commutative algebras Sym• $\mathcal { F }$ , and the sheaf of skew-commutative algebras $\wedge ^ { \bullet } { \mathcal { F } }$ .

14.3 More Pleasant Properties of Finite Type and Coherent Sheaves
We now describe a number of important facts about quasicoherent sheaves when we have some finiteness conditions.

a “finite presentation” of $\mathcal { F }$ locally: on U X, $\mathcal { O } ^ { \oplus \mathrm { m } } \to \mathcal { O } ^ { \oplus \mathrm { n } } \to \mathcal { F } \to 0$ . Coherent sheaves for which the “double dual” morphism is an isomorphism are called reflexive sheaves, but we won’t use this notion. 14.3.E. USEFUL EXERCISE (FOR FINITELY PRESENTED SHEAVES, LOCALLY FREE $=$ FREE STALKS). Suppose $\mathcal { F }$ is a finitely presented sheaf on a scheme X. Show that if ${ \mathcal { F } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a free $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { p } }$ - module for some ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ then $\mathcal { F }$ is locally free in some open neighborhood of p. Hence $\mathcal { F }$ is locally free if and only if ${ \mathcal { F } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a free ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } }$ -module for all $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ . Translation: For a finitely presented sheaf, local freeness is a stalk-local property, and free stalks imply local freeness nearby.

Extended hint: Find an open neighborhood U of p, and n elements of $\mathcal { F } ( \mathrm { u } )$ that generate $\mathcal { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ . Using Geometric Nakayama, Exercise 14.3.D, show that the sections generate $\mathcal { F } _ { \mathbf { q } }$ for all q in some open neighborhood Y of p in U. Thus you have described a surjection $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } ^ { \oplus \mathfrak { n } } \to \mathcal { F } | _ { \Upsilon }$ . Show that the kernel of this map is finite type (using Lemma 6.4.6, “finitely presented implies always finitely presented”), and hence has closed support (say, $Z \subset { \mathsf { Y } }$ ), which does not contain p. Thus $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon \setminus Z } ^ { \oplus \mathfrak { n } } \to \mathcal { F } | _ { \Upsilon \setminus Z }$ is an isomorphism.

This is enlightening in a number of ways. It shows that for finitely presented sheaves, local freeness is a stalk-local condition. Furthermore, on an integral scheme, any finitely presented sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ is automatically free over the generic point (do you see why?), so every finitely presented sheaf on an integral scheme is locally free over a dense open subset. And any finitely presented sheaf that is 0 at the generic point of an irreducible scheme is necessarily 0 on a dense open subset. The last two sentences show the utility of generic points; such statements would have been more mysterious in classical algebraic geometry. An example showing the necessity of finiteness hypotheses: the $\mathbb { Z }$ -module $\ A \subset \mathbb { Q } ,$ , where A consists of fractions with square-free denominator; see [Stacks, tag 065J].

14.3 More Pleasant Properties of Finite Type and Coherent Sheaves
14.3.3. The fiber and rank of a quasicoherent sheaf at a point.

We now apply the definitions of fiber and rank of an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module at a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ (§4.3.8) to a quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on a scheme X. The fiber of $\mathcal { F }$ at p is the vector space

$$
\mathcal {F} | _ {\mathrm {p}} := \mathcal {F} _ {\mathrm {p}} / \mathfrak {m}. \mathcal {F} _ {\mathrm {p}} = \mathcal {F} _ {\mathrm {p}} \otimes_ {\mathcal {O} _ {X, \mathrm {p}}} \kappa (\mathfrak {p}),
$$

where m is the maximal ideal of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X , { \mathfrak { p } } } ,$ , and $\kappa ( \mathfrak { p } )$ is as usual the residue field $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X , p } } / \mathsf { m }$ at p. A section of $\mathcal { F }$ over an open set containing p can be said to take on a value at p, which is an element of this vector space. The rank of a quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ at a point p is $\dim _ { \kappa ( \mathfrak { p } ) } \mathcal { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } } / \mathfrak { m } \mathcal { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ (possibly infinite). More explicitly, on any affine set Spec A where ${ \mathfrak { p } } = [ { \mathfrak { p } } ]$ and $\mathcal { F } ( \mathrm { S p e c } \boldsymbol { A } ) = \boldsymbol { M } .$ , the rank is $\dim _ { \mathsf { K ( A / p ) } } M _ { \mathfrak { p } } / { \mathfrak { p } } M _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ . Note that this definition of rank is consistent with the notion of rank of a locally free sheaf. In the locally free case, the rank is a (locally) constant function of the point. The converse is sometimes true; see Exercise 14.3.K below.

You can use the notion of rank to help visualize finite type quasicoherent sheaves, or even quasicoherent sheaves. For example, finite type quasicoherent sheaves generalize finite rank vector bundles as follows: to each point there is an associated vector space, and although the ranks can jump, they fit together in families as well as one might hope. You might try to visualize the example of Example 14.3.G. Nonreducedness can fit into the picture as well—how would you picture the coherent sheaf on Spec k[ϵ]/ $\left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } \right)$ corresponding to k[ϵ]/(ϵ) (Exercise 14.3.K(c))? How about ${ \sf k } [ \epsilon ] / ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) \oplus { \sf k } [ \epsilon ] / ( \epsilon ) ?$

14.3.4. Degree of a finite morphism at a point. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a finite morphism. Then $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ is a finite type (quasicoherent) sheaf on Y, and the rank of this sheaf at a point p is called the degree of the finite morphism at p. By Exercise 14.3.J, the degree of $\pi$ is a upper semicontinuous function on Y. The degree can jump: consider the closed embedding of a point into a line corresponding to $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } [ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { t } } ]  \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } }$ given by $\mathrm { t } \mapsto 0$ . It can also be constant in cases that you might initially find surprising—see Exercise 10.3.4, where the degree is always 2, but the 2 is obtained in a number of different ways.

14.4 Pushforwards of Quasicoherent Sheaves
We conclude the chapter by discussing pushforward and pullbacks of quasicoherent sheaves, their properties, and some applications.

Suppose $\mathrm { B } \to A$ is a morphism of rings. Recall (from Exercise 1.4.E) that $\left( \cdot \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \lambda , \cdot _ { \mathrm { B } } \right)$ is an adjoint pair between the categories of A-modules and B-modules: we have a bijection

$$
\operatorname {H o m} _ {\mathrm {A}} (\mathrm {N} \otimes_ {\mathrm {B}} \mathrm {A}, \mathrm {M}) \cong \operatorname {H o m} _ {\mathrm {B}} (\mathrm {N}, \mathrm {M} _ {\mathrm {B}})
$$

functorial in both arguments. These constructions behave well with respect to localization (in an appropriate sense), and hence work (often) in the category of quasicoherent sheaves on schemes (and, indeed, always in the category of $\mathcal { O }$ -modules on ringed spaces—see Definition 14.5.6— although we won’t particularly care). The easier construction $( M \mapsto M _ { \mathrm { B } }$ ) will turn into our old friend pushforward. The other $( \Nu \mapsto A \otimes _ { \tt B } \Nu$ ) will be a relative of pullback, whom $\mathrm { I ^ { \prime } m }$ reluctant to call an “old friend.”
We begin with the pushforwards, for which we have already done much of the work.

The main moral of this section is that in “reasonable” situations, the pushforward of a quasicoherent sheaf is quasicoherent, and that this can be understood in terms of one of the module constructions defined above. We begin with a motivating example:
14.4 Pushforwards of Quasicoherent Sheaves

14.4.A. EXERCISE. Let $\pi$ : Spec A Spec B be a morphism of affine schemes, and suppose $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ is an A-module, so $\widetilde { M }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Spec A. Give an isomorphism $\pi _ { * } { \widetilde { M } } { \xrightarrow { \sim } } { \widetilde { M _ { \mathrm { B } } } }$ . (Hint: fThere is only one reasonable way to proceed: look at distinguished open sets.)
In particular, $\pi _ { * } \widetilde { M }$ is quasicoherent. Perhaps more important, this implies that the pushforward of a quasicoherent sheaf under an affine morphism is also quasicoherent.

The following result generalizes the fact that the pushforward of a quasicoherent sheaf under an affine morphism is also quasicoherent.

14.4.C. ESSENTIAL EXERCISE (COROLLARY TO EXERCISE 6.2.G: ${ } ^ { \prime \prime } \pi _ { * } : Q C o h _ { \times } \to Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ $^ { \prime \prime } \pi _ { * }$ FOR QCQS $\pi ^ { \prime \prime }$ ). Show that $\pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Y. (For counterexamples without the quasicompact or quasiseparated hypotheses, see [Stacks, tag 078C].)

Coherent sheaves do not always push forward to coherent sheaves. For example, consider the structure morphism π : $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbf { S p e c k } ,$ corresponding to ${ \bf k } \to { \bf k } [ { \bf t } ]$ . Then $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } }$ is the quasicoherent sheaf corresponding to k[t], which is not a finitely generated $\mathtt { k }$ -module. But in good situations, coherent sheaves do push forward. For example:

14.4.D. EXERCISE. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a finite morphism of locally Noetherian schemes. If $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X, show that $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf. Hint: Show first that $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ is finite type.

Once we define cohomology of quasicoherent sheaves, we will quickly prove that if $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ then $\Gamma ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { F } )$ is a finite-dimensional k-module, and, more generally, if $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on Proj S , then $\Gamma ( \mathrm { P r o j } S _ { \bullet } , { \mathcal F } )$ is a coherent A-module (where ${ \mathsf { S } } _ { 0 } = { \mathsf { A } }$ ). This is a special case of the fact that the “pushforwards of coherent sheaves by projective morphisms are also coherent sheaves” (Theorem 18.7.1(d)). (The notion of projective morphism, a relative version of Proj S Spec A, will be defined in §17.3.)
→More generally, given Noetherian hypotheses, pushforwards of coherent sheaves by proper morphisms are also coherent sheaves (Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1).

14.4.1. Remark. Vector bundles do not always pushforward to vector bundles, which is another reason to work in the larger category of (quasi)coherent sheaves. (Can you think of an example? Hint: Spec k[t]/(t) Spec k[t].) But in particularly good circumstances they can, which is one of the purposes of the Cohomology and Base Change Theorem 25.1.6.
14.5 Pullbacks of Quasicoherent Sheaves: Three Different Perspectives

We next discuss the pullback of a quasicoherent sheaf: if π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes, $\pi ^ { * }$ is a covariant functor $Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }  Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ . (In fact, our discussion will apply readily to pulling back $\mathcal { O }$ - modules on ringed spaces; see §14.5.5 and §14.5.6.) The notion of the pullback of a quasicoherent sheaf can be confusing on first (and second) glance. (For example, it is not the inverse image sheaf, although we will see that it is related to it.)

14.5.1. Here are three contexts in which you have seen the pullback, or can understand it quickly. It may be helpful to keep these in mind, to keep you anchored in the long discussion that follows. Suppose $\mathcal { G }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on a scheme Y.

(i) (restriction to open subsets) If i : $\mathsf { u } \hookrightarrow \mathsf { Y }$ is an open embedding, then ${ \mathfrak { i } } ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ is $\scriptstyle { \mathcal { G } } | _ { \mathrm { U } } ,$ the restriction of $\mathcal { G }$ to U (Example 2.2.8).

(ii) (restriction to points) If i : $\mathfrak { p } \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ is the “inclusion” of a point p in Y (for example, the closed embedding of a closed point; see Exercise 7.3.L(b)), then $\mathfrak { i } ^ { \ast } \mathcal { G }$ is $\mathcal { G } | _ { \mathfrak { p } } ,$ the fiber of $\mathcal { G }$ at p (Definition 4.3.8).
Chapter 14 More on Quasicoherent and Coherent Sheaves

The similarity of the notation $\mathcal { G } | _ { \mathrm { U } }$ and $\mathcal { G } | _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is precisely because both are pullbacks. Pullbacks (especially to locally closed subschemes or generic points) are often called restriction. For this reason, if π : $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is some sort of inclusion (such as a locally closed embedding, or an “inclusion of a generic point”), then $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ is often written as $\mathcal { G } | _ { X }$ and called the restriction of $\mathcal { G }$ to $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ when $\pi$ can be interpreted as some type of “inclusion.”

(iii) (pulling back vector bundles) Suppose $\mathcal { G }$ is a locally free sheaf on Y, and $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is any morphism. Then $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ is the pullback of $\mathcal { G }$ as a vector bundle, as defined in §14.1.7. (This will be established in §14.5.6.)
14.5.2. Strategy. We will see three different ways of thinking about the pullback. Each has significant disadvantages, but together they give a good understanding.

list_items: (a) Because we are understanding quasicoherent sheaves in terms of affine open sets, and modules over the corresponding rings, we begin with an interpretation in this vein. This will be very useful for proving and understanding facts. The disadvantage is that it is annoying to make a definition out of this (when the target is not affine), because gluing arguments can be tedious.

(b) As we saw with fibered product, gluing arguments can be made simpler using universal properties, so our second “definition” will be by universal property. This is elegant, but has the disadvantage that it still needs a construction, and because it works in the larger category of $\mathcal { O }$ -modules, it isn’t clear from the universal property that it takes quasicoherent sheaves to quasicoherent sheaves. But if the target is affine, our construction of (a) is easily seen to satisfy the universal property. Furthermore, the universal property is “local on the target”: if $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is any morphism, i: ${ \mathfrak { u } } \hookrightarrow { \mathfrak { Y } }$ is an open embedding, and $\mathcal { G }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Y, then if $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ exists, then its restriction to $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } )$ is (canonically identified with) $( \pi | _ { \mathrm { U } } ) ^ { * } ( \mathcal { G } | _ { \mathrm { U } } )$ . Thus if the pullback exists in general (even as an $\mathcal { O }$ -module), affine-locally on Y it looks like the construction of (a) (and thus is quasicoherent).

(c) The third definition is one that works on ringed spaces in general. It is short, and is easily seen to satisfy the universal property. It doesn’t obviously take quasicoherent sheaves to quasicoherent sheaves (at least in the way that we have defined quasicoherent sheaves)— a priori it takes quasicoherent sheaves to $\mathcal { O }$ -modules. But thanks to the discussion at the end of (b) above, which used (a), this shows that the pullback of a quasicoherent sheaf is indeed quasicoherent.

text: 14.5.3. First attempt at describing the pullback, using affines. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes, and $\mathcal { G }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Y. We want to define the pullback quasicoherent sheaf $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ on X in terms of affine open sets on X and Y. Suppose Spec A ⊂ X, Spec $\textsf { B C Y }$ are affine open sets, with $\pi ( { \mathrm { S p e c } } A ) \subset { \mathrm { S p e c } } \mathrm { B }$ . Suppose $\mathcal { G } | _ { \mathrm { S p e c B } } \cong \widetilde { \mathsf { N } }$ . Perhaps motivated by the fact that pullback should relate to tensor product, we want

More precisely, we would like $\Gamma ( \mathrm { S p e c } \ A , \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { G } )$ $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ and $\Nu \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } A$ to be identified. This could mean that we use this to construct a definition of $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ , by “gluing all this information together” (and showing it is well-defined). Or it could mean that we define $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ in some other way, and then find a natural identification (14.5.3.1). The first approach can be made to work (and §14.5.4 is the first step), but we will follow the second.

14.5.4. We begin this project by fixing an affine open subset Spec B  Y. To avoid confusion, let $\Phi = \pi | _ { \pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathrm { S p e c } } \mathrm { B } ) } \colon \pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathrm { S p e c } } \mathrm { B } ) \to { \mathrm { S p e c } } \mathrm { B }$ . We will define a quasicoherent sheaf on $\pi ^ { - 1 }$ (Spec B) that will turn out to be $\Phi ^ { * } ( \mathcal { G } | _ { \operatorname { S p e c } \mathrm { \ B } } )$ (and will also be the restriction of $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ to $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathsf { S p e c B } } )$ ).
If Spec Af ⊂ Spec A is a distinguished open set, then

$$
\Gamma (\operatorname {S p e c} A _ {f}, \phi^ {*} \mathscr {G}) = N \otimes_ {B} A _ {f} = (N \otimes_ {B} A) _ {f} = \Gamma (\operatorname {S p e c} A, \phi^ {*} \mathscr {G}) _ {f},
$$
14.5 Pullbacks of Quasicoherent Sheaves: Three Different Perspectives

where $\mathit { \Omega } ^ { \prime \prime } = \mathit { \Omega } ^ { \prime \prime }$ means “canonically isomorphic.” Define the restriction map $\Gamma ( \mathrm { S p e c } \ A , \Phi ^ { * } \mathcal { G } ) $ Γ (Spec Af, $\Phi ^ { * } \mathcal { G }$ ),

by $\alpha \mapsto \alpha \otimes 1$ (of course). Thus $\Phi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ is (or: extends to) a quasicoherent sheaf on $\pi ^ { - 1 } \left( { \mathrm { S p e c } } \mathrm { B } \right)$ (by Exercise 6.2.D).

We have now defined a quasicoherent sheaf on $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathsf { S p e c B } } )$ , for every affine open subset Spec $\textsf { B C Y }$ . We want to show that this construction, as Spec B varies, glues into a single quasicoherent sheaf on X.
You are welcome to do this gluing appropriately, for example, using the distinguished affine base of Y (§6.2.1). This works, but can be confusing, so we take another approach.

text: 14.5.5. Universal property definition of pullback. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of ringed spaces, and $\mathcal { G }$ is an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ -module. (We are of course interested in the case where $\pi$ is a morphism of schemes, and $\mathcal { G }$ is quasicoherent. Even once we specialize our discussion to schemes, much of our discussion will extend without change to this more general situation.) We “define” the pullback $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ as an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module, using the following adjointness universal property: for any ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module $\mathcal { F }$ , there is a bijection $\mathrm { H o m } _ { { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { X } } } ( \pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } } , { \mathcal { F } } ) { \overset { \sim } { \longleftrightarrow } } \mathrm { H o m } _ { { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } } } ( { \mathcal { G } } , \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } } ) ,$ and these bijections are functorial in $\mathcal { F }$ . By universal property nonsense, this determines $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ up to unique isomorphism; we just need to make sure that it exists (which is why the word

“define” is in quotes). Notice that we avoid worrying about when the pushforward of a quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ is quasicoherent by working in the larger category of $\mathcal { O }$ -modules.

list_items: 14.5.A. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. If Y is affine, say, $\Upsilon =$ Spec B, show that the construction of the quasicoherent sheaf in §14.5.4 satisfies this universal property of pullback of $\mathcal { G }$ . Thus calling this sheaf $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ is justified. Hint: Interpret both sides of the alleged bijection explicitly. The adjointness in the ring/module case should turn up.

14.5.B. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Suppose i: ${ \mathfrak { u } } \hookrightarrow x$ is an open embedding of ringed spaces, and $\mathcal { F }$ is an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module. Show that $\mathcal { F } | _ { \mathrm { U } }$ satisfies the universal property of $i ^ { * } { \mathcal { F } }$ (and thus deserves to be called $i ^ { * } { \mathcal { F } }$ ). In other words, for each $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } }$ -module ${ \mathcal { E } } _ { \ell } ^ { \circ }$ , describe a bijection

text:
$$
\mathrm {H o m} _ {\mathcal {O} _ {\mathsf {U}}} (\mathcal {F} | _ {\mathsf {U}}, \mathcal {E}) \xleftarrow {\sim} \mathrm {H o m} _ {\mathcal {O} _ {\mathsf {X}}} (\mathcal {F}, \mathfrak {i} _ {*} \mathcal {E}),
$$
functorial in $\mathcal { E } ^ { \mathcal { C } }$ .

We next show that if $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ satisfies the universal property (for the morphism $\pi \colon X \to \mathsf { Y } .$ ), then if $\mathfrak { j } \colon \mathsf { V } \hookrightarrow \mathsf { Y }$ is any open subset, and i: $\mathsf { U } = \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { V } ) \hookrightarrow \mathsf { X }$ (see (14.5.5.1)), then $( \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { G } ) | _ { \mathrm { U } }$ satisfies the universal property for $\pi | _ { \mathsf { U } } : \mathsf { U } \to \mathsf { V }$ . Thus $( \pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } } ) | _ { \mathrm { U } }$ deserves to be called $\pi | _ { \mathrm { U } } ^ { * } ( \mathcal { G } | _ { \mathrm { V } } )$ . You will notice that we really need to work with $\mathcal { O }$ -modules, not just with quasicoherent sheaves.
If $\mathcal { F } ^ { \prime }$ is an $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } }$ -module, we have a series of bijections (using Important Exercise 14.5.B and adjointness of pullback and pushforward):
Chapter 14 More on Quasicoherent and Coherent Sheaves
We have thus described a bijection

$$
\operatorname {H o m} _ {\mathcal {O} _ {\mathrm {U}}} \left(\left(\pi^ {*} \mathcal {G}\right) | _ {\mathrm {U}}, \mathcal {F} ^ {\prime}\right) \xleftarrow {\sim} \operatorname {H o m} _ {\mathcal {O} _ {\mathrm {V}}} \left(\mathcal {G} | _ {\mathrm {V}}, \left(\pi | _ {\mathrm {U}}\right) _ {*} \mathcal {F} ^ {\prime}\right),
$$

which is (by construction) functorial in $\mathcal { F } ^ { \prime }$ . Hence the discussion in the first paragraph of §14.5.5 is justified. For example, thanks to Important Exercise 14.5.A, the pullback $\pi ^ { * }$ exists if Y is an open subset of an affine scheme.

At this point, we could show that the pullback exists, following the idea behind the construction of the fibered product: we would start with the definition when Y is affine, and “glue.” We will instead take another route.

14.5.6. Third definition: Pullback of $\mathcal { O }$ -modules via explicit construction. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of ringed spaces, and $\mathcal { G }$ is an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ -module. Of course, our case of interest is: $\pi$ is a morphism of schemes, and $\mathcal { G }$ is quasicoherent. Now $\pi ^ { - 1 } \mathcal { G }$ is a $\pi ^ { - 1 } \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon }$ -module. (Notice that we are using the ringed space $( X , \pi ^ { - 1 } \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } )$ , not $( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } )$ . The inverse image construction $\pi ^ { - 1 }$ was discussed in §2.7.) Furthermore, ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ is also a $\pi ^ { - 1 } \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon }$ -module, via the map $\pi ^ { - 1 } \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } \to \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon }$ that is part of the data of the morphism π. Define the pullback of $\mathcal { G }$ by $\pi$ as the ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module

It is immediate that pullback is a covariant functor $\pi ^ { * } \colon M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } }  M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } } ,$ , and that $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { O } } _ { \Upsilon }$ is canonically isomorphic to ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ . We have now established §14.5.1(iii), that pullback of quasicoherent sheaves subsumes our older definition of pullback of locally free sheaves.

14.5.C. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Show that this definition (14.5.6.1) of pullback satisfies the universal property (§14.5.5). Thus the pullback exists, at least as a functor $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } } \longrightarrow M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } }$ .

14.5.D. IMPORTANT EXERCISE (PULLBACK IS A COVARIANT FUNCTOR ON QUASI-COHERENT SHEAVES). Show that if π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes, then $\pi ^ { * }$ gives a covariant functor $Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }  Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ . (You might use $\ S 1 4 . 5 . 4 ,$ Exercise 14.5.B, Exercise 14.5.A, and/or §14.5.5.)

If $\mathcal { G }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Y, note that we can interpret a global section of Y as a map $s : \mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon }  \mathcal { G }$ (the section of $\mathcal { G }$ is the image of the section 1 of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ ). Note that a section of $\mathcal { G }$ is the data of a map $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon }  \mathcal { G }$ . By Exercise 14.5.D, if $s \colon { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } } \to { \mathcal { G } }$ is a section of $\mathcal { G }$ , then there is a natural section $\pi ^ { * } s$ $\pi ^ { * } s \colon \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } \to \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { G }$ of $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ . We call $\pi ^ { * } s$ the pullback of the section s.

The following is immediate from the universal property, and Exercise 14.5.D. The “quasicompact and quasiseparated” hypotheses are solely to ensure that $\pi _ { * }$ indeed sends $Q C o h _ { X }$ to $Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ (Exercise 14.4.C).

14.5.7. Proposition — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a quasicompact, quasiseparated morphism. Then $( \pi ^ { * } \colon Q C o h _ { \Upsilon } \longrightarrow Q C o h _ { \Upsilon } , \pi _ { * } \colon Q C o h _ { \Upsilon } \longrightarrow Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ ) are an adjoint pair: there is an isomorphism
functorial in both $\mathcal { F } \in Q C o h _ { \chi }$ and $\mathcal { G } \in Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ .

14.5.E. IMPORTANT EXERCISE (PULLBACK IS A right-exact FUNCTOR). Suppose $\pi { : } X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes. Show that $\pi ^ { * }$ : $Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }  Q C o h _ { \Upsilon }$ is a right-exact functor. Possible hint: $\otimes$ is right-exact.
For an important class of morphisms, called flat morphisms, we will see that pullback is an exact functor (Exercise 24.1.H).

14.5.F. EXERCISE (PULLBACK PRESERVES FINITE TYPE AND FINITELY PRESENTED QUASI-COHERENT SHEAVES). Suppose that $\pi { : } X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes, and $\mathcal { G }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Y. If $\mathcal { G }$ is finite type (resp., finitely presented), show that $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ is as well. Hint: Cover Y by open sets for which there are surjections $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { n } } \to \mathcal { G } | _ { \mathsf { U } } \to 0$ (resp., $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { U } } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { m } } \to \mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { U } } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { n } } \to \mathcal { G } | _ { \mathrm { U } } \to 0 ,$ ). Apply the the right-exact functor $\pi ^ { * }$ . (Hence if $x$ is locally Noetherian, and $\mathcal { G }$ is coherent, then so is $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { G } }$ . It is not always true that the pullback of a coherent sheaf is coherent, and the interested reader can think of a counterexample.)

Show that there is a canonical isomorphism $\xi ^ { * } \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { G } \longleftrightarrow$ $( \pi \circ \xi ) ^ { * } \mathcal { G }$ .

$$

Possible hint: Given a ring map $\mathrm { B } \to \bar { A }$ with $[ \mathfrak { m } ] \mapsto [ \mathfrak { n } ]$ , where ${ \mathfrak { m } } \subset A$ and ${ \mathfrak { n } } \subset { \mathrm { B } }$ are maximal ideals, show that $( \mathsf { N } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \mathsf { A } ) \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { m } ) \cong ( \mathsf { N } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } ( \mathsf { B } / \mathsf { n } ) ) \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } / \mathsf { n } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { m } )$ by showing both sides are isomorphic to $\Nu \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } ( \cal { A } / \mathfrak { m } )$ .

14.5.9. Remark: Pulling back ideal sheaves. There is an important subtlety in pulling back quasicoherent ideal sheaves. Suppose i: $\tt X \hookrightarrow \tt Y$ is a closed embedding, and $\mu$ : $\Upsilon ^ { \prime }  \Upsilon$ is an arbitrary morphism. Let $\mathsf { X } ^ { \prime } : = \mathsf { X } \times \mathsf { \mathrm { _ Y } Y } ^ { \prime }$ . As “closed embeddings pull back to closed embeddings” (§10.2.2), the pulled back map i′ : $\Chi ^ { \prime } \to \mathsf { Y } ^ { \prime }$ is a closed embedding.

Now $\mu ^ { * }$ induces canonical isomorphisms $\mu ^ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \gamma } \longleftrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \gamma \prime }$ and $\mu ^ { * } ( \mathfrak { i } _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } ) \longleftrightarrow ( \mathfrak { i } _ { * } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } ^ { \prime } } )$ . (Why is the latter isomorphism true? Hint: Check affine-locally.) But it is not necessarily true that $\mu ^ { * } \mathcal { I } _ { X / Y } =$ $\mathcal { I } _ { \mathsf { X } ^ { \prime } / \mathsf { Y } ^ { \prime } }$ . (Exercise 14.5.J yields an example.) This is because the application of $\mu ^ { * }$ to the closed subscheme exact sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {I} _ {X / Y} \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {Y} \longrightarrow i _ {*} \mathcal {O} _ {X} \longrightarrow 0
$$
yields something that is a priori only right-exact:

$$
\mu^ {*} \mathcal {I} _ {X / Y} \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {Y ^ {\prime}} \longrightarrow i _ {*} ^ {\prime} \mathcal {O} _ {X ^ {\prime}} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

Thus, as $\mathcal { I } _ { \mathsf { X ^ { \prime } / Y ^ { \prime } } }$ is the kernel of $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon ^ { \prime } }  \mathrm { i } _ { * } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { O } _ { X ^ { \prime } } ,$ we see that $\mathcal { I } _ { \mathsf { X } ^ { \prime } / \mathsf { Y } ^ { \prime } }$ is the image of $\mu ^ { * } { \mathcal { I } } _ { X / Y }$ in ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y ^ { \prime } } }$ . We can also see this explicitly from Exercise 10.2.B: affine-locally, the ideal of the pullback is generated by the pullback of the ideal.

Note also that if $\mu$ is an exact functor (which we will later see as a consequence of flatness, in Exercise 24.1.H), then $\mu ^ { * } { \mathcal { I } } _ { X / Y }  { \mathcal { I } } _ { X ^ { \prime } / Y ^ { \prime } }$ is an isomorphism.
14.5 Pullbacks of Quasicoherent Sheaves: Three Different Perspectives
14.6 The Quasicoherent Sheaf Corresponding to a Graded Module

We now describe quasicoherent sheaves on a projective A-scheme. Recall that a projective Ascheme is produced from the data of a $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ -graded ring S•, with $\mathrm { S } _ { 0 } = \mathrm { A } ,$ and $S _ { + }$ is a finitely generated ideal (a “finitely generated graded ring over $\lambda , ^ { \prime \prime } \ S 4 . 5 . 6$ ). The resulting scheme is denoted by Proj S .

Suppose M is a graded S -module, graded by Z. (While reading the next section, you may wonder why we don’t grade by $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ . You will see that it doesn’t matter. A $\mathbb { Z }$ -grading will make things cleaner when we produce an M from a quasicoherent sheaf on Proj S .) We define the quasicoherent sheaf $\smash { \widetilde { M _ { \bullet } } }$ as follows. (I will avoid calling it ${ \widetilde { M } } ,$ as this might cause confusion with the affine case; but $\smash { \widetilde { M _ { \bullet } } }$ is not graded in any way.) For each homogeneous f of positive degree, we define a quasicoherent sheaf M^(f) on the distinguished open $\mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { f } ) = \{ \mathsf { p } \ : \ \mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { p } ) \neq 0 \}$ by $( ( \widetilde { M _ { \bullet } } ) _ { \ell } ) _ { 0 } -$ note that $\left( \left( M _ { \bullet } \right) _ { \mathfrak { f } } \right) _ { 0 }$ is an $( ( S _ { \bullet } ) _ { \mathfrak { f } } ) _ { 0 }$ -module, and recall that D(f) is identified with $\mathrm { S p e c } ( ( \mathsf { S } _ { \bullet } ) _ { \mathsf { f } } ) _ { 0 }$ (Exercise 4.5.I). As in (4.5.7.1), the subscript 0 means “the 0-graded piece.” We have obvious isomorphisms of the restriction of $\widetilde { M _ { \bullet } ( f ) }$ and $\widetilde { M _ { \bullet } ( { \mathfrak { g } } ) }$ to $\operatorname { D } ( \mathrm { f } _ { 9 } )$ , satisfying the cocycle conditions. (Quick proof of the first two: if M is finitely generated, resp., finitely presented, so is the quasicoherent sheaf $\smash { \widetilde { M _ { \bullet } } }$ on the affine cone Spec M . 14.6 The Quasicoherent Sheaf Corresponding to a Graded Module
Chapter 15
Line Bundles, Maps to Projective Space, and Divisors

When learning algebraic geometry for the first time, it is surprising to hear that line bundles (invertible sheaves) are of such vital importance. i) They are, of course, the simplest vector bundles. ii) We can often classify them, using “codimension $1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ information. In particular, we can build them and manipulate them. iii) Knowing line bundles on a variety X helps us classify X, and understand maps to projective space, and to other varieties. iv) And this is only the beginning.

In this chapter, we next describe convenient and powerful ways of working with and classifying line bundles. We begin with a fundamental example, the line bundles $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ on projective space, §15.1. We then discuss how line bundles and some sections give maps to projective space, and indeed how all maps to projective schemes arise in this way, §15.2. This is simultaneously elementary, subtle, and powerful. In $\ S 1 5 . 3$ , we describe a concretization of the idea that “oneparameter families to projective space have unique limits.” We then come to the most challenging part of the chapter, in §15.4. We introduce Weil divisors, and use them to determine all the line bundles on X in a number of circumstances. Our hard work leads to many fun examples we can work out by hand, in §15.5. We then discuss a different means of easily describing some line bundles as sheaves of ideals that happen to be invertible (effective Cartier divisors, $\ S 1 5 . 6 \AA$ . We conclude with a brief section once again connecting quasicoherent sheaves on projective schemes to graded modules, §15.7.
15.1 Some Line Bundles on Projective Space
We now describe an important family of invertible sheaves on projective space over a field k.

As a warm-up, we begin with the invertible sheaf $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \bf k } ^ { 1 } } ( 1 )$ on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 } = \operatorname { P r o j } \mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 0 } , \mathbf { x } _ { 1 } ]$ . The subscript $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ refers to the space on which the sheaf lives, and is often omitted when it is clear from the context. We describe the invertible sheaf $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ using transition functions. It is trivial on the usual affine open sets $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 } = \mathrm { D } ( \boldsymbol { x } _ { 0 } ) = \mathrm { S p e c k } [ \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 / 0 } ]$ and $\mathrm { U } _ { 1 } = \mathrm { D } ( \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } ) = \mathrm { S p e c } \mathrm { k } [ \boldsymbol { x } _ { 0 / 1 } ]$ . (We continue to use the convention $x _ { \mathrm { i / j } }$ for describing coordinates on patches of projective space; see §4.4.9.) Thus the data of a section over $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ is a polynomial in $x _ { 1 / 0 }$ . The transition function from $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ to $\mathrm { u } _ { 1 }$ is multiplication by $x _ { 0 / 1 } = x _ { 1 / 0 } ^ { - 1 }$ . The transition function from $\mathrm { u } _ { 1 }$ to $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ is hence multiplication by $x _ { 1 / 0 } = x _ { 0 / 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ .
This information is summarized below:
open cover

$$
U _ {0} = \operatorname {S p e c} k \left[ x _ {1 / 0} \right]
$$

$$
U _ {1} = \operatorname {S p e c} k \left[ x _ {0 / 1} \right]
$$
trivialization and transition functions

$$
k \left[ x _ {1 / 0} \right] \xrightarrow [ \leftarrow ]{\times x _ {0 / 1} = x _ {1 / 0} ^ {- 1}} k \left[ x _ {0 / 1} \right]
$$

To test our understanding, let’s compute the global sections of $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ . This will generalize our hands-on calculation that $\Gamma ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } } ) \cong \mathrm { k }$ (Example 4.4.6). A global section is a polynomial $\mathsf { f } \left( \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 / 0 } \right) \in$ $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } _ { 1 / 0 } ]$ and a polynomial ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 / 1 } ) \in { \mathbf { k } } [ { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 / 1 } ]$ such that $\mathsf { f } ( 1 / \mathsf { x } _ { 0 / 1 } ) \mathsf { x } _ { 0 / 1 } = \mathsf { g } ( \mathsf { x } _ { 0 / 1 } )$ . A little thought will show that f must be linear: $\mathsf { f } \left( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 / 0 } \right) = \mathtt { a x } _ { 1 / 0 } + \mathtt { b } ,$ , and hence ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 / 1 } ) = { \mathfrak { a } } + { \mathfrak { b } } { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 / 1 }$ . Thus

$$
\dim \Gamma (\mathbb {P} _ {\mathrm {k}} ^ {1}, \mathcal {O} (1)) = 2 \neq 1 = \dim \Gamma (\mathbb {P} _ {\mathrm {k}} ^ {1}, \mathcal {O}).
$$
Thus $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ is not isomorphic to $\mathcal { O } _ { i }$ , and we have constructed our first (proved) example of a nontrivial line bundle!

We next define more generally $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . It is defined in the same way, except that the transition functions are the mth powers of those for $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ .
open cover

$$
U _ {0} = \operatorname {S p e c} k \left[ x _ {1 / 0} \right]
$$

$$
U _ {1} = \operatorname {S p e c} k \left[ x _ {0 / 1} \right]
$$
trivialization and transition functions

$$
k \left[ x _ {1 / 0} \right] \xrightarrow [ \begin{array}{c} \times x _ {0 / 1} ^ {m} = x _ {1 / 0} ^ {- m} \\ \times x _ {1 / 0} ^ {m} = x _ {0 / 1} ^ {- m} \end{array} ] k \left[ x _ {0 / 1} \right]
$$

In particular, thanks to the explicit transition functions, we see that $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) = \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { m } }$ (with the obvious meaning if m is negative: $( \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) ^ { \otimes ( - \mathfrak { m } ) } ) ^ { \vee } .$ ). Clearly, also $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) \otimes \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ^ { \prime } ) = \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } + \mathfrak { m } ^ { \prime } )$ .

15.1.1. Example. Long ago (in Exercise 2.6.K(a)), we warned that sheafification was necessary when tensoring ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -modules: if $\mathcal { F }$ and $\mathcal { G }$ are two ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -modules on a ringed space, then it is not necessarily true that ${ \mathcal { F } } ( X ) \otimes _ { { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ( X ) } { \mathcal { G } } ( X ) \cong ( { \mathcal { F } } \otimes { \mathcal { G } } ) ( X )$ . We now have an example: let $\mathsf { X } = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { 1 } , \mathcal { F } =$ O(1), $\mathcal { G } = \mathcal { O } ( - 1 )$ , and use the fact that $\mathcal { O } ( - 1 )$ has no nonzero global sections.

15.1.B. EXERCISE. Show that if $\mathfrak { m } \ne \mathfrak { m ^ { \prime } }$ , then $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) \not \cong \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ^ { \prime } )$ . It is useful to identify the global sections of $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ with the homogeneous polynomials of degree m in $x _ { 0 }$ and $_ { x _ { 1 } }$ , i.e., with the degree m part of $\mathbb { k } [ x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } ]$ (cf. §15.1.2 for the generalization to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ ). Can you see this from your solution to Exercise 15.1.A? We will see that this identification is natural in many ways. The section $\boldsymbol { x } _ { 0 } + \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 }$ of $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ can be multiplied by the section $x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ of $\mathcal { O } ( 2 )$ to get a section of $\mathcal { O } ( 3 )$ . Which one? Where does the rational section $\mathsf { x } _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } ( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } + \mathsf { x } _ { 0 } ) / \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } ^ { 7 }$ of $\mathcal { O } ( - 2 )$ have zeros and poles, and to what order? (We saw the notions of zeros and poles in Definition 13.5.7, and will meet them again in $\ S 1 5 . 4 ,$ , but you should intuitively be able to answer these questions already.)

We now define the invertible sheaf $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { F } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ on the projective space $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { \mathtt { n } }$ . On the usual affine open set $\mathrm { U _ { i } } = \mathrm { S p e c } \ k [ x _ { 0 / \mathrm { i } } , \dotsc , x _ { \mathrm { n / i } } ] / ( x _ { \mathrm { i / i } } - \stackrel { \cdot } { 1 } ) = \mathrm { S p e c } A _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ it is trivial, so sections (as an $A _ { \mathrm { i } }$ -module) are isomorphic to $A _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . The transition function from $\mathrm { { u } _ { i } }$ to $\mathrm { \Delta \ u _ { j } }$ is multiplication by ${ x _ { \mathrm { i / j } } ^ { \mathrm { m } } = { x _ { \mathrm { j / i } } ^ { - \mathrm { m } } } }$ .

$$
U _ {i} = \operatorname {S p e c} k \left[ x _ {0 / i}, \dots , x _ {n / i} \right] / \left(x _ {i / i} - 1\right) \quad U _ {j} = \operatorname {S p e c} k \left[ x _ {0 / j}, \dots , x _ {n / j} \right] / \left(x _ {j / j} - 1\right)
$$

$$
k \left[ x _ {0 / i}, \dots , x _ {n / i} \right] / \left(x _ {i / i} - 1\right) \xrightarrow [ \underset {\times x _ {j / i} ^ {m} = x _ {i / j} ^ {- m}} ]{\times x _ {i / j} ^ {m} = x _ {j / i} ^ {- m}} k \left[ x _ {0 / j}, \dots , x _ {n / j} \right] / \left(x _ {j / j} - 1\right)

15.1.2. As in the case of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , sections of $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { \mathtt { n } }$ are naturally identified with homogeneous degree m polynomials in our $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ variables. (The connection to homogeneous polynomials will be put on a more precise footing in the next exercise.) Thus $x + y + 2 z$ is a section of $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . It isn’t a function, but we know where this section vanishes—precisely where $x + y + 2 z = 0$ .

Also, notice that for fixed n, $\binom { n + m } { n }$ is a polynomial in the variable ${ \mathfrak { m } } ,$ of degree n, but only when $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0$ (or better: when $\mathsf { m } \geq - \mathsf { n }$ ). This should be telling you that this function “wants to

be a polynomial,” but won’t succeed without assistance. We will later define $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { m } ) ) { : = }$ $\dim _ { \mathbb { k } } \Gamma ( { \mathbb P } _ { \mathbb { k } } ^ { { \mathsf n } } , \sigma ( { \mathsf m } ) ) ,$ and later still we will define higher cohomology groups, and we will define the Euler characteristic $\begin{array} { r } { \chi ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) ) { : = } \sum _ { \mathrm { i } = 0 } ^ { \infty } ( - 1 ) ^ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { h } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) ) } \end{array}$ ) (cohomology will vanish in degree higher than n). We will discover the moral that the Euler characteristic is better behaved than $\mathtt { h } ^ { 0 }$ , and so we should now suspect (and later prove; see Theorem 18.1.2, Remark 18.3.5, and Exercise 18.3.C) that this polynomial is in fact the Euler characteristic, and the reason that it agrees with $\mathrm { { h } } ^ { 0 }$ for $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0$ is that all the other cohomology groups vanish.

Motivated by this, if S is a graded ring generated in degree 1, we define $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { P r o j } \thinspace s _ { \bullet } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ (or simply $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) .$ , where S is implicit) by $\widetilde { \cal S ( \mathfrak { m } ) } .$ .

text: If S is generated in degree 1, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Proj S , define $\mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } ) { : = }$ $\mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ . This is often called twisting $\mathcal { F }$ by $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) .$ , or twisting $\mathcal { F }$ by m. More generally, if $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf, then $\mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { L }$ is often called twisting $\mathcal { F }$ by $\mathcal { L }$ .

15.1.6.* Unimportant remark. Even if S is not generated in degree 1, then (if S is finitely generated) by Exercise 7.4.G, $\boldsymbol { S } _ { \mathrm { d } \bullet }$ is generated in degree 1 for some d. In this case, we may define the invertible sheaves $\mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { d } \mathrm { m } )$ for ${ \mathfrak { m } } \in \mathbb { Z }$ . This does not mean that we can’t define $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ ; this depends on S . For example, if S is the polynomial ring $\mathsf { k } [ \boldsymbol { x } , \boldsymbol { y } ]$ with the usual grading, except without linear terms (so $\mathsf { S _ { \bullet } } = \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 } , \mathsf { x y } , \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } , \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 } , \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 } \mathsf { y } , \mathsf { x y } ^ { 2 } , \mathsf { y } ^ { 3 } ] ) ,$ , then $\mathsf { S } _ { 2 \bullet }$ and $\mathsf { S } _ { 3 \bullet }$ are both generated in degree 1, meaning that we may define $\mathcal { O } ( 2 )$ and $\mathcal { O } ( 3 )$ . There is good reason to call their “difference” $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ . (This algebra may remind of you of the pinched plane, Exercise 13.5.H.)
15.2 Line Bundles and Maps to Projective Space

Recall that affine space $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ had a short description as a representable functor: maps (from X) to $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ are exactly the same data as a choice of n functions (on X), Exercise 7.6.E(a). In this section, we accidentally reinterpret projective space in the same way.
Suppose $x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } ,$ and $x _ { 2 }$ are projective coordinates on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ . Do you see how

gives a morphism $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 } ?$ The entries of (15.2.0.1) aren’t functions, so we can’t make sense of →them individually. But for any “point of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } , \prime \prime$ their ratios can make sense nonetheless. For example, the point [1, 1, 1] is sent to [1, 1, 2, 1], and the same point [2, 2, 2] is sent to [8, 8, 16, 8], so there is no contradiction—the entries of (15.2.0.1) are all homogeneous of degree 3, so the quotient of one by another is a rational function. On the other hand,

doesn’t give a morphism $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 } ,$ because the point [0, 0, 1] “doesn’t know where to go.” But you might be able to interpret (15.2.0.2) as giving a morphism $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { 2 } \setminus \{ [ 0 , 0 , 1 ] \}  \mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { 3 } ,$ or a rational map $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 }$ . This may even remind you of Exercise 7.3.O. We now make this precise, and state it much more generally. In particular, we won’t need to work over a field.

15.2.A. EASY EXERCISE (A VITALLY IMPORTANT CONSTRUCTION). Suppose $s _ { 0 } , . . . , s _ { \mathrm { n } }$ are $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ global sections of an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on a scheme $\Sigma ,$ with no common zero. Define a

corresponding map to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ : $\begin{array} { r } { \chi \xrightarrow { [ s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { \mathsf { n } } ] } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } } . } \end{array}$

Hint: If U is an open subset on which $\mathcal { L }$ is trivial, choose a trivialization, then translate the $s _ { \mathrm { i } }$ into functions using this trivialization, and use Exercise 7.3.O(a) to obtain a morphism $\mathrm { u } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . Then show that all of these maps (for different U and different trivializations) “agree,” using Exercise 7.3.O(b).

(In Theorem 15.2.2, we will see that this yields all maps to projective space.) Note that this exercise as written “works over $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \prime \prime }$ (as all morphisms are “over” the final object in the category of schemes), although many readers will just work over a field k.
Chapter 15 Line Bundles, Maps to Projective Space, and Divisors

15.2.1. Definitions. If $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on X, then those points where all global sections of $\mathcal { L }$ vanish are called the base points of ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ , and the set of base points is called the base locus of $\mathcal { L }$ ; it is a closed subset of X. (We can refine this to a closed subscheme: by taking the schemetheoretic intersection of the vanishing loci of the sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ we obtain the scheme-theoretic base locus.) The complement of the base locus is the base-point-free locus. If $\mathcal { L }$ has no base points, it is base-point-free. The base-point-free locus is an open subset of $x$ .

More generally, if we have a bunch of global sections of $\mathcal { L }$ (perhaps not all of them), we can define the base points (and all the related notions, of the previous paragraph) of these sections. Thus, for example, Exercise 15.2.A states that if s0, …, $s _ { \mathrm { n } }$ are a base-point-free family of global sections of an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on X, then they define a map to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ .

A linear series (or linear system) on a k-scheme $x$ is a k-vector space $\vee$ (usually finitedimensional), an invertible sheaf ${ \mathcal { L } }$ , and a linear map $\lambda \colon V \to \Gamma ( X , { \mathcal { L } } )$ . Such a linear series is often called $^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { V } , ^ { \prime \prime }$ with the rest of the data left implicit. If the map $\lambda$ is an isomorphism, it is called a complete linear series, and is often written $| { \mathcal { L } } |$ . The language of base points (base-point-free, base locus, scheme-theoretic base locus, . . .) readily applies to linear series (base-point-free linear series, . . .).

As a reality check, Exercise 15.2.A states that an $( \mathsf { n } + \mathsf { 1 } )$ -dimensional linear series on a k-scheme X, with choice of basis, with base-point-free locus U, defines a morphism $\mathbb { U } \longrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \boldsymbol { \kappa } } ^ { \boldsymbol { \mu } }$ .
15.2.B. EASY EXERCISE. If $\mathcal { L }$ and $\mathcal { M }$ are base-point-free invertible sheaves, show that $\mathcal { L } \otimes \mathcal { M }$ is as well.

15.2.C. EXERCISE. Suppose we have a morphism $\pi \colon X \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ corresponding to the base-pointfree linear series $\Gamma ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) ) \to \Gamma ( X , \mathcal { L } )$ (so ${ \mathcal { L } } = \pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { O } } ( 1 )$ ). If the scheme-theoretic image of X in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ lies in a hyperplane, we say that the linear series (or X itself) is degenerate (and otherwise, nondegenerate). Show that a base-point-free linear series $\mathsf { V } \to \Gamma ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { L } )$ is nondegenerate if and only if the map $\mathsf { V } \to \Gamma ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { L } )$ is an inclusion. In particular, a complete linear series is always nondegenerate.

Theorem 15.2.2, the converse or completion to Exercise 15.2.A, will give one reason why line bundles are crucially important: They tell us about maps to projective space, and more generally, to quasiprojective A-schemes. Given that we have had a hard time naming any non-quasiprojective schemes, they tell us about maps to essentially all interesting schemes.

15.2.2. Important theorem — For a fixed scheme X, morphisms $\Chi \to \mathbb { P } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ are in bijection with the data $\left( \mathcal { L } , s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n } \right)$ , where $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf and s0, . . . , $s _ { \mathrm { n } }$ →are sections of $\mathcal { L }$ with no common zeros (i.e., $\left( s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n } \right)$ is base-point-free), up to isomorphism of these data.

(This works over $\mathbb { Z } ,$ or indeed any base.) Informally: morphisms to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ correspond to $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ sections of a line bundle, not all vanishing at any point, modulo global sections of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathrm { X } } ^ { * } ,$ as multiplication by an invertible function gives an automorphism of $\mathcal { L }$ . This is one of those important theorems in algebraic geometry that is easy to prove, but quite subtle in its effect on how one should think. It takes some time to properly digest. A “coordinate-free” version is given in Exercise 15.2.G.

15.2.3. Theorem 15.2.2 describes all morphisms to projective space, and hence by the Yoneda philosophy, this can be taken as the definition of projective space: It defines projective space up to unique isomorphism. Projective space $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (over $\mathbb { Z }$ ) is the moduli space of line bundles $\mathcal { L }$ along with $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ sections of $\mathcal { L }$ with no common zeros. (Can you give an analogous definition of projective space over X, denoted by PnX?) Or, if you prefer, maps $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ of k-schemes correspond to $( \mathsf { n } + \mathsf { 1 } )$ -dimensional basepoint-free linear series on X, along with choice of basis. (How would you rephrase this if $\mathtt { k }$ is replaced by another ring?)
Every time you see a map to projective space, you should immediately simultaneously keep in mind the invertible sheaf and sections (or, if you prefer, the linear series).
15.2 Line Bundles and Maps to Projective Space

Maps to projective schemes can be described similarly. For example, if $\Upsilon \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ is the curve ${ \tt x } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } { \tt x } _ { 0 } = { \tt \bar { x } } _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } - { \tt x } _ { 1 } { \tt \bar { x } } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ,$ then maps from a scheme X to Y are given by an invertible sheaf on $x$ along with three sections $s _ { 0 } , s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } ,$ with no common zeros, satisfying $s _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \bar { s } _ { 0 } - s _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } + s _ { 1 } s _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = 0$ .

15.2.4. The central idea of the proof of Theorem 15.2.2 is straightforward: we need to show a bijection, so we just figure out what the maps are from left to right, and from right to left, and show that they are indeed inverse. The key will be thinking about transition functions between the trivializing neighborhoods.

Here more precisely is the correspondence of Theorem 15.2.2. Any $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ sections of $\mathcal { L }$ with no common zeros determine a morphism to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ , by Exercise 15.2.A. Conversely, if you have a map to projective space $\mathfrak { c } \colon \mathsf { X } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ , then we have $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ sections of $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { n } } ( 1 )$ , corresponding to the hyperplane sections, $x _ { 0 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ . Then $\pi ^ { * } x _ { 0 } , \ldots , \pi ^ { * } x _ { \mathrm { n } }$ are sections of $\pi ^ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( 1 )$ , and they have no common zero. (Reminder: It is helpful to think of pulling back invertible sheaves in terms of pulling back transition functions.)
So to prove Theorem 15.2.2, we just need to show that these two constructions compose to give the identity in either direction.

Proof of Important Theorem 15.2.2. Suppose we are given $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ sections $s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n }$ of an invertible sheaf ${ \mathcal { L } } $ , with no common zeros, that (via Exercise 15.2.A) induce a morphism π: $\colon X \to \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ . For each $s _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ we get a trivialization of $\mathcal { L }$ on the open set ${ \tt X } _ { s _ { \mathrm { i } } , }$ , where si doesn’t vanish. The transition functions for $\mathcal { L }$ are precisely $s _ { \mathrm { i } } / s _ { \mathrm { j } }$ on ${ \sf X } _ { s _ { \mathrm { i } } } \cap { \sf X } _ { s _ { \mathrm { j } } }$ . As $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ is trivial on the standard affine open sets $\mathrm { D } _ { + } ( { \sf x } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , $\pi ^ { * } ( \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) )$ is trivial on ${ X _ { s } } _ { \mathrm { i } } = \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { D } _ { + } ( { x _ { \mathrm { i } } } ) )$ . Moreover, $s _ { \mathrm { i } } / s _ { \mathrm { j } } = \pi ^ { * } ( x _ { \mathrm { i } } / x _ { \mathrm { j } } )$ (directly from the construction of $\pi$ in Exercise 15.2.A). This gives an isomorphism ${ \mathcal { L } } \longleftrightarrow \pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { O } } ( 1 )$ —the two invertible sheaves have the same transition functions.
15.2.D. EXERCISE. Show that this isomorphism can be chosen so that

$$
(\mathscr {L}, s _ {0}, \dots , s _ {n}) \xleftarrow {\sim} (\pi^ {*} \mathscr {O} (1), \pi^ {*} x _ {0}, \dots , \pi^ {*} x _ {n}),
$$
thereby completing one of the two implications of the theorem.

For the other direction, suppose we are given a map π: $\Chi \to \mathbb { P } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ . Let $s _ { \mathrm { i } } = \pi ^ { * } x _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \Gamma ( X , \pi ^ { * } ( { \mathcal { O } } ( 1 ) ) )$ . As the $\boldsymbol { \mathbf { \rho } } _ { \mathrm { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } } ^ { }$ ’s have no common zeros on $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ , the $s _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ ’s have no common zeros on X. The map $[ s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n } ]$ is the same as the map $\pi$ . We see this as follows. The preimage of $\mathrm { D } ( { \boldsymbol x } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ (by the morphism $[ s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n } ] )$ is $\mathsf { D } ( s _ { \mathrm { i } } ) = \mathsf { D } ( \pi ^ { \ast } x _ { \mathrm { i } } ) = \pi ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { D } ( x _ { \mathrm { i } } ) ,$ , so “the right open sets go to the right open sets.” To show the two morphisms $\mathrm { D } ( \mathfrak { s } _ { \mathrm { i } } ) \to \mathrm { D } ( \mathfrak { x } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ (induced from $\left( s _ { 1 } , \ldots , s _ { n } \right)$ and $\pi$ ) are the same, we use the fact that maps to an affine scheme $\mathrm { D } ( { \boldsymbol x } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ are determined by their maps of global sections in the opposite direction (Essential Exercise 7.3.G). Both morphisms $\mathrm { D } ( \mathfrak { s } _ { \mathrm { i } } ) \to \mathrm { D } ( \mathfrak { x } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ correspond to the ring map $\pi ^ { \sharp } \colon x _ { \mathrm { j / i } } = x _ { \mathrm { j } } / x _ { \mathrm { i } } \mapsto s _ { \mathrm { j } } / s _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . □

15.2.5. Remark: Extending Theorem 15.2.2 to rational maps. Suppose $s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n }$ are sections of an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on a scheme X. Then Theorem 15.2.2 yields a morphism $\textsf { X } \setminus \nabla ( s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { \mathfrak { n } } )  \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ . In particular, if $x$ is integral, and the $s _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are not all 0, these data yield a rational map $X \mathrm { ~ -- \ p h ~ }$ .
15.2.6. Examples and applications.

15.2.7. Example: The Veronese embedding is $| \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { F } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( \mathrm { d } ) |$ . Consider the line bundle $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { F } _ { \bf k } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( \mathrm { d } )$ on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . We have checked that the sections of this line bundle form a vector space of dimension $\binom { \mathfrak { n } + \mathfrak { d } } { \mathfrak { d } }$ , with a basis corresponding to homogeneous degree d polynomials in the projective coordinates for $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (Exercises 15.1.C and 15.1.D). Also, they have no common zeros (as, for example, the subset of sections $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { x } } _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { d } } , \boldsymbol { \mathrm { x } } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { d } } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { \mathrm { x } } _ { \mathrm { n } } ^ { \mathrm { d } }$ have no common zeros). Thus the complete linear series is base-point-free, 0 1  and determines a morphism $\boldsymbol { v } _ { \mathrm { d } } : \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { \binom { \mathrm { n + d } } { \mathrm { d } } - 1 }$ . This is the Veronese embedding (Definition 9.3.8). For example, if ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 2$ and $\mathrm { d } = 2 ,$ →, we get a map $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 5 }$ .
In $\ S 9 . 3 . 8 ,$ →, we saw that this is a closed embedding. The following is a more general method of checking that maps to projective space are closed embeddings.

Define the graded rings $\mathtt { R } _ { \bullet } = \mathtt { k } [ \mathtt { u } , \nu , w ] / ( \mathtt { u } w - \nu ^ { 2 } )$ and $S _ { \bullet } = \ k [ x , y ]$ (with all variables having degree 1). You may be able to show that, after “regrading,” the isomorphism Pro $\mathbb { R } _ { \bullet } \longleftrightarrow$ Proj S does arise from a map of graded rings $( S _ { 2 ^ { \bullet } } \to { \mathbb { R } } _ { \bullet }$ ).
