16.2.3. Proof of Theorem 16.2.2 in the case $x$ is Noetherian. Noetherian hypotheses are used at only one point in the proof, and we explain how to remove them.

text: We work roughly from the easiest to the hardest implications. Obviously, (a’) implies (a), and (c’) implies both (c) and $( c ^ { \prime \prime } )$ .

We now show that (c) implies $( c ^ { \prime } )$ . Suppose we have a point p in an open subset U of X. We seek an affine $X _ { \mathrm { f } }$ containing p and contained in U. By shrinking U, we may assume that U is affine. From (c), U contains some $X _ { \mathrm { f } }$ containing p. But this $X _ { \mathrm { f } }$ is affine, as it is the complement of the vanishing locus of a section of a line bundle on an affine scheme (Exercise 8.3.E), so (c’) holds. Note for future reference that the equivalence of (c) and $\left( \mathrm { c ^ { \prime } } \right)$ did not require the hypothesis of properness.

We next show that (a) implies (c). We embed X in projective space by some power of $\mathcal { L }$ . Given a closed subset $Z \subset X ,$ and a point p of the complement $\times \setminus Z ,$ we seek a section of some ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { N } }$ that vanishes on Z and not on p. The existence of such a section follows from the fact that ${ \cal V } ( \mathrm { I } ( Z ) ) = Z$ (Exercise 4.5.K(c)): there is some (homogeneous) element of I(Z) that does not vanish on p. (This also shows that (a) implies $\left( \mathrm { c ^ { \prime } } \right)$ as well, but we won’t need it.)

We next show that (b) implies (c). Suppose we have a point p in an open subset U of X. We seek a section of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { N } }$ that doesn’t vanish at p, but vanishes on X \ U. Let $\mathcal { I }$ be the sheaf of ideals of functions vanishing on $\times \backslash \sqcup$ (the quasicoherent sheaf of ideals cutting out X \ U, with reduced structure). As $x$ is Noetherian, $\mathcal { I }$ is finite type, so

(Noetherian note: This is the only part of the argument where we use Noetherian hypotheses. They can be removed as follows. Show that for a quasicompact quasiseparated scheme, every ideal sheaf is generated by its finite type subideal sheaves. Indeed, any quasicoherent sheaf on a quasicompact quasiseparated scheme is the union of its finite type quasicoherent subsheaves. This can be proved by hand, and is a suitable but hard exercise; see, for example, [Stacks, tag 01PJ]. One of these finite type ideal sheaves doesn’t vanish at p; use this as $\mathcal { I }$ instead.)
We now have to start working harder.
We next show that $( c ^ { \prime \prime } )$ implies (b).

We wish to show that ${ \mathcal { F } } \otimes { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ is globally generated for $\mathsf { n } \gg 0$ . Use quasicompactness of $x$ to select a finite number of affine $\Chi _ { \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } } , \ldots , \mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { n } } }$ ${ \sf X } _ { { \sf f } _ { n } }$ that cover $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ where $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \Gamma ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { N } _ { \mathrm { i } } } )$ . By replacing $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ with

Then ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { N } }$ is generated by these global sections.

We next show that it suffices to show that for all finite type quasicoherent sheaves $\mathcal { F }$ , $\mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathrm { m N } }$ is globally generated for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ . For if we knew this, we could apply it to $\mathcal { F }$ , F  L , . . . , $\mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes ( \mathsf { N } - 1 ) }$ (a finite number of times), and the result would follow. For this reason, we can replace $\mathcal { L }$ by ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { N } }$ . In other words, to show that $\left( \mathrm { c } ^ { \prime \prime } \right)$ implies (b), we may also assume the additional hypothesis that $\mathcal { L }$ is globally generated.

For each closed point p, choose an affine open neighborhood of the form $X _ { \mathrm { f } } ,$ using $\left( \mathrm { c } ^ { \prime \prime } \right)$ ). Then $\mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { f } } }$ is generated by a finite number of global sections (Easy Exercise 16.1.B—a finite type quasicoherent sheaf on Spec A corresponds to a finitely generated A-module). By Exercise 15.4.N, each of these generators can be expressed as a quotient of a section (over X) of $\mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes M ( \mathfrak { p } ) }$ by $\mathsf { f } ^ { \boldsymbol { M } ( \mathsf { p } ) }$ . Easy Exercise 16.1.C). From quasicompactness of $\Sigma ,$ a finite number of these $\mathrm { u _ { p } }$ cover $\Sigma ,$ so we are done (by taking the maximum of these $M ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ ).

At this point, we note for future reference that we have shown the following. If $X {  } \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda$ is finite type, and $\mathcal { L }$ satisfies $( \mathrm { c ^ { \prime \prime } } )$ , then X is a locally closed embedding into a projective A-scheme. Finally, we note that (a) and (b) together imply $( a ^ { \prime } )$ : if ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \ N }$ is very ample (from (a)), and ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ is base-point-free for ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } } \geq { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } } _ { 0 }$ (from (b)), then ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ is very ample for ${ \mathfrak { n } } \geq { \mathfrak { n } } _ { 0 } + { \mathbb N }$ by Exercise 16.2.C. □

16.2.4.** Semiample line bundles. Just as an invertible sheaf is ample if some tensor power of it is very ample, an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ is said to be semiample if some tensor power of it is basepoint-free. (Translation: $\mathcal { L }$ is ample if some power gives a closed embedding into projective space, and $\mathcal { L }$ is semiample if some power gives just a morphism to projective space.) We won’t use this notion.

16.2.5.* Ampleness in the absolute setting. (We will not use this section in any serious way later.) Note that global generation is already an absolute notion, i.e., is defined for a quasicoherent sheaf on a scheme, with no reference to any morphism. An examination of the proof of Theorem 16.2.2 shows that ampleness may similarly be interpreted in an absolute setting. We make this precise. Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on a quasicompact scheme X. We say that $\mathcal { L }$ is ample if as f runs over the sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ $( \mathfrak { n } > 0 )$ ), the open subsets ${ \mathsf { X } } _ { \mathsf { f } } = \{ { \mathsf { p } } \in { \mathsf { X } } : { \mathsf { f } } ( { \mathsf { p } } ) \neq 0 \}$ form a base for

the topology of X. (We emphasize that quasicompactness in X is part of the condition of ampleness of $\mathcal { L }$ .) For example, (i) if $x$ is an affine scheme, every invertible sheaf is ample, and (ii) if $x$ is a projective A-scheme, $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ is ample.
16.2.M. EASY EXERCISE (PROPERTIES OF ABSOLUTE AMPLENESS).

list_items: (a) Fix a positive integer n. Show that $\mathcal { L }$ is ample if and only if ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ is ample.
(b) Show that if $Z \hookrightarrow X$ is a closed embedding, and $\mathcal { L }$ is ample on X, then $\mathcal { L } | _ { Z }$ is ample on Z.

text: The following result will give you some sense of how ampleness behaves. We will not use it, and hence omit the proof (which is given in [Stacks, tag 01Q3]). However, many parts of the proof are identical to (or generalize) the corresponding arguments in Theorem 16.2.2. The labeling of the statements parallels the labelling of the statements in Theorem 16.2.2.
16.2.6. Theorem (cf. Theorem 16.2.2) — Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on a quasicompact scheme X. Then the following are equivalent.

list_items: (b) X is quasiseparated, and for every finite type quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ , there is an n0 such that for $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } \geq \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } _ { 0 } , \mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } } }$ is globally generated.

(c) As f runs over the global sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \ n }$ (n > 0), the open subsets ${ \mathsf { X } } _ { \mathsf { f } } = \{ { \mathsf { p } } \in { \mathsf { X } } : { \mathsf { f } } ( { \mathsf { p } } ) \neq 0 \}$ form a base for the topology of $x$ (i.e., $\mathcal { L }$ is ample).
(c’) As f runs over the global sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \ n }$ $( n > 0 )$ ), those open subsets $X _ { \mathrm { f } }$ that are affine form a base for the topology of X.

$( c ^ { \prime \prime } )$ As f runs over the global sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ $( \mathfrak { n } > 0 ,$ , those open subsets $X _ { \mathrm { f } }$ that are affine cover X.

(d) Let S• be the graded ring $\Phi _ { \mathfrak { n } \geq 0 } \Gamma ( X , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } } )$ . Then the open sets $X _ { \mathrm { f } }$ (with $\mathsf { f } \in \mathsf { S } _ { + }$ homogeneous of positive degree) cover $\mathsf { X } ,$ and the associated map $X \to \operatorname { P r o j } S _ { \bullet }$ is an open embedding. (Warning: S need not be finitely generated, and Proj S is not necessarily finite type.)

16.2.7.* Transporting global generation, base-point-freeness, and ampleness to the relative situation.

These notions can be “relativized.” We could do this right now, but we wait until ${ \ S } 1 7 . 3 . 5 ,$ when we will have defined the notion of a projective morphism, and thus a “relatively very ample” line bundle.
16.3 Applications to Curves

We now apply what we have learned to curves. (The only reason this discussion comes so late is because we need Exercise 16.2.G, which implies that if $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is finite, and Y is projective over ${ \sf k } ,$ then X is projective over k too.) Because the word “curve” can have different meanings, we will try to be pedantic about hypotheses in statements of theorems. (In common usage, a curve often means a variety over a field k of pure dimension 1.)

16.3.1. Theorem (every integral curve has a birational model that is regular and projective) — If C is an integral finite type one-dimensional k-scheme, then there exists a regular projective k-variety $C ^ { \prime }$ (of dimension 1) birational to C.

Proof. We can assume C is affine. By the Noether Normalization Lemma 12.2.4 , we can find some $x \in { \mathsf { K } } ( { \mathsf { C } } ) \setminus { \mathsf { k } }$ with ${ \sf K } ( { \sf C } ) / { \sf k } ( { \sf x } )$ a finite extension of fields. By identifying a standard open subset of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ with Spec k[x], and taking the normalization of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ in the function field K(C) (defined in Exercise 10.7.I), we obtain a finite morphism $C ^ { \prime } \to \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ,$ where $C ^ { \prime }$ is a curve (finiteness by Exercise 10.7.M, dim $C ^ { \prime } { = } \dim \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ by Exercise 12.1.G), and regular (it

image_caption: Figure 16.3 Constructing a projective regular model of a curve C over k via a finite cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ .

text: 16.3 Applications to Curves

is reduced, hence regular at the generic point, and regular at the closed points by the main theorem on discrete valuation rings in §13.5). Also, $C ^ { \prime }$ is birational to C as they have isomorphic function fields (Exercise 7.5.E).

Finally, $C ^ { \prime } \to { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } }$ is finite, and $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } \to \mathsf { S p e c k }$ is projective, so by Exercise 16.2.G, $\mathrm { C ^ { \prime } }  \mathrm { S p e c k }$ is projective. □

16.3.2. Theorem — If C is an irreducible, regular, one-dimensional k-variety, then there is an open embedding $C \hookrightarrow C ^ { \prime }$ into some projective regular curve $C ^ { \prime }$ (over k).
Proof. We first prove the result in the case where C is affine. As in the proof of Theorem 16.3.1, we have the diagram

where $\pi ^ { \prime }$ is the normalization of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ in the function field extension ${ \sf K } ( { \sf C } ) / { \sf K } ( { \sf A } _ { \sf k } ^ { 1 } ) ,$ , and $C ^ { \prime }$ is projective. By the universal property of normalization in a function field extension, the normalization of $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ in the function field extension $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf C ) / \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { 1 } )$ is C itself, as C is already normal. Thus $( \pi ^ { \prime } ) ^ { - 1 } ( \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 } ) = \mathsf { C } ,$ yielding the desired open embedding $C \to C ^ { \prime }$ .

→We next consider the case of general C. Let $C _ { 1 }$ be any nonempty affine open subset of C. By the discussion in the previous paragraph, we have a regular projective compactification $\widetilde { \overline { { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 1 }$ . The Curve-to-Projective Extension Theorem 15.3.1 (applied successively to the finite number of points $\mathbf { C } \setminus \left( \mathbf { \bar { \Gamma } } _ { 1 } \right)$ implies that the morphism $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } _ { 1 } \hookrightarrow { \widetilde { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 1 }$ extends to a birational morphism $\mathrm { C }  \widetilde { \overline { { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 1 }$ . Because points of a separated regular curve are determined by their valuation (Exercise 13.7.B), this is an inclusion of sets. Because the topology on curves is the “cofinite topology,” it expresses C as an open subset of $\widetilde { \overline { { C } } } _ { 1 }$ . But we are not done yet!

We must show that $\mathrm { C }  \widetilde { \overline { { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 1 }$ is an open embedding of schemes. We show it is an open embedding near a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in C$ as follows. Let $C _ { 2 }$ be an affine open neighborhood of p in C. We repeat the construction we used on $\mathrm { C } _ { 1 } ,$ to obtain the following diagram, with open embeddings marked.

By the Curve-to-Projective Extension Theorem 15.3.1, the map $\mathrm { C } _ { 1 } \overset { \sim } { \underset { \sim } { \longrightarrow } } \overset { \sim } { \overline { { \mathrm { C } } } _ { 2 } }$ extends to $\pi _ { 1 2 } \colon \widetilde { \overline { { { \cal { C } } } } } _ { 1 }  \widetilde { \overline { { { \cal { C } } } } } _ { 2 } ,$ and we similarly have a morphism $\pi _ { 2 1 } \colon \widetilde { \overline { { { \cal { C } } } } } _ { 2 }  \widetilde { \overline { { { \cal { C } } } } } _ { 1 } ,$ , extending $C _ { 2 }  \overline { { C } } _ { 1 }$ . The composition $\pi _ { 2 1 } \circ \pi _ { 1 2 }$ is the identity morphism (as it is the identity rational map; see Theorem 11.3.2). The same is true for $\pi _ { 1 2 } \circ \pi _ { 2 1 }$ , so $\pi _ { 1 2 }$ and $\pi _ { 2 1 }$ are isomorphisms. The enhanced diagram

commutes (by Theorem 11.3.2 again, implying that morphisms of reduced separated schemes are determined by their behavior on dense open sets). But $C _ { 2 }  \bar { C } _ { 1 }$ is an open embedding (in particular, at p), so $C  { \widetilde { \overline { { C } } } } _ { 1 }$ is an open embedding at p as well. □
16.3.A. EXERCISE. Show that all regular proper curves over k are projective.
16.3.3. Theorem (various categories of curves are the same) — The following categories are equivalent:

list_items: (i) integral regular projective one-dimensional k-varieties, and surjective k-morphisms;
(ii) integral regular projective one-dimensional k-varieties, and dominant k-morphisms;
(iii) integral regular projective one-dimensional k-varieties, and dominant rational maps over k;
(iv) integral one-dimensional k-varieties, and dominant rational maps over k; and
(v) the opposite category (defined in §1.1.16) of finitely generated fields of transcendence degree 1 over k, and k-homomorphisms.

text: All morphisms and maps in the following discussion are assumed to be defined over k.

(Aside: The interested reader can tweak the proof below to show the following variation of the theorem. In (i)–(iv), consider only geometrically irreducible curves, and in (v), consider only fields K such that ${ \sf K } \cap { \sf k } ^ { s } = { \sf k }$ in K. This variation allows us to exclude “weird” curves we may not want to consider. For example, if ${ \mathrm { k } } = \mathbb { R } ,$ , then we are allowing curves such as $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ that are not geometrically irreducible, as $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } \times _ { \mathbb { R } } { \sf S p e c } \mathbb { C } \cong \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } \coprod \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ .)

Proof. Every surjective morphism is a dominant morphism, and every dominant morphism is a dominant rational map, and each integral regular projective curve is a quasiprojective curve, so we have shown (informally speaking) how to get from (i) to (ii) to (iii) to (iv). To get from (iv) to (i), suppose we have a dominant rational map ${ \mathsf { C } } _ { 1 } \to { \mathsf { C } } _ { 2 }$ of integral curves. Replace $C _ { 1 }$ by a dense open set so the rational map is a morphism ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { 1 } \to { \mathrm { C } } _ { 2 }$ . This induces a map of normalizations ${ \widetilde { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 1 } \to { \widetilde { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 2 } ^ { \bullet }$ of regular irreducible curves. Let $\overline { { \widetilde { C } } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ →be a regular projective compactification of $\widetilde { \mathsf { C } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (for $\mathfrak { i } = 1 , 2 ,$ e), as in Theorem 16.3.2. Then the morphism $\tilde { \mathsf { C } } _ { 1 } \overset { \vartriangle } {  } \overline { { \tilde { \mathsf { C } } } } _ { 2 }$ extends to a morphism $\overline { { \widetilde { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 1 }  \overline { { \widetilde { \mathrm { C } } } } _ { 2 }$ eby the Curve-to-Projective Extension Theorem 15.3.1. This morphism is surjective (do you see why?), so we have produced a morphism in category (i).
16.3.B. EXERCISE. Put the above pieces together to describe equivalences of categories (i) through (iv).
It remains to connect (v). This is essentially the content of Exercise 7.5.E; details are left to the reader. □
Theorem 16.3.3 has a number of implications. For example, each quasiprojective reduced curve is birational to precisely one projective regular curve. Here is another interesting consequence.
16.3.4. Important notion: Degree of a projective morphism from a curve to a regular curve.

You might already have a reasonable sense that a map of compact Riemann surfaces has a wellbehaved degree, that the number of preimages of a point of $C ^ { \prime }$ is constant, so long as the preimages are counted with appropriate multiplicity. For example, if f locally looks like $z \mapsto z ^ { \mathsf { m } } = \mathsf { y }$ , then near
16.3 Applications to Curves

$\mathfrak { y } = 0$ and $z = 0$ (but not at $z = 0$ ), each point has precisely m preimages, but as y goes to 0, the m preimages coalesce. Enlightening Example 10.3.4 showed this phenomenon in a more complicated context.

We now show the algebraic version of this fact. Suppose π: $C \to C ^ { \prime }$ is a surjective (or, equivalently, dominant) map of regular projective curves. We will show that π has a well-behaved degree, in a sense that we will now make precise.

First we show that $\pi$ is finite. Theorem 18.1.6 (finite $=$ projective $^ +$ finite fibers) implies this, but we haven’t proved it yet. So, instead, we show the finiteness of $\pi$ as follows. Let $C ^ { \prime \prime }$ be the normalization of $C ^ { \prime }$ in the function field of C. Then we have an isomorphism ${ \sf K } ( { \sf C } ) \cong { \sf K } ( { \sf C } ^ { \prime \prime } )$ (really, equality) which leads to birational maps $\mathrm { C } \prec - \succ \mathrm { C ^ { \prime \prime } , }$ , which extend to morphisms as both C and $\mathrm { C } ^ { \prime \prime }$ are regular and projective (by the Curve-to-Projective Extension Theorem 15.3.1). Thus this yields an isomorphism of C and $C ^ { \prime \prime }$ . But $C ^ { \prime \prime } \to C ^ { \prime }$ is a finite morphism by the finiteness of normalization (Exercise 10.7.M).

16.3.5. Proposition — Suppose that π: $C \to C ^ { \prime }$ is a finite morphism, where $C ^ { \prime }$ is a regular Noetherian scheme of pure dimension 1, and C is a Noetherian scheme of pure dimension 1 with no embedded points (the most important case: C is reduced). Then $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } }$ is locally free of finite rank.

The “no embedded points” hypothesis is the same as requiring that every associated point of C map to a generic point of (some component of) $C ^ { \prime }$ . Notice that if C is reduced, then there are no restrictions on how bad the singularities of C can be!

We will prove Proposition 16.3.5 in §16.3.11, after showing how useful it is. The regularity hypothesis on $C ^ { \prime }$ is necessary: the normalization of a nodal curve (Figure 8.4) is an example where most points have one preimage, and one point (the “node”) has two. (We will later see, in Remark 24.3.8 and §24.3.12, that what matters in the hypotheses of Proposition 16.3.5 is that the morphism is finite and flat.)
16.3.6. Definition. If $C ^ { \prime }$ is irreducible, the rank of this locally free sheaf is the degree of π.

16.3.C. EXERCISE. Recall that the degree of a rational map from one integral curve to another is defined as the degree of the function field extension (Definition 12.2.2). Show that (with the notation of Proposition 16.3.5) if C and $C ^ { \prime }$ are integral, the degree of $\pi$ as a rational map is the same as the rank of $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } }$ .

16.3.7. Remark for those with complex-analytic background (algebraic degree $=$ analytic degree). If $C \to C ^ { \prime }$ is a finite map of regular complex algebraic curves, Proposition 16.3.5 establishes that the algebraic degree as defined above is the same as the analytic degree (counting preimages, with multiplicity).

16.3.D. EXERCISE. We continue the notation and hypotheses of Proposition 16.3.5. Suppose p is a closed point of $C ^ { \prime }$ . The scheme-theoretic preimage $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ of $\mathfrak { p }$ is a dimension 0 scheme over k.

Show that $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } = ( \deg \pi ) ( \deg \mathfrak { p } )$ . (The degree of a point was defined in §5.3.8.)

Suppose t is a uniformizer of the discrete valuation ring $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } ^ { \prime } , \mathsf { p } }$ . Show that

\deg \pi = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {m} \left(\operatorname {v a l} _ {p _ {i}} \pi^ {*} t\right) \deg \left(\kappa \left(p _ {i}\right) / \kappa (p)\right),
$$

where $\mathrm { d e g } ( \kappa ( \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ) / \kappa ( \mathfrak { p } ) )$ denotes the degree of the field extension of the residue fields. If you are a number theorist, can you recognize (b) in terms of splitting prime ideals in extensions of rings of integers in number fields?)

16.3.E. EXERCISE. Suppose that C is an irreducible regular projective curve over ${ \sf k } ,$ and s is a nonzero rational function on C. Show that the number of zeros of s (counted with appropriate multiplicity) equals the number of poles. Hint: Recognize this as the degree of a morphism $s \colon C \to$ $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . Use Exercise 16.3.D(b) for $0 \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ and $\infty \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ →. (In the complex category, this is an important consequence of the Residue Theorem.)

16.3.8.** Important definition we could make now (although we wait until §18.4.2). At this point, we can easily make the important definition of the degree of a line bundle on a smooth projective curve. Feel free to think this through now, although we wait until §18.4.2 to make a (seemingly different) definition, and show it is equivalent to this in Important Easy Exercise 18.4.C. Suppose C is an irreducible regular projective curve over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ and $\mathcal { L }$ is a line bundle on C. Then $\mathcal { L }$ has a nonzero rational section s (do you see why?). If s and t are any two nonzero rational sections, then by Exercise 16.3.E, for the the rational function $s / \mathrm { t } ,$ the number of zeros equals the number of poles (counted correctly). Thus the number of zeros minus the number of poles (counted correctly) of a nonzero rational section s is independent of the choice of s. This integer could be taken to be the definition of the degree of the line bundle $\mathcal { L }$ on the curve C. (Because we are not taking this as our “official definition,” we do not write this phrase in bold.)

16.3.9. Remark. In Exercise 18.4.E, we will see that the number of zeros (and poles) in the previous exercise is the degree of the line bundle giving the map to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (via Important Theorem 15.2.2).

16.3.10. Revisiting Example 10.3.4. Proposition 16.3.5 and Exercise 16.3.D make precise what general behavior we observed in Example 10.3.4. Suppose $C ^ { \prime }$ is irreducible, and that d is the rank of this allegedly locally free sheaf. Then the fiber over any point of $C ^ { \prime }$ with residue field K is the Spec of an algebra of dimension d over K. This means that the number of points in the fiber, counted with appropriate multiplicity, is always d.

As a motivating example, we revisit Example 10.3.4, the map $\mathbb { Q } [ \mathsf { y } ] \to \mathbb { Q } [ \mathsf { x } ]$ given by $\mathsf { x } \mapsto \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 }$ , the projection of the parabola $\ x = \ y ^ { 2 }$ to the $x$ -axis. We observed the following.

list_items: (i) The fiber over $x = 1$ is $\mathbb { Q } [ \mathsf { y } ] / ( \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } - 1 )$ , so we get 2 points.
(ii) The fiber over $\boldsymbol { x } = 0$ is $\mathbb { Q } [ \boldsymbol { y } ] / ( \boldsymbol { y } ^ { 2 } )$ — we get one point, with multiplicity 2, arising because of the nonreducedness.

(iii) The fiber over $x = - 1$ is $\mathbb { Q } [ \mathfrak { y } ] / ( \mathfrak { y } ^ { 2 } + 1 ) \cong \mathbb { Q } ( \mathfrak { i } )$ — we get one point, with multiplicity 2, arising because of the field extension.

(iv) Finally, the fiber over the generic point Spec $\mathbb { Q } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ is Spec $\mathbb { Q } ( { \mathfrak { y } } )$ , which is one point, with multiplicity 2, arising again because of the field extension (as $\mathbb { Q } ( \mathfrak { y } ) / \mathbb { Q } ( \mathfrak { x } )$ is a degree 2 extension).

text: We thus see three sorts of behaviors ((iii) and (iv) are really the same). Note that even if you only work with algebraically closed fields, you will still be forced to deal with this third type of behavior, because residue fields at generic points are usually not algebraically closed (witness case (iv) above).
16.3.11. Proof of Proposition 16.3.5. The key idea, useful in other circumstances, is to reduce to a fact about discrete valuation rings.

The question is local on the target, so we may assume that $C ^ { \prime }$ is affine. Now $C ^ { \prime }$ is normal, so by Exercise 5.4.B, its connected components are irreducible. Replacing $C ^ { \prime }$ by each of its irreducible components, we may also assume $C ^ { \prime }$ is integral.

By Exercise 14.3.K, if the rank of the finite type quasicoherent sheaf $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } }$ is constant, then (as $C ^ { \prime }$ is reduced) $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ is locally free. We will show this by showing the rank at any closed point p of $C ^ { \prime }$ is the same as the rank at the generic point. (This is another example of the usefulness of the generic point.)

Suppose $\mathrm { { C ^ { \prime } } } = \mathrm { { S p e c } } \mathrm { { A ^ { \prime } } }$ , where $A ^ { \prime }$ is an integral domain, and ${ \mathfrak { p } } = [ { \mathfrak { m } } ]$ . As $\pi$ is an affine morphism, C is an affine scheme as well; say, $C =$ Spec A.
16.3 Applications to Curves

We wish to show that (i) $\dim _ { A ^ { \prime } / { \mathfrak { m } } } ( A / { \mathfrak { m } } )$ (the rank of $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { C }$ at p) equals (ii) $\dim _ { \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { A } ^ { \prime } ) } ( { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \prime \times } ) ^ { - 1 } { \mathsf { A } }$ (the rank of $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { C } }$ at the generic point). In other words, we take A (considered as an $A ^ { \prime }$ -module), and (i) quotient by m, and (ii) invert all nonzero elements of $A ^ { \prime }$ , and in each case compute the result’s dimension over the appropriate field.

Both (i) and (ii) factor through localizing at m, so it suffices to show that $A _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ is a finite rank free ${  { \lambda } } _ { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { \prime }$ -module, of rank d, say, as the answers to both (i) and (ii) will then be d.

Now ${  { \lambda } } _ { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { \prime }$ is a discrete valuation ring; let t be its uniformizer. We can assume that $\mathbf { t } \in { \cal { A } } ^ { \prime }$ (as otherwise, we replace $A ^ { \prime }$ by a suitable localization, at the “denominators” of t). Then $A _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ is a finitely generated ${  { A _ { \mathrm { m } } } } ^ { \prime }$ -module, and hence by Remark 6.4.4 is a finite sum of principal modules, of the form ${  { A _ { \mathrm { m } } } } ^ { \prime }$ or $\textstyle { \mathcal { A } _ { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { \prime } } / ( \mathfrak { t } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } )$ (for various n). We wish to show that there are no summands of the latter type. But if there were, then t (interpreted as an element of $A _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ ) would be a zerodivisor of $\lambda _ { \mathfrak { m } } ,$ and thus (interpreted as an element of A) a zerodivisor of A. But then by Proposition 6.6.13, there is an associated point of C in $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ , contradicting the hypotheses that C has no embedded points.
16.4* The Grassmannian as a Moduli Space
(In this section and others, the symbol k is used both for a field, and for a dimension. For this we apologize, and hope it does not cause too much confusion.)

We first defined projective space inelegantly in §4.4.9, and in $\ S 1 5 . 2 . 3$ we gave a clean (if perhaps surprising) functorial definition. Similarly, in §7.7, we gave a preliminary description of the Grassmannian. We are now in a position to give a better definition. Before proceeding, you should first go back and read the doubled-starred $\ S 1 0 . 1 . 7 ,$ if you have not done so already.

We describe the “Grassmannian (contravariant) functor” (which we also denote by ${ \sf G } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } )$ ), then show that it is representable (§7.6.2). The construction works over an arbitrary base scheme, so we work over the final object Spec Z. (You should think through what to change if you wish to work with, for example, complex schemes.) The functor is defined as follows. To a scheme B, we associate the set of locally free rank k quotients of the rank n free sheaf,

up to isomorphism. An isomorphism of two such quotients $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ : $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \oplus { \mathrm n } } \to \mathcal { Q } \to 0$ and $\phi ^ { \prime } \colon { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathtt { B } } ^ { \oplus { \mathtt { n } } } \to { \mathcal { Q } } ^ { \prime } \to 0$ is an isomorphism σ: ${ \mathcal { Q } } \longrightarrow { \mathcal { Q } } ^ { \prime }$ such that the diagram
commutes. By Exercise 14.2.H(a), ker $\Phi$ is locally free of rank n − k. (Thus, if you prefer, you can extend (16.4.0.1), and instead consider the functor to take B to short exact sequences
of locally free sheaves over B, of ranks ${ \mathfrak { n } } - { \mathfrak { k } } , { \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ respectively.)

It may surprise you that we are considering rank k quotients of a rank n sheaf, not rank k subobjects, given that the Grassmannian should parametrize k-dimensional subspace of an ndimensional space. This is done for several reasons. One is that the kernel of a surjective map of locally free sheaves must be locally free, while the cokernel of an injective map of locally free sheaves need not be locally free (Exercise 14.2.H(a) and (b), respectively). (If you prefer, rather than surjections of finite rank locally free sheaves, you can equivalently consider injections of finite
Chapter 16 Properties of Line Bundles

rank locally free sheaves that are “injections of vector bundles” in the sense of Definition 14.2.4.) Another reason: We will see in $\ S 2 5 . 3 . 3$ that the geometric incarnation of this problem indeed translates to this. We can already see a key example here: if ${ \mathrm { k } } = 1$ , our definition yields one-dimensional quotients ${ \mathcal { O } } ^ { \oplus { \mathfrak { n } } } \to { \mathcal { L } } \to 0$ . But this is precisely the data of n sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ , with no common zeros, which by Theorem 15.2.2 (the functorial description of projective space) corresponds precisely to maps to $\mathbb { P } ^ { n - 1 }$ , so the ${ \mathrm { k } } = 1$ case parametrizes what we want.
We now show that the Grassmannian (contravariant) functor is representable for given n and k.

So far we have described what the Grassmannian functor does on objects B; to fully describe a functor, we need to say what it does to morphisms $\mathrm { B } _ { 1 }  \mathrm { B } _ { 2 }$ . Can you see what the definition must be?

Chapter 17
Projective Morphisms, and Relative Versions of Spec and Proj

In this chapter, we will use universal properties to define two useful constructions, Spec of a sheaf of algebras $\mathcal { R }$ and Proj of a sheaf of graded algebras $\mathcal { S } _ { \bullet }$ on a scheme X. These will generalize (globalize) our constructions of both Spec of A-algebras and Proj of graded A-algebras. We will see that affine morphisms are precisely those of the form Spec ${ \mathcal { R } } \to X ,$ and so we will define projective morphisms to be those of the form Proj $\mathcal { S } _ { \bullet }  X$ .

In both cases, our plan is to make a notion we know well over a ring work more generally over a scheme. The main issue is how to glue the constructions over each affine open subset together. The slick way we will proceed is by giving a universal property, then showing that the affine construction satisfies this universal property, then showing that the universal property behaves well with respect to open subsets, then using the idea that lets us glue together the fibered product (or normalization) to do all the hard gluing work. The most annoying part of this plan is finding the right universal property, especially in the Proj case.
17.1 Relative Spec of a (Quasicoherent) Sheaf of Algebras

Given an A-algebra, R, we can take its Spec to get an affine scheme over Spec A: Spec $R \to S { \mathrm { p e c } } A$ . We will now see a universal property description of a globalization of that notation. Consider an arbitrary scheme $\Sigma ,$ and a quasicoherent sheaf of algebras $\mathcal { R }$ on it. We will define how to take Spec of this sheaf of algebras, and we will get a scheme $S p e c { \mathcal { R } }  X$ that is “affine over $\mathrm { X , \ ^ { \prime \prime } }$ i.e., the structure morphism is an affine morphism. You can think of this in two ways.

list_items: 17.1.1. First, and most concretely, for any affine open set Spec A ⊂ X, $\Gamma ( \mathrm { S p e c } \ A , { \mathcal { R } } )$ is some A-algebra; call it R. Then above Spec A, Spec $\mathcal { R }$ will be Spec R.

17.1.2. Second, it will satisfy a universal property. We could define the A-scheme Spec R by the fact that morphisms to Spec R (from an A-scheme W, over Spec A) correspond to maps of A-algebras ${ \sf R } \to \Gamma ( W , { \mathcal { O } } _ { W } )$ (this is our old friend Exercise 7.3.G). The universal property for $\beta \colon { \mathcal { S } } p e c { \mathcal { R } } \to X$ generalizes this. Given a morphism $\mu \colon W \to X ,$ the X-morphisms $W {  } S p e c \mathcal { R }$ are in functorial (in W) bijection with morphisms $\propto$ of sheaves of algebras making

Here the map $\mathcal { O } _ { X } \to \mu _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { W }$ is that coming from the map of ringed spaces, and the map $\mathcal { O } _ { X }  \mathcal { R }$ comes from the ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -algebra structure on $\mathcal { R }$ . (For experts: It needn’t be true that $\mu _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { W }$ is quasicoherent, but that doesn’t matter. Non-experts should completely ignore this parenthetical comment.)
By universal property nonsense, these data determine $\beta \colon { \mathcal { S } } p e c { \mathcal { R } } \to X$ up to unique isomorphism, assuming that it exists.
Fancy translation: In the category of $x$ -schemes, $\beta \colon { \mathcal { S } } p e c { \mathcal { R } } \to X$ represents the functor

$$
(\mu \colon W \to X) \longmapsto \left\{\left(\alpha \colon \mathcal {R} \to \mu_ {*} \mathcal {O} _ {W}\right) \right\}.
$$

17.2 Relative Proj of a (Quasicoherent) Sheaf of Graded Algebras

In parallel with the relative version Spec of Spec, we define a relative version of Proj, denoted by Proj (called “relative Proj” or “sheaf Proj”), of a quasicoherent graded sheaf of algebras (satisfying some hypotheses) on a scheme X. We have already done the case where the base X is affine, in §4.5.7, using the regular Proj construction over a ring A. The elegant way to proceed would be to state the right universal property, and then use this cleverly to glue together the constructions over each affine, just as we did in the constructions of fibered product, normalization, and Spec. But because graded rings and graded modules make everything confusing, we do not do this. Instead we guiltily take a more pedestrian approach. (The universal property can be made to work; see [Stacks, tag 01O0]. But I recommend against it—a universal property should make your life easier, not harder.)

text: 17.3 Projective Morphisms

In §17.1, we reinterpreted affine morphisms: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is an affine morphism if there is an isomorphism $x { \longleftrightarrow } s p e c { \mathcal { B } }$ →of Y-schemes for some quasicoherent sheaf of algebras $\mathcal { B }$ on Y. We will define the notion of a projective morphism similarly.
17.3 Projective Morphisms

You might think that because projectivity is such a classical notion, there should be some obvious definition that is reasonably behaved. But this is not the case, and there are many possible variant definitions of projective (see [Stacks, tag 01W8]). All are imperfect, including the accepted definition we give here (see the warnings in §17.3.4).
17.3.1. Definition. A morphism π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is projective if there is an isomorphism

for a quasicoherent sheaf of algebras $\mathcal { S } _ { \bullet }$ on Y satisfying “finite generation in degree $1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ (Hypotheses 17.2.1). We say X is a projective Y-scheme, or X is projective over Y. Using Exercise 7.4.D, this generalizes the notion of a projective A-scheme.

You will then have dealt with the important case where Y is projective, but missed out on other potentially interesting cases, such as when Y is affine or otherwise quasiprojective (but not proper).) $$

Then use Exercise 17.2.G.
17.3.2. Properties of projective morphisms.

The property of a morphism being projective is clearly preserved by base change, as the Proj construction behaves well with respect to base change (Exercise 17.2.E). Also, projective morphisms are proper: properness is local on the target (Theorem 11.4.4(b)), and we saw earlier that projective A-schemes are proper over A (Theorem 11.4.5). In particular (by definition of properness), projective morphisms are separated, finite type, and universally closed.

17.3.B. IMPORTANT EXERCISE: FINITE MORPHISMS ARE PROJECTIVE (CF. EXERCISE 8.3.I). Show that finite morphisms are projective as follows. Suppose $Z \to X$ is finite, so that ${ \cal Z } \cong { \cal S p e c } \mathcal { B } ,$ , where $\mathcal { B }$ is a finite type quasicoherent sheaf of algebras on X. Describe a sheaf of graded algebras $\mathcal { S } _ { \bullet }$ where $\mathcal { S } _ { 0 } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ and $\mathcal { S } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \cong \mathcal { B }$ for $\mathfrak { n } > 0$ . Describe an X-isomorphism $Z \longleftrightarrow \mathcal { P r o j } \mathcal { S } .$ .
Chapter 17 Projective Morphisms, and Relative Versions of Spec and Proj
In particular, closed embeddings are projective. We have the sequence of implications for morphisms

$$
\text {c l o s e d} \Rightarrow \text {f i n i t e} \Rightarrow \text {p r o j e c t i v e} \Rightarrow \text {p r o p e r}.
$$

17.3.3. We have shown that finite morphisms are projective (Exercise 17.3.B), and have finite fibers (Exercise 8.3.J). We will show the converse in Theorem 18.1.6, and state the extension to proper morphisms immediately after (although we won’t prove it until Theorem 28.5.2).
17.3.4. Warnings about projective morphisms.

First, notice that $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ , an important part of the concept of Proj, is not mentioned in the definition. (I would prefer that it be part of the definition, but this isn’t accepted practice.)

Second, [Ha1, p. 103] defines projective morphism differently; we follow the more general definition of Grothendieck. Although these definitions are the same in most circumstances (Exercise 17.3.A(c)), you should be cautious when reading the literature. For example, finite morphisms are not always projective in the sense of [Ha1], while they are projective with this definition (Exercise 17.3.B).

Third, projective morphisms are not reasonable in the sense of §8.1! Although projective morphisms are preserved by base change (§17.3.2), the property of being projective is not local on the target (see §24.7.7 for an example). (With the additional data of the invertible sheaf $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ , and in Noetherian circumstances, it is; see §17.3.8.) Furthermore, we will show that projective morphisms are preserved by composition only when the final target is affine or Noetherian (Exercise 17.3.I).

But many of the consequences of reasonableness still hold, as the next few exercises show. We first prove that the class of projective morphisms, despite its unreasonable nature, still satisfies the Cancellation Theorem 11.1.1.

17.3.5.* Global generation and (very) ampleness in the relative setting.

We extend the discussion of $\ S 1 6 . 1$ to the relative setting, in order to give ourselves the language of relatively base-point-freeness. We won’t use this discussion, so on a first reading you should skip it. But these ideas come up repeatedly in the research literature.

Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a quasicompact quasiseparated morphism. If $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on $\Sigma ,$ we say that $\mathcal { F }$ is relatively globally generated or globally generated with respect to $\pi$ if the natural map of quasicoherent sheaves $\pi ^ { * } \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }  { \mathcal { F } }$ is surjective. Quasicompactness and quasiseparatedness are needed ensure that $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf, Exercise 14.4.C. But these hypotheses are not very restrictive. Global generation is most useful only in the quasicompact setting, and most people won’t be bothered by quasiseparated hypotheses. (Unimportant aside: These hypotheses can be relaxed considerably. If $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of locally ringed spaces— not necessarily schemes—with no other hypotheses, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on X, then we say that $\mathcal { F }$ is relatively globally generated or globally generated with respect to $\pi$ if the natural map $\pi ^ { * } \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }  { \mathcal { F } }$ of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -modules is surjective.)
17.3 Projective Morphisms

Thanks to our hypotheses, as the natural map $\pi ^ { * } \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }  { \mathcal { F } }$ is a morphism of quasicoherent sheaves, the condition of being relatively globally generated is affine-local on Y.

Suppose now that $\mathcal { L }$ is a locally free sheaf on $\mathsf { X } ,$ and π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism. We say that $\mathcal { L }$ is relatively base-point-free or base-point-free with respect to $\pi$ if it is relatively globally generated.

17.3.10.** Quasiprojective morphisms. In analogy with projective and quasiprojective Aschemes (§4.5.10), one may define quasiprojective morphisms. If Y is quasicompact, we say that $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a quasiprojective morphism if $\pi$ can be expressed as a quasicompact open embedding into a scheme projective over Y. This is not a great notion, and we will not use it. (The general definition of quasiprojective morphism is slightly delicate—see [Gr-EGA, II.5.3]—and we won’t need it.)

17.3.11.** Quasiaffine morphisms.

Because we have introduced quasiprojective morphisms, we briefly introduce quasiaffine morphisms (and quasiaffine schemes), as some readers may have cause to use them. Many of these ideas could have been introduced long before, but because we will never use them, we deal with them all at once.

A scheme X is quasiaffine if it admits a quasicompact open embedding into an affine scheme. This implies that X is quasicompact and separated. If X is Noetherian (the most relevant case for most people), then any open embedding is of course automatically quasicompact.

17.3.J. EXERCISE. Show that X is quasiaffine if and only if the canonical map ${ \cal X } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \Gamma ( X , \mathcal { O } _ { \cal X } )$ (defined in Exercise 7.3.G and the paragraph following it) is a quasicompact open embedding. Thus a quasiaffine scheme comes with a canonical quasicompact open embedding into an affine scheme. Hint: Let $\boldsymbol { A } = \boldsymbol { \Gamma } ( \boldsymbol { X } , \mathcal { O } \boldsymbol { x } )$ for convenience. Suppose $X { \hookrightarrow } \operatorname { S p e c } \mathbb { R }$ is a quasicompact open embedding. We wish to show that $X { \hookrightarrow } \operatorname { S p e c } A$ is a quasicompact open embedding. Factor $X { \hookrightarrow } \operatorname { S p e c } \mathbb { R }$ through $\chi \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \ : \lambda \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \ : \mathbb { R } .$ . Show that $X {  } \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda$ is an open embedding in an open neighborhood of any chosen point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ as follows. Choose ${ \mathfrak { r } } \in { \mathbb { R } }$ such that $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { D } ( \mathfrak { r } ) \subset X$ . Notice that if $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } _ { \mathrm { r } } = \{ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { q } } \in \boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } }$ : ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { r } } } ( { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { q } } } ) \neq { \boldsymbol { 0 } } \} ,$ then $\Gamma ( X _ { \mathrm { r } } , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) = \Gamma ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) _ { \mathrm { r } }$ by Exercise 15.4.N, using the fact that X is quasicompact and quasiseparated. Use this to show that the map $X _ { \mathrm { r } }  \mathsf { S p e c } \ A _ { \mathrm { r } }$ is an isomorphism.
It is not hard to show that $x$ is quasiaffine if and only if ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ is ample, but we won’t use this fact.

A morphism π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is quasiaffine if the inverse image of every affine open subset of Y is a quasiaffine scheme. By Exercise 17.3.J, this is equivalent to $\pi$ being quasicompact and separated, and the natural map $X  S p e c \pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ being a quasicompact open embedding. This implies that the notion of quasiaffineness is local on the target (may be checked on an open cover), and also affinelocal on the target (one may choose an affine cover, and check that the preimages of these open sets are quasiaffine). Quasiaffine morphisms are preserved by base change: if a morphism $x \hookrightarrow$ Z over Y is a quasicompact open embedding into a scheme affine over Y, then for any $W \to \mathsf { Y } ,$ ${ \sf X } \times _ { \sf Y } { \sf W } \hookrightarrow { \cal Z } \times _ { \sf Y } { \sf W }$ is a quasicompact open embedding into a scheme affine over W. (Interestingly, Exercise 17.3.J is not the right tool to use to show this base change property.)

One may readily check that quasiaffine morphisms are preserved by composition, [Stacks, tag 01SN] (so quasiaffine morphisms form a “reasonable class”). Thus quasicompact locally closed embeddings are quasiaffine. If X is affine, then $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is quasiaffine if and only if it is quasicompact (as the preimage of any affine open subset of Y is an open subset of an affine scheme, namely, X). In particular, from the Cancellation Theorem 11.1.1 for quasicompact morphisms, any morphism from an affine scheme to a quasiseparated scheme is quasiaffine.

Chapter 18
Č ech Cohomology of Quasicoherent Sheaves

This topic is surprisingly simple and elegant. You may think cohomology must be complicated, and that this is why it appears so late in the book. But you will see that we need very little background. After defining schemes, we could have immediately defined quasicoherent sheaves, and then defined cohomology, and verified that it had many useful properties.
18.1 (Desired) Properties of Cohomology
Rather than immediately defining cohomology of quasicoherent sheaves, we first discuss why we care, and what properties it should have.

As $\Gamma ( \boldsymbol { X } , \cdot )$ is a left-exact functor, if $0 \to { \mathcal { F } } \to { \mathcal { G } } \to { \mathcal { H } } \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of quasicoherent sheaves on a scheme X, then

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {F} (X) \longrightarrow \mathcal {G} (X) \longrightarrow \mathcal {H} (X)
$$

is exact. We dream that this sequence continues to the right, giving a long exact sequence. More explicitly, there should be some covariant functors ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ $( \mathrm { i } \geq 0 )$ from quasicoherent sheaves on $x$ to groups such that $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 }$ is the global section functor $\Gamma ,$ and so that there is a “long exact sequence in cohomology.”

(In general, whenever we see a left-exact or right-exact functor, we should hope for this, and in good cases our dreams will come true. The machinery behind this usually involves derived functors, which we will discuss in Chapter 23.)

Before defining cohomology groups of quasicoherent sheaves explicitly, we first describe their important properties, which are in some ways more important than the formal definition. The boxed properties will be the important ones.

Suppose X is a separated and quasicompact A-scheme. For each quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ we will define A-modules $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } )$ . In particular, if $\mathsf { A } = \mathsf { k } ,$ they are $\mathrm { k }$ -vector spaces. In this case, we define $\mathrm {  ~ \ h ^ { i } ~ } ( X , \mathcal { F } ) = \mathrm { \ d i m _ { k } \ } \mathrm {  ~ \cal H ^ { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } )$ (where $\mathtt { k }$ is left implicit on the left side).

(vi) (affine cover cohomology vanishing) If X can be covered by n affine open sets, then $\boxed { \ H ^ { \ i } ( X , \mathcal { F } ) = 0 }$ for ${ \mathfrak { i } } \geq { \mathfrak { n } }$ for all $\mathcal { F }$ . In particular, on affine schemes, all higher $( \mathfrak { i } > 0 )$ quasicoherent cohomology groups vanish. The vanishing of $\mathsf { H } ^ { \intercal }$ in this case, along with the long exact sequence (iii), implies that Γ is an exact functor for quasicoherent sheaves on affine schemes, something we already knew (Exercise 6.3.A).

text: 18.1.1. Remarks (not part of property (vi)). It is also true that if $\dim { \mathsf { X } } = { \mathsf { n } } ,$ , then ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } ) = 0$ for all ${ \mathfrak { i } } > { \mathfrak { n } }$ and for all $\mathcal { F }$ (dimensional cohomology vanishing). We will prove this for projective $\mathrm { k }$ -schemes (Theorem 18.2.6) and even quasiprojective $\mathrm { k }$ -schemes (Exercise 22.4.T). See $\ S 1 8 . 2 . 7$ for discussion of the general case.
The converse to (vi) in the case when $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ is Serre’s cohomological criterion for affineness; see $\ S 1 8 . 8 . 4$ .

(b) $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( \mathfrak { m } ) )$ is a free A-module of rank $\binom { - \mathsf { m } - 1 } { - \mathsf { n } - \mathsf { m } - 1 }$ if $\mathfrak { m } \le - \mathfrak { n } - 1$
(c) $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( \mathfrak { m } ) ) = 0$ otherwise.

text: We have already shown the first statement in Essential Exercise 15.1.C (see also §15.1.2). We saw there (and in Exercise 15.1.D) that it is advantageous to interpret $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } } ( \mathsf { m } ) )$ as the degree m homogeneous polynomials in $A [ x _ { 0 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ]$ . Similarly, we will find that $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } ( \mathfrak { m } ) )$ can be interpreted as the homogeneous degree $- \mathsf { m }$ terms of $( \mathfrak { x } _ { 0 } \mathfrak { x } _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \mathfrak { x } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ) ^ { - 1 } A [ \mathfrak { x } _ { 0 } ^ { - 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \mathfrak { x } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { - \hat { 1 } } ]$ (see Remark 18.3.5).
Theorem 18.1.2 has a number of features that will be the first appearances of facts that we will prove later.

list_items: • The cohomology of these bundles vanish in degree above n ((vi) above, “affine cover cohomology vanishing”).

• These cohomology groups are always finitely generated A-modules. This will be true for all coherent sheaves on projective A-schemes (Theorem 18.1.3(i)), and indeed (with more work) on proper A-schemes (Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1).
• The top cohomology group vanishes for $m > - n - 1$ . We will later see this as an example of Kodaira vanishing; see §21.5.7.

text: If A is a field k:
• The top cohomology group is one-dimensional for $\mathfrak { m } = - \mathfrak { n } - 1$ . This is the first appearance of the canonical or dualizing sheaf.

• There is a natural perfect pairing

$$
H ^ {i} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m)\right) \times H ^ {n - i} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (- n - 1 - m)\right) \longrightarrow H ^ {n} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (- n - 1)\right).
$$
This is the first appearance of Serre duality. (For the case ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 1 _ { \cdot }$ , see Example 18.5.4.)

• The alternating sum $\sum ( - 1 ) ^ { \mathrm { i } } \mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \sigma ( \mathsf { m } ) )$ is a polynomial in m. This is the first example of a Hilbert polynomial.
Before proving these facts, let’s first use them to prove interesting things, as motivation.

By Theorem 16.1.1, for any coherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ we can find a surjection $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } ) ^ { \oplus \mathrm { j } } \to \mathcal { F }$ which yields the exact sequence
for some coherent sheaf $\mathcal { G }$ . We can use this to prove the following.

18.1.3. Theorem — (i) For any coherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on a projective A-scheme X where A is Noetherian, $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } )$ is a coherent (finitely generated) A-module.
(ii) (Serre vanishing) Furthermore, for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ , $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { m } ) ) = 0$ for all $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ .
(A slightly fancier version of Serre vanishing will be given in Theorem 18.7.F.)
Proof. Because cohomology of a closed subscheme can be computed on the ambient space ((v) above), we may immediately reduce to the case ${ X = \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { n } }$ .
(i) Consider the long exact sequence:

$$
0 \longrightarrow H ^ {0} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {G}\right) \longrightarrow H ^ {0} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m) ^ {\oplus j}\right) \longrightarrow H ^ {0} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {F}\right) \longrightarrow
$$

$$
\mathsf {H} ^ {1} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {A} ^ {n}, \mathcal {G}\right) \longrightarrow \mathsf {H} ^ {1} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {A} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m) ^ {\oplus j}\right) \longrightarrow \mathsf {H} ^ {1} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {A} ^ {n}, \mathcal {F}\right) \longrightarrow \dots
$$

$$
\dots \longrightarrow H ^ {n - 1} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\Lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {G}\right) \longrightarrow H ^ {n - 1} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\Lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m) ^ {\oplus j}\right) \longrightarrow H ^ {n - 1} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\Lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {F}\right) \longrightarrow
$$

$$
\mathrm {H} ^ {n} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\mathcal {A}} ^ {n}, \mathcal {G}\right) \longrightarrow \mathrm {H} ^ {n} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\mathcal {A}} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m) ^ {\oplus j}\right) \longrightarrow \mathrm {H} ^ {n} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\mathcal {A}} ^ {n}, \mathcal {F}\right) \longrightarrow 0
$$

The exact sequence ends here because $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is covered by $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open sets ((vi) above, “affine cover cohomology vanishing”). Then $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathsf { m } ) ^ { \oplus \mathsf { j } } )$ ) is finitely generated by Theorem 18.1.2(b) and (vii), hence $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { F } )$ is finitely generated for all coherent sheaves $\mathcal { F }$ . Hence, in particular, $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { G } )$ is finitely generated. As $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n - 1 } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { m } ) ^ { \oplus \mathrm { j } } )$ is finitely generated, and $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { G } )$ is too, we have that $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { n } - 1 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { F } )$ is finitely generated for all coherent sheaves $\mathcal { F }$ . We continue inductively downward.
(ii) Twist (18.1.2.1) by $\mathcal { O } ( \mathsf { N } )$ for $\Nu \gg 0$ . Then

$$
H ^ {n} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\Lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m + N) ^ {\oplus j}\right) = H ^ {n} \left(\mathbb {P} _ {\Lambda} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m + N)\right) ^ {\oplus j} = 0
$$

(by (vii) above), so $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { N } ) ) = 0$ . Translation: For any coherent sheaf, its top cohomology vanishes once you twist by $\mathcal { O } ( \mathsf { N } )$ for N sufficiently large. Hence this is true for $\mathcal { G }$ as well. Hence from the long exact sequence, $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { n } - 1 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { N } ) ) { = } 0$ for $\Nu \gg 0$ . As in (i), we induct downward, until we get that $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { N } ) ) = 0$ . (The induction stops here, as it is not true that $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathsf { m } +$ $\mathsf { N } ) ^ { \oplus \mathsf { j } } ) = 0$ for large N—quite the opposite.) □
In particular, we have proved the following, which we would have cared about even before we knew about cohomology.
18.1 (Desired) Properties of Cohomology

18.1.4. Corollary — Any projective k-scheme has a finite-dimensional space of functions. More generally, if A is Noetherian and F is a coherent sheaf on a projective A-scheme, then $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( { \mathsf { X } } , { \mathcal { F } } )$ is a coherent A-module.

(We will generalize this in Theorem 18.7.1.) I want to emphasize how remarkable this proof is. It is a question about global sections, i.e., ${ \sf H } ^ { 0 }$ , which we think of as the most down-to-earth cohomology group, yet the proof is by downward induction for $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ , starting with n large.
Corollary 18.1.4 is true more generally for proper $\mathrm { k }$ -schemes, not just projective $\mathrm { k }$ -schemes (Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1).
Here are some important consequences. They can also be shown directly, without the use of cohomology, but with much more elbow grease.

18.1.A. CRUCIAL EXERCISE (PUSHFORWARDS OF COHERENT SHEAVES BY PROJECTIVE MORPHISMS ARE COHERENT). Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a projective morphism of locally Noetherian schemes. Show that the pushforward of a coherent sheaf on X is a coherent sheaf on Y. (See Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorems 18.7.1 and 18.9.1 for generalizations.)
Finite morphisms are affine (from the definition) and projective (Exercise 17.3.B). We can now show that this is a characterization of finiteness.
18.1.5. Corollary (projective $^ +$ affine $=$ finite) — Suppose Y is locally Noetherian. Then a morphism π : $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is projective and affine if and only if π is finite.
We will see in Exercise 18.9.A that the projective hypotheses can be relaxed to proper.

Proof. We already know that finite morphisms are affine (by definition) and projective (Exercise 17.3.B), so we show the converse. Suppose $\pi$ is projective and affine. By Exercise 18.1.A, $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ is coherent and hence finite type. □
The following result was promised in §17.3.3, and has a number of useful consequences.

18.1.6. Theorem (projective $^ +$ finite fibers $=$ finite) — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism, with Y Noetherian. Then π is projective and has finite fibers if and only if it is finite. Equivalently, π is projective and quasifinite if and only if it is finite.

(Recall that quasifinite $=$ finite fibers $^ +$ finite type. But projective includes finite type.) It is true more generally that (with Noetherian hypotheses) proper $^ +$ finite fibers $=$ finite; see Theorem 28.5.2.

Proof. We have already shown that finite morphisms are projective (Exercise 17.3.B) and have finite fibers (Exercise 8.3.J). 18.1.B. EXERCISE (UPPER SEMICONTINUITY OF FIBER DIMENSION ON THE TARGET, FOR PRO-JECTIVE MORPHISMS). Use a similar argument as in Theorem 18.1.6 to prove upper semicontinuity of fiber dimension of projective morphisms: Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a projective morphism where Y is locally Noetherian (or, more generally, ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ is coherent over itself). Show that $\{ \mathfrak { q } \in \Upsilon : \dim \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathfrak { q } ) > \ker { \mathfrak { a } } \}$ is a Zariski-closed sub-

(You can interpret the case $k = - 1$ as the fact that projective morphisms are closed, which is basically the Fundamental Theorem of Elimination Theory 8.4.10; cf. §17.3.2.) This exercise is rather important for having a sense of how projective morphisms behave. (The case of varieties was done earlier, in Theorem 12.4.3(b). This approach is much simpler.)

18.2 Definitions and Proofs of Key Properties

This section could be read much later; the facts we will use are all stated in the previous section. However, the arguments are not complicated, so you want to read this right away. As you read this, you should go back and check off all the facts in the previous section, to assure yourself that you understand everything promised.

18.2.1. Čech cohomology. Čech cohomology in general settings is defined using a limit over finer and finer covers of a space. In our algebro-geometric setting, the situation is much cleaner, and we can use a single cover.

Suppose X is quasicompact and separated, which is true, for example, if X is quasiprojective over A. In particular, X may be covered by a finite number of affine open sets, and the intersection of any two affine open sets is also an affine open set (by separatedness, Proposition 11.2.13). We will use quasicompactness and separatedness only in order to ensure these two nice properties.

Suppose $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf, and $\mathcal { U } = \{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} _ { \mathrm { i } = 1 } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is a finite collection of affine open sets covering X. For ${ \mathrm { I } } \subset \{ 1 , \ldots , { \mathfrak { n } } \}$ define ${ \mathrm { U } } _ { \mathrm { I } } = \cap _ { \mathrm { i } \in \mathrm { I } } { \mathrm { U } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ which is affine by the separated hypothesis. (Here is a strong analogy for those who have seen cohomology in other contexts: cover a topological space X with a finite number of open sets $\mathrm { { U } _ { i } , }$ such that all intersections $\cap _ { \mathrm { i } \in \mathrm { I } } { \mathrm { U } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are contractible.)

The maps are defined as follows. The map from $\mathcal { F } ( \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { I } } ) \longrightarrow \mathcal { F } ( \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { J } } )$ is 0 unless ${ \mathrm { I } } \subset { \mathrm { J } } ,$ , i.e., $J = \operatorname { I } \cup \{ j \}$ . If j is the kth element of J, then the map is $( - 1 ) ^ { \mathrm { k - 1 } }$ → times the restriction map $\mathrm { r e s u _ { I } , u _ { J } }$ .

Theorem/definition — Our standing assumption is that X is quasicompact and separated. $\mathsf { H } _ { \mathcal { U } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { F } )$ is independent of the choice of (finite) cover $\{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \}$ . More precisely, for any two covers $\{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} \subset \{ \mathrm { V } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} ,$ the maps $\mathsf { H } _ { \{ \mathsf { V } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } ) \to \mathsf { H } _ { \{ \mathsf { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } )$ induced by the natural map of Čech complexes (18.2.1.1) are isomorphisms. Define the Čech cohomology group $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } )$ to be this group.
If you are unsure of what the “natural map of Čech complexes” is, by (18.2.3.1) it should become clear.

18.2.3. For experts: Maps of complexes inducing isomorphisms on cohomology groups are called quasiisomorphisms. We are actually getting a finer invariant than cohomology out of this construction; we are getting an element of the derived category of A-modules.

Proof. We need only prove the result when $\lvert \{ \mathsf { V } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} \rvert = \lvert \{ \mathsf { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} \rvert + 1$ . We will show that if $\{ \mathsf { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} _ { 1 \le \mathrm { i } \le \mathrm { n } }$ is a cover of $\Sigma ,$ and $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ is any other affine open set, then the map $\mathsf { H } _ { \{ \mathsf { U } _ { i } \} _ { 0 \leq i \leq n } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathscr { F } ) \to$ $\mathsf { H } _ { \{ \mathsf { U } _ { i } \} _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } )$ i is an isomorphism. Throughout, ${ \mathrm { I } } \subset \{ 0 , \ldots , \mathfrak { n } \}$ . The bottom two rows are Čech complexes with respect to two covers, and the map between them induces the desired map on cohomology. We get a long exact sequence of cohomology from this short exact sequence of complexes (Theorem 1.5.8). Thus we wish to show that the top row is exact and thus has vanishing cohomology. (Note that $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 } \cap \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ is affine by our separatedness hypothesis, Proposition 11.2.13.) But the ith cohomology of the top row is precisely $\mathsf { H } _ { \{ \mathsf { U } _ { j } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } \} _ { j > 0 } } ^ { \mathrm { i } - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } , \mathcal { F } )$ except at the start, where we get 0 (because the complex starts off $\begin{array} { r } { 0 \longrightarrow \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ) \longrightarrow \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { \mathsf { n } } \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { j } ) . } \end{array}$ ). So it suffices to show that higher Čech groups of affine schemes are 0. Hence we are done by the following result. □

18.2.4. Theorem — The higher Čech cohomology $\mathsf { H } _ { \mathcal { U } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { F } )$ of an affine A-scheme X vanishes (for any affine cover $\boldsymbol { \mathcal U }$ , $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ , and quasicoherent $\mathcal { F }$ ).
This is another “partition of unity” argument, in the spirit of the proof of Theorem 4.1.2.
Proof. We want to show that the “extended” complex

(where the global sections $\mathcal { F } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ have been appended to the start) has no cohomology, i.e., is exact. (Incidentally, this extended complex is called the Čech resolution of $\mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { X } )$ , with respect to this choice of cover $\boldsymbol { \mathcal U }$ .) We do this with a trick.

Suppose first that some $\mathrm { { U } _ { i } , }$ say $\mathtt { U } _ { 0 } ,$ is X. We deal with this case by sleight of hand. The complex (18.2.4.1) is the middle row of the following short exact sequence of complexes.
18.2 Definitions and Proofs of Key Properties

The top row is the same as the bottom row, slid over by 1. The corresponding long exact sequence of cohomology shows that the central row has vanishing cohomology. (You should show that the “connecting homomorphism” on cohomology is indeed an isomorphism.) This might remind you of the mapping cone construction (Exercise 1.6.E). (Here is a trick to accomplish the goal of this paragraph from a different point of view. Let id be the identity map between complex (18.2.4.1) and itself, and let 0 be the zero map between the complex (18.2.4.1) and itself. Describe an explicit homotopy between id and 0; cf. §1.5.7. The homotopy will use the “same idea” as (18.2.3.1). Then the identity map on homology equals the zero map on homology, Exercise 1.5.E, so the homology must be zero. This idea can be enlightening because it is used in other circumstances.)

text: Finally, we prove the general case by another sleight of hand. Say $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } =$ $X { = } \mathrm { S p e c } \mathrm { R }$ . We wish to show that the complex of A-modules (18.2.4.1) is exact. It is also a complex of Rmodules, so we wish to show that the complex of R-modules (18.2.4.1) is exact. To show that it is exact, it suffices to show that for a cover of Spec R by distinguished open sets $\mathrm { D } ( \mathrm { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ $( 1 \leq$ $\mathrm { i } \leq \mathrm { r } )$ (i.e., $( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { r } } ) = 1$ in R) the complex is exact. (Translation: Exactness of a sequence of sheaves may be checked locally.) We choose a

cover so that each $\mathrm { D } ( \mathrm { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ is contained in one of our affine open subsets $\mathrm { u _ { j } }$ (see Figure 18.2). The complex (18.2.4.1) localized at $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ is the Čech complex for the affine cover of $\mathrm { D } ( \mathrm { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ by $\{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { k } } \cap \mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } ) \} ,$ . We want to show that this complex (for our fixed i and j) is exact. But this is the case dealt with in the previous paragraph—one of the affine open sets, $\mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { j } } \cap \mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } ) ,$ , equals $\mathrm { D } ( \mathrm { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ . □

We have now proved properties (i)–(iii) of the previous section. The “affine cover cohomology vanishing” property (vi) is also straightforward: if X is covered by n affine open sets, use these as the cover $\boldsymbol { \mathcal U }$ , and notice that the Čech complex ends by the nth step.

18.2.I. EXERCISE (BASE-POINT-FREENESS IS INDEPENDENT OF EXTENSION OF BASE FIELD). Suppose X is a quasicompact separated k-scheme, $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on X, and K/k is a field extension. Show that $\mathcal { L }$ is base-point-free if and only if its pullback to $X \times \mathrm { { _ { S p e c k } } }$ Spec K is basepoint-free. (Hint: Exercise 18.2.H with $\mathfrak { i } = 0$ implies that a basis of sections of $\mathcal { L }$ over $\mathtt { k }$ becomes, after tensoring with $\mathsf { K } ,$ a basis of sections of $\mathcal { L } \otimes _ { \mathsf { k } } \mathsf { K }$ .)

18.2.6. Theorem (dimensional cohomology vanishing for quasicoherent sheaves on projective $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } }$ -schemes) — Suppose $x$ is a projective k-scheme, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on X. Then ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } ) = 0$ for i > dim X.
In other words, cohomology vanishes above the dimension of X.

Proof. Suppose $\chi \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathbb { N } }$ , and let $\mathtt { n } = \mathtt { d i m } X$ . We show that X may be covered by $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open sets. Exercise 12.3.D shows that there are $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ effective Cartier divisors on $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { N } }$ such that their complements $\mathbb { U } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathbb { U } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ cover X. Then $\mathrm { { u } _ { i } }$ is affine, so $\mathrm { { u } } _ { \mathrm { { i } } } \cap \mathsf X$ is affine, and thus we have covered X with $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open sets. □

(It turns out that $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open sets are always necessary. One way of proving this is by showing that the complement of a dense affine open subset is always pure codimension 1; see, for example, [RotV, Lem. 2.3].)

18.2.7.* Dimensional cohomology vanishing more generally. Using the theory of blowing up, Theorem 18.2.6 can be extended to quasiprojective k-schemes; see §22.4.15. Dimensional cohomology vanishing is even true in much greater generality. To state it, we need to define cohomology with the more general machinery of derived functors (Chapter 23). If X is a Noetherian topological space (§3.6.14) and $\mathcal { F }$ is any sheaf of abelian groups on X, we have $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } ) = 0$ for all $\mathrm { i } > \mathrm { d i m } X$ . (Grothendieck sketches his elegant proof in [GrS, pp. 29–30]; see [Ha1, Theorem III.2.7] for a more detailed explanation.) In particular, if X is a k-variety of dimension n, we always have dimensional cohomology vanishing, even for crazy varieties that can’t be covered with $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open subsets (see §22.4.15).
18.3 Cohomology of Line Bundles on Projective Space

We finally prove the last promised basic fact about cohomology, property (viii) of $\ S 1 8 . 1 ,$ , Theorem 18.1.2, on the cohomology of line bundles on projective space. But first we discuss a seemingly different problem, of the cohomology of the structure sheaf of affine space minus the origin.

18.3 Cohomology of Line Bundles on Projective Space
18.4 Riemann–Roch, and Arithmetic Genus

We have seen some powerful uses of Čech cohomology, to prove things about spaces of global sections, and to prove Serre vanishing. We will now see some classical constructions come out very quickly and cheaply.

In this section, we will work over a field k. Suppose $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on a projective k-scheme X. Recall the notation (§18.1) $\begin{array} { r } { \vert \mathrm { h } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } ) : = \dim _ { \mathrm { k } } \mathrm { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } ) } \end{array}$ . By Theorem 18.1.3, $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } )$ is finite. (The arguments in this section will extend without change to proper X once we have this finiteness for proper morphisms, by Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1.) Define the Euler
characteristic of $\mathcal { F }$ by

$$
\chi (X, \mathcal {F}) := \sum_ {\mathrm {i} = 0} ^ {\dim X} (- 1) ^ {\mathrm {i}} h ^ {\mathrm {i}} (X, \mathcal {F}).
$$
We will see repeatedly here and later that Euler characteristics behave better than individual cohomology groups. As one sign, notice that for fixed n, and $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0 ,$ ,

$$
h ^ {0} (\mathbb {P} _ {k} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m)) = \left( \begin{array}{c} n + m \\ m \end{array} \right) = \frac {(m + 1) (m + 2) \cdots (m + n)}{n !}.
$$

Notice that the expression on the right is a polynomial in m of degree n. (For later reference, notice also that the leading term is ${ \mathfrak { m } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } / { \mathfrak { n } } ! .$ ) But it is not true that
for all m—it breaks down for $\mathfrak { m } \le - \mathfrak { n } - 1$ . Still, you can check (using Theorem 18.1.2) that

$$
\chi \left(\mathbb {P} _ {k} ^ {n}, \mathcal {O} (m)\right) = \frac {(m + 1) (m + 2) \cdots (m + n)}{n !}.
$$

So one lesson is this: If one cohomology group (usually the top or bottom) behaves well in a certain range, and then messes up, likely it is because (i) it is actually the Euler characteristic which behaves well always, and (ii) the other cohomology groups vanish in that certain range.

In fact, we will see that it is often hard to calculate cohomology groups (even $\mathtt { h } ^ { 0 }$ ), but it can be easier calculating Euler characteristics. So one important way of getting a hold of cohomology groups is by computing the Euler characteristic, and then showing that all the other cohomology groups vanish. Hence the ubiquity and importance of vanishing theorems. (A vanishing theorem usually states that a certain cohomology group vanishes under certain conditions.) We will see this in action when discussing curves. (One of the first applications will be (19.2.5.1).)
The following exercise shows another way in which the Euler characteristic behaves well: it is additive in exact sequences.

18.4.A. EXERCISE. (Hint: Consider the long exact sequence in cohomology.) More generally, if

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {F} _ {1} \longrightarrow \dots \longrightarrow \mathcal {F} _ {n} \longrightarrow 0
$$
is an exact sequence of coherent sheaves, show that
(This exercise both generalizes the “exact” case of Exercise 1.5.B—consider the case where $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } =$ Spec k—and uses it in the proof.)
