18.4.2. Important definition: Degree of a line bundle on a projective curve (generalized in §18.4.7). Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on a projective curve C over k. (We make no assumption on the regularity, or even reducedness, of C!) Define the degree of $\mathcal { L }$ (denoted by $\deg \mathcal { L }$ or $\deg _ { C } \mathcal { L } )$ by

$$
\deg \mathcal {L} := \chi (\mathsf {C}, \mathcal {L}) - \chi (\mathsf {C}, \mathcal {O} _ {\mathsf {C}}).
$$

Thus we have a degree map deg : Pic $C \to \mathbb { Z }$ . The set of degree d line bundles on C is denoted by ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { \mathrm { d } } { \mathsf { C } } : = \mathbf { d e g } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathbf { d } )$ .

18.4.C. IMPORTANT EASY EXERCISE: DEGREE OF A DIVISOR $=$ DEGREE OF THE CORRE-SPONDING LINE BUNDLE (JUSTIFYING REMARK 16.3.8). The next few exercises give useful applications.

18.4.4. Arithmetic genus.

Motivated by geometry (Miracle 18.4.5 below), we define the arithmetic genus of a dimension n scheme $x$ as $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { a } } ( X ) { : = } ( - 1 ) ^ { \mathfrak { n } } ( { \chi } ( X , \mathcal { O } _ { { X } } ) - 1 )$ . For integral projective curves over an algebraically closed field, $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) = 1$ (§11.4.7), so ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathfrak { a } } ( X ) = { \mathfrak { h } } ^ { 1 } ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } )$ . (In higher dimension, this is a less natural notion.)
We can restate the Riemann–Roch formula for curves (Exercise 18.4.B) as:
This is the most common formulation of the Riemann–Roch formula.

18.4.5. Miracle. If C is a regular irreducible projective complex curve, then the corresponding complex-analytic object, a compact Riemann surface, has a notion called the genus g, which is (informally speaking) the number of holes (see Figure 18.3). Miraculously, ${ \mathfrak { g } } = { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ in this case (see Exercise 21.4.I), and for this reason, we will often write g for $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ when discussing regular (projective irreducible) curves, over any field. We will discuss genus further in $\ S 1 8 . 6 . 7 ,$ , when we will be able to compute it in many interesting cases. (Warning: The arithmetic genus of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ as an $\mathbb { R }$ -variety is $- 1 !$ )

Suppose C is an irreducible reduced curve (possibly singular), over a field k. If $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on C, recall (from §14.3.3) that the rank of $\mathcal { F }$ , denoted by rank $\mathcal { F }$ , is its rank at the generic point of C.

If $\mathcal { F }$ is an invertible sheaf (or if, more generally, the rank is the same on each irreducible component), we can drop the irreducibility hypothesis.

The question of a rational point on a conic is one of arithmetic. (Think: $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = z ^ { 2 }$ .) Our solution was topological. The unification of topology and arithmetic in this example is the beginning of a long and fruitful story in algebraic geometry.
18.4.11.* Numerical equivalence, the Néron–Severi group, nef line bundles, and the nef and ample cones.

Suppose X is a proper $\mathrm { k }$ -variety, and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on X. If i: $C \hookrightarrow X$ is a one-dimensional closed reduced subscheme of X, define the degree of $\mathcal { L }$ on C by deg $\mathcal { L } \mathrm { : = d e g _ { C } \mathrm { i } ^ { \ast } \mathcal { L } . }$ . If deg $\mathcal { L } = 0$ for all C, we say that $\mathcal { L }$ is numerically trivial.

text: 18.4.12. Numerical equivalence. By part (d), the numerically trivial invertible sheaves form a subgroup of $\operatorname { P i c } X ,$ denoted by $\operatorname { P i c } ^ { \tau } X$ . Two lines bundles equivalent modulo the subgroup of numerically trivial line bundles are called numerically equivalent. A property of invertible sheaves stable under numerical equivalence is said to be a numerical property. We will see that “nefness” and ampleness are numerical properties (Definition 18.4.13 and Remark 20.4.2, respectively).

We will later define the Néron–Severi group NS(X) of X as Pic X modulo algebraic equivalence (Exercise 24.7.5). (We will define algebraic equivalence once we have discussed flatness.) The highly nontrivial Néron–Severi Theorem (or Theorem of the Base) states that NS(X) is a finitely generated group. (See [ChJLO] for a thorough modern discussion, and [GH1, p. 462] for a simpler proof over $\mathbb { C } .$ .) The group $\operatorname* { P i c } X / \operatorname* { P i c } ^ { \tau } X$ is denoted by $\mathsf { N } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ . We will see (in §24.7.5) that it is a quotient of NS(X), so it is also finitely generated. As the group $\mathsf { N } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { X } } } )$ is clearly abelian and torsion-free, it is a finite free $\mathbb { Z }$ -module (by the classification of finitely generated modules over a
Chapter 18 Č ech Cohomology of Quasicoherent Sheaves

principal ideal domain; see $\ S 0 . 3$ ). The rank of $\mathsf { N } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ is called the Picard number, and is denoted by $\rho ( X )$ (although we won’t have need of this notion, except in our discussion of the Hodge Index Theorem in §20.2.11). For example, $\rho ( { \mathbb { P } } ^ { { \mathrm { n } } } ) = 1$ and $\rho ( ( { \mathbb P } ^ { 1 } ) ^ { \mathfrak { n } } ) = \mathfrak { n }$ . We define $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( X ) { : = } \mathsf { N } ^ { 1 } ( \mathsf { X } ) \otimes _ { \mathbb { Z } } \mathbb { Q }$ (so $\mathsf { \rho } _ { 0 } ( X ) = \dim _ { \mathbb { Q } } \mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( X ) ) .$ , and call the elements of this group $\mathbb { Q }$ -line bundles, for lack of any common term in the literature.

text: 18.4.W. EXERCISE. Define what it means for a $\mathbb { Q }$ -line bundle to be nef. Show that the nef $\mathbb { Q }$ -line bundles form a closed cone in $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { X } } )$ . This is called the nef cone.

18.4.X. EXERCISE. Describe the nef cones of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ and $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } \times _ { \mathrm { k } } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . (Notice in the latter case that the two boundaries of the cone correspond to linear series contracting one of the P1’s. This is true in general: informally speaking, linear series corresponding to the boundaries of the cone give interesting contractions. Another example will be given in Exercise 20.2.F.)

It is a surprising fact that whether an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on X is ample depends only on its class in $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { X } } ) ,$ , i.e., on how it intersects the curves in X. Because of this (as for any $\mathfrak { n } \in \mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ , $\mathcal { L }$ is ample if and only if ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ is ample; see Theorem 16.2.2), it makes sense to define when a $\mathbb { Q }$ -line bundle is ample. Then by Exercise 16.2.H, the ample divisors form a cone in $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( X )$ , necessarily contained in the nef cone by Exercise 18.4.V(e). It turns out that if X is projective, the ample divisors are precisely the interior of the nef cone. The new facts in this paragraph are a consequence of Kleiman’s criterion for ampleness, Theorem 20.4.6.
18.5 A First Glimpse of Serre Duality
A common version of Riemann–Roch involves Serre duality, which, unlike Riemann–Roch, is hard to prove.

18.5.1. Theorem (Serre duality for smooth projective varieties) — Suppose X is a geometrically irreducible smooth projective k-variety, of dimension n. Then there is an invertible sheaf ωX (or simply ω) on X such that

$$
h ^ {i} (X, \mathcal {F}) = h ^ {n - i} (X, \omega_ {X} \otimes \mathcal {F} ^ {\vee})
$$
for all i ∈ Z and all finite rank locally free sheaves $\mathcal { F }$ .
18.5 A First Glimpse of Serre Duality

The invertible sheaf $\omega _ { X }$ is called the canonical sheaf or dualizing sheaf, which will be formally defined in §29.1.5. We will see in Chapter 29 that Theorem 18.5.1 is a consequence of a perfect pairing
and that smoothness can be relaxed somewhat.

18.5.2. Further miracle: The sheaf of algebraic volume forms is Serre-dualizing. The invertible sheaf $\omega _ { X }$ turns out to be the determinant $\mathcal { K } _ { \sf X }$ of the cotangent bundle $\Omega _ { X / \kappa }$ of X (see §21.5.3); we will define the cotangent bundle in Chapter 21. We make the connection $\omega _ { X } = \mathcal { H } _ { x }$ in §29.4; see Desideratum 29.1.1.

18.5.3. Back to Riemann–Roch. For the purposes of restating Riemann–Roch for a curve C, it suffices to note that $\mathsf { h } ^ { 1 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) = \mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \omega _ { \mathsf C } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \vee } )$ . Then the Riemann–Roch formula can be rewritten as

$$
\mathsf {h} ^ {0} (C, \mathcal {L}) - \mathsf {h} ^ {0} (C, \omega_ {C} \otimes \mathcal {L} ^ {\vee}) = \deg \mathcal {L} - \mathrm {p} _ {a} (C) + 1.
$$
18.5.A. EXERCISE (ASSUMING SERRE DUALITY). Suppose C is a geometrically integral smooth projective curve over k.

list_items: (a) Show that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \omega _ { \mathsf C } )$ is the genus g of C. (The genus $^ { 9 \prime }$ in the guise of the arithmetic genus $\scriptstyle { \mathsf { p } } _ { \mathrm { a } } ,$ was defined in §18.4.4–18.4.5.)
(b) Show that de $\mathrm { g } \omega _ { \mathrm { C } } = 2 \mathrm { g } - 2$ . (Hint: Riemann–Roch for ${ \mathcal { L } } = \omega _ { C }$ .)

text: 18.5.4. Example. If $\mathsf C = \mathbb P _ { \mathsf k } ^ { 1 } ,$ Exercise 18.5.A implies that $\omega _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { C } } \cong \mathcal { O } ( - 2 )$ . Moreover, we also have a natural perfect pairing (cf. (18.5.1.1))

$$
\mathrm {H} ^ {0} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {1}, \mathcal {O} (\mathfrak {n})\right) \times \mathrm {H} ^ {1} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {1}, \mathcal {O} (- 2 - \mathfrak {n})\right) \longrightarrow \mathrm {k} \cong \mathrm {H} ^ {1} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {1}, \mathcal {O} (- 2)\right).
$$

We can interpret this pairing as follows. If $\mathfrak { n } < 0 ,$ , both factors on the left are 0, so we assume $\mathfrak { n } > 0$ . Then $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } , \mathcal { O } ( { \mathsf { n } } ) )$ corresponds to homogeneous degree n polynomials in $x$ and $^ { 1 , }$ and $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } , \sigma ( - 2 - \mathsf { n } ) )$ corresponds to homogeneous degree $- 2 - \mathfrak { n }$ Laurent polynomials in $x$ and y so that the degrees of $x$ and $_ { 9 }$ are both at most $^ { - 1 }$ (see Remark 18.3.5). You can quickly check that the dimension of both vector spaces are $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ . The pairing is given as follows: Multiply the polynomial by the Laurent polynomial, to obtain a Laurent polynomial of degree −2. Read off the coefficient of $\ x ^ { - 1 } \ y ^ { - 1 }$ . (This works more generally for $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { { \mathrm n . } } $ see the discussion after the statement of Theorem 18.1.2.)

18.5.B. EXERCISE (ASSUMING SERRE DUALITY): AMPLE DIVISORS ON A CONNECTED SMOOTH PROJECTIVE VARIETY ARE CONNECTED. Suppose X is a connected smooth projective $\bar { \mathsf k }$ -variety of dimension at least 2, and D is an effective ample divisor. Show that D is connected. Hint: Suppose $\mathrm { D } = \mathrm { V } ( s ) .$ , where s is a section of an ample invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ . Then $V ( s ^ { \mathfrak { n } } ) = \mathsf { V } ( s )$ (as vanishing sets, not vanishing schemes) for all ${ \mathfrak { n } } > 0$ , so we may replace $\mathcal { L }$ with a high power of our choosing. Use the long exact sequence for $0 \to \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } ( - \mathsf { n D } ) \to \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } \to \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { V } ( s ^ { \mathrm { n } } ) } \to 0$ to show that for $\mathsf { n } \gg 0 , \mathsf { h } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { V } ( s ^ { \mathrm { n } } ) } ) = 1$ . (This may remind you of §11.4.7.)

Once we know that Serre duality holds for Cohen–Macaulay projective schemes (§29.3), this result will automatically extend to these schemes. (A related result is Exercise 18.6.U, which doesn’t use Serre duality.) On the other hand, the result is false if X is the union of two 2-planes in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 4 }$ meeting at a point (why?), so this will imply that this X is not Cohen–Macaulay. (We will show this in another way in Counterexample 26.2.2.)
18.5.5.* Classification of vector bundles on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$

As promised in Example 17.2.6, we classify the vector bundles on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . We discuss this in §18.5 because you might use Serre duality at one step (although you might not).

18.5.6. Theorem — If $\mathcal { E } ^ { \mathcal { C } }$ is a rank r vector bundle on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ,$ then $\begin{array} { r } { \mathcal { E } \cong \mathcal { O } ( \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } ) \oplus \cdots \oplus \mathcal { O } ( \mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { r } } ) , } \end{array}$ , for a unique nondecreasing sequence of integers $\mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ .
Chapter 18 Č ech Cohomology of Quasicoherent Sheaves

This result was proved independently many times (see, for example, Dedekind and Weber’s article [DW, §22]), and is a special case of a theorem of Grothendieck, [Gr2]. It is sometimes called Grothendieck’s Theorem, because Grothendieck doesn’t have enough theorems named after him.

18.5.7. For $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ more generally, the case $\mathfrak { r } = 1$ was shown in §15.4.15, but the statement is false for $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon } > 1$ and $\mathsf n > 1$ . A counterexample is given in Exercise 21.3.H, which shows that it is not even possible to filter the rank 2 bundle $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } }$ on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ by line bundles. One true generalization is a theorem of Horrocks, which states that a finite rank locally free sheaf $\mathcal { E } ^ { \mathcal { C } }$ on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ splits precisely when “all the middle cohomology of all of its twists is zero”—when $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathcal { E } ( \mathfrak { m } ) ) = 0$ for $0 < i < n$ and all ${ \mathfrak { m } } \in \mathbb { Z }$ . This has the surprising consequence that if $\mathfrak { n } \geq 2 ,$ then a finite rank locally free sheaf on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ splits if and only if its restriction to some previously chosen 2-plane $( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } )$ splits—all of the additional complication turns up already in dimension 2. See [OSS, §2.3] for more.

Proof. Note that the classification makes no reference to cohomology, but the proof uses cohomology in an essential way. It is possible to prove Theorem 18.5.6 with no cohomological machinery (see, for example, [HM] or [GW, p. 314-5]), or with more cohomological machinery (see, for example, [Ha1, Ex. V.2.6]).

18.6 Hilbert Functions, Hilbert Polynomials, and Genus
If $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on a projective k-scheme $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , define the Hilbert function of $\mathcal { F }$ by

$$
h _ {\mathcal {F}} (m) := h ^ {0} \left(X, \mathcal {F} (m)\right).
$$

Note that the Hilbert function depends on the projective embedding of X (by way of its line bundle), although this dependency is suppressed in the notation. The Hilbert function of X is the Hilbert function of the structure sheaf. The ancients were aware that the Hilbert function is “eventually polynomial,” i.e., for large enough ${ \mathfrak { m } } ,$ it agrees with some polynomial. This polynomial contains lots of interesting geometric information, as we will soon see. In modern language, we expect that this “eventual polynomiality” arises because the Euler characteristic should be a polynomial, and that for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ , the higher cohomology vanishes. This is indeed the case, as we now verify.

18.6.1. Theorem — If $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on a projective k-scheme $\mathbb { X } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ , $\chi ( X , { \mathcal { F } } ( { \mathfrak { m } } ) )$ ) is a polynomial of degree equal to dim Supp $\mathcal { F }$ . Hence by Serre vanishing (Theorem 18.1.3 (ii)), for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ , $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( X , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { m } ) )$ is a polynomial $p _ { \mathcal { F } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ of degree dim Supp $\mathcal { F }$ . In particular, for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ , $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ( { \mathsf { m } } ) )$ ) is polynomial with degree equal to dim X.

18.6.2. Definition. The polynomial $p _ { \mathcal { F } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ defined in Theorem 18.6.1 is called the Hilbert polynomial. If $\ b { \mathrm { X } } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is a projective k-scheme, define $\mathfrak { p } x ( \mathfrak { m } ) { : = } \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ .

Both the degree of the 0 polynomial and the dimension of the empty set are defined to be $^ { - 1 }$ . In particular, the only coherent sheaf with Hilbert polynomial 0 is the zero-sheaf.

This argument uses the notion of associated points of a coherent sheaf on a locally Noetherian scheme, §6.6.2. (The resolution given by the Hilbert Syzygy Theorem 16.1.3 can give a shorter proof; but we won’t prove it until §24.4.)

We first use Exercise 18.2.H to reduce to the case where $\mathrm { k }$ is algebraically closed, and, in particular, infinite. (This is one of those cases where even if you are concerned with potentially arithmetic questions over some non-algebraically closed field like $\mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } . }$ , you are forced to consider the “geometric” situation where the base field is algebraically closed.)
The coherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ has a finite number of associated points (§6.6.17). We show a useful fact that we will use again.

From the Hilbert polynomial, we can extract many invariants, of which two are particularly important. The first is the degree, and the second is the arithmetic genus (§18.6.7). The degree of a projective k-scheme of dimension n is defined to be the leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial (the coefficient of $\mathfrak { m } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ ) times n!.
Using the examples above, we see that the degree of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ in itself is 1. The degree of the twisted cubic is 3.

list_items: 18.6.4. Bézout’s Theorem for plane curves. As a consequence, we have Bézout’s Theorem for plane curves: if C and D are plane curves of degrees m and n respectively, with no common components, then C and D meet at mn points, counted with appropriate multiplicity. (To apply Exercise 18.6.J, you need to know that plane curves have no embedded points. You can do this either by using Exercise 6.6.F, or by assuming that one of the curves is reduced.) The multiplicity of intersection is just the degree of the intersection as a k-scheme.

Show that C is a line. Hint: Reduce to the case $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ . Suppose p and q are distinct closed points on C. Use Bézout’s Theorem (Exercise 18.6.J) to show that any hyperplane containing p and q must contain C, and thus that C ⊂ pq.

18.6.M. EXERCISE (ANOTHER FORM OF BÉZOUT’S THEOREM). Classically, the degree of a complex projective variety of dimension n was defined as follows. We slice the variety with n generally chosen hyperplanes. Then the intersection will be a finite number of reduced points, by Exercise 13.4.C (a consequence of Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2). The degree is this number of points. Use Bézout’s Theorem to make sense of this in a way that agrees with our definition of degree.

text: Thus the classical definition of the degree, which involved making a choice and then showing that the result is independent of choice, has been replaced by making a cohomological definition involving Euler characteristics. This should remind you of how we got around to “correctly” understanding the degree of a line bundle. It was traditionally defined as the degree of a divisor of any nonzero rational section (Important Exercise 18.4.C), and we found a better definition in terms of Euler characteristics (§18.4.6).

18.6.5.** Aside: Connection to the topological definition of degree (for those with sufficient background). Another definition of degree of a dimension d complex projective variety $\ b { \mathrm { X C P _ { C } ^ { n } } }$ is as the number d such that [X] is d times the “positive” generator of $\mathsf { H } _ { 2 \mathrm { d } } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } , \mathbb { Z } )$ . You can show this by induction on d as follows. Suppose X is a complex projective variety of dimension d. 18.6.7. Arithmetic genus, again.

There is another central piece of information residing in the Hilbert polynomial. Notice that $\mathfrak { p } x ( 0 ) = \chi ( X , \mathcal { O } _ { X } )$ is an intrinsic invariant of the scheme X, independent of the projective embedding. It has the same information as the arithmetic genus $( - 1 ) ^ { \dim x } ( \chi ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) - 1 )$ (§18.4.4).

Imagine how amazing this must have seemed to the ancients: they defined the Hilbert function by counting how many “functions of various degrees” there are; then they noticed that when the degree gets large, it agrees with a polynomial; and then when they plugged 0 into the polynomial— extrapolating backward, to where the Hilbert function and Hilbert polynomials didn’t agree—they found a magic invariant! Furthermore, in the case when $x$ is a complex curve, this invariant was basically the topological genus!

We can now see a large family of curves over an algebraically closed field that is provably not $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } !$ ! Note that the Hilbert polynomial of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is $( \mathsf { m } + 1 ) / 1 = \mathsf { m } + 1 ,$ , so $\begin{array} { r } { x ( \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } } ) = 1 . } \end{array}$ Suppose C is a degree d curve in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . Then the Hilbert polynomial of $C$ is

Plugging in $\mathfrak { m } = 0$ gives us $- ( \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } - 3 \mathrm { d } ) / 2$ . Thus when $\mathrm { d } > 2 ,$ we have a curve that cannot be isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } !$ (And it is not hard to show that there exists a regular degree d curve, Exercise 13.3.E.)

Now from $0 \longrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } } \left( - \mathrm { d } \right) \longrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } } \longrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { C } } \longrightarrow 0 ,$ using $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { h } ^ { 1 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } } \left( - \mathsf { d } \right) ) = 0 , } \end{array}$ , we have that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf C } ) = 1$ . As $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { h } } ^ { 0 } - \boldsymbol { \mathrm { h } } ^ { 1 } = \boldsymbol { \chi } ,$ → we have

We now revisit an interesting question we first saw in $\ S 7 . 5 . 1 1$ . If $\mathrm { k }$ is an algebraically closed field, is every finitely generated transcendence degree 1 extension of k isomorphic to $\operatorname { k } ( \mathbf { x } ) ?$ In that section, we found ad hoc (but admittedly beautiful) examples showing that the answer is “no.” But we now have a better answer. The question initially looks like an algebraic question, but we now recognize it as a fundamentally geometric one. There is an integer-valued cohomological invariant of such field extensions that has good geometric meaning: the genus.

Equation (18.6.7.1) yields examples of curves of genus $0 , 1 , 3 , 6 , \ldots$ (corresponding to degree 1 or 2, 3, 4, 5, . . .). This begs some questions, such as: Are there curves of other genera? (We will see soon, in §19.5.5, that the answer is yes.) Are there other genus 0 curves? (Not if k is algebraically closed, but sometimes yes otherwise—consider $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 0$ in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 2 } ,$ which has no $\mathbb { R }$ -points and hence is not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 1 } ;$ we will discuss this more in §19.3.) Do we have all the curves of genus 3? (Almost all, but not quite. We will see more in §19.7.) Do we have all the curves of genus 6? (We are missing “most of them,” as will be suggested by §19.8.4.)

18.6.8. Caution. The Euler characteristic of the structure sheaf is an incomplete invariant. We will now see that it doesn’t always distinguish between isomorphism classes of irreducible smooth projective varieties.

18.7 Higher Pushforward (or Direct Image) Sheaves

Cohomology groups were defined for $X \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \lambda ,$ where the structure morphism is quasicompact and separated; for any quasicoherent $\mathcal { F }$ on X, we defined $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } )$ . We will now define a “relative” version of this notion, for quasicompact and separated morphisms π: $\Chi \to \ Y ;$ : for any quasicoherent $\mathcal { F }$ on $\Sigma ,$ we will define $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ , a quasicoherent sheaf on Y. (Now would be a good time to do Exercise 1.5.I, the FHHF Theorem, if you haven’t done it before.)
We have many motivations for doing this. In no particular order:

list_items: (1) It “globalizes” what we did before with cohomology.

(2) If $0 \to { \mathcal { F } } \to { \mathcal { G } } \to { \mathcal { H } } \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of quasicoherent sheaves on X, then we know that $0 \to \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } } \to \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { G } } \to \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { H } }$ is exact, and higher pushforwards will extend this to a long exact sequence.

(3) We will later see that this will show how cohomology groups vary in families, especially in “nice” situations. Intuitively, if we have a nice family of varieties, and a family of sheaves on them, we could hope that the cohomology varies nicely in families, and in fact, in “nice” situations, this is true. (As always, “nice” usually means “flat,” whatever that means. We will see that Euler characteristics are locally constant in proper flat families in $\ S 2 4 . 7 ,$ , and the Cohomology and Base Change Theorem 25.1.6 will show that in particularly good situations, dimensions of cohomology groups are constant.)

text: All of the important properties of cohomology described in §18.1 will carry over to this more general situation. Best of all, there will be no extra work required.
18.7 Higher Pushforward (or Direct Image) Sheaves

In the notation $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ for higher pushforward sheaves, the $" \mathrm { R } ^ { \prime \prime }$ stands for “right derived functor,” and corresponds to the fact that we get a long exact sequence in cohomology extending to the right (from the 0th terms). In Chapter 23, we will see that in good circumstances, if we have a left-exact functor, there is a long exact sequence going off to the right, in terms of right derived functors. Similarly, if we have a right-exact functor (e.g., if M is an A-module, then $\otimes _ { A } M$ is a rightexact functor from the category of A-modules to itself), there may be a long exact sequence going off to the left, in terms of left derived functors.

Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon ,$ and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on X. For each Spec A Y, we have Amodules ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { S p e c } A ) , \mathcal F )$ . $$

The following fact uses the same trick as Theorem 18.1.6 and Exercise 18.1.B.

18.7.5. Theorem (relative dimensional cohomology vanishing) — If π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a projective morphism and Y is locally Noetherian, then the higher pushforwards vanish in degree higher than the maximum dimension of the fibers.

This is false without the projective hypothesis (see Example 18.7.6 below). In particular, you might hope that just as dimensional cohomology vanishing generalized from projective varieties to quasiprojective varieties or more general settings (§18.2.7), relative dimensional cohomology vanishing would generalize from projective morphisms to quasiprojective morphisms, but this is not the case.

18.7.6. Example. Consider the open embedding $\pi \colon \mathbb { A } _ { \boldsymbol { \mathbf { k } } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } - \{ 0 \} \to \mathbb { A } _ { \boldsymbol { \mathbf { k } } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ for $\mathfrak { n } > 1$ . From §18.3.1–18.3.2, $\mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { n } - 1 } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { A } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } - \{ 0 \} } \neq 0$ .
Proof of Theorem 18.7.5. Let m be the maximum dimension of all the fibers.

The question is local on Y, so we will show that the result holds near a point p of Y. We may assume that Y is affine, and hence that $\mathsf X \hookrightarrow \mathbb P _ { \mathsf Y } ^ { \mathrm n }$ .

Let k be the residue field at p. Then $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ is a projective k-scheme of dimension at most m. By Exercise 12.3.D we can find affine open sets $\mathrm { D } ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } ) , \hdots , \mathrm { D } ( \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { m } + 1 } )$ that cover $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ . In other words, the intersection of the $\mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ does not intersect $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ .

If $\mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { S p e c } { \boldsymbol { A } }$ and ${ \mathfrak { p } } = [ { \mathfrak { p } } ]$ (so $\mathsf { k } = \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } } / \mathsf { p } \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ ), then arbitrarily lift each $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ from an element of $\mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ to an element $\mathrm { f _ { i } ^ { \prime } }$ of $A _ { \mathfrak { p } } [ x _ { 0 } , \ldots , x _ { \mathfrak { n } } ]$ . Let F be the product of the denominators of the $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } ;$ note that $\mathsf { F } \notin \mathfrak { p } .$ , i.e., $\mathfrak { p } = [ \mathfrak { p } ] \in \mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { F } )$ . Then $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } \in \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { F } } [ \mathsf { x } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ . The intersection of their zero loci $\cap _ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathsf { V } } ( \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } ) \subset \mathbb { P } _ { A _ { \mathrm { F } } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is a closed subscheme of $\mathbb { P } _ { A _ { F } } ^ { { n } }$ . Intersect it with $x$ to get another closed subscheme of $\mathbb { P } _ { A _ { F } } ^ { { n } }$ . Take its image under $\pi ;$ as projective morphisms are closed, we get a closed subset of $\mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { F } ) { = } \mathsf { S p e c } \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { F } }$ . But this closed subset does not include p; hence we can find an affine open neighborhood Spec B of p in Y missing the image. Serre’s Characterizations of Ampleness and Affineness

Theorem 16.2.2 gave a number of characterizations of ampleness, in terms of projective geometry, global generation, and the Zariski topology. Here is another characterization, this time cohomological, under Noetherian hypotheses. Because (somewhat surprisingly) we won’t use this result, this section is starred.

18.8.1. Theorem (Serre’s cohomological criterion for ampleness) — Suppose A is a Noetherian ring, X is a proper A-scheme, and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on X. Then the following are equivalent.
(a–c) The invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ is ample on X (over A).

(e) For all coherent sheaves $\mathcal { F }$ on $\mathsf { X } ,$ there is an n0 such that for n  n0, ${ \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes { \mathrm { n } } } ) = 0$ for all $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ .

The label (a–c) is intended to reflect the statement of Theorem 16.2.2. We avoid the label (d) because it appeared in Theorem 16.2.6. (Aside: The “properness” assumption cannot be removed; see Example 18.7.6.) Before getting to the proof, we motivate this result by giving some applications. (As a warm-up, you can give a second solution to Exercise 16.2.G in the Noetherian case, using the affineness of $\pi$ to show that $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { i } } ( X , \mathcal { F } \otimes ( \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { L } ) ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } ) = \mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { i } } ( \mathsf { Y } , ( \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ) \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } )$ .)

18.9* From Projective to Proper Hypotheses: Chow’s Lemma and Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem

The main proofs in this section are double-starred because the results are not absolutely necessary in the rest of our discussions, and may not be worth reading right now. But just knowing the statement of Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1 (generalizing Theorem 18.7.1(d)) will allow you to immediately translate many of our arguments about projective schemes and morphisms to proper schemes and morphisms, and Chow’s Lemma is a multipurpose tool to extend results from the projective situation to the proper situation in general.

So you have four choices: (i) Read the proof of Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem; (ii) skim the proof and convince yourself that you could understand it if you wanted to; (iii) skip the proof and assume it is true; or (iv) just retreat to projective hypotheses whenever Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem is invoked. Life is full of compromises, and I won’t judge you.

18.9.1. Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a proper morphism of locally Noetherian schemes. Then for any coherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on X, $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is coherent on Y.
The case $\mathfrak { i } = 0$ has already been mentioned a number of times.

Use Theorem 18.9.1 to show that if π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is proper and affine and Y is Noetherian, then $\pi$ is finite. (Hint: Mimic the proof of the weaker result where proper is replaced by projective, Corollary 18.1.5.)

The proof of Theorem 18.9.1 requires two sophisticated facts. The first is the Leray spectral sequence (Theorem 23.4.5, which applies in this situation because of Exercise 23.5.F). Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ and $\rho \colon \Upsilon  Z$ are quasicompact separated morphisms. Then for any quasicoherent sheaf

$\mathcal { F }$ on X, there is a spectral sequence with the $\mathsf { E } _ { 2 }$ term given by $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \rho _ { * } \big ( \mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { q } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } \big )$ converging to $\mathsf { R } ^ { \mathsf { p } + \mathsf { q } } \left( \rho \circ \pi \right) _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ . Because this would be a reasonable (but hard) exercise in the case we need it (where Z is affine), we will feel comfortable using it. But because we will later prove it, we won’t prove it now.
We will also need Chow’s Lemma.

18.9.2. Chow’s Lemma (proper $=$ projective minus birational projective) — Suppose π : $x $ Spec A is a proper morphism, and A is Noetherian. Then there exists $\mu \colon X ^ { \prime } \to X$ which is surjective and projective, such that $\pi \circ \mu$ is also projective, and such that $\mu$ is an isomorphism on a dense open subset of X:
In particular, if X is a proper $\mathrm { k }$ -variety, it admits a projective birational morphism from a projective $\mathrm { k }$ -variety.

Many generalizations of results from projective to proper situations go through Chow’s Lemma. We will prove this version, and state other versions of Chow’s Lemma, in $\ S 1 8 . 9 . 4$ . Assuming these two facts, we now prove Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1 in a series of exercises.

18.9.3.** Proof of Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1, assuming Chow’s Lemma 18.9.2. $$
X \longrightarrow \prod \bar {U} _ {i} \longrightarrow \operatorname {S p e c} A
$$
is projective).

412 Chapter 18 Č ech Cohomology of Quasicoherent Sheaves
Chapter 19
Application: Curves

We now use what we have developed to study something explicit—curves. Throughout this chapter, we will assume that all curves are projective, geometrically integral, regular curves over a field k. We will sometimes add the hypothesis that k is algebraically closed. Most people are happy with working over algebraically closed fields, and those people should ignore the adverb “geometrically.”

We certainly do not need the massive machinery we have developed in order to understand curves, but with the perspective we have gained, the development is quite clean. The key ingredients we will need are as follows. We use a criterion for a morphism to be a closed embedding, which we prove in §19.1. We use the “black box” of Serre duality (to be proved in Chapter 29). In §19.2, we use this background to observe a very few useful facts, which we will use repeatedly. Finally, in the course of applying them to understand curves of various genera, we develop the theory of hyperelliptic curves in a hands-on way (§19.5), in particular, proving a special case of the Riemann–Hurwitz formula.

If you are jumping into this chapter without reading much beforehand, you should skip §19.1, taking Theorem 19.1.1 as a black box. Depending on your background, you may want to skip §19.2 as well, taking the “crucial tools” as a black box.
19.1 A Criterion for a Morphism to Be a Closed Embedding

We will repeatedly use a criterion for when a morphism is a closed embedding, which is not special to curves. Before stating it, we recall some facts about closed embeddings. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a closed embedding. Then π is projective, and it is injective on points. This is not enough to ensure that it is a closed embedding, as the example of the normalization of the cusp shows (Figure 10.5). Another example is the following.

19.1.A. EXERCISE (FROBENIUS; CF. §8.3.Q). Show that $\pi \colon \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } } } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } } } ^ { 1 }$ given by $x \mapsto x ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ is a bijection on points, and induces an isomorphism of residue fields on closed points, yet is not a closed embedding.
The additional information you need to ensure that a morphism $\pi$ is a closed embedding is that the tangent map is injective at all closed points.

19.1.B. EXERCISE. Show (directly, not invoking Theorem 19.1.1) that in the two examples described above (the normalization of a cusp and the Frobenius morphism), the tangent map is not injective at all closed points.

19.1.1. Theorem — Suppose $k = { \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ,$ , and π : $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a projective morphism of finite type k-schemes that is injective on closed points and injective on tangent vectors at closed points. Then π is a closed embedding.
The converse is straightforward, so you should think of this result as “if and only if.”

(Remark: In this case of finite type schemes over an algebraically closed field, “injective on tangent vectors at closed points” is equivalent to “unramified”; see Exercise 21.7.E. We will define unramified in §21.7.)

The example Spec $\mathbb { C } \longrightarrow \mathrm { S p e c } \mathbb { R }$ shows that we need the hypothesis that k is algebraically closed in Theorem 19.1.1. Those allergic to algebraically closed fields should still pay attention, as we will use this to prove things about curves over k where k is not necessarily algebraically closed (see also Exercises 10.2.K and 19.1.F).

image_caption: Figure 19.1 (variant of Figure 8.4) The projectivity hypothesis in Theorem 19.1.1 cannot be dropped.

text: We need the hypothesis that the morphism be projective, as shown by the example of Figure 19.1. It is the normalization of the node, except we erase one of the preimages of the node. We map $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ to the plane, so that its image is a curve with one node. We then consider the morphism we get by discarding one of the preimages of the node. Then this morphism is an injection on points, and is also injective on tangent vectors, but it is not a closed embedding. (In the world of differential geometry, this fails to be an embedding because the map doesn’t give a homeomorphism onto its image.)
Theorem 19.1.1 appears to be fundamentally a statement about varieties, but it isn’t. We will reduce it to the following result.

list_items: 19.1.2. Theorem — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a finite morphism of Noetherian schemes whose degree at every point of Y (§14.3.4) is 0 or 1. Then π is a closed embedding.

19.1.C. EXERCISE. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a finite morphism whose degree at every point of Y is 0 or 1. Show that $\pi$ is injective on points (easy). If ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ is any point, show that $\pi$ induces an isomorphism of residue fields $\kappa ( \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) ) \xrightarrow { \sim } \kappa ( \mathfrak { p } )$ . Show that π induces an injection of tangent spaces. Thus key hypotheses of Theorem 19.1.1 are implicitly in the hypotheses of Theorem 19.1.2.
19.1.3. Reduction of Theorem 19.1.1 to Theorem 19.1.2. The property of being a closed embedding is local on the target, so we may assume that Y is affine, say Spec B.

text: I next claim that π has finite fibers, not just finite fibers above closed points: the fiber dimension for projective morphisms is upper semicontinuous (Exercise 18.1.B, or Theorem 12.4.3(b)), so the locus where the fiber dimension is at least 1 is a closed subset, so if it is nonempty, it must contain a closed point of Y. Thus the fiber over any point is a dimension 0 finite type scheme over that point, hence a finite set.
Hence π is a projective morphism with finite fibers, thus finite by Theorem 18.1.6.

19.1.D. EXERCISE. Show that the degree of $\pi$ is at most 1 at all closed points of Y. (Hint 1: You will use the “injectivity on tangent vectors” hypothesis here. Hint 2: What are the finite morphisms to Spec $\overline { { \mathbb { k } } } ?$ )
But the degree of a finite morphism is upper semicontinuous, (§14.3.4), hence is at most 1 at all points (do you see why?). We have thus shown that Theorem 19.1.1 would follow from Theorem 19.1.2.

19.1.4. Proof of Theorem 19.1.2. The problem is local on Y, so we may assume Y is affine, say, $\Upsilon =$ Spec B. Thus X is affine too, say, Spec A, and $\pi$ corresponds to a ring morphism $\mathrm { B } \to A$ . We wish to show that this is a surjection of rings, or (equivalently) of B-modules. Let K be the cokernel of this morphism of B-modules:

We wish to show that $\mathsf K = 0$ . It suffices to show that for any maximal ideal n of B, ${ \sf K } _ { \sf n } = 0$ . (Do you remember why?) Localizing (19.1.4.1) at n, we obtain the exact sequence

$$
B _ {n} \longrightarrow A _ {n} \longrightarrow K _ {n} \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Applying the right-exact functor $\otimes _ { \mathtt { B _ { n } } }$ $\mathrm { \Delta B _ { n } / n B _ { n } }$ ), we obtain

Now Spec $\lambda _ { \mathfrak { n } } / { \mathfrak { n } } A _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is the scheme-theoretic preimage of $[ \boldsymbol { \mathsf { n } } ] \in S \mathrm { p e c } \boldsymbol { \mathsf { B } } _ { \mathrm { \scriptscriptstyle . } }$ , so, by hypothesis, it is either empty, or the map $\propto$ is an isomorphism (in which case we will see that the map $\propto$ will turn out to

be the map of residue fields). In the first case, $\lambda _ { \mathfrak { n } } / { \mathfrak { n } } \lambda _ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0$ , from which ${ \sf K } _ { \mathfrak { n } } / { \mathfrak { n } } { \sf K } _ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0$ . In the second case, ${ \sf K } _ { \mathfrak { n } } / { \mathfrak { n } } { \sf K } _ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0$ as well. Applying Nakayama’s Lemma 8.2.9 (noting that A is a finitely generated B-module, hence K is too, hence ${ \sf K } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is a finitely generated $\mathrm { B } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ -module), ${ \sf K } _ { \sf n } = 0$ as desired. □
Exercise 10.2.K can be used to extend Theorem 19.1.1’s reach to general fields ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ not necessarily algebraically closed. The next exercise is an example of how.

Do the same for the Segre embedding from $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { m } } \times _ { S \mathrm { p e c } \mathrm { k } } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . 19.1.F. LESS IMPORTANT EXERCISE. text: 19.2 A Series of Crucial Tools

We are now ready to start understanding curves in a hands-on way. We will repeatedly make use of the following series of crucial remarks, and it will be important to have them at the tip of your tongue.

list_items: 19.2.1. In what follows, C will be a projective, geometrically regular, geometrically integral curve over a field k, and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on C.

19.2.2. Reminder: Serre duality. Serre duality (Theorem 18.5.1) on a geometrically irreducible regular genus g curve C over k involves an invertible sheaf $\omega _ { C }$ (of degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2 ,$ with g linearly independent sections, Exercise 18.5.A), such that for any finite rank locally free sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on C, $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { C } , \mathcal { F } ) = \mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { l - i } } ( \mathsf { C } , \omega _ { \mathsf { C } } \otimes \mathcal { F } ^ { \vee } )$ for $\mathrm { i } = 0 , 1$ . (Better: there is a duality between the two cohomology groups.)

19.2.3. Negative degree line bundles have no nonzero section. If $\deg \mathcal { L } < 0$ , then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) = 0$ . Reason: deg $\mathcal { L }$ is the number of zeros minus the number of poles (suitably counted) of any rational section (Important Exercise 18.4.C). If there is a regular section (i.e., with no poles), then this is necessarily nonnegative. Refining this argument yields the following.

19.2.4. Degree 0 line bundles, and recognizing when they are trivial. If $\deg \mathcal { L } = 0 ,$ , then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) = 0$ or 1; and if $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) = 1$ then ${ \mathcal { L } } \cong { \mathcal { O } } _ { C }$ . Reason: If there is a nonzero section s, it has no poles, and hence no zeros, because deg $\mathcal { L } = 0$ . Then div $s = 0$ , so $\mathcal { L } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } ( \mathrm { d i v } s ) = \theta _ { \mathrm { C } }$ . (Recall how this works; cf. Important Exercise 15.4.G: s gives a trivialization for the invertible sheaf. We have a natural bijection for any open set $\Gamma ( \mathrm { U } , \mathcal { L } ) \longleftrightarrow \Gamma ( \mathrm { U } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { U } } ) ,$ , where the map from left to right is $s ^ { \prime } \mapsto s ^ { \prime } / s ,$ and the map from right to left is $\ell \mapsto s \ell$ .) Conversely, for a geometrically integral projective variety, $\mathbf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathcal { O } ) = 1$ . (§11.4.7 shows this for k algebraically closed—this is where geometric integrality is used—and Exercise 18.2.H shows that cohomology commutes with base field extension.)

text: Serre duality turns these statements about line bundles of degree at most 0 into statements about line bundles of degree at least $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2 ,$ as follows.
19.2.5. We know $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathscr { L } )$ if the degree is sufficiently high. If $\deg \mathcal { L } > 2 g - 2 ,$ then
19.2 A Series of Crucial Tools

So we know $ { \mathsf { h } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C ,  { \mathcal { L } } )$ if $\deg \mathcal { L } \gg 0$ . (This is important—remember this!) Reason: $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { h } } ^ { 1 } ( \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } , \mathcal { L } ) =$ $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf { C } , \omega _ { \mathsf { C } } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \vee } )$ ; but $\omega _ { \mathbf { C } } \otimes \mathcal { L } ^ { \vee }$ has negative degree (as $\omega _ { C }$ has degree $2 9 - 2 )$ , and thus this invertible sheaf has no sections. The result then follows from the Riemann–Roch Theorem in the guise of (18.4.4.1).

list_items: 19.2.A. USEFUL EXERCISE (RECOGNIZING $\omega _ { C }$ AMONG DEGREE $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2$ LINE BUNDLES). Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is a degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2$ invertible sheaf. Show that it has ${ \mathfrak { g } } - 1$ or g sections, and it has g sections if and only if ${ \mathcal { L } } \cong \omega _ { \mathrm { { C } } }$ .

19.2.B. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a curve of genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } > { \mathfrak { l } } ,$ over a field k that is not necessarily algebraically closed. Show that C has a closed point of degree at most $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2$ over the base field. (For comparison: if ${ \mathfrak { g } } = 1$ , for any n, there is a genus 1 curve over $\mathbb { Q }$ with no point of degree less than n, [MO286390]!)

19.2.6. Twisting $\mathcal { L }$ by a (degree 1) point changes $\mathtt { h } ^ { 0 }$ by at most 1. Suppose p is any closed point of degree 1 (i.e., the residue field of $\mathfrak { p }$ is k). Then $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ) - \mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ( - \mathsf p ) ) = 0$ or 1. (The twist of $\mathcal { L }$ by a divisor, such as ${ \mathcal { L } } ( - { \mathfrak { p } } )$ , was defined in §15.4.17.) Reason: Consider $0 \to \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } ( - { \mathsf { p } } ) \to$ $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } \longrightarrow \mathcal { O } | _ { \mathsf { p } } \longrightarrow 0$ ; tensor with $\mathcal { L }$ (this is exact as $\mathcal { L }$ is locally free) to get

text:
$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {L} (- p) \longrightarrow \mathcal {L} \longrightarrow \mathcal {L} | _ {p} \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Then $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L | _ { \mathsf { p } } ) = 1$ , so as the long exact sequence of cohomology starts off

$$
0 \longrightarrow H ^ {0} (C, \mathcal {L} (- p)) \longrightarrow H ^ {0} (C, \mathcal {L}) \longrightarrow H ^ {0} (C, \mathcal {L} | _ {p}),
$$
we are done.

19.2.7. Criterion for $\mathcal { L }$ to be base-point-free. Suppose for this remark that $\mathrm { k }$ is algebraically closed, so all closed points have degree 1 over k. Then if $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) - \mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ( - \mathsf p ) ) = 1$ for all closed points p, then $\mathcal { L }$ is base-point-free, and hence induces a morphism from C to projective space (Theorem 15.2.2). Reason: Given any p, our equality shows that there exists a section of $\mathcal { L }$ that does not vanish at p—so, by definition, p is not a base point of $\mathcal { L }$ .

19.2.8. Next, suppose p and q are distinct (closed) points of degree 1. Then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) -$ $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ( - \mathsf { p } - \mathsf { q } ) ) = \boldsymbol { 0 } , \mathsf 1 ,$ , or 2 (by repeating the argument of Remark 19.2.6 twice). If $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) -$ $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ( - \mathsf { p } - \mathsf { q } ) ) = 2 ,$ then necessarily

Then the linear series $\mathcal { L }$ separates points p and q, i.e., the corresponding map f to projective space satisfies ${ \sf f } ( { \sf p } ) \neq { \sf f } ( { \sf q } )$ . Reason: There is a hyperplane of projective space passing through f(q) but not passing through $\mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { p } ) .$ , or, equivalently, there is a section of $\mathcal { L }$ vanishing at q but not vanishing at p. This is because of the last equality in (19.2.8.1).

19.2.9. By the same argument as above, if p is a (closed) point of degree 1, then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) -$ $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ( - 2 \mathsf { p } ) ) = 0 , \ 1 ,$ , or 2. I claim that if this is 2, then the map corresponding to $\mathcal { L }$ (which is already seen to be base-point-free at p from the above) separates the tangent vectors at p. To show this, we need to show that the cotangent map is surjective. To show surjectivity onto a onedimensional vector space, we just need to show that the map is nonzero. So it suffices to find a function on the target vanishing at the image of p that pulls back to a function that vanishes at p to order 1 but not 2. In other words, we want a section of $\mathcal { L }$ vanishing at p to order 1 but not 2. But that is the content of the statement $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ( - \mathsf p ) ) - \mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ( - 2 \mathsf p ) ) = 1$ .

19.2.10. Criterion for $\mathcal { L }$ to be very ample. Combining some of our previous comments: suppose C is a curve over an algebraically closed field ${ \sf k } ,$ and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf such that for all closed points p and q, not necessarily distinct, $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ) - \mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ( - \mathsf p - \mathsf { q } ) ) = 2 ,$ ; then $\mathcal { L }$ gives a closed embedding into projective space, as it separates points and tangent vectors, by Theorem 19.1.1.

list_items: 19.2.C. EXERCISE. Suppose that $\mathrm { k }$ is algebraically closed, so the previous remark applies. Show that $C \setminus \{ \mathfrak { p } \}$ is affine. Hint: Show that if $\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { j } \gg 0 } \end{array}$ , then $\mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { j } \mathfrak { p } )$ is base-point-free and has at least two linearly independent sections, one of which has divisor jp. Use these two sections to map to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ so that the set-theoretic preimage of $\infty$ is p. Argue that the map is finite, and that $C \setminus \{ \mathfrak { p } \}$ is the preimage of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ . (A trivial variation of this argument shows that ${ \mathsf { C } } \setminus \{ { \mathsf { p } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \mathsf { p } } _ { \mathsf { n } } \}$ is affine if $\mathfrak { n } > 0 .$ )

19.2.11. Powerful conclusion: Numerical criteria for $\mathcal { L }$ to be base-point-free and very ample. We can combine much of the above discussion to give the following useful fact. If k is algebraically closed, then de $\xi \mathcal { L } \ge 2 9$ implies that $\mathcal { L }$ is base-point-free (and hence determines a morphism to projective space). Also, $\deg \mathcal { L } \ge 2 9 + 1$ implies that for any basis of sections $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { s } } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathbf { \boldsymbol { s } } _ { \mathrm { d e g } } \mathbf { \boldsymbol { \mathcal { L } } } - \mathbf { \boldsymbol { g } }$ of ${ \mathcal { L } } $ , the morphism given by $[ s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { \mathrm { d e g } } \mathcal { L } _ { - 9 } ]$ is in fact a closed embedding into $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { d e g } \mathcal { L } - { \mathfrak { g } } }$ (so $\mathcal { L }$ is very ample). Remember this!

19.2.D. EXERCISE (ON A PROJECTIVE, REGULAR, INTEGRAL CURVE OVER ${ \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ,$ AMPLE = POSITIVE DEGREE). Show that an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on a projective, regular, integral curve over $\bar { \mathsf { k } }$ is ample if and only if $\deg \mathcal { L } > 0$ .

text: (This can be extended to curves over general fields using Exercise 19.2.E below.) Thus there is a blunt purely numerical criterion for ampleness of line bundles on curves. This generalizes to projective varieties of higher dimension; this is called the Nakai–Moishezon criterion for ampleness, Theorem 20.4.1.

list_items: 19.2.E. EXERCISE (EXTENSION TO NON-ALGEBRAICALLY CLOSED FIELDS). Show that the statements in §19.2.11 hold even without the hypothesis that k is algebraically closed. (Hint: To show one of the facts about some curve C and line bundle ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ consider instead $\mathrm { C } \times _ { \mathrm { S p e c k } } \mathrm { S p e c k }$ . Then show that if the pullback of $\mathcal { L }$ here has sections giving you one of the two desired properties, then there are sections downstairs with the same properties. You may want to use facts that we have used, such as the fact that base-point-freeness is independent of any extension of the base field, Exercise 18.2.I, or that the property of an affine morphism over k being a closed embedding holds if and only if it does after an extension of k, Exercise 10.2.K.)

19.2.F. EXERCISE (EXTENDING EXERCISE 19.2.D). Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on a projective, geometrically regular, geometrically integral curve C (over a field k). Show that $\mathcal { L }$ is ample if and only if deg $\mathcal { L } > 0$ . Hint: Reduce to the case where k is algebraically closed, with the help of Exercise 10.2.K.

19.2.G. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a curve (in the sense of §19.2.1, as usual in this section), and ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in C$ is a closed point. Show that $C \setminus \{ \mathfrak { p } \}$ is affine. Hint: Exercise 19.2.F.

text: We are now ready to take these facts and go to the races, in §19.3.
19.2.12.* Remark: Proper curves are projective.

(In this final part of §19.2, we no longer assume our curves are smooth, or even regular or reduced.) Our discussion here, along with Serre’s cohomological criterion for ampleness (§18.8), is the key ingredient for showing that every proper curve over a field is projective.

list_items: 19.2.H. EXERCISE. Show that every reduced proper curve C is projective. Hint: Choose a regular point on each irreducible component of C, and let $\mathcal { L }$ be the corresponding invertible sheaf. We hope that $\mathcal { L }$ will be ample. Recall that a line bundle on C is ample if its pullback to the normalization of C is ample (Tricky Exercise 18.8.C). So show that the pullback to the normalization is ample.
19.2.I. EXERCISE. Show that every proper (not necessarily reduced) curve C is projective, using Exercise 18.8.A, after first finding an invertible sheaf on C that you will show to be ample.
19.2.J. EXERCISE. Show that a line bundle on a projective curve is ample if and only if it has positive degree on each component.

text: 19.2 A Series of Crucial Tools
19.3 Curves of Genus 0
We are now ready to (in some form) answer the question: What are the curves of genus 0?

In §7.5.10, we saw a genus 0 curve (over a field k) that was not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \bar { 1 } } { : } x ^ { 2 } + { \mathsf { y } } ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } =$ 0 in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 2 }$ . (It has genus 0 by (18.6.7.1).) We have already observed that this curve is not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 1 } ,$ because it doesn’t have an $\mathbb { R }$ -valued point. On the other hand, we haven’t seen a genus 0 curve over an algebraically closed field with this property. This is no coincidence: the lack of an existence of a k-valued point is the only obstruction to a genus 0 curve being $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ .
19.3.1. Proposition — Suppose C is genus 0, and C has a k-valued (degree 1) point. Then $C \cong \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ .
Thus we see that all genus 0 (integral, regular, projective) curves over an algebraically closed field are isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ .

Proof. Let p be the point, and consider $\mathcal { L } = \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } ( \mathsf { p } )$ . Then deg $\mathcal { L } = 1$ , so we can apply what we know above: first, $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) = 2$ (Remark 19.2.5), and second, these two sections give a closed embedding into $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { l } }$ (Remark 19.2.11, as extended in Exercise 19.2.E). But the only closed embedding of a curve into the integral curve $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { 1 } }$ is an isomorphism! □

As a bonus, Proposition 19.3.1 implies that $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } = 0$ in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 2 }$ has no line bundles of degree 1 over $\mathbb { R }$ ; otherwise, we could just apply the above argument to the corresponding line bundle. This example shows us that over a non-algebraically closed field, there can be genus 0 curves that are not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . The next result lets us get our hands on them as well.
19.3.2. Claim — All genus 0 curves can be described as conics in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ .

Proof. Any genus 0 curve has a degree $^ { - 2 }$ line bundle—the canonical sheaf $\omega _ { C }$ . Thus any genus 0 curve has a degree 2 line bundle: $\mathcal { L } = \omega _ { \mathrm { c } } ^ { \vee }$ . We apply Exercise 19.2.E: deg $\mathcal { L } = 2 \geq 2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 1 _ { \mathbf { \ell } }$ , so this line bundle gives a closed embedding i : $\mathbf { C } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . Exercise 18.6.H ensures that the image is degree 2 as a closed subscheme, but we haven’t quite shown that the curve can be cut out by one homogeneous polynomial of degree 2. (This is more subtle than you might think—you can try to make your own argument before reading on.) We have the isomorphism $\mathrm { S y m } ^ { 2 } \mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } , \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) ) \xrightarrow { \sim } \mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } , \mathcal { O } ( 2 ) )$ (both sides are the homogeneous quadratic polynomials in three variables). Consider the map $\operatorname { S y m } ^ { 2 } { \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { C } , { \mathcal { L } } ) \to { \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { C } , { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes 2 } ) ,$ , which (by (19.2.5.1)) is a map from a vector space of dimension 6 to a vector space of dimension 5. This map has nontrivial kernel, so i(C) is scheme-theoretically contained in $\mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { f } )$ for some homogeneous conic f.
19.3.A. EXERCISE. Use Exercise 18.6.E to show that $\mathrm { i } ( \mathrm { C } ) = \mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { f } )$ scheme-theoretically, completing the proof. □
19.3.B. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a genus 0 curve (projective, geometrically integral, and regular). Show that C has a point of degree at most 2. (The degree of a point was defined in §5.3.8.)

The geometric means of finding Pythagorean triples presented in $\ S 7 . 5 . 9$ looked quite different, but was really the same. There was a genus 0 curve C (a plane conic) with a k-valued point p, and we proved that it was isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . The line bundle used to show the isomorphism wasn’t the degree 1 line bundle ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { C } ( { \mathfrak { p } } ) .$ ; it was the degree 1 line bundle $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } } ( 1 ) | _ { \mathsf { C } } \otimes \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } ( - { \mathsf { p } } )$ .
19.4 Classical Geometry Arising from Curves of Positive Genus
We will use the following proposition and corollary later, and we take this as an excuse to revisit some very classical geometry from a modern standpoint.

19.4.1. Proposition — Recall our standing assumptions for this chapter (§19.2.1), that C is a projective, geometrically regular, geometrically integral curve over a field k. Suppose C is not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ (with no assumptions on the genus of C), and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf of degree 1. Then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal L ) < 2$ .
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

Proof. We could prove this similarly to Proposition 19.3.1 (and the reader may wish to do so). We instead prove it “by hand” because Proposition 19.3.1 requires Serre Duality, which we will not prove until Chapter 29 (and which is hard), and we wish to emphasize that we do not need Serre Duality for this result. Also, some readers may read this argument without ever reading about Serre Duality.

Assume the statement is false, and let $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$ be two (independent) sections. As the degree of the divisor of zeros of $s _ { \mathrm { i } }$ is the degree of $\mathcal { L }$ , each vanishes at a single point ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (to order 1).

Now ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } \neq { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 2 }$ . (Otherwise, if ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } = { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 2 }$ , then $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ has no poles or zeros, and hence is an invertible function by Algebraic Hartogs’s Lemma—see §13.5.20—and a constant function using §11.4.7 after base change to $\bar { \mathsf k }$ . This contradicts the linear independence of $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$ .)

Thus $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$ generate a base-point-free linear series, so we have an induced morphism $\mathbb { C } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . This is a finite degree 1 morphism from one regular curve to another (Exercise 18.4.E), which hence induces a degree 1 extension of function fields, i.e., an isomorphism of function fields, which means that the curves are isomorphic (cf. Theorem 16.3.3). But we assumed that C is not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ,$ so we have a contradiction. □

list_items: 19.4.2. Corollary — If C is a projective, geometrically regular, geometrically integral curve over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ not isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ,$ and p and q are degree 1 points, then $\mathcal { O } _ { \sf C } ( { \sf p } ) \cong \mathcal { O } _ { \sf C } ( { \sf q } )$ if and only $i f p = { \mathsf { q } }$ .
19.4.A. EXERCISE. Prove this. (Possibly useful: Show/use that $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { C } } ( \mathfrak { p } )$ has a global section s with $\mathrm { d i v } ( s ) = { \mathfrak { p } } .$ .)
19.4.B. EXERCISE. Show that if $\mathtt { k }$ is algebraically closed, then C has genus 0 if and only if all degree 0 line bundles are trivial.

19.4.C. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a regular plane curve of degree $e > 2 ,$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ are two plane curves of the same degree d not containing C. By Bézout’s Theorem for plane curves (§18.6.4), $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ meets C at de points, counted “correctly.” Suppose $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ meet C at de − 1 of the “same points,” plus one more. Show that the remaining points are the same as well. More precisely, suppose there is a divisor E on C of degree de − 1, and degree 1 (k-valued) points ${ \mathsf { p } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \tt p } _ { 2 }$ such that $\mathsf { D } _ { \mathrm { i } } \cap \mathsf C = \mathsf E + \mathsf p _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (as divisors on C). Show that ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } = { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 2 }$ . (The case $d = e = 3$ is Chasles’s Theorem, and is the first case of the Cayley–Bacharach Theorem; see [EGH].)

text: As an entertaining application of Exercise 19.4.C, we can prove two classical results. For convenience, in this discussion we will assume k is algebraically closed, although this assumption can be easily removed.
19.4.3. Pappus’s Theorem (Pappus of Alexandria)

— (See Figure 19.2.) Suppose ℓ and m are distinct lines in the plane, and α, $\beta$ , and γ are distinct points on $\ell ,$ , and $\alpha ^ { \prime }$ , $\beta ^ { \prime }$ , and $\gamma ^ { \prime }$ are distinct points on m, and all six points are distinct from $\ell \cap { \mathfrak { m } }$ . Then points $\tt x = \overline { { \alpha \beta ^ { \prime } } } \cap \overline { { \alpha ^ { \prime } \beta } } , y = \overline { { \tt a \gamma } } \cap \overline { { \alpha \gamma ^ { \prime } } }$ , and $z = \overline { { \beta \gamma ^ { \prime } } } \cap \overline { { \beta ^ { \prime } \gamma } }$ are collinear.

image_caption: Figure 19.2 Pappus’s Theorem.

text: Pascal’s “Mystical Hexagon” Theorem was discovered by Pascal at age 16. (What were you doing at age 16?)

19.4.4. Pascal’s “Mystical Hexagon” Theorem — (See Figure 19.3.) If a hexagon $\alpha \gamma ^ { \prime } \beta \alpha ^ { \prime } \gamma \beta ^ { \prime }$ is inscribed in a smooth conic K, and opposite pairs of sides are extended until they meet, the three intersection points $\mathsf { x } = \overline { { \alpha \beta ^ { \prime } } } \cap \overline { { \alpha ^ { \prime } \beta } }$ , $\mathsf { y } = \overline { { \alpha ^ { \prime } \gamma } } \cap \overline { { \alpha \gamma ^ { \prime } } }$ , and $z = \overline { { \beta \gamma ^ { \prime } } } \cap \overline { { \beta ^ { \prime } \gamma } }$ are collinear.

Pappus’s Theorem can be seen as a degeneration of Pascal’s Theorem: the conic degenerates into the union of two lines, and the six points degenerate so that $\alpha , \beta .$ , and $\gamma$ are on one, and $\alpha ^ { \prime }$ , $\beta ^ { \prime }$ , and $\gamma ^ { \prime }$ are on the other. We thus prove Pascal’s Theorem, and you should check that the proof readily applies to proving Pappus’s Theorem.
19.4 Classical Geometry Arising from Curves of Positive Genus

image_caption: Figure 19.3 Pascal’s “Mystical Hexagon” Theorem.

text: 19.4.D. EXERCISE. Suppose $\alpha , \beta , \gamma , \alpha ^ { \prime }$ , $\beta ^ { \prime }$ are five points in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { R } } ^ { 2 } ,$ no three on a line. Show that there is a unique conic K passing through the five points. Show that K is regular. Explain how to construct the tangent to K at $\propto$ using only a straightedge. (Hint for the last part: Apply Pascal’s Theorem, taking $\gamma ^ { \prime } = \alpha$ . You may need to first figure out why you can apply Pascal’s Theorem in this degenerate case.)

In particular, given an ellipse $\mathsf { K } \subset \mathbb { R } ^ { 2 }$ and a point $\alpha \in { \mathsf { K } } ,$ you will now be able to construct the tangent to K at $\propto$ using only a straightedge.

19.4.5. Proof of Pascal’s Theorem 19.4.4. We wish to show that the line xy meets $\overline { { \beta \gamma ^ { \prime } } }$ and $\overline { { \beta ^ { \prime } \gamma } }$ at the same point. Let C be the curve that is the union of K and the line xy. (Warning, cf. §19.2.1: For one time in this chapter, we are not assuming C to be regular!)

Let $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ be the union of the three lines $\overline { { \alpha \beta ^ { \prime } } }$ , $\overline { { { \beta \gamma ^ { \prime } } } }$ , and ${ \overline { { \gamma \alpha ^ { \prime } } } } ,$ , and let $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ be the union of the three lines $\overline { { \alpha ^ { \prime } \beta } } , \ \overline { { \beta ^ { \prime } \gamma } } ,$ and $\overline { { \gamma ^ { \prime } \alpha } }$ . Note that $\mathrm { D } _ { 1 }$ meets C at the nine points $\alpha , \beta , \gamma , \alpha ^ { \prime } , \beta ^ { \prime } , \gamma ^ { \prime } , x , y ,$ and $\overline { { \boldsymbol { x } \boldsymbol { y } } } \cap \overline { { \beta \boldsymbol { \gamma } ^ { \prime } } } ,$ , and $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 }$ meets C at the same nine points, except $\overline { { x y } } \cap \overline { { \beta \gamma ^ { \prime } } }$ is replaced by $\overline { { { \bf x y } } } \cap \overline { { { \bf \beta } ^ { \prime } { \bf \gamma } } }$ . Thus if we knew Corollary 19.4.2 so that it applied to our C (which is singular), then we would be done (cf. Exercise 19.4.C).

So we extend Corollary 19.4.2 to our situation. So to do this, we extend Proposition 19.4.1 to our situation. We have a plane cubic C that is the union of a line L and a conic $\mathsf { K } ,$ and two points $\mathsf { p } = \overline { { \mathsf { x y } } } \cap \overline { { \mathsf { \beta } \gamma ^ { \prime } } }$ and $\mathsf { q } = \overline { { \mathsf { x y } } } \cap \overline { { \mathsf { \beta } ^ { \prime } \gamma } } ,$ with $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { p } ) \cong \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { q } )$ . (Reason: Both are the restriction of $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } } \left( 3 \right)$ 0 to C, twisted by $- ( \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \alpha ^ { \prime } + \beta ^ { \prime } + \gamma ^ { \prime } + x + y )$ .) Call this invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ . Suppose ${ \mathfrak { p } } \neq { \mathfrak { q } }$ . Then the two sections of $\mathcal { L }$ with zeros at p and q give a base-point-free linear series, and thus a morphism π: $\mathbf { C } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 1 }$ , with $\pi ^ { - 1 } \left( 0 \right) = \mathfrak { p }$ and $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \infty ) = \mathbf { q }$ .

By the argument in the proof of Proposition 19.4.1, $\pi$ gives an isomorphism of L with $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . As $\pi$ is proper, $\pi ( \mathsf { K } )$ is closed, and as K is irreducible, $\pi ( \mathsf { K } )$ is irreducible. As $\pi ( \mathsf { K } )$ does not contain 0 (or $\infty$ ), it can’t be all of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . Hence $\pi ( \mathsf { K } )$ is a point.

The conic K meets the line L in two points with multiplicity (by Bézout’s Theorem for plane curves, $\ S 1 8 . 6 . 4 ,$ or by simple algebra). If K L consists of two points a and b, we have a contradiction: K can’t be contracted to both $\pi ( \mathfrak { a } )$ and $\pi ( \mathfrak { b } )$ . But what happens if K meets L at one point, i.e., if the conic is tangent to the line?

19.4.E. EXERCISE. Finish the proof of Pascal’s Theorem by dealing with this case. Hint: Nilpotents will come to the rescue. The intuition is as follows: K L is a subscheme of L of length 2 (the length of a scheme was defined in §6.5.8), but the scheme-theoretic image $\pi ( \mathsf { K } )$ can be shown to be a reduced closed point. □

19.4.6. Remark. The key motivating fact that makes our argument work, Proposition 19.4.1, is centrally about curves not of genus 0, yet all the curves involved in Pappus’s Theorem and Pascal’s Theorem have genus 0. The insight to keep in mind is that union of curves of genus 0 need not have genus 0. In our case, it mattered that cubic curves have genus 1, even if they are a union of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } { } ^ { \prime } { } _ { \mathrm { S } }$ .
Chapter 19 Application: Curves
19.5 Hyperelliptic Curves
We next discuss an important class of curves, the hyperelliptic curves. In this section, we assume k is algebraically closed of characteristic not 2. (These hypotheses can be relaxed, at some cost.)

A (projective regular integral) genus g curve C is hyperelliptic if it admits a double cover of (i.e., degree 2, necessarily finite, morphism to) $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ . (See $\ S 1 6 . 3 . 4$ for a discussion of degrees of maps from curves to curves.) For convenience, when we say C is hyperelliptic, we will implicitly have in mind a choice of double cover π: $C \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . (We will later see that if ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 2 ,$ then there is at most one such double cover, Proposition 19.5.7, so this is not a huge assumption.) The map $\pi$ is called the hyperelliptic map.

By Exercise 16.3.D, the preimage of any closed point p of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ consists of either one point or two points. If $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ is a single point, we say p is a branch point, and $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ is a ramification point of $\pi$ . (The notion of ramification will be defined more generally in §21.7.)

19.5.1. Theorem (hyperelliptic Riemann–Hurwitz formula) — Suppose $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ and char $\ k \neq 2 ,$ $\pi \colon { \mathrm { C } } \to { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } }$ is a double cover by a projective regular irreducible genus g curve over k. Then $\pi$ has $2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2$ branch points.

This is a special case of the Riemann–Hurwitz formula, which we will state and prove in §21.4. You may have already heard about genus 1 complex curves double covering $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , branched over four points.
To prove Theorem 19.5.1, we first prove the following.

19.5.2. Proposition — Assume char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ and $\operatorname { k } = { \overline { { \operatorname { k } } } }$ . Given r distinct points $\mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { r } } \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , there is precisely one double cover branched at precisely these points if r is even, and none if r is odd.

Proof. Pick points 0 and $\infty$ of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ distinct from the r branch points. All r branch points are in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } -$ $\infty { \stackrel { \cdot } { = } } \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } = \operatorname { S p e c } \mathbb { k } [ x ]$ . Suppose we have a double cover of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ , $C ^ { \prime } \to \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ , where $x$ is the coordinate on A1. This induces a quadratic field extension K over $\operatorname { k } ( x )$ . As char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2 ,$ , this extension is Galois. Let $\sigma \colon K \to K$ be the Galois involution. Let $_ { 9 }$ be a nonzero element of K such that $\sigma ( y ) = - y ,$ so 1 and $_ { 9 }$ form a basis for K over the field $\ k ( x )$ , and are eigenvectors of $\sigma$ . Now $\sigma ( \boldsymbol { \psi } ^ { 2 } ) = \ y ^ { 2 } ,$ , so ${ \mathfrak { y } } ^ { 2 } \in { \mathfrak { k } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } )$ . We can replace $_ { 9 }$ by an appropriate $\operatorname { k } ( x )$ -multiple so that $\mathsf { y } ^ { 2 }$ is a polynomial, with no repeated factors, and monic. (This is where we use the hypothesis that k is algebraically closed, to get leading coefficient 1.)

Thus $y ^ { 2 } = { \tt x } ^ { \tt N } + { \tt a } _ { \tt N - 1 } { \tt x } ^ { \tt N - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \tt a } _ { 0 } ,$ where the polynomial on the right (call it $\mathsf { f } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ ) has no repeated roots. The Jacobian criterion for regularity (in the guise of Exercise 13.2.F) implies that this curve $C _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ in $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { S p e c k } [ \mathbb { x } , \mathbb { y } ]$ is regular. Then $C _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ is normal and has the same function field as $C ^ { \prime }$ . Thus $C _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ are both normalizations of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ in the finite extension of fields generated by y, and hence are isomorphic. Thus we have identified $C ^ { \prime }$ in terms of an explicit equation.

The branch points correspond to those values of $x$ for which there is exactly one value of $^ { 1 , }$ i.e., the roots of $\mathsf { f } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ . In particular, $\mathsf { N } = \mathsf { r } ,$ , and

$$
where the ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are interpreted as elements of $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ .

Having mastered the situation over $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ , we return to the situation over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . We will examine the branched cover over the affine open set $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \setminus \{ 0 \} = \operatorname { S p e c } \ k [ \mathrm { u } ] .$ , where $\mathrm { u } = 1 / x$ . The previous argument applied to Spec k[u] rather than Spec k[x] shows that any such double cover must be of the form
