19.5.A. EXERCISE. Suppose char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ . Show that $x$ does not have a square root in the field $\operatorname { k } ( x ) [ y ] / ( y ^ { 2 } - f ( x ) ) ,$ where $\mathsf { f }$ is a polynomial with nonzero roots $\mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathfrak { p } _ { r }$ . 19.5.3. If k is not algebraically closed. If $\mathrm { k }$ is not algebraically closed (but of characteristic not 2), the above argument shows that if we have a double cover of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ , then it is of the form $\boldsymbol { \ y } ^ { 2 } = \boldsymbol { \mathbf { a } } \mathsf { f } ( \boldsymbol { \mathbf { \mathscr { x } } } ) ,$ , where f is monic (and $\mathtt { a } \neq 0$ ). Furthermore, if a and $\mathrm { ~ \tt ~ { ~ a ~ } ~ } ^ { \prime }$ differ (multiplicatively) by an element of $( \mathsf { k } ^ { \times } ) ^ { 2 }$ , then $\mathfrak { y } ^ { 2 } = \mathfrak { a } \mathfrak { f } ( \mathfrak { x } )$ is isomorphic to $\mathfrak { y } ^ { 2 } =  { \mathbf { a } } ^ { \prime } \mathfrak { f } (  { \boldsymbol { \times } } )$ . You may be able to use this to show that (assuming that ${ \sf k } ^ { \sf x } \ne ( { \sf k } ^ { \sf x } ) ^ { 2 } )$ a double cover is not determined by its branch points. Moreover, this failure is classified by $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \times } / ( \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \times } ) ^ { 2 }$ . Thus we have lots of curves that are not isomorphic over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ but become isomorphic over $\bar { \mathsf { k } }$ . These are often called twists of each other.

(In particular, once we define elliptic curves, you will be able to show that there exist two elliptic curves over $\mathbb { Q }$ with the same j-invariant that are not isomorphic; see Exercise 19.10.H.)

19.5.4. Back to proving the hyperelliptic Riemann–Hurwitz formula, Theorem 19.5.1. Our explicit description of the unique double cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ branched over r different points will allow us to compute the genus, thereby completing the proof of Theorem 19.5.1.

We continue the notation (19.5.2.1) of the proof of Proposition 19.5.2. Suppose $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ has affine cover by Spec k[x] and Spec k[u], with $\mathrm { u } = 1 / \mathrm { x } ,$ , as usual. Suppose $\mathbf { C } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 1 }$ is a double cover, given by ${ \mathfrak { y } } ^ { 2 } = { \mathfrak { f } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } )$ over Spec k[x], where f has degree r, and $z ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { u } ^ { \mathrm { r } } \mathrm { f } ( 1 / \mathrm { u } )$ . Then C has an affine open cover by Spec $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] / ( \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } - \mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { x } ) )$ and Spec $\mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { u } , z ] / ( z ^ { 2 } - \mathsf { u } ^ { \mathrm { r } } \mathsf { f } ( 1 / \mathsf { u } ) )$ . The corresponding Čech complex for ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { C }$ is

The second (and last) nonzero part of the complex has basis consisting of monomials $x ^ { \mathfrak { n } } { \mathfrak { y } } ^ { \in }$ , where $\mathfrak { n } \in \mathbb { Z }$ and $\epsilon = 0$ or 1. To compute the genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } = { \mathfrak { h } } ^ { 1 } ( { \mathsf { C } } , { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { C } } ) .$ , we must compute coker d. We can use the first factor $\operatorname { k } [ x , y ] / ( y ^ { 2 } - f ( x ) )$ to hit the monomials $x ^ { \mathfrak { n } } { \mathfrak { y } } ^ { \epsilon }$ , where $\mathfrak { n } \in \mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ and $\epsilon = 0$ or 1. The image of the second factor is generated by elements of the form $\mathfrak { u } ^ { \mathfrak { m } } z ^ { \epsilon }$ , where $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0$ and $\epsilon = 0$ or 1. But $\mathfrak { u } ^ { \mathfrak { m } } z ^ { \epsilon } = \mathfrak { x } ^ { - \mathfrak { m } } ( \mathfrak { y } / \mathfrak { x } ^ { \mathfrak { r } / 2 } ) ^ { \epsilon } .$ . By inspection, the cokernel has basis generated by monomials $x ^ { - 1 } y ,$ $\mathsf { x } ^ { - 2 } \mathsf { y } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } ^ { - \mathsf { r } / 2 + 1 } \mathsf { y } ,$ and thus has dimension $r / 2 - 1 .$ Hence ${ \mathfrak { g } } = { \mathfrak { r } } / 2 - 1$ , from which Theorem 19.5.1 follows. □

19.5.5. Curves of every genus. As a consequence of the hyperelliptic Riemann–Hurwitz formula (Theorem 19.5.1), we see that there are curves of every genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 0$ over an algebraically closed field of characteristic not 2: to get a curve of genus $^ { 9 \prime }$ consider the branched cover branched over
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

$2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2$ distinct points. The unique genus 0 curve is of this form, and we will see in §19.6.2 that every genus 2 curve is of this form. We will soon see that every genus 1 curve (reminder: over an algebraically closed field!) is too (§19.9.5). But it is too much to hope that all curves are of this form, and we will soon see (§19.7.4) that there are genus 3 curves that are not hyperelliptic, and we will even get heuristic evidence that “most” genus 3 curves are not hyperelliptic. We will later give vague evidence (that can be made precise) that “most” genus g curves are not hyperelliptic if ${ \mathfrak { g } } > 2$ (§19.8.4).

19.5.B. EXERCISE. Verify that a curve C of genus at least 1 admits a degree 2 cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ if and only if it admits a degree 2 invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ with $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf C , \mathcal { L } ) = 2$ . Possibly in the course of doing this, verify that if C is a curve of genus at least 1, and C has a degree 2 invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ with at least 2 (linearly independent) sections, then $\mathcal { L }$ has precisely two sections, and that this $\mathcal { L }$ is base-point-free and gives a hyperelliptic map.

19.5.6. Proposition — Assume ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 2$ . If $\mathcal { L }$ corresponds to a hyperelliptic cover $\mathbf { C } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 1 }$ , then $\mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes ( { \mathfrak { g } } - 1 ) } \cong \omega _ { \mathrm { C } }$ .
Proof. Compose the hyperelliptic map with the $( { \mathfrak { g } } - 1 ) { \mathrm { t h } }$ Veronese embedding
The composition corresponds to ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes ( 9 - 1 ) }$ . This invertible sheaf has degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2$ (by Exercise 18.4.G).

We next show that the pullback $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 9 - 1 } , \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) ) \to \mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { C } , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes ( 9 - 1 ) } )$ is injective, i.e., any nonzero $s \in \mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 9 - 1 } , \mathcal { O } ( 1 ) )$ →pulls back to a nonzero element of ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( { \mathsf { C } } , { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes ( 9 - 1 ) } )$ , i.e., the image of C in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 9 - 1 }$ is nondegenerate. This is because the image of C in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 9 - 1 }$ is the image of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ by $\mathrm { v } _ { 9 - 1 }$ (a rational normal curve; see §9.3.6); but the image of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ under the complete linear system $| \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } } ( { \mathfrak { g } } - 1 ) |$ is nondegenerate (Exercise 15.2.C).

Thus ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes ( 9 - 1 ) }$ has at least g sections. But by Exercise 19.2.A, the only invertible sheaf of degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2$ with (at least) $9$ sections is the canonical sheaf. □

As an added bonus, we see that the composition of (19.5.6.1) is the complete linear series $\lvert \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes ( 9 - 1 ) } \rvert$ |—all sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes ( 9 - 1 ) }$ come up in this way.

19.5.7. Proposition (a genus $\geq 2$ curve can be hyperelliptic in only one way) — Any curve C of genus at least 2 admits at most one double cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . More precisely, if $\mathcal { L }$ and $\mathcal { M }$ are two degree 2 line bundles yielding maps $C \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , then $\mathcal { L } \cong \mathcal { M }$ .

Proof. If C is hyperelliptic, then we can recover the hyperelliptic map by considering the canonical linear series given by $\omega _ { C }$ (the canonical map, which we will use again repeatedly in the next few sections): it is a double cover of a degree ${ \mathfrak { g } } - 1$ rational normal curve (by the previous proposition), which is isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . This double cover is the hyperelliptic cover (also by the proof of the previous proposition). Thus the hyperelliptic cover $C \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is determined by the canonical map (so there can be at most one). This map $\mathbf { C } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 1 }$ must be induced by both $\mathcal { L }$ and $\mathcal { M }$ , from which we have ${ \mathcal { L } } \cong { \mathcal { M } }$ (using Theorem 15.2.2, relating maps to projective space and line bundles).

19.5.C. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a hyperelliptic curve of genus at least 2. Show that the hyperelliptic involution commutes with any other automorphism g of C. (Hint: What happens when you conjugate the involution by g?)

19.5.8. The “space of hyperelliptic curves”. Thanks to Proposition 19.5.7, we can now classify hyperelliptic curves of genus at least 2. Hyperelliptic curves of genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 2$ correspond to precisely $2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2$ distinct points on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ modulo $S _ { 2 9 + 2 } ,$ and modulo automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . Thus “the space of hyperelliptic curves” has dimension

$$
2 g + 2 - \dim \operatorname {A u t} \mathbb {P} ^ {1} = 2 g - 1.
$$
19.5 Hyperelliptic Curves

This is not a well-defined statement, because we haven’t rigorously defined “the space of hyperelliptic curves”—it is an example of a moduli space. For now, take this as a plausibility statement. It is also plausible that this space is irreducible and reduced—it is the image of something irreducible and reduced.
19.6 Curves of Genus 2

19.6.1. The reason for leaving genus 1 for later. It might make most sense to jump to genus 1 at this point, but the theory of elliptic curves is especially rich and subtle, so we will leave it for $\ S 1 9 . 9$ .

In general, curves have quite different behaviors (topologically, arithmetically, geometrically) depending on whether ${ \mathfrak { g } } = 0$ , ${ \mathfrak { g } } = 1 .$ , or ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 2$ . This trichotomy extends to varieties of higher dimension. We already have some inkling of it in the case of curves. Arithmetically, genus 0 curves can have lots and lots of rational points, genus 1 curves can have lots of rational points, and by Faltings’s Theorem (Mordell’s Conjecture) any curve of genus at least 2 has at most finitely many rational points. (Thus even before the proof of important cases of the Taniyama–Shimura conjecture [Wi, TWi], and hence Fermat’s Last Theorem, we knew that $x ^ { \mathrm { n } } + y ^ { \mathrm { n } } = z ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ has at most finitely many rational solutions for ${ \mathfrak { n } } \geq 4 _ { i }$ , as such curves have genus ${ \binom { n - 1 } { 2 } } > 1$ ; see (18.6.7.1).) In the language of differential geometry, Riemann surfaces of genus 0 are positively curved, Riemann surfaces of genus 1 are flat, and Riemann surfaces of genus at least 2 are negatively curved. It is a fact that curves of genus at least 2 have finite automorphism groups (see §21.4.9), while curves of genus 1 have one-dimensional automorphism groups (see Question 19.10.6), and the unique curve of genus 0 over an algebraically closed field has a three-dimensional automorphism group (see Exercises 15.5.A and 15.5.B). (See Exercise 21.5.F for more on this issue.)
19.6.2. Back to curves of genus 2.

Over an algebraically closed field, we saw in §19.3 that there is only one genus 0 curve. In $\ S 1 9 . 5$ we established that there are hyperelliptic curves of genus 2. How can we get a hold of curves of genus 2? For example, are they all hyperelliptic? “How many” are there? We now tackle these questions.

Fix a curve C of genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } = 2$ . Then $\omega _ { C }$ is degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2 = 2 ,$ and has two sections (Exercise 19.2.A). By Exercise 19.5.B, $\omega _ { C }$ is base-point-free, and thus induces a double cover $C \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (unique up to automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , which we studied in $\ S 1 5 . 5 . 2$ ). Conversely, any double cover $\mathbf { C } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 1 }$ arises from a degree 2 invertible sheaf with at least two sections. Hence if ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathsf { C } } ) = 2 ,$ this invertible sheaf must be the canonical sheaf, just because it is a degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2 = 2$ line bundle with ${ \mathfrak { g } } = 2$ sections (or, alternatively, by the easiest case of Proposition 19.5.6).

Hence we have a natural bijection between genus 2 curves and genus 2 double covers of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (up to automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ ). If the characteristic is not 2, the hyperelliptic Riemann–Hurwitz formula (Theorem 19.5.1) shows that the double cover is branched over $2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2 = 6$ geometric points. In particular, we have a “three-dimensional space of genus 2 curves.” This is not rigorous, but we can certainly show that there are an infinite number of genus 2 curves. Precisely:
19.6.A. EXERCISE. Fix an algebraically closed field k of characteristic not 2. Show that there are an infinite number of (pairwise) nonisomorphic genus 2 curves over k.
19.6.B. EXERCISE. Show that every genus 2 curve (over any field of characteristic not 2) has finite automorphism group.
19.7 Curves of Genus 3
19.7.1. The canonical embedding.
We begin with some general discussion about genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } > { \mathfrak { l } }$ , then specialize to genus 3.
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

19.7.A. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Assume $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ (purely to avoid distraction—feel free to remove this hypothesis). Suppose C is a genus g curve, with ${ \mathfrak { g } } > { \mathfrak { l } }$ . Show that if C is not hyperelliptic, then the canonical invertible sheaf gives a closed embedding ${ \mathrm { C } } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { { 9 } - { 1 } }$ . (In the hyperelliptic case, we have already seen that the canonical sheaf gives us a double cover of a rational normal curve.) Hint: Recall that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \omega _ { \mathsf C } ) = \mathsf { g }$ (Exercise 19.2.A). Use our “closed embedding test” of §19.1, in the form of §19.2.10. Recall that if $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { C } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { p } + \mathfrak { q } ) ) = 2 ,$ then C is hyperelliptic (Exercise 19.5.B).
This exercise gives a second proof that all genus 2 curves are hyperelliptic.

19.7.2. Definition. Such a curve embedded in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 9 - 1 }$ by the canonical sheaf (also known as the canonical line bundle) is called a canonical curve, and this closed embedding is called the canonical embedding of C.
19.7.3. Back to genus 3.

Suppose now that C is a curve of genus 3. Then $\omega _ { C }$ has degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2 = 4 ,$ , and has ${ \mathfrak { g } } = 3$ sections. By Exercise 19.7.A, if (and only if) C is not hyperelliptic, the canonical map describes C as a degree 4 curve in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ .

Conversely, any quartic plane curve is canonically embedded. Reason: The curve has genus 3 (see (18.6.7.1)), and is mapped by an invertible sheaf of degree 4 with three sections. But by Exercise 19.2.A, the only invertible sheaf of degree $2 { \mathfrak { g } } - 2$ with g sections is $\omega _ { C }$ .

In particular, each nonhyperelliptic genus 3 curve can be described as a quartic plane curve in precisely one way (up to automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ , which were described in Exercise 15.5.A). We have shown a bijection between nonhyperelliptic genus 3 curves and plane quartic curves up to projective linear transformations (§15.5.2).
19.7.4. Remark. In particular, as there exist regular plane quartics (Exercise 13.3.E), there exist nonhyperelliptic genus 3 curves.

19.7.B. EXERCISE (CF. §19.5.8). Give a heuristic (non-rigorous) argument that the nonhyperelliptic curves of genus 3 form a family of dimension 6. (Hint: Count the dimension of the family of regular quartic plane curves, and quotient by Aut $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } =$ PGL(3).)

The genus 3 curves thus seem to come in two families: the hyperelliptic curves (a family of dimension 5), and the nonhyperelliptic curves (a family of dimension 6). This is misleading— they actually come in a single family of dimension 6. In fact, hyperelliptic curves are naturally limits of nonhyperelliptic curves. We can write down an explicit family. (This explanation necessarily requires some hand-waving, as it involves topics we haven’t seen yet.) Suppose we have a hyperelliptic curve branched over $2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2 = 8$ points of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . Choose an isomorphism of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ with a conic in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . There is a regular quartic plane curve meeting the conic at precisely those eight points. (This requires a short argument using Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2, which we omit.) Then if $\mathsf { f }$ is the equation of the conic, and g is the equation of the quartic, then $\mathsf { f } ^ { 2 } + \mathsf { t } ^ { 2 } \mathsf { g }$ is a family of quartics that are smooth for most t (smoothness is an open condition in $\mathrm { t , }$ as we will see in Theorem 21.6.6 on generic smoothness). The $\mathrm { t } = 0$ case is a double conic. Then it is a fact that if you normalize the total space of the family, the central fiber (above $\mathrm { t } = 0$ ) turns into our hyperelliptic curve. Thus we have expressed our hyperelliptic curve as a limit of nonhyperelliptic curves.

19.7.C. UNIMPORTANT EXERCISE. A (projective) curve (over a field k) admitting a degree 3 cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is called trigonal. Show that every nonhyperelliptic genus 3 complex curve is trigonal, by taking the quartic model in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ , and projecting to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ from any point on the curve. Do this by choosing coordinates on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ so that p is at $[ 0 , 0 , 1 ]$ . (The same idea, applied to cubics rather than quartics, will be used in §19.9.9.)
19.7 Curves of Genus 3
19.7.5.* A genus 3 curve with no nontrivial automorphisms.
We have seen that a (smooth projective integral) curve of genus at most 2 always has nontrivial automorphisms. It turns out that there are genus 3 curves with no nontrivial automorphisms.

19.7.D. EXERCISE. Suppose $C ^ { \prime } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ is a smooth plane quartic curve (over any field k). Show that there is bijection between automorphisms of $C ^ { \prime }$ and automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ preserving $C ^ { \prime }$ .

Thus to find a genus 3 curve with no nontrivial automorphisms, we need only find a smooth quartic plane curve $C ^ { \prime }$ such that the only automorphism of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ fixing $C ^ { \prime }$ as a set must be the identity. Your intuition may (correctly) tell you that most quartics have this property. But exhibiting a specific $C ^ { \prime }$ (with proof) requires rolling up our sleeves and getting to work. Poonen gives automorphism free curves over any field in [P1]; an example in characteristic 0 is
19.7.E. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a smooth projective curve with no nontrivial automorphisms. Show that no two distinct open subsets of C are isomorphic.
19.7.6. Genus 3 curves with nontrivial automorphisms.
Certainly genus 3 curves can have nontrivial automorphisms: witness hyperelliptic curves. More impressive is the Klein quartic

in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 2 } ,$ which has 168 automorphisms. (Can you find them all?) In fact, the automorphism group of the Klein quartic is the unique finite simple group of order 168 (the second-smallest nonabelian finite simple group). In §21.4.9, we will see that no genus 3 curve can have more automorphisms (assuming the hard fact that curves of genus greater than 1 have finite automorphism groups).
19.8 Curves of Genus 4 and 5

We first consider nonhyperelliptic curves C of genus 4. Note that deg $\omega _ { C } = 6$ and $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \omega _ { \mathsf C } ) = 4 , } \end{array}$ , so the canonical map expresses C as a sextic curve in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ . Let $\mathrm { i } \colon { \mathsf { C } } \hookrightarrow { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 3 }$ be the canonical (closed) embedding. We shall see that all such C are complete intersections of quadric surfaces and cubic surfaces, and conversely all regular complete intersections of quadric surfaces and cubic surfaces are genus 4 nonhyperelliptic curves, canonically embedded.
By (19.2.5.1) (Riemann–Roch and Serre duality),

$$
h ^ {0} (C, i ^ {*} \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P} ^ {3}} (2)) = h ^ {0} (C, \omega_ {C} ^ {\otimes 2}) = \deg \omega_ {C} ^ {\otimes 2} - g + 1 = 1 2 - 4 + 1 = 9.
$$

On the other hand, $\begin{array} { r } { \mathtt { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 } } \left( 2 \right) ) = { \binom { 3 + 2 } { 2 } } = 1 0 } \end{array}$ (Exercise 15.1.C). Thus the restriction map

$$
\mathrm {H} ^ {0} \left(\mathbb {P} ^ {3}, \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P} ^ {3}} (2)\right) \longrightarrow \mathrm {H} ^ {0} \left(\mathrm {C}, \mathrm {i} ^ {*} \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P} ^ {3}} (2)\right)
$$

must have a nontrivial kernel, so there is at least one quadric surface Q in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ that contains our curve C. Now quadric surfaces are either double planes, or the union of two planes, or cones, or regular quadrics. (They correspond to quadric forms of rank 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively. The rank of a quadratic form was defined in Exercise 5.4.J.) But C can’t lie in a plane, so Q must be a cone or regular. In particular, Q is irreducible.

Now C can’t lie on two (distinct) such quadrics, say, Q and $\mathrm { Q ^ { \prime } }$ . Otherwise, as Q and $\mathrm { Q ^ { \prime } }$ have no common components (they are irreducible and not the same!), $\mathsf { Q } \cap \mathsf { Q } ^ { \prime }$ is a curve (not necessarily reduced or irreducible). By Bézout’s Theorem (Exercise 18.6.J), $\mathsf { Q } \cap \mathsf { Q } ^ { \prime }$ is a curve of degree 4. Thus our curve C, being of degree 6, cannot be contained in $\mathbf { Q } \cap \mathbf { Q } ^ { \prime }$ . (If you don’t see why directly, Exercise 18.6.E might help.)
19.8.1. We next consider cubic surfaces. By (19.2.5.1) again,

$$
h ^ {0} (C, \omega_ {C} ^ {\otimes 3}) = \deg \omega_ {C} ^ {\otimes 3} - g + 1 = 1 8 - 4 + 1 = 1 5.
$$
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

Now $\mathrm { S y m } ^ { 3 } \Gamma ( \mathrm { C } , \omega _ { \mathrm { C } } )$ has dimension ${ \binom { 3 + 3 } { 3 } } = 2 0$ . Thus C lies on at least a five-dimensional vector space of cubics. Now a four-dimensional subspace comes from multiplying the quadratic form Q by a linear form $( \ref { 2 } x + \ref { 2 } x + \ref { 2 } y + \ref { 2 } z )$ . Hence there is still one cubic K whose underlying form is not divisible by the quadric form Q (i.e., K doesn’t contain Q.) Then K and Q share no component, so K Q is a complete intersection containing C as a closed subscheme. Now K Q and C are both degree 6 (the former by Bézout’s Theorem, Exercise 18.6.J, and the latter because C is embedded by a degree 6 line bundle, Exercise 18.6.H). Also, K  Q and C both have arithmetic genus 4 (the former by Exercise 18.6.S). These two invariants determine the (linear) Hilbert polynomial, so K  Q and C have the same Hilbert polynomial. Hence $C = { \mathsf { K } } \cap Q$ by Exercise 18.6.E.

We now show the converse, and that any regular complete intersection C of a quadric surface with a cubic surface is a canonically embedded genus 4 curve. By Exercise 18.6.S, such a complete intersection has genus 4.
19.8.A. EXERCISE. Show that $\mathcal { O } _ { C } ( 1 )$ has at least four sections. (Translation: C doesn’t lie in a hyperplane.)

The only degree 2g − 2 invertible sheaf with (at least) g sections is the canonical sheaf (Exercise 19.2.A), so $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } ( 1 ) \cong \mathsf { w } _ { \mathsf { C } } ,$ , and C is indeed canonically embedded.
19.8.B. EXERCISE. Give a heuristic argument suggesting that the nonhyperelliptic curves of genus 4 “form a family of dimension 9.”
On to genus 5!

19.8.C. EXERCISE. Suppose C is a nonhyperelliptic genus 5 curve. Show that the canonical curve is degree 8 in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 4 }$ . Show that it lies on a three-dimensional vector space of quadric surfaces (i.e., it lies on three linearly independent quadrics). Show that a regular complete intersection of three quadric surfaces is a canonical(ly embedded) genus 5 curve.

Unfortunately, not all canonical genus 5 curves are the complete intersection of three quadric surfaces in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 4 }$ . But in the same sense that most genus 3 curves can be described as plane quartics, most canonical genus 5 curves are complete intersections of three quadric surfaces, and most genus 5 curves are nonhyperelliptic. The correct way to say this is that there is a dense Zariski-open locus in the moduli space of genus 5 curves consisting of nonhyperelliptic curves whose canonical embedding is cut out by three quadric surfaces.

(Those nonhyperelliptic genus 5 canonical curves not cut out by a three-dimensional vector space of quadric surfaces are precisely the trigonal curves; see Exercise 19.7.C. The triplets of points mapping to the same point of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ under the trigonal map turn out to lie on a line in the canonical embedding. Any quadric vanishing along those three points must vanish along the line—basically, any quadratic polynomial with three zeros must be the zero polynomial.)

list_items: 19.8.D. EXERCISE. Assuming the discussion above, count complete intersections of three quadric surfaces to give a heuristic argument suggesting that the curves of genus 5 “form a family of dimension 12.”
19.8.2. We have now understood curves of genus 3 through 5 by thinking of canonical curves as complete intersections. Sadly, our luck has run out.

19.8.E. EXERCISE. Show that if $C \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { 9 - 1 }$ is a canonical curve of genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 6 ,$ then C is not a complete intersection. (Hint: Bézout’s Theorem, Exercise 18.6.J.)

19.8.3. There is still something beautiful going on. For example, for most genus 6 curves over $\operatorname { k } = { \overline { { \operatorname { k } } } } ,$ the canonical embedding (degree 10 in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 5 }$ ) arises in the following way. Start with the complete intersection of $\mathsf { G } ( 2 , 5 )$ (dimension 6; do you see why?) under its Plücker embedding in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 9 }$ . Intersect it with a linear space of codimension 4, yielding a smooth surface in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 5 }$ . This surface is isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ blown up at four points. (It is a del Pezzo surface, to be defined in Exercise 21.5.H.

text: 19.8 Curves of Genus 4 and 5
This particular surface appears in the table in Remark 27.3.7.) Slice (intersect) the surface with a quadric hypersurface to obtain a genus 6 curve.

19.8.4. Some discussion on curves of general genus. However, we still have some data. If $\mathcal { M } _ { 9 }$ is this ill-defined “moduli space of genus g curves,” we have heuristics to find its dimension for low g. In genus 0, over an algebraically closed field, there is only one genus 0 curve (Proposition 19.3.1), so it appears that dim $\mathcal { M } _ { 0 } = 0$ . In genus 1, over an algebraically closed field, we will soon see that the elliptic curves are classified by the j-invariant (Exercise 19.10.G), so it appears that dim $\mathcal { M } _ { 1 } = 1$ . We have also informally computed dim $\mathcal { M } _ { 2 } = 3$ , dim $\iota \mathcal { M } _ { 3 } = 6 ,$ , dim $\mathcal { M } _ { 4 } = 9$ , dim $\mathcal { M } _ { 5 } = 1 2 .$ What is the pattern? In fact, in some strong sense it was known by Riemann that dim $\mathcal { M } _ { 9 } = 3 { \mathfrak { g } } - 3$ for ${ \mathfrak { g } } > { \mathfrak { l } }$ . What goes wrong in genus 0 and genus 1? As a clue, recall our insight when discussing Hilbert functions (§18.6) that whenever some function is “eventually polynomial,” we should assume that it “wants to be polynomial,” and there is some better function (usually an Euler characteristic) that is polynomial, and that cohomology-vanishing ensures that the original function and the better function “eventually agree.” Making sense of this in the case of $\mathcal { M } _ { 9 }$ is far beyond the scope of our current discussion, so we will content ourselves by observing the following facts. Every regular curve of genus greater than 1 has a finite number of automorphisms—a zero-dimensional automorphism group. Every regular curve of genus 1 has a one-dimensional automorphism group (see Question 19.10.6). And the only regular curve of genus 0 has a three-dimensional automorphism group (Exercise 15.5.B). (See Aside 21.5.12 for more discussion.) So notice that for all ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 0$ ,

$$
\dim \mathcal {M} _ {g} - \dim \operatorname {A u t} C _ {g} = 3 g - 3,
$$
where Aut $C _ { 9 }$ means the automorphism group of any curve of genus g.

In fact, in the language of stacks (or orbifolds), it makes sense to say that the dimension of the moduli space of (smooth projective geometrically irreducible) genus 0 curves is $^ { - 3 , }$ and the dimension of the moduli space of genus 1 curves is 0.
19.9 Curves of Genus 1

Finally, we come to the very rich case of curves of genus 1. We will present the theory by discussing interesting things about line bundles of steadily increasing degree. Although it is not particularly essential to us now, recall that the degree d line bundles on C are denoted by $\mathrm { P i c } ^ { \mathrm { d } } \mathrm { C }$ (Definition 18.4.2).
19.9.1. Line bundles of degree 0 $\mathrm { ( P i c ^ { 0 } C ) }$ .

Suppose C is a genus 1 curve. Then deg $\begin{array} { r } { \omega _ { \mathrm { { C } } } = 2 { \mathrm { g } } - 2 = 0 } \end{array}$ and $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \omega _ { \mathsf C } ) = \mathsf { g } = 1$ (by Exercise 19.2.A). But the only degree 0 invertible sheaf with a section is the structure sheaf (§19.2.4), so we conclude that $\omega _ { C } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { C }$ . (If you know that complex genus 1 curves are of the form $\mathbb { C }$ modulo a lattice, and Miracle 18.5.2 that the sheaf of differentials is the canonical sheaf, then you might not be surprised.)
We move on to line bundles of higher degree. Next, note that if deg $\mathcal { L } > 0$ , then Riemann–Roch and Serre duality (19.2.5.1) give

$$
\mathrm {h} ^ {0} (\mathcal {C}, \mathcal {L}) = \deg \mathcal {L} - \mathrm {g} + 1 = \deg \mathcal {L}.
$$
19.9.2. Line bundles of degree 1 $\operatorname { ( P i c } ^ { 1 } \operatorname { ( C ) } )$ .

Each degree 1 (k-valued) point q determines a line bundle ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { C } ( { \mathfrak { q } } ) ,$ and two distinct points determine two distinct line bundles (as a degree 1 line bundle has only one section, up to scalar multiples). Conversely, any degree 1 line bundle $\mathcal { L }$ is of the form $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } } ( \mathsf { q } )$ (as $\mathcal { L }$ has a section—then just take its divisor of zeros).

Thus we have a canonical bijection between degree 1 line bundles and degree 1 (closed) points. (If k is algebraically closed, as all closed points have residue field ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ this means that we have a canonical bijection between degree 1 line bundles and closed points.)
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

Define an elliptic curve to be a genus 1 curve E with a choice of k-valued point p. The choice of this point should always be considered part of the definition of an elliptic curve—“elliptic curve” is not a synonym for “genus 1 curve.” (Note: A genus 1 curve need not have any kvalued points at all! For example, the hypereliptic genus 1 curve obtained (as in §19.5.2) from ${ \mathsf y } ^ { 2 } = - { \mathsf x } ^ { 4 } - 1$ in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 2 }$ has no $\mathbb { R }$ -points, and hence no $\mathbb { Q }$ -points. Of course, if $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } , } \end{array}$ then any closed point is k-valued, by the Nullstellensatz 3.2.5.) We will often denote elliptic curves by E rather than C.

If $\textstyle ( \mathsf { E } , \mathsf { p } )$ is an elliptic curve, then there is a canonical bijection between the set of degree 0 invertible sheaves (up to isomorphism) and the set of degree 1 points of E: twist the degree 1 line bundles by ${ \mathcal { O } } ( - { \mathfrak { p } } )$ . Explicitly, the bijection is given by

$$
\mathscr {L} \longmapsto \operatorname {d i v} (\mathscr {L} (p))
$$

$$
\mathcal {O} (q - p) \leftarrow q
$$
But the degree 0 invertible sheaves form a group (under tensor product), so we have proved:

19.9.3. Proposition (the group law on the degree 1 points of an elliptic curve) — The above bijection defines an abelian group structure on the degree 1 points of an elliptic curve, where p is the identity.
From now on, we will identify closed points of E with degree 0 invertible sheaves on E without comment.

For those familiar with the complex analytic picture, this is not surprising: E is isomorphic to the complex numbers modulo a lattice: $\operatorname { E } \cong \mathbb { C } / \Lambda$ . (We haven’t shown this, of course.)

Proposition 19.9.3 is currently just a bijection of sets. Given that E has a much richer structure (it has a generic point, and the structure of a variety), this is a sign that there should be a way of defining some scheme ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { E } )$ , and that this should be an isomorphism of schemes. We will soon show (Theorem 19.10.4) that this group structure on the degree 1 points of E comes from a group variety structure on E.

19.9.4. Aside: The Mordell–Weil Theorem, group, and rank. This is a good excuse to mention the Mordell–Weil Theorem: for any elliptic curve E over $\mathbb { Q } ,$ the $\mathbb { Q }$ -points of E form a finitely generated abelian group, often called the Mordell–Weil group. By the classification of finitely generated abelian groups (a special case of the classification of finitely generated modules over a principal ideal domain, Remark 6.4.4), the $\mathbb { Q }$ -points are a direct sum of a torsion part, and of a free $\mathbb { Z }$ -module. The rank of the $\mathbb { Z }$ -module is called the Mordell–Weil rank.
19.9.5. Line bundles of degree 2 $( \operatorname { P i c } ^ { 2 } ( \operatorname { E } ) )$ ; see Figure 19.4.

Note that $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathtt { E } } ( 2 \mathfrak { p } )$ has two sections, so E admits a double cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (Exercise 19.5.B). One of the ramification points is p: one of the sections of $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathtt { E } } ( 2 \mathfrak { p } )$ vanishes at p to order 2, so there is a point of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ whose preimage consists of p (with multiplicity 2). Assume now that $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ and char $\operatorname { k } \neq 2 ,$ so we can use the hyperelliptic Riemann– Hurwitz formula (Theorem 19.5.1), which implies that E has four ramification points (p and three others). Conversely, given four points in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , there exists a unique

double cover branched at those four points (Proposition 19.5.2). Thus elliptic curves correspond to four distinct points in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , where one is marked P, up to automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . Equivalently, by placing P at $\infty _ { \astrosun }$ , elliptic curves correspond to three points in $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ , up to affine maps $x \mapsto \mathbf { a } x + \mathbf { b }$ .

19.9.A. EXERCISE. Show that the other three ramification points are precisely the (non-identity) 2-torsion points in the group law. (Hint: If one of the points is q, show that $\mathcal { O } ( 2 \mathfrak { q } ) \cong \mathcal { O } ( 2 \mathfrak { p } )$ , but $\mathcal { O } ( \mathbf { q } )$ is not congruent to $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { p } )$ .)

Thus (if char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ and $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ ) every elliptic curve has precisely four 2-torsion points. If you are familiar with the complex picture $\mathsf { E } \cong \mathbb { C } / \Lambda ,$ this isn’t surprising.

19.9.6. Follow-up remark. An elliptic curve with full level n structure is an elliptic curve with an isomorphism of its n-torsion points with $( \mathbb { Z } / { \mathfrak { n } } ) ^ { 2 }$ . (This notion has problems if n is divisible by char k.) Thus an elliptic curve with full level 2 structure is the same thing as an elliptic curve with an ordering of the three other branch points in its degree 2 cover description. Thus (if $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ ) these objects are parametrized by the λ-line, which we discuss below.

Follow-up to the follow-up. There is a notion of moduli spaces of elliptic curves with full level n structure. Such moduli spaces are smooth curves (where this is interpreted appropriately—they are stacks), and have smooth compactifications. A weight k level n modular form is a section of $\omega _ { M } ^ { \otimes \mathbf { k } }$ where $\omega _ { M }$ is the canonical sheaf of this moduli space (“modular curve”).

19.9.7. The cross-ratio and the j-invariant. (We maintain the assumption that $\mathrm { k }$ is algebraically closed and characteristic not 2.) If the three other branch points are temporarily labeled $\mathrm { Q } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { Q } _ { 2 } , \mathrm { Q } _ { 3 } \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , there is a unique automorphism of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ taking P, Q1, $Q _ { 2 }$ to $\infty , 0 , 1 .$ , respectively (as Aut $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is 3-transitive, Exercise 15.5.B). Suppose that $Q 3$ ∞is taken to some number $\lambda$ under this map, where necessarily $\lambda \neq 0 , 1 , \infty$ .

The value $\lambda$ is called the cross-ratio of the four points $\left( \mathsf { P } , \mathsf { Q } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { Q } _ { 2 } , \mathsf { Q } _ { 3 } \right)$ of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (invented by Pappus of Alexandria, [MO152008]).

19.9.B. EXERCISE. Show that isomorphism classes of four ordered distinct (closed) points on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (over an algebraically closed field k), up to projective equivalence (automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ ), are classified by the cross-ratio.

19.9.C. EXERCISE. Show that the cross-ratio of $( \mathbf { a } , \mathbf { b } , \mathbf { c } , \mathbf { d } )$ is the same as the cross-ratio of (b, a, d, c). The group $S _ { 4 }$ acts on 4-tuples of points on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , and hence on their cross-ratios. Show that the action of $S _ { 4 }$ on the cross-ratio is trivial when restricted to the “Klein 4-group” ${ \sf V } _ { 4 } \subset { \sf S } _ { 4 }$ .

We have not defined the notion of moduli space, but the previous exercise illustrates the fact that $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \setminus \{ 0 , 1 , \infty \}$ (the image of the cross-ratio map) is the moduli space for four ordered distinct points of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ up to projective equivalence.
Notice:

list_items: If we had instead sent P, $Q _ { 2 } , Q _ { 1 }$ to $\left( \infty , 0 , 1 \right)$ , then $Q _ { 3 }$ would have been sent to $1 - \lambda$
If we had instead sent P, Q1, $Q _ { 3 }$ to $\left( \infty , 0 , 1 \right)$ , then $Q _ { 2 }$ would have been sent to $1 / \lambda$ .
• If we had instead sent P, $Q _ { 3 }$ , $Q _ { 1 }$ to $\left( \infty , 0 , 1 \right)$ , then $Q _ { 2 }$ would have been sent to $1 - 1 / \lambda = ( \lambda - 1 ) / \lambda$
• If we had instead sent P, $Q _ { 2 }$ , $Q _ { 3 }$ to $\left( \infty , 0 , 1 \right)$ , then $Q 1$ would have been sent to $1 / ( 1 - \lambda )$
If we had instead sent P, Q , $Q _ { 2 }$ to $\left( \infty , 0 , 1 \right)$ , then $Q _ { 1 }$ would have been sent to $1 - 1 / ( 1 - \lambda ) =$ $\lambda / ( \lambda - 1 )$ .

text: Thus these six values (which correspond to $S _ { 3 }$ , the symmetric group permuting $Q _ { 1 } , Q _ { 2 } ,$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { Q } _ { 3 } }$ ) yield the same elliptic curve, and this elliptic curve will (upon choosing an ordering of the other three branch points) yield one of these six values.

This is fairly satisfactory already. To check if two elliptic curves (E, p), $( \mathsf { E } ^ { \prime } , \mathsf { p } ^ { \prime } )$ over $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ are isomorphic, we write both as double covers of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ ramified at p and $\mathsf { p ^ { \prime } }$ , respectively, then order the remaining branch points, then compute their respective λ’s (say $\lambda$ and $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ , respectively), and see if they are related by one of the six expressions above:

$$
\lambda^ {\prime} = \lambda , 1 / \lambda , 1 - \lambda , 1 / (1 - \lambda), (\lambda - 1) / \lambda , \text {o r} \lambda / (\lambda - 1).
$$
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

It would be far more convenient if, instead of a “six-valued invariant” $\lambda ,$ there were a single invariant (let’s call it j), such that $\mathfrak { j } ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { j } ( \lambda ^ { \prime } )$ if and only if one of the equalities of (19.9.7.1) holds. This j-function should presumably be algebraic, so it would give a map j from the λ-line $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } \setminus \{ 0 , 1 \}$ to the j-line A1. By the Curve-to-Projective Extension Theorem 15.3.1, this would extend to a morphism $ { \mathrm { j } } : \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . By Exercise 16.3.D, because this is (for most $\lambda$ ) a 6-to-1 map, the degree of this cover is 6 (or more correctly, at least 6).

We can make this dream more precise as follows. The elliptic curves over k correspond to kvalued points of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \setminus \{ 0 , 1 , \infty \} ,$ modulo the action of $S _ { 3 }$ on λ given above. Consider the subfield K of $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } ( \lambda )$ fixed by $S _ { 3 }$ . Then the extension $\kappa ( \lambda ) / \kappa$ is necessarily Galois (see, for example, [DF, §14.2, Thm. 9]), and degree 6. We are hoping that this subfield is of the form $\mathsf { k } ( \mathsf { j }$ ), and if so, we would obtain the j-map $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ as described above. One could show that K is finitely generated over ${ \sf k } ,$ and then invoke Lüroth’s Theorem, which we will soon prove in Example 21.4.7; but we won’t need this.

Instead, we will just hunt for such a j. Note that λ should satisfy a sextic polynomial over $\ k ( \mathrm { j } )$ (or, more precisely, given what we know right now, a polynomial of degree at least 6), as for each j-invariant, there are six values of λ in general.
As you are undoubtedly aware, there is such a j-invariant. Here is the formula for the j-invariant that everyone uses:

You can readily check that $\mathfrak { j } ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { j } ( 1 / \lambda ) = \mathfrak { j } ( 1 - \lambda ) = \cdot \cdot \cdot ,$ , and that as j (in lowest terms) has a degree 6 numerator and degree $< 6$ denominator, j indeed determines a degree 6 map from $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (with coordinate λ) to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (with coordinate j). But this complicated-looking formula begs the question: where did this formula come from? How did someone think of it? We will largely answer this, but we will ignore the $2 ^ { 8 }$ . This, as you might imagine, arises from characteristic 2 issues, and in order to invoke the results of §19.5, we have been assuming char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ . (To see why the $2 ^ { 8 }$ is forced upon us by characteristic 2, see [De, p. 64]. From a different, complex-analytic, point of view, the $2 ^ { 8 }$ comes from the fact that the $\mathbf { q }$ -expansion of j begins $\mathbf { j } = \mathbf { q } ^ { - 1 } + 7 4 4 + \cdots$ : the $\mathsf { q } ^ { - 1 }$ term has coefficient 1; see [Se5, p. 90, Rem. 2]. These seemingly different explanations are related by the theory of the Tate curve; see [Si2, Thm. V.3.1].)

Rather than using the formula handed to us, let’s try to guess what j is. We won’t expect to get the same formula as (19.9.7.2), but our answer should differ by an automorphism of the j-line $( \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ )—we will get $\dot { \mathsf { J } } ^ { \prime } = ( \mathsf { a } \dot { \mathsf { J } } + \mathsf { b } ) / ( \mathsf { c } \dot { \mathsf { J } } + \mathsf { d } )$ for some a, b, c, d (Exercise 15.5.A).

We are looking for some $\mathrm { j } ^ { \prime } ( \lambda )$ such that $\dot { \mathbf { \zeta } } ^ { \prime } ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { j } ^ { \prime } ( 1 / \lambda ) = \cdot \cdot \cdot .$ Hence we want some expression in λ that is invariant under this $S _ { 3 }$ -action. A first possibility would be to take the product of the six numbers:

$$
\lambda \cdot (1 - \lambda) \cdot \frac {1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac {\lambda - 1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac {1}{1 - \lambda} \cdot \frac {\lambda}{\lambda - 1}.
$$
This is silly, as the product is obviously 1.
A better idea is to add them all together:

$$
\lambda + (1 - \lambda) + \frac {1}{\lambda} + \frac {\lambda - 1}{\lambda} + \frac {1}{1 - \lambda} + \frac {\lambda}{\lambda - 1}.
$$
This also doesn’t work, as they add to 3—the six terms come in pairs adding to 1.

(Another reason you might realize this can’t work: If you look at the sum, you will realize that you will get something of the form “degree at most $3 ^ { \prime \prime }$ divided by “degree at most 2.” Then if $\mathfrak { j } ^ { \prime } = \mathfrak { p } ( \lambda ) / \mathfrak { q } ( \lambda )$ , then λ is a root of a cubic over j. But we said that $\lambda$ should satisfy a sextic over $\mathrm { j } ^ { \prime }$ . The only way we avoid a contradiction is if $\mathrm { j ^ { \prime } } \in \mathbb { k } .$ )

But you will undoubtedly have another idea immediately. One good idea is to take the second symmetric function in the six roots. An equivalent one that is easier to work out by hand is to add up the squares of the six terms. Even before doing the calculation, we can see that this will work: it will clearly produce a fraction whose numerator and denominator have degree at most 6, and it
19.9 Curves of Genus 1

is not constant, as when λ is some fixed small number (say 1/2), the sum of squares is some small real number, while when $\lambda$ is a large real number, the sum of squares will have to be some large real number (different from the value when $\lambda = 1 / 2$ ).
When you add up the squares by hand (which is not hard), you will get

$$
j ^ {\prime} = \frac {2 \lambda^ {6} - 6 \lambda^ {5} + 9 \lambda^ {4} - 8 \lambda^ {3} + 9 \lambda^ {2} - 6 \lambda + 2}{\lambda^ {2} (\lambda - 1) ^ {2}}.
$$
Indeed $\mathbf { k } ( \mathrm { j } ) \cong \mathbf { k } ( \mathrm { j } ^ { \prime } )$ : you can check (again by hand) that

$$
2 j / 2 ^ {8} = \frac {2 \lambda^ {6} - 6 \lambda^ {5} + 1 2 \lambda^ {4} - 1 4 \lambda^ {3} + 1 2 \lambda^ {2} - 6 \lambda + 2}{\lambda^ {2} (\lambda - 1) ^ {2}}.
$$
Thus $2 \mathrm { j } / 2 ^ { 8 } - \mathrm { j } ^ { \prime } = 3$
19.9.8. Line bundles of degree $3 ( \mathrm { P i c } ^ { 3 } ( \mathsf { E } ) )$ .

In the discussion of degree 2 line bundles in $\ S 1 9 . 9 . 5 ,$ we assumed char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ and $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } , } \end{array}$ in order to invoke the Riemann–Hurwitz formula. In this section, we will start with no assumptions, and add them as we need them. In this way, you will see what partial results hold with weaker assumptions.

By Riemann–Roch (19.2.5.1), $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf { E } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { E } } ( 3 \mathsf { p } ) ) = \mathsf { d e g } ( 3 \mathsf { p } ) - \mathsf { g } + 1 = 3$ . As deg $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathtt { E } } ( 3 \mathfrak { p } ) > 2 \mathfrak { g } ,$ , this gives a closed embedding (Remark 19.2.11 and Exercise 19.2.E). Thus we have a closed embedding $\mathbb { E } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ as a cubic curve. Moreover, there is a line in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { 2 }$ meeting E at point p with multiplicity 3, corresponding to the section of $\mathcal { O } ( 3 \mathfrak { p } )$ vanishing precisely at p with multiplicity 3. (A line in the plane meeting a smooth curve with multiplicity at least 2 is a tangent line; see Definition 13.3.3. A line in the plane meeting a smooth curve with multiplicity at least 3 is said to be a flex line, and that point is a flex point or flex of the curve.) (See Figure 19.5.)

Choose projective coordinates on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ so that p maps to $[ 0 , 1 , 0 ] ,$ and the flex line is the line at infinity $z = 0$ . Then the cubic is of the following form:

The coefficient of $y ^ { 2 } z$ is not 0 either (or else this cubic is singular at $\scriptstyle x = z = 0$ ). This is called the Weierstrass normal form of the curve.

19.9.9. From the Weierstrass cubic to the double cover. We see the hyperelliptic (double cover) description of the curve by setting $z = 1 ,$ or, more precisely, by working in the distinguished open set $z \neq 0$ and using inhomogeneous coordinates. Here is the geometric explanation of why the double cover description is visible in the cubic description. Project the cubic from ${ \mathfrak { p } } = [ 0 , 1 , 0 ]$ (see Figure 19.6). This is a map $\mathsf { E } \setminus \mathsf { p }  \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (given by $[ x , y , z ] \to [ x , z ] ) ,$ and is basically the vertical projection of the cubic to the $x$ -axis. (Figure 19.6 may help you visualize this.) By the Curve-to-Projective Extension Theorem 15.3.1, the morphism $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { 1 }$ extends over p. If $\mathcal { L } : = \mathcal { O } ( 3 \mathfrak { p } )$ is the line bundle giving the morphism $\mathbb { E } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ (via Theorem 15.2.2 describing maps to projective space in terms of line bundles), then the two sections $x$ and $z$ giving the map to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ vanish at p to order 1 and 3, respectively. To “resolve” the rational map into an honest morphism, we interpret $x$ and $z$ as sections of $\mathcal { L } ( - \mathfrak { p } ) = \mathcal { O } ( 2 \mathfrak { p } )$ , and now they

text: generate a base-point-free linear series, and thus a morphism to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . (You may be able to interpret this as implementing the proof of Theorem 15.2.2 in a different language.) A similar idea, applied to quartics rather than cubics, was used in Exercise 19.7.C.
As a consequence, with a little sweat, we can compute the j-invariant as a function of a and b:

19.9.D. EXERCISE. Show that the flexes of the cubic are the 3-torsion points in the group E. (“Flex” was defined in §19.9.8: it is a point where the tangent line meets the curve with multiplicity at least 3 at that point. In fact, if k is algebraically closed and char $\mathtt { k } \neq 3$ , there are nine of them. This won’t be surprising if you are familiar with the complex story, $\mathsf { E } \cong \mathbb { C } / \Lambda .$ )
19.9.10. The group law, geometrically.

The group law has a beautiful classical description in terms of the Weierstrass form. Consider Figure 19.7. In the Weierstrass coordinates, the origin p is the only point of E meeting the line at infinity $( z = 0 )$ ; in fact, the line at infinity corresponds to the tautological section of $\mathcal { O } ( 3 \mathfrak { p } )$ . If a line meets E at three points $\mathsf { p } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { p } _ { 2 } , \mathsf { p } _ { 3 }$ , then

$$
\mathcal {O} \left(\mathrm {p} _ {1} + \mathrm {p} _ {2} + \mathrm {p} _ {3}\right) \cong \mathcal {O} (3 \mathrm {p}),
$$

image_caption: Figure 19.7 The group law on the elliptic curve, geometrically.

text: from which (in the group law) ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } + { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 2 } + { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 3 } = 0 .$

Hence to find the inverse of a point s, we consider the intersection of E with the line sp; −s is the third point of intersection. To find the sum of two points q and r, we consider the intersection of E with the line qr, and call the third point s. We then compute −s by connecting s to p, obtaining $\mathbf { q } + \mathbf { r }$ .

We could give this description of a group law on a cubic curve in Weierstrass normal form to anyone familiar with the notion of projective space, and the notion of a group, but we would then have to prove that the construction we are giving indeed defines a group. In particular, we would have to prove associativity, which is not a priori clear. But in this case we have already established that the degree 1 points form a group, by giving a bijection to ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { 0 } \operatorname { E } ,$ and we are merely interpreting the group law on ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { 0 } \operatorname { E }$ .
19.9 Curves of Genus 1

Note that this description works even in characteristic 2 and 3; we do not need the cubic to be in Weierstrass normal form, and we need only that $\mathcal { O } ( 3 \mathfrak { p } )$ gives a closed embedding into $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ .

19.9.11. Degree 4 line bundles. You have probably forgotten that we began by studying line bundles degree by degree. The story doesn’t stop in degree 3. In the same way that we showed that a canonically embedded nonhyperelliptic curve of genus 4 is the complete intersection in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathtt { k } } ^ { 3 }$ of a quadric surface and a cubic surface (§19.8), we can show the following.

19.9.E. EXERCISE. Show that the complete linear series for $\mathcal { O } ( 4 \mathfrak { p } )$ embeds E in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ as the complete intersection of two quadric surfaces. Hint: Show the image of E is contained in at least two linearly independent quadric surfaces. Show that neither can be reducible, so they share no components. Use Bézout’s Theorem (Exercise 18.6.J) and Exercise 18.6.E, as in §19.8.1.

The beautiful structure doesn’t stop with degree 4, but it gets more complicated. For example, the degree 5 embedding is not a complete intersection (of hypersurfaces), but is the complete intersection of $\mathsf { G } ( 2 , 5 )$ under its Plücker embedding with five hyperplanes (or, perhaps better, a codimension 5 linear space). In seemingly different terminology, its equations are $4 \times 4$ Pfaffians of a general $5 \times 5$ skew-symmetric matrix of linear forms, although I won’t say what this means. (This construction may remind you of a similar construction for genus 6 curves in §19.8.3. This is no coincidence.)
19.10 Elliptic Curves Are Group Varieties
Ever since 1949, I considered the construction of an algebraic theory of the Picard variety as the task of greatest urgency in abstract algebraic geometry.
— A. Weil [Weil, p. 537]

We initially described the group law on the degree 1 points of an algebraic curve in a rather abstract way. From that definition, it was not clear that over $\mathbb { C }$ the group operations (addition, inverse) are continuous. But the explicit description in terms of the Weierstrass cubic makes this clear. In fact, we can observe even more: addition and inverse are algebraic in general. Better yet, elliptic curves are group varieties. (Thus they are abelian varieties; see Definition 11.4.11.)

19.10.1. (This is a clue that ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { E } )$ really wants to be a scheme, and not just a group. Once the notion of “moduli space of line bundles on a variety” is made precise, this can be shown; see, for example, [FGIKNV, Part 5].)
We begin with the “group inverse” morphism, as a warm-up.

19.10.2. Proposition — If char $k \neq 2 , 3 .$ , there is a morphism of k-varieties $\mathbb { E } \to \mathbb { E }$ sending a (degree 1) point to its inverse, and this construction behaves well under field extension of k.

In other words, the “inverse map” in the group law actually arises from a morphism of schemes—it isn’t just a set map. (You are welcome to think through the two remaining characteristics, and to see that essentially the same proof applies. But the proof of Theorem 19.10.4 will give you a better sense of how to proceed.)
Proof. Consider the map (the hyperelliptic involution) $y \mapsto - y$ of the Weierstrass normal form.

The algebraic description of addition would be a big mess if we were to write it down. We will be able to show algebraicity by a trick—not by writing it down explicitly, but by thinking through how we could write it down explicitly. The main part of the trick is the following proposition. We give it in some generality just because it can be useful, but you may prefer to assume that $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ and C is a regular cubic. If C is a curve, let ${ \mathrm { C } } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } }$ be the regular points of C, or, equivalently, the normal points (Theorem 13.5.8), which form an open subset of C (Exercise 10.7.O).
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

19.10.3. Proposition — Suppose ${ \sf C } \subset \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ is a geometrically integral cubic curve (so, in particular, C contains no lines). There is a unique morphism t : $C ^ { r e g } \times C ^ { r e g }  C ^ { r e g }$ such that

(a) if p and q are distinct regular k-valued points of C, then t(p, q) is obtained by intersecting the line pq with C, and taking the third “residual” point of intersection with C. More precisely, pq will meet C at three points with multiplicity (Exercise 9.3.D), including p and q; t(p, q) is the third point; and (b) this property remains true after extension to $\overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ .

Furthermore, if p is a k-valued point of $C ^ { r e g }$ , then $\cdot ( \boldsymbol { \mathsf { p } } , \boldsymbol { \mathsf { p } } )$ is where the tangent line ℓ to C at p meets C again. More precisely, ℓ will meet C at three points with multiplicity, which includes p with multiplicity 2; t(p, p) is the third point.

We will need property (b) because C may have few enough k-valued points (perhaps none!) that the morphism t cannot be determined by its behavior on them. In the course of the proof, we will see that (b) can be extended to “this property remains true after any field extension of k.”

Proof. We first show (in this paragraph) that if p and q are distinct regular points, then the third point r of intersection of pq with C is also regular. If ${ \mathfrak { r } } = { \mathfrak { p } }$ or $\mathrm { \bf r = q } .$ , we are done. Otherwise, the cubic obtained by restricting C to pq has three distinct (hence reduced, i.e., multiplicity 1) roots, p, q, and r. Thus C ∩ pq is regular at r, so r is a regular point of C by the slicing criterion for regularity, Exercise 13.2.C.

We now assume that $\operatorname { k } = { \overline { { \operatorname { k } } } } ,$ and leave the general case to the end. Fix p, q, and ${ \mathfrak { r } } ,$ where ${ \mathfrak { p } } \neq { \mathfrak { q } } ,$ and r is the “third” point of intersection of pq with C. We will describe a morphism $\mathrm { t _ { p , q } }$ in an open neighborhood of $( \mathsf { p } , \mathsf { q } ) \in C ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \times C ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } ,$ . By Exercise 11.3.B, showing that morphisms of varieties over $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ are determined by their behavior on closed $\overbar { \mathbf { k } }$ -valued) points, these morphisms glue together (uniquely) to give a morphism $\mathrm { t , }$ completing the proof in the case $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ .

In these coordinates, the curve C is cut out by some cubic, which we also sloppily denote by ${ \dot { \mathbf { \eta } } } : { \mathsf { C } } ( { \mathsf { x } } _ { 1 } , { \mathsf { x } } _ { 2 } ) = { \mathsf { 0 } }$ .

To do this explicitly requires ugly high school algebra, but because we know how it looks, we will be able to avoid dealing with any details!

This is a cubic in $x _ { 1 } ,$ say,

The coefficients of $\gamma$ are rational functions of $\mathsf { P } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { P } _ { 2 } , \mathsf { Q } _ { 1 } ,$ and $Q _ { 2 }$ . The cubic $\gamma$ has three roots (with multiplicity) so long as $\mathtt { A } \neq 0$ , which is a Zariski-open condition on m and $^ { \mathrm { b , } }$ and hence a Zariski-open condition on $\mathsf { P } _ { 1 }$ , P2, Q1, Q2. Since the line $\overline { { \mathsf { P Q } } }$ contains p q, and r, the third root of $\gamma ( \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } ) = 0$ is ${ \sf R } _ { 1 }$ . The sum of the roots of $\gamma ( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } ) = 0$ is $- \mathtt { B } / \mathtt { A }$ (by Viète’s formula), so the third root of $\gamma$ is $\mathsf { R } _ { 1 } : = - \mathsf { B } / \mathsf { A } - \mathsf { P } _ { 1 } - \mathsf { Q } _ { 1 }$ . Thus if we take $\mathsf { R } _ { 2 } = \mathsf { m } \mathsf { R } _ { 1 } + \mathsf { b } ,$ we have found the third point of intersection of $\overline { { \mathsf { P Q } } }$ with C (which happily lies in $\mathrm { u } _ { 0 }$ ). We have thus described a morphism from the open subset of $\begin{array} { r } { \big ( \mathbf { C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } \big ) \times \big ( \mathbf { C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } \big ) , } \end{array}$ containing (p, q), that does what we want. (Precisely, the open subset is defined by $\mathtt { A } \neq 0 ,$ , which can be explicitly unwound.) 19.10 Elliptic Curves Are Group Varieties

We extend this to Proposition 19.10.3 for every field k except $\mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ . Suppose $\mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { [ \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { x } _ { 2 } ] } =$ S $\mathrm { p e c k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , \mathbf { x } _ { 2 } ]$ is any affine open subset of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } ,$ along with choice of coordinates. (The awkward superscript $^ { \prime \prime } [ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ] ^ { \prime \prime }$ is there to emphasize that the particular coordinates are used in the construction.) Then the construction above gives a morphism defined over k from an open subset of $( \mathsf { C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { [ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ] } ) \times ( \mathsf { C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { [ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ] } )$ (note that all of the hypothetical algebra was done over k) that sends P and Q to the third point of intersection of $\overline { { \mathsf { P Q } } }$ with C. Note that this construction commutes with any field extension, as the construction is insensitive to the field we are working over. Thus after base change to the algebraic closure, the map also has the property that it takes as input two points, and spits out the third point of intersection of the line with the cubic. Furthermore, all of these maps (as $\mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { [ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ] }$ varies over all complements $\mathrm { { U } } _ { 0 }$ of lines “with k-coefficients,” and choices of coordinates on $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ ) can be glued together: they agree on their pairwise overlaps (as after base change to $\bar { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } }$ they are the same, by our previous discussion, and two maps that are the same after base change to $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ were the same to begin with by Exercise 10.2.J), and this is what is required to glue them together (Exercise 7.2.A).

We can geometrically interpret the open subset $( \mathsf { C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { [ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ] } ) \times ( \mathsf { C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \cap \mathsf { U } _ { 0 } ^ { [ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ] } )$ by examining the construction: it is defined in the locus $\{ \mathsf { P } = ( \mathsf { P } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { P } _ { 2 } ) , \mathsf { Q } = ( \mathsf { Q } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { Q } _ { 2 } ) \} ,$ where (i) $\mathsf { P 1 } \neq \mathsf { Q 1 } ,$ , and (ii) the third point of intersection R of $\overline { { \mathsf { P Q } } }$ with C also lies in $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ .
So which points (P, Q) of ${ \cal C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } } \times { \cal C } ^ { \mathrm { r e g } }$ are missed? Most important: we have omitted the case where $\mathsf { P } = \mathsf { Q }$ .

19.10.A. EXERCISE. Use the algebra of Extended Example 15.3.4 to deal with the case where $\mathsf { P } = \mathsf { Q }$ . (This will take some time and care. Ideally you should avoid doing any messy algebra.)

We now deal with the remaining cases where $\mathsf { P } \neq \mathsf { Q }$ . Condition (i) isn’t important; if (P, Q) satisfies (ii) but not (i), we can swap the roles of $_ { x _ { 1 } }$ and $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 2 }$ , and (P, Q) will then satisfy (i). The only way (P, Q) cannot be covered by one of these open sets is if there is no $\mathrm { U } _ { 0 }$ (a complement of a line defined over k) that includes P, Q, and R.

19.10.B. EXERCISE. Use $| \mathbf k | > 2$ to show that there is a linear form on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ with coefficients in k that misses P, Q, and R. (This is sadly not true if $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ —do you see why?)
19.10.C. EXERCISE. Prove the last statement of Proposition 19.10.3.

19.10.D.** UNIMPORTANT EXERCISE. Complete the proof by dealing with the case $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ . Hint: First produce the morphism t over $\mathbb { F } _ { 4 }$ . The goal is then to show that this t is really “defined over” $\mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ (“descends to” $\mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ ). The morphism t is initially described locally by considering the complement of a line defined over $\mathbb { F } _ { 4 }$ (and then letting the line vary). Instead, look at the map by looking at the complement of a line and its “conjugate.” The complement of the line and its conjugate is an affine $\mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ -variety. The partially defined map t on this affine variety is a priori defined over $\mathbb { F } _ { 4 }$ , and is preserved by conjugation. Show that this partially defined map is “really” defined over $\mathbb { F } _ { 2 }$ . (If you figure out what all of this means, you will have an important initial insight into the theory of “descent.”) □
We can now use this to define the group variety structure on E.

19.10.4. Theorem — Suppose (E, p) is an elliptic curve (a regular genus 1 curve over ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ with a kvalued point p). Take the Weierstrass embedding of E in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } ,$ via the complete linear series $| \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { E } } ( 3 \mathsf { p } ) |$ |. Define the k-morphism e : Spec $\boldsymbol k \to \boldsymbol \mathsf E$ by sending Spec k to p. Define the k-morphism i : $\mathbb { E } \to \mathbb { E }$ via ${ \mathfrak { q } } \mapsto { \mathfrak { t } } ( { \mathfrak { p } } , { \mathfrak { q } } )$ , or, more precisely, as the composition

$$
E \xrightarrow {(i d , e)} E \times E \xrightarrow {t} E.
$$

Define the k-morphism m : $\mathsf { E } \times \mathsf { E } \to \mathsf { E }$ via $( \mathsf { q } , \mathsf { r } ) \mapsto \mathsf { t } ( \mathsf { p } , \mathsf { t } ( \mathsf { q } , \mathsf { r } ) )$ . Then $( \mathsf { E } , e , \mathsf { i } , \mathsf { m } )$ is a group variety over k.
By the construction of $\mathrm { t , }$ all of these morphisms “commute with arbitrary base extension.”
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

Proof. We need to check that various pairs of morphisms described in axioms (i)–(iii) of $\ S 7 . 6 . 4$ are equal. For example, in axiom (iii), we need to show that ${ \mathfrak { m } } \circ ( { \mathfrak { i } } , { \mathfrak { i } } { \mathrm { d } } ) = { \mathfrak { m } } \circ ( { \mathrm { i d } } , { \mathrm { i } } )$ ; all of the axioms are clearly of this sort.

Assume first that $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ . Then each of these pairs of morphisms agree as maps of $\bar { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } }$ -points: Pic E is a group, and under the bijection between Pic E and E of Proposition 19.9.3, the group operations translate into the maps described in the statement of Theorem 19.10.4 by the discussion of §19.9.10.

But morphisms of $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ -varieties are determined by their maps on the level of $\bar { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } }$ -points (Exercise 11.3.B), so each of these pairs of morphisms are the same.

For general ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ we note that, from the $\bar { \mathsf k }$ case, these morphisms agree after base change to the algebraic closure. Then by Exercise 10.2.J, they must agree to begin with. □

list_items: 19.10.5. Features of this construction. The most common derivation of the properties of an elliptic curve is to describe the elliptic curve as a cubic, and then to describe addition using the explicit construction with lines. Then one has to work to prove that the multiplication described is associative.

Instead, we started with something that was patently a group (the degree 0 line bundles). We interpreted the maps used in the definition of the group (addition and inverse) geometrically using our cubic interpretation of elliptic curves. This allowed us to see that these maps were algebraic.
As a bonus, we see that, in some vague sense, the Picard group of an elliptic curve wants to be an algebraic variety.
19.10.E. EXERCISE. Suppose p and q are k-points of a genus 1 curve E. Show that there is an automorphism of E sending p to q.

19.10.F. EXERCISE. Suppose (E, p) is an elliptic curve over an algebraically closed field k of characteristic not 2 or 3. Show that the automorphism group of $\textstyle ( \mathsf { E } , \mathsf { p } )$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb { Z } / 2 , \mathbb { Z } / 4 ,$ , or $\mathbb { Z } / 6$ . (An automorphism of an elliptic curve $\textstyle ( \mathsf { E } , \mathsf { p } )$ over $\mathrm { k } = \bar { \mathrm { k } }$ is an automorphism of E fixing p scheme-theoretically, or, equivalently, fixing the $\mathrm { k }$ -valued point p by Exercise 11.3.B.) Hint: Reduce to the question of automorphisms of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ fixing a point $\infty$ and a set of distinct three points $\{ \mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \mathfrak { p } _ { 2 } , \mathfrak { p } _ { 3 } \} \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \setminus \{ \infty \}$ . (The algebraic closure of k is not essential, so feel free to remove this hypothesis, using Exercise 10.2.J. What happens if the characteristic is 3?)

19.10.G. EXERCISE. Explain why genus 1 curves over an algebraically closed field of characteristic not 2 are classified (up to isomorphism) by j-invariant. (Caution: Note that the problem says “genus $1 , \prime \prime$ not “elliptic.”)
19.10.H. EXERCISE. Give (with proof) two elliptic curves over $\mathbb { Q }$ with the same j-invariant that are not isomorphic. (Hint: §19.5.3.)

19.10.6. Vague question. What are the possible automorphism groups of a genus 1 curve over an algebraically closed k of characteristic not 2? You should be able to convince yourself that the group has “dimension 1.”

Consider the arithmetic genus 1 curve ${ \sf C } \subset \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ given by $y ^ { 2 } z = x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } z ,$ with the point ${ \mathfrak { p } } = [ 0 , 1 , 0 ]$ . Emulate the above argument to show that $C \setminus \{ [ 0 , 0 , 1 ] \}$ is a group variety (where the operation is that given by tensoring line bundles). Show that it is isomorphic to $\mathbb { G } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ (the multiplicative group scheme Spec k[t, $\mathrm { t } ^ { - 1 } ]$ ; see Exercise 7.6.E) with coordinate $\mathfrak { t } = ( \mathfrak { y } - \mathfrak { x } ) / ( \mathfrak { y } + \mathfrak { x } ) .$ , by showing an isomorphism of schemes, and showing that multiplication and inverse in both group varieties agree under this isomorphism.

Consider the arithmetic genus 1 curve ${ \sf C } \subset \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ given by ${ \boldsymbol { \ y } } ^ { 2 } { \boldsymbol { z } } = { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { 3 }$ , with the point ${ \mathfrak { p } } = [ 0 , 1 , 0 ]$ . Emulate the above argument

to show that $C \setminus \{ [ 0 , 0 , 1 ] \}$ is a group variety (where the operation is that given by tensoring line bundles). Show that it is isomorphic to $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ (with additive group structure) with coordinate $\mathfrak { t } = \mathfrak { x } / \mathfrak { y } ,$ , by showing an isomorphism of schemes, and showing that multiplication/addition and inverse in both group varieties agree under this isomorphism.
19.10.7.* Toward Poncelet’s Porism.
These ideas lead to a beautiful classical fact, Poncelet’s Porism. (A porism is an archaic name for a type of mathematical result.)

19.10.8. Poncelet’s Porism — Suppose C and D are two ellipses in $\mathbb { R } ^ { 2 }$ , with C containing D. Choose any point p0 on C. Choose one of the two tangents $\ell _ { 1 }$ from ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 0 }$ to D. Then $\ell _ { 1 }$ meets C at two points in total: p0 and another point p1. From p1, there are two tangents to D, $\ell _ { 1 }$ and another line $\ell _ { 2 }$ . The line $\ell _ { 2 }$ meets C

image_caption: Figure 19.8 Poncelet’s Porism 19.10.8.

text: at some other point ${ \tt p } _ { 2 }$ . Continue this to get a sequence of points p0, p1, p2, . . . . Suppose this sequence starting with ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 0 }$ is periodic, i.e., ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 0 } = { \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ for some n. Then it is periodic with any starting point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in C$ (see Figure 19.8).

It is possible to prove Poncelet’s Porism in an elementary manner, but a proof involving elliptic curves is quite beautiful, and gives connections to more sophisticated ideas. Rather than proving Poncelet’s Porism, we discuss some related facts.
