19.11 Counterexamples and Pathologies Using Elliptic Curves

We now give some fun counterexamples using our understanding of elliptic curves. The extra juice elliptic curves give us comes from the fact that elliptic curves are the simplest varieties with “continuous Picard groups.”
19.11.1. A scheme that is factorial, yet no affine open neighborhood of any point is Spec of a unique factorization domain.

Suppose E is an elliptic curve over $\mathbb { C }$ (or some other uncountable algebraically closed field). Consider $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { E }$ . The local ring $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { E } , \mathsf { p } }$ is a discrete valuation ring and hence a unique factorization domain (Theorem 13.5.8). Then any affine open neighborhood of E is of the form $\mathsf { E } \setminus \mathsf { q } _ { 1 } \setminus \cdots \setminus \mathsf { q } _ { \mathsf { n } }$ for some points ${ \mathfrak { q } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \mathfrak { q } } _ { n } .$ I claim that its Picard group is nontrivial. Recall the exact sequence:

$$
\mathbb {Z} ^ {\oplus n} \xrightarrow {(a _ {1} , \dots , a _ {n}) \mapsto a _ {1} q _ {1} + \dots + a _ {n} q _ {n}} \operatorname {P i c} E \longrightarrow \operatorname {P i c} (E \backslash q _ {1} \backslash \dots \backslash q _ {n}) \longrightarrow 0.
$$

But the group on the left is countable, and the group in the middle is uncountable, so the group on the right, $\operatorname { P i c } ( \mathbb { E } \setminus \mathfrak { q } _ { 1 } \setminus \cdots \setminus \mathfrak { q } _ { n } ) ,$ , is nonzero. We have shown that every nonempty open subset of E has nonzero line bundles, as promised in Remark 14.2.

As $\mathsf { E } \setminus \mathsf { q } _ { 1 } \setminus \cdots \setminus \mathsf { q } _ { \mathsf { n } }$ is affine, by Exercise 15.5.P the corresponding ring is not a unique factorization domain. To summarize: complex elliptic curves are factorial, but no affine open subset has a ring that has unique factorization, as promised in §5.4.4.

19.11.A. EXERCISE. The above argument shows that over an uncountable field, Pic E is not a finitely generated group. Show that even over the countable field ${ \overline { { \mathbb { Q } } } } ,$ , Pic E is not a finitely generated group, as follows. If the elliptic curve E is generated by ${ \mathfrak { q } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \mathfrak { q } } _ { n } ,$ then there is a finite extension of fields K of $\mathbb { Q }$ over which all ${ \bf q } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are defined—the compositum of the residue fields of the ${ \bf q } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . (Careful—do you see what it means for the ${ \bf q } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ to be “defined” over a smaller field than $\overline { { \mathbb { Q } } } ?$ ) Show that any point in the subgroup of E generated by the ${ \bf q } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ must also be defined over K. Show that E has a point not defined over K. Use this to show that Pic E is not finitely generated. (The same argument works with $\mathbb { Q }$ replaced by $\overline { { \mathbb { F } } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ .)
19.11.2. Remark. In contrast to the above discussion, over $\mathbb { Q } ,$ the Mordell–Weil Theorem states that Pic E is finitely generated (see Aside 19.9.4).
19.11.3.** A complex surface with infinitely many algebraic structures (for those with sufficient complex geometric background).

As remarked in ${ \ S } 7 . 3 . 1 6 ,$ a complex manifold may have many algebraic structures. The following example of M. Kim gives an example with infinitely many algebraic structures. Suppose E is an elliptic curve over $\mathbb { C }$ with origin p, and let $\mathcal { L }$ be a nontrivial line bundle on $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p }$ . Then $\mathcal { L }$ is analytically trivial because $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p }$ is a Stein space, so the analytification of the total space is independent of the choice of $\mathcal { L }$ . However, you know enough to show (with work) that there are infinitely
19.11 Counterexamples and Pathologies Using Elliptic Curves

many pairwise (algebraically) nonisomorphic ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { \ell }$ , and also that their total spaces are likewise pairwise (algebraically) nonisomorphic. This gives a complex surface with infinitely many algebraic structures (indeed a continuum of them). See [MO68421] for more details.
19.11.4. Counterexamples using a non-torsion point.
We next give a number of counterexamples using the existence of a non-torsion point of a complex elliptic curve. We first show the existence of such a point.

19.11.5. We have a “multiplication by ${ \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ map [n] : $\mathsf { E } \to \mathsf { E } ,$ which sends p to np. If $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ , the map [n] has degree 0 (even though [n] isn’t dominant, degree is still defined; see Definition 12.2.2). If $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ , the map [n] has degree 1. Given the complex picture of a torus, you might not be surprised that the degree of [n] is ${ \mathfrak { n } } ^ { 2 }$ . If ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 2$ , we have almost shown that it has degree 4, as we have checked that there are precisely four points q such that $\boldsymbol { 2 } \boldsymbol { \mathrm { p } } = \boldsymbol { 2 } \boldsymbol { \mathrm { q } }$ (Exercise 19.9.A). All that really shows (using Exercise 16.3.D(b)) is that the degree is at least 4. (We could check by hand that the degree is 4 is we really wanted to.)

19.11.6. Proposition — Suppose E is an elliptic curve over a field k of characteristic not 2. For each $\mathfrak { n } > 0$ , the “multiplication by $\mathrm { ~ n ~ } ^ { \prime \prime }$ morphism [n] has positive degree, so there are only a finite number of n-torsion points.
Proof. We may assume $\mathsf { k } = \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } ,$ as the degree of a map of curves is independent of field extension.
We prove the result by induction; it is true for $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ and ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 2$ .

If n is odd $( 2 k + 1 ,$ say), then assume otherwise that $\mathbf { n } \mathbf { q } = 0$ for all closed points q. Let r be a nontrivial 2-torsion point, so $2 \boldsymbol { \mathrm { r } } = 0$ . But ${ \mathfrak { n r } } = 0$ as well, so ${ \boldsymbol { \ r } } = ( { \boldsymbol { \ n } } - 2 { \boldsymbol { \mathbf { k } } } ) { \boldsymbol { \mathbf { r } } } = 0 ,$ contradicting $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { r } } \neq \boldsymbol { 0 }$ .

If n is even, then $[ { \mathfrak { n } } ] = [ 2 ] \circ [ { \mathfrak { n } } / 2 ]$ (degree is multiplicative under composition of rational maps, $\ S 1 2 . 2 . 2 )$ , and by our inductive hypothesis both [2] and [n/2] have positive degree. □

In particular, the total number of torsion points on E is countable. If k is an uncountable field, then E has an uncountable number of closed points (consider an open subset of the curve as $\mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } =$ $\mathbf { x } ^ { 3 } + \mathbf { a x } + \mathbf { b }$ ; there are uncountably many choices for $x ,$ and each of them has one or two choices for $_ { 9 }$ ). Thus we have the following.
19.11.7. Corollary — If E is a curve over an uncountable algebraically closed field of characteristic not 2 (e.g., C), then E has a non-torsion point.

Proof. For each n, there are only finitely many n-torsion points. Thus there are (at most) countably many torsion points. The curve E has uncountably many closed points. (One argument for this: Take a double cover π: $\colon \mathsf { E } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . Then $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ has uncountably many closed points, and $\pi$ is surjective on closed points.) □

19.11.8. Remark. This argument clearly breaks down over countable fields. In fact, over $\overline { { \mathbb { F } } } _ { \mathfrak { p } } ,$ all points of an elliptic curve E are torsion. (Any given point is defined over some finite field $\mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathtt { r } } }$ . The points defined over $\mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathtt { r } } }$ form a subgroup of E, using the explicit geometric construction of the group law, and there are finite number of points over $\mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathtt { r } } }$ —certainly no more than the number of $\mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathtt { r } } }$ -points of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ .) But over ${ \overline { { \mathbb { Q } } } } ,$ there are elliptic curves with non-torsion points. Even better, there are examples over $\mathbb { Q }$ : [2, 1, 8] is a $\mathbb { Q }$ -point of the elliptic curve $y ^ { 2 } z = x ^ { 3 } + 4 x z ^ { 2 } - z ^ { 3 }$ that is not torsion. The proof would carry us too far afield, but one method is to use the Nagell–Lutz Theorem (see, for example, [Si1, Cor. 7.2]).
We now use the existence of a non-torsion point to create some interesting pathologies.
19.11.9. A map of projective varieties not arising from a map of graded rings, even after regrading.

19.11.B. EXERCISE. Suppose $\mathbb { E } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 2 }$ is a smooth complex plane cubic (hence genus 1), yielding a graded ring S . Let t : $\mathsf E \longrightarrow \mathsf E$ be translation by a non-torsion point. Show that t does not correspond to a map of graded rings $S _ { \bullet } \to S _ { \bullet } ,$ , even after regrading (cf. Remark 15.2.9). (If you wish, you can
Chapter 19 Application: Curves

show the following. If u: $\mathbb { E } \to \mathbb { E }$ is a translation, show that u corresponds to some map of regraded rings $S _ { \mathrm { n } \bullet }  S _ { \mathrm { n } \bullet }$ if and only if $\mathfrak { u }$ is translation by a torsion point.)
19.11.10. An affine open subset of an affine scheme that is not a distinguished open set.

We can use this to construct an example of an affine scheme X and an affine open subset Y that is not distinguished in X. Let ${ \sf X } = { \sf E } - { \sf p } ,$ which is affine (see Exercise $1 9 . 2 . \mathrm { C } ,$ or, better, note that the linear series $\mathcal { O } ( 3 \mathfrak { p } )$ sends E to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ in such a way that the “line at infinity” meets E only at p; then $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p }$ has a closed embedding into the affine scheme $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ ).

Let q be another point on E so that ${ \mathfrak { q } } - { \mathfrak { p } }$ is non-torsion. Then $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p } - \mathsf { q }$ is affine (Exercise 19.2.C). Assume that it is distinguished. Then there is a function f on E − p that vanishes on q (to some positive order d). Thus f is a rational function on E that vanishes at q to order $\mathrm { d } ,$ and (as the total number of zeros minus poles of f is 0) has a pole at p of order d. But then $\mathbf { d } ( \mathfrak { p } - \mathfrak { q } ) = 0$ in ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { 0 } \operatorname { E } ,$ contradicting our assumption that $\mathsf { p } - \mathsf { q }$ is non-torsion.

In particular, $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p }$ is an affine scheme, and q is locally cut out by one equation, but it is not globally cut out even set-theoretically by one equation. This was promised in Exercise 8.3.E and $\ S 9 . 5 . 1$ .
19.11.11. A proper (nonprojective) surface with no nontrivial line bundles.

We next use a non-torsion point p on an elliptic curve E to construct a proper nonprojective surface with no nontrivial line bundles. Let $\mathsf { X } _ { 1 }$ be $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ , and $Z _ { 1 }$ a smooth cubic in $\Chi _ { 1 } ,$ , identified with E. Let $Z _ { 2 } \subset X _ { 2 }$ be exactly the same. Glue $X _ { 1 }$ to $X _ { 2 }$ along the isomorphism $Z _ { 1 } \longleftrightarrow Z _ { 2 }$ given not by the identity but by translation by p. (This gluing construction was described in §15.2.12.) Call the result X. By Exercise 15.2.M (and the sentence thereafter), X is proper.

19.11.C. EXERCISE. Show that every line bundle on X is trivial. Hint: Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on X. Then $\mathcal { L } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { i } } }$ is $\mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { d } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ for some ${ \mathrm { d } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (for $i = 1 , 2$ ). The restriction of $\mathcal { L } | _ { X _ { 1 } }$ to $Z _ { 1 }$ must agree with the restriction of $\mathcal { L } | _ { X _ { 2 } }$ to $Z _ { 2 }$ . Use this to show that ${ \bf d } _ { 1 } = { \bf d } _ { 2 } = 0 .$ . Explain why gluing two trivial bundles (on $X _ { 1 }$ and $\mathsf { X } _ { 2 } ,$ respectively) together in this way yields a trivial bundle on $x$ .
19.11.D. EXERCISE. Show that X is not projective.
See Exercise 20.2.G for a somewhat simpler example of a proper nonprojective surface.
19.11.12. A Picard group that has no chance of being a scheme.

We informally observed that the Picard group of an elliptic curve “wants to be” a scheme (see §19.10.5). This is true of projective (and even proper) varieties in general (see [FGIKNV, Ch. 9]). On the other hand, if we work over $\mathbb { C } ,$ the affine scheme $\mathsf { E } - \mathsf { p } - \mathsf { q }$ (in the language of §19.11.10 above) has a Picard group that can be interpreted as $\mathbb { C }$ modulo a lattice modulo a non-torsion point (e.g., $\mathbb { C } / { \langle 1 , \ i , \pi \rangle }$ ). This has no reasonable interpretation as a manifold, let alone a variety. So the fact that the Picard group of proper varieties turns out to be a scheme should be seen as quite remarkable.
19.11.13. A variety whose ring of functions is not finitely generated.

We next show an example of a complex variety whose ring of global sections is not finitely generated. (An example over $\mathbb { Q }$ can be constructed in the same way using the curve of Remark 19.11.8. If you wish, you can show that these examples are even quasiaffine.) This is related to Hilbert’s fourteenth problem (see [Mu5, §3]).

19.11.E. PRELIMINARY EXERCISE. Suppose $x$ is a scheme, and L is the total space of a line bundle corresponding to invertible sheaf ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { i }$ , so $\mathrm { L } = { \mathcal { S } } p e c \oplus _ { \mathfrak { n } \geq 0 } ( { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \vee } ) ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { n } }$ . (This construction first appeared in Definition 17.1.5.) Show that if X is quasicompact, then ${ \sf H } ^ { \circ } ( \mathrm { L } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathrm { L } } ) = \oplus { \sf H } ^ { \circ } ( X , ( \mathcal { L } ^ { \vee } ) ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } } )$ . (Possible hint: Choose a trivializing cover for $\mathcal { L }$ . Rhetorical question: Can you figure out the more general statement if $\mathcal { L }$ is a rank r locally free sheaf?)
19.11 Counterexamples and Pathologies Using Elliptic Curves

Let E be an elliptic curve over some ground field k, $\mathcal { N }$ a degree 0 non-torsion invertible sheaf on E, and $\mathcal { P }$ a positive-degree invertible sheaf on E. Then ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathbb { E } , \mathcal { N } ^ { \mathrm { m } } \otimes \mathcal { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } )$ is nonzero if and only if either (i) $\mathfrak { n } > 0 ,$ or (ii) $\mathfrak { m } = \mathfrak { n } = 0$ (in which case the sections are elements of k).

list_items: 19.11.F. EASY EXERCISE. Show that the ring $\mathsf { R } = \oplus _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } \geq 0 } { \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { E } , \mathcal { N } ^ { \mathsf { m } } \otimes \mathcal { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } } )$ is not finitely generated.

19.11.G. EXERCISE. Let X be the total space of the vector bundle associated to $( { \mathcal { N } } \oplus { \mathcal { P } } ) ^ { \vee }$ over E. Show that the ring of global sections of X is $\mathsf { R } ,$ and hence is not finitely generated. (Hint: Interpret X as a line bundle over a line bundle over E.)
19.11.H. EXERCISE. Show that X (as in the above exercise) is a variety (and, in particular, Noetherian) whose ring of global sections is not Noetherian.

text: Chapter 19 Application: Curves
Chapter 20*
Application: A Glimpse of Intersection Theory
This section is intended to show some of the interesting things we can do with what we have built so far. But it can be skipped on a first reading, by people in a hurry.
The only reason this chapter appears after Chapter 19 is because we will use Exercise 19.2.F in the double-starred proof of the Nakai–Moishezon criterion for ampleness (§20.4.5).
Throughout this chapter, X will be a k-variety. In most applications, X will be projective.
20.1 Intersecting n Line Bundles with an n-Dimensional Variety
The central tool in this chapter is the following.

20.1.1. Definition: Intersection product, or intersection number $\left( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdots { \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right)$ , $\bigl ( \mathscr { L } _ { 1 } \cdots \mathscr { L } _ { n } \cdot \mathscr { F } \bigr ) ,$ $\left( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdots { \mathcal { L } } _ { n } \cdot \ Y \right)$ .

Suppose $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X with proper support (automatic if X is proper) of dimension at most n, and $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ are invertible sheaves on X. Let $( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \cdot { \mathcal { F } } )$ be the signed sum over the $2 ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ subsets of $\{ 1 , \ldots , \mathsf { n } \}$ :

We call this the intersection of $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ with $\mathcal { F }$ . (Never forget that whenever we write $\left( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdots { \mathcal { L } } _ { n } \cdot { \mathcal { F } } \right)$ , we are implicitly assuming that dim Supp $\mathcal { F } \leq \mathfrak { n }$ .)

The case we will find most useful is if $\mathcal { F }$ is the structure sheaf of a closed subscheme Y (of dimension at most n). In this case, we may write it $( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \cdot \Upsilon )$ , and call it the intersection of $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ with Y.

If $\mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { X } ,$ , we may write it $\left( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right)$ , and call it the intersection of $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ (with the “X” left implicit).

If the $\mathcal { L } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are all the same, say ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ one often writes $( \mathcal { L } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } \cdot \mathcal { F } )$ or $( \mathcal { L } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \cdot \mathsf { Y } )$ or $\left( \mathcal { L } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } \right)$ . (Be careful with this confusing notation: ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ does not mean ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ .)
In some circumstances the convention is to omit the parentheses.

We will prove many things about the intersection product in this chapter. One fact will be left until we study flatness (Exercise 24.7.D): that it is “deformation-invariant”—that it is constant in “nice” families.

20.1.A. EXERCISE (REALITY CHECK). Show that if $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ then $( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \cdot { \mathcal { F } } ) = 0$ .
20.1.B. EXERCISE. Show that the intersection product (20.1.1.1) is preserved by field extension of k.
The following exercise suggests that the intersection product might be interesting, as it “interpolates” between two useful notions: the degree of a line bundle on a curve and Bézout’s Theorem.
20.1.C. EXERCISE.

list_items: (a) If X is a projective curve, and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on $\Sigma ,$ show that $( \mathcal { L } \cdot \boldsymbol { X } ) = \mathrm { d e g } _ { \boldsymbol { x } } \mathcal { L }$

(b) Suppose k is an infinite field, $X = \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { N } }$ , and Y is a dimension n subvariety of X. If $\mathsf { H } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { H } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ are generally chosen hypersurfaces of degrees $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { d } _ { n } ,$ respectively (so $\dim ( \mathsf { H } _ { 1 } \cap \cdots \cap \mathsf { H } _ { n } \cap$ $\Upsilon ) = 0$ by Exercise 12.3.D(d)), then by an appropriate version of Bézout’s Theorem (see perhaps

text: Exercise 13.4.C or Exercise 18.6.J),

$$
\deg (H _ {1} \cap \dots \cap H _ {n} \cap Y) = d _ {1} \dots d _ {n} \deg (Y).
$$
Show that

$$
\left(\mathcal {O} _ {X} \left(H _ {1}\right) \dots \mathcal {O} _ {X} \left(H _ {n}\right) \cdot Y\right) = d _ {1} \dots d _ {n} \deg (Y).
$$
We now describe some of the properties of the intersection product. In the course of proving Exercise 20.1.C(b) you may in effect solve the following exercise.

20.1.D. EXERCISE. Suppose D is an effective Cartier divisor on X that restricts to an effective Cartier divisor $\mathsf { D } | _ { \mathsf { Y } } = \mathsf { D } \cap \mathsf { Y }$ on Y (i.e., remains locally not a zerodivisor on Y). Show that

$$
\left(\mathcal {L} _ {1} \dots \mathcal {L} _ {n - 1} \cdot \mathcal {O} (D) \cdot Y\right) = \left(\mathcal {L} _ {1} \dots \mathcal {L} _ {n - 1} \cdot D | _ {Y}\right).
$$
More generally, if D is an effective Cartier divisor on X that does not contain any associated point of $\mathcal { F }$ , show that

$$
\left(\mathcal {L} _ {1} \dots \mathcal {L} _ {n - 1} \cdot \mathcal {O} (D) \cdot \mathcal {F}\right) = \left(\mathcal {L} _ {1} \dots \mathcal {L} _ {n - 1} \cdot \mathcal {F} | _ {D}\right).
$$
(A similar idea came up in the proof that the Hilbert polynomial is actually polynomial; see the discussion around (18.6.3.1).)

20.1.2. Definition. For this reason, if D is an effective Cartier divisor, in the symbol for the intersection product, we often write D instead of $\mathcal { O } ( \mathsf { D } )$ . We interchangeably think of intersecting effective Cartier divisors rather than line bundles. For example, we will discuss the special case of intersection theory on a surface in $\ S 2 0 . 2 ,$ and when we intersect two curves C and D, we will write the intersection as $\left( \boldsymbol { C } \cdot \boldsymbol { \mathrm { D } } \right)$ or even ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } } \cdot { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { D } } }$ .

20.1.3. Proposition — Assume X is projective. For fixed $\mathcal { F }$ , the intersection product $\left( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdots { \mathcal { L } } _ { n } \cdot { \mathcal { F } } \right)$ is a symmetric multilinear function of the $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ .

Proposition 20.1.3 is actually true with “projective” replaced by “proper”; see [Kl1, Prop. 2] or [Ko1, Prop. VI.2.7]. Unlike most extensions to the proper case, this is not just an application of Chow’s Lemma. It involves a different approach, involving a beautiful trick called dévissage.

20.1.4. Important remark: Additive notation for line bundles. There is a standard, useful, but confusing convention suggested by the multilinearity of the intersection product: we sometimes write tensor product of invertible sheaves additively. This is convenient for working with multilinearity. (Some people try to avoid confusion by using divisors rather than line bundles, as we add divisors when we “multiply” the corresponding line bundles. This is psychologically helpful, but may add more confusion, as one then has to worry about the whether and why and how and when line bundles correspond to divisors.) We will use this, for example, in Exercises 20.2.B–20.2.G, §20.2.11, and §20.4.
20.1.5. Proof of Proposition 20.1.3. Symmetry is clear. By Exercise 20.1.B, we may assume that k is infinite (e.g., algebraically closed). We now prove the result by induction on n.
20.1.E. EXERCISE (BASE CASE). Prove the result when $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ . Hint: Exercise 18.4.M.

We now assume the result for smaller values of n. We use a trick. We wish to show that (for arbitrary $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \mathcal { L } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \mathcal { L } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ) .$ ,
is 0.
20.1.F. EXERCISE. Rewrite (20.1.5.1) as

(There are now $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ line bundles appearing in the product, but this does not contradict the definition of the intersection product, as dim Supp $\mathcal { F } \leq \mathsf { n } < \mathsf { n } + 1 .$ )
Chapter $2 0 ^ { * }$ Application: A Glimpse of Intersection Theory

20.1.G. EXERCISE. Use the inductive hypothesis to show that (20.1.5.1) is 0 if ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathrm { n } } \cong \emptyset ( \mathrm { D } )$ for D an effective Cartier divisor missing the associated points of $\mathcal { F }$ .

In particular, if ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ is very ample, then (20.1.5.1) is 0, as Exercise 18.6.A shows that there exists a section of ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ nonvanishing at the associated points of $\mathcal { F }$ . (Caution: Exercise 18.6.A requres the field to be infinite. How would you edit this to work if $\mathrm { k }$ were finite? Hint: Show a variant of Exercise 18.6.A, that there exists a section of ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { N } }$ nonvanishing at the associated points of $\mathcal { F }$ for some $\Nu \gg 0$ . Hint for this: Compare Exercise 12.3.D(c) and Exercise 12.3.D(d).)

By the symmetry of its incarnation as (20.1.5.2), expression (20.1.5.1) vanishes if $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 }$ is very ample. We will use this observation repeatedly in the final equation display of the proof.

By Exercise 16.2.F, any invertible sheaf on $x$ may be written “as the difference of two very amples,” so write $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } = \mathcal { A } \otimes \mathcal { B } ^ { \vee }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } \otimes ( \mathcal { B } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { \vee } .$ , where $\mathcal { A } , \mathcal { B } , \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } ,$ , and ${ \mathcal { B } } ^ { \prime }$ are very ample. Then
as desired.
We have an added bonus arising from the proof.

20.1.H. EXERCISE. Suppose X is projective. Show that if dim Supp $\mathcal { F } < \mathfrak { n } + 1 ,$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \mathcal { L } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ , $\mathcal { L } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ are invertible sheaves on X, then (20.1.5.2) vanishes. In other words, the intersection product of $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ invertible sheaves with a coherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ vanishes if the dim Supp $\mathcal { F } < \mathfrak { n } + \mathbb { 1 }$ . (In fact, the result holds with “projective” replaced by “proper,” as the results it relies on hold in this greater generality.)
20.1.6. Proposition — Suppose X is projective. The intersection product depends only on the numerical equivalence classes of the $\mathcal { L } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ .

(Numerical equivalence was defined in §18.4.12—two line bundles are linearly equivalent if their degrees are the same on any curve.) Again, the result remains true with “projective” replaced by “proper.” But we use the fact that every line bundle is the difference of two very ample line bundles in both the proof of Proposition 20.1.3 and in the proof of Proposition 20.1.6 itself. For the proof of the proposition in the proper case, see [FGIKNV, Prop. B.20].

Proof. Suppose $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 }$ is numerically equivalent to $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ , and $\mathcal { L } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ and $\mathcal { F }$ are arbitrary. We know all the coefficients of this polynomial if X is a curve, by Riemann–Roch (see (18.4.7.1)), or basically by definition. We will know/interpret all the coefficients if X is a regular projective surface and $\mathcal { F }$ is an invertible sheaf when we prove Riemann–Roch for surfaces (Exercise 20.2.B(b)). To understand the general case, we need the theory of Chern classes. The result is the Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch Theorem, which can be further generalized to the celebrated Grothendieck–Riemann–Roch Theorem (see [F2, §15.2]).

Suppose π: $\Chi _ { 1 } \to \mathsf { X } _ { 2 }$ is a proper morphism of proper varieties, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on $X _ { 1 }$ with dim Supp $\mathcal { F } \leq \mathfrak { n }$ (so dim Supp $\pi _ { * } { \mathcal F } \leq \mathsf { n } ,$ using Exercise 12.2.C). $$

$$

This is also called the projection formula (and generalizes, in a nonobvious way, Exercise 20.1.J). Proofs are given in [FGIKNV, B.15] and [Ko1, Prop. VI.2.11]. Because we won’t use this version of the projection formula, we do not give a proof here.

This means that we cannot hope to understand intersection numbers using moving lemmas as in topology—if we have some irreducible curve E with $\mathsf { E } ^ { 2 } < 0$ , we cannot “move” E to something (call it F) “equivalent” to E, to compute E · E as E · F.

20.2.11. The Hodge Index Theorem.
We use what we have learned to prove a celebrated result, the Hodge Index Theorem. The “index” in the name comes from the following notion.

20.2.13. The Hodge Index Theorem — Suppose $x$ is an irreducible smooth projective surface (over a field k) with ${ \mathcal { L } } , { \mathcal { H } } \in \operatorname { P i c } X ,$ with $\mathcal { H } \cdot \mathcal { H } > 0$ and $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { H } = 0$ . Then (a) $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { L } \leq 0$ , and (b) equality holds if and only if $\mathcal { L }$ is numerically trivial.

20.2.14. Proof of the Hodge Index Theorem 20.2.13. Motivated by Exercise 20.2.U, we first prove the result under the additional assumption that $\mathcal { H }$ is ample.

20.2.15. We begin with the following fact. If $\mathcal { H }$ is an ample invertible sheaf, $\mathcal { M }$ is any invertible sheaf, and $\mathcal { M } \cdot \mathcal { H } > \omega _ { \sf X } \cdot \mathcal { H } ,$ then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 2 } ( X , \mathcal { M } ) = 0$ . (Translation: For all invertible sheaves “more positive than $\omega _ { X } , ^ { \prime \prime }$ there is no top cohomology; cf. §19.2.5, the corresponding statement in dimension 1.) 20.2.17. We are now ready to prove the Hodge Index Theorem for ample $\mathcal { H }$ . Assume first that $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { L } > 0$ (and $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { H } = 0$ ). Then $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { H } ^ { \prime } = \mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { L } > 0 ,$ so by §20.2.16, ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes { \mathfrak { n } } }$ is effective, which implies that $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { H } = ( \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { n } } \cdot \mathcal { H } ) / \mathfrak { n } > 0$ (using Exercise 20.1.K again), contradicting our hypothesis.

20.2.18. Assume finally that $\mathcal { L } \cdot \mathcal { H } = 0 ,$ , and (i) ${ \mathcal { L } } \cdot { \mathcal { L } } = 0$ , but that (ii) $\mathcal { L }$ is not numerically trivial. By (ii), we can find an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { Q }$ such that $\boldsymbol { \mathcal { Q } } \cdot \boldsymbol { \mathcal { L } } \neq 0$ . Then we can find an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { R }$ such that $\mathcal { R } \cdot \mathcal { L } \neq 0$ and $\mathcal { R } \cdot \mathcal { H } = 0$ : take

$$

Then take $\mathcal { L } ^ { \prime } = \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { n } } \otimes \mathcal { R } ,$ so $\mathcal { L } ^ { \prime } \cdot \mathcal { H } = 0$ , but because $\mathcal { R } \cdot \mathcal { L } \neq 0$ , we can find some $\mathfrak { n } \in \mathbb { Z }$ so that

Then the argument of §20.2.17 applies, with ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \prime }$ in the place of $\mathcal { L }$ . We have thus proved “the Hodge Index Theorem for ample line bundles.”

20.2.20. Generalizations and variations. The hypotheses can be weakened considerably. We used smoothness only because we need Serre duality, with an invertible sheaf $\omega _ { X }$ . We will see in $\ S 2 9 . 4$ that we need less than smoothness for this. But we can do much better, and a proof where X is merely required to be geometrically irreducible and proper requires only a minor modification of our argument; see [FGIKNV, Thm. B.27].
20.3 The Grothendieck Group of Coherent Sheaves, and an Algebraic Version of Homology

The construction of the intersection product (20.1.1.1) may leave you hungry for something more, especially in light of the cohomological interpretation of $\ S 2 0 . 1 . 9$ . You may want some sort of
452 Chapter $2 0 ^ { * }$ Application: A Glimpse of Intersection Theory
homology-like theory that is a repository for cycles of different dimensions, on which (Chern classes of) line bundles can act. We can actually do this easily, given what we know.

20.3.1. Definition. If X is a k-variety, we define the Grothendieck group of coherent sheaves, which we denote by $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { X } )$ (and which is often denoted by $\mathsf { K } _ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } ) .$ ), as the abelian group generated by symbols of the form $[ \mathcal { F } ] ,$ where $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X, subject to the relations that $[ \mathcal { F } ] = [ \mathcal { F } ^ { \prime } ]$ if $\mathcal { F } \cong \mathcal { F } ^ { \prime }$ and $[ \mathcal { F } ^ { \prime } ] + [ \mathcal { F } ^ { \prime \prime } ] = [ \mathcal { F } ]$ if there is a short exact sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {F} ^ {\prime} \longrightarrow \mathcal {F} \longrightarrow \mathcal {F} ^ {\prime \prime} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

(Caution: The notation $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { X } )$ is now used for two concepts, the Grothendieck group and the function field. The meaning of the symbol should be clear from the context.) By construction, the Grothendieck group is the universal construction of an operator on the category of coherent sheaves that “behaves well in exact sequences.” For example, if $x$ is proper, then:

(i) We have a map χ: $C o h x  \mathbb { Z } ,$ as the Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf is finite by Theorem 18.1.3(i) in the projective case, and, more generally, by Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1 in the proper case. This map descends to $\chi \colon \mathsf { K } ( X ) \to \mathbb { Z }$ by Exercise 18.4.A (which extends without change to the proper case).
(ii) If X is integral, then the rank function
descends to a function rank: $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { X } ) \to \mathbb { Z }$ . (The argument of Exercise 14.3.I applies.)

20.3.2. Definition and nonstandard notation. The Grothendieck group is filtered by dimension: let ${ \mathsf { K } } ( { \mathsf { X } } ) ^ { \leq \mathrm { d } }$ be the subgroup of K(X) generated by coherent sheaves supported in dimension at most d. (This is called the dimensional filtration or coniveau filtration on K(X), although we won’t need these names.) Let $\boldsymbol { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol { X } )$ be the dth graded piece of $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { X } )$ , i.e., $\textstyle \mathsf { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( X ) : = \mathsf { K } ( X ) ^ { \leq \mathrm { d } } / \mathsf { K } ( X ) ^ { \leq \mathrm { d } - 1 }$ .

If $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on $\Sigma ,$ define $\mathcal { L } \mathbf { \cdot } \colon \mathsf { K } ( X ) \to \mathsf { K } ( X )$ by $\mathcal { L } \cdot \left[ \mathcal { F } \right] = \left[ \mathcal { F } \right] - \left[ \mathcal { L } ^ { \vee } \otimes \mathcal { F } \right]$ . (Do you see why this operator is well-defined?)

(Hint: For each fixed $\mathcal { F }$ supported on a subset of dimension at most $\mathrm { d } ,$ write $\mathcal { L }$ as a difference of two very ample invertible sheaves, and choose sections of those two, missing the associated points of $\mathcal { F }$ .)
20.3.3. Remark. The previous exercise holds true without $\mathrm { k }$ being infinite or $x$ being proper; see [Ko1, Prop. VI.2.5].

20.3.4. For the rest of this section, we assume $x$ is projective and $\mathrm { k }$ is infinite. (But in light of Remark 20.3.3, these hypotheses can be removed.) By Exercise 20.3.A, $\mathcal { L }$ descends to a map $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \mathsf { X } ) \to \mathsf { A } _ { \mathrm { d } - 1 } ^ { \prime } ( \mathsf { X } ) ;$ ; we denote this operator by $\displaystyle \mathbf { c } _ { 1 } \left( { \mathcal { L } } \right) \cap _ { }$ . (It is the action of the first Chern class.)

If $\mathcal { F }$ has support of dimension at most ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ are n invertible sheaves, we can now reinterpret the intersection product $\left( \mathscr { L } _ { 1 } \cdot \mathscr { L } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \mathscr { L } _ { n } \cdot \mathscr { F } \right)$ as

$$

$$

where $[ \mathcal { F } ]$ is interpreted as lying in either $\mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { X } )$ or $A _ { \mathrm { n } } ^ { \prime } ( x )$ . You can now go back and read $\ S 2 0 . 1 ,$ , and reprove all the results with this starting point, sometimes obtaining interesting generalizations. We
20.3 The Grothendieck Group of Coherent Sheaves, and an Algebraic Version of Homology

have now promoted the intersection product from an integer to a class in $A _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ( X ) .$ , which may have much more information. (This is true even if X is a curve of positive genus over $\mathbb { C } ,$ , using the theory developed in [F2].)

These $\boldsymbol { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol { X } )$ behave like homology groups in a number of ways. If $\Upsilon \subset X$ is a closed subvariety of pure dimension $\mathrm { d } ,$ we have a class $[ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } : = [ \mathcal { O } _ { \sf Y } ] \in A _ { \sf d } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol { X } )$ . The groups $\boldsymbol { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol { X } )$ have appropriate functorial properties. For example, if $\pi \colon X _ { 1 }  X _ { 2 }$ is a proper morphism (even if the $X _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are not themselves proper), we have a map $\pi _ { * } \colon \mathsf { K } ( X _ { 1 } ) \to \mathsf { K } ( X _ { 2 } )$ given by

(using the long exact sequence for higher pushforwards, Theorem 18.7.1 (c)) that descends to a map $\mathrm { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { X } _ { 1 } ) \longrightarrow \mathrm { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { X } _ { 2 } ) ,$ where the “later terms” on the right side of (20.3.4.1) disappear, so $\pi _ { * } [ \mathcal { F } ] =$ $[ \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } } ]$ . This pushforward interacts well with the first Chern class of line bundles, yielding the projection formula of Remark 20.1.8.

If $\pi$ is instead a flat morphism, soon to be discussed at length in Chapter 24, then $\pi ^ { * }$ is exact (Exercise 24.1.H), so we have a map $\pi ^ { * } \colon { \mathsf { K } } ( { \mathsf { X } } _ { 2 } ) \to { \mathsf { K } } ( { \mathsf { X } } _ { 1 } )$ . If the “relative dimension” of this map is $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { r } }$ (to be properly defined in Definition 24.5.7), this yields a map $\pi ^ { * } : { \cal A } _ { \sf d } ^ { \prime } ( X _ { 2 } ) \longrightarrow { \cal A } _ { \sf d + r } ^ { \prime } ( X _ { 1 } )$ . This interacts well with first Chern classes and proper pushforwards.

If $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \mathbb { C }$ and X is proper, there is a map $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \mathsf { X } ) \to \mathsf { H } _ { 2 \mathrm { d } } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathbb { Q } ) ,$ , which behaves as you might hope (for example, in its interaction with Chern classes of line bundles). If X is not proper (but $\mathrm { k } = \mathbb { C }$ ), then the map is to Borel-Moore homology rather than usual homology.

Our groups $\lambda _ { \mathrm { d } } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol { X } )$ are a good approximation of the theory of Chow groups $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathrm { d } } ( \boldsymbol { \mathsf { X } } ) ,$ , as developed in [F2]. In fact, there is a surjective map
[F2, Examp. 15.1.5], and this map is an isomorphism once tensored with $\mathbb { Q }$ [F2, Thms. 18.2 and 18.3]. This is the beginning of a long and rich story in algebraic geometry.

20.3.5. Side remark. The surjection map (20.3.4.2) need not be an isomorphism; see [SGA6, Exp. XIV, §4.5–4.7] (although its kernel must be torsion, as described above). As a tantalizing example, if X is the group $\mathsf { E } _ { 8 }$ , the kernel has 2-torsion, 3-torsion, and 5-torsion; see [KN, DZ].
$\mathbf { 2 0 . 4 * * }$ The Nakai–Moishezon and Kleiman Criteria for Ampleness

Exercise 20.1.K stated that if X is projective $\mathtt { k }$ -variety, and $\mathcal { L }$ is an ample line bundle on $\Sigma ,$ then for any subvariety Y of X of dimension n, $( \mathcal { L } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \cdot \mathsf { Y } ) > 0$ . The Nakai–Moishezon criterion states that this is a characterization:

20.4.1. Theorem (Nakai–Moishezon criterion for ampleness) — If $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on a projective k-scheme X, and for every irreducible subvariety Y of X, $( \mathcal { L } ^ { \mathrm { d i m } \Upsilon } . \Upsilon ) > 0 ,$ , then $\mathcal { L }$ is ample.
20.4.2. Remarks. We note that X need only be proper for this result to hold ([Kl1, Thm. III.1.1]).

Before proving the Nakai–Moishezon criterion, we point out some consequences related to our discussion of numerical equivalence in §18.4.11. By Proposition 20.1.6, $( \mathcal { L } ^ { \mathrm { d i m } \Upsilon } . \Upsilon )$ depends only on the numerical equivalence class of ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ , so ampleness is a numerical property. As a result, the notion of ampleness makes sense on $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { X } } )$ . As the tensor product of two ample invertible sheaves is ample (Exercise 16.2.H), the ample $\mathbb { Q }$ -line bundles in $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { X } } )$ form a cone, called the ample cone of X.
20.4.3. Proposition — If X is a projective k-scheme, the ample cone is open.

20.4.4. In the rest of this section, we often use additive notation for the tensor product of invertible sheaves, as described in Remark 20.1.4. This is because we want to deal with intersections on the

20.4.5. Proof of the Nakai–Moishezon criterion, Theorem 20.4.1. We prove the Nakai–Moishezon criterion in several steps.
20.4.A. UNIMPORTANT EXERCISE. Prove the case where dim $x = 0$ .

Step 1: Initial reductions. Suppose $\mathcal { L }$ satisfies the hypotheses of the theorem; we wish to show that $\mathcal { L }$ is ample. By Exercises 18.8.A and 18.8.B, we may assume that X is integral. Moreover, we can work by induction on dimension, so we can assume that $\mathcal { L }$ is ample on any closed subvariety. The base case is dimension 1, which was done in Exercise 19.2.H.

So by replacing $\mathcal { L }$ by a suitably large multiple (ampleness is independent of taking tensor powers, Theorem 16.2.2), we may assume $\mathcal { L }$ has a section, with corresponding effective divisor D. We now use D as a crutch.

As $\mathrm { D }$ is effective, ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { m } }$ is globally generated on the complement of D: we have a section nonvanishing on that big open set. Thus any base locus must be contained in D. Consider the short exact sequence

Now ${ \mathcal { L } } | _ { \mathrm { D } }$ is ample by our inductive hypothesis. Choose m so large that ${ \sf H } ^ { 1 } ( X , { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } | _ { \mathrm { D } } ) = 0$ (Serre vanishing, Theorem 18.1.3(ii)). From the exact sequence associated to (20.4.5.3),

$$
\phi_ {m}: H ^ {1} (X, \mathcal {L} ^ {\otimes (m - 1)}) \longrightarrow H ^ {1} (X, \mathcal {L} ^ {\otimes m})
$$

is surjective for $\mathbf { m } \gg 0$ . Using the fact that the $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( X , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } )$ are finite-dimensional vector spaces, as m grows, $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( X , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } )$ must eventually stabilize, so the $\Phi _ { \mathrm { { m } } }$ are isomorphisms for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ .

Thus for large m, from the long exact sequence in cohomology for (20.4.5.3), ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( X , { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } ) \to$ $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } | _ { \mathsf { D } } )$ is surjective for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ . But $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } | _ { \mathsf { D } } )$ has no base points by our inductive hypothesis (applied to D), i.e., for any point p of D there is a section of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { m } } | _ { \mathrm { D } }$ not vanishing at p, so ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( X , { \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } )$ has no base points on D either, completing Step 3.
Step 4. Thus $\mathcal { L }$ is a base-point-free line bundle with positive degree on each curve (by hypothesis of Theorem 20.4.1), so by Exercise 18.4.O we are done. □
The following result is the key to proving Kleiman’s criterion for ampleness, Theorem 20.4.7.

20.4.6. Kleiman’s Theorem — Suppose X is a projective k-scheme. If $\mathcal { L }$ is a nef invertible sheaf on $\mathsf { X } ,$ then $( \mathcal { L } ^ { \bf k } \cdot \mathsf { V } ) \ge 0$ for every irreducible subvariety $\mathsf { V } \subset \mathsf { X }$ of dimension k.
As usual, this extends to the proper case; see [Kl1, Thm. IV.2.1]. And, as usual, we postpone the proof until after we appreciate the consequences.
20.4.B. EXERCISE.

list_items: (a) (limit of amples is nef) If $\mathcal { L }$ and $\mathcal { H }$ are any two invertible sheaves such that $\mathcal { L } + \epsilon \mathcal { H }$ is ample for all sufficiently small $\epsilon > 0$ , show that $\mathcal { L }$ is nef. (Hint: $\mathrm { l i m } _ { \epsilon \to 0 }$ . This doesn’t require Kleiman’s Theorem.)

(b) $( n e f + a m p l e = a m p l e )$ Suppose $x$ is a projective k-scheme, $\mathcal { H }$ is ample, and $\mathcal { L }$ is nef. Show that $\mathcal { L } + \epsilon \mathcal { H }$ is ample for all $\epsilon \in \mathbb { Q } ^ { \geq 0 }$ . (Hint: Use the Nakai–Moishezon criterion; show that $( ( \mathcal { L } + \epsilon \mathcal { H } ) ^ { \sf k } \cdot \mathsf { V } ) > 0$ . This may help you appreciate the additive notation.)

text: 20.4.7. Theorem (Kleiman’s criterion for ampleness) — Suppose X is a projective k-scheme.

list_items: (a) The nef cone is the closure of the ample cone.
(b) The ample cone is the interior of the nef cone.

text: Side remark: Of course, (a) is false if “projective” is relaxed to “proper” (as the ample cone of a proper nonprojective variety is empty, but 0 is nef so the nef cone is nonempty). However, part (b) is true if X is proper and factorial (see [Kl1, Thm. IV.2.2] for a proof and a more general statement). Hence if X is smooth, proper, and nonprojective, then the interior of the nef cone is empty.

20.4.C. EXERCISE. In [Kl1, p. 326, Ex. 2], Kleiman describes Mumford’s example of a nonample line bundle on a smooth projective surface that meets every curve positively. Doesn’t this contradict Theorem 20.4.7? Did Mumford and Kleiman make a mistake?

list_items: Proof. (a) Ample invertible sheaves are nef (Exercise 18.4.V(e)), and the nef cone is closed (Exercise 18.4.W), so the closure of the ample cone is contained in the cone. Conversely, each nef element of $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathbb { Q } } ^ { 1 } ( { \boldsymbol { X } } )$ is the limit of ample classes by Exercise 20.4.B(a), so the nef cone is contained in the closure of the ample cone.

(b) As the ample cone is open (Proposition 20.4.3), the ample cone is contained in the interior of the nef cone. Conversely, suppose $\mathcal { L }$ is in the interior of the nef cone, and $\mathcal { H }$ is any ample class. Then $\mathcal { L } - \epsilon \mathcal { H }$ is nef for all small enough positive ϵ. Then by Exercise 20.4.B(b), $\mathcal { L } = ( \mathcal { L } - \epsilon \mathcal { H } ) +$ $\epsilon { \mathcal { H } }$ is ample. □

Chapter 21
Differentials

Differentials are an intuitive geometric notion, and we are going to figure out the right description of them algebraically. The algebraic manifestation is somewhat nonintuitive, so it is helpful to understand differentials first in terms of geometry. Also, although the algebraic statements are odd, none of the proofs are hard or long. You will notice that this topic could have been done as soon as we knew about morphisms and quasicoherent sheaves. We have usually introduced new ideas through a number of examples, but in this case we will spend a fair amount of time discussing theory, and only then get to examples.
21.1 Motivation and Game Plan
Suppose X is a “smooth” k-variety. We would like to define a tangent bundle. We will see that the right way to do this will easily apply in much more general circumstances.

list_items: We will see that the cotangent sheaf is more “natural” for schemes than tangent bundle/sheaf. This is similar to the fact that the Zariski cotangent space is more natural than the tangent space (i.e., if A is a ring and m is a maximal ideal, then ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ is “more natural” than $( \mathfrak { m } / \mathfrak { m } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \vee } ,$ , as we have repeatedly discussed since $\ S 1 3 . 1$ . In both cases this is because we are understanding “spaces” via their (sheaf of) functions on them, which is somehow dual to the geometric pictures you have of spaces in your mind. So we will define the cotangent sheaf first. An element of the (co)tangent space will be called a (co)tangent vector.

Our undergraduate intuition will continue to work—we can calculate using the Jacobian matrix, the answers will be the same in cases where our undergraduate calculations apply, and we will even have the advantage of avoiding δ’s and ϵ’s.

Our construction will automatically apply for general X, even if X is not “smooth” (or even at all nice, e.g., finite type). The cotangent sheaf will not necessarily be locally free, but it will still be a quasicoherent sheaf.

Better yet, this construction will naturally work “relatively.” For any π: $\Chi \to \mathsf Y ,$ we will define $\Omega _ { \pi } = \Omega _ { X / Y } ,$ a quasicoherent sheaf on X, the sheaf of relative differentials. The fiber of this sheaf at a point will be the cotangent vectors of the fiber of the map. This will specialize to the earlier case by taking Y = Spec k. The idea is that this glues together the cotangent sheaves of the fibers of the family. Figure 21.1 is a sketch of the relative tangent space of a map $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ at a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in { \mathsf { X } } -$ it is the tangent to the fiber. (The tangent space is easier to draw than the cotangent space!) An element of the relative (co)tangent space is called a relative (co)tangent vector.

text: Thus the central concept of this chapter is the cotangent sheaf $\Omega _ { \pi } = \Omega _ { X / Y }$ for a morphism $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ of schemes. A good picture to have in your mind is the following. If $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a submersion of manifolds (a map inducing a surjection on tangent spaces), you might hope that the tangent spaces to the fibers at each point $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ might fit together to form a vector bundle. This is the relative tangent bundle (of $\pi$ ), and its dual is $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ (see Figure 21.1). Even if you are not geometrically minded, you will find this useful. (For an arithmetic example, see Exercise 21.2.H.)

image_caption: Figure 21.1 The relative tangent space of a morphism $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ at a point p.

text: 21.2 Definitions and First Properties
21.2.1. The affine case: Three definitions.

We first study the affine case. Suppose A is a B-algebra, so we have a morphism of rings $\Phi \colon \mathrm { B }  A$ and a morphism of schemes Spec A Spec B. I will define an A-module $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ in three ways. This is called the module of relative differentials or the module of Kähler differentials. The module of differentials will be defined to be this module, as well as a map $\mathrm { d } \colon \lambda \to \Omega _ { A / \mathrm { B } }$ satisfying three properties. (Caution: Although d sends an A-module to an A-module, it is not in general A-linear. A priori we take it as a homomorphism of abelian groups, but we will momentarily make it a homomorphism of B-modules, Exercise 21.2.A.)

list_items: (i) additivity: $\mathrm { d } \mathbf { a } + \mathrm { d } \mathbf { a } ^ { \prime } = \mathbf { d } ( \mathbf { a } + \mathbf { a } ^ { \prime } )$ .
(ii) Leibniz: ${ \bf d } ( { \bf a } { \bf a } ^ { \prime } ) = { \bf a } { \bf d } { \bf a } ^ { \prime } + { \bf a } ^ { \prime }$ da.
(iii) triviality on pullbacks: db $= 0$ for $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } \in \boldsymbol { \Phi } ( \mathrm { B } )$

text: These properties will not be surprising if you have seen differentials in any other context.

State and prove the chain rule for $\operatorname { d } ( \operatorname { f } ( { \mathfrak { g } } ) )$ , where f is a polynomial with Bcoefficients, and ${ \mathfrak { g } } \in A$ . (As motivation, think of the case $\mathrm { B } = \mathrm { k }$ . So, for example, $\mathbf { \bar { d a } ^ { n } } = \mathbf { n a } ^ { \mathbf { n } - 1 } \mathbf { d a } ,$ and, more generally, if $\mathsf { f }$ is a polynomial in one variable, $\mathrm { d } { \mathsf { f } } ( { \mathbf { a } } ) = { \mathsf { f } } ^ { \prime } ( { \mathbf { a } } ) \ \mathrm { d } { \mathbf { a } } ,$ where $\mathsf { f ^ { \prime } }$ is defined formally: if $\mathsf { f } = \sum \mathsf { c } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathsf { x } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ then $\mathsf { f } ^ { \prime } = \sum \mathsf { c } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { i } \mathsf { x } ^ { \mathrm { i } - 1 }$ .)

text: We will now see three definitions of the module of Kähler differentials, which will soon “sheafify” to the sheaf of relative differentials. The first definition is a concrete hands-on definition. The second is by universal property. And the third will globalize well, and will allow us to define $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ conveniently in general.

list_items: 21.2.2. First definition of differentials: Explicit description. We define $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ to be finite A-linear combinations of symbols $\because \mathrm { d } \mathrm { a } ^ { \prime \prime }$ for ${ \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } } { \mathrm { \Omega } }$ , subject to the three rules (i)–(iii) above. For example, take $\textstyle { \mathcal { A } } = \ker [ x , y ]$ , $\mathrm { B } = \mathrm { k }$ . Then a sample differential is $3 x ^ { 2 } \mathrm { d } y + 4 \mathrm { d } x \in \Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ . We have identities such as $\mathrm { d } ( 3 x y ^ { 2 } ) = 3 y ^ { 2 } \mathrm { d } x + 6 x y \mathrm { d } y$ .

21.2.3. Key fact. Note that if A is generated over B (as an algebra) by $x _ { \mathrm { i } } \in A$ (where i lies in some index set, possibly infinite), subject to some relations $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { r } } _ { \mathrm { j } }$ (where j lies in some index set, and each is a polynomial in the $x _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ), then the A-module $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ is generated by the $\mathrm { { d } x _ { \mathrm { { i } } } , }$ subject to the relations $\mathrm { d r _ { j } } = 0$ . In short, we needn’t take every single element of A; we can take a generating set. And we needn’t take every single relation among these generating elements; we can take generators of the relations.

21.2.D. EXERCISE. Verify Key Fact 21.2.3. (If you wish, use the affine conormal exact sequence, Theorem 21.2.12, to verify it; different people prefer to work through the theory in different orders. Just take care not to make circular arguments.)

text: In particular:

21.2.4. Proposition — If A is a finitely generated B-algebra, then $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ is a finite type (i.e., finitely generated) A-module. If A is a finitely presented B-algebra, then $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ is a finitely presented A-module.
Recall (§8.3.13) that a ring A is finitely presented over another ring B if it can be expressed with finite number of generators and finite number of relations:

$$
A = B \left[ x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n} \right] / \left(r _ {1} \left(x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n}\right), \dots , r _ {j} \left(x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n}\right)\right).
$$
If A is Noetherian, then finitely presented is the same as finite type, as the “finite number of relations” comes for free, so most of you will not care.
Chapter 21 Differentials

Let’s now see some examples. Among these examples are three particularly important building blocks for ring maps: adding free variables; localizing; and taking quotients. If we know how to deal with these, we know (at least in theory) how to deal with any ring map. (They were similarly useful in understanding the fibered product in practice, in $\ S 1 0 . 2 .$ )

21.2.5. Example: Taking a quotient. If $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbb { A } = \mathbb { B } / \mathrm { I } , } \end{array}$ , then $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } } = 0$ : $\mathrm { d } \mathrm { a } = 0$ for all ${ \mathrm { \mathfrak { a } } } \in A ,$ as each such a is the image of an element of B. This should be believable; in this case, there are no “relative tangent vectors.”

21.2.6. Example: Adding variables. If $\mathsf { A } = \mathsf { B } [ \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } ] ,$ then $\Omega _ { A / \mathrm { B } } = { \cal A } \mathrm { d } \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } \oplus \cdot \cdot \cdot \oplus { \cal A } \mathrm { d } \mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ . (Note that this argument applies even if we add an arbitrarily infinite number of indeterminates.) The intuitive geometry behind this makes the answer very reasonable. The cotangent bundle of affine n-space should indeed be free of rank n.

21.2.7. Explicit example: An affine plane curve. Consider the plane curve $\mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } = \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 } - \mathsf { x }$ in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 } ,$ where the characteristic of k is not 2 (see Figure 19.7 for a picture). Let $\mathsf { A } = \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] / ( \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } - \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 } + \mathsf { x } )$ and $\mathrm { B } = \mathrm { k }$ . By Key Fact 21.2.3, the module of differentials $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ is generated by $\mathrm { d } x$ and $\mathrm { d } \mathfrak { y }$ , subject to the relation

$$
2 y d y = \left(3 x ^ {2} - 1\right) d x.
$$

We can interpret $\mathrm { d } x$ and dy geometrically. Where does the differential $\mathrm { d } x$ vanish? The previous paragraph shows that it doesn’t vanish on the patch where $2 \ y \neq 0$ . On the patch where $3 x ^ { 2 } - 1 \neq 0 ,$ , where $\mathrm { d } \mathfrak { y }$ is a generator, $\mathrm { d } \mathsf { x } = ( 2 \mathsf { y } / ( 3 \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ) \mathrm { d } \mathsf { y } ,$ from which we see that dx vanishes precisely where $\mathfrak { y } = 0$ . You should find this believable from the picture. We have shown that $\mathrm { d } x = 0$ precisely where the curve has a vertical tangent vector. Once we can pull back differentials (Exercise 21.2.K(a) or Theorem 21.2.26), we can interpret $\operatorname { d } x$ as the pullback of a differential on the $x$ -axis to Spec A (pulling back along the projection to the $x$ -axis). When we do that, using the fact that dx doesn’t vanish on the $x$ -axis, we can interpret the locus where $\mathtt { d x } = 0$ as the locus where the projection map branches. (Can you compute where $\mathrm { d } { \mathfrak { y } } = 0 ,$ and interpret it geometrically?)

This discussion applies to plane curves more generally. Suppose $\mathsf { A } = \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] / \mathsf { f } ( \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ) .$ , where, for convenience, $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ . Then the same argument as the one given above shows that $\widetilde \Omega _ { A / \kappa }$ is free of rank 1 on the open set $\mathrm { D } ( \mathfrak { d f } / \mathfrak { d x } )$ , and also on $\mathsf { D } ( \mathsf { a f } / \mathsf { a y } )$ . If Spec A is a regular curve, then these two sets cover all of Spec A. (Conversely, Exercise 13.2.F—basically the Jacobian criterion for regularity—gives regularity at the closed points. Furthermore, the curve must be reduced, or else as the nonreduced locus is closed, it would be nonreduced at a closed point, contradicting regularity. Finally, reducedness at a generic point is equivalent to regularity—a scheme whose underlying set is a point is reduced if and only if it is regular. Alternatively, we could invoke a big result, Fact 13.8.2, to get regularity at the generic point from regularity at the closed points.)

If, on the other hand, the plane curve is singular, then $\Omega$ is not locally free of rank 1. For example, consider the plane curve Spec A where $\mathtt { A } = \mathbb { C } [ { \sf x } , { \sf y } ] / ( { \sf y } ^ { 2 } - { \sf x } ^ { 3 } ) ,$ , so

$$
\Omega_ {A / \mathbb {C}} = (A d x \oplus A d y) / (2 y d y - 3 x ^ {2} d x).
$$

Then the fiber of $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / C } }$ over the origin (computed by setting $\scriptstyle x = y = 0$ ) is rank 2, as it is generated by $\operatorname { d } x$ and $\mathrm { d y } .$ , with no relation.
Implicit in the above discussion is the following exercise, showing that $\Omega$ can be computed using the Jacobian matrix.
21.2 Definitions and First Properties

21.2.E. IMPORTANT BUT EASY EXERCISE (JACOBIAN DESCRIPTION OF $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ ). Suppose $A =$ $\mathbb { B } [ x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ] / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathsf { f } _ { r } )$ . Then

$$
\Omega_ {A / B} = \left(\bigoplus_ {i} A d x _ {i}\right) / \left(A d f _ {1} + \dots + A d f _ {r}\right)
$$
may be interpreted as the cokernel of the Jacobian matrix (13.1.7.1)

$$
J \colon A ^ {\oplus r} \longrightarrow A ^ {\oplus n}.
$$
(You might notice that if J has full rank everywhere, so A is smooth over B, then $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ is locally free; see Exercise 21.2.Q for more.)
The next result connects the cotangent module $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ to the cotangent space at a (rational) point.

21.2.F. EXERCISE (THE FIBER OF $\Omega$ AT A RATIONAL POINT IS THE COTANGENT SPACE). Suppose B is a finite type k-algebra, and ${ \mathfrak { m } } \subset { \mathrm { B } }$ is a maximal ideal with residue field k. Then there is an isomorphism of k-vector spaces $\delta \colon { \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 } \{ \substack { \sim } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B / k } } \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathsf { k }$ (where the k on the right is a B-module via the isomorphism $\mathsf { k } \longleftrightarrow \mathsf { B } / \mathfrak { m }$ ). Hint: Both are “computed” using the Jacobian matrix.

(Exercise 21.2.F is true even without the finite type hypothesis, although the hint doesn’t apply in that case. You are welcome to prove this more general statement, using the universal property of $\Omega _ { \mathrm { B / k } }$ . But we won’t need it.)

Corollary 21.2.33 will give a quite different proof, and generalize it to the case where $\beta / { \mathfrak { m } }$ is a finite separable extension of k. It will allow us to extend the Jacobian description of the Zariski cotangent space to closed points whose residue field is a separable algebraic extension of the ground field, not just the ground field itself.

21.2.G. EXERCISE. Suppose X is a finite type scheme over an algebraically closed field. Show that the function from the closed points of X to $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ given by ${ \mathfrak { p } } \mapsto \dim { \mathfrak { T } } _ { \mathfrak { p } } X$ is upper semicontinuous in the Zariski topology. (Be clear on what the Zariski topology is on the set of closed points; cf. §8.4.8.) Corollary 21.2.33 will immediately extend this to all fields of characteristic 0, and all finite fields.

21.2.8. Example: Localization. If T is a multiplicative subset of B, and $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { A } = \mathsf { T } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { B } , } \end{array}$ , then $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } } = 0$ . Reason: By the quotient rule (Exercise 21.2.B), if $\mathrm { { \mathbf { a } = b / t } , }$ then $\mathrm { d } \mathrm { a } = \mathrm { ( t ~ } \mathrm { d } \mathrm { b } - \mathrm { b } \mathrm { ~ } \mathrm { d t } \mathrm { ) } / \mathrm { t } ^ { 2 } = 0 ,$ . If $\mathsf { A } = \mathsf { B } _ { \mathsf { f } } ,$ this is intuitively believable; then Spec A is an open subset of Spec B, so there should be no relative (co)tangent vectors.

21.2.H. IMPORTANT EXERCISE (FIELD EXTENSIONS). This notion of relative differentials is interesting even for finite extensions of fields. In other words, even when you map a reduced point to a reduced point, something interesting can happen with differentials.
