(Hint: For any $\alpha \in { \mathsf { K } } ,$ there is a polynomial $\mathsf { f } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ such that $\mathsf { f } ( \alpha ) = 0$ and $\mathsf { f } ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) \neq 0 .$ )

(b) Suppose k is a field of characteristic p, ${ \sf K } = { \sf k } ( { \sf t ^ { p } } ) , { \sf L } = { \sf k } ( { \sf t } )$ . Compute $\Omega _ { \mathrm { L / K } }$ (where $K \hookrightarrow \operatorname { L }$ is the “obvious” inclusion).

text: We now delve a little deeper, and discuss two useful and geometrically motivated exact sequences.
21.2.9. Theorem (cotangent exact sequence, affine version) — Suppose $\mathrm { C } \to \mathrm { B } \to \mathrm { A }$ are ring morphisms. Then there is a natural exact sequence of A-modules

$$
A \otimes_ {B} \Omega_ {B / C} \xrightarrow {a \otimes d b \mapsto a d b} \Omega_ {A / C} \xrightarrow {d a \mapsto d a} \Omega_ {A / B} \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Chapter 21 Differentials

The proof will be quite straightforward algebraically, but the statement comes fundamentally from geometry, and that is how best to remember it. Figure 21.2 is a sketch of a map $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ . Here X should be interpreted as Spec A, Y as Spec B, and Spec C (we will soon call it Z) is a point. (If you would like a picture with a higher-dimensional Spec C, just “take the product of Figure 21.2 with a curve.”) In the Figure, Y is “smooth,” and X is “smooth over Y”— which means roughly that all the fibers are smooth. Suppose p is a point of X. Then the tangent space of the fiber of $\pi$ at p is certainly a subspace of the tangent space of the total space of X at p. The cokernel is naturally the pullback of the tangent space of Y at $\pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ . This short exact sequence for each p should be part of a short exact sequence of “relative tangent sheaves”

image_caption: Figure 21.2 The geometry behind the cotangent exact sequence.

text: on X. (We will formally define “relative tangent sheaf” in §21.2.18.) Dualizing this yields

This is precisely the statement of Theorem 21.2.9, except we also have left-exactness. This discrepancy is because the statement of the theorem is more general; we will see in Exercise 21.2.S that in the “smooth” case, we indeed have left-exactness.

21.2.10. Intriguing remark. As always, whenever you see something right-exact, you should suspect that there should be some sort of (co)homology theory so that this is the end of a long exact sequence. This is indeed the case, and this exact sequence involves André-Quillen homology (see [E, p. 386] for more). You should expect that the next term to the left should be the first homology corresponding to A/B, and in particular shouldn’t involve C. So if you already suspect that you have exactness on the left in the case where A/B and B/C are “smooth” (whatever that means), and the intuition of Figure 21.2 applies, then you should expect further that all that is necessary is that A/B be “smooth,” and that this would imply that the first André-Quillen homology should be zero. Even though you wouldn’t precisely know what all the words meant, you would be completely correct! You would also be developing a vague inkling about the cotangent complex. We will see examples when left-exactness holds in a sufficiently “smooth” situation in Proposition 21.4.2 and Exercise 21.2.S. For a more general statement, see [Stacks, tag 06B6]. See also [Liu, Cor. 6.3.22] and [Liu, Prop. 6.3.11] for clean discussions of the next two terms in the sequence: “cotangent exact sequence,” “conormal exact sequence,”.

21.2.11. Proof of Theorem 21.2.9 (the cotangent exact sequence, affine version). First, note that surjectivity of $\Omega _ { A / C }  \Omega _ { A / \mathrm { B } }$ is clear, as this map is given by $\mathrm { d } \mathrm { a } \mapsto \mathrm { d } \mathrm { a }$ (where ${ \mathfrak { a } } \in { \mathcal { A } }$ ).
→ →Next, the composition over the middle term is clearly 0, as this composition is given by a $\otimes$ $\mathrm { d } \mathsf { b } \mapsto \mathrm { a d } \mathsf { b } \mapsto 0$ .

Finally, we wish to identify $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ as the cokernel of $\lambda \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B / C } } \longrightarrow \Omega _ { A / C } . \mathrm { N o w } \Omega _ { A / \mathrm { B } }$ $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / B } }$ is exactly the same as $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / C } } ,$ except we have extra relations: $\mathrm { d } \mathrm { b } = 0$ for $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } \in \mathbb { B }$ . These are precisely the images of $1 \otimes \mathrm { d b }$ on the left. □
21.2 Definitions and First Properties

21.2.12. Theorem (conormal exact sequence, affine version) — Suppose B is a C-algebra, I is an ideal of B, and $\mathtt { A } = \mathtt { B } / \mathrm { I }$ . Then there is a natural exact sequence of A-modules

$$
I / I ^ {2} \xrightarrow {\delta} A \otimes_ {B} \Omega_ {B / C} \xrightarrow {a \otimes d b \mapsto a d b} \Omega_ {A / C} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

The map δ is given by ${ \mathrm { i } } \mapsto 1 \otimes { \mathrm { d i } } ,$ or, more formally, $1 \otimes \mathrm { d } \colon \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { I } \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathrm { I } \to \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { I } \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { C } } ,$ .

(You will recognize the map $\mathsf { A } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B / C } } \to \Omega _ { A / C }$ from the cotangent exact sequence, Theorem 21.2.9. A special case of the map δ secretly appeared in Exercise 21.2.F.) The proof is algebraic, so the geometric discussion afterward may help clarify how you should really think of it.

Proof. We will identify the cokernel of $\delta \colon \mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 } \to \mathsf { A } \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B / C } }$ with $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / C } }$ . Consider $A \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B / C } }$ . As an A-module, it is generated by db (where $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } \in \mathbb { B }$ ), subject to three types of relations: $\operatorname { d c } = 0$ for $\mathsf { c } \in \phi ( \mathsf { C } )$ (where $\Phi \colon { \mathrm { C } } \to { \mathrm { B } }$ describes B as a C-algebra), additivity, and the Leibniz rule. Given any relation in B, d of that relation is 0.

Now $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / C } }$ is defined similarly, except there are more relations in A; these are precisely the elements of ${ \mathrm { I } } \subset { \mathrm { B } }$ . Thus we obtain $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / C } }$ by starting out with $A \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \Omega _ { \mathrm { B / C } } ,$ , and adding the additional relations $\mathrm { d i } ,$ where $\mathfrak { i } \in \mathrm { I }$ . But this is precisely the image of δ! (Be sure that you see how the identification of the cokernel of δ with $\Omega _ { \mathrm { A / C } }$ is precisely via the map a ⊗ db 7 a db.) □
We now give a geometric interpretation of the conormal exact sequence, and in particular define conormal modules/sheaves/bundles.

image_caption: Figure 21.3 The geometry behind the conormal exact sequence.

text: As with the cotangent exact sequence (Theorem 21.2.9), the conormal exact sequence is fundamentally about geometry. To motivate it, consider the sketch of Figure 21.3. In the sketch, everything is “smooth,” X is one-dimensional, Y is twodimensional, j is the inclusion j : $\textstyle \chi \hookrightarrow \Upsilon ,$ and Z is a point. Then at a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ the tangent space $\mathcal { T } _ { X } | _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ clearly injects into the tangent space of j(p) in Y, and the cokernel is the normal vector space to X in Y at p. This should give an exact sequence of bundles on X:

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {T} _ {X} \longrightarrow j ^ {*} \mathcal {T} _ {Y} \longrightarrow \mathcal {N} _ {X / Y} \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Dualizing this should give

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {N} _ {X / Y} ^ {\vee} \longrightarrow j ^ {*} \Omega_ {Y / Z} \longrightarrow \Omega_ {X / Z} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

This is precisely what appears in the statement of the theorem, except (i) the exact sequence in algebraic geometry is not necessarily exact on the left, and (ii) we see $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ instead of $\mathcal { N } _ { \mathrm { S p e c } \mathrm { A / \thinspace S p e c \thinspace B } } ^ { \vee }$ .

list_items: 21.2.13. We resolve the first issue (i) by expecting that the sequence of Theorem 21.2.12 is exact on the left in appropriately “smooth” situations, and this is indeed the case; see Theorem 21.2.31 and Remark 21.2.32. (If you enjoyed Remark 21.2.10, you might correctly guess several things. The next term on the left should be the first André-Quillen homology of $A / C ,$ so we should only need that A/C is smooth, and B should be irrelevant. Also, if $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbb { A } = \mathbb { B } / \mathrm { I } , } \end{array}$ then we should expect that $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ is the first André-Quillen homology of A/B.)

21.2.14. Conormal modules and conormal sheaves. We resolve the second issue (ii) by declaring $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ to be the conormal module, and in Definition 21.2.15 we will define the obvious analog as the conormal sheaf.

text: Here is some geometric intuition as to why we might want to call (the sheaf associated to) $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ the conormal sheaf, which will likely confuse you, but may offer some enlightenment. First, if Spec A is a closed point of Spec B, we expect the conormal space to be precisely the cotangent space.
Chapter 21 Differentials

And indeed if $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbb { A } = \mathbb { B } / \mathfrak { m } , } \end{array}$ the Zariski cotangent space is ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ . (We made this subtle connection in §13.1.) In particular, at some point you will develop a sense of why the conormal $\left( = \right.$ cotangent) space to the origin in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { S p e c k } [ \mathbb { x } , \mathbb { y } ]$ is naturally the space of linear forms $\alpha x + \beta y$ . But then consider the $z$ -axis in Spec $\begin{array} { r } { { \bf k } [ x , y , z ] = \mathbb { A } _ { \bf k } ^ { 3 } , } \end{array}$ cut out by $\operatorname { I } = ( { \boldsymbol { x } } , { \boldsymbol { y } } )$ . Elements of $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ may be written as $\propto ( z ) x + \beta ( z ) y ,$ where $\alpha ( z )$ and $\beta \left( z \right)$ are polynomial. This reasonably should be the conormal space to the $z$ -axis: as $z$ varies, the coefficients of $x$ and $_ { 9 }$ vary. More generally, the same idea suggests that the conormal module/sheaf to any coordinate k-plane inside n-space corresponds to $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ . Now consider a $\mathrm { k }$ -dimensional manifold $x$ inside an n-dimensional manifold Y, with the classical topology. We can apply the same construction: if $\mathcal { I }$ is the ideal sheaf of X in Y, then $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ can be identified with the conormal sheaf (essentially the conormal vector bundle), because, analytically locally, $\chi \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ can be identified with $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathsf { k } } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { R } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ . For this reason, you might hope that in algebraic geometry, if Spec A $\hookrightarrow$ Spec B is an inclusion of something “smooth” in something “smooth, $' / \mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ should be the conormal module (or, after applying the functor $\sim ,$ the conormal sheaf). Motivated by this, we define the conormal module as $\mathrm { I } / \mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ always, and then notice that it has good properties (such as Theorem 21.2.12), but take care to learn what unexpected behavior it might have when we are not in the “smooth” situation, by working out examples such as that of §21.2.7.

21.2.15. Definition. Suppose i: $\tt X { \hookrightarrow \tt Y }$ is a closed embedding of schemes cut out by ideal sheaf $\mathcal { I }$ →. Define the conormal sheaf for a closed embedding by $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ , denoted by $\mathcal { N } _ { X / Y } ^ { \vee }$ . (The product of quasicoherent ideal sheaves was defined in Exercise 15.6.E.) Important: We interpret $\mathcal { N } _ { x / \Upsilon } ^ { \vee }$ as a quasicoherent sheaf on X, not (just) Y—be sure you understand why we may do so. (Exercise 17.1.E is one reason, but it may confuse the issue more than help.)

Define the normal sheaf as its dual $\mathcal { N } _ { \sf X / Y } : = \mathcal { H } o m ( \mathcal { N } _ { \sf X / Y } ^ { \vee } , \mathcal { O } _ { \sf X } )$ . This is imperfect notation, because it suggests that the dual of $\mathcal { N }$ is always $\mathcal { N } ^ { \vee }$ . This is not always true, as for A-modules, the natural morphism from a module to its double-dual is not always an isomorphism. (Modules for which this is true are called reflexive, but we won’t use this notion.)
If the normal sheaf is locally free of finite rank, then we call the associated vector bundle the normal bundle of X in Y.

21.2.I. EASY EXERCISE. Define the conormal sheaf $\mathcal { N } _ { x / \Upsilon } ^ { \vee }$ (and hence the normal sheaf NX/Y) for a locally closed embedding i : $\tt X \hookrightarrow \tt Y$ of schemes, a quasicoherent sheaf on X. (Make sure your definition is well-defined!)

In the good situation of a regular embedding, the conormal sheaf (and hence the normal sheaf) is locally free of finite rank (or, more informally, a vector bundle). In particular, the dual of $\mathcal { N }$ is indeed ${ \mathcal { N } } ^ { \vee }$ . As a warm-up we deal with the important codimension 1 case.

21.2.J. EXERCISE: NORMAL BUNDLES TO EFFECTIVE CARTIER DIVISORS. Suppose $\operatorname { D } \subset X$ is an effective Cartier divisor (§9.5.1). Show that the conormal sheaf $\mathcal { N } _ { \mathrm { D / X } } ^ { \vee }$ is $\mathcal { O } ( - \mathsf { D } ) | _ { \mathsf { D } }$ (and, in particular, is an invertible sheaf), and hence that the normal sheaf is $\mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { D } ) | _ { \mathrm { D } }$ . This justifies our earlier definition of normal bundles to effective Cartier divisors, in $\ S 1 5 . 6 . 2$ . It may be surprising that the normal sheaf should be locally free if $\mathsf { X } \cong \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ and D is the union of the two axes (and, more generally, if $x$ is regular but D is singular), because you may be used to thinking that a “tubular neighborhood” is isomorphic to the normal bundle.
We now treat the general case.

21.2.17. Second definition: Universal property. Here is a second definition that is important philosophically, by universal property. Of course, it is a characterization rather than a definition: by universal property nonsense, it shows that if the module exists (with the d map), then it is unique up to unique isomorphism, and then one still has to construct it to make sure that it exists.

Suppose A is a B-algebra, and M is an A-module. A B-linear derivation of A into M is a map $\mathrm { d } \colon  { \mathcal { A } } \to M$ of B-modules (not necessarily a map of A-modules) satisfying the Leibniz rule: $\operatorname { d } ( \operatorname { f } _ { 9 } ) = \operatorname { f } \ \operatorname { d } { } 9 + { } 9$ df. As an example, suppose ${ \mathrm { B } } = { \mathrm { k } } ,$ and $\lambda = \operatorname { k } [ x ] ,$ and $M = A$ . Then $\mathrm { d } / \mathrm { d } x$ is a klinear derivation. As a second example, if $\mathrm { B } = \mathrm { k } ,$ $\mathtt { B } = \mathtt { k } , \mathtt { A } = \mathtt { k } [ \mathtt { x } ]$ , and ${ \cal M } = \mathrm { k } ,$ then $\left( \mathrm { d } / \mathrm { d } \boldsymbol { x } \right) | _ { 0 }$ (the operator “evaluate the derivative at $0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ) is a $\mathrm { k }$ -linear derivation.

A third example is $\mathrm { d } \colon \lambda \to \Omega _ { A / \mathrm { B } }$ , and indeed $\mathrm { d } \colon \lambda \to \Omega _ { A / \mathrm { B } }$ is the universal B-linear derivation of A. text: 21.2 Definitions and First Properties

21.2.18. Third definition: Global. We now want to globalize this definition for an arbitrary morphism of schemes $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ . We could do this “affine by affine”; we just need to make sure that the above notion behaves well with respect to “change of affine sets.” Thus a relative differential on X would be the data of, for every affine $\mathrm { u } \subset \mathrm { X } ,$ a differential of the form $\sum { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathrm { d } } { \mathrm { b } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ and on the intersection of two affine open sets $\mathrm { u } \cap \mathrm { u } ^ { \prime }$ , with representatives $\sum { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { d } \mathsf { b } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ on U and $\sum { { \bf { a } } _ { \mathrm { { i } } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { { d } } { \bf { b } } _ { \mathrm { { i } } } ^ { \prime } }$ on $\mathrm { u } ^ { \prime }$ , and equality on the overlap. Instead, we take a different approach. I will give the (seemingly unintuitive) definition, then tell you how to think about it, and then get back to the definition.

Let π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ be any morphism of schemes. Recall that $\delta \colon X \to X \times _ { Y } X$ is a locally closed embedding (Proposition 11.2.1(b)). Define the relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ (or $\Omega _ { \pi }$ ) as the conormal sheaf $\mathcal { N } _ { x / x \times \gamma } ^ { \vee } x$ of the diagonal (see §21.2.14—and if $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is separated you needn’t even worry about Exercise 21.2.I). (Now is also as good a time as any to define the relative tangent sheaf $\mathcal { T } _ { X / Y }$ as the dual $\mathcal { H } o m ( \Omega _ { X / \Upsilon } , \mathcal { O } _ { X } )$ to the relative cotangent sheaf. If we are working in the category of $\mathrm { k }$ -schemes, then $\Omega _ { X / \kappa }$ and $\mathcal { T } _ { \mathrm { X / k } }$ are often called the cotangent sheaf and tangent sheaf of $\mathsf { X } ,$ respectively.)

Let $\mathfrak { p r } _ { 1 } \colon X \times _ { \Upsilon } X \to X$ and ${ \mathfrak { p r } } _ { 2 } \colon X \times { \mathfrak { r } } X \to X$ be the two projections. Then define d: $\mathcal { O } _ { X } \to \Omega _ { X / \Upsilon }$ on the open set U as follows:

Say, for example, that Y is a point, and X a manifold. Then the tangent bundle ${ \sf T } _ { { \sf X } \times { \sf X } }$ on $x \times x$ is pr∗TX $\mathfrak { p r } _ { 2 } ^ { * } \bar { \Gamma } _ { X } ,$ where pr1 and ${ \mathsf { p r } } _ { 2 }$ are the projections from $x \times x$ onto its two factors. Restrict this to the diagonal $\Delta ,$ and look at the normal bundle exact sequence:

$$

The derivation is $\mathrm { d } \colon \mathsf { A } \to \mathsf { A } \otimes \mathsf { B } \mathsf { A } ,$ , ${ \bf a } \longmapsto \left( 1 \otimes { \bf a } - { \bf a } \otimes 1 \right)$ (an element of I, taken modulo $\mathrm { I } ^ { 2 }$ ). (We shouldn’t really call this $\mathit { \Pi } ^ { \prime \prime } { \mathrm { { d } } \mathit { \Pi } ^ { \prime \prime } }$ until we have verified that it agrees with our earlier definition, but we irresponsibly will anyway.)

Thus the global construction indeed naturally “glues together” the affine construction.
We can now use our understanding of how $\Omega$ works on affine open sets to generalize previous statements to non-affine settings.

(a) (cotangent exact sequence) Suppose $\textsf { X } \stackrel { \quad \pi } { \to } \textsf { Y } \stackrel { \quad \rho } { \to } \textsf { Z }$ are morphisms of schemes. Then there is an exact sequence of quasicoherent sheaves on X,

$$
\pi^ {*} \Omega_ {Y / Z} \longrightarrow \Omega_ {X / Z} \longrightarrow \Omega_ {X / Y} \longrightarrow 0,
$$

(b) (conormal exact sequence) Suppose $\rho \colon \Upsilon  Z$ is a morphism of schemes, and i : $\chi \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ is a closed embedding, with conormal sheaf $\mathcal { N } _ { X / Y } ^ { \vee }$ . Then there is an exact sequence of sheaves on X;

$$
\mathcal {N} _ {X / Y} ^ {\vee} \xrightarrow {\delta} i ^ {*} \Omega_ {Y / Z} \longrightarrow \Omega_ {X / Z} \longrightarrow 0,
$$

You should expect these exact sequences to be left-exact as well in the presence of appropriate smoothness (see Remark 21.2.10 and §21.2.13). The following is an important special case for the cotangent exact sequence. (Theorem 21.2.31 will give a similar statement for the conormal exact sequence, for smooth varieties.)

Hint: The $( \mathfrak { m } + \mathfrak { n } ) \times ( \mathfrak { m } + \mathfrak { n } )$ matrix you used in Exercise 13.6.D is “block upper triangular.”
21.2.25. Pulling back relative differentials. Not surprisingly, the sheaf of relative differentials pulls back, and it behaves well under base change.

(a) If

is a commutative diagram of schemes, there is a natural homomorphism $\mu ^ { * } \Omega _ { X / Y }  \Omega _ { X ^ { \prime } / Y ^ { \prime } }$ of quasicoherent sheaves on $x ^ { \prime }$ . An important special case is $\mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { Y } ^ { \prime }$ .

(b) (Ω behaves well under base change) If furthermore the above diagram is a Cartesian square (so $x ^ { \prime } \cong$ $\mathsf { X } \times \mathsf { \boldsymbol { Y } } ^ { \prime } ,$ ), then $\mu ^ { * } \Omega _ { X / Y }  \Omega _ { X ^ { \prime } / Y ^ { \prime } }$ is an isomorphism.
21.2.T. EXERCISE. Derive Theorem 21.2.26 from Exercise 21.2.K. (Why does the construction of Exercise 21.2.K(a) “glue well”?)

As a particular case of Theorem 21.2.26(b), the fiber of the sheaf of relative differentials is indeed the sheaf of differentials of the fiber. Thus the sheaf of differentials notion indeed “glues together” the differentials on each fiber.

21.2.U. EXERCISE. Suppose $\alpha \colon X \to Z$ and $\beta \colon \Upsilon \to Z$ are two morphisms, and let $\alpha ^ { \prime } : X \times _ { Z } Y $ Y and $\beta ^ { \prime } : X \times _ { Z } \Upsilon \to X$ be the morphisms induced by fibered product. Describe an isomorphism $\Omega _ { X \times _ { Z } \Upsilon / Z } \stackrel { \sim } { \longleftrightarrow } ( \beta ^ { \prime } ) ^ { * } \Omega _ { X / Z } \oplus ( \alpha ^ { \prime } ) ^ { * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / Z } .$ (The right side is often written as $\Omega _ { \scriptscriptstyle { X / Z } } \boxplus \Omega _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \Upsilon / Z } }$ .)
21.2.27. Important: Smoothness of varieties revisited.
Suppose k is a field. Since $\ S 1 3 . 2 . 3 ,$ , we have used an awkward definition of k-smoothness, and we now make our definition of k-smoothness more robust.

21.2.28. Redefinition. A k-scheme X is k-smooth of dimension n or smooth of dimension n over $\mathrm { k }$ if it is locally of finite type, of pure dimension ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and $\Omega _ { X / \mathrm { k } }$ is locally free of rank n. The dimension n is often omitted, and one might (possibly) want to call something smooth if it is the (scheme-theoretic) disjoint union of things smooth of various dimensions.
21.2.V. EXERCISE. Show that Redefinition 21.2.28 of k-smoothness agrees with Definition 13.2.4. (Hint: Both can be described by the cokernel of the Jacobian.)
21.2.29. Remark. We will also revisit the definition of smooth morphism (Definition 13.6.2) before long, in Theorem 24.8.8.

21.2.W.** EXERCISE (FOR THOSE WITH BACKGROUND IN COMPLEX GEOMETRY). Suppose X is a complex algebraic variety. Show that the analytification $X ^ { \mathrm { a n } }$ of X (defined in Exercise 7.3.P) is smooth (in the differential-geometric sense) if and only if X is smooth (in the algebro-geometric sense, over $\mathbb { C }$ ). In this case, show that complex dimension of the complex manifold $X ^ { \mathrm { a n } }$ (half the real dimension) is dim X. Hint: The Jacobian criterion applies in both settings.
21.2.30. Left-exactness of the conormal exact sequence for embeddings of smooth varieties.
As described in §21.2.13, we expect the conormal exact sequence to be exact on the left in appropriately “smooth” situations.

21.2.31. Theorem (conormal exact sequence for smooth varieties) — Suppose i: $\chi \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ is a closed embedding of smooth varieties over a field k, with conormal sheaf $\mathcal { N } _ { X / Y } ^ { \vee }$ . Then $\mathcal { N } _ { X / Y } ^ { \vee }$ is locally free, and the conormal exact sequence (Theorem 21.2.24(b)) .is exact on the left:
is exact.
By dualizing, i.e., applying $\mathcal { H } o m ( \cdot , \mathcal { O } _ { \boldsymbol { X } } )$ , we obtain the normal exact sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {T} _ {\mathrm {X} / \mathrm {k}} \longrightarrow \mathcal {T} _ {\mathrm {Y} / \mathrm {k}} | _ {\mathrm {X}} \longrightarrow \mathcal {N} _ {\mathrm {X} / \mathrm {Y}} \longrightarrow 0
$$
(exact by Exercise 14.2.I), which is geometrically more intuitive (see Figure 21.3 and the discussion after the proof of Theorem 21.2.12).

21.2.32. Remark. The conormal sequence is exact on the left in even more general circumstances. Essentially all that is required is appropriate smoothness of $\mathfrak { i o g } \colon X \to Z ;$ see [Stacks, tag 06B7], and [Stacks, tag 06BB] for related facts. In particular, the smoothness of Y is irrelevant! As a consequence, Important Corollary 21.2.33 below also holds for finite type Y in general.
Proof.

21.2.X. EXERCISE. Show that Theorem 21.2.31 is true if and only if it is true upon base change to the algebraic closure $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ of k. Hint: Show that construction of all the terms in (21.2.31.1) plays well
21.2 Definitions and First Properties
with algebraic field extensions. For the case of differentials, see Theorem 21.2.26. For the case of the conormal sheaf, check that the definition plays well with algebraic field extensions.

We may thus assume that k is algebraically closed, and thus (by Exercise 13.2.J) closed points of smooth k-schemes are regular. Let $\mathcal { I }$ be the ideal sheaf of $\operatorname { i } : X \hookrightarrow \Upsilon .$ . For any closed point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in { \mathsf { X } } ,$ by Exercise 13.2.M(b), i is a regular embedding in an open neighborhood of p. Hence i is a regular embedding. (Do you see why?) Thus by Proposition 21.2.16(b), $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ is locally free of rank r. By Exercise 6.6.G(a), the associated points of ker δ are a subset of the associated points of $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ , so to show that ker $\delta = 0$ , it suffices to check this at the associated points of $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ , which are precisely the associated points of X (as $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ is locally free).

By Proposition 13.2.13 (smooth implies reduced), X is reduced. Thus we need only check at the generic points of (the irreducible components of) X (as X has no embedded points; see $\ S 6 . 6 . 2$ or Exercise 6.6.R). But this involves checking the left-exactness of a right-exact sequence of vector spaces (X reduced implies that the stalk at the generic point is a vector space), and as the dimension of the left (the codimension of X in Y by Proposition 21.2.16(b)) is precisely the difference of the other two, we are done. □

21.2.33. Important corollary — Suppose Y is a smooth k-variety, and $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ is a closed point whose residue field $\kappa ( \ q )$ is separable over k. (This is automatic if char ${ \sf k } = 0$ or if k is a finite field.) Then the conormal exact sequence for ${ \mathfrak { q } } \hookrightarrow { \mathsf { Y } }$ yields an isomorphism of the Zariski cotangent space of Y at q with the fiber of $\Omega _ { \Upsilon / { \sf k } }$ at q.

Proof. Apply Theorem 21.2.31 with $\mathtt { X } = \mathtt { q }$ . Note that q is indeed a smooth $\mathtt { k }$ -variety (using separability of $\kappa ( \mathsf { q } ) / \mathsf { k ! } )$ , and that $\Omega _ { \mathbf { q } / \mathbf { k } } = 0$ . □

21.2.34. Remark. This result generalizes Exercise 21.2.F to separable closed points if Y is smooth, and as mentioned in Remark 21.2.32, even this smoothness condition on Y can be removed. As described after the statement of Exercise 21.2.F, this has a number of consequences. For example, it extends the Jacobian description of the Zariski tangent space to separable closed points.

21.2.Y. EXERCISE (CF. §13.2.15). Suppose p is a closed point of a k-variety X, with residue field $\kappa ( \mathfrak { p } )$ that is separable over k. Define π $: X _ { \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } : = X \times _ { \mathtt { k } } \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } \to X$ by base change from Spec $\bar { \kappa } $ Spec k. Suppose $\mathfrak { q } \in \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathfrak { p } )$ . Show that $x _ { \mathrm { \overline { { k } } } }$ is regular at q if and only if X is regular at p.
21.3 Examples
The examples below are organized by topic, not by difficulty.

21.3.1. The geometric genus of a curve. A smooth irreducible projective curve C (over a field k) has geometric genus $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } \mathopen { } \mathclose \bgroup \left( \mathrm { C } , \Omega _ { \mathrm { C / k } } \aftergroup \egroup \right)$ . (This will be generalized to higher dimension in §21.5.3.) This is always finite, as $\Omega _ { \mathrm { C / k } }$ is coherent (Exercise 21.2.P), and coherent sheaves on projective k-schemes have finite-dimensional spaces of sections (Theorem 18.1.3(i)). Sadly, this isn’t really a new invariant, by the following.

21.3.A. EASY EXERCISE. Assuming Miracle 18.5.2 (that $\Omega _ { \mathrm { C / k } }$ is Serre-dualizing), show that for a smooth irreducible projective curve C, the geometric genus equals the arithmetic genus, i.e., $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf C , \Omega _ { \mathsf C / \mathsf { k } } ) = \mathsf { h } ^ { \intercal } ( \mathsf C , \bar { \boldsymbol { \sigma } } _ { \mathsf C } )$ .
Now let’s compute some differentials!

21.3.2. The projective line. As an important first example, consider $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ,$ with the usual projective coordinates $x _ { 0 }$ and $_ { x _ { 1 } }$ . As usual, the first patch corresponds to $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 0 } \neq 0$ , and is of the form Spec $\mathbb { k } [ x _ { 1 / 0 } ] ,$ where $x _ { 1 / 0 } = x _ { 1 } / x _ { 0 }$ . The second patch corresponds to $\qquad \times _ { 1 } \neq 0 ,$ and is of the form Spec $\operatorname { k } [ \mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 0 / 1 } ] ,$ where ${ \boldsymbol x } _ { 0 / 1 } = { \boldsymbol x } _ { 0 } / x _ { 1 }$ .
Chapter 21 Differentials

Both patches are isomorphic to $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ,$ and $\Omega _ { \mathbb { A } _ { \bf k } ^ { 1 } } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { A } _ { \bf k } ^ { 1 } }$ . (More precisely, $\Omega _ { { \bf k } [ { \bf x } ] / { \bf k } } = { \bf k } [ { \bf x } ] \mathrm { d } { \bf x } .$ ) Thus $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 } }$ is an invertible sheaf (a line bundle). The invertible sheaves on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ are of the form $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ . So kwhich invertible sheaf is $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } / \kappa }$ ?

Let’s take a section, $\mathrm { d } x _ { 1 / 0 }$ on the first patch. It has no zeros or poles there, so let’s check what happens on the other patch. As $x _ { 1 / 0 } = 1 / x _ { 0 / 1 } ,$ we have $\mathrm { d } { \bf x } _ { 1 / 0 } = - ( 1 / { \nu } _ { 0 / 1 } ^ { 2 } ) \mathrm { d } { \bf x } _ { 0 / 1 } .$ . Thus this section has a double pole where $x _ { 0 / 1 } = 0$ . Hence $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 } / \mathbf { k } } \cong \mathcal { O } ( - 2 )$ . As a consequence, we have shown that the geometric genus of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ is 0.

Note that the above argument works equally well if $\mathrm { k }$ were replaced by $\mathbb { Z }$ : our theory of Weil divisors and line bundles of Chapter 15 applies $( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { 1 }$ is factorial), so the previous argument essentially without change shows that $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { 1 } / \mathbb { Z } } \cong \mathcal { O } ( - 2 )$ . And because $\Omega$ behaves well with respect to base PZ Z change (Theorem 21.2.26(b)), and any scheme maps to Spec $\mathbb { Z } ,$ this implies that $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { 1 } / \mathrm { B } } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { 1 } } ( - 2 )$ for any base scheme B.

(Also, as suggested by §18.5.2, this shows that $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } / \kappa }$ is the canonical sheaf for $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ;$ see also Example 18.5.4. But given that we haven’t yet proved Serre duality, this isn’t so meaningful.)
Side remark: the fact that the degree of the tangent bundle is 2 is related to the “Hairy Ball Theorem” (the dimension 2 case of [Hat, Thm. 2.28]).

21.3.3. Hyperelliptic curves. Throughout this discussion of hyperelliptic curves, we suppose that $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ and char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2 ,$ , so we may apply the discussion of $\ S 1 9 . 5 .$ Consider a double cover π: $C $ $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ by a regular projective curve C, branched over $2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2$ distinct points. We will use the explicit coordinate description of hyperelliptic curves of (19.5.2.1). In particular, $\pi$ is unbranched at 0. By Theorem 19.5.1, C has genus g.

list_items: 21.3.5. Projective space and the Euler exact sequence. We next examine the differentials of projective space $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ or, more generally, $\mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ where A is an arbitrary ring. As projective space is covered by affine open sets of the form $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ , on which the differentials form a rank n locally free sheaf, $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathtt { n } } / A }$ is also a rank n locally free sheaf.
21.3.6. Theorem (the Euler exact sequence) — The sheaf of differentials $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } _ { A } ^ { \mathtt { n } } / A }$ satisfies the following exact sequence:

$$
0 \longrightarrow \Omega_ {\mathbb {P} _ {\lambda} ^ {n} / A} \xrightarrow {\alpha} \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P} _ {\lambda} ^ {n}} (- 1) ^ {\oplus (n + 1)} \xrightarrow {\phi} \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P} _ {\lambda} ^ {n}} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

$$

21.3.9. Generalizations of the Euler exact sequence. Generalizations of the Euler exact sequence are quite useful. We won’t use them later, so no proofs will be given. First, the argument generalizes readily if Spec A is replaced by an arbitrary base scheme. The Euler exact sequence generalizes further in a number of ways. As a first step, suppose $\mathcal { V }$ is a rank $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ locally free sheaf (or vector bundle) on a scheme X. Suppose $\pi { : \mathbb { P } } { \mathcal { V } }  X$ is the corresponding projective bundle. (See §17.2.3 for the definition of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathcal { V } }$ , including a caution about duals.) Then $\Omega _ { \mathbb { P } } \nu _ { / X }$ sits in an Euler exact sequence:

text:
$$
0 \longrightarrow \Omega_ {\mathbb {P V} / X} \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P V}} (- 1) \otimes \pi^ {*} \mathcal {V} ^ {\vee} \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {P V}} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

It is not obvious that this is useful, but we have already implicitly seen it in the case of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ -bundles over curves, in Exercise 20.2.J, where the normal bundle to a section was identified in this way.

21.4 The Riemann–Hurwitz Formula

The Riemann–Hurwitz formula generalizes our calculation of the genus g of a double cover of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ branched at $2 { \mathfrak { g } } + 2$ points, Theorem 19.5.1, to higher degree covers, and to higher genus target curves.
Chapter 21 Differentials

21.4.1. Definition. A finite morphism between integral schemes $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is said to be separable if it is dominant, and the induced extension of function fields ${ \sf K } ( { \sf X } ) / { \sf K } ( { \sf Y } )$ is a separable extension. Similarly, a generically finite morphism is generically separable if it is dominant, and the induced extension of function fields is a (finite) separable extension. Note that finite morphisms of integral schemes are automatically separable in characteristic 0.

21.4.2. Proposition — If π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a generically separable morphism of irreducible varieties of the same dimension n, and Y is smooth, then the cotangent exact sequence (Theorem 21.2.24) is exact on the left as well:
This is an example of left-exactness of the cotangent exact sequence in the presence of appropriate “smoothness”; see Remark 21.2.10.

Proof. We must check that $\Phi$ is injective. Now $\Omega _ { \mathrm { Y / k } }$ is a rank n locally free sheaf on Y, so $\pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \mathrm { Y / k } }$ is a rank n locally free sheaf on X. A locally free sheaf on an integral scheme (such as $\pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \mathrm { Y / k } }$ ) is torsion-free (any nonzero section over any open set is nonzero at the generic point; see §6.1.4), so if a subsheaf of it (such as ker $\Phi$ ) is nonzero, it is nonzero at the generic point. Thus to show the injectivity of $\Phi .$ , we need only check that $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ is an inclusion at the generic point. We thus tensor with ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ,$ where $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { \Pi } } \boldsymbol { \mathrm { \Pi } }$ is the generic point of X. Tensoring with $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is an exact functor (localization is exact, Exercise 1.5.G), and $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \otimes \Omega _ { X / Y } = 0$ (as ${ \sf K } ( { \sf X } ) / { \sf K } ( { \sf Y } )$ is a separable extension by hypothesis, and $\Omega$ for separable field extensions is 0 by Exercise 21.2.H(a)). Also, $\theta _ { \mathfrak { n } } \otimes \pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / \mathfrak { k } }$ and $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \otimes \Omega _ { X / \mathfrak { k } }$ are both n-dimensional $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ -vector spaces (they are the stalks of rank n locally free sheaves at the generic point). Thus by considering

$$
\mathcal {O} _ {\eta} \otimes \pi^ {*} \Omega_ {Y / k} \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {\eta} \otimes \Omega_ {X / k} \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {\eta} \otimes \Omega_ {X / Y} \longrightarrow 0
$$

(which is ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \oplus \mathfrak { n } } \to { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \oplus \mathfrak { n } } \to 0 \to 0 ;$ ) we see that $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \otimes \pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / \mathfrak { k } } \longrightarrow \mathcal { O } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \otimes \Omega _ { X / \mathfrak { k } }$ is injective, and thus that $\pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / { \bf k } }  \Omega _ { X / { \bf k } }$ is injective. □

People not confined to characteristic 0 should note what goes wrong for non-separable morphisms. For example, suppose $\mathrm { k }$ is a field of characteristic p, and consider the map $\pi \colon \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } =$ $\mathrm { S p e c k [ t ] }  \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 1 } = \mathrm { S p e c k [ u ] }$ given by $\mathrm { u } = \mathrm { t } ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ . Then $\Omega _ { \pi }$ is the trivial invertible sheaf generated by dt. As another (similar but different) example, if ${ \sf K } = { \sf k } ( { \sf x } )$ and ${ \sf K } ^ { \prime } = { \sf k } ( { \sf x } ^ { \sf p } )$ , then the inclusion $K ^ { \prime } \hookrightarrow K$ induces π : Spec K[t] Spec K0[t]. Once again, $\Omega _ { \pi }$ is an invertible sheaf, generated by $\mathrm { d } x$ (which in this case is pulled back from $\Omega _ { \sf K / K ^ { \prime } }$ on Spec K). In both of these cases, we have maps from one affine line to another, and there are nonzero relative tangent vectors.

21.4.A. EXERCISE. If X and Y are smooth varieties of dimension ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is generically separable, show that the locus of non-smoothness is pure codimension 1, and has a natural interpretation as an effective divisor, as follows. Interpret $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ as an $n \times n$ Jacobian matrix (13.1.7.1) in appropriate local coordinates, and hence interpret the locus where $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ is not an isomorphism as (locally) the vanishing scheme of the determinant of an ${ \mathfrak { n } } \times { \mathfrak { n } }$ matrix.

We call the locus of non-smoothness (in X) the ramification locus, and its image (in Y) the branch locus. Because of this exercise, we use the terms ramification divisor and (assuming the image of the ramification divisor is indeed a divisor) branch divisor.

Before getting down to our case of interest, dimension 1, we begin with something (literally) small but fun. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a surjective k-morphism from a smooth k-scheme that contracts a subset of codimension greater than 1. More precisely, suppose $\pi$ is an isomorphism over an open subset of Y, from an open subset U of X whose complement has codimension greater than 1. Then by Exercise 21.4.A, Y cannot be smooth. (Small resolutions, to be defined in Exercise 22.4.N, are examples of such π. In particular, you can find an example there.)
21.4 The Riemann–Hurwitz Formula

Suppose now that X and Y are dimension 1. Then the ramification locus is a finite set (ramification points) of $\Sigma ,$ and the branch locus is a finite set (branch points) of Y. (Figure 21.4 shows a morphism with two ramification points and one branch point.) Now assume that $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ . We examine $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ near a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ .

As motivation for what we will see, we note that in complex geometry, nonconstant maps from (complex) curves to curves may be written in appropriate local coordinates as $x \mapsto$ ${ \mathfrak { x } } ^ { \mathfrak { m } } = { \mathfrak { y } } .$ , from which we see that dy pulls back to $\operatorname* { m x } ^ { \mathrm { m - 1 } } \mathrm { d } x ,$ → so $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ locally looks like functions times dx modulo multiples of $\mathrm { m } \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \mathrm { m } - 1 } \mathrm { d } \boldsymbol { x }$ .

text: Consider now our map π: $\mathsf X \to \mathsf Y ,$ , and fix ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ and ${ \mathfrak { q } } = \pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ . Because the construction of $\Omega$ behaves well under base change (Theorem 21.2.26(b)), we may replace Y with Spec of the local ring $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , { \bf q } }$ at q, i.e., we may assume $\mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { S p e c B }$ , where B is a discrete valuation ring (as Y is a regular curve), with residue field $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ corresponding to q. By restricting to an affine open neighborhood of p in X, we assume that X is affine too. Similarly, as the construction of $\Omega$ behaves well with respect to localization (Exercise 21.2.L), we may replace X by Spec ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X , { \mathrm { p } } } ,$ and thus assume ${ \mathsf { X } } { = } { \mathsf { S p e c } } { \mathsf { A } } ,$ , where A is a discrete valuation ring, and $\pi$ corresponds to $\mathrm { B }  \mathrm { A } ,$ inducing an isomorphism of residue fields (with $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \boldsymbol { \bar { \mathrm { k } } }$ ).
Suppose their uniformizers are s and $\mathrm { t , }$ respectively, with $\mathbf { t } \mapsto \mathbf { u } s ^ { \mathbf { n } }$ , where u is an invertible element of A.
Recall that the differentials of a discrete valuation ring over k are generated by d of the uniformizer (Exercise 21.3.E). Then

$$
d t = d \left(u s ^ {n}\right) = u n s ^ {n - 1} d s + s ^ {n} d u.
$$

This differential on Spec A vanishes to order at least $n - 1$ , and precisely $n - 1$ if n is not divisible by the characteristic. If the order is $\mathfrak { n } - 1$ , we call it tame ramification, while if the order is greater, it is called wild ramification. We define one plus this order to be the the ramification index at this point of X.

21.4.B. EXERCISE. Show that the degree of $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ at $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ is precisely the ramification order of $\pi$ at p, minus 1. (The degree of a coherent sheaf on a curve was defined in §18.4.6. You will need to make sense of the length of a coherent sheaf at a point; see, for example, Exercise 6.5.M.)

21.4.C. EXERCISE: INTERPRETING THE RAMIFICATION DIVISOR IN TERMS OF NUMBER OF PREIMAGES. For this exercise, we take the base field k to be algebraically closed. Suppose all the ramification above $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ is tame (which is always true in characteristic 0). Show that the degree of the branch divisor at q is $\deg \pi - | \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { q } ) |$ . Thus the multiplicity of the branch divisor counts the extent to which the number of preimages is less than the degree (see Figure 21.4).

21.4.4. Example. The degree of R is always even: any cover of a curve must be branched over an even number of points (counted with appropriate multiplicity).
21.4.E. EASY EXERCISE. Show that there is no nonconstant map from a smooth projective irreducible genus 2 curve to a smooth projective irreducible genus 3 curve. (Hint: $\deg \ R \geq 0 .$ .)

21.4.5. Definition. If the only connected finite étale cover of a connected scheme X is an isomorphism, we say that X is simply connected in the étale topology. Do you see why “simply connected” is a reasonable name for the concept? We won’t use this terminology beyond the following brief discussion.

21.4.6. Example $( ^ { \prime \prime } \mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { 1 }$ is simply connected”). If $\operatorname { k } = { \overline { { \operatorname { k } } } } ,$ the only connected unbranched finite separable cover of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ is the isomorphism, for the following reason. Suppose X is connected and $\Chi \to { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } }$ has no ramification. Then X is a curve, and regular by Exercise 24.8.F(a). (Feel free to take this for granted, since this exercise is in the future. There will be no circularity.) Applying the Riemann– Hurwitz formula, using that the ramification divisor is 0, we have $2 - 2 { \mathfrak { g } } x = 2 { \mathrm { d } } ,$ with $\mathrm { d } \geq 1$ and ${ \mathfrak { g } } _ { X } \geq 0 ,$ , from which $\mathrm { d } = 1$ and ${ \mathfrak { g } } x = 0$ .

21.4.F. EXERCISE (“IN CHARACTERISTIC 0, $\mathbb { A } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { 1 }$ IS SIMPLY CONNECTED”). Show that if $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } = \overline { { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } }$ has characteristic 0, the only connected unbranched cover of $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ is itself. (Aside: In characteristic p, this needn’t hold; $\operatorname { S p e c k } [ \mathbf { x } , \mathbf { y } ] / ( \mathbf { y } ^ { \mathrm { p } } - \mathbf { y } - \mathbf { x } ) \to \operatorname { S p e c k } [ \mathbf { x } ]$ is such a map, as you can show yourself. Once the theory of the algebraic fundamental group is developed, this translates to: $^ { \prime \prime } \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ is not simply connected in characteristic p.” This cover is an example of an Artin-Schreier cover. Fun fact: The group $\mathbb { Z } / { \mathfrak { p } }$ acts on this cover via the map $y \mapsto y + 1 .$ . This is an example of a Galois cover; you can check that the extension of function fields is Galois.)

21.4.G. UNIMPORTANT EXERCISE. Extend Example 21.4.6 and Exercise 21.4.F, by removing the $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ hypothesis, and changing “connected” to “geometrically connected.”

21.4.7. Example: Lüroth’s Theorem. Continuing the notation of Theorem 21.4.3, suppose ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( X ) = 0$ . Then from the Riemann–Hurwitz formula (21.4.3.1), ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathsf { Y } } ) = 0$ . (Otherwise, if ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathsf { Y } } )$ were at least 1, then the right side of the Riemann–Hurwitz formula would be nonnegative, and thus couldn’t be $^ { - 2 , }$ which is the left side.) Informally: the only maps from a genus 0 curve to a curve of positive genus are the constant maps. This has a nonobvious algebraic consequence, by our identification of covers of curves with field extensions (Theorem 16.3.3): all subfields of $\ k ( x )$ containing k are of the form $\mathbb { k } ( \mathfrak { y } ) .$ , where ${ \mathfrak { y } } = { \mathfrak { f } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } )$ for some ${ \sf f } \in { \sf k } ( { \sf x } )$ .
(It turns out that the hypotheses char ${ \mathrm { k } } = 0$ and $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ are not necessary.) This is Lüroth’s Theorem.

21.4.H. EXERCISE. Use Lüroth’s Theorem to give new geometric solutions to Exercises 7.5.K and 7.5.M. (These arguments will be less ad hoc, and more suitable for generalization, than the algebraic solutions suggested in the hints to those exercises.)

21.4.I.* EXERCISE (GEOMETRIC GENUS EQUALS TOPOLOGICAL GENUS). Suppose C is an irreducible regular projective complex curve. Show that there is an algebraic nonconstant map $\tau : { \mathrm { C } } \to { \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 } }$ . Describe the corresponding map of Riemann surfaces. Use the Riemann–Hurwitz formula to show that the algebraic notion of genus (as computed using the branched cover $\pi$ ) agrees with the topological notion of

genus (using the same branched cover). (Recall that assuming Miracle 18.5.2—that the canonical bundle is Serre-dualizing—we know that the geometric genus equals the arithmetic genus, Exercise 21.3.A.)

21.4.8. Informal example: The degree of the discriminant of degree d polynomials in one variable. You may be aware that there is a degree $2 \mathrm { d } - 2$ polynomial in the coefficients $\mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { d } } , \ldots , \mathbf { a } _ { 0 }$ of the degree d polynomial

text: that vanishes precisely when $\mathsf { f } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ has a multiple root. For example, when ${ \mathrm { d } } = 2$ , the discriminant is $\mathfrak { a } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 4 \mathfrak { a } _ { 0 } \mathfrak { a } _ { 2 }$ . We can “compute” this degree $2 \mathrm { d } - 2$ using the Riemann–Hurwitz formula as follows. (You should try to make sense of the following informal and imprecise discussion.) We work over an algebraically closed field k of characteristic 0 for the sake of simplicity. If we take two general degree d polynomials, ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( x )$ and $\mathrm { h } ( x )$ , the degree of the discriminant “should be” the number of $\lambda \in \mathbb { k }$ for which ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } ) - \lambda { \mathrm { h } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } )$ has a double root. Consider the morphism $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ given by $x \mapsto [ { \mathfrak { g } } ( x ) , { \mathfrak { h } } ( x ) ]$ . (Here we use “affine coordinates” on the source $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \colon$ by $x$ we mean $[ x , 1 ]$ →.) Then this morphism has degree d. A ramification point a mapping to the branch point [λ, 1] in the target $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ corresponds to a being a double root of ${ \mathfrak { g } } ( x ) - \lambda { \mathrm { h } } ( x )$ . Thus the number of branch points should be the desired degree of the discriminant. By the Riemann–Hurwitz formula there are $2 \mathrm { d } - 2$ branch points (admittedly, with multiplicity). It is possible to turn this into a proof, and it is interesting to do so.
21.4.9.* Bounds on automorphism groups of curves.

It is a nontrivial fact that irreducible smooth projective curves of genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 2$ have finite automorphism groups. (See [FK, §5.1] for a proof over C. See [Schm] for the first proof in arbitrary characteristic, although better approaches are now available.) Granting this fact, we can show that in characteristic 0, the automorphism group has order at most $8 4 ( { \mathfrak { g } } - 1 )$ (Hurwitz’s Automorphisms Theorem), as follows.

Suppose C is an irreducible smooth projective curve over an algebraically closed field $\mathrm { k } = \overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ of characteristic 0, of genus ${ \mathfrak { g } } \geq 2 .$ . Suppose that G is a finite group of automorphisms of C. We now show that $\left| \operatorname { G } \right| \leq 8 4 ( { \mathfrak { g } } - 1 )$ . (The case where $\mathrm { k }$ is not algebraically closed is quickly dispatched by base-changing to $\overline { { \mathrm { k } } }$ )

For example [Stic], in characteristic p, if $\mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is a prime power that is not 2, then the completion (“compactification”) of the affine curve $\boldsymbol { \ y } ^ { \mathrm { p } ^ { \mathrm { n } } } = \boldsymbol { x } + \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \mathrm { p } ^ { \mathrm { n } } + \hat { 1 } }$ n = x + xpn+1 has genus ${ \mathfrak { g } = \mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ( \mathfrak { p } ^ { \mathrm { n } } - 1 ) / 2 }$ and automorphism group of order $\mathsf { p } ^ { 3 \mathrm { n } } ( \mathsf { p } ^ { 3 \mathrm { n } } + 1 ) ( \mathsf { p } ^ { 2 \mathrm { n } } - 1 )$ .
21.5 Understanding Smooth Varieties Using Their Cotangent Bundles

In this section, we construct birational invariants of varieties over algebraically closed fields (such as the geometric genus), motivate the notion of an unramified morphism, show that varieties are “smooth almost everywhere,” and get a first glimpse of Hodge theory.
21.5.1. The geometric genus, and other birational invariants from i-forms.

Suppose X is a projective scheme over k. Then $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \Omega _ { \mathsf { X } / \mathsf { k } } )$ is an invariant of X. In fact it and related invariants are birational invariants if X is smooth, as shown in the following Exercise 21.5.A. We first define the sheaf of (relative) i-forms $\Omega _ { X / Y } ^ { \mathrm { i } } { : = } \wedge ^ { \mathrm { i } } \Omega _ { X / Y }$ . Sections of $\Omega _ { X / Y } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ (over some open set) are called (relative) i-forms (over that open set).
21.5.2. Joke. Old MacDonald had a form; $e _ { \mathrm { i } } \wedge e _ { \mathrm { i } } = 0$ .

list_items: 21.5.A. EXERCISE $( \mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \Omega _ { \mathsf { X } / \mathsf { K } } ^ { \mathrm { I } } )$ ARE BIRATIONAL INVARIANTS). Suppose X and $x ^ { \prime }$ are birational irreducible smooth projective $\mathrm { k }$ -varieties. By pulling back i-forms from $X ^ { \prime }$ to U, we get a map $\Phi ^ { * } \colon \mathsf { H } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ( X ^ { \prime } , \Omega _ { X ^ { \prime } / \mathtt k } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { i } } )  \mathsf { H } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ( \mathsf { U } , \Omega _ { X / \mathtt k } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { i } } )$ . If $\Psi \colon X ^ { \prime } \to X$ is the inverse rational map, we similarly get a map $\psi ^ { * } \colon \mathsf { H } ^ { \circ } ( X , \Omega _ { X / \updownarrow } ^ { \mathrm { i } } )  \mathsf { H } ^ { \circ } ( X ^ { \prime } , \Omega _ { X ^ { \prime } / \updownarrow } ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ . 21.5.3. The canonical bundle $\mathcal { H } _ { X }$ and the geometric genus ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 9 } ( X )$ . If X is a dimension n smooth k-variety, the invertible sheaf (or line bundle) det $\Omega _ { X / \mathrm { k } } = \Omega _ { X / \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (the sheaf of “algebraic volume forms”) has particular importance, and is called the canonical (invertible) sheaf, or the canonical (line) bundle. It is denoted by $\mathcal { K } _ { \sf X }$ (or $\mathcal { H } _ { \mathrm { X / k } }$ ). As mentioned in §18.5.2, if X is projective, then $\mathcal { K } _ { \sf X }$ is the canonical sheaf $\omega _ { X }$ appearing in the statement of Serre duality, something we will establish in §29.4 (see Desideratum 29.1.1).
21.5.B. EXERCISE (THE ADJUNCTION FORMULA FOR $\mathcal { K } _ { \sf X }$ ). Suppose $x$ is a smooth variety, and Z is a smooth subvariety of X. Show that

text:
$$
\mathcal {K} _ {Z} \cong \mathcal {K} _ {X} | _ {Z} \otimes \det  \mathcal {N} _ {Z / X}.
$$
(Hint: Apply Exercise 14.2.P to Theorem 21.2.31.) In particular, by Exercise 21.2.J, if Z is codimension 1, then

$$
\mathcal {K} _ {\mathbb {Z}} \cong (\mathcal {K} _ {\mathbb {X}} \otimes \mathcal {O} _ {\mathbb {X}} (Z)) | _ {\mathbb {Z}},
$$
21.5 Understanding Smooth Varieties Using Their Cotangent Bundles

which is more commonly and compactly written as $\mathcal { H } _ { X } ( Z ) | _ { Z } .$ . The adjunction formula is often used inductively, for complete intersections; see Exercise 21.5.G for an example. (See Exercise 20.2.B(a) and §29.4 for other versions of the adjunction formula.)

21.5.4. Definition. If X is a projective (or even proper) smooth k-variety, the birational invariant $\mathtt { h } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { H } _ { \mathsf { X } } ) = \mathtt { h } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \Omega _ { \mathsf { X } / \mathtt { k } } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathfrak { n } } )$ has particular importance. It is called the geometric genus, and is denoted by ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 9 } ( X )$ . We saw this in the case of curves in §21.3.1. If X is an irreducible variety that is not smooth or projective, the phrase geometric genus refers to $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf X ^ { \prime } , \mathcal H _ { \mathsf X ^ { \prime } } )$ for some smooth projective $X ^ { \prime }$ birational to X. (By Exercise 21.5.A, this is independent of the choice of $x ^ { \prime }$ .) For example, if $x$ is an irreducible reduced projective curve over ${ \sf k } ,$ the geometric genus is the geometric genus of the normalization of X. (But in higher dimension, it is not clear if there exists such an $x ^ { \prime }$ . It is a nontrivial fact that this is true in characteristic 0, and it is not yet known in positive characteristic; see Remark 22.4.6.)

It is a miracle that for a complex curve the geometric genus is the same as the topological genus and the arithmetic genus. Exercise 18.5.A showed that the geometric and arithmetic genus are equal (assuming Miracle 18.5.2 that the canonical bundle is Serre-dualizing). We will connect the geometric genus to the topological genus in our discussion of the Riemann–Hurwitz formula soon (Exercise 21.4.I).

21.5.C. EXERCISE. Suppose Z is a regular degree d surface in $\mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { 3 } .$ . Compute the geometric genus ${ \sf p _ { 9 } } ( Z )$ of Z. Show that no regular quartic surface in $\mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { 3 }$ is rational (i.e., birational to $\mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } ^ { \prime } } ^ { 2 }$ Definition 7.5.5). (Such quartic surfaces are examples of K3 surfaces; see Exercise 21.5.I.)
21.5.5. Important classes of varieties: Fano, Calabi-Yau, general type.

Suppose X is a smooth projective k-variety. Then X is said to be Fano if $\mathcal { H } _ { X } ^ { \vee }$ is ample, and Calabi-Yau if $\mathcal { K } _ { X } \cong \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ . (Caution: There are other definitions of Calabi-Yau.)

21.5.D. EXERCISE. For all $\geq 0$ , the jth plurigenus of a smooth projective k-variety is $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { K } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { j } } )$ . Show that the jth plurigenus is a birational invariant.

(By contrast, $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( X , \mathcal { H } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { j } } )$ is not a birational invariant if $\mathrm { j } < 0$ . For example, you can show that $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { H } ^ { \vee } )$ differs for $X = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ and $X = \mathrm { B l _ { p } } \ : \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ .)

The Kodaira dimension of $\Sigma ,$ denoted by $\kappa ( \boldsymbol { \chi } )$ , tracks the rate of growth of the plurigenera. It is the smallest k such that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \circ } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { K } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { j } } ) / \mathsf { j } ^ { \mathsf { k } }$ is bounded (as j varies through the positive integers), except that if all the plurigenera $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { H } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { j } } )$ are 0, we say $\kappa ( X ) = - 1 .$ It is a nontrivial fact that the Kodaira dimension always exists, and that it is the maximum of the dimensions of images of the jth “pluricanonical rational maps.” The latter fact implies that the Kodaira dimension is an integer between $^ { - 1 }$ and dim X inclusive. Exercise 21.5.D shows that the Kodaira dimension is a birational invariant. If $\kappa ( X ) = \dim X ,$ , we say that $x$ is of general type. (For more information on the Kodaira dimension, see [Ii, §10.5].)
21.5.E. EXERCISE. Show that if $\mathcal { K } _ { \sf X }$ is ample then X is of general type.
21.5.F. EXERCISE. Show that a smooth geometrically irreducible projective curve (over a field k) is Fano (resp., Calabi-Yau, general type) if its genus is 0 (resp., 1, greater than 1).

The “trichotomy” of Exercise 21.5.F is morally the reason that curves behave differently depending on which of the three classes they lie in (see §19.6.1). In some sense, important aspects of this trichotomy extend to higher dimension, which is part of the reason for making these definitions. We now explore this trichotomy in the case of complete intersections.

21.5.G. EXERCISE. Suppose X is a smooth complete intersection in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathsf { N } }$ of hypersurfaces of degree $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { d } _ { n } ,$ where $\mathrm { k }$ is algebraically closed.

list_items: (a) Show that $\mathcal { H } _ { X } \cong \mathcal { O } ( - \mathsf { N } - 1 + \mathsf { d } _ { 1 } + \cdots + \mathsf { d } _ { \mathsf { n } } ) | _ { X }$

(b) Show that if $- \mathsf { N } - 1 + \mathrm { d } _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \mathrm { d } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ is negative (resp., zero, positive) then X is Fano (resp., Calabi-Yau, general type).

21.5.H. EASY EXERCISE. A smooth Fano surface is called a del Pezzo surface (part of the Enriques–Kodaira classification of surfaces). Show that the following surfaces are del Pezzo: (a) $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ ; (b) $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 } ;$ and (c) smooth surfaces in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 }$ of degree at most 3. (This list has some redundancy! The remaining examples of del Pezzo surfaces are described in Remark 27.3.7. One of them appeared earlier in §19.8.3.)

21.5.I. EXERCISE. A K3 surface over a field $\mathrm { k }$ is a proper smooth geometrically connected Calabi-Yau surface X over k such that $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) = 0$ . (Weil has written that K3 surfaces were named in honor of Kummer, Kähler, and Kodaira, and the mountain K2, [BPV, p. 288].) 21.5.6. Tantalizing side remark. If you compare the degrees of the hypersurfaces cutting out complete intersection K3 surfaces (in Exercise 21.5.G(c)), with the degrees of hypersurfaces cutting out complete intersection canonical curves (see $\ S 1 9 . 8 . 2$ and Exercise 19.8.E), you will notice a remarkable coincidence. Of course, this is not a coincidence at all.

21.5.J. EXERCISE (NOT EVERYTHING FITS INTO THIS TRICHOTOMY). Suppose C is a smooth projective irreducible complex curve of genus greater than 1. Show that $\mathsf { C } \times _ { \mathbb { C } } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ is neither Fano, nor Calabi-Yau, nor general type. Hint: Exercise 21.2.U.
21.5.7.* Kodaira vanishing.
The Kodaira vanishing theorem is an important tool in a number of different areas of algebraic geometry. We state it for the sake of culture, but do not prove it (and hence will not use it).

21.5.8. The Kodaira Vanishing Theorem — Suppose k is a field of characteristic 0, and $x$ is a smooth projective k-variety. Then for any ample invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ , ${ \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , \mathcal { H } _ { X } \otimes \mathcal { L } ) = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ .

21.5.9. The restriction on characteristic is necessary; Raynaud gave an example where Kodaira vanishing fails in positive characteristic, [Ra]. The original proof is by proving the complex-analytic version, and then transferring it into a complex algebraic statement using Serre’s GAGA Theorem [Se3]. The general characteristic 0 case can be reduced to $\mathbb { C }$ —any reduction of this sort is often called (somewhat vaguely) an application of the Lefschetz principle. (For a precise formulation of the Lefschetz principle, with a short proof that applies in any characteristic, see [Ek]. See [FR] for a generalization. See [MO90551] for more context.) Raynaud later gave a dramatic algebraic proof of Kodaira vanishing using positive characteristic (!); see [DeI] or [Il].

21.5.K. EXERCISE. Prove the Kodaira Vanishing Theorem in the case $\dim X = 1$ , and for $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . (Neither case requires the characteristic 0 hypothesis.)
21.5.10.* A first glimpse of Hodge theory.

The invariant $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { j } } ( \mathsf { X } , \Omega _ { \mathsf { X } / \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ is called the Hodge number $\mathfrak { h } ^ { \mathrm { i , j } } ( X )$ . By Exercise 21.5.A, $\mathrm { h } ^ { \mathrm i , 0 }$ are birational invariants. We will soon see (in Exercise 21.5.O) that this isn’t true for all $\mathrm { h } ^ { \mathrm { i , j } }$ .

21.5.L. EXERCISE. Suppose X is a smooth projective variety. Assuming Miracle 18.5.2 (that the canonical bundle is Serre-dualizing), show that Hodge numbers satisfy the symmetry $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathsf { p } , \mathsf { q } } =$ $\mathfrak { h } ^ { \mathfrak { n } - \mathfrak { p } , \mathfrak { n } - \mathfrak { q } }$ . (Exercise 14.2.O will be useful.)

Show that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathsf { p } , \mathsf { q } } \left( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } \right) = 1$ if $0 \leq { \mathfrak { p } } = { \mathfrak { q } } \leq { \mathfrak { n } }$ and $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathsf { p } , \mathsf { q } } \left( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } \right) { = } 0$ otherwise. Hint: Use the Euler exact sequence (Theorem 21.3.6) and apply Exercise 14.2.N.
21.5 Understanding Smooth Varieties Using Their Cotangent Bundles

21.5.12.* Aside: Infinitesimal deformations and automorphisms.

It is beyond the scope of this book to make this precise, but if X is a variety, $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { T } _ { \mathsf { X } } )$ parametrizes infinitesimal automorphisms of $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ and $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { T } _ { \mathsf { X } } )$ parametrizes infinitesimal deformations. As an

text: 21.6 Generic Smoothness, and Consequences
We can now verify something you may already have intuited. In positive characteristic, this is a hard theorem, in that it uses a result from commutative algebra that we have not proved.

21.6.1. Theorem (generic smoothness of varieties) — If X is an integral finite type k-scheme over a perfect field k of dimension n, there is a dense open subset U of X such that U is smooth (over k) of dimension n.

Hence, by Exercise 13.2.R combined with Fact 13.8.2 (localization of regular is regular), U is regular. Theorem 21.6.4 will generalize this to smooth morphisms, at the expense of restricting to characteristic 0.
See §13.2.9 for an example of what goes wrong if k is not perfect.
Proof. The $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ case is immediate, so we assume $\mathfrak { n } > 0$ .

We will show that the rank at the generic point is n. Then by upper semicontinuity of the rank of a coherent sheaf (Exercise 14.3.J), it must be n in an open neighborhood of the generic point, and we are done.

We thus have to check that if K is the fraction field of a dimension n integral finite type kscheme, i.e., (by Theorem 12.2.1) if $\mathsf { K } / \mathsf { k }$ is a transcendence degree n extension, then $\Omega _ { \mathsf { K / k } }$ is an n-dimensional vector space. But every finitely generated extension of a perfect field is separably generated (Theorem 10.5.17), so the result follows by Exercise 21.2.M(b). □

21.6.2. Proof of Theorem 13.8.3, that the localization of a regular local ring is regular in the case of varieties over perfect fields. Theorem 21.6.1 allows us to prove Theorem 13.8.3 (an important case of Fact 13.8.2).

Suppose Y is a variety over a perfect field k that is regular at its closed points (so Y is smooth, by Exercise 13.2.R), and let η be a point of Y. We will show that Y is regular at η. Let $x = \overline { { \eta } }$ . By Theorem 21.6.1, X contains a dense $\left( = \right.$ nonempty) open subset of smooth points. By shrinking Y by discarding the points of X outside that open subset, we may assume X is smooth.

Then Theorem 21.2.31 (exactness of the conormal exact sequence for smooth varieties) implies that $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ is a locally free sheaf of rank codim $\mathsf { \Pi } _ { \mathsf { X / Y } } = \dim { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } , \mathsf { \Pi } }$ .

list_items: 21.6.A. EXERCISE. Let m be the maximal ideal of $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \mathfrak { \eta } }$ . Identify the stalk of $\mathcal { I } / \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 }$ at the generic point η of X with ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ . Conclude the proof of Theorem 13.8.3. □

21.6.B. TRICKY EXERCISE (LOCALIZATION OF REGULAR LOCAL RINGS OF VARIETIES ARE REGULAR, PROMISED JUST AFTER THEOREM 13.8.3). Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a regular local ring that is the localization of a finitely generated k-algebra, where $\mathtt { k }$ is perfect. Show that the localization of A at a prime is also a regular local ring.

text: 21.6 Generic Smoothness, and Consequences
21.6.3. Generic smoothness for morphisms, and the Kleiman–Bertini Theorem.
We will now discuss a number of important results that fall under the rubric of “generic smoothness for morphisms.” All require working in characteristic 0.
21.6.4. Theorem (generic smoothness on the source) —

list_items: (a) Suppose $\pi { : } X \to \Upsilon$ is a dominant finite type morphism of integral Noetherian schemes, such that char K(Y) $( =$ char K(X)) is zero. Then there is a nonempty $( =$ dense) open set U ⊂ X such that $\pi | _ { \mathrm { U } }$ is smooth of relative dimension trdeg K(X)/K(Y).

(b) Let k be a field of characteristic 0, and let π : $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ be a dominant morphism of integral k-varieties. Then there is a nonempty $( =$ dense) open set U ⊂ X such that $\pi | _ { \mathrm { U } }$ is smooth of relative dimension dim X − dim Y.

text: 21.6.C. EXERCISE (PROMISED IN EXERCISE 12.2.D). Suppose $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathsf { n } } \in \mathbf { k } [ \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } ] ,$ where $\mathrm { k }$ is a field of characteristic 0. Show that $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } _ { { n } }$ are algebraically dependent if and only if the determinant of the Jacobian matrix is identically zero. Hint: Why is this exercise given just after Theorem 21.6.4?

21.6.5. Example. Theorem 21.6.4 fails in positive characteristic: consider the purely inseparable extension $\mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } } ( \mathrm { t } ) / \mathbb { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } } ( \mathrm { t } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } )$ . The same problem can arise even over an algebraically closed field of characteristic p: consider $\mathbb { A } _ { \sf k } ^ { 1 } = \sf { S p e c k [ t ] }  \sf { S p e c k [ u ] } = \mathbb { A } _ { \sf k } ^ { 1 } ,$ given by $\mathfrak { u } \mapsto \mathfrak { t } ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ .

Proof. Let $\mathfrak { n } { = } \mathrm { t r d e g } \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { X } ) / \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { Y } )$ . We may replace Y by an affine open subset Spec B, and then replace X by an affine open subset Spec A, so $\pi$ corresponds to the ring map $\mathrm { B } \to A$ . Choose n elements $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \in A$ that form a transcendence basis for ${ \sf K } ( \sf A ) / { \sf K } ( \sf B )$ . (Do you see why we may choose them to lie in A?) Choose additional elements ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathfrak { n } + 1 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathsf { N } } \in { \mathfrak { A } }$ so that $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { N }$ generate A as a B-algebra.

For $\mathrm { i } = 0 , \ldots , \mathrm { n , }$ let $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ be the subfield of K(A) generated over K(B) by $\mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , . . . , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . For example, $\mathsf { F } _ { 0 } = \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } )$ , $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathsf { N } } = \mathsf { K } ( \boldsymbol { A } ) ,$ and $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { i } } / \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } )$ is a purely transcendental field extension for ${ \mathfrak { i } } \leq { \mathfrak { n } }$ . For $\mathfrak { i } = \mathfrak { n } +$ $1 , \ldots , \Nu ,$ , the minimal polynomial for ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in { \mathsf { K } } ( A )$ over $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 }$ (an element of $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 }$ [t]) may be interpreted as $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } } ( { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { x } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 } , \mathrm { t } )$ for some $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) [ \mathsf { y } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathsf { y } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 }$ , t]. By multiplying $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ by the products of the denominators of its coefficients, we obtain $M _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathrm { B } [ { \mathfrak { y } } _ { 1 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { y } } _ { i - 1 } , \mathrm { t } ]$ so that $M _ { \mathrm { i } } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { \mathrm { i - 1 } } , \mathrm { t } ) \in$ $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 }$ [t] is also a minimal polynomial for ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in { \mathsf { K } } ( A )$ over $\mathsf { F } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 }$ . Thus
where

$$
I = \left(M _ {n + 1} \left(x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n}, y _ {n + 1}\right), M _ {n + 2} \left(x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n}, y _ {n + 1}, y _ {n + 2}\right), \dots , M _ {N} \left(x _ {1}, \dots , x _ {n}, y _ {n + 1}, \dots , y _ {N}\right)\right).
$$

Let $X ^ { \prime } { : = } S p e c A ^ { \prime }$ . Let $Z _ { 1 , }$ , …, $Z _ { s }$ be the irreducible components of $x ^ { \prime }$ . (There are finitely many by Noetherianity of B: Exercise 3.6.T and Proposition 3.6.15.) Precisely one of them, say, $Z = Z _ { 1 }$ , dominates Spec B (because $\mathsf { A } ^ { \prime } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { B } ) \cong \mathsf { K } ( \mathsf { A } )$ by (21.6.5.1)). Let $\mathrm { u } ^ { \prime } \subset \mathrm { x } ^ { \prime }$ be the open subset $Z _ { 1 } \setminus Z _ { 1 } \cap ( Z _ { 2 } \cup \cdot \cdot \cdot Z _ { s } )$ .

At the generic point of $\mathrm { u } ^ { \prime }$ , the Jacobian matrix of $M _ { \mathfrak { n } + 1 } ( \mathfrak { x } _ { \mathfrak { n } + 1 } ) , \dots , M _ { \mathsf { N } } ( \mathfrak { x } _ { \mathsf { N } } )$ $M _ { \mathsf { N } } ( { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } } _ { \mathsf { N } } )$ with respect to $\boldsymbol { \times } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { \times } _ { \ N }$ has corank n. (Here we use the separability of the minimal polynomials $M _ { \mathrm { i } }$ .) Thus there is an open subset $\mathrm { u } ^ { \prime \prime } \subset \mathrm { u } ^ { \prime }$ where the Jacobian has corank ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ so $\mathrm { u } ^ { \prime \prime } {  } \mathrm { s p e c } \mathrm { B }$ is smooth of relative dimension n. Furthermore $\mathrm { U } ^ { \prime \prime }$ is irreducible. Now $\mathrm { ~ U ~ } ^ { \prime \prime }$ and $x$ are birational, so by Proposition 7.5.6 there is an open subset $\mathsf { u } \subset \mathsf { X }$ that is isomorphic to an open subset of $\mathrm { U } ^ { \prime \prime }$ . This is the open subset we seek.
