21.6.6. Theorem (generic smoothness on the target) — Suppose π: $\colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of k-varieties, where char $\ k = 0$ , and X is smooth (over k). Then there is a dense open subset U of Y such that $\left. \pi \right| _ { \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { U } ) }$ is a smooth morphism.
Note that $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } )$ may be empty! Indeed, if $\pi$ is not dominant, we will have to take such a U.
To prove Theorem 21.6.6, we use a neat trick. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes that are finite type over ${ \sf k } ,$ where char ${ \sf k } = 0$ . Define

$$
X _ {r} = \left\{p \in X: \operatorname {r a n k} \left(\pi^ {*} \Omega_ {Y / k} \rightarrow \Omega_ {X / k}\right) \mid_ {p} \leq r \right\}.
$$

21.6.E. EXERCISE. Show that $X _ { r }$ is a constructible subset of X. Hint: If X and Y are both smooth, show that the rank condition implies that $X _ { \mathrm { r } }$ is cut out by “determinantal equations,” or use an appropriate form of upper semicontinuity.
By Chevalley’s Theorem 8.4.2, $\pi ( X _ { \mathrm { r } } )$ is constructible, so we can make sense of dim $\pi ( X _ { \mathrm { r } } )$
21.6.7. Lemma — If π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes that are finite type over k, where char $\mathrm { k } = 0 \mathrm { \Omega }$ then dim $\pi ( X _ { \mathrm { r } } ) \leq r$ .

Proof. In this proof, we make repeated use of the identification of Zariski cotangent spaces at closed points with the fibers of cotangent sheaves, using Corollary 21.2.33. We can replace X by an irreducible component of $\mathsf { X } _ { \mathrm { r } } ,$ and Y by the closure of that component’s image in Y (with reduced subscheme structure; see §9.4.9). The resulting map will have all of X contained in $\mathsf { X } _ { \mathrm { r } } ,$ so we may as well assume ${ \boldsymbol { \mathsf { X } } } = { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { X } } } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ . This boils down to the following linear algebra observation: if a linear map $\rho \colon \mathsf { V } _ { 1 }  \mathsf { V } _ { 2 }$ has rank at most r, and $\mathrm { V _ { i } ^ { \prime } }$ is a quotient of ${ \mathrm { V } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ with $\rho$ sending $\mathsf { V } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ to ${ \sf V } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ,$ then the restriction of $\rho$ to $\mathsf { V } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ has rank at most that of $\rho$ itself. Thus we have a dominant morphism $\pi \colon X \to \mathsf { Y } ,$ and we wish to show that $\dim \Upsilon \leq r$ . By generic smoothness of varieties →(Theorem 21.6.1), Y has a dense open set that is smooth, so we may assume that Y is smooth. By generic smoothness on the source for $\pi \colon X \to \mathsf { Y } .$ , there is a nonempty open subset $\mathsf { u } \subset \mathsf { X }$ such that $\pi \colon \mathrm { U } \to \Upsilon$ is smooth. But then for any point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in \mathrm { { U } } ,$ , the cotangent map $\pi ^ { * } \Omega \gamma | _ { \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) } \to \Omega \chi | _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is injective (Exercise 21.2.S), and has rank at most r. Taking p to be a closed point, we have dim Y = $\dim _ { \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) } \Upsilon \leq \dim \Omega _ { \Upsilon } | _ { \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) } \leq \mathfrak { r } ,$ . □
There is not much left to do to prove the theorem.

21.6.8. Proof of Theorem 21.6.6. Reduce to the case where Y is smooth over $\mathrm { k }$ (by restricting to a smaller open set, using generic smoothness of Y, Theorem 21.6.4(b)). Say $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } = \dim \boldsymbol { \mathrm { Y } }$ . Now $\dim { \overline { { \pi ( X _ { n - 1 } ) } } } \leq n - 1$ by Lemma 21.6.7 , so remove $\overline { { \pi ( X _ { \mathfrak { n } - 1 } ) } }$ from Y as well. Then the rank of $\pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / { \bf k } }  \Omega _ { X / { \bf k } }$ is at least n for each closed point of X. But as Y is regular of dimension n, we have that $\pi { ^ * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / { \ k } } \to \Omega _ { X / { \ k } }$ is injective for every closed point of $\Sigma ,$ and hence $\pi ^ { * } \Omega _ { \Upsilon / { \bf k } }  \Omega _ { X / { \bf k } }$ is an injective map of sheaves (do you see why?). Thus $\pi$ is smooth by Exercise 24.8.G(b). □

21.6.9.** The Kleiman–Bertini Theorem. The same idea of bounding the dimension of some “bad locus” can be used to prove the Kleiman–Bertini Theorem 21.6.10 (due to Kleiman), which is useful in (for example) enumerative geometry. Throughout this discussion, $\operatorname { k } = { \overline { { \operatorname { k } } } } ,$ although the definitions and results can be generalized. Suppose G is a group variety (over $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ ), and we have a G-action on a variety X. We say that the action is transitive if it is transitive on closed points. A better definition (which you can show is equivalent) is that the action morphism $\mathsf { G } \times \mathsf { X } \to \mathsf { X }$ restricted to a fiber above any closed point of X is surjective. A variety X with a transitive G-action is said to be a homogeneous space for G. For example, G acts on itself by left-translation, and via this action G is a homogeneous space for itself.

21.6.F. EASY EXERCISE. Suppose G is a group variety over an algebraically closed field k of characteristic 0. Show that every homogeneous space X for G is smooth. (In particular, taking ${ \tt X } = { \sf G } _ { \mathrm { \Delta } }$ , we see that G is smooth.) Hint: X has a dense open set U that is smooth by Theorem 21.6.4(b), and G acts transitively on the closed points of $\Sigma ,$ so we can cover X with translates of U.
21.6 Generic Smoothness, and Consequences

21.6.10. The Kleiman–Bertini Theorem, [Kl2, Thm. 2] — Suppose X is homogeneous space for a group variety G (over a field $\mathbf { k } = \overline { { \mathbf { k } } }$ of characteristic 0). Suppose α : $\Upsilon  X$ and $\beta \colon Z \to X$ are morphisms from smooth k-varieties Y and Z.
(a) Then there is a nonempty open subset V ⊂ G such that for every $\sigma \in \mathsf { V } ( \kappa )$ , $\Upsilon \times _ { X } Z$ defined by
(Y is “translated by σ”) is smooth of dimension dim Y + dim Z − dim X (but possibly empty).
(b) Furthermore, there is a nonempty open subset V G such that
is a smooth morphism of relative dimension dim Y + dim Z − dim X over V.

The first time you hear this, you should think of the special case where $\Upsilon  X$ and $Z \to X$ are locally closed embeddings (Y and Z are smooth subvarieties of X). In this case, the Kleiman–Bertini theorem says that the second subvariety will meet a “general translate” of the first “transversely.” (There is no such thing as a smooth morphism of negative relative dimension. So what happens if dim $\Upsilon + \dim Z - \dim X$ is negative?)
Proof. It is more pleasant to describe this proof “backward,” by considering how we would prove it ourselves. We will use generic smoothness twice.
Clearly (b) implies (a), so we prove (b).

In order to show that the morphism (21.6.10.1) is smooth over a nonempty open set ${ \mathsf { V } } \subset { \mathsf { G } } ,$ , it would suffice to apply generic smoothness on the target (Theorem 21.6.6) to (21.6.10.1). Hence it suffices to show that $( \mathsf { G } \times _ { \mathsf { k } } \mathsf { Y } ) \times _ { \mathsf { X } } Z$ is a smooth k-variety. Now Z is smooth over ${ \sf k } ,$ so it suffices to show that $( \mathsf { G } \times _ { \mathsf { k } } \mathsf { Y } ) \times _ { \mathsf { X } } Z \to Z$ is a smooth morphism (as the composition of two smooth morphisms is smooth, Exercise 13.6.D). But this is obtained by base change from ${ \sf G } \times _ { \sf k } { \sf Y }  { \sf X } ,$ so it suffices to show that this latter morphism is smooth (as smoothness is preserved by base change).

Now $\mathsf { G } \times _ { \mathsf { k } } \mathsf { Y } \to X$ is a G-equivariant morphism. (By $^ { \prime \prime } G$ -equivariant,” we mean that the Gaction on both sides respects the morphism.) By generic smoothness of the target (Theorem 21.6.6), this is smooth over a dense open subset X. But then by transitivity of the G-action on X, this morphism is smooth everywhere.
21.6.G. EXERCISE. Refine the above argument to show the desired statement about relative dimension. □

In characteristic 0, Exercise 21.6.I is a good improvement on Bertini’s Theorem. For example, we don’t need $\mathcal { L }$ to be very ample, or X to be projective. But unlike Bertini’s Theorem, Exercise 21.6.I fails in positive characteristic, as demonstrated by the one-dimensional linear series $\{ \mathfrak { p } Q : \mathrm { Q } \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \}$ . This is essentially Example 21.6.5. (Do you see why this does not contradict Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2?)
21.7 Unramified Morphisms

Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes. The support of the quasicoherent sheaf $\Omega _ { \pi } = \Omega _ { X / Y }$ is called the ramification locus, and the image of its support, $\pi ( \mathrm { S u p p } \Omega _ { X / Y } )$ , is called the branch locus. If $\Omega _ { \pi } = 0$ , we say that $\pi$ is formally unramified, and if $\pi$ is also furthermore locally of finite type, we say $\pi$ is unramified. (Caution: There is some lack of consensus in the definition of “unramified”; “locally of finite type” is sometimes replaced by “locally of finite presentation,” which was the definition originally used in [Gr-EGA].)

(Thus if $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { n } }$ is the generic point of an integral scheme Y, then Spec $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \mathfrak { \eta } }  \Upsilon$ is formally unramified.)
(c) Show that finite separable field extensions (or, more correctly, the corresponding maps of schemes) are unramified.

21.7.1. Arithmetic side remark: The different and the discriminant. If B is the ring of integers in a number field (§10.7.1), the different ideal of B is the annihilator of $\Omega _ { \mathrm { B / \mathbb { Z } } }$ . It measures how “(un)ramified” Spec $\mathrm { B \to S p e c \mathbb { Z } }$ is, and is a ring-theoretic version of the ramification locus. The discriminant ideal can be interpreted as the ideal of $\mathbb { Z }$ corresponding to effective divisor on Spec $\mathbb { Z }$ that is the “push forward” (not defined here, but defined as you might expect) of the divisor corresponding to the different. It is a ring-theoretic version of the branch locus. If B/A is an extension of rings of integers of number fields, the relative different ideal (of B) and relative discriminant ideal (of A) are defined similarly. (We won’t use these ideas.)

text: Chapter 21 Differentials
Chapter 22*
Blowing Up

We next discuss an important construction in algebraic geometry, the blow-up of a scheme along a closed subscheme (cut out by a finite type ideal sheaf). The theory could have mostly been developed immediately after Chapter 17, but the interpretation in terms of the conormal cone/bundle/sheaf of many classical examples makes it natural to discuss blowing up after differentials.

We won’t use blowing up much in later chapters, so feel free to skip this topic for now. But it is an important tool. For example, one can use it to resolve singularities, and, more generally, indeterminacy of rational maps. In particular, blow-ups can be used to relate birational varieties to each other.

We will start with a motivational example that will give you a picture of the construction in a particularly important (and the historically earliest) case, in §22.1. We will then see a formal definition, in terms of a universal property, §22.2. The definition won’t immediately have a clear connection to the motivational example. We will deduce some consequences of the definition (assuming that the blow-up actually exists). We will then prove that the blow-up exists, by describing it quite explicitly, in $\ S 2 2 . 3$ . As a consequence, we will find that the blow-up morphism is projective, and we will deduce more consequences from this. In §22.4, we will do a number of explicit computations, to see various sorts of applications, and to see that many things can be computed by hand.
22.1 Motivating Example: Blowing Up the Origin in the Plane

We will generalize the following notion, which will correspond to “blowing up” the origin of the affine plane $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ (Exercise 10.3.F). Our discussion will be informal. Consider the subset of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ corresponding to the following. We interpret $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ as parametrizing the lines through the origin. Consider the subvariety

$$
\operatorname {B l} _ {(0, 0)} \mathbb {A} ^ {2} := \left\{\left(p \in \mathbb {A} ^ {2}, [ \ell ] \in \mathbb {P} ^ {1}\right): p \in \ell) \right\},
$$

which is the data of a point p in the plane, and a line ℓ containing both p and the origin. Algebraically: let $x$ and $_ { 9 }$ be coordinates on $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ , and $x$ and Y be projective coordinates on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (“corresponding” to x and $_ { 9 }$ ); we will consider the subset $\mathrm { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ corresponding to ${ \tt x Y - y X } = 0$ . We have the useful diagram

You can verify that $\mathrm { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ is smooth over k (Definition 13.2.4 or 21.2.28) directly (you can now make the paragraph after Exercise 10.3.F precise), but here is an informal argument, using the projection $\mathbf { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } \longrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . The projective line $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is smooth, and for each point [ℓ] in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , we have a smooth choice of points on the line ℓ. Thus we are verifying smoothness by way of a fibration over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ .

We next consider the projection to $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ , $\beta \colon \mathtt { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ . This is an isomorphism away from the origin. Loosely speaking, if $\mathfrak { p }$ is not the origin, there is precisely one line containing p and the origin. On the other hand, if p is the origin, then there is a full $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ of lines containing p and the
origin. Thus the preimage of $( 0 , 0 )$ is a curve, and hence a divisor (an effective Cartier divisor, as the blown-up surface is regular). This is called the exceptional divisor of the blow-up.

If we have some curve $\mathsf { C } \subset \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ singular at the origin, it can be potentially partially desingularized, using the blow-up, by taking the closure of $C \setminus \{ ( 0 , 0 ) \}$ in $\mathrm { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ . (A desingularization or a resolution of singularities of a variety $x$ is a proper birational morphism $\widetilde { x } \to x$ from a regular scheme.) For example, consider the curve $y ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } ,$ which is regular except for a node at the origin. We can take the preimage of the curve minus the origin, and take the closure of this locus in the blow-up, and we will obtain a regular curve; the two branches of the node downstairs are separated upstairs. (You can check this in Exercise 22.4.B once we have defined things properly. The result will be called the proper transform (or strict transform) of the curve.) We are interested in desingularizations for many reasons. Because we understand regular curves quite well, we could hope to understand other curves through their desingularizations. This philosophy holds true in higher dimension as well.

More generally, we can blow up $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ at the origin (or, more informally, “blow up the ori-$\mathrm { g i n ^ { \prime \prime } } )$ , getting a subvariety of $\mathbb { A } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } - 1 }$ . Algebraically, if $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are coordinates on $\mathbb { A } ^ { { \mathfrak { n } } }$ , and $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are projective coordinates on $\mathbb { P } ^ { { \mathrm { n } } - 1 }$ , then the blow-up $\mathsf { B l } _ { \vec { 0 } } \mathsf { A } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ is given by the equations $\mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathsf { X } _ { \mathrm { j } } - \mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { j } } \mathsf { X } _ { \mathrm { i } } = 0$ . Once again, this is smooth: $\mathbb { P } ^ { n - 1 }$ is smooth, and for each point $[ \ell ] \in \mathbb { P } ^ { n - 1 }$ , we have a smooth choice of $\mathfrak { p } \in \ell$ .

We can extend this further, by blowing up $\mathbb { A } ^ { n + m }$ along a coordinate m-plane $\mathbb { A } ^ { \mathfrak { m } }$ by adding m more variables $x _ { n + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n + \mathfrak { m } }$ to the previous example; we get a subset of $\mathbb { A } ^ { \mathsf { n } + \mathsf { m } } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } - \mathsf { l } }$ .

Because, in complex geometry, submanifolds of manifolds locally “look like” coordinate mplanes in n-space, you might imagine that we could extend this to blowing up a regular subvariety of a regular variety. In the course of making this precise, we will accidentally generalize this notion greatly, defining the blow-up of any finite type quasicoherent sheaf of ideals in a scheme. In general, blowing up may not have such an intuitive description as in the case of blowing up something regular inside something regular—it can do great violence to the scheme—but even then, it is very useful.

Our description will depend only on the closed subscheme being blown up, and not on coordinates. That remedies a defect that was already present in the first example, of blowing up the plane at the origin. It is not obvious that if we picked different coordinates for the plane (preserving the origin as a closed subscheme) that we wouldn’t have two different resulting blow-ups.

As is often the case, there are two ways of understanding the notion of blowing up, and each is useful in different circumstances. The first is by universal property, which lets you show some things without any work. The second is an explicit construction, which lets you get your hands dirty and compute things (and implies, for example, that the blow-up morphism is projective).
The motivating example here may seem like a very special case, but if you understand the blow-up of the origin in n-space well enough, you will understand blowing up in general.
22.2 Blowing Up, by Universal Property
We now define the blow-up by a universal property. The disadvantage of starting here is that this definition won’t obviously be the same as (or even related to) the examples of $\ S 2 2 . 1$ .

Suppose $\chi \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ is a closed subscheme corresponding to a finite type quasicoherent sheaf of ideals. (If Y is locally Noetherian, the “finite type” hypothesis is automatic, so Noetherian readers can ignore it.)
The blow-up of $\tt X \hookrightarrow \tt Y$ is a Cartesian diagram

such that $\mathsf { E x Y }$ (the scheme-theoretic preimage of X by β) is an effective Cartier divisor (defined in §9.5.1) on $\mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon$ , such that any other such Cartesian diagram
where D is an effective Cartier divisor on W, factors uniquely through it:

We call $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon$ the blow-up (of Y along $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ or of Y with center X). (Other somewhat archaic terms for this are monoidal transformation, σ-process, quadratic transformation, and dilation.) We call $\mathsf { E x Y }$ the exceptional divisor of the blow-up. (Bl and $\beta$ stand for “blow-up,” and E stands for “exceptional.”)

(Caution: In the minimal model program, “exceptional divisor” has a slightly different meaning; if $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a birational morphism, then any Weil divisor on X whose image in Y is smaller dimension is called an exceptional divisor in this context.)

By a typical universal property argument, if the blow-up exists, it is unique up to unique isomorphism. (We can even recast this more explicitly in the language of Yoneda’s Lemma: consider the category of diagrams of the form (22.2.0.2), where morphisms are diagrams of the form
Then the blow-up is a final object in this category, if one exists.)

If $Z \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ is any closed subscheme of Y, then the scheme-theoretic preimage $\beta ^ { - 1 } Z$ is called the total transform of Z. We will soon see that $\beta$ is an isomorphism away from X (Observation 22.2.2). $\beta ^ { - 1 } ( Z - X )$ is called the proper transform or strict transform or birational transform of Z. (We will use the first terminology. We will also define it in a more general situation.) We will soon see (in the Blow-up Closure Lemma 22.2.7) that the proper transform is naturally isomorphic to $\mathrm { B l } _ { Z \cap X } Z ,$ where Z X is the scheme-theoretic intersection.
We will soon show that the blow-up always exists, and describe it explicitly. We first make a series of observations, assuming that the blow-up exists.

22.2.1. Observation. If X is the empty set, then $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } Y = \Upsilon .$ More generally, if X is an effective Cartier divisor, then the blow-up is an isomorphism. (Reason: idY : $\Upsilon  \Upsilon$ satisfies the universal property.)

22.2.6. The bottom closed embedding of (22.2.5.2) is locally cut out by one equation, and thus the same is true of the top closed embedding as well. However, the local equation on $Z \times _ { \Upsilon } \mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon$ need not be a non-zerodivisor, and thus the top closed embedding is not necessarily an effective Cartier divisor.

22.2 Blowing Up, by Universal Property

22.2.7. Blow-up Closure Lemma — Using the preceding notation: (BlW Z, EWZ) is canonically isomorphic to $( \overline { { Z } } , \mathsf E _ { \overline { { Z } } } )$ . More precisely: if the blow-up $\mathrm { B l } _ { X }$ Y exists, then $( \overline { { Z } } , \mathsf E _ { \overline { { Z } } } )$ is the blow-up of Z along W.

This will be very useful. We make a few initial comments. The first three apply to the special case where $Z \to \Upsilon$ is a closed embedding, and the fourth comment basically tells us we shouldn’t have concentrated on this special case.

list_items: (1) First, note that if $Z \to \Upsilon$ is a closed embedding, then the Blow-Up Closure Lemma states that the proper transform (as defined in §22.2) is the blow-up of Z along the scheme-theoretic intersection $W { = } X \cap Z$ .

(2) In particular, the Blow-Up Closure Lemma lets you actually compute blow-ups, and we will do lots of examples soon. For example, suppose C is a plane curve, singular at a point p, and we want to blow up C at p. Then we could instead blow up the plane at p (which we have already described how to do, even if we haven’t yet proved that it satisfies the universal property of blowing up), and then take the scheme-theoretic closure of $C \setminus \{ \mathfrak { p } \}$ in the blow-up.

(3) More generally, if W is some nasty subscheme of Z that we wanted to blow up, and Z is a finite type k-scheme, then the same trick would work. We could work locally (Exercise 22.2.A), so we may assume that Z is affine. If W is cut out by r equations $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \hdots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } \in \Gamma ( Z , \mathcal { O } _ { Z } ) ,$ , then complete the f’s to a generating set $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ of $\Gamma ( Z , \mathcal { O } _ { Z } )$ . This gives a closed embedding of Z into An such that W is the scheme-theoretic intersection of Z with a coordinate linear space $\mathbb { A } ^ { n - r }$ .

(4) Most generally still, this reduces the existence of the blow-up to a specific special case. (If you prefer to work over a fixed field ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ feel free to replace $\mathbb { Z }$ by $\mathrm { k }$ in this discussion.) Suppose that for each n, $\mathrm { B l } _ { \mathrm { U } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) }$ ) Spec Z[x1, . . . , xn] exists. Then I claim that the blow-up always exists. Here’s why. We may assume that Y is affine, say Spec B, and $X { = } 5 \mathrm { p e c } \mathrm { B } / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { n } } )$ . Hence by the blow-up closure lemma, $\mathrm { B l _ { X } Y }$ exists. (We won’t follow this approach, however.)

22.3 The Blow-up Exists, and Is Projective

22.3.1. It is now time to show that the blow-up always exists. We will see two arguments, which are enlightening in different ways. Both will imply that the blow-up morphism is projective, and hence quasicompact, proper, finite type, and separated. In particular, if $\Upsilon  Z$ is quasicompact (resp., proper, finite type, separated), so is $\mathsf { B l } _ { X } \mathsf { Y } \to Z$ . (And if $\Upsilon  Z$ is projective, and Z is quasicompact, then $\mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon  Z$ is projective. See the solution to Exercise 17.3.I for the reason for this annoying extra hypothesis.) The blow-up of a k-variety is a k-variety (using the fact that reducedness is preserved, Exercise 22.2.C), and the blow-up of an irreducible k-variety is an irreducible k-variety (using the fact that irreducibility is preserved, also Exercise 22.2.C).

22.3.2. Theorem (Proj description of the blow-up) — Suppose $x \hookrightarrow \mathbf { \nu }$ Y is a closed subscheme cut out by a finite type quasicoherent sheaf of ideals ${ \mathcal { S } } \hookrightarrow { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ . Then

$$
\mathcal {P r o j} _ {\Upsilon} \left(\mathcal {O} _ {\Upsilon} \oplus \mathcal {I} \oplus \mathcal {I} ^ {2} \oplus \mathcal {I} ^ {3} \oplus \dots\right) \longrightarrow \Upsilon
$$
satisfies the universal property of blowing up.
(We made sense of products of ideal sheaves, and hence ${ \mathcal { I } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , in Exercise 15.6.E.)

We will prove Theorem 22.3.2 soon (§22.3.3), after seeing what it tells us. Because $\mathcal { I }$ is finite type, the graded sheaf of algebras has degree 1 piece that is finite type. The graded sheaf of algebras is also clearly generated in degree 1. Thus the sheaf of algebras satisfy Hypotheses 17.2.1 (“finite generation in degree 1”).

But first, we should make sure that the preimage of $x$ is indeed an effective Cartier divisor. We can work affine-locally (Exercise 22.2.A), so we may assume that $\Upsilon =$ Spec B, and X is cut out by the finitely generated ideal I. Then

$$
\mathrm {B l} _ {\mathrm {X}} \mathrm {Y} = \operatorname {P r o j} _ {\mathrm {B}} \left(\mathrm {B} \oplus \mathrm {I} \oplus \mathrm {I} ^ {2} \oplus \dots\right).
$$

We are slightly abusing notation by using the notation $\mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon ,$ as we haven’t yet shown that this satisfies the universal property. Recall (§13.9.1) that the graded ring $\mathbf { B } \oplus \mathbf { I } \oplus \cdots$ , denoted by B(I) , is called the Rees algebra of the ideal I in B.

The preimage of X isn’t just any effective Cartier divisor; it corresponds to the invertible sheaf $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ on this Proj . Indeed, $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ corresponds to taking our graded ring, chopping off the bottom piece, and sliding all the graded pieces to the left by 1 (§15.1.4); it is the invertible sheaf corresponding to the graded module

$$
\mathrm {I B} (\mathrm {I}) _ {\bullet} = \mathrm {I} \oplus \mathrm {I} ^ {2} \oplus \mathrm {I} ^ {3} \oplus \dots
$$
(where that first summand I has grading 0). But this can be interpreted as the scheme-theoretic preimage of $\Sigma ,$ which corresponds to the ideal I of B:

$$
\mathrm {I B} (\mathrm {I}) _ {\bullet} = \mathrm {I} \left(\mathrm {B} \oplus \mathrm {I} \oplus \mathrm {I} ^ {2} \oplus \dots\right) \xrightarrow {} \mathrm {B} \oplus \mathrm {I} \oplus \mathrm {I} ^ {2} \oplus \dots = \mathrm {B} (\mathrm {I}) _ {\bullet}.
$$
Thus the scheme-theoretic preimage of $\tt X { \hookrightarrow \tt Y }$ to

$$
\operatorname {P r o j} \left(\mathcal {O} _ {\mathrm {Y}} (\mathcal {I}) _ {\bullet}\right) = \operatorname {P r o j} \left(\mathcal {O} _ {\mathrm {Y}} \oplus \mathcal {I} \oplus \mathcal {I} ^ {2} \oplus \dots\right),
$$

corresponding to the ideal sheaf corresponding to $\mathcal { I O } _ { \mathsf { Y } } ( \mathcal { I } ) _ { \bullet } = \mathcal { I } \oplus \mathcal { I } ^ { 2 } \oplus \mathcal { I } ^ { 3 } \oplus \cdots ,$ is an effective Cartier divisor in class $\mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ . Here are some applications and observations arising from this construction of the blow-up. First, we can verify that our initial motivational examples are indeed blow-ups. 22.3.4. Normal bundles to exceptional divisors. The normal bundle to an effective Cartier divisor D is the (space associated to the) invertible sheaf $\mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { D } ) | _ { \mathrm { D } } ,$ the invertible sheaf corresponding to the D on the total space, then restricted to D (Exercise 21.2.J). Thus in the case of the blow-up of a point in the plane, the exceptional divisor has normal bundle $\mathcal { O } ( - 1 )$ . (As an aside: Castelnuovo’s Criterion, Theorem 28.6.1, states that conversely given a smooth surface containing $\mathsf { E } \cong \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ with normal bundle $\mathcal { O } ( - 1 )$ , E can be blown-down to a point on another smooth surface.) In the case of the blow-up of a regular subvariety of a regular variety, the blow-up turns out to be regular (see Theorem 22.3.10), the exceptional divisor is a projective bundle over $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ and the normal bundle to the exceptional divisor restricts to $\mathcal { O } ( - 1 )$ (Exercise 22.3.D).

22.3.5. The normal cone. Motivated by (22.3.2.1), as well as Exercise 22.3.D below, we make the following definition. If X is a closed subscheme of Y cut out by $\mathcal { I }$ , then the normal cone $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathsf { X } } \mathsf { Y }$ of X in Y is defined as

This can profitably be thought of as an algebro-geometric version of an infinitesimal “tubular neighborhood.” But some cautions are in order. If Y is smooth, $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathsf { X } } \mathsf { Y }$ may not be smooth. (You can work out the example of $\Upsilon = \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ and $X = \mathrm { V } ( \mathrm { x y } )$ .)

And even if X and Y are smooth, then although $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathsf { X } } \mathsf { Y }$ is smooth (as we will see shortly, Exercise 22.3.D), it doesn’t “embed” in any way in Y. As a simple example, consider a smooth point $\mathsf X = \mathsf p$ of a non-rational curve $\mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { C }$ . Then $\mathsf { N } _ { \mathsf { X } } \mathsf { Y }$ is rational and cannot admit an open embedding into Y.

22.3.6. The tangent cone. If X is a closed point p, then the normal cone is called the tangent cone to Y at p. The projectivized tangent cone is the exceptional divisor $\mathsf { E x Y }$ (the Proj of the same graded sheaf of algebras). Following §9.3.12, the tangent cone and the projectivized tangent cone can be put together in the projective completion of the tangent cone, which contains the tangent cone as an open subset, and the projectivized tangent cone as a complementary effective Cartier divisor.

In Exercise 22.3.D, we will see that at a regular point of Y, the tangent cone may be identified with the tangent space, and the normal cone may often be identified with the total space of the normal bundle.

22.3.7. Blowing up regular embeddings.
The case of blow-ups of regular embeddings $\textsf { X C Y }$ is particularly pleasant. For example, the exceptional divisor is a projective bundle over X.

text: 22.3.10. Theorem — Suppose $\tt X \hookrightarrow \tt Y$ is a closed embedding of smooth varieties over k. Then $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon$ is also smooth.
Proof. We use the fact that smooth varieties are regular, the Smoothness-Regularity Comparison Theorem 13.2.7(b), whose proof we still have to complete.

We may assume that $\mathsf { k } = \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } ,$ because the statement of the result is preserved by base change to the algebraic closure. Reason: Smoothness can be checked after base change to ${ \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ,$ by Exercise 13.2.H. The blow-up construction commutes with base change to the algebraic closure: you can examine the construction, or wait until you can invoke the generalization in Exercise 24.1.P(a)

We need only check smoothness of $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon$ at the points of $\mathsf { E x Y } ,$ , by Observation 22.2.2. By Exercise 13.2.M(b), $\chi \hookrightarrow \Upsilon$ is a regular embedding. Then by Exercise 22.3.D(a), $\mathsf { E x Y }$ is a projective bundle over $\Sigma ,$ and thus smooth, and hence regular at its closed points. But $\mathsf { E x Y }$ is an effective Cartier divisor on $\mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon .$ . By the slicing criterion for regularity (Exercise 13.2.C), it follows that $\mathrm { B l _ { X } Y }$ is regular at the closed points of $\mathsf { E x Y }$ , hence smooth at all points of $\mathsf { E x Y }$ . □
22.3.11.* Proving Theorem 22.3.8.

The proof of Theorem 22.3.8 may reasonably be skipped on a first reading. We prove Theorem 22.3.8 following [F2, A.6.1], which in turn follows [Dav]. The proof will be completed in §22.3.13. To begin, let $\propto$ be the map of graded rings

$$
\alpha : (A / I) \left[ X _ {1}, \dots , X _ {d} \right] \longrightarrow \oplus_ {n = 0} ^ {\infty} \Gamma^ {n} / \Gamma^ {n + 1},
$$

In particular, as A is a subring of its total fraction ring, the map $\mathrm { A } \longrightarrow \mathrm { A } [ \mathbf { a } _ { 2 } / \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { d } } / \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } ]$ is an injection. $$

22.3.12. Lemma — The kernel of $\beta$ is $\left( \mathrm { L } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mathrm { L } _ { \mathrm { d } } \right)$ .

22.4 Examples and Computations

In this section we will work through a number of explicit of examples, to get a sense of how blowups behave, how they are useful, and how one can work with them explicitly. Throughout we work over a field ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ and we assume throughout that char ${ \mathrm { k } } = 0$ to avoid distraction. The examples and exercises are loosely arranged by topic, but not in order of importance.
22.4.1. Example: Blowing up the plane along the origin.

Let’s first blow up the plane $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ along the origin, and see that the result agrees with our discussion in §22.1. Let $x$ and $_ { 9 }$ be the coordinates on A2. The blow-up is P $\mathrm { r o j } \mathsf { k } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } , \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { Y } ] ,$ , where ${ \mathfrak { x } } \Upsilon - { \mathfrak { y } } { \mathfrak { X } } = 0$ . (Here $x$ and $_ { 9 }$ have degree 0 and X and Y have degree 1.) This is naturally a closed subscheme of $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ , cut out (in terms of the projective coordinates X and $\Upsilon _ { 0 \mathrm { n } } \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ ) by ${ \tt x Y - y X } = 0$ . We consider the two usual patches on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \colon [ \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { Y } ] = [ \mathsf { s } , 1 ]$ and [1, t]. The first patch yields $\operatorname { S p e c k } [ x , y , s ] / ( s y - x ) ,$ and the second gives Spec k[x, y, t]/ $( \mathfrak { y } - \mathfrak { x } \mathrm { t } )$ . Notice that both are smooth: the first is Spec k[y, $s ] \cong$ $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ , and the second is Spec $\mathbf { k } [ x , \mathrm { t } ] \cong \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ .

We now describe the exceptional divisor. We first consider the first (s) patch. The ideal is generated by $\left( { x , y } \right)$ , which in our ys-coordinates is $( \mathfrak { y s } , \mathfrak { y } ) = ( \mathfrak { y } )$ , which is indeed principal. Thus on this patch the exceptional divisor is generated by y. Similarly, in the second patch, the exceptional divisor is cut out by $x$ . (This can be a little confusing, but there is no contradiction!) This explicit description will be useful in working through some of the examples below.

22.4.A. EXERCISE. Let p be a k-valued point of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . Exhibit an isomorphism between $\mathrm { B l _ { p } } \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ and the Hirzebruch surface $\mathbb { F } _ { 1 } = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } } \left( \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } } \oplus \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } } \left( 1 \right) \right)$ (Example 17.2.6). (The map Blp $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ informally corresponds to taking a point to the line connecting it to the origin. Do not be afraid: You can do this by explicitly working with coordinates.)

list_items: 22.4.5. Remark: ADE-surface singularities and Dynkin diagrams (see Figure 22.2). A ksingularity analytically isomorphic to $z ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } y + y ^ { n - 1 }$ (resp., $z ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 3 } + y ^ { 4 }$ , $z ^ { 2 } = \overline { { { x } ^ { 3 } } } + \mathrm { x y } ^ { 3 }$ , $z ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 3 } +$ $\mathsf { y } ^ { 5 } .$ ) is a called a $\mathrm { D } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ surface singularity (resp., E6, E7, E8 surface singularity). We will make this precise in Exercise 28.2.C, and you will then be able to guess the definition of the corresponding curve singularity. If you (minimally) desingularize each of these surfaces by sequentially blowing up singular points as in Exercise 22.4.F, and look at the arrangement of exceptional divisors (the various exceptional divisors and how they meet), you will discover the corresponding Dynkin diagram. More precisely, if you create a graph where the vertices correspond to exceptional divisors, and two vertices are joined by an edge if the two divisors meet, you will find the underlying graph of the corresponding Dynkin diagram. This is the start of several very beautiful stories; see Remark 27.3.7 for a first glimpse of one of them.

22.4.6. Remark: Resolution of singularities. Hironaka’s Theorem on resolution of singularities implies that this idea of trying to resolve singularities by blowing up singular loci in general can succeed in characteristic 0 (see [Hir], and [Ko2]). (The case of dimension 1 will be shown in §28.4.4, and the case of dimension 2 will be discussed in §28.6.4.) It is not known if an analogous statement is true in positive characteristic (except in dimension at most 3; see [CP]), but de Jong’s Alteration Theorem [dJ] gives a result that is good enough for most applications. Rather than producing a birational proper map $\widetilde { x } \to x$ from something regular, it produces a proper map from something regular that is generically finite (and the corresponding extension of function fields is separable).
Here are some other exercises related to resolution of singularities.

22.4.7. Multiplicity of a function at a regular point. In order to pose Exercise 22.4.J, we introduce a useful concept. If f is a function on a locally Noetherian scheme X, its multiplicity at a regular point p is the largest m such that f lies in the mth power of the maximal ideal in the local ring $\mathcal { O } _ { { X , \mathfrak { p } } }$ . For example, if $\mathsf { f } \neq 0$ , V(f) is singular at p if and only if $\mathsf { m } > 1 .$ (Do you see why?)

(For example, in the case of the nodal and cuspidal curves above,

list_items: 22.4.L. HARDER BUT USEFUL EXERCISE (BLOW-UPS RESOLVE BASE LOCI OF RATIONAL MAPS TO PROJECTIVE SPACE). Suppose we have a scheme Y, an invertible sheaf ${ \mathcal { L } } ,$ and a number of sections $s _ { 0 } , \ldots , s _ { n }$ of $\mathcal { L }$ (a linear series, Definition 15.2.1). Then away from the closed subscheme X cut out by $s _ { 0 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = s _ { \mathrm { n } } = 0$ (the base locus of the linear series), these sections give a morphism to $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . Show that this morphism extends uniquely to a morphism $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } { \mathsf { Y } } \to { \mathbb { P } } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ , where this morphism corresponds to the invertible sheaf $( \beta ^ { * } { \mathcal { L } } ) ( - \mathsf { E } x \mathsf { Y } )$ , where $\beta \colon \mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon  \Upsilon$ is the blowup morphism. In other words, “blowing up the scheme-theoretic base locus resolves this rational map.” Hint: It suffices to consider an affine open subset of Y where $\mathcal { L }$ is trivial. Uniqueness might use Exercise 11.3.G.

22.4.9. Remarks. (i) Exercise 22.4.L immediately implies that blow-ups can be used to resolve rational maps to projective schemes $\Upsilon \mathrm { ~ -- } \not \geq Z \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ .

(ii) The following interpretation is enlightening. The linear series on Y pulls back to a linear series on $\mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon ,$ , and the base locus of the linear series on Y pulls back to the base locus on $\mathrm { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon .$ . The base locus on $\mathtt { B l } _ { X } \mathtt { Y }$ is $\mathsf { E x Y } ,$ an effective Cartier divisor. Because $\mathsf { E x Y }$ is not just locally principal, but also locally a non-zerodivisor, it can be “divided out” from the $\beta ^ { * } s _ { \mathrm { i } }$ (yielding a section of $( \beta ^ { * } { \mathcal { L } } ) ( - \mathsf { E } _ { X } \mathsf { Y } ) )$ , thereby removing the base locus, and leaving a base-point-free linear series. (In a sense that can be made precise through the universal property, this is the smallest “modification” of Y that can remove the base locus.) If X is already Cartier (as, for example, happens with any nontrivial linear series if Y is a regular pure-dimensional curve), then we can remove a base locus by just “dividing out X.”
(iii) You may wish to revisit Exercise 19.7.C, and interpret it in terms of Exercise 22.4.L.

22.4.10. Examples. (i) The rational map $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ given by $[ { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 } , \ldots , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathrm { n } } ] \to - { \mathfrak { x } } [ { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { \mathrm { n } } ] ,$ defined away from $\mathfrak { p } = [ 1 , 0 , \ldots , 0 ] ,$ is resolved by blowing up p. Then by the Blow-up Closure Lemma 22.2.7, if Y is any locally closed subscheme of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , we can project to $\mathbb { P } ^ { { \mathrm { n } } - 1 }$ once we blow up p in Y, and the invertible sheaf giving the map to $\mathbb { P } ^ { { \mathrm { n } } - 1 }$ is (somewhat informally speaking) $\beta ^ { * } ( \mathcal { O } _ { \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } } } ( 1 ) ) \otimes \mathcal { O } ( - \mathsf { E } _ { \mathsf { p } } \mathsf { Y } )$ .

(ii) Consider two general homogeneous cubic functions $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ in three variables, yielding two cubic curves in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . They are smooth (by Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2), and meet in nine points $\mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathfrak { p } _ { 9 }$ (using our standing assumption that we work over an algebraically closed field). Then $[ C _ { 1 } , C _ { 2 } ]$ gives a rational map $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } \mathrel { - } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . To resolve the rational map, we blow up p1, . . . , p9. The result is (generically) an elliptic fibration $\operatorname { B l } _ { \{ \ p _ { 1 } , \dots , p _ { 9 } \} } \mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . (This is by no means a complete argument or even a precise statement.)

(iii) Fix six general points $\mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathfrak { p } _ { 6 }$ in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ . There is a four-dimensional vector space of cubics vanishing at these points, and they vanish scheme-theoretically precisely at these points. This yields a rational map $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ , which is resolved by blowing up the six points. The resulting morphism turns out to be a closed embedding, and the image in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ is a (smooth) cubic surface. This is the famous fact that the blow-up of the plane at six general points may be represented as a

text: Chapter $2 2 ^ { * }$ Blowing Up
(smooth) cubic in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ . (Again, this argument is not intended to be complete.) See §27.3.5 for a more precise and more complete discussion.
In reasonable circumstances, Exercise 22.4.L has an interpretation in terms of graphs of rational maps.

Exercise 9.3.N), followed by the isomorphism $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { Q } } \stackrel { \sim } { \longrightarrow } \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (Example 10.6.2), followed by projection onto one of the factors.

This is an example of a small resolution. (A small resolution $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a resolution where the locus of points of Y where the fiber has dimension r is of codimension greater than 2r. We will not use this notion again in any essential way. Caution: This is the definition of “small resolution” used in intersection homology; in the minimal model program, a small resolution is a resolution that is an isomorphism in codimension 1. So pay attention to the context of what you are reading.) Notice that this resolution of the morphism involves blowing up the base locus $w = { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } } = 0$ , which is a cone over one of the lines on the quadric surface $w z = { \tt x y }$ . We are blowing up an effective Weil divisor, which is necessarily not Cartier as the blow-up is not an isomorphism. In Exercise 13.1.E, we saw that $( w , x )$ was not principal, while here we see that $( w , x )$ is not even locally principal. Essentially by Exercise 15.5.N, ${ \mathsf { V } } ( w , x )$ cannot even be the support of a locally principal divisor.
22.4.11. Remark: Nonisomorphic small resolutions. If you instead resolved the map $[ w , \mathfrak { y } ] ,$ you would obtain a similar looking small resolution

$$
\pi^ {\prime} \colon X ^ {\prime} \longrightarrow \operatorname {S p e c k} [ w, x, y, z ] / (w z - x y)
$$

(it is an isomorphism away from the origin, and the fiber over the origin is $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ ). But it is different! More precisely, there is no morphism $x \to X ^ { \prime }$ making the following the diagram commute.
The birational map $X \mathrm { ~ - }  X ^ { \prime }$ is called the Atiyah flop, [At1].
22.4 Examples and Computations

22.4.12. Factorization of birational maps. We end our discussion of resolution of rational maps by noting that just as Hironaka’s Theorem states that one may resolve all singularities of varieties in characteristic 0 by a sequence of blow-ups along smooth subvarieties (§22.4.6), the Weak Factorization Theorem (first proved by Włodarczyk [Wł]) states that any two birational varieties X and Y in characteristic 0 may be related by blow-ups and “blow-downs” along smooth subvarieties. More precisely, there are varieties $X _ { 0 } , \dotsc , X _ { \mathrm { n } } , \ X _ { 0 1 } , \dotsc , X _ { ( \mathrm { n - 1 } ) \mathrm { n } } ,$ with $\mathsf X _ { 0 } = \mathsf X$ and ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { X } } } _ { \mathrm { n } } = { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { Y } } } ,$ with morphisms $\mathsf X _ { \mathrm { i } ( \mathrm { i } + 1 ) } \to \mathsf X _ { \mathrm { i } }$ and $\Chi _ { \mathrm { i ( i + 1 ) } } \to \Chi _ { \mathrm { i + 1 } }$ $( 0 \leq \mathrm { i } < \mathsf { n } )$ that are blow-ups of smooth subvarieties.
22.4.13. Blow-ups and line bundles.

$$

22.4.14. Change of the canonical line bundle under blow-ups.

As motivation for how the canonical line bundle changes under blowing up, consider the blow-up $\begin{array} { r } { \beta \colon \mathrm { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } \longrightarrow \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}$ . Let $\mathsf { X } = \mathsf { B l } _ { ( 0 , 0 ) } \hat { \mathsf { A } } ^ { 2 }$ and $\Upsilon = \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ for convenience. We use Exercise 21.4.A to relate $\beta ^ { * } { \mathcal { K } } _ { \sf Y }$ to $\mathcal { K } _ { \sf X }$ .

We pick a generator for $\mathcal { H } _ { \Upsilon }$ near $( 0 , 0 )$ : dx ∧ dy. (This is, in fact, a generator for $\mathcal { H } _ { \Upsilon }$ everywhere on $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ , but for the sake of generalization, we point out that all that matters is that it is a generator at $( 0 , 0 )$ , and hence near $( 0 , 0 )$ by geometric Nakayama, Exercise 14.3.D.) When we pull it back to $\Sigma ,$ we can interpret it as a section of ${ \mathcal { K } } _ { X } ,$ which will generate $\mathcal { H } _ { X }$ away from the exceptional divisor E, but may contain E with some multiplicity $\mu$ . Recall that X can be interpreted as the data of a point in $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ as well as the choice of a line through the origin. We consider the open subset U where the line is not vertical, and thus can be written as $y = \mathsf { m } x$ . Here we have natural coordinates: $\mathsf { U } { = } { \mathsf { S p e c k } } [ { \mathsf { x } } , { \mathsf { y } } , \mathsf { m } ] / ( { \mathsf { y } } - \mathsf { m } { \mathsf { x } } ) ,$ , which we can interpret as Spec k[x, m]. The exceptional divisor E meets U, at $\boldsymbol { x } = 0$ (in the coordinates on U), so we can calculate $\mu$ on this open set. Pulling back dx ∧ dy to U, we get

$$
d x \wedge d y = d x \wedge d (x m) = m (d x \wedge d x) + x (d x \wedge d m) = x (d x \wedge d m)
$$
as $\mathrm { d } \boldsymbol { x } \wedge \mathrm { d } \boldsymbol { x } = 0$ . Thus $\beta ^ { * } ( \mathrm { d } x \wedge \mathrm { d } y )$ vanishes to order 1 along E.

text: 22.4.15.* Dimensional cohomology vanishing for quasiprojective schemes (promised in $\ S 1 8 . 2 . 7 )$ ).

Using the theory of blowing up, Theorem 18.2.6 (dimensional cohomology vanishing for quasicoherent sheaves on projective k-schemes) can be extended to quasiprojective k-schemes. Suppose X is a quasiprojective k-variety of dimension n. We show that X may be covered by $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open subsets. As X is quasiprojective, there is some projective variety Y with an open embedding $\tt X { \hookrightarrow \tt Y }$ . By replacing Y with the closure of X in Y, we may assume that $\dim \Upsilon = { \mathfrak { n } }$ . Put any subscheme structure Z on the complement of X in Y (for example, the reduced subscheme structure, $\ S 9 . 4 . 9 $ ). Let $\mathsf { Y } ^ { \prime } = \mathsf { B } \mathsf { l } _ { Z } \mathsf { Y } .$ . Then ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is a projective variety (§22.3.1), which can be covered by $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open subsets. The complement of X in ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is an effective Cartier divisor $( \mathsf E z Y )$ , so the restriction to X of each of these affine open subsets of Y is also affine, by Exercise 8.3.E.

list_items: 22.4.16. Remarks. (i) You might then hope that any dimension n variety can be covered by $\mathfrak { n } + 1$ affine open subsets. This is not true. For each integer ${ \mathfrak { m } } ,$ there is a threefold that requires at least m affine open sets to cover it; see [RotV, Ex. 4.9]. By the discussion above, this example is necessarily not quasiprojective. (ii) Here is a fact useful in invariant theory, which can be proved in the same way. Suppose $\mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ are closed points on a quasiprojective k-variety X. Then there is an affine open subset of X containing all of them.

22.4.T. EXERCISE (DIMENSIONAL COHOMOLOGY VANISHING FOR QUASIPROJECTIVE VARIETIES). Suppose X is a quasiprojective $\mathrm { k }$ -scheme of dimension d. Show that for any quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on X, ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } ) = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } > \mathfrak { d }$ .

text: 22.4 Examples and Computations
22.4.17.** Deformation to the normal cone (Figure 22.3).
The following construction is key to the modern understanding of intersection theory in algebraic geometry, as developed by Fulton and MacPherson, [F2].
22.4.U. EXERCISE: DEFORMATION TO THE NORMAL CONE. Suppose Y is a k-variety, and $\tt X \hookrightarrow \tt Y$ is a closed subscheme.

(a) Show that the exceptional divisor of $\beta \colon \mathrm { B l } _ { X \times 0 } ( \mathsf { Y } \times \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } )  \mathsf { Y } \times \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ is isomorphic to the projective completion of the normal cone to X in Y.

(b) Let $\pi$ : ${ \tt B l } _ { X \times 0 } ( \mathsf { Y } \times \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } ) \to \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ be the composition of $\beta$ with the projection to $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ . Show that $\pi ^ { - 1 } \mathopen { } \mathclose \bgroup \left( 0 \aftergroup \egroup \right)$ →is the scheme-theoretic union of $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon$ with the projective completion of the normal cone to $x$ in Y, and the intersection of these two subschemes may be identified with $\mathsf { E x Y } ,$ which is a closed subscheme of $\mathtt { B l x } \mathtt { Y }$ in the usual way (as the exceptional divisor of the blowup $\operatorname { B l } _ { X } \Upsilon \to \Upsilon$ , and a closed subscheme of the projective completion of the normal cone as described in Exercise 9.3.O.
The map

is called the deformation to the normal cone (short for deformation of Y to the normal cone of X in Y). Notice that the fiber above every k-point away from $0 \in \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ is canonically isomorphic to Y, and the fiber over 0 is the normal cone. Because this family is “nice” (more precisely, flat, the topic of Chapter 24), we can prove things about general Y (near $x$ ) by way of this degeneration. (We will see in §24.3.10 that the deformation to the normal cone is a flat morphism, which is useful in intersection theory.)

PART VI
More Cohomological Tools
VI
Chapter 23
Derived Functors
Ça me semble extrêmement plaisant de ficher comme ça beaucoup de choses, pas drôles quand on les prend séparément, sous le grand chapeau des foncteurs dérivés.
I find it very agreeable to stick all sorts of things, which are not much fun when taken individually, together under the heading of derived functors.
—A. Grothendieck, letter to J.-P. Serre, February 18, 1955 [GrS, p. 6]

In this chapter, we discuss derived functors, introduced by Grothendieck in his celebrated “Tôhoku article” [Gr1], and their applications to sheaves. For quasicoherent sheaves on quasicompact separated schemes, derived functor cohomology will agree with Čech cohomology (§23.5). Čech cohomology will suffice for most of our purposes, and is quite down-to-earth and computable, but derived functor cohomology is worth seeing. First, it will apply much more generally in algebraic geometry (e.g., étale cohomology) and elsewhere, although this is beyond the scope of this book. Second, it will easily provide us with some useful notions, such as the Ext functors and the Leray spectral sequence. But derived functors can be intimidating the first time you see them, so feel free to just skim the main results, and to return to them later.
23.1 The Tor Functors

We begin with a warm-up: the case of Tor. This is a hands-on example, but if you understand it well, you will understand derived functors in general. Tor will be useful to prove facts about flatness, which we will discuss in $\ S 2 4 . 2$ . Tor is short for “torsion” (see Remark 24.2.1).
If you have never seen this notion before, you may want to just remember its properties. But I will prove everything anyway—it is surprisingly easy.
The idea behind Tor is as follows. Whenever we see a right-exact functor, we always hope that it is the end of a long exact sequence. Informally, given a short exact sequence

we hope $\mathsf { M } \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } \mathsf { N } ^ { \prime } \to \mathsf { M } \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } \mathsf { N } \to \mathsf { M } \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } \mathsf { N } ^ { \prime \prime } \to 0$ will extend to a long exact sequence

More precisely, we are hoping for covariant functors $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( { \cal M } , \cdot )$ from A-modules to A-modules (functoriality giving 2/3 of the morphisms in (23.1.0.2)), with $\operatorname { T o r } _ { 0 } ^ { \boldsymbol { A } } ( M , \mathbb { N } ) \equiv \boldsymbol { M } \otimes _ { \boldsymbol { A } } \mathbb { N } ,$ , and natural connecting homomorphisms

$$
\delta \colon \operatorname {T o r} _ {i + 1} ^ {A} (M, N ^ {\prime \prime}) \longrightarrow \operatorname {T o r} _ {i} ^ {A} (M, N ^ {\prime})
$$

for every short exact sequence (23.1.0.1) giving the long exact sequence (23.1.0.2). (“Natural” means: given a morphism of short exact sequences, the natural square you would write down involving the δ-morphism must commute.)
It turns out to be not too hard to make this work, and this will also motivate derived functors. Let’s now define $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ .
Take any resolution $\mathcal { R }$ of $\mathsf { N }$ by free modules:

$$
\dots \longrightarrow A ^ {\oplus n _ {2}} \longrightarrow A ^ {\oplus n _ {1}} \longrightarrow A ^ {\oplus n _ {0}} \longrightarrow N \longrightarrow 0.
$$

More precisely, build this resolution from right to left. Start by choosing generators of N as an A-module, giving us ${ \cal A } ^ { \oplus { \bf n } _ { 0 } } \to { \bf N } \to 0$ . Then choose generators of the kernel, and so on. Note that we are not requiring the ${ \mathrm { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ to be finite (although we could, if N is a finitely generated module and A is Noetherian). Truncate the resolution, by stripping off the last term N (replacing $ \mathsf { N }  0$ with $ 0$ ). Then tensor with M (which does not preserve exactness). Note that $M \otimes ( { \cal A } ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } } ) = M ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } }$ →, as tensoring with M commutes with arbitrary direct sums (Exercise 1.2.M). Let $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } }$ be the homology of this complex at the ith stage $( \mathrm { i } \geq 0 )$ . The subscript $\mathcal { R }$ reminds us that our construction depends on the resolution, although we will soon see that it is independent of $\mathcal { R }$ .
We make some quick observations.

list_items: • $\operatorname { T o r } _ { 0 } ^ { \boldsymbol { A } } ( M , \mathbb { N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } } \cong M \otimes _ { \boldsymbol { A } } \mathbb { N } ,$ canonically. Reason: As tensoring is right-exact, and $\lambda ^ { \oplus n _ { 1 } } $ $\mathsf { A } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { n } _ { 0 } } \to \mathsf { N } \to 0$ is exact, we have that $M ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { 1 } } \to M ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { 0 } } \to M \otimes _ { A } \mathsf { N } \to 0$ →is exact, and hence that the homology of the truncated complex $M ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { 1 } } \to M ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { 0 } } \to 0$ is $M \otimes _ { { \cal A } } \mathsf { N }$ .

• If $M \otimes \cdot$ is exact (i.e., M is flat, §1.5.13), then $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } ) _ { \mathscr { R } } = 0$ for all $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ . (This characterizes flatness; see Exercise 23.1.C.)

text: Now given two modules N and $\mathsf { N } ^ { \prime }$ and resolutions $\mathcal { R }$ and $\mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ of $\mathsf { N }$ and $\mathsf { N } ^ { \prime }$ , we can “lift” any morphism $\Nu \to \Nu ^ { \prime }$ to a morphism of the two resolutions:

We do this inductively on i. Here we use the freeness of $A ^ { \oplus { \mathfrak { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } }$ : if a1, …, $\mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { n _ { i } } }$ are generators of $\boldsymbol { A } ^ { \oplus { \mathrm { n } _ { \mathrm { i } } } }$ , to lift the map b: $A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } } \to A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 } ^ { \prime } }$ (the composition of the differential $A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } } \to A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } } - 1$ with the previously constructed $A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 } }  A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } - 1 } ^ { \prime } } \rangle$ ) to $\mathsf { c } \colon \mathsf { A } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { n } _ { \mathrm { i } } } \to \mathsf { A } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } }$ →, we arbitrarily lift $\mathbf { b } ( \mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { j } } )$ from $\lambda ^ { \oplus \hat { \mathbf { n } _ { \mathrm { i - 1 } } ^ { \prime } } }$ to $\bar { A } ^ { \mathrm { { \phi n _ { i } ^ { \prime } } } }$ →, and declare this to be ${ \mathrm { c } } ( { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { j } } )$ →. (Warning for people who care about such things: we are using the Axiom of Choice here.)

Denote the choice of lifts by $\mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ . Now truncate both complexes (remove column $\Nu \to \Nu ^ { \prime }$ ) and tensor with M. Maps of complexes induce maps of homology (Exercise 1.5.D), so we have described maps (a priori depending on $\mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ )

$$
\operatorname {T o r} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {A} (M, N) _ {\mathscr {R}} \longrightarrow \operatorname {T o r} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {A} (M, N ^ {\prime}) _ {\mathscr {R} ^ {\prime}}.
$$

Recall (§1.5.7) that we say two maps of complexes f : $\mathrm { C } _ { \bullet } \to \mathrm { C } _ { \bullet } ^ { \prime }$ and ${ \mathfrak { g } } \colon { \mathsf { C } } _ { \bullet } \to { \mathsf { C } } _ { \bullet } ^ { \prime }$ are homotopic if there is a sequence of maps w: $C _ { \mathrm { i } } \to C _ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } ^ { \prime }$ such that $\mathsf { f } - \mathsf { g } = \mathrm { d } w + w \mathrm { d }$ . We saw that two homotopic maps give the same map on homology (Exercise 1.5.E).
23.1.A. CRUCIAL EXERCISE. Show that any two lifts $\mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ are homotopic.
We now pull these observations together. (Be sure to digest these completely!)

list_items: (1) We get a map of A-modules $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { A } ( M , { \bf N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } } \longrightarrow \mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { A } ( M , { \bf N } ^ { \prime } ) _ { \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } } ,$ independent of the lift $\mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ .

(2) Hence for any two resolutions $\mathcal { R }$ and $\mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ of an A-module $\mathsf { N } _ { - }$ , we get a canonical isomorphism $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( { \cal M } , { \sf N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } } \longleftrightarrow \mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( { \cal M } , { \sf N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } } .$ Here’s why. Choose lifts $\mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ and $\mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } \to \mathcal { R }$ . The composition $\mathcal { R }  \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }  \mathcal { R }$ is homotopic to the identity (as it is a lift of the identity map $\Nu \to \Nu$ →). Thus if f ${ \bf \Psi } _ { ? \to \infty } \prime \colon \mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , { \sf N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } } \to \mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , { \sf N } ) _ { \mathcal { R } } ,$ is the map induced by $\mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ , and

text: Chapter 23 Derived Functors

similarly $\mathbf { f } _ { \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }  \mathcal { R } }$ is the map induced by ${ \mathcal { R } } ^ { \prime } \to { \mathcal { R } } ,$ then $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }  \mathcal { R } } \circ \mathsf { f } _ { \mathcal { R }  \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } }$ is the identity, and similarly $\boldsymbol { \mathfrak { f } } _ { \mathcal { R } \to \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } } \circ \boldsymbol { \mathfrak { f } } _ { \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } \to \mathcal { R } }$ is the identity.

(3) Hence $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \cdot )$ → doesn’t depend on the choice of resolution. It is a covariant functor $M o d _ { \mathrm { A } } $ $M o d _ { A }$ .

23.1.1. Remark. Note that if $\mathsf { N }$ is a free module, then $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } ) { = } 0$ for all $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ and all ${ \mathfrak { i } } > 0 ,$ , as N has the trivial resolution $0 \to \mathsf { N } \to \mathsf { N } \to 0$ (it is “its own resolution”).
Finally, we get long exact sequences:
23.1.2. Proposition — For any short exact sequence (23.1.0.1) we get a long exact sequence of Tor’s (23.1.0.2).

Proof. Given a short exact sequence (23.1.0.1), choose resolutions of $\mathsf { N } ^ { \prime }$ and $\mathsf { N } ^ { \prime \prime }$ . Then use these to get a resolution for $\mathsf { N }$ as follows.

The map $A ^ { \oplus ( { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i + 1 } } ^ { \prime } + { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i + 1 } } ^ { \prime \prime } ) }  A ^ { \oplus ( { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } + { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime } ) }$ is the composition $\mathbf { A } ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i + 1 } } ^ { \prime } } \to \mathbf { A } ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } } \hookrightarrow \mathbf { A } ^ { \oplus ( { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } + { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime } ) }$ along with a lift of $A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } }  A ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime } }$ to ${ \cal A } ^ { \oplus ( { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } + { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime } ) }$ →  →  ensuring that the middle row is a complex.

23.1.B. EXERCISE. Verify that it is possible to choose such a lift of $\smash { \lambda ^ { \oplus 1 } \mathrm { \Omega } _ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } \to \lambda ^ { \oplus 1 } \mathrm { \Omega } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime } }$ to ${ \cal A } ^ { \oplus ( { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } + { \bf n } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime } ) }$ .

Hence the middle row of (23.1.2.1) is exact (not just a complex), using the long exact sequence in cohomology (Theorem 1.5.8), and the fact that the top and bottom rows are exact. Thus the middle row is a resolution, and (23.1.2.1) is a short exact sequence of resolutions. (This is sometimes called the horseshoe construction, as the filling in of the middle row looks like filling in the middle of a horseshoe.) It may be helpful to notice that the columns other than the “N-column” are all “direct sum exact sequences,” and the horizontal maps in the middle row are “block upper triangular.”

Then truncate (removing the right column $0 \to { \mathsf { N } } ^ { \prime } \to { \mathsf { N } } \to { \mathsf { N } } ^ { \prime \prime } \to 0$ ), tensor with M (obtaining a short exact sequence of complexes), and take cohomology, yielding the desired long exact sequence. □

23.2 Derived Functors in General

23.2.1. Projective resolutions. We used very little about free modules in the above construction of Tor—in fact, we used only that free modules are projective, i.e., those modules P such that for any surjection $M \longrightarrow \mathbb { N }$ , it is possible to lift any morphism $\mathsf { P } \longrightarrow \mathsf { N }$ to $\mathsf { P } \to { M }$ :

(As noted in $\ S 2 3 . 1 ,$ , this uses the Axiom of Choice.) Equivalently, $\mathrm { H o m } ( \mathsf { P } , \cdot )$ is an exact functor (recall that $\mathrm { H o m } ( \mathrm { Q } , \cdot )$ is always left-exact for any Q). More generally, the same idea yields the definition of a projective object in any abelian category. Hence by following through our entire argument with projective modules replacing free modules throughout, (i) we can compute $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ by taking any projective resolution of N, and (ii) $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } ) { = } 0$ for any projective A-module N.

23.2.A. EXERCISE. Show that an object P is projective if and only if every short exact sequence $0 \to \mathrm { Q } \to \mathbb { R } \to \mathsf { P } \to 0$ splits. Hence show that an A-module P is projective if and only if P is a direct summand of a free module.

23.2.B. EXERCISE. Show that projective modules are flat. Hint: Exercise 23.2.A. Be careful if you want to use Exercise 23.1.C; see Caution 23.1.3. (In fact, finitely generated projective modules over local rings are even free; see Remark 24.3.6.)
23.2.2. Definition: Derived functors.

The above description was low-tech, but immediately generalizes drastically. All we are using is that $M \otimes _ { A } .$ is a right-exact functor, and that for any A-module N, we can find a surjection $\mathsf { P } \longrightarrow \mathsf { N }$ from a projective module. In general, if F is any right-exact covariant functor from the category of A-modules to any abelian category, this construction will define a sequence of functors $\mathrm { L } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { F }$ such that ${ \mathrm { L } } _ { 0 } { \mathsf { F } } = { \mathsf { F } }$ and the $\mathrm { L } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { F } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { s }$ give a long exact sequence. We can make this more general still. We say that an abelian category has enough projectives if for any object N there is a surjection onto it from a projective object. Then if F is any right-exact covariant functor from an abelian category with enough projectives to any abelian category, then we can define the left derived functors to F, denoted by $\mathrm { L } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { F }$ $( \mathrm { i } \geq 0 )$ . You should reread §23.1 and see that throughout we only use the fact that we have a projective resolution (repeatedly lifting maps as in (23.2.1.1)), as well as the fact that F sends products to products (a consequence of additivity of
Chapter 23 Derived Functors
the functor; see Remark 1.5.2), to show that F applied to (23.1.2.1) preserves the exactness of the columns.
23.2.C. EXERCISE. The notion of an injective object in an abelian category is dual to the notion of a projective object.

list_items: (a) State precisely the definition of an injective object.

(b) Define derived functors for (i) covariant left-exact functors (these are called right derived functors), (ii) contravariant left-exact functors (also called right derived functors), and (iii) contravariant right-exact functors (these are called left derived functors), making explicit the necessary assumptions of the category having enough injectives or projectives.

23.2.3. Notation. Recall from Definition 23.2.2 that if F is a right-exact functor, its (left) derived functors are denoted by $\mathrm { L } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathrm { F }$ $( \mathrm { i } \geq 0 ,$ with $\mathrm { L } _ { 0 } \mathsf { F } = \mathsf { F }$ ). Similarly, if F is a left-exact functor, its (right-) derived functors are denoted by ${ \mathsf { R } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathsf { F } } .$ The i is a superscript, to indicate that the long exact sequence is “ascending in i.”
23.2.4. The Ext functors.

23.2.D. EASY EXERCISE (AND DEFINITION): Ext FUNCTORS FOR A-MODULES, FIRST VERSION. As $\mathrm { H o m } ( \cdot , \mathsf { N } )$ is a contravariant left-exact functor in $M o d _ { A } ,$ , which has enough projectives, define $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ as the ith right derived functor of $\mathrm { H o m } ( \cdot , \mathsf { N } ) .$ , applied to M. State the corresponding long exact sequence for Ext-modules.

23.2.E. EASY EXERCISE (AND DEFINITION): Ext FUNCTORS FOR A-MODULES, SECOND VERSION. The category $M o d _ { A }$ has enough injectives (see $\ S 2 3 . 2 . 5 )$ . As $\mathrm { H o m } ( M , \cdot )$ is a covariant left-exact functor in $M o d _ { \mathrm { A } } ,$ , define $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ as the ith right derived functor of ${ \mathrm { H o m } } ( M , \cdot ) ,$ applied to N. State the corresponding long exact sequence for Ext-modules.

We seem to have a problem with the previous two exercises: we have defined $\mathrm { E x t } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ twice, and we have two different long exact sequences! Fortunately, these two definitions of Ext agree (see Exercise 23.3.B), and two long exact sequences for Ext are better than one.

23.2.F. EASY EXERCISE. Use the definition of Ext in Exercise 23.2.D to show that if A is a Noetherian ring, and M and N are finitely generated A-modules, then $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ is a finitely generated A-module.
Ext-functors (for sheaves) will play a key role in the proof of Serre duality; see §29.2.

23.2.6.* Universal δ-functors.

(This discussion is best skipped on a first reading; you should move directly to §23.3. We won’t use the notion of δ-functors until §29.2.2.) We now describe a more general variant of derived functors, as you may use them in the discussion of Serre duality in Chapter 29. The advantage of the notion of universal δ-functor is that we can apply it even in cases where $\mathcal { A }$ does not have enough injectives.

Abstracting key properties of derived functors, we define the data of a (cohomological) δ-functor, from an abelian category $\mathcal { A }$ to another abelian category $\mathcal { B }$ . A δ-functor is a collection of additive functors ${ \mathsf { T } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ : ${ \mathcal { A } } \to { \mathcal { B } }$ (where ${ \mathsf { T } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ is taken to be 0 if $\mathfrak { i } < 0$ ), along with morphisms $\delta ^ { \ i } \colon \mathsf { T } ^ { \ i } ( \mathsf { A } ^ { \prime \prime } ) \to \mathsf { T } ^ { \ i + 1 } ( \mathsf { A } ^ { \prime } )$ →for each short exact sequence
in $\mathcal { A } ,$ , satisfying two properties:
(i) (short exact sequences yield long exact sequences) For each short exact sequence (23.2.6.1), the sequence

$$
\dots \longrightarrow T ^ {i - 1} (A ^ {\prime \prime}) \xrightarrow {\delta^ {i - 1}} T ^ {i} (A ^ {\prime}) \longrightarrow T ^ {i} (A) \longrightarrow T ^ {i} (A ^ {\prime \prime}) \xrightarrow {\delta^ {i}} T ^ {i + 1} (A ^ {\prime}) \longrightarrow \dots
$$
(where the unlabeled maps come from the covariance of the ${ \mathsf { T } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ ) is exact. In particular, ${ \mathsf { T } } ^ { 0 }$ is left-exact.
(ii) (functoriality of (i)) For each morphism of short exact sequences in $\mathcal { A }$
(where the squares commute), the $\delta ^ { \mathrm { i } \prime } \mathsf { \varepsilon }$ give a commutative diagram
(where the vertical arrows come from the covariance of ${ \mathsf { T } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ and $\mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { i } + 1 }$ ).
Derived functor cohomology is clearly an example of a δ-functor; Čech cohomology of sheaves on quasicompact separated schemes is another. (You can make these statements precise if you wish.)

23.2.L. EXERCISE. Figure out the right definition of morphism of δ-functors ${ \mathcal { A } } \to { \mathcal { B } }$ . (It should then be clear that the δ-functors from $\mathcal { A }$ to $\mathcal { B }$ form a category.)

23.2.8. Definition. A (cohomological) δ-functor $( \mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ is universal if for any other δ-functor $( \mathsf { T } ^ { \prime \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \prime \mathrm { i } } ) .$ , and any natural transformation of functors $\alpha \colon \mathsf { T } ^ { 0 } \to \mathsf { T } ^ { \prime 0 }$ , there is a unique morphism of δ-functors $( \mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \mathrm { i } } ) \to ( \mathsf { T } ^ { \prime \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \prime \mathrm { i } } )$ extending $\propto$ →. By universal property nonsense (and Exercise 23.2.L), given any covariant left-exact functor F: ${ \mathcal { A } } \to { \mathcal { B } } ,$ there is at most one universal δ-functor $( \mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ extending F (i.e., with a natural isomorphism ${ \sf T } ^ { 0 } \longleftrightarrow \sf F$ ). The key fact about universal δ-functors is the following.

23.2.9. Theorem — Suppose $( \mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ is a covariant δ-functor from $\mathcal { A }$ to ${ \mathcal { B } } ,$ , and for all $\boldsymbol { A } \in \mathcal { A } ,$ , there exists a monomorphism $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { A \longrightarrow J } w i t h \mathsf { T ^ { i } } \mathsf { J } { = } 0 } \end{array}$ for all $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ . Then $( \mathsf { T } ^ { \mathrm { i } } , \delta ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ is universal.

23.2.M.** EXERCISE. Prove Theorem 23.2.9. Partial hint: Motivated by Corollary 23.2.11 below, follow our discussion of derived functors. Better hint (because this exercise is hard): Follow the hints in [Weib, Exercise 2.4.5], or follow the proof of [Lan, Ch. XX, Thm. 7.1].

23.2.10. Remark. An additive functor F: ${ \mathcal { A } } \to { \mathcal { B } }$ is said to be effaceable if for every $\boldsymbol { A } \in \mathcal { A }$ , there is a monomorphism $\lambda  { \mathrm { J } }$ with $\mathsf { F } ( \boldsymbol { \mathrm { J } } ) = 0$ . The hypotheses of Theorem 23.2.9 can be weakened to require only that ${ \mathsf { T } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ is effaceable for each $\mathrm { i } > 0 ,$ and you are welcome to prove that instead. (Indeed, [Weib, Exercise 2.4.5], [Lan, Ch. XX, Thm. 7.1], and the original source [Gr1, II.2.2.1] deal with this case.) We give the statement of Theorem 23.2.9 for simplicity, as we will only use this version.

23.2.11. Corollary — If $\mathcal { A }$ has enough injectives, and F is a left-exact covariant functor $\mathcal { A } \xrightarrow { } \mathcal { B } _ { \boldsymbol { i } }$ , then the ${ \mathsf { R } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathsf { F } }$ (with the $\delta ^ { \mathrm i }$ that accompany them) form a universal δ-functor.

Proof. Each element of $\mathcal { A }$ admits a monomorphism into an injective element; this is just the definition of “enough injectives” (Exercise 23.2.C). Higher derived functors of an injective object I are always 0: just compute the higher derived functor by taking the injective resolution of I “by itself.” □
23.3 Derived Functors and Spectral Sequences

A number of useful facts can be easily proved using spectral sequences. By doing these exercises, you will lose any fear of spectral sequence arguments in similar situations, as you will realize they are all the same.
Before you read this section, you should read $\ S 1 . 6$ on spectral sequences.
23.3.1. Symmetry of Tor.

23.3.A. EXERCISE (SYMMETRY OF Tor). Show that there is an isomorphism $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , { \mathsf { N } } ) \longleftrightarrow$ $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( \mathsf { N } , M )$ . (Hint: Take a free resolution of M and a free resolution of N. Take their “product” to somehow produce a double complex. Use both orientations of the obvious spectral sequence and see what you get.)
