23.3.2. Derived functors can be computed using acyclic resolutions. Suppose F: $\mathcal { A } $ $\mathcal { B }$ is a right-exact additive functor of abelian categories, and that $\mathcal { A }$ has enough projectives. We
23.3 Derived Functors and Spectral Sequences

say that $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { A } } \in \mathcal { A }$ is F-acyclic (or just acyclic if the F is clear from context) if $\operatorname { L } _ { \mathrm { i } } \operatorname { F } A = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ . In Exercise 23.3.D, we will see that derived functors $\mathrm { L } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mathsf { F } \mathsf { B }$ of an object B of $\mathcal { A }$ can be computed by using “acyclic resolutions.” We set the stage with a useful construction.

23.3.3. Building a “projective resolution” of an exact sequence. Suppose $\cdot \cdot \cdot \longrightarrow \mathsf { E } _ { 2 } \longrightarrow \mathsf { E } _ { 1 } \longrightarrow \mathsf { E } _ { 0 } \longrightarrow 0$ is an exact sequence in an abelian category with enough projectives. We explain how to inductively build a double complex of projectives
such that the rows and columns are all exact. Suppose you have built part of the complex, and are trying to build the $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathfrak { m } , \mathfrak { n } }$ term:

For reasons that will soon become clear, we assume that for any $\mathsf { b } \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ whose image in both $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 2 }$ and $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 2 , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ is zero, there is a $\mathsf { c } \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ whose image in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ is $^ { \mathrm { ~ b , ~ } }$ and whose image in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 2 }$ is zero; and symmetrically there is a $\mathsf { c } ^ { \prime } \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ whose image in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ is b, and whose image in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 2 , \mathrm { n } }$ is zero.
Consider

$$
K := \ker \left(P _ {m, n - 1} \oplus P _ {m - 1, n} \to P _ {m, n - 2} \oplus P _ {m - 1, n - 1} \oplus P _ {m - 2, n}\right).
$$

If K surjects onto both $\ker ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } - 1 , \mathsf { n } } \longrightarrow \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } - 2 , \mathsf { n } } )$ ) and $\ker ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 1 } \to \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 2 } )$ , then we could take any surjection from a projective object $\mathsf { P } \to \mathsf { K }$ , then take $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathfrak { m } , \mathfrak { n } }$ to be P (with its map to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ and the negative of its map to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ ). With this choice, we would have ensured “horizontal exactness” at $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } } ,$ and “vertical exactness” at $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 } ,$ and commutativity of the square in (23.3.3.1).

We now verify our two desired surjections, by “diagram-chasing” (which we can do; see $\ S 1 . 5 . 5 )$ . Suppose $\mathsf { a } \in \ker ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } - 1 , \mathsf { n } } \longrightarrow \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } - 2 , \mathsf { n } } )$ , and let b be its image in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ . We wish to find $\mathsf { c } \in \ker ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 1 } \to \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 2 } )$ so that c maps to $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } }$ in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ and 0 in $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 2 }$ . (Then $( - c , \mathbf { a } ) \in$ $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 } \oplus \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ would be our desired element of K mapping to $\mathsf { a } \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } } )$ ). But such a c exits by our assumption! And, symmetrically, we get surjectivity $\mathsf { K } \to \mathsf { k e r } ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 1 } \to \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 2 } )$ .

→The final task is to ensure these assumptions hold for later stages in the building of our double complex. We need to ensure that for any $\mathsf { b } ^ { \prime } \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ that maps to $( 0 , 0 ) \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 } \oplus \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 2 , \mathrm { n } } ,$ there is an element of $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathfrak { m } , \mathfrak { n } }$ mapping to $( 0 , \mathsf { b } ^ { \prime } ) \in \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } - 1 } \oplus \mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } - 1 , \mathsf { n } } .$ . And we have to ensure the analogous
Chapter 23 Derived Functors

statement with the roles of m and n reversed. We take $Q ^ { \prime } =  { \ker ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m - 1 , n } } \to \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m - 1 , n - 1 } } \oplus \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m - 2 , n } } ) }$ , and $\scriptstyle Q ^ { \prime \prime } = \ker ( \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 } \to \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 2 } \oplus \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } - 1 } )$ , and take surjections $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime } \to \mathsf { Q } ^ { \prime }$ →and $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime \prime } \to \mathsf { Q } ^ { \prime \prime }$ from projec-→tive objects. Then take $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathsf { m } , \mathsf { n } }$ to be $\mathsf { P } \oplus \mathsf { P ^ { \prime } } \oplus \mathsf { P ^ { \prime \prime } }$ , where the maps from P to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ and $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ are as described above; the map $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime }$ to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ is the above-described map (via $\mathrm { ~ Q ~ } ^ { \prime }$ ); the map $\mathsf { P ^ { \prime } }$ to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ is zero; and the opposite for $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the map to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } - 1 , \mathrm { n } }$ is zero, and the map to $\mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { n } - 1 }$ is as implied above, by way of $\mathrm { ~ Q ~ } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ). The summand $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime }$ ensures our first desired assumption, and the summand $\mathsf { P } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ensures our second.

23.3.D. EXERCISE. Show that you can compute the derived functors of an objects B of $\mathcal { A }$ using acyclic resolutions (not just projective resolutions), i.e., by taking a resolution

by F-acyclic objects $\lambda _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ truncating, applying F, and taking homology. 23.3.4. The Grothendieck composition-of-functors spectral sequence.

Suppose A , B, and $\mathcal { C }$ are abelian categories, F: ${ \mathcal { A } } \to { \mathcal { B } }$ and $\hat { \mathbf { \Omega } } : \mathcal { B } \to \mathcal { C }$ are a left-exact additive covariant functors, and $\mathcal { A }$ and $\mathcal { B }$ have enough injectives. Thus right derived functors of F, G, and G  F exist. A reasonable question is: How are they related?

23.3.5. Theorem (Grothendieck composition-of-functors spectral sequence). — Suppose F : ${ \mathcal { A } } \to { \mathcal { B } }$ and G : $\mathcal { B } \xrightarrow { } \mathcal { C }$ are left-exact additive covariant functors, and $\mathcal { A }$ and $\mathcal { B }$ have enough injectives. Suppose further that F sends injective elements of $\mathcal { A }$ to G-acyclic elements of $\mathcal { B }$ . Then for each $\boldsymbol { x } \in \mathcal { A }$ , there is a spectral sequence with $\to \mathsf { E } _ { 2 } ^ { \flat , \mathsf { q } } = \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathsf { q } } \mathsf { G } ( \mathsf { R } ^ { \flat } \mathsf { F } ( \boldsymbol { X } ) )$ converging to $\mathsf { R } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathsf { G } \circ \mathsf { F } ) ( \boldsymbol { X } )$ .
We will soon see the Leray spectral sequence as an application (Theorem 23.4.5).

There is more one might want to extract from the proof of Theorem 23.3.5. For example, although E0 page of the spectral sequence will depend on some choices (of injective resolutions), the $\mathsf { E } _ { 2 }$ page will be independent of choice. For our applications, we won’t need this refinement.
We will have to work to establish Theorem 23.3.5, so the proof is possibly best skipped on a first reading.
23.3.6.* Proving Theorem 23.3.5.
Before we give the proof (in §23.3.8), we begin with some preliminaries to motivate it. In order to discuss derived functors applied to X, we choose an injective resolution of X:

$$

$$
To compute the derived functors $\mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \mathsf { F } ( { \mathsf { X } } )$ , we apply F to the injective resolution I•:

$$

$$

Note that $\mathsf { F } ( \mathrm { I } ^ { \mathrm { p } } )$ is G-acyclic, by hypothesis of Theorem 23.3.5. If we were to follow our nose, we might take simultaneous injective resolutions $\mathbf { I } ^ { \bullet , \bullet }$ of the terms in the above complex F(I•) (the “dual” of §23.3.3—note that only the columns are required to be exact), and apply G, and consider

Chapter 23 Derived Functors
23.4 Derived Functor Cohomology of $\mathcal { O }$ -Modules

We wish to apply the machinery of derived functors to define cohomology of quasicoherent sheaves on a scheme X. Rather than working in the category $Q C o h _ { \scriptscriptstyle { X } } ,$ for a number of reasons it is simpler to work in the larger category $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } }$ (but see Unimportant Remark 23.5.7).

list_items: 23.4.1. Theorem — Suppose $( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } )$ is a ringed space. Then the category of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -modules $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } }$ has enough injectives.

As a side benefit (of use to others more than us), taking ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X } = \mathbb { Z } ,$ we see that the category of sheaves of abelian groups on a fixed topological space has enough injectives.

We will exhibit an injection $\mathcal { F } \hookrightarrow \mathcal { Q } ^ { \prime }$ into an injective ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module. For each ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X ,$ , choose an inclusion ${ \mathcal { F } } _ { \mathfrak { p } } \hookrightarrow { \mathsf { Q } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ into an injective $\mathcal { O } _ { { X , \mathfrak { p } } }$ -module (which is possible as the category of $\mathcal { O } _ { { X , \mathfrak { p } } }$ -modules has enough injectives, Exercise 23.2.K).

We can now make a number of definitions.

23.4.3. Definition. If $( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } )$ is a ringed space, and $\mathcal { F }$ is an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module, define $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X , { \mathcal { F } } )$ as $\mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \Gamma ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { F } )$ . If furthermore π $: ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } ) \to ( \mathsf { Y } , \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { Y } } )$ is a map of ringed spaces, we have higher pushforwards (or derived pushforwards) $\mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \colon M o d _ { \sigma _ { \mathsf { X } } }  M o d _ { \sigma _ { \mathsf { Y } } }$ .

We have defined these notions earlier in special cases, for quasicoherent sheaves on quasicompact separated schemes (for ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ ), or for quasicompact separated morphisms of schemes (for $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * }$ ), in Chapter 18. We will soon (§23.5) show that these older definitions agree with Definition 23.4.3. Thus the derived functor definition applies much more generally than our Čech definition. But it is worthwhile to note that almost everything we use will come out of the Čech definition. A notable exception is the Leray spectral sequence, which we now discuss.
23.4.4. The Leray spectral sequence.

23.4.5. Theorem (Leray spectral sequence) — Suppose π: $( X , { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ) \to ( \Upsilon , { \mathcal { O } } _ { Y } )$ is a morphism of ringed spaces. For any ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -module $\mathcal { F }$ , there is a spectral sequence with $\mathsf { E } _ { 2 }$ term given by ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { q } } ( \mathsf { Y } , \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } )$ abutting to $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } + \mathsf { q } } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { F } )$ .

This is an immediate consequence of the Grothendieck composition-of-functors spectral sequence (Theorem 23.3.5) once we prove that the pushforward of an injective $\mathcal { O }$ -module is an acyclic $\mathcal { O }$ -module. We do this now.

23.4.6. Definition. We make an intermediate definition that is independently important. A sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on a topological space is flasque (also sometimes called flabby) if all restriction maps are surjective, i.e., if resU V : $\mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { V } ) \to \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { U } )$ is surjective for all $\mathsf { u } \subset \mathsf { V }$ .

text: 23.4.7. Extension by zero, an occasional left adjoint to the inverse image functor. In order to prove that injective sheaves are flasque (Exercise 23.4.H), we introduce the “extension by zero” functor. In addition to always being a left adjoint, $\pi ^ { - 1 }$ can sometimes be a right adjoint, when $\pi$ is an inclusion of an open subset. Note that $\mathrm { i } _ { ! } \mathcal { F }$ is an ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ -module, and that this defines a functor. 23.4.8.** The category of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -modules on a ringed space need not have enough projectives. In contrast to Theorem 23.4.1, the category of ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ -modules on a ringed space need not have enough projectives. For example, let X be $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 1 }$ with the Zariski-topology (in fact, we will need to know very little about X—only that it is not an Alexandrov space), but take ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X }$ to be the constant sheaf $\mathbb { Z }$ . We will see that $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } }$ —i.e., the category of sheaves of abelian groups on X—does not have enough projectives. If $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } }$ had enough projectives, then there would be a surjection ψ : $\mathsf { P } \to \mathbb { Z }$ from a projective sheaf. Fix a closed point $\mathsf { q } \in \boldsymbol { X }$ . We will show that the map on stalks $\psi _ { \mathrm { q } } : \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { q } } \to \underline { { \mathbb Z } } _ { \mathrm { q } }$ is the zero map, contradicting the surjectivity of $\psi$ . For each open subset U of $\Sigma ,$ denote by $\mathbb { Z } _ { \mathrm { u } }$ the extension by zero from U to X of the constant sheaf with values in $\mathbb { Z }$ (§23.4.7— $\underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { \mathrm { u } } ( \mathrm { V } ) = \mathbb { Z }$ if ${ \mathsf { V } } \subset { \mathsf { U } } ,$ and $\underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { \mathrm { u } } ( \mathrm { V } ) = 0$ otherwise). For each open neighborhood V of ${ \bf q } ,$ let W be a strictly smaller open neighborhood. Consider the surjection $\underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { \mathsf { X } - \mathsf { q } } \oplus \underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { W } \to \underline { { \mathbb { Z } } }$ . By projectivity of P, the surjection $\psi$ lifts to $\mathsf { P } \longrightarrow \underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { X - \mathsf { q } } \oplus \underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { W }$ . The map $\mathsf { P } ( \mathsf { V } ) \to \underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } ( \mathsf { V } )$ factors through $\underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { x - \mathbf { q } } ( \mathsf { V } ) \oplus \underline { { \mathbb { Z } } } _ { W } ( \mathsf { V } ) = 0 ,$ , and hence must be the zero map. Thus the map $\psi _ { \mathrm { q } } : \mathsf { P } _ { \mathrm { q } } \to \underline { { \mathbb Z } } _ { \mathrm { q } }$ is zero as well (do you see why?), as desired.
23.5 Č ech Cohomology and Derived Functor Cohomology Agree
We next prove that Čech cohomology and derived functor cohomology agree, where the former is defined.

23.5.1. Theorem — Suppose X is a quasicompact separated scheme, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf. Then the Čech cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ agrees with the derived functor cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ .

This statement is not as precise as it should be. We would want to know that this isomorphism is functorial in $\mathcal { F } ,$ and that it respects long exact sequences (so the connecting homomorphism defined for Čech cohomology agrees with that for derived functor cohomology). There is also an important extension to higher pushforwards. We leave these issues for the end of this section, §23.5.5.

In case you are curious: so long as it is defined appropriately, Čech cohomology agrees with derived functor cohomology in a wide variety of circumstances outside of scheme theory (if the underlying topological space is paracompact), but not always (see [Gr1, §3.8] for a counterexample). Remark 23.5.6 is also related.

The central idea in the proof (albeit with a twist) is a spectral sequence argument in the same style as those of §23.3, and uses two “cohomology-vanishing” ingredients, one for each orientation of the spectral sequence.
23.5 Č ech Cohomology and Derived Functor Cohomology Agree

23.5.6. Remark. If you wish, you can use the above argument to prove the following theorem of Leray. Suppose we have a sheaf of abelian groups $\mathcal { F }$ on a topological space X, and some covering $\{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \}$ of X such that the (derived functor) cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ in positive degree vanishes on every finite intersection of the $\mathrm { { u } _ { i } }$ . Then the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ can be calculated by the Čech cohomology of the cover $\{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} ;$ there is no need to pass to the colimit of all covers, as is the case for Čech cohomology in general.

23.5.7. Unimportant remark: Working in $Q C o h _ { X }$ rather than $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \times } }$ . In our definition of derived functors of quasicoherent sheaves on X, we could have tried to work in the category of quasicoherent sheaves $Q C o h _ { X }$ itself, rather than in the larger category $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } }$ . There are several reasons why this would require more effort. It is not hard to show that $Q C o h _ { X }$ has enough injectives if X is Noetherian (see, for example, [Ha1, Exer. III.3.6(a)]). Because we don’t have “extension by zero” (§23.4.7) in QCoh, the proofs that injective quasicoherent sheaves on an open set U restrict to injective quasicoherent sheaves on smaller open subsets V (the analog of Exercise 23.5.A) and that injective quasicoherent sheaves are flasque (the analog of Exercise 23.4.H) are harder. You can use this to show that ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ and $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * }$ computed in QCoh are the same as those computed in $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } }$ (once you make the statements precise). It is true that injective elements of $Q C o h _ { X }$ (X Noetherian) are injective in $M o d _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \times } }$ , but this requires work (see [Mur, Prop. 68]).

It is true that $Q C o h _ { X }$ has enough injectives for any scheme X, but this is much harder; see [EE]. And as is clear from the previous paragraph, “enough injectives” is only the beginning of what we want.

23.5.8. Unimportant remark. Theorem 23.5.1 implies that if π $: X \to \Upsilon$ is quasicompact and separated, then $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * }$ sends $Q C o h _ { X }$ to $Q C o h _ { \mathsf { Y } }$ →(by showing an isomorphism with Čech cohomology). If X and Y are Noetherian, the hypothesis “separated” can be relaxed to “quasiseparated,” using the ideas of Unimportant Remark 23.5.7. But because morphisms from a Noetherian scheme are automatically quasiseparated (Easy Exercise 8.3.B(b)), we can remove the redundant “quasiseparated” assumption. This sounds exciting, but it is not nearly as useful as the separated case, because without Čech cohomology, it is hard to compute anything.
Chapter 23 Derived Functors
Chapter 24
Flatness
The concept of flatness is a riddle that comes out of algebra, but which technically is the answer to many prayers.
—D. Mumford [Mu7, III.10]

We return to the important concept of flatness. We could have discussed flatness at length as soon as we had discussed quasicoherent sheaves and morphisms. But it is an unexpected idea, and the algebra and geometry are not obviously connected, so we have left it for relatively late. Serre has stated that he introduced flatness purely for reasons of algebra in his landmark “GAGA” paper [Se3], and that it was Grothendieck who recognized its geometric significance. “Exact” seems more descriptive terminology than “flat” (so we would have “exact modules” and “exact morphisms”), but this has not caught on.

24.0.1. A flat morphism π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is the right notion of a “nice,” or “nicely varying” family over Y. For example, if $\pi$ is a projective flat family over a connected Noetherian base (translation: π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a projective flat morphism, with Y connected and Noetherian), we will see that various numerical invariants of fibers are constant, including the dimension (§24.5.5), and numbers interpretable in terms of an Euler characteristic (see §24.7):

list_items: (a) the Hilbert polynomial (Corollary 24.7.2),
(b) the degree (in projective space) (Exercise 24.7.A(a)),

(b’) (as a special case of (b)) if $\pi$ is finite, the degree of $\pi$ (recovering and extending the fact that the degree of a projective map between regular curves is constant, §16.3.4; see Exercise 24.3.G and §24.3.12),
(c) the arithmetic genus (Exercise 24.7.A(b)),
(d) the degree of a line bundle if the fiber is a curve (Corollary 24.7.3), and
(e) intersections of divisors and line bundles (Exercise 24.7.D).

text: One might think that the right hypothesis might be smoothness, or more generally some sort of equisingularity, but we only need something weaker. And this is a good thing: branched covers are not fibrations in any traditional sense, yet they still behave well—the double cover $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } \to \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ given by $\mathfrak { y } \mapsto \mathfrak { x } ^ { 2 }$ has constant degree 2 (§10.3.4, revisited in §16.3.10). Another key example is that of a family of smooth curves degenerating to a nodal curve (Figure 24.1)—the topology of the (underlying analytic) curve changes, but the arithmetic genus remains constant. One can prove things about regular curves by first proving them about a

image_caption: Figure 24.1 A flat family of smooth curves degenerating to a nodal curve: $\mathtt { y } ^ { 2 } = \mathtt { x } ^ { 3 } + \mathtt { x } ^ { 2 } + \mathtt { t } \mathtt { x }$ .

text: nodal degeneration, and then showing that the result behaves well in flat families. Degeneration techniques such as this are ubiquitous in algebraic geometry.

Given the cohomological nature of the constancy of Euler characteristic result, you should not be surprised that the hypothesis needed (flatness) is cohomological in nature—it can be characterized by the vanishing of Tor (Exercise 23.1.C), which we use to great effect in §24.2.

But flatness is important for other reasons too. As a start: as this is the right notion of a “nice family,” it allows us to correctly define the notion of moduli space. For example, the Hilbert scheme of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ “parametrizes closed subschemes of $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ .” Maps from a scheme B to the Hilbert scheme correspond to (finitely presented) closed subschemes of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ flat over B. By universal property nonsense, this defines the Hilbert scheme up to unique isomorphism (although we of course must show that it exists, which takes some effort—see Theorem 25.3.1 for more). The moduli space of smooth projective curves is defined by the universal property that maps to the moduli space correspond to projective flat (finitely presented) families whose geometric fibers are smooth curves. (Sadly, this moduli space does not exist.) On a related note, flatness is central in deformation theory: it is key to understanding how schemes (and other geometric objects, such as vector bundles) can deform (cf. §21.5.12). Finally, the notion of Galois descent generalizes to (faithfully) “flat descent,” which allows us to “glue” in more exotic Grothendieck topologies in the same way we do in the Zariski topology (or more classical topologies); but this is beyond the scope of our current discussion.

Flatness has many aspects of different flavors, and it is easy to lose sight of the forest for the trees. Because the algebra of flatness seems so unrelated to the geometry, it can be nonintuitive. We will necessarily begin with algebraic foundations, but you should focus on the following points: methods of showing things are flat (both general criteria and explicit examples), and classification of flat modules over particular kinds of rings. You should try every exercise dealing with explicit examples such as these.
Here is an outline of the chapter, to help focus your attention.

list_items: • In §24.1, we discuss some of the easier facts, which are algebraic in nature.

• §24.2, §24.3, and $\ S 2 4 . 6$ give ideal-theoretic criteria for flatness. §24.2 and $\ S 2 4 . 3$ should be read together. The first uses Tor to understand flatness, and the second uses these insights to develop ideal-theoretic criteria for flatness. §24.6, on local criteria for flatness, is harder.

§24.5 is relatively freestanding, and could be read immediately after §24.1. It deals with topological aspects of flatness, such as the fact that flat morphisms are open maps in good situations. (Recall that a map of topological spaces is an open map if the image of every open set is an open set.)

• In §24.7, we explain the fact that “the Euler characteristic of quasicoherent sheaves is constant in flat families” (with appropriate hypotheses), and its many happy consequences. This section is surprisingly easy given its utility.

text: You should focus on what flatness implies and how to “picture” it, but also on explicit criteria for flatness in different situations, such as for integral domains (Observation 24.1.2), principal ideal domains (Exercise 24.3.B), discrete valuation rings (Exercise 24.3.C), the dual numbers (Exercise 24.3.D), and local rings (Theorem 24.3.5).
Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way. (See, for example, Figure 24.2, and Exercises 24.1.G(d), 24.1.L, and 24.3.H.)
—L. Tolstoy, Anna Karenina, [To, l. 1]
24.1 Easier Facts

Many facts about flatness are easy or immediate, although a number are tricky. As always, I will try to make clear which is which, to help you remember the easy facts and the key ideas of proofs of the harder facts. We will pick the low-hanging fruit first.
Chapter 24 Flatness

We recall the definition of a flat A-module (§1.5.13). If $M \in M o d _ { A }$ , $M \otimes _ { A } .$ is always right-exact (Exercise 1.2.H). We say that M is a flat A-module (or flat over A or A-flat) if $M \otimes _ { A } .$ is an exact functor. We say that a ring morphism $\mathrm { B } \to A$ is flat if A is flat as a B-module. (In particular, the algebra structure of A is irrelevant.)
24.1.1. Two key examples.

list_items: (i) Free A-modules (even of infinite rank) are clearly flat. More generally, projective modules are flat (Exercise 23.2.B).

(ii) Localizations are flat: Suppose S is a multiplicative subset of B. Then $\mathrm { B } \to { \mathsf { S } } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { B }$ is a flat ring morphism because localization is exact (Exercise 1.5.G(a)).

text: 24.1.A. EASY EXERCISE: FIRST EXAMPLES.

list_items: (a) (trick question) Classify flat modules over a field k.
(b) Show that $A [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ]$ is a flat A-module.

(c) Show that the ring morphism $\mathbb { Q } [ \mathsf { x } ] \to \mathbb { Q } [ \mathsf { y } ]$ , with $x \mapsto y ^ { 2 }$ , is flat. (This will help us understand Example 10.3.4 better; see §24.3.12.)

text: We make some quick but important observations.
24.1.2. Important observation. If $x$ is a non-zerodivisor of $\mathsf { A } ,$ and M is a flat A-module, then

$$
M \xrightarrow {\times x} M
$$

is injective. (Apply the exact functor $M \otimes _ { \cal A } .$ to the exact sequence $0 \xrightarrow { } \mathrm { A } \xrightarrow { \times x } \mathrm { A }$ .) In particular, flat modules are torsion-free. (Torsion-freeness was defined in §6.1.4.) This observation gives an easy way of recognizing when a module is not flat. We will use it many times.

list_items: 24.1.B. EXERCISE: ANOTHER EXAMPLE. Show that a finitely generated module over a principal ideal domain is flat if and only if it is torsion-free if and only if it is free. Hint: Remark 6.4.4 classifies finitely generated modules over a principal ideal domain. (Exercise 24.3.B sheds more light on flatness over a principal ideal domain. Proposition 14.3.2 is also related.)

24.1.C. EXERCISE (FLATNESS IS PRESERVED BY CHANGE OF BASE RING). Show that if M is a flat B-module, and $\mathrm { B } \to \bar { A }$ is a homomorphism, then $M \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } A$ is a flat A-module.
24.1.D. EXERCISE (FLATNESS IS PRESERVED BY COMPOSITION). Show that if A is a flat Balgebra, and M is A-flat, then M is also B-flat.

24.1.3. Proposition (flatness is a stalk/prime-local property) — An A-module M is flat if and only if $M _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a flat $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ -module for all prime ideals ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset A$ .

text: You are welcome to prove that it suffices to check only maximal ideals, but we don’t need this extension.

is also exact. Now (24.1.3.1) is also an exact sequence of A-modules, so

is exact (by A-flatness of $M$ ). But $M \otimes _ { \mathsf { A } } \mathsf { N }$ is canonically isomorphic to $M \otimes _ { A } { \cal A } _ { \mathfrak { p } } \otimes _ { A _ { \mathfrak { p } } } { \mathsf { N } } = M _ { \mathfrak { p } } \otimes _ { A _ { \mathfrak { p } } }$ N (and similarly for $\Nu ^ { \prime } , \Nu ^ { \prime \prime }$ ), so we can rewrite (24.1.3.3) as (24.1.3.2) (make sure you see why the maps in (24.1.3.3) and (24.1.3.2) are the same), as desired.

Assume next that $M _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a flat $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ -module for all p. Given any short exact sequence (24.1.3.1), we seek to show that (24.1.3.3) is exact. By right-exactness of tensor product (Exercise 1.2.H), (24.1.3.3) is exact except possibly on the left. Let K be $\operatorname { k e r } ( M \otimes _ { A } \mathsf { N } ^ { \prime } \to M \otimes _ { A } \mathsf { N } )$ ), so

is exact; we wish to show that $\mathsf { K } = 0$ . Given any ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \ r { i } }$ , localizing (24.1.3.1) at $\mathfrak { p }$ and tensoring with the exact $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ -module $M _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ yields

Localizing (24.1.3.4) at $\mathfrak { p }$ and using the isomorphisms $M _ { \mathfrak { p } } \otimes _ { A _ { \mathfrak { p } } } \mathsf { N } _ { \mathfrak { p } } \longleftrightarrow ( M \otimes _ { A } \mathsf { N } ) _ { \mathfrak { p } } ,$ we obtain the exact sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow K _ {p} \longrightarrow M _ {p} \otimes_ {A _ {p}} N _ {p} ^ {\prime} \longrightarrow M _ {p} \otimes_ {A _ {p}} N _ {p} \longrightarrow M _ {p} \otimes_ {A _ {p}} N _ {p} ^ {\prime \prime} \longrightarrow 0,
$$

which is the same as the exact sequence (24.1.3.5) except for the $K _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ . Hence ${ \mathsf K } _ { \mathfrak p } = 0$ for all primes ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset A ,$ so $\mathsf { K } = 0$ (by Exercise 4.1.F), as desired.
24.1.4. Flatness for schemes.
Motivated by Proposition 24.1.3, the extension of the notion of flatness to schemes is straightforward.

24.1.5. Definition: Flat quasicoherent sheaf. We say that a quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on a scheme X is flat at ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ if ${ \mathcal { F } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a flat $\mathcal { O } _ { { X , \mathfrak { p } } }$ -module. We say that a quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on a scheme $x$ is flat (over X) if it is flat at all ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ . In light of Proposition 24.1.3, we can check this notion on an affine open cover of $x$ .

24.1.6. Definition: Flat morphism. Similarly, we say that a morphism of schemes $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is flat at ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ if $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { p } }$ is a flat $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) }$ -module. We say that a morphism of schemes π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is flat if it is flat at all ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ . We can check flatness locally on the source and the target.
We can combine these two definitions into a single fancy definition.

24.1.7. Definition: Flat quasicoherent sheaf over a base. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on X. We say that $\mathcal { F }$ is flat (over Y) at ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ if $\mathcal { F } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a flat $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \pi ( \mathfrak { p } ) }$ -module. We say that $\mathcal { F }$ is flat (over Y) if it is flat at all $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ .

Definitions 24.1.5 and 24.1.6 correspond to the cases $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { \Upsilon }$ and $\mathcal { F } = \mathcal { O } _ { X } ,$ respectively. (Definition 24.1.7 applies without change to the category of ringed spaces, but we won’t use this.)
24.1.E. EASY EXERCISE (REALITY CHECK). Show that open embeddings are flat.
Our results about flatness over rings above carry over easily to schemes.

24.1.F. EXERCISE. Show that a map of rings $\mathrm { B } \to \bar { A }$ is flat if and only if the corresponding morphism of schemes Spec A Spec B is flat. More generally, if $\mathrm { B } \to \bar { A }$ is a map of rings, and M is an A-module, show that $M$ → is B-flat if and only if $\widetilde { M }$ → is flat over Spec B. (Be careful: this requires more fthan merely invoking Proposition 24.1.3.) Hint: Working indirectly in both directions is a good way to proceed. “Suppose $M _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is not $\mathrm { B } _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ -flat. Then there is an exact sequence of $\mathrm { B } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ -modules (hence also B-modules) $0 \to \mathsf { N } ^ { \prime } \to \mathsf { N } \to \mathsf { N } ^ { \prime \prime } \to 0$ such that . . . .” “Suppose $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ is not B-flat. Then there is an exact sequence of B-modules . . . .”

Thus if $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is an affine morphism, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on X, then $\mathcal { F }$ is flat over Y if and only if $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is flat over Y.

24.1.9. Theorem (cohomology commutes with flat base change) — Suppose

is a Cartesian diagram, and $\pi$ (and thus $\pi ^ { \prime }$ ) is quasicompact and separated (so higher pushforwards of quasicoherent sheaves by $\pi$ and $\pi ^ { \prime }$ exist, as described in $\$ 18.7$ . Suppose also that ρ is flat, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on X. Then the natural “push-pull” morphisms (Exercise 18.7.B(a)) $\rho ^ { * } ( \mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ) $ $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol \rho ^ { \prime * } \mathcal { F } )$ are isomorphisms.

24.1.M. EXERCISE. Prove Theorem 24.1.9. Hint: Exercise 18.7.B(b) is the special case where ρ is affine. Extend it to the quasicompact and separated case using the same idea as the solution to Exercise 14.4.C (which used Exercise 6.2.G). Your proof of the case $\mathfrak { i } = 0$ will only need a quasiseparated hypothesis in place of the separated hypothesis.

A useful special case is where ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is the generic point of a reduced component of Y. In other words, in light of Exercise 24.1.G(a), the stalk of the higher pushforward of $\mathcal { F }$ at the generic point is the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ on the fiber over the generic point. This is a first example of something important: understanding cohomology of (quasicoherent sheaves on) fibers in terms of higher pushforwards. (We would certainly hope that higher pushforwards would tell us something about higher cohomology of fibers, but this is certainly not a priori clear!) In comparison to this result, which shows that cohomology of any quasicoherent sheaf commutes with flat base change, $\ S 2 4 . 7$ and Chapter 25 deal with when and how cohomology of a flat quasicoherent sheaf commutes with any base change.
24.1.10. Pulling back closed subschemes (and ideal sheaves) by flat morphisms.

Closed subschemes pull back particularly well under flat morphisms, and this can be helpful to keep in mind. As pointed out in Remark 14.5.9, in the case of flat morphisms, pullback of ideal sheaves as quasicoherent sheaves agrees with pullback in terms of the pullback of the corresponding closed subschemes. In other words, closed subscheme exact sequences pull back (remain exact) under flat pullbacks. This is in fact not just a necessary condition for flatness; it is also sufficient, which can be shown using the ideal-theoretic criterion for flatness (Theorem 24.3.1). There is an analogous fact about pulling ideal sheaves of flat subschemes by arbitrary pullbacks; see §24.2.2.

text: 24.2 Flatness through Tor

We defined the Tor (bi-)functor in §23.1: $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ is obtained by taking a free resolution of N, removing the N, tensoring it with $M _ { ☉ }$ , and taking homology. Exercise 23.1.C characterized flatness in terms of Tor: M is A-flat if $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \cal A } ( { \cal M } , { \sf N } ) = 0$ for all N. In this section, we reap the easier benefits of this characterization, recalling key properties of Tor when needed. In §24.3, we work harder to extract more from Tor.
It is sometimes possible to compute Tor from its definition, as shown in the following exercise that we will use repeatedly.

24.2.A. EXERCISE. Define $( M ; x )$ as $\{ \mathfrak { m } \in \mathfrak { M } : \mathtt { x m } = 0 \} \subset \mathfrak { M }$ —it consists of the elements of M annihilated by $x$ . If $x$ is not a zerodivisor, show that

$$
\operatorname {T o r} _ {i} ^ {A} (M, A / (x)) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} M / x M & \text {i f} i = 0; \\ (M: x) & \text {i f} i = 1; \\ 0 & \text {i f} i > 1. \end{array} \right.
$$
Hint: Use the resolution

$$
0 \longrightarrow A \xrightarrow {\times x} A \longrightarrow A / (x) \longrightarrow 0
$$
of $\boldsymbol { A } / ( \boldsymbol { x } )$

24.2.1. Remark. As a corollary of Exercise 24.2.A, we see again that flat modules over an integral domain are torsion-free (and, more generally, Observation 24.1.2). Also, Exercise 24.2.A gives the reason for the notation Tor—it is short for torsion.
24.2 Flatness through Tor

24.2.B. EXERCISE. If B is A-flat, use the FHHF Theorem (Exercise 1.5.I(c)) to give an isomorphism $\mathsf { B } \otimes _ { A } \mathsf { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathsf { A } } ( \mathsf { M } , \mathsf { N } ) \longleftrightarrow \mathsf { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathsf { B } } ( \mathsf { B } \otimes \mathsf { M } , \mathsf { B } \otimes \mathsf { N } )$ .

Recall that the Tor functor is symmetric in its entries (there is an isomorphism $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , { \mathsf { N } } ) \longleftrightarrow$ $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( \mathsf { N } , M )$ , Exercise 23.3.A). This gives us a quick but very useful result.

We would have cared about this result long before seeing Tor, so it gives some motivation for learning about Tor. (Unimportant side question: Can you prove this without Tor, using a diagram chase?)

24.2.2. Pulling back quasicoherent ideal sheaves of flat closed subschemes by arbitrary morphisms (promised in §24.1.10). Suppose

is a fibered product, and $V \hookrightarrow X$ is a closed subscheme. Then $\Upsilon \times _ { Z } \vee$ is a closed subscheme of W (§10.2.2). 24.2.3.* The Künneth formula for quasicoherent sheaves.

It is worthwhile to know that the Künneth formula holds for quasicoherent sheaves, and that the proof is reasonable. But we will not use it, so you can read this section only if you particularly feel like it.

(Partial hint: The Cancellation Theorem 11.1.1 for affine morphisms.)

Suppose further that ${ X = \cup _ { \mathrm { i = 1 } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \mathrm { U } \mathrm { } \mathrm { } \mathrm { i } }$ is a cover of $x$ by a finite number of affine open sets, so X is quasicompact. (We still assume X separated.) Denote the cover by $\mathfrak { U } : = \{ \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } } \}$ . As usual with Čech covers, for ${ \mathrm { I } } \subset \{ 1 , \ldots , \mathsf { n } \} ,$ define ${ \mathrm { U } } _ { \mathrm { I } } = \cap _ { \mathrm { i \in I } } { \mathrm { U } } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ and let $\mathfrak { j } ^ { \mathrm { I } } : \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { I } } \hookrightarrow X$ be the corresponding open embedding.

$$
0 \longrightarrow \oplus_ {| I | = 1} j _ {*} ^ {I} \mathcal {O} u _ {I} \longrightarrow \oplus_ {| I | = 2} j _ {*} ^ {I} \mathcal {O} u _ {I} \longrightarrow \dots \longrightarrow \oplus_ {| I | = n} j _ {*} ^ {I} \mathcal {O} u _ {I} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

Define the augmented Čech complex of sheaves for ${ \boldsymbol { \mathsf { X } } } = \{ { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { U } } } _ { \mathrm { i } } \} ,$ denoted by $\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { C } _ { \mathrm { X , a u g } } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathcal { F } ) . } \end{array}$ , by prepending $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { X } } = \oplus _ { | \mathrm { I } | = 0 } \mathrm { j } _ { * } ^ { \mathrm { I } } \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { U } _ { \mathrm { I } } }$ :

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathcal {O} _ {X} \longrightarrow \oplus_ {| I | = 1} j _ {*} ^ {I} \mathcal {O} _ {U _ {I}} \longrightarrow \oplus_ {| I | = 2} j _ {*} ^ {I} \mathcal {O} _ {U _ {I}} \longrightarrow \dots \longrightarrow \oplus_ {| I | = n} j _ {*} ^ {I} \mathcal {O} _ {U _ {I}} \longrightarrow 0.
$$
(Be sure you understand the definitions of the maps in these complexes.)

24.2.J. EXERCISE. Show that the augmented Čech complex of sheaves $\mathrm { C } _ { \mathrm { } X , \mathrm { a u g } } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathfrak { U } )$ is an exact sequence of flat quasicoherent sheaves on X. (Hint: Check on any affine open subset of X.)

Before proceeding further, we prove a useful homological statement. Recall that a finite exact sequence of flat modules remains exact upon tensoring with any other module (Exercise 24.2.F). Recall also that an exact sequence of modules remains exact upon tensoring with any flat module (a version of the FHHF theorem, Exercise 1.5.I(c)). The following statement generalizes both of these naturally.

24.2.K. EXERCISE. Suppose $C ^ { \bullet }$ is a finite exact sequence of A-modules, and $\mathsf { F } ^ { \bullet }$ is a finite exact sequence of flat A-modules. Show that the total complex of the double complex $\Gamma ^ { \bullet } \otimes _ { \mathrm { A } } \mathsf { F } ^ { \bullet }$ is also an exact sequence of A-modules. (If you wish, you might show that if ${ \mathbb C } ^ { \bullet }$ is merely a complex, not necessarily exact, then $\Gamma ^ { \bullet } \otimes _ { \mathrm { A } } \mathsf { F } ^ { \bullet }$ has “the same cohomology” as $C ^ { \bullet }$ , further extending the FHHF theorem. You may also wish to remove the finiteness assumptions irrelevant to your solution.)

24.2.L. EXERCISE. We return to the situation of Exercise 24.2.J. Suppose $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf (not necessarily flat) on X (which is quasicompact and separated). Show that $\mathcal { F } \otimes C _ { x , \mathrm { a u g } } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathfrak { U } )$ is an exact sequence sequence of quasicoherent sheaves on X. (Equivalently, $\mathcal { F } \otimes C _ { x } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathfrak { U } )$ is a complex, exact except at the first step, where it has kernel/cohmology sheaf canonically identified with $\mathcal { F }$ .) Hint: Check on each affine open subset of X.
Following Serre, you might interpret Exercises 24.2.J and 24.2.L in terms of partitions of unity.

We use these pleasant exact sequences to prove a form of the Künneth formula. Suppose that X and Y are both quasicompact separated $\mathtt { k }$ -schemes (e.g., varieties over k). Name the projection maps $\pi _ { \mathbb { X } } : \mathbb { X } \times _ { \mathbb { k } } \mathbb { Y } \to X$ and $\pi _ { \Upsilon } : X \times _ { \mathbf { k } } \Upsilon  \Upsilon .$ Let $\mathfrak { U } _ { X }$ and $\mathfrak { U } _ { \Upsilon }$ be finite covers of X and Y (respectively) by affine open sets.

24.2.M. EXERCISE. Show that $\pi _ { x } ^ { * } C _ { x , \mathrm { a u g } } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathfrak { U } _ { x } )$ is an exact complex of flat quasicoherent sheaves on $X \times _ { \mathsf { k } } \ Y$ .

Define $\Gamma _ { \mathrm { } } ^ { \bullet } \boxtimes \Gamma _ { \mathrm { } } ^ { \bullet } : = \pi _ { \mathrm { } } ^ { \ast } \mathbf { } C _ { \mathrm { } } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathfrak { U } _ { \mathrm { } } ) \otimes \pi _ { \mathrm { Y } } ^ { \ast } \mathbf { } C _ { \mathrm { Y } } ^ { \bullet } ( \mathfrak { U } _ { \mathrm { Y } } ) ,$ , interpreted as the total complex associated to the double complex of the right side.

24.2.N. EXERCISE. Show that $\mathrm { C } _ { \mathrm { X } } ^ { \bullet } \boxtimes \mathrm { C } _ { \mathrm { Y } } ^ { \bullet }$ is a complex of flat quasicoherent sheaves on ${ \boldsymbol { \times } } \times _ { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } } { \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ,$ , exact except at the first step, where the cohomology/kernel is canonically identified with ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { X \times Y }$ . (You may find it helpful to prove a similarly statement for a similar defined ${ \mathrm { C } } _ { \mathrm { { X , a u g } } } ^ { \bullet } \boxtimes { \mathrm { C } } _ { \mathrm { { Y , a u g } } } ^ { \bullet }$ .)
24.2 Flatness through Tor

24.2.O. EXERCISE. Suppose now that $\mathcal { F }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ and $\mathcal { G }$ is a quasicoherent sheaf on Y. Show that $\left( \mathcal { F } \boxtimes \mathcal { G } \right) \otimes \left( \mathbf { C } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \bullet } \boxtimes \mathbf { C } _ { \mathsf { Y } } ^ { \bullet } \right)$ is a complex of quasicoherent sheaves on ${ \mathsf X } \times _ { \mathsf k } { \mathsf Y } ,$ exact except at the first step, where the cohomology/kernel is canonically identified with $\mathcal { F }$ - $\mathcal { G }$ .

By suitably identifying $\left( \mathcal { F } \boxtimes \mathcal { G } \right) \otimes \left( \mathbf { C } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \bullet } \boxtimes \mathbf { C } _ { \mathsf { Y } } ^ { \bullet } \right)$ with $\left( \mathcal { F } \otimes \mathsf { C } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \bullet } \right) \boxtimes \left( \mathcal { G } \otimes \mathsf { C } _ { \mathsf { Y } } ^ { \bullet } \right)$ , show that for all n,

$$
\mathrm {H} ^ {n} \left(X \times_ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {Y}, \mathcal {F} \boxtimes \mathcal {G}\right) = \oplus_ {\mathrm {i} + \mathrm {j} = \mathrm {n}} \mathrm {H} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \left(X, \mathcal {F}\right) \otimes_ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {H} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \left(\Upsilon , \mathcal {G}\right).
$$

(Hint: Don’t use spectral sequences; work directly with the complexes. Show by direct calculation that if you have two complexes of k-modules, the cohomology of the total complex of their tensor product is the “direct sum of the tensor product of their cohomologies.”)
24.2.4. Remarks on hypotheses. Why did we require quasicoherence? Where did we use the fact that we were working over a field k?

24.2.5.** Extensions. Given quasicompact separated morphisms of schemes $\boldsymbol \rho ^ { x } : x \to Z$ and $\boldsymbol { \rho } ^ { \mathsf { Y } } : \mathsf { Y }  \mathsf { Z } ,$ the resulting $\boldsymbol { \rho } ^ { x \times Y } : X \times z Y  Z ,$ and quasicoherent sheaves $\mathcal { F }$ and X and $\mathcal { G }$ →on Y, what →is the relationship between $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \rho _ { * } ^ { x \times \Upsilon } \mathcal { F } \mathbb { E } _ { Z } \mathcal { G } , \mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \rho _ { * } ^ { x } \mathcal { F } , } \end{array}$ and ${ \sf R } ^ { \mathrm { j } } \rho _ { \ast } ^ { \mathrm { Y } } \mathcal { G } ?$ By following through your proof, you may be able to extend the statement of Exercise 24.2.P considerably.
24.3 Ideal-Theoretic Criteria for Flatness

The following theorem will allow us to classify flat modules over a number of rings. It is a refined version of Exercise 23.1.C, that M is a flat A-module if and only if $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathsf { A } } ( M , \mathsf { N } ) { = } 0$ for all A-modules N.

24.3.1. Theorem (ideal-theoretic criterion for flatness) — The A-module M is flat if and only $i f \mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( \mathrm { M } , \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { I } ) { = } 0$ for every ideal I.

24.3.2. Remarks. Before getting to the proof, we make some side remarks that may give some insight into how to think about flatness. Theorem 24.3.1 is profitably stated without the theory of Tor. It is equivalent to the statement that M is flat if and only if for all ideals I A, $\mathrm { I } \otimes _ { \mathrm { A } } M \to M$ is an injection, and you can reinterpret the proof in this guise. Perhaps better, M is flat if and only if $\mathrm { I } \otimes _ { \mathrm { A } } M \to \mathrm { I } M$ is an isomorphism for every ideal I.

Flatness is often informally described as “continuously varying fibers,” and this can be made more precise as follows. An A-module M is flat if and only if it restricts nicely to closed subschemes of Spec A. More precisely, what we lose in this restriction, the submodule IM of elements which “vanish on $Z , \prime \prime$ is easy to understand: it consists of formal linear combinations of elements ${ \mathrm { i } } \otimes { \mathrm { m } } ,$ with no surprise relations among them—i.e., the tensor product $\operatorname { I } \otimes _ { A } M$ . This is the content of the following exercise, in which you may use Theorem 24.3.1.

24.3.A.* EXERCISE (THE EQUATIONAL CRITERION FOR FLATNESS). Show that an A-module M is flat if and only if for every relation $\sum { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathrm { m } } _ { \mathrm { i } } = 0$ with $\mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathsf { A }$ and $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in M _ { \cdot }$ , there exist $\mathfrak { m } _ { \mathrm { j } } ^ { \prime } \in M$ and $\mathbf { a } _ { \mathrm { i j } } \in A$ such that $\sum _ { \mathrm { j } } { \bf a } _ { \mathrm { i j } } \mathsf { m } _ { \mathrm { j } } ^ { \prime } = \mathsf { m } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ for all i and $\sum _ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { i } } { \mathrm { a } } _ { \mathrm { i } \mathrm { j } } = 0$ in A for all j. (Translation: Whenever elements of $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ satisfy an A-linear relation, this is “because” of linear equations holding in A.)

24.3.3. Unimportant remark. In the statement of Theorem 24.3.1, it suffices to check only finitely generated ideals. This is essentially the content of the following statement, which you can prove if you wish: Show that an A-module M is flat if and only if for all finitely generated ideals I, the natural map $\mathrm { I } \otimes _ { \mathrm { A } } M \to M$ is an injection. Hint: Use a counterexample for an ideal J that is not finitely generated to find another counterexample for an ideal I that is finitely generated.
Chapter 24 Flatness

24.3.4. Proof of the ideal-theoretic criterion for flatness, Theorem 24.3.1. By Exercise 23.1.C, we need only show that $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { A } } ( M , \mathrm { A / I } ) = 0$ for all I implies $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \cal A } ( { \cal M } , { \sf N } ) = 0$ for all A-modules N, and hence that M is flat.

We first prove that $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathsf { A } } ( M , { \mathsf { N } } ) { = } 0$ for all finitely generated modules N, by induction on the number n of generators $\mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { a } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ of N. The base case (if $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ , so $\mathsf { N } \cong \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { A n n } ( \mathsf { a } _ { 1 } )$ —our first use of the annihilator ideal in a long time) is our assumption. If $\mathfrak { n } > 1$ , then $\mathrm { A a } _ { \mathrm { n } } \cong \mathrm { A } / \mathrm { A n n } ( \mathrm { a } _ { \mathrm { n } } )$ is a submodule of N, and the quotient Q is generated by the images of $\mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \dots , \mathbf { a } _ { n - 1 } ,$ so the result follows by considering the Tor1 portion of the Tor long exact sequence for

$$
0 \longrightarrow A / \operatorname {A n n} \left(a _ {n}\right) \longrightarrow N \longrightarrow Q \longrightarrow 0.
$$

We deal with the case of general N by abstract nonsense. Notice that N is the union of its finitely generated submodules $\{ \mathsf { N } _ { \alpha } \}$ . In fancy language, this union is a filtered colimit—any two finitely generated submodules are contained in a finitely generated submodule (specifically, the submodule they generate). Filtered colimits of modules commute with cohomology (Exercise 1.5.M), so $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ( M , \mathsf { N } )$ is the colimit over $\propto$ of $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ( { \cal M } , { \sf N } _ { \alpha } ) = 0 \mathrm { \small { ~ \Omega ~ } }$ , and is thus 0.
We now use Theorem 24.3.1 to get explicit characterizations of flat modules over three (types of) rings: principal ideal domains, dual numbers, and some local rings.
Recall Observation 24.1.2, that flatness implies torsion-free. The converse is true for principal ideal domains:
24.3.B. EXERCISE (FLAT $=$ TORSION-FREE FOR A PID). Show that a module over a principal ideal domain is flat if and only if it is torsion-free.

24.3.C. EXERCISE (FLATNESS OVER A DVR). Suppose M is a module over a discrete valuation ring A with uniformizer t. Show that M is flat if and only if t is not a zerodivisor on M, i.e., (using the notation defined in Exercise 24.2.A) $( M : \mathfrak { t } ) = 0 .$ (See Exercise 24.1.B for the case of finitely generated modules.) This yields a simple and very important geometric interpretation of flatness over a regular curve, which we discuss in $\ S 2 4 . 3 . 9$ .

24.3.D. EXERCISE (FLATNESS OVER THE DUAL NUMBERS). Show that M is flat over k[t]/ $\left( \mathrm { t } ^ { 2 } \right)$ if and only if the “multiplication by t” map M/tM tM is an isomorphism. (This fact is important in deformation theory and elsewhere.) Hint: k[t]/ $\big ( \mathrm { t } ^ { 2 } \big )$ has only three ideals.

24.3.5. Important theorem (flat $=$ free $=$ projective for finitely presented modules over local rings) — Suppose (A, m) is a local ring (not necessarily Noetherian), and M is a finitely presented A-module. Then the following are equivalent.

list_items: (a) M is free.
(b) M is projective.
(c) M is flat.
(d) $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { A } ( M , A / { \mathfrak { m } } ) = 0 .$

text: 24.3.6. Remarks. Warning: Modules over local rings can be flat without being free: $\mathbb { Q }$ is a flat $\mathbb { Z } _ { ( \mathtt { p } ) }$ - algebra $\left( \mathbb { Z } _ { \mathtt { ( p ) } } \right.$ is the localization of $\mathbb { Z }$ at p, not the p-adics), as all localizations are flat (§24.1.1), but it is not free (do you see why?).

Also, non-Noetherian people may be pleased to know that with a little work, “finitely presented” can be weakened to “finitely generated”: use [Mat2, Thm. 7.10] in the proof below, where finite presentation comes up.
Finally, Proposition 24.4.5 is a close variant of Theorem 24.3.5 for graded rings.

Proof. For any ring, free modules are projective (§23.2.1), and projective modules are flat (Exercise 23.2.B), and flat modules M satisfy $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \cal A } ( { \cal M } , { \sf N } ) = 0$ for any N (Exercise 23.1.C), so we need only show that if $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { A } ( M , A / mathfrak { m } ) = 0 ,$ , then M is free.
24.3 Ideal-Theoretic Criteria for Flatness

(At this point, you should see Nakayama coming from a mile away.) Now $M / { \mathfrak { m } } M$ is a finitedimensional vector space over the field $A / { \mathfrak { m } }$ . Choose a basis of $M / { \mathfrak { m } } M ,$ , and lift it to elements $\mathfrak { m } _ { 1 } , . . . , \mathfrak { m } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \in M$ . Consider $\mathsf { A } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { n } } \to M$ given by $e _ { \mathrm { i } } \mapsto { \mathsf { m } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . We will show this is an isomorphism. It is surjective by Nakayama’s Lemma (see Exercise 8.2.H): the image is all of M modulo the maximal ideal, hence is everything. As M is finitely presented, by Lemma 6.4.6 (“finitely presented implies always finitely presented”), the kernel K is finitely generated. Tensor $0 \to { \mathsf { K } } \to { \mathsf { A } } ^ { \oplus { \mathsf { n } } } \to { \mathsf { M } } \to 0$ with $A / { \mathfrak { m } }$ . As M is flat, the result is still exact (Exercise 24.2.C):

$$
0 \longrightarrow K / \mathfrak {m} K \longrightarrow (A / \mathfrak {m}) ^ {\oplus n} \longrightarrow M / \mathfrak {m} M \longrightarrow 0.
$$

But $( A / { \mathfrak { m } } ) ^ { \oplus { \mathfrak { n } } } {  } M / { \mathfrak { m } } M$ is an isomorphism by construction, so ${ \mathsf { K } } / { \mathfrak { m } } { \mathsf { K } } = 0 .$ . As K is finitely generated, $\mathsf { K } = 0$ by Nakayama’s Lemma 8.2.9. □
Here is an immediate and useful corollary—really just a geometric interpretation.
24.3.7. Corollary (flat $=$ locally free for finitely presented sheaves) — A finitely presented sheaf F on X is flat (over X) if and only if it is locally free.
Proof. Local freeness of a finitely presented sheaf can be checked at the stalks, Exercise 14.3.E.
24.3.E. EXERCISE. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a finite flat morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a finite rank locally free sheaf on X.

list_items: (a) Show that $\pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }$ is a finite rank locally free sheaf on Y.

(b) If Y is irreducible with generic point ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ the degree of $\pi$ above $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { n } }$ is ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ and $\mathcal { F }$ is locally free of rank r, show that $\pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }$ is locally free of rank nr. (This may be trickier than it looks.)
24.3.8. Remark. In particular, taking ${ \mathcal { F } } = { \mathcal { O } } _ { X } ,$ we see that in the locally Noetherian setting, finite flat morphisms have locally constant degree.

24.3.F.* EXERCISE (INTERESTING VARIANT OF THEOREM 24.3.5, BUT UNIMPORTANT FOR US). Suppose A is a ring (not necessarily local), and $M$ is a finitely presented A-module. Show that M is flat if and only if it is projective. Hint: Show that M is projective if and only if $M _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ is free for every maximal ideal m. The harder direction of this implication uses the fact that $\mathrm { H o m } _ { A _ { \mathfrak { m } } } ( M _ { \mathfrak { m } } , \mathsf { N } _ { \mathfrak { m } } ) { = } \mathrm { H o m } _ { A } ( M , \mathsf { N } ) _ { \mathfrak { m } } ,$ which follows from Exercise 1.5.H. (Remark: There exist finitely generated flat modules that are not projective. They are necessarily not finitely presented. Example without proof: Let $\begin{array} { r } { \boldsymbol { A } = \prod _ { \mathrm { i } = 1 } ^ { \infty } \mathbb { F } _ { 2 } , } \end{array}$ interpreted as functions $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 } \to \mathbb { Z } / 2 ,$ and let M be the module of functions modulo those of proper support, i.e., those vanishing at almost all points of $\mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ .)

24.3.G. EXERCISE. Prove the following useful criterion for flatness: Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a finite morphism, and Y is reduced and locally Noetherian. Then $\pi$ is flat if and only if $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ is locally free, if and only if the rank of $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X }$ is locally constant $( \dim _ { \kappa ( { \mathfrak { q } } ) } ( \pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { X } ) _ { \mathfrak { q } } \otimes \kappa ( { \mathfrak { q } } )$ is a locally constant function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ ). Partial hint: Exercise 14.3.K.

24.3.H. EXERCISE. Show that the normalization of the node (see Figure 8.4) is not flat. Hint: Use Exercise 24.3.G. (This exercise can be strengthened to show that nontrivial normalizations are never flat.)
24.3.I. EXERCISE. In $\mathbb { A } _ { \sf k } ^ { 4 } = \sf { S p e c k } [ w , x , y , z ] .$ $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbf { k } } ^ { 4 } =$ , let X be the union of the wx-plane with the yz-plane:

text: The projection $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 4 } \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 }$ given by ${ \bf k } [ { \bf a } , { \bf b } ]  { \bf k } [ w , x , y , z ]$ with ${ \mathfrak { a } } \longmapsto w - y , \ { \mathfrak { b } } \longmapsto x - z$ restricts to a morphism $\Chi \to \mathbb { A } _ { \sf k } ^ { 2 }$ → →. Show that this morphism is not flat.
24.3.9. Flat families over regular curves. Exercise 24.3.C gives an elegant geometric criterion for when morphisms to regular curves are flat.
Chapter 24 Flatness

24.3.J. EXERCISE (CRITERION FOR FLATNESS OVER A REGULAR CURVE). Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism from a locally Noetherian scheme to a regular (locally Noetherian) curve. (The local Noetherian hypothesis on X is so we can discuss its associated points.) Show that π is flat if and only if all associated points of X map to a generic point of Y. (This is a partial converse to Exercise 24.1.K, that flat maps always send associated points to associated points. As with Exercise 24.1.K, feel free to immediately generalize your argument to a coherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on X.)

24.3.10. For example, a nonconstant map from an integral (locally Noetherian) scheme to a regular curve must be flat. (As another example, the deformation to the normal cone, discussed in the double-starred section $\ S 2 2 . 4 . 1 7$ and depicted in Figure 22.3, is flat.) Exercise 24.3.H (and the comment after it) shows that the regular condition is necessary. The example of two planes meeting at a point in Exercise 24.3.I shows that the dimension 1 condition is necessary.

24.3.11.* Unimportant remark: A valuative criterion for flatness. Exercise 24.3.J shows that flatness over a regular curve is geometrically intuitive (and is “visualizable”). It gives a criterion for flatness in general: Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is finitely presented morphism. If $\pi$ is flat, then for every morphism $\Upsilon ^ { \prime }  \Upsilon$ where ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is the Spec of a discrete valuation ring, $\pi ^ { \prime } \colon X \times _ { Y } \Upsilon ^ { \prime } \to Y ^ { \prime }$ is flat, so no associated points of $\mathsf { X } \times \mathsf { \boldsymbol { Y } } ^ { \prime }$ map to the closed point of ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ . If Y is reduced and locally Noetherian, then this is a sufficient condition; this can reasonably be called a valuative criterion for flatness. (Reducedness is necessary: consider Exercise 24.1.G(d).) This gives an excellent way to visualize flatness, which you should try to put into words (perhaps after learning about flat limits below). See [Gr-EGA, $\mathrm { I V } _ { 3 }$ .11.8.1] for a proof (and an extension without Noetherian hypotheses).

24.3.12. Revisiting the degree of a projective morphism from a curve to a regular curve. As hinted after the statement of Proposition 16.3.5, we can now better understand why nonconstant projective morphisms from a curve to a regular curve have a well-defined degree, which can be determined by taking the preimage of any point (§16.3.4). (Example 10.3.4 was particularly enlightening.) This is because such maps are flat by Exercise 24.3.J, and then the degree is constant by Remark 24.3.8 (see also Exercise 24.3.G). Also, Exercise 24.3.G yields a new proof of Proposition 16.3.5.

24.3.13.* Flat limits. Here is an important consequence of Exercise 24.3.J, which we can informally state as: We can take flat limits over one-parameter families. More precisely: suppose A is a discrete valuation ring, and let 0 be the closed point of Spec A and η the generic point. Suppose X is a locally Noetherian scheme over A, and Y is a closed subscheme of $X | _ { \eta }$ . Let ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ be the schemetheoretic closure of Y in X. Then $\pi \colon \Upsilon ^ { \prime } \to$ Spec A has no associated points over 0 by Exercise 9.4.D, so ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is flat over Spec A by Exercise 24.3.J. Similarly, suppose Z is a one-dimensional Noetherian scheme, 0 is a regular point of Z, and π: $x \to Z$ is a morphism from a locally Noetherian scheme to Z. If Y is a closed subscheme of $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( Z \backslash \{ 0 \} )$ , flat over $Z \backslash \{ 0 \} ,$ and ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is the scheme-theoretic closure of Y in X, then ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is flat over Z. In both cases, the closure $\Upsilon ^ { \prime } | _ { 0 }$ is often called the flat limit of Y. (Feel free to weaken the Noetherian hypotheses on X.)

24.3.K. EXERCISE. Suppose (with the language of the previous paragraph) that A is a discrete valuation ring, X is a locally Noetherian A-scheme, and Y is a closed subscheme of the generic fiber $X | _ { \eta }$ . Show that there is only one closed subscheme ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ of X such that $\mathsf { Y } ^ { \prime } | _ { \mathsf { \Pi } } = \mathsf { Y } ,$ , and ${ \boldsymbol { \Upsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ is flat over A.

text: 24.3 Ideal-Theoretic Criteria for Flatness

24.3.M. EXERCISE. Suppose ${ \mathsf C } = { \mathsf V } ( { \mathsf f } ( x , y ) )$ is a smooth plane curve in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 2 } ,$ and p is a closed point of C, so $\mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { C } , \mathsf { p } }$ is a discrete valuation ring. Consider the family of pairs of points of C, parametrized by $\mathsf { q } \in { \mathsf { C } } \setminus \{ \mathsf { p } \} ,$ given by {p, q}. Explain why the flat limit over p (the “limit as $\mathsf { q } \mapsto \mathsf { p } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ) can be identified with the tangent direction to C at p. Translation: The tangent line is indeed the limit of secants, without deltas and epsilons.

24.3.N. HARDER EXERCISE (AN EXPLICIT FLAT LIMIT). Let $\begin{array} { r } { \boldsymbol { X } = \mathbb { A } ^ { 3 } \times \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }  \boldsymbol { Z } = \mathbb { A } ^ { 1 } } \end{array}$ over a field $\mathrm { k } ,$ where the coordinates on $\mathbb { A } ^ { 3 }$ are $x , y ,$ , and $z ,$ and the coordinates on $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ are t. Define Y away from $\mathrm { t } = 0$ as the union of the two lines $y = z = 0$ (the $x$ -axis) and $x = z - \mathrm { t } = 0$ (the $_ { 9 }$ -axis translated by t). Find the flat limit at $\mathrm { t } = 0$ . (Hints: (i) It is not the union of the two axes, although it includes this union. The flat limit is nonreduced at the node, and the “fuzz” points out of the plane they are contained in. (ii) $( \mathfrak { y } , z ) ( \mathfrak { x } , z ) \neq ( \mathfrak { x } \mathfrak { y } , z )$ . (iii) Once you have a candidate flat limit, be sure to check that it is the flat limit. (iv) If you get stuck, read Example 24.3.14 below.)

Consider a projective version of the previous example, where two lines in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ degenerate to meet. The limit consists of two lines meeting at a node, with some nonreduced structure at the node. Before the two lines come together, their space of global sections is two-dimensional. When they come together, it is not immediately obvious that their flat limit also has two-dimensional space of global sections as well. The reduced version (the union of the two lines meeting at a point) has a one-dimensional space of global sections, but the effect of the nonreduced structure on the space of global sections may not be immediately clear. However, we will see that “cohomology groups can only jump up in flat limits,” as a consequence (indeed, the main moral) of the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1.

24.3.14.** Example of variation of cohomology groups in flat families. We can use a variant of Exercise 24.3.N to see an example of a cohomology group actually jumping. We work over an algebraically closed field to avoid distractions. Before we get down to explicit algebra, here is the general idea. Consider a twisted cubic C in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ . A projection ${ \mathsf { p r } } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ from a random point $\mathsf { p } \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ will take C to a nodal plane cubic curve. Picture this projection “dynamically,” by choosing coordinates so p is at [1, 0, 0, 0], and considering the map $\Phi _ { \mathrm { t } } \colon [ w , x , y , z ] \mapsto [ \mathrm { t } w , x , y , z ] ; \Phi _ { 1 }$ is the identity on $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ , $\Phi \mathrm { t }$ is an automorphism of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ for $\mathrm { t } \neq 0 .$ , and $\Phi _ { 0 }$ is the projection. The limit of $\phi _ { \mathrm { t } } ( C )$ as $\mathrm { t } \to 0$ will be a nodal cubic, with nonreduced structure at the node “analytically the same” as what we saw when two lines came together in Exercise 24.3.N. (The phrase “analytically the same” can be made precise once we define completions in $\ S 2 8 .$ .)

Let’s now see this in practice. Rather than working directly with the twisted cubic, we use another example where we saw a similar picture. Consider the nodal (affine) plane cubic ${ \mathfrak { y } } ^ { 2 } =$ $\boldsymbol { x } ^ { 3 } + \boldsymbol { x } ^ { 2 }$ . We use the variable m rather than t (used in §8.3.5) in order to reserve t for the parameter for the flat family.

What are the equations for C? Clearly, they include ethe equations $\mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } = \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 } + \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 }$ and $y = \mathsf { m } x ,$ e, but these are not enough—the m-axis (i.e., $\scriptstyle x = y = 0$ ) is also in $\mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } - \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 } - \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 } , \mathsf { y } - \mathsf { m } \mathsf { x } )$ . A little thought (and the algebra we have seen earlier in this example) will make clear that we have a third equation $\mathsf { x } = \mathsf { m } ^ { 2 } - 1 ,$ which along with $y = \mathsf { m } x$ implies $\mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } = \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 } + \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 }$ . At time $\mathrm { t } = 1$ , we “start with” C, and at time $\mathrm { t } = 0$ we have (set-theoretically) C. Thus at time $\mathrm { t } \neq 0 ,$ , the

curve $\widetilde { \mathrm { C } }$ is sent to the curve

$$
The family over Spec k[t, $\mathrm { t } ^ { - 1 } ]$ is thus

Thus in this example we see that $\left( \mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathcal { O } ) , \mathsf { h } ^ { 1 } ( \mathcal { O } ) \right) = \left( 1 , 0 \right)$ for the general member of the family (twisted cubics are isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ ), and the special member (the flat limit) has $\left( \mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathcal { O } ) , \mathsf { h } ^ { 1 } ( \mathcal { O } ) \right) = ( 2 , 1 )$ . Notice that both cohomology groups have jumped, yet the Euler characteristic has remained the same. The first behavior, as stated after Exercise 24.3.N, is an example of the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1. The second, constancy of Euler characteristics in flat families, is the subject of §24.7. (It is no coincidence that the example had a singular limit; see §25.1.3.)
24.4** Aside: The Koszul Complex and the Hilbert Syzygy Theorem

To show that the theory we have developed so far is already very useful, we use it to prove the Hilbert Syzygy Theorem 16.1.3. This in turn gives us an excuse to introduce an important tool in commutative algebra, the Koszul complex. We will not use the contents of this section, so you should read it only if you feel like it.

24.4.1. The Koszul complex. Suppose E is a rank r free A-module. Given an element $s \in \mathbb { E } ^ { \vee }$ , i.e., an A-linear map $s : \mathbb { E } \to \lambda ,$ the Koszul complex K (s) associated to s is the chain complex:

$$
0 \longrightarrow \wedge^ {r} E \xrightarrow {d _ {r}} \wedge^ {r - 1} E \xrightarrow {d _ {r - 1}} \dots \xrightarrow {d _ {2}} \wedge^ {1} E \xrightarrow {d _ {1}} A \longrightarrow 0.
$$
The differential is defined by

$$
d _ {k} \left(e _ {1} \wedge \dots \wedge e _ {k}\right) = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {k} (- 1) ^ {i + 1} s \left(e _ {i}\right) e _ {1} \wedge \dots \wedge \hat {e} _ {i} \wedge \dots \wedge e _ {k},
$$

where $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { \mathrm { r } } \in \mathsf { E }$ (and $\hat { e } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ as usual means “omit the term $\boldsymbol { e } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ). It is not hard to see that this is a complex (and it also follows from Exercise 24.4.B). It is a little harder to see that it is well-defined.
Chapter 24 Flatness

For convenience, choose a basis of $\scriptstyle \mathrm { E , }$ and let suppose $s { : } A ^ { \oplus \ r } \to A$ sends the ith basis element of $\mathsf { E } = \mathsf { A } ^ { \oplus \mathsf { r } }$ to $\mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . In this case we denote Koszul complex by $\mathsf { K } _ { \bullet } ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { r } )$ .

$$

$$

Hence if $\mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ is a regular sequence, the Koszul complex gives a free resolution of $\boldsymbol { A } / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } )$ of length r, called the Koszul resolution of $\boldsymbol { A } / ( \mathsf { f } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { f } _ { \mathrm { r } } )$ .

24.4.2. Remarks. (a) The ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 2$ case of the Koszul complex implicitly came up in the proof of Theorem 9.5.6—it is the total complex of the tiny double complex (9.5.6.1). (b) One can similarly take the Koszul complex of an A-module $\mathsf { M } \colon \mathsf { K } _ { \bullet } ( s , M ) \mathrel { \mathop : } = \mathsf { K } _ { \bullet } ( s ) \otimes _ { A } M$ . (c) The cohomology of the Koszul complex is called Koszul homology. It measures the failure of a sequence to be regular.

24.5 Topological Implications of Flatness

We now discuss some topological aspects and consequences of flatness, which boil down to the Going-Down Theorem for flat morphisms (§24.5.3), which in turn comes from faithful flatness. Because dimension in algebraic geometry is a topological notion, we will show that dimensions of fibers behave well in flat families (§24.5.5).
24.5.1. Faithful flatness. The notion of faithful flatness is handy for many reasons, and we describe only a few. A B-module M is faithfully flat if for all complexes of B-modules

$$
N ^ {\prime} \longrightarrow N \longrightarrow N ^ {\prime \prime},
$$
Chapter 24 Flatness
(24.5.1.1) is exact if and only if $( 2 4 . 5 . 1 . 1 ) { \otimes } _ { \mathrm { B } } M$ is exact. A B-algebra A is faithfully flat if it is faithfully flat as a B-module.
24.5.A. EXERCISE. Show that a flat B-module M is faithfully flat if and only if for all B-modules N $\downarrow , M \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathsf { N } = 0$ implies that ${ \mathsf N } = 0$ .

list_items: (a) M is faithfully flat;
(b) for all prime ideals ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset { \mathrm { B } } .$ , $M \otimes _ { \mathbb { B } } \mathsf { \kappa } ( \mathsf { p } )$ is nonzero;
(c) for all maximal ideals ${ \mathfrak { m } } \subset { \mathfrak { B } }$ , $M \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \kappa ( \mathfrak { m } ) = M / \mathfrak { m } M$ is nonzero.

text: Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes. We say that $\pi$ is faithfully flat if it is flat and surjective. (Unlike flatness, faithful flatness is not particularly useful for quasicoherent sheaves, so we do not define faithfully flat quasicoherent sheaves over a base.)

24.5.2. Faithfully flat morphisms behave “reasonably.” Faithfully flat morphisms behave “reasonably” in the sense of §8.1 (preserved by composition and base change, and local on the target) because both flatness and surjectivity have this property (§24.1.8 and Exercise 10.4.G, respectively).

(b) Hence show that every flat (local) morphism of local rings (Definition 7.3.1) is faithfully flat. (Morphisms of local rings are assumed to be local, i.e., the maximal ideal pulls back to the maximal ideal.)
