(The statement is not coincidentally reminiscent of the Going-Down Theorem for finite extensions of integrally closed domains, Theorem 12.2.12.)
24.5.E. EXERCISE (GOING-DOWN THEOREM FOR FLAT MORPHISMS).

list_items: (a) Suppose that $\mathrm { B } \to A$ is a flat morphism of rings, corresponding to a map $\pi$ : Spec $A \to S { \mathrm { p e c } } \mathrm { B }$ . Suppose ${ \mathfrak { q } } \subset { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime }$ are prime ideals of B, and $\mathfrak { p ^ { \prime } }$ is a prime ideal of A with $\pi ( [ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \prime } ] ) = [ { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \prime } ]$ . Show that there exists a prime ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset { \mathfrak { p ^ { \prime } } }$ of A with $\pi ( \left[ { \mathfrak { p } } \right] ) = \left[ { \mathfrak { q } } \right]$ . Hint: Show that $\mathrm { B } _ { \mathfrak { q ^ { \prime } } } \to \bar { A } _ { \mathfrak { p ^ { \prime } } }$ is a flat local ring homomorphism, and hence faithfully flat by the Exercise 24.5.D(b).
(b) Part (a) gives a geometric consequence of flatness. Draw a picture illustrating this.
(c) Recall the Going-Up Theorem, described in §8.2.4. State the Going-Down Theorem for flat morphisms in a way parallel to Exercise 8.2.F, and prove it.

text: 24.5.F. EXERCISE. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is an integral (e.g., finite) flat morphism, and Y has pure dimension n. Show that X has pure dimension n. (This generalizes Exercise 12.1.H(a).) Hint: π satisfies both Going-Up (see Exercise 8.2.F) and Going-Down (Exercise 24.5.E).

24.5.G. IMPORTANT EXERCISE: FLAT MORPHISMS ARE OPEN MAPS (IN REASONABLE SITUATIONS). Suppose π: $\colon X \to \Upsilon$ is locally of finite type and flat, and Y (and hence X) is locally Noetherian. Show that $\pi$ is an open map (i.e., sends open sets to open sets). Hint: Reduce to showing that $\pi ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is open for all such π. Reduce to the case where X and Y are both affine. Use Chevalley’s Theorem 8.4.2 to show that $\pi ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is constructible. Use the Going-Down Theorem for flat morphisms, Exercise 24.5.E, to show that $\pi ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is stable under generization. Conclude using Exercise 8.4.C.
24.5 Topological Implications of Flatness
24.5.4. Remarks.

list_items: (i) Of course, not all open morphisms are flat: witness $\mathrm { 3 p e c k [ t ] / ( t ) }  \mathrm { S p e c k [ t ] / ( t ^ { 2 } ) }$ .
(ii) Also, in quite reasonable circumstances, flat morphisms are not open: witness Spec $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } ( \boldsymbol { \mathrm { t } } ) $ Spec k[t] (flat by Example 24.1.1(ii)).

text: 24.5.H.* EXERCISE. Weaken the hypotheses of “locally of finite type” and “locally Noetherian” to just “locally finitely presented.” Hint: As with the similar generalization in Exercise 10.3.I of Chevalley’s Theorem 8.4.2, use the fact that any such morphism is “locally” pulled back from a Noetherian situation.
24.5.5. Dimensions of fibers are well-behaved for flat morphisms.

24.5.6. Proposition — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a flat morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, with p ∈ X and $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ such that $\pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } ) = { \mathfrak { q } }$ . Then

$$
\operatorname {c o d i m} _ {X} p = \operatorname {c o d i m} _ {Y} q + \operatorname {c o d i m} _ {\pi^ {- 1} (q)} p
$$
(see Figure 12.4).

Informal translation: The dimension of the fibers is the difference of the dimensions of X and Y (at least locally). Compare this to Exercise 12.4.A, which stated that without the flatness hypothesis, we would only have inequality $( \leq )$ .

24.5.I. EXERCISE. Prove Proposition 24.5.6 as follows. As just mentioned, Exercise 12.4.A gives one inequality, so show the other. Given a chain of irreducible closed subsets in Y containing q, and a chain of irreducible closed subsets in $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathfrak { q } ) \subset X$ containing p, construct a chain of irreducible closed subsets in X containing p, using the Going-Down Theorem for flat morphisms (Exercise 24.5.E).

As a consequence of Proposition 24.5.6, if π $: X \to \mathsf { Y }$ is a flat map of irreducible varieties, then the fibers of $\pi$ all have pure dimension $\dim X - \dim Y$ . (Warning: $\operatorname { S p e c k [ t ] } / ( { \mathrm { t } } ) \to \operatorname { S p e c k [ t ] } / ( { \mathrm { t } } ^ { 2 } )$ does not exhibit dimensional jumping of fibers, is open, and sends associated points to associated points—cf. Exercise 24.1.K—but is not flat. If you prefer a reduced example, the normalization of the cuspidal plane cubic Spe $c \mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { t } ] \to { \mathrm { S p e c k } } [ \mathbf { x } , \mathbf { y } ] / ( \mathbf { y } ^ { 2 } - \mathbf { x } ^ { 3 } ) ,$ , shown in Figure 10.5, also has these properties.) This leads us to the following useful definition.

24.5.7. Definition. If $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a flat morphism that is locally of finite type, and all fibers of $\pi$ have pure dimension ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ we say that $\pi$ is flat of relative dimension n. (In particular, when one says a morphism is flat of relative dimension n, the locally finite type hypotheses are implied. Remark 24.5.9 motivates this hypothesis.)

24.5.J. EXERCISE. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is a flat morphism of finite type k-schemes, and Y is pure dimensional (so “codimension is the difference of dimensions”; cf. Theorem 12.2.9). Show that the following are equivalent.

list_items: (i) The scheme X has pure dimension $\dim \Upsilon + { \mathfrak { n } }$
(ii) The morphism $\pi$ is flat of relative dimension n.

24.5.K. EXERCISE. Show that the notion of a morphism being “flat of relative dimension $\mathrm { \Omega } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \prime \prime$ is preserved by arbitrary base change. Hint to show that fiber dimension is preserved: Exercise 12.2.M.

24.5.L. EXERCISE. Suppose $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ and $\rho \colon \Upsilon  Z$ are flat of relative dimension m and n, respectively (hence locally of finite type). Show that $\rho \circ \pi$ is flat of relative dimension $\mathsf { m } + \mathsf { n }$ .
24.5.8. Remark. For practice, you can now give a second solution to Exercise 12.2.F, that dimension is additive for products of varieties.

In particular, we want our notion of “flatness of relative dimension $\mathrm { \Omega } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \prime \prime$ to be preserved by base change. Consider the Cartesian diagram

24.5.10. Morphisms to Spec k are universally open. We now deliver on a promise made in §10.5.6.
24.5.11. Theorem — Every morphism $x $ Spec k is universally open, i.e., remains open after any base change.

24.5.12. Fancier flatness facts without proof.
We conclude with a couple of important facts, although we won’t use them, so we will not prove them.

”Generic flatness” would be better called “general flatness” (cf. the discussion after Definition 3.6.11). Because we’re not proving it, we may as well state it in some generality. For a slick proof of generic flatness under Noetherian hypotheses, see [Mu2, pp. 57–58].
24.5 Topological Implications of Flatness

24.5.13. Theorem (generic flatness, [Stacks, tag 052B]) — If π: X Y is a finite type morphism of schemes, Y is reduced, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a finite type quasicoherent sheaf on X, then there is a dense open subscheme U  Y such that $\mathcal { F } \big | _ { \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } ) }$ is of finite presentation over $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathsf { U } )$ , and flat over U.

24.5.14. Interpretation of the degree of a generically finite morphism. We use this to interpret the degree of a rational map of varieties in terms of counting preimages, fulfilling a promise made in $\ S 1 2 . 2 . 2$ . Suppose ${ \sf c } \colon \mathsf X \longrightarrow \mathsf Y$ is a generically finite rational map of k-varieties of degree d. For simplicity we assume that X and Y are irreducible. (Feel free to relax this.) Replace X by an open subset on which $\pi$ is a morphism. By Proposition 12.4.4 (“generically finite implies generally finite”), there is a dense open subset V of Y over which $\pi$ is finite. By Theorem 24.5.13, there is a dense open subset $V ^ { \prime }$ of V over which $\pi$ is finite and flat. Then by Remark 24.3.8 (“finite flat morphisms have locally constant degree”), over this open subset $V ^ { \prime }$ , π has “locally constant degree,” which is necessarily d.

24.5.15. The flat locus is open (flatness is an open condition). Generic flatness can be used to show one last topological aspect of flatness: in reasonable circumstances, the locus where a quasicoherent sheaf is flat over a base is an open subset. More precisely:
24.5.16. Theorem (the flat locus is open) — Suppose π: X Y is a locally finite type morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X.

list_items: (a) The locus of points of $x$ at which $\mathcal { F }$ is Y-flat is an open subset of X.

(b) If π is closed (e.g., proper), then the locus of points of Y over which F is flat (i.e. those q  Y for which $\mathcal { F }$ is flat at all $\mathfrak { p } \in \pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathfrak { q } ) )$ is an open subset of Y.

text: Part (b) follows immediately from part (a). Part (a) reduces to a nontrivial statement in commutative algebra; see, for example, [Mat2, Thm. 24.3] or [Gr-EGA, $\mathrm { I V } _ { 3 }$ .11.1.1]. As is often the case, Noetherian hypotheses can be dropped in exchange for local finite presentation hypotheses on the morphism $\pi$ ; see [Gr-EGA, IV3.11.3.1] or [Stacks, tag 00RC].
24.6 Local Criteria for Flatness
For a Noetherian local ring, there is a greatly improved version of the ideal-theoretic criterion of Theorem 24.3.1: we need check only one ideal—the maximal ideal.

24.6.1. Theorem (local criterion for flatness) — Suppose $\left( \mathtt { B } , \mathtt { n } \right) \longrightarrow \left( A , \mathtt { m } \right)$ is a local morphism of Noetherian local rings (§7.3.1), and that M is a finitely generated A-module. Then M is B-flat if and only $i f \mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( \boldsymbol { M } , \mathrm { B } / { \mathfrak { n } } ) = 0$ .
This is a miracle: flatness over all of Spec B is determined by what happens over the closed point.

We have already seen Theorem 24.6.1 in a special case: if $( \mathbb { B } , \mathfrak { n } )  ( \mathcal { A } , \mathfrak { m } )$ is an isomorphism, it is an immediate consequence of Theorem 24.3.5.
The following proof is due to Ogus, [OB]. To highlight what makes it work, we make a temporary and unneeded definition.

24.6.2. Definition. Suppose F : $\mathcal { B } \xrightarrow { } \mathcal { A }$ is an additive functor of abelian categories. For the sake of this section only, we say F is half-exact if for every short exact sequence $0 \to M ^ { \prime } \to M \to M ^ { \prime \prime } \to 0$ in $\mathcal { B } , \mathsf { F } M ^ { \prime } \to \mathsf { F } M \to \mathsf { F } M ^ { \prime \prime }$ is exact in $\mathcal { A }$ . Examples are left-exact functors, right-exact functors, and derived functors of either.

24.6.3. Theorem (Nakayama’s Lemma for half-exact functors) — Suppose $\Gamma : ( \mathbb { B } , \mathfrak { n } ) $ $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a local morphism of Noetherian local rings, and the additive functor $\mathsf { T } : f . g . M o d _ { \mathrm { B } } \to f . g . M o d _ { \mathrm { A } }$ is B-linear and half-exact. If ${ \sf T } ( \mathrm { B } / { \sf n } ) = 0$ then $\mathsf { T } ( \mathsf { N } ) = 0$ for all finitely generated B-modules N (i.e., T is the zero functor).

If $\mathrm { I } = \mathrm { B }$ , then ${ \mathsf N } = 0$ , so ${ \mathsf { T } } ( { \mathsf { N } } ) = 0 .$ If $\operatorname { I } = { \mathfrak { n } }$ , then $\mathsf { N } = ( \mathsf { B } / \mathsf { n } ) ^ { \oplus \mathsf { m } }$ (N Noetherian implies m is finite), so ${ \sf T } ( { \sf N } ) { \sf = } { \sf T } ( { \sf B } / { \sf n } ) ^ { \oplus \sf m } { \sf = } 0 .$ . 24.6.4. Proof of Theorem 24.6.1. Suppose $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ is finitely generated A-module. One direction is easy: if $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ is flat over B, then $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M , \mathrm { B / } { \mathfrak { n } } ) = 0$ by the Tor criterion (Exercise 23.1.C).

For the harder direction, suppose now that $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M , \mathrm { B / } { \mathfrak { n } } ) = 0$ . Define $\mathsf { T } : f . g . M o d _ { \mathrm { B } } \to f . g . M o d _ { \mathrm { A } }$ by ${ \mathsf { N } } \mapsto { \mathrm { T o r } } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M , { \mathsf { N } } )$ . Then T is A-linear (do you see why?) and half-exact.

By Nakayama’s Lemma for half-exact functors, $\mathsf { T } ( \mathsf { B } / \mathsf { n } ) = 0$ implies that $\mathsf { T } ( \mathsf { N } ) = 0$ for all finitely generated B-modules, and, in particular, all B/I, from which we see that M is flat over B by the ideal-theoretic criterion for flatness (Theorem 24.3.1). □
24.6.5. Slicing criteria for flatness.

A useful variant of the local criterion is the following. Suppose t is a non-zerodivisor of B in n (geometrically: an effective Cartier divisor on the target passing through the closed point). If M is flat over B, then t is not a zerodivisor of M (Observation 24.1.2). Also, $M / { \mathrm { t } } M$ is a flat B/tB-module (flatness commutes with base change, Exercise 24.1.J). The next result says that this is a characterization of flatness, at least when M is finitely generated, or somewhat more generally.

24.6.6. Theorem (slicing criterion for flatness on the target, see Figure 24.3) — Suppose $( \mathbb { B } , \mathfrak { n } )  ( \mathcal { A } , \mathfrak { m } )$ is a local morphism of Noetherian local rings, M is a finitely generated A-module, and t  n is a non-zerodivisor on B. Then M is B-flat if and only if

image_caption: Figure 24.3 The (necessary and sufficient) slicing criterion for flatness on the target, Theorem 24.6.6.

list_items: (i) t is not a zerodivisor on M, and
(ii) M/tM is flat over B/(t).

text: 24.6 Local Criteria for Flatness
(For slicing criteria for other properties, see Exercises 13.2.C, Theorem 24.6.9, and Theorem 26.2.3.)
To prove this, we show a lemma that we will also use to prove Theorem 24.6.14. (Notice that it has no hypotheses of local rings or finite generation.)

24.6.7. Lemma — Suppose $\mathrm { I } \subset \mathrm { B }$ is an ideal, and $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { M } }$ is a B-module. Suppose that (i) $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { I } , M ) = 0 ,$ , and (ii) M/IM is flat over B/I. Then $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { J } , M ) = 0$ for every ideal J containing I.

Proof. The functor $\otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { J }$ is the composition of the functor $\otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { I }$ with the functor $\otimes _ { \mathrm { B / I } } \mathrm { B / J }$ . From the Grothendieck composition-of-functors spectral sequence (§23.3.5), there is a spectral sequence with $\mathsf { E } _ { 2 }$ given by $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathbf { q } } ^ { \mathsf { B } \hat { / } \mathrm { I } } ( \mathsf { B } / \mathrm { J } , \mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \mathsf { B } } ( \mathsf { B } / \mathrm { I } , M ) ]$ converging to $\mathrm { T o r } _ { \mathfrak { p } + \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \mathtt { B } } ( \mathtt { B } / \mathfrak { J } , M )$ . The hypotheses of the theorem guarantee that

$$
\operatorname {T o r} _ {0} ^ {\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {I}} (\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {J}, \operatorname {T o r} _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {B}} (\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {I}, \mathrm {M})) = 0
$$
by (i), and

$$
\operatorname {T o r} _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {I}} (\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {J}, \operatorname {T o r} _ {0} ^ {\mathrm {B}} (\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {I}, \mathrm {M})) = \operatorname {T o r} _ {1} ^ {\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {I}} (\mathrm {B} / \mathrm {J}, \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {I M}) = 0
$$

by (ii) (using the ideal-theoretic criterion for flatness for $\mathrm { B / I } ,$ Theorem 24.3.1). Thus $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { J } , M ) =$ $0 ,$ as desired. (This can also be proved readily by hand without the machinery of spectral sequences.) □

24.6.8. Proof of the slicing criterion for flatness on the target, Theorem 24.6.6. Theorem 24.6.6 then follows by applying the previous lemma with $\mathrm { I } = \left( \mathrm { t } \right)$ and $\ J = \mathfrak { n } ,$ using $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { t B } , M ) =$ $\ker ( M \xrightarrow { \times \mathrm { t } } M )$ from Exercise 24.2.A, which is zero by hypothesis. □

image_caption: Figure 24.4 The (sufficient) slicing criterion for flatness on the source, Theorem 24.6.9.

text: 24.6.A. EXERCISE. Use the slicing criterion to give a second solution to Exercise 24.3.I, on two planes in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 4 }$ meeting at a point. (This will use only the “easy direction” of the slicing criterion.)
Here is a similar slicing criterion for flatness, but in the source. Note that the criterion only goes one way.

24.6.9. Theorem (slicing criterion for flatness on the source; see Figure 24.4) — Suppose $\left( \mathtt { B } , \mathtt { n } \right) \longrightarrow \left( A , \mathtt { m } \right)$ is a local morphism of Noetherian local rings, M is a finitely generated Amodule, and $\mathrm { t } \in \mathfrak { m } \subset \mathcal { A }$ is a non-zerodivisor on $M / { \mathfrak { n } } M$ . If M is flat over B, then M/tM is flat over B.
Proof. We have an exact sequence of A-modules (and hence Bmodules),

$$
M \xrightarrow {\times t} M \longrightarrow M / t M \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Extend this to a flat resolution of B-modules (for example, by building it leftward using free B-modules):

We can compute $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { t } M , \mathrm { B } / \mathfrak { n } )$ using any flat resolution (Exercise 23.3.D), so we use (24.6.9.1). We apply $\otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathrm { B } / { \mathfrak { n } }$ to (24.6.9.1) and truncate the right term to get the complex whose cohomology will give us $\mathrm { T o r _ { i } ^ { B } } ( \mathrm { B } / \mathrm { t } M , \mathrm { B } / { \mathfrak { n } } )$ :

$$
\dots \longrightarrow M / \mathfrak {n} \xrightarrow {\times t} M / \mathfrak {n} \longrightarrow 0.
$$

But $\times \mathrm { t } : M / \mathfrak { n } \longrightarrow M / \mathfrak { n }$ has kernel zero by hypothesis, so the cohomology at the first step is zero, →giving us that $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M / \mathrm { t } M , \mathrm { B } / { \mathfrak { n } } ) = 0$ . Then by the local criterion for flatness (Theorem 24.6.1), $M / { \mathrm { t N } }$ is flat over B as desired. □
Chapter 24 Flatness

24.6.10.** Relative effective Cartier divisors. Theorem 24.6.9 has an immediate geometric interpretation: “Suppose $\tau \colon X \to Y$ is a morphism of Noetherian schemes, $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on $\Sigma ,$ and $Z \hookrightarrow X$ is a locally principal subscheme . . . .” In the special case where $\mathcal { F } = \mathcal { O } _ { X } ,$ this leads to the notion of a relative effective Cartier divisor: a locally principal subscheme of X that is an effective Cartier divisor on all the fibers of π. Theorem 24.6.9 implies that if $\pi$ is flat, then any relative effective Cartier divisor is also flat. (See Exercise 25.3.H for an important application.)
24.6.11. Over a field, regular embeddings are locally flat covers of the coordinate axes in affine space.

24.6.12.** Fibral flatness. We conclude with a criterion for flatness that can be very useful, but which we will not need in the rest of the book. It applies when we have the following commutative diagram of locally Noetherian schemes, and where $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X.
For each point $\mathrm { \Delta } \mathrm { r } \in { \mathrm { Z } } ,$ let $\pi _ { \mathrm { r } } : X _ { \mathrm { r } } \to \mathsf { Y } _ { \mathrm { r } }$ be the base-change of π.

24.6.13. Fibral flatness theorem, version one — Suppose F is flat over Z, and for every point r Z, $\mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { r } } }$ is flat over $\Upsilon _ { r }$ . Then $\mathcal { F }$ is flat over Y.

24.6 Local Criteria for Flatness
24.6.14. Fibral flatness theorem, commutative algebra version one — Suppose

is a commutative diagram of Noetherian local rings (sending maximal ideals to maximal ideals as usual), and M is a finitely generated A-module. Suppose M is flat over R, and $M / { \mathfrak { r } } M$ is flat over B/rB. Then M is flat over B.

Proof. We will use Lemma 24.6.7. We wish to show that $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M , \mathrm { B / } { \mathfrak { n } } ) = 0$ (to invoke the local criterion for flatness, Theorem 24.6.1), and we know that $M / { \mathfrak { r } } M$ is flat over $\mathrm { B } / \mathrm { r B }$ , so we wish to apply Lemma 24.6.7 for the module M, the ring ${ \mathrm { B } } ,$ and the ideals ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { J } } } = { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { n } } } $ and $\mathrm { I } = \mathrm { r } \mathrm { B }$ . The only hypothesis of Lemma 24.6.7 remaining to show is that Tor $\mathbf { \omega } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M , \mathrm { B / } \tau \mathrm { B } ) = 0$ .

Applying the right-exact functor $\mathbf { \nabla } \cdot \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } { M }$ to the surjection of B-modules $\colon \otimes _ { \mathbb { R } } \mathrm { B } \twoheadrightarrow \tau \mathrm { B }$ yields a surjection

$$
\alpha : \mathfrak {r} \otimes_ {\mathbb {R}} M = \mathfrak {r} \otimes_ {\mathbb {R}} B \otimes_ {\mathbb {B}} M \longrightarrow (\mathfrak {r} B) \otimes_ {\mathbb {B}} M.
$$
Consider the composition $\gamma$ of maps of B-modules,

$$
\mathfrak {r} \otimes_ {\mathrm {R}} M \xrightarrow {\alpha} (\mathfrak {r} B) \otimes_ {\mathrm {B}} M \xrightarrow {\beta} M.
$$

Now $\propto$ is surjective, and the composition $\beta \circ \alpha$ is injective (apply $\boldsymbol { \cdot } \otimes _ { R } \boldsymbol { M }$ to $0 \to \mathfrak { r } \to \mathbb { R } ,$ , and use that M is R-flat). Thus $\propto$ must be injective and thus an isomorphism, from which $\beta$ must be injective.
Now apply $\boldsymbol { M } \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } .$ to the short exact sequence

$$
0 \longrightarrow \mathrm {t B} \longrightarrow \mathrm {B} \longrightarrow \mathrm {B} / \mathrm {t B} \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Since B is flat over B, $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { B } } ( M , \mathrm { B } ) = 0 .$ , so we have an exact sequence
From the injectivity of $\beta$ we have $\mathrm { T o r } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathsf { R } } ( M , \mathrm { B / } \mathfrak { r } \mathrm { B } ) = 0 \mathrm { . }$ , as desired.
Theorem 24.6.14 can be extended to the following (which can also lead to a geometric statement similar to Theorem 24.6.13).
24.6.15. Fibral flatness theorem, commutative algebra version two — Suppose (24.6.14.1)
is a commutative diagram of Noetherian local rings, and M is a nonzero finitely generated A-module. Then (a) is true if and only if (b) is true.

list_items: (a) (i) M is flat over R, and (ii) M/rM is flat over B/rB.
(b) (i) M is flat over B, and (ii) B is flat over R.

text: 24.6.H. EASY EXERCISE. Show that (b) implies (a).
Proof. Most of the result was shown in Theorem 24.6.14 and Exercise 24.6.H. It only remains to show that (a)(i), (a)(ii), and (b)(i) imply (b)(ii).
24.6.I. EXERCISE. Show that (a)(i), (a)(ii), and (b)(i) imply that M is faithfully flat over B.

We now show directly that B is flat over R. Let N be an exact sequence of R-modules. We wish to show that $\mathrm { { B } } \otimes _ { \mathrm { { R } } } \mathrm { { N } } _ { \bullet }$ is exact. But by (a)(i), $M \otimes _ { \mathbb { R } } \mathbb { N } _ { \bullet }$ is exact, and $M \otimes _ { \mathbb { R } } \mathbb { N } _ { \bullet } = M \otimes _ { \mathbb { B } }$ $( \mathrm { B } \otimes _ { \mathrm { R } } \mathrm { N } _ { \bullet } )$ is exact. As M is faithfully flat over B, we have that B $\otimes _ { \mathsf { R } } \mathsf { N } _ { \bullet }$ is exact, as desired. □
Chapter 24 Flatness

We come to an important consequence of flatness promised in §24.0.1. We will see that this result implies many answers and examples to questions that we would have asked before we even knew about flatness.

24.7.1. Important theorem (Euler characteristic is constant in flat families) — Suppose π : $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a projective morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X, flat over Y. Then $\begin{array} { r } { \chi ( X _ { \mathfrak { q } } , \mathcal { F } | \boldsymbol { x } _ { \mathfrak { q } } ) = \sum _ { \mathfrak { i } \geq 0 } ( - 1 ) ^ { \mathfrak { i } } \mathfrak { h } ^ { \mathfrak { i } } ( X _ { \mathfrak { q } } , \mathcal { F } | \boldsymbol { x } _ { \mathfrak { q } } ) } \end{array}$ is a locally constant function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ (where ${ \sf X } _ { { \sf q } } =$ $\pi ^ { - 1 } ( \mathbf { q } ) .$ ).

This is the first sign that “cohomology behaves well in flat families.” (We will soon see a second: the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1. A different proof of Theorem 24.7.1, giving an extension to the proper case, will be given in §25.2.5.) The Noetherian hypotheses are used to ensure that $\pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ is a coherent sheaf.
Theorem 24.7.1 gives a necessary condition for flatness. Converses (yielding a sufficient condition) are given in Exercise 24.7.C.

Proof. We make three quick reductions. (i) The question is local on the target Y, so we may reduce to case Y is affine, say, $\Upsilon =$ Spec B, so π factors through a closed embedding $\boldsymbol { X } { \hookrightarrow } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ for some n. (ii) We may reduce to the case $X = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , by considering $\mathcal { F }$ as a sheaf on $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ . (iii) We may reduce to showing that for $\mathsf { m } \gg 0$ , $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( X _ { \mathsf { q } } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { m } ) | _ { X _ { \mathsf { q } } } )$ is a locally constant function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y } ,$ and $\mathrm { \mathsf { h } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } ) | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } ) = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ . (Serre vanishing ensures that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } ) | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } ) = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } > 0$ for $m \gg 0 ,$ but a priori there could be a different m for each ${ \bf q } ,$ and we need m to be independent of q.)

Twist by $\mathcal { O } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ for ${ \mathfrak { m } } \gg 0 ,$ , so that all the higher cohomology $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } > 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { F } ( \mathsf { m } ) )$ of $\mathcal { F }$ vanishes (by Serre vanishing, Theorem 18.1.3(ii)). Now consider the Čech complex $\mathcal { C } ^ { \bullet }$ for $\mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ . Note that all the terms in the Čech complex $\mathcal { C } ^ { \bullet }$ are flat, because $\mathcal { F }$ is flat. (Do you see why?) As all higher cohomology groups (higher pushforwards) vanish, $\mathcal { C } ^ { \bullet }$ is exact except at the first term, where the cohomology is $\Gamma ( \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } ) )$ . We add the module $\Gamma ( \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } } ( \mathfrak { m } ) )$ to the front of the complex, so it is once again exact:

(We have done this trick of tacking on a module before, for example, in (18.2.4.1).) Thus, by Exercise 24.2.G, as we have an exact sequence in which all but the first terms are flat, the first term is flat as well. Thus $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ is a flat coherent sheaf on Y, and hence locally free (Corollary 24.3.7), and thus has locally constant rank.

Suppose $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y } .$ . We wish to show that the Hilbert function $\mathsf { h } _ { \mathcal { F } | _ { \mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { q } } } } ( \mathsf { m } )$ is a locally constant function of q. To compute $\mathsf { h } _ { \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } } ( \mathsf { m } ) ,$ we tensor the Čech resolution with $\kappa ( \ q )$ and take cohomology. Now the extended Čech resolution (with $\Gamma ( \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ( \mathfrak { m } ) )$ tacked on the front), (24.7.1.1), is an exact sequence of flat modules, and hence remains exact upon tensoring with $\kappa ( \ q )$ (Exercise 24.2.F). Thus

$$
\Gamma (\pi_ {*} \mathcal {F} (m)) \otimes \kappa (q) \cong \Gamma (\pi_ {*} \mathcal {F} (m) | _ {q}),
$$

so the Hilbert function $\mathsf { h } _ { \mathcal { F } | _ { \mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { q } } } } ( \mathsf { m } )$ is the rank at q of a locally free sheaf, which is a locally constant function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ . □
We now give some ridiculously useful consequences of Theorem 24.7.1.

list_items: 24.7.2. Corollary — Assume the same hypotheses and notation as in Theorem 24.7.1. Then the Hilbert polynomial of $\mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathfrak { q } } }$ is locally constant as a function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ .

text: Another consequence of Corollary 24.7.2 is something remarkably useful.
24.7.3. Corollary — An invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on a flat projective family of curves X has locally constant degree on the fibers.
(Recall that the definition of the degree of a line bundle on a projective curve requires no hypotheses on the curve such as regularity; see (18.4.7.1).)

Proof. An invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on a flat family of curves is always flat (as locally it is isomorphic to the structure sheaf). Hence $\chi ( \mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { q } } , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathsf { q } } )$ is a constant function of q. By the definition of degree given in (18.4.7.1), $\deg ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathrm { q } } ) = \chi ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathrm { q } } ) - \chi ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { O } _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } )$ . The result follows from the local constancy of $\chi ( \boldsymbol { \mathsf { X } } _ { \mathfrak { q } } , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { q } } )$ and $\chi ( X _ { \mathfrak { q } } , \mathcal { O } _ { X _ { \mathfrak { q } } } )$ (Theorem 24.7.1). □

24.7.D.* EXERCISE FOR THOSE WHO HAVE READ STARRED CHAPTER 20: INTERSECTION NUMBERS ARE LOCALLY CONSTANT IN FLAT FAMILIES. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a flat projective morphism to a connected scheme; $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ are line bundles on $\mathsf X$ ; and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X, flat over Y, such that the support of $\mathcal { F }$ when restricted to any fiber of $\pi$ has dimension at most n. If $\mathbf { q }$ is any point of Y, define (the temporary notation) $( { \mathcal { L } } _ { 1 } \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot { \mathcal { L } } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \cdot { \mathcal { F } } ) _ { \mathfrak { q } }$ to be the intersection on the fiber $X _ { \mathrm { q } }$ of $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathcal { L } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ with $\mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } }$ (Definition 20.1.1). Corollary 24.7.3 motivates the following discussion.

24.7.4. Definition. Suppose $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { 2 }$ are line bundles on a k-variety X. We say that $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { 2 }$ are algebraically equivalent if there exist a connected (but not necessarily irreducible) k-variety Y with two k-valued points ${ \bf q } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \bf q } _ { 2 }$ and a line bundle $\mathcal { L }$ on $\boldsymbol { x } \times \boldsymbol { Y }$ such that the restriction of $\mathcal { L }$ to the fibers over ${ \bf q } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \bf q } _ { 2 }$ are isomorphic to $\mathcal { L } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { 2 } ,$ respectively.

text: 24.7.5. The Néron–Severi group is the group of line bundles Pic X modulo algebraic equivalence, $\operatorname* { P i c } X / \operatorname* { P i c } ^ { 0 } X$ . (This definition was promised in §18.4.12.) By Corollary 24.7.3, algebraic equivalence implies numerical equivalence (§18.4.12): $\operatorname { P i c } ^ { 0 } X \subset \operatorname { P i c } ^ { \tau } X .$ . (Side remark: A line bundle $\mathcal { L }$ on a proper k-scheme X is numerically trivial if and only if there exists an integer $\mathfrak { m } \ne 0$ with ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { m } }$ algebraically trivial. Thus ${ \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { \tau } X / { \mathrm { P i c } } ^ { 0 } X$ is torsion. See [SGA6, XIII, Thm. 4.6] for a proof, or [Laz, Cor. 1.4.38] for the projective case.)
24.7.6.* Hironaka’s example of a proper nonprojective smooth threefold.

In $\ S 1 5 . 2 . 1 1 $ , we produced a proper nonprojective variety, but it was singular. We can use Corollary 24.7.3 to give a smooth example, due to Hironaka. It is in in some sense “locally a blow-up of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ .”

Inside $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 } ,$ fix two conics $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 } ,$ which meet in two (k-valued) points, ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \tt p } _ { 2 }$ . We construct a proper map π: $\Chi \to { \mathbb { P } } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 }$ as follows. For $i = 1$ and 2, let $\mathrm { { U } _ { i } = \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 } \setminus \{ p _ { i } \} }$ . For both ${ \mathrm { i } , }$ consider the projective morphism $\pi _ { \mathrm { i } } : X _ { \mathrm { i } }  \mathrm { U } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ obtained by blowing up $\mathrm { C } _ { \mathrm { i } } ,$ and then blowing up the proper transform of ${ C _ { 3 - i } }$ . The two $\pi _ { \mathrm { i } }$ →glue together over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 } \setminus \{ \mathsf { p } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { p } _ { 2 } \}$ to give a morphism $\pi { : } X \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ . (Away from ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathsf { p } _ { 2 } , \mathsf { C } _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ are disjoint, so blowing up one and then the other is the same as blowing up their union.)

Note that $\pi$ is proper, as it is proper over $\mathrm { u } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathrm { U } _ { 2 } ,$ and the notion of properness is local on the target (Proposition 11.4.4(b)). As $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 3 }$ is proper (over k, Theorem 8.4.10), and compositions of proper morphisms are proper (Proposition 11.4.4(c)), X is proper.

24.7.7. The notion of “projective morphism” is not local on the target. Note that $\pi \colon X \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ is not projective, as otherwise $x$ would be projective (as the composition of projective morphisms is projective if the final target is quasicompact, Exercise 17.3.I). But away from each $\mathsf { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } , \pi$ is projective (as it is a composition of blow-ups, which are projective by construction, and the final target is quasicompact, so Exercise 17.3.I applies). Thus the notion of “projective morphism” is not local on the target. (A simpler but non-smooth example can be constructed using §15.2.11.)

text: 24.7 Flatness Implies Constant Euler Characteristic
24.8 Smooth and Étale Morphisms, and Flatness

We defined smooth and étale morphisms earlier (Definition 13.6.2), but we are now in a position to understand them much better. We will see that the notion of unramified morphism (§21.7) is a natural companion to them.

Our three algebro-geometric definitions won’t be so obviously a natural triplet, but we will discuss the definitions given in [Gr-EGA] (§24.8.13), and in this context the three types of morphisms look very similar. (We briefly mention other approaches and definitions in §24.8.14.)
The three classes of morphisms we will discuss in this chapter are the analogs of the following types of maps of manifolds, in differential geometry (cf. §13.6.1).

list_items: • Submersions are maps inducing surjections of tangent spaces everywhere. They are useful in the notion of a fibration. (Perhaps a more relevant notion from differential geometry, allowing singularities, is: “locally on the source a smooth fibration.”)
• Isomorphisms locally on the source (or local isomorphisms) are maps inducing isomorphisms of tangent spaces.
• Immersions are maps inducing injections of tangent spaces.

text: (Recall our warning from §9.2: “immersion” is often used in algebraic geometry with a different meaning.)

In order to better understand smooth and étale morphisms, we temporarily forget our earlier definitions, and consider some examples of things we want to be analogs of “local isomorphism” (or “locally on the source an isomorphism”) and “locally on the source a smooth fibration,” and see if they help us make good definitions.

image_caption: Figure 24.6 Sketches of notions from differential geometry: (a) maps “locally on the source a smooth fibration,” (b) local isomorphisms, and (c) immersions. (In algebraic geometry: (a) smooth morphisms, (b) étale morphisms, and (c) unramified morphisms.)

text: 24.8.1. “Local isomorphisms” (étale morphisms).

Consider the parabola $\ x = \ y ^ { 2 }$ projecting to the x-axis, over the complex numbers. (This example has come up repeatedly, in one form or another, including in Exercise 13.6.F.) We might reasonably want this to be a local isomorphism (on the source) away from the origin. We might also want the notion of local isomorphism space to be an open condition: the locus where a morphism is a local isomorphism should be open on the source. This is true for the differential geometric definition. But then this morphism should be a local isomorphism over the generic point, and here we get a nontrivial residue field extension $( \mathbb { C } ( \mathfrak { y } ) / \mathbb { C } ( \mathfrak { y } ^ { 2 } ) )$ , not an isomorphism. Thus we are forced to consider (the Spec’s of) certain finite extensions of fields to be “isomorphisms locally on the source”—in this case, even “covering spaces.” (We will see in Exercise 24.8.D that we want precisely the finite separable extensions; you could have shown this earlier.)
Note also in this example there are no (nonempty)

Zariski-open subsets $\mathsf { U } \subset \mathsf { S p e c } \mathbb { C } [ \mathsf { x } ] \setminus \{ 0 \}$ and $\vee \subset S p e c \mathbb { C } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] / ( \mathsf { x } - \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } ) \setminus \{ ( 0 , 0 ) \}$ where the map sends U into V isomorphically (Exercise 13.6.F), so this is not a local isomorphism in a way you may have seen before. This leads to the notion of the étale topology, which is not even a topology in the usual sense, but a “Grothendieck topology” (§6.2.10). The étale topology is beyond the scope of this book.
24.8.2. Submersions (smooth morphisms).
24.8.3. Fibers are smooth varieties. As a first approximation of the algebro-geometric version of submersion, we will want the fibers to be smooth varieties (over the residue field). So the
Chapter 24 Flatness

very first thing we need is to generalize the notion of “variety” over a base. It is reasonable to do this by having a locally finite type hypothesis. For somewhat subtle reasons, we will require the stronger condition that the morphism be locally of finite presentation. (If you really care, you can see where it comes up in our discussion. But, of course, there is no difference for Noetherian readers.)

The fibers are not just varieties; they should be smooth varieties (of dimension n, say). From the case of smoothness over a field, or from our intuition of what smooth varieties should look like, we expect the sheaf of differentials on the fibers to be locally free of rank ${ \mathfrak { n } } ,$ or, even better, the sheaf of relative differentials to be locally free of rank n.

24.8.4. Flatness. At this point, our first approximation of “smooth morphism” is some version of “locally finitely presented, and fibers are smooth varieties.” But that isn’t quite enough. For example, a horrible map from a scheme X to a curve Y that maps a different regular variety to each point of Y (X is the infinite disjoint union of these) should not be considered a smooth fibration in any reasonable sense. Also, we might not want to consider Spec $\mathbf { k } \longrightarrow \mathbf { S p e c k } [ \epsilon ] / ( \epsilon ^ { 2 } )$ to be a submersion; for example, this isn’t surjective on tangent spaces, and, more generally, the picture “doesn’t look like a fibration.”

Both problems are failures of $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ to be a nice, “’continuous” family. Whenever we are looking for some vague notion of “niceness” we know that “flatness” will be in the definition.

For comparison, note that “unramified” has no flatness hypothesis, and indeed we didn’t expect it, as we would want the inclusion (closed embedding) of the origin into $\mathbb { A } ^ { 1 }$ to be unramified. But then weird things may be unramified. For example, if $\begin{array} { r } { X = \coprod _ { z \in \mathbb { C } } \operatorname { S p e c } \mathbb { C } , } \end{array}$ then the morphism $\mathrm { X } \to \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ sending the point corresponding to $z$ to the point $z \in \mathbb { A } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 1 }$ is unramified. Such is life.
24.8.5. Desired alternate definitions. We might hope that a morphism $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is smooth of relative dimension n if and only if

list_items: (i) $\pi$ is locally of finite presentation,
(ii) $\pi$ is flat of relative dimension n, and
(iii) $\Omega _ { X / Y }$ is locally free of rank n.

text: We might similarly hope that a morphism π: X Y is étale if and only if

list_items: (i) $\pi$ is locally of finite presentation,
(ii) $\pi$ is flat, and
(iii) $\Omega _ { \scriptscriptstyle { X / Y } } = 0$

image_caption: Figure 24.7 We don’t want this to be a “smooth morphism.”

text: We will shortly show (in Theorem 24.8.8 and Exercise 24.8.C, respectively) that these desired definitions are equivalent to Definition 13.6.2.
24.8.6. Different characterizations of smooth and étale morphisms.

The main result in this section is a description of equivalent characterizations of smooth morphisms, Theorem 24.8.8. We will state the theorem, and then give consequences, and then finally give a proof. As an important precursor:
24.8.7. Proposition — Smooth morphisms are flat.

Proof. We prove this first in the Noetherian setting. Suppose $\pi { : } X \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of locally Noetherian schemes that is smooth of relative dimension n. We wish to show that it is flat at a point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ . By the definition of smooth morphisms (§13.6.2), we may replace X and Y with affine neighborhoods of p and $\pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } )$ , respectively, so that $\pi$ is the morphism
and p is a point where the Jacobian matrix is full rank.

We now come to the central result of this section.
24.8.8. Theorem — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes. Then the following are equivalent.

list_items: (i) The morphism π is smooth of relative dimension n (Definition 13.6.2).
(ii) The morphism π is locally finitely presented and flat of relative dimension n; and $\Omega _ { \pi }$ is locally free of rank n (Desired Alternate Definition 24.8.5).
(iii) The morphism π is locally finitely presented and flat, and the fibers are smooth k-schemes of pure dimension n.
(iv) The morphism π is locally finitely presented and flat, and the geometric fibers are smooth k-schemes of pure dimension n.

text: We first dispatch with the parts of this theorem we have basically already proved.

Then $\pi$ is smooth of dimension n (e.g., étale, taking $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ ) if and only if $\tau$ is. One direction is Exercise 13.6.D, and the other is Exercise 21.7.G(b). 24.8.11.** Proof of Theorem 24.8.8.
By Exercise 24.8.B, it remains only to show that (iii) implies (i). (This argument is reminiscent of the proof of Theorem 13.6.6.)

(However, we cannot assume that $\mathbf { q }$ is a closed point of Y.)

text: 24.8.12.** Formally unramified, smooth, and étale.
[Gr-EGA] takes a different starting point for the definition of unramified, smooth, and étale morphisms. The definitions there make clear that these three definitions form a family.

The cost of these definitions are that they are perhaps less immediately motivated by geometry, and it is harder to show some basic properties. The benefit is that it is possible to show more (for example, left-exactness of the cotangent and conormal exact sequences, and good interpretations in terms of completions of local rings). But we simply introduce these ideas here, and do not explore them. See [BLR, §2.2] for an excellent discussion. (You should largely ignore what follows, unless you find later in life that you really care.)

24.8.13. Definition. We say that $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ is formally smooth (resp., formally étale, formally unramified) if for all affine schemes Z, and every closed subscheme $Z _ { 0 } \subset Z$ defined by a nilpotent ideal, and every morphism $Z \to \Upsilon ,$ , the canonical map $\mathrm { H o m } _ { \Upsilon } ( Z , X )  \mathrm { H o m } _ { \Upsilon } ( Z _ { 0 } , X )$ is surjective (resp., bijective, injective). This is summarized in the following diagram, which is reminiscent of the valuative criteria for separatedness and properness.
(You can check that this is the same as the definition we would get by replacing “nilpotent” by “square-zero.” This is sometimes an easier formulation to work with.)
Then [Gr-EGA] defines “smooth” as morphisms that are formally smooth and locally of finite presentation, and similarly for unramified and étale.

One can show that [Gr-EGA]’s definitions of formally unramified and smooth agree with the definitions we give. For “formally unramified” (where our definition given in §21.7 is not obviously the same as the definition of [Gr-EGA] given here), see [Gr-EGA, $\mathrm { { I V } _ { 4 } }$ .17.2.1] or [Stacks, tag 00UO]. For “smooth,” see [Gr-EGA, IV4.17.5.1] or [Stacks, tag 00TN]. (Our characterization of étale as smooth of relative dimension 0 then agrees with [Gr-EGA]. Our definition of unramified as formally unramified plus locally of finite type disagrees with [Gr-EGA]; see §21.7.)

24.8.14. Other starting points. There are a number of quite different ways of defining smooth, étale, and unramified morphisms, and it is nontrivial to relate them. We have just described the approach of [Gr-EGA]. Another common approach is the characterization of smooth morphisms as locally finitely presented, flat, and with regular geometric fibers (see Theorem 24.8.8). Yet another definition is via a naive version of the cotangent complex; this is the approach taken by [Stacks, tag 00T2], and is less frightening than it sounds. Finally, the different characterizations of Exercise 24.8.D give alternate initial definitions of étaleness.
24.8 Smooth and Étale Morphisms, and Flatness
Chapter 25
Cohomology and Base Change Theorems

Cohomology and base change theorems are powerful results relating the higher pushforwards of coherent sheaves with the cohomology of those sheaves on fibers, and more generally explaining when higher pushforward commutes with base change. We first describe some of their statements, and immediate applications, in §25.1. We give their unexpectedly elegant proofs in §25.2. Then in §25.3 we use them to introduce a number of important ideas in moduli theory.
25.1 Statements and Applications

Higher pushforwards are easy to define, but it is hard to get a geometric sense of what they are, or how they behave. For example, given a morphism $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon ,$ and a quasicoherent sheaf $\mathcal { F }$ on $\Sigma ,$ you might reasonably hope that the fibers of $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ are the cohomologies of $\mathcal { F }$ along the fibers. More precisely, given ψ: ${ \mathfrak { q } } \to { \mathsf { Y } }$ corresponding to the inclusion of a point (better: $\psi$ : Spec $\kappa ( { \bf q } ) \to \mathsf { Y } ,$ ), yielding the Cartesian diagram
one might hope that the morphism

(the push-pull map in cohomology given in Exercise 18.7.C) is an isomorphism. (Note: $\mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } }$ and $( \boldsymbol { \Psi } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { * } \mathcal { F }$ are symbols for the same thing. The first is often preferred, but we sometimes use the second because we will consider more general $\boldsymbol { \Psi }$ and $\boldsymbol { \Psi } ^ { \prime }$ .) We could then picture $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ as somehow fitting together the cohomology groups of fibers into a coherent sheaf. (Warning: This is too much to hope for; see Exercise 25.1.A.)

It would also be wonderful if $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( \mathsf { X } _ { \mathsf { q } } , ( \Psi ^ { \prime } ) ^ { * } \mathcal { F } )$ was constant, and $\Phi _ { \mathrm { } \mathrm { } } ^ { \flat }$ put them together into a nice locally free sheaf (vector bundle) $R ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { F } }$ .

The base change $\psi \colon { \mathfrak { q } } \to \mathsf { Y }$ should not be special. As long as we are dreaming, we may as well hope that in good circumstances, given a Cartesian diagram (18.7.4.1)
the natural push-pull morphism

of sheaves on Z (Exercise 18.7.B(a)) is an isomorphism. (In some cases, we can already address this question. For example, cohomology commutes with flat base change, Theorem 24.1.9, so the result holds if $\psi$ is flat.)

We formalize our dreams into three nice properties that we might wish in this situation. We will see that they are closely related. Suppose $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on $\mathsf X , \pi \colon \mathsf X \to \mathsf Y$ is proper, Y (hence X) is Noetherian, and $\mathcal { F }$ is flat over Y.

list_items: (a) Given a Cartesian square (25.1.0.1), is $\Phi _ { \mathbf { q } } ^ { \mathsf { p } } \colon \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathsf { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } \otimes \mathsf { \boldsymbol { \kappa } } ( \mathsf { q } ) \longrightarrow \mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( X _ { \mathsf { q } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathsf { q } } } )$ an isomorphism?

(b) Given a Cartesian square (25.1.0.2), is $\phi _ { Z } ^ { \mathtt { p } } \colon \Psi ^ { * } ( \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathtt { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ) \longrightarrow \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathtt { p } } \pi _ { * } ^ { \prime } ( \Psi ^ { \prime } ) ^ { * } \mathcal { F }$ an isomorphism?
(c) Is $R ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ locally free?

text: We turn first to property (a). The dimension of the left side $\mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } \otimes \mathsf { \kappa } ( \mathsf { q } )$ is an upper semicontinuous function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ by upper semicontinuity of rank of finite type quasicoherent sheaves (Exercise 14.3.J). The Semicontinuity Theorem states that the dimension of the right is also upper semicontinuous. More formally:

25.1.1. Semicontinuity Theorem — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a proper morphism of Noetherian schemes, and $\mathcal { F }$ →is a coherent sheaf on X flat over Y. Then for each $\mathfrak { p } \geq 0 .$ , the function $\Upsilon \to \mathbb { Z }$ given by ${ \bf q } \mapsto$ $\dim _ { \kappa ( { \mathfrak { q } } ) } { \mathsf { H } } ^ { { \mathfrak { p } } } ( X _ { \mathfrak { q } } , { \mathcal F } | _ { X _ { \mathfrak { q } } } )$ is an upper semicontinuous function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ .
Translation: Ranks of cohomology groups are upper semicontinuous in proper flat families. (A proof will be given in §25.2.4.)

25.1.2. Example. You may already have seen an example of cohomology groups jumping, in §24.3.14. Here is a simpler example, albeit not of the structure sheaf. Let $\left( \mathsf { E } , \mathsf { p } _ { 0 } \right)$ be an elliptic curve over a field ${ \sf k } ,$ and consider the projection π: $\mathsf { E } \times \mathsf { E } \to \mathsf { E }$ to the second factor. Let $\mathcal { L }$ be the invertible sheaf (line bundle) on $\mathsf { E } \times \mathsf { E }$ corresponding to the divisor that is the diagonal, minus the section of ${ \sf p } _ { 0 } \times { \sf E }$ of $\pi$ (where $\mathfrak { p } _ { 0 } \in \mathbb { E }$ ). Then $\mathcal { L } | _ { \mathfrak { p } _ { 0 } }$ (i.e., $\mathcal { L } | _ { \mathsf { E } \times \mathsf { p } _ { 0 } }$ ) is trivial, but ${ \mathcal { L } } | _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is nontrivial for any $\mathfrak { p } \ne \mathfrak { p } _ { 0 }$ (as we showed in our study of genus 1 curves, in §19.9). Thus $\mathsf { h } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { E } , \mathscr { L } | _ { \mathsf { p } } )$ is 0 in general, but jumps to 1 for ${ \mathfrak { p } } = { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 0 }$ .

25.1.A. EXERCISE. Show that $\pi _ { * } { \mathcal { L } } = 0$ . Thus we cannot picture $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { L }$ as “gluing together” $\mathtt { h } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 }$ of the fibers; in this example, cohomology does not commute with “base change” or “taking fibers.”

25.1.3. Side remark. In characteristic 0, the cohomology of $\mathcal { O }$ doesn’t jump in smooth families. Over $\mathbb { C } ,$ this is because Betti numbers are constant in connected families, and (21.5.11.1) (from Hodge theory) expresses the Betti constants $\mathsf { h } _ { \mathrm { B e t t i } } ^ { \mathrm { k } }$ as sums (over $\mathbf { i } + \mathbf { j } = \mathbf { k }$ ) of upper semicontinuous functions $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { h } } ^ { \mathrm { j } } ( \Omega ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ , so the Hodge numbers $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { h } } ^ { \mathrm { j } } ( \Omega ^ { \mathrm { i } } )$ must in fact be constant. The general characteristic 0 case can be reduced to $\mathbb { C }$ by an application of the Lefschetz principle (which also arose in §21.5.9). But ranks of cohomology groups of $\mathcal { O }$ for smooth families of varieties can jump in positive characteristic (see, for example, [MO70920]). Also, the example of §24.3.14 shows that the “smoothness” hypothesis cannot be removed.

25.1.4. Grauert’s Theorem. If $R ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is locally free (property (c)) and $\Phi _ { \mathbf { q } } ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is an isomorphism (property (a)), then $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { p } } ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } )$ is clearly locally constant. The following is a partial converse.

25.1.5. Grauert’s Theorem — If π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is proper, Y is reduced and locally Noetherian, $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X flat over Y, and $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { p } } ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } )$ is a locally constant function of $\mathbf { q } \in \mathsf { Y } ,$ then $R ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is locally free, and $\boldsymbol { \Phi } _ { Z } ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ is an isomorphism for all $\psi : Z \to \mathsf { Y }$ .

In other words, if cohomology groups of fibers have locally constant dimension (over a reduced base), then they can be fit together to form a vector bundle, and the fiber of the pushforward is identified with the cohomology of the fiber. Our dreams at the start of this chapter have come true!
(See $8 2 5 . 2 . 1 0 $ to remove Noetherian hypotheses.)

We further note that if Y is integral, $\pi$ is proper, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X flat over Y, then by the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1 there is a dense open subset of Y on which $R ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is locally free (and on which the fiber of the pth higher pushforward is the pth cohomology of the fiber).
The following statement is even more magical than Grauert’s Theorem 25.1.5.
Chapter 25 Cohomology and Base Change Theorems

25.1.6. Cohomology and Base Change Theorem — Suppose π is proper, Y is locally Noetherian, $\mathcal { F }$ is coherent over X and flat over Y, and $\Phi _ { \mathbf { q } } ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is surjective. Then the following hold.

(i) There is an open neighborhood U of q such that for any ψ : Z U, $\Phi _ { Z } ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is an isomorphism. In particular, $\Phi _ { \mathbf { q } } ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is an isomorphism.
(ii) Furthermore,

list_items: (a) $\Phi _ { \mathrm { q } } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 }$ is surjective (hence an isomorphism by (i)) if and only if

(b) $R ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is locally free in some open neighborhood of q (or, equivalently, $\left( \mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } \right) _ { \mathrm { q } }$ is a free $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \mathbf { q } }$ - module, Exercise 14.3.E).

text: (These then imply that $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { p } } ( X _ { \mathsf { r } } , { \mathcal { F } } | _ { X _ { \mathsf { r } } } )$ is constant for r in an open neighborhood of q.)

(Proofs of Theorems 25.1.5 and 25.1.6 will be given in §25.2. Note in (ii) that if $\mathsf { p } = 0 , \mathsf { \Phi } \mathsf { \Phi } _ { \mathsf { q } } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 }$ is automatically surjective, as $\phi _ { \mathrm { q } } ^ { - 1 }$ is the zero map. See §25.2.10 to remove Noetherian hypotheses.)

This is amazing: the hypothesis that $\Phi _ { \mathbf { q } } ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is surjective involves what happens only over points q of X, with reduced structure, yet it has implications over the (possibly nonreduced) scheme as a whole! This might remind you of the local criterion for flatness (Theorem 24.6.1), which indeed is the key technical ingredient of the proof.
Here are some consequences.

25.1.7. An important class of morphisms: Proper, $\mathcal { O }$ -connected morphisms $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ of locally Noetherian schemes.

If a morphism π $\colon X \to \Upsilon$ satisfies the property that the natural map $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon } \longrightarrow \pi _ { * } \mathcal { O } _ { \Chi }$ is an isomorphism, we say that $\pi$ is $\mathcal { O }$ -connected. Clearly the notion of $\mathcal { O }$ -connectedness is local on the target, and preserved by composition.
25.1.H. EASY EXERCISE. Show that proper $\mathcal { O }$ -connected morphisms of locally Noetherian schemes are surjective.

25.1.8. We will soon meet Zariski’s Connectedness Lemma 28.4.1, which shows that proper, $\mathcal { O }$ - connected morphisms of locally Noetherian schemes have connected fibers. In some sense, this class of morphisms is really the right class of morphisms capturing what we might want by “connected fibers”; this is the motivation for the terminology. The following result gives some evidence for this point of view, in the flat context.

25.1.10. We next address the following question. Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of schemes. Given an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { L }$ on $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ we ask when it is the pullback of an invertible sheaf $\mathcal { M }$ on Y. For this to be true, we certainly need that $\mathcal { L }$ is trivial on the fibers. We will see that if $\pi$ is a proper $\mathcal { O }$ - connected morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, then this often suffices. Given ${ \mathcal { L } } _ { i }$ , we recover $\mathcal { M }$ as $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { L }$ ; the fibers of $\mathcal { M }$ are one-dimensional, and glue together to form a line bundle. 25.1.11. Proposition — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a flat, proper morphism of locally Noetherian schemes with geometrically connected and geometrically reduced fibers (hence $\mathcal { O }$ -connected, by Exercise 25.1.I). Suppose also that Y is reduced, and $\mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on X that is trivial on the fibers of π (i.e., $\mathcal { L } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } }$ is a trivial

text: Chapter 25 Cohomology and Base Change Theorems

invertible sheaf on $X _ { \mathrm { q } }$ for all $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y } )$ ). Then $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { L }$ is an invertible sheaf on Y (call it $\mathcal { M }$ ), and the natural map $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { M } }  { \mathcal { L } }$ is an isomorphism.

Proof. By Grauert’s Theorem 25.1.5, $\pi _ { * } \mathcal { L }$ is locally free of rank 1 (again, call it $\mathcal { M }$ ), and the map $\mathcal { M } \otimes _ { \mathcal { O } _ { \mathsf { Y } } } \kappa ( { \mathsf { q } } ) \longrightarrow { \mathsf { H } } ^ { 0 } ( X _ { \mathsf { q } } , \mathcal { L } | _ { X _ { \mathsf { q } } } )$ is an isomorphism. We have a natural map of invertible sheaves $\pi ^ { * } { \mathcal { M } } = \pi ^ { * } \pi _ { * } { \mathcal { L } }  { \mathcal { L } }$ . To show that it is an isomorphism, we need only show that it is surjective. (Do you see why? If A is a ring, and $\Phi \colon { \bar { A } } \to { \bar { A } }$ is a surjection of A-modules, why is $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ an isomorphism?) For this, it suffices to show that it is surjective on the fibers of $\pi$ . (Do you see why? Hint: If the cokernel of the map is not 0, then it is not 0 above some point of Y.) But this follows from the first line of the proof (using, for example, that ${ \mathcal { M } } { \cong } { \mathcal { O } }$ in a neighborhood of q). □

Proposition 25.1.11 has some pleasant consequences. For example, if you have two invertible sheaves $\mathcal { A }$ and $\mathcal { B }$ on X that are isomorphic on every fiber of $\pi _ { \rho }$ , then they differ by a pullback of an invertible sheaf on Y: just apply Proposition 25.1.11 to $\mathcal { A } \otimes \mathcal { B } ^ { \vee }$ .

25.2 Proofs of Cohomology and Base Change Theorems

The key to proving the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1, Grauert’s Theorem 25.1.5, and the Cohomology and Base Change Theorem 25.1.6 is the following wonderful idea of Mumford (see [Mu3, p. 47 Lem. 1]). It turns questions of pushforwards (and how they behave under arbitrary base change) into something computable with vector bundles (hence questions of linear algebra). After stating it, we will interpret it.
25.2.1. Key theorem — Suppose π: X Spec B is a proper morphism, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on X, flat over Spec B, where B is Noetherian. Then there is a complex
of finitely generated free B-modules and an isomorphism of functors
for all p, for all ring maps B A. (Here A needn’t be Noetherian.)
The complex (25.2.1.1) is sometimes called the Mumford complex.

Because (25.2.1.1) is a complex of free B-modules, all of the information is contained in the maps, which are matrices with entries in B. This will turn questions about cohomology (and base change) into questions about linear algebra. For example, semicontinuity will turn into the fact that ranks of matrices (with functions as entries) drop on closed subsets (§12.2.16(ii)).

Although the complex (25.2.1.1) is infinite, by (25.2.1.2) it has no cohomology in negative degree, even after any ring extension $\mathrm { B } \to \bar { A }$ (as the left side of (25.2.1.2) is 0 for $\mathfrak { p } < 0$ ).

The idea behind the proof is as follows: take the Čech complex, produce a complex of finite rank free modules mapping to it “with the same cohomology” (a quasiisomorphic complex, §18.2.3). We first construct the complex so that (25.2.1.2) holds for $\mathrm { B } = \mathcal { A }$ in the next lemma, and then show the same complex works for general $\mathsf { A } ,$ in Lemma 25.2.3 immediately thereafter.

25.2.2. Lemma — Let B be a Noetherian ring. Suppose $C ^ { \bullet }$ is a complex of B-modules such that $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { C } ^ { \bullet } )$ are finitely generated B-modules, and such that $C ^ { \mathfrak { p } } = 0$ for ${ \mathfrak { p } } > { \mathfrak { n } }$ . Then there exist a complex $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet }$ of finite rank free B-modules such that $\mathsf { K } ^ { \mathsf { p } } = 0 f o r \mathsf { p } > \mathsf { n }$ ${ \tt K } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } = 0$ , and a homomorphism of complexes $\alpha \colon \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } \to \mathsf { C } ^ { \bullet }$ such that α induces isomorphisms ${ \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( { \sf K } ^ { \bullet } ) \stackrel { \sim } { \longrightarrow } { \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( { \sf C } ^ { \bullet } )$ for all i.

Proof. We build this complex inductively. (This may remind you of §23.3.3.) Assume we have defined $( \mathsf { K } ^ { \flat } , \alpha ^ { \flat } , \delta ^ { \flat } )$ for ${ \mathfrak { p } } \geq { \mathfrak { m } } + 1$ (as in (25.2.2.1)) such that the squares commute, and the top row is a complex, and $\propto ^ { \mathrm { q } }$ defines an isomorphism of cohomology ${ \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { q } } ( { \sf K } ^ { \bullet } )  { \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { q } } ( { \sf C } ^ { \bullet } )$ for ${ \mathfrak { q } } \geq { \mathfrak { m } } + 2$ and a surjection $\ker ( \delta ^ { \mathsf { m } + 1 } ) \longrightarrow \mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { m } + 1 } ( \mathsf { C } ^ { \bullet } ) .$ , and the $K ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ →are finite rank free B-modules. (Our base case is ${ \mathfrak { m } } = { \mathfrak { p } }$ : take ${ \sf K } ^ { \mathfrak { n } } = 0$ for ${ \mathfrak { n } } > { \mathfrak { p } }$ .)

We construct $( \mathsf { K } ^ { \mathsf { m } } , \delta ^ { \mathsf { m } } , \alpha ^ { \mathsf { m } } )$ . Choose generators of ${ \sf H } ^ { \mathrm { m } } ( { \sf C } ^ { \bullet } )$ , say, ${ \mathsf { c } } _ { 1 } , \hdots , { \mathsf { c } } _ { M }$ . Let

$$
D ^ {m + 1} := \ker \left(\ker (\delta^ {m + 1}) \xrightarrow {\alpha^ {m + 1}} H ^ {m + 1} (C ^ {\bullet})\right).
$$

Choose generators of $\mathrm { D } ^ { \mathrm { m } + 1 }$ , say, $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { d } _ { \mathsf { N } }$ . (This is where we use the Noetherian hypotheses— to ensure this kernel $\mathrm { D } ^ { \mathrm { m } + 1 }$ is finitely generated.) Let ${ \mathsf { K } } ^ { \mathsf { m } } = { \mathsf { B } } ^ { \oplus ( M + { \mathsf { N } } ) }$ . Define $\delta ^ { \mathrm { m } } \colon \mathsf { K } ^ { \mathrm { m } } \to \mathsf { K } ^ { \mathrm { m } + 1 }$ by sending the last N generators to $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { d } _ { \mathsf { N } } ,$ , and the first $M$ generators to 0. Define $\alpha ^ { \mathfrak { m } }$ →by sending the first M generators of $\mathsf { B } ^ { \oplus ( M + N ) }$ to (lifts of) ${ \mathsf { c } } _ { 1 } , \hdots , { \mathsf { c } } _ { M } ,$ and sending the last $\mathsf { N }$ generators to arbitrarily chosen lifts of the $\alpha ^ { \mathrm { m } + 1 } ( \mathrm { d } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ (as the $\alpha ^ { \mathrm { m } + 1 } ( \mathrm { d } _ { \mathrm { i } } )$ are 0 in ${ \mathsf { H } } ^ { \mathsf { m } + 1 } ( { \mathsf { C } } ^ { \bullet } ) ,$ and thus lie in the image of $\epsilon ^ { \mathfrak { m } }$ ), so the square (with upper left corner ${ \mathsf { K } } ^ { \mathsf { m } }$ ) commutes. Then, by construction, we have completed our inductive step:

25.2.3. Lemma — Suppose $\alpha \colon \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } \to \mathsf { C } ^ { \bullet }$ is a morphism of complexes of flat B-modules, bounded on the right (i.e., $\mathsf { K } ^ { \mathrm { n } } = \mathsf { C } ^ { \mathrm { n } } = 0$ for ${ \mathfrak { n } } \gg 0$ →), inducing isomorphisms of cohomology (a quasiisomorphism, $\ S 1 8 . 2 . 3 )$ . Then “this quasiisomorphism commutes with arbitrary change of base ring”: for every B-algebra A, the maps $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \mathsf { A } ) \to \mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( \mathsf { C } ^ { \bullet } \otimes _ { \mathsf { B } } \mathsf { A } )$ are isomorphisms.

Proof. The mapping cone $M ^ { \bullet }$ of $\alpha \colon { \mathsf { K } } ^ { \bullet } \to { \mathsf { C } } ^ { \bullet }$ is exact by Exercise 1.6.E. Then M• B A is still exact, by Exercise 24.2.F. But $M ^ { \bullet } \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } A$ is the mapping cone of

$$
\alpha \otimes_ {B} A: K ^ {\bullet} \otimes_ {B} A \longrightarrow C ^ {\bullet} \otimes_ {B} A,
$$
so by Exercise 1.6.E, $\alpha \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } } A$ induces an isomorphism of cohomology (i.e., is a quasiisomorphism) too. □

Proof of Key Theorem 25.2.1. Choose a finite affine covering of X. Take the Čech complex $C ^ { \bullet }$ for $\mathcal { F }$ with respect to this cover. By Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1 (which had Noetherian hypotheses), the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ is coherent. (Theorem 18.9.1 required serious work. If you need Theorem 25.2.1 only in the projective case, the analogous statement with projective hypotheses, Theorem 18.7.1(d), is much easier.) Apply Lemma 25.2.2 to get the nicer variant $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet }$ of the same complex $C ^ { \bullet }$ . By Lemma 25.2.3, if we tensor with A and take cohomology, we get the same answer whether we use $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet }$ or $C ^ { \bullet }$ . □

We now use Theorem 25.2.1 to prove some of the fundamental results stated earlier: the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1, Grauert’s Theorem 25.1.5, and the Cohomology and Base Change Theorem 25.1.6. We will also (§25.2.5) give a new proof of Theorem 24.7.1, that Euler characteristics are locally constant in flat families (which applies more generally in proper situations).
25.2.4. Proof of the Semicontinuity Theorem 25.1.1. The result is local on Y, so we may assume Y is affine. Let $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet }$ be a complex as in Key Theorem 25.2.1.
Then for $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y } ,$

(Reason: The locus where the dimension is less than some number N is obtained by setting all $\Nu \times \Nu$ minors of the matrix ${ \sf K } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } \to { \sf K } ^ { \mathfrak { p } + 1 }$ to 0; cf. §12.2.16(ii)).) 25.2.5. A new proof (and extension to the proper case) of Theorem 24.7.1 that Euler characteristics of flat sheaves are locally constant.

If $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet }$ were finite “on the left” as well—if ${ \sf K } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } = 0$ for $\mathfrak { p } \ll 0$ — then we would have a short proof of Theorem 24.7.1. By taking alternating sums (over p) of (25.2.4.1), we would have that

$$
\chi \left(X _ {q}, \mathcal {F} \mid_ {X _ {q}}\right) = \sum (- 1) ^ {p} h ^ {p} \left(X _ {q}, \mathcal {F} \mid_ {X _ {q}}\right) = \sum (- 1) ^ {p} \operatorname {r a n k} K ^ {p},
$$

which is locally constant. The only problem is that the sums are infinite. We patch this problem by truncating the complex $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet }$ below where there is cohomology. Define J• by $J ^ { \mathrm { p } } = { \mathsf { K } } ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ for $\mathfrak { p } \geq 0 ,$ , $J ^ { \mathrm { p } } = 0$ for $\mathsf { p } < - 1 ,$ , and $\mathsf { J } ^ { - 1 } : = \ker ( \mathsf { K } ^ { - 1 } \to \mathsf { K } ^ { 0 } )$ . Then J• is a complex in the obvious way, and the map of complexes $\mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } \to \mathsf { C } ^ { \bullet }$ clearly factors through J•:
Clearly, $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { J } } ^ { \bullet }  \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } ^ { \bullet }$ induces an isomorphism on cohomology (recall both have 0 cohomology for $\mathfrak { p } < 0$ ).
Now $\ J ^ { - 1 }$ (the kernel of a map of coherent modules) is coherent. Consider the mapping cone $M ^ { \bullet }$ of $\beta \colon J ^ { \bullet }  { \mathsf { C } } ^ { \bullet }$ :

$$
0 \to J ^ {- 1} \to C ^ {- 1} \oplus J ^ {0} \to C ^ {0} \oplus J ^ {1} \to \dots \to C ^ {n - 1} \oplus J ^ {n} \to C ^ {n} \to 0.
$$

From Exercise 1.6.E, as $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { J } } ^ { \bullet }  \boldsymbol { \mathrm { C } } ^ { \bullet }$ induces an isomorphism on cohomology, the mapping cone has no cohomology — it is exact. All terms in it are flat except possibly $\ J ^ { - 1 }$ (the $C ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ are flat by assumption, and ${ \boldsymbol { \mathrm { J } } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ is free for $\mathrm { i } \neq - 1$ ). Hence $\ J ^ { - 1 }$ is flat too, by Exercise 24.2.G. But flat coherent sheaves are locally free (Corollary 24.3.7). Then Theorem 24.7.1 follows from

$$
\chi \left(X _ {q}, \mathcal {F} \mid_ {X _ {q}}\right) = \sum (- 1) ^ {p} h ^ {p} \left(X _ {q}, \mathcal {F} \mid_ {X _ {q}}\right) = \sum (- 1) ^ {p} \operatorname {r a n k} J ^ {p}.
$$
25.2.6. Proof of Grauert’s Theorem 25.1.5 and the Cohomology and Base Change Theorem 25.1.6 (following E. Larson).

You might even want to interpret this in terms of the Čech complex as an object of the derived category of B-modules.)
Thanks to Theorem 25.2.1, Theorems 25.1.5 and 25.1.6 are now statements about a complex of free modules over a Noetherian ring.

25.2.7. Proof of Grauert’s Theorem 25.1.5. By hypothesis, $\mathsf { h } ^ { \mathrm { p } } ( X _ { \mathrm { q } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { q } } } )$ is a locally constant function of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ . From (25.2.4.1), $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { h } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( X _ { \mathsf { q } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathsf { q } } } ) = \mathtt { r a n k } \mathsf { K } ^ { \mathsf { p } } - \mathtt { r a n k } \mathsf { i m } ( \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } } | _ { \mathsf { q } } ) - \mathtt { r a n k } \mathsf { i m } ( \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } | _ { \mathsf { q } } ) } \end{array}$ . But rank $K ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is constant, and rank im $\left( { \delta } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } | _ { \mathfrak { q } } \right)$ and rank i $\mathbf { n } ( \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } | _ { \mathbf { q } } )$ are lower semicontinuous, so in fact rank $\mathrm { i m } ( \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } } | _ { \mathsf { q } } )$ and rank i $\mathbf { n } ( \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } | _ { \mathbf { q } } )$ must be locally constant. By Exercise 14.3.L, both $\delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } - 1 }$ and $\delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ are maps of vector bundles (in the sense of Definition 14.2.4). Then by Observation 14.2.5(ii), coker $\ S ^ { \mathrm { p - 1 } }$ and im δp are both locally free (of finite rank). In the short exact sequence

(Exercise 1.5.6.4, the “dual” definition of cohomology), both coker $\delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } - 1 }$ and $\mathrm { i m } \delta ^ { \mathrm { p } }$ correspond to finite rank locally free sheaves. 25.2.9. Proof of the Cohomology and Base Change Theorem 25.1.6. We focus on the complex near the pth step, and its restriction to the point $\mathsf { q } \in \boldsymbol { X }$ :

list_items: • By hypothesis, $\phi _ { \mathfrak { q } } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } : ( \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } ) | _ { \mathfrak { q } } \to \mathsf { H } ^ { \mathfrak { p } } ( X _ { \mathfrak { q } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathfrak { q } } } )$ is surjective.

• By Lemma 25.2.8, this is equivalent to $( \ker \delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } } ) \otimes \mathsf { \boldsymbol { \kappa } } ( \mathsf { \boldsymbol { q } } ) \longrightarrow \ker ( \delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } } \otimes \mathsf { \boldsymbol { \kappa } } ( \mathsf { \boldsymbol { q } } ) )$ being surjective.

text: Now $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } ) = \mathsf { c o k e r } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } \to \mathsf { k e r } \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } } )$ ), i.e., we have the exact sequence

$$
K ^ {p - 1} \longrightarrow \ker \delta^ {p} \longrightarrow H ^ {p} (K ^ {\bullet}) \longrightarrow 0.
$$
Thus $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { p } } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } )$ commutes with any base change (as tensor product is right exact). This completes the proof of part (i) of the theorem.

For part (ii), consider again the map $\mathsf { K } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } \to \ker \delta ^ { \mathsf { p } }$ of finite rank locally free sheaves, whose cokernel is $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } )$ . Now $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } )$ is locally free if and only if $\mathsf { K } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } \to \ker \bar { \delta } ^ { \mathsf { p } }$ is a map of vector bundles, if and only if (since $\delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } }$ is a map of vector bundles) $\delta ^ { \mathfrak { p } - 1 }$ is a map of vector bundles, if and only if $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } ) \to \mathsf { H } ^ { \mathsf { p } - 1 } ( \mathsf { K } ^ { \bullet } | _ { \mathsf { q } } )$ is surjective. This completes the proof of part (ii). □
25.2.10.* Removing Noetherian conditions.
It can be helpful to have versions of the theorems of §25.1 without Noetherian conditions; important examples come from moduli theory, and will be discussed in the next section. Noetherian

conditions can often be exchanged for finite presentation conditions. We begin with an extension of Exercise 10.3.H.

list_items: (a) the constancy of Euler characteristic in flat families (Theorem 24.7.1, extended to the proper case as in §25.2.5);
