text: 25.2.12. Necessity of finite presentation conditions. The finite presentation conditions are necessary. There is a projective flat morphism to a connected target where the fiber dimension jumps. There is a finite flat morphism where the degree of the fiber is not locally constant. There is a projective flat morphism to a connected target where the fibers are curves, and the arithmetic genus is not constant. See [Stacks, tag 05LB] for the first example; the other two use the same idea.
25.3 Applying Cohomology and Base Change to Moduli Problems

The theory of moduli relies on ideas of cohomology and base change. We explore this by examining two special cases of one of the primordial moduli spaces, the Hilbert scheme: the Grassmannian, and the fact that degree d hypersurfaces in projective space are “parametrized” by another projective space (corresponding to degree d polynomials; see Remark 4.5.3).

As suggested in $\ S 2 4 . 0 . 1 _ { \cdot }$ , the Hilbert functor HilbY $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ of $\mathbb { P } _ { \Upsilon } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ parametrizes flat finitely presented closed subschemes of $\mathbb { P } _ { \Upsilon } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ where Y is an arbitrary scheme. More precisely, it is a contravariant functor sending the Y-scheme X to the set of finitely presented closed subschemes of $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { X } \times \mathsf { \mathrm { { Y } } } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { Y } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } = \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { X } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , } \end{array}$ flat over X (and sending morphisms $\Chi _ { 1 } \to \mathsf { X } _ { 2 }$ to pullbacks of flat families). An early achievement of Grothendieck was the construction of the Hilbert scheme, which can then be cleverly used to construct many other moduli spaces.
Chapter 25 Cohomology and Base Change Theorems
25.3.1. Theorem (Grothendieck) — $\operatorname { H i l b } _ { \mathbb { Z } } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is representable by a scheme locally of finite type.
(Grothendieck’s original argument is in [Gr4]. A readable construction is given in [Mu2], and in [FGIKNV, Ch. 5].)

We won’t use the following exercise, but it is conceptually very important, and explains why “moduli spaces tautologically always have universal families” (with the “correct” definition of “moduli space”). The proof will remind you of Yoneda’s Lemma. (You have already seen universal families of moduli spaces under other names, such as the universal hyperplane, Definition 13.4.1, or tautological bundles, §16.4.1.)

25.3.A. EXERCISE (THE UNIVERSAL FAMILY OVER THE HILBERT SCHEME). Assume Theorem 25.3.1. Show that there exists a closed subscheme Z of $\mathrm { H i l b _ { \mathbb { Z } } P ^ { n } } \times \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { n }$
(which we call the universal family over the Hilbert scheme) such that a flat family
corresponds to a map $\pi : \mathrm { B } \to \mathrm { H i l b } _ { \mathbb { Z } } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ if and only if Y is the pullback of Z by $\pi ,$ i.e., diagram
is a Cartesian diagram. Hint: The Hilbert functor applied to the Hilbert scheme by definition corresponds to the set Maps(Hilb Pn, Hilb Pn). The universal family will correspond to the identity map.

list_items: 25.3.B. EASY EXERCISE. Assuming Theorem 25.3.1, show that $\operatorname { H i l b } _ { \mathsf { Y } } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is representable, by showing that it is represented by $\mathrm { H i l b _ { \mathbb { Z } } } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } \times _ { \mathbb { Z } } \cdot$ Y. Thus the general case follows from the “universal” case of $\Upsilon = \mathbb { Z }$ .

25.3.C. EXERCISE. Assuming Theorem 25.3.1, show that $\mathrm { H i l b } _ { \mathbb { Z } } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ is the disjoint union of schemes $\mathrm { H i l b } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { \mathrm { p ( m ) } } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } } .$ , each one corresponding to finitely presented closed subschemes of $\mathbb { P } _ { Z } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ whose fibers have fixed Hilbert polynomial p(m). Hint: Corollary 24.7.2.
Motivated by Exercise 25.3.C, what Grothendieck actually proved was the following.
25.3.2. Theorem (Grothendieck) — Each $\mathrm { H i l b } _ { \mathbb { Z } } ^ { \mathsf { p } ( \mathsf { m } ) } \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is projective over Z.
25.3.D. EXERCISE. Prove that Theorem 25.3.2 implies Theorem 25.3.1.

text: In order to get some feeling for the Hilbert scheme, we discuss two important examples, without relying on Theorem 25.3.1.
25.3 Applying Cohomology and Base Change to Moduli Problems
25.3.3. The Grassmannian.

We have defined the Grassmannian ${ \sf G } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } )$ twice before, in $\ S 7 . 7$ and $\ S 1 6 . 4$ . The second time involved showing the representability of a (contravariant) functor (from Sheaves to Sets), of rank k locally free quotient sheaves of a rank n free sheaf.

We now consider a parameter space for a more geometric problem. The space will again be ${ \sf G } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } ) .$ , but because we won’t immediately know this, we invent some temporary notation. Let ${ \sf G } ^ { \prime } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } )$ be the contravariant functor (from Schemes to Sets) that assigns to a scheme B the set of finitely presented closed subschemes of $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n - 1 } }$ , flat over B, whose fiber over any point $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { b } } \in \mathbb { B }$ is a (linearly embedded) $\mathbb { P } _ { \kappa ( \mathbf { b } ) } ^ { \mathbf { k } - 1 }$ in $\mathbb { P } _ { \kappa ( \mathbf { \boldsymbol { b } } ) } ^ { \mathbf { \boldsymbol { n } } - 1 }$ :
(This describes the map to Sets; you should think through how pullback makes this into a contravariant functor.)
25.3.4. Theorem — The functor ${ \sf G } ^ { \prime } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } )$ is represented by ${ \sf G } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } )$ .

Translation: There is a natural (i.e., functorial) bijection between diagrams of the form (25.3.3.1) (where the fibers are $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathbf { k } - 1 } \mathbf { \prime } _ { \mathbf { S } } )$ and diagrams of the form (16.4.0.1) (the diagrams that ${ \sf G } ( { \sf k } , { \sf n } )$ parametrizes, or represents).
Proof. One direction is notably easier. Suppose we are given a diagram of the form (16.4.0.1) over a scheme B,

where $\mathcal { Q }$ is locally free of rank k. Applying $\mathcal { P } r o j _ { \mathrm { ~ B ~ } }$ to the Sym• construction on both ${ \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \oplus { \mathfrak { n } } }$ and ${ \mathcal { Q } } ,$ we obtain a closed embedding
(as, for example, in Exercise 17.2.H).

The fibers are linearly embedded $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathbb { k } - 1 }$ ’s (as base change, in this case to a point of B, commutes with the Proj construction, Exercise 17.2.E). 25.3.5. Hypersurfaces.

Ages ago (in Remark 4.5.3), we informally said that hypersurfaces of degree d in $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ are parametrized by a $\mathbb { P } ^ { \left( { n + d } \right) - 1 }$ . We now make this precise. We work over a base $\mathbb { Z }$ for suitable generality. You are welcome to replace $\mathbb { Z }$ by a field of your choice, but by the same argument as in Easy Exercise 25.3.B, all other cases are obtained from this one by base change.

Define the contravariant functor $\mathsf { H } _ { \mathrm { d } , \mathrm { n } } \colon S c h \longrightarrow S e t$ ts from schemes to sets as follows. To a scheme B, we associate the set of all closed subschemes $\mathbb { X } { \hookrightarrow } \mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { B } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ , flat and finitely presented over B, all of whose fibers are degree d hypersurfaces in $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ (over the appropriate residue field). To a morphism $\mathrm { B } _ { 1 }  \mathrm { B } _ { 2 } ,$ we obtain a map $\mathsf { H } _ { \mathrm { d } , \mathrm { n } } \big ( \mathsf { B } _ { 2 } \big ) \longrightarrow \mathsf { H } _ { \mathrm { d } , \mathrm { n } } \big ( \mathsf { B } _ { 1 } \big )$ by pullback.
25.3.6. Proposition — The functor $\mathsf { H } _ { \mathrm { d } , \mathrm { n } }$ is represented by $\mathbb { P } ^ { ( \mathfrak { n } + \mathfrak { d } } ) - 1$ .
As with the case of the Grassmannian, one direction is easy, and the other requires cohomology and base change.

text: Chapter 25 Cohomology and Base Change Theorems
Chapter 26
Depth and Cohen–Macaulayness

We now introduce the notion of depth. Depth is an algebraic rather than a geometric concept, so we concentrate on developing some geometric sense of what it means. Most important is the geometric bound on depth by dimension of (closures of) associated points (Theorem 26.1.3). A central tool to understanding depth is the Koszul complex (briefly discussed in §24.4.1), but we avoid this approach, as we can prove what we need directly.

When the depth of a local ring equals its dimension, the ring is said to be Cohen–Macaulay, and this is an important way in which schemes can be “nice.” For example, regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay (§26.2.5), as are regular embeddings in smooth varieties (Proposition 26.2.6). Cohen–Macaulayness will be key to the proof of Serre duality in Chapter 29, through the Miracle Flatness Theorem 26.2.11.

Another application of depth is Serre’s $\mathsf { R } _ { 1 } + \mathsf { S } _ { 2 }$ criterion for normality (Theorem 26.3.2), which we will use to prove that regular schemes are normal (§26.3.5) without having to show that regular local rings are factorial (the Auslander–Buchsbaum Theorem, Fact 13.8.5), and to prove that regular embeddings in smooth schemes are normal if they are regular in codimension 1 (§26.3.3).
26.1 Depth

list_items: 26.1.1. Informal introduction. Given a point p on a scheme $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ sometimes you can locally “slice” through the point—there is an effective Cartier divisor through p (in a neighborhood of p, say). You can do this if and only if the point is not an associated point of X. Sometimes you can slice again, if the first slice still doesn’t have p as an associated point. The depth of (X at) the point p is how deep you can slice—how many slices you can make before you are blocked by the point becoming associated. Obviously you can’t make more than dim X slices, since at each stage you lose a dimension, and after dim X slices, you will be left (set-theoretically) with just the point p (which is then an associated point). Thus the depth is bounded by the dimension. In figuring out the depth, you might think you would have to slice with care in order to maximize the number of slices you can take. You needn’t worry; you can slice haphazardly, and will always end at the same step.

26.1.2. Definition. Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring, and M is a finitely generated A-module. The depth of M (denoted by depth M) is the length of the longest M-regular sequence with elements in m. (The theory of regular sequences was discussed in §9.5.3.)

(More generally, if R is a Noetherian ring, $\mathrm { I } \subset \mathbb { R }$ is an ideal, and M is a finitely generated R-module, then the I-depth of $M ,$ denoted by dept ${ \mathfrak { u } } _ { \mathrm { { I } } } { \mathfrak { M } } ,$ is the length of the longest M-regular sequence with elements in I. We won’t need this.)

26.1.A. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Suppose M is a nonzero finitely generated module over a Noetherian local ring $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ . Show that depth $M = 0$ if and only if every element of m is a zerodivisor of M if and only if m is an associated prime of M.
26.1.B. EXERCISE. Suppose M is a finitely generated module over a Noetherian local ring A. Show that depth $M \leq \dim \operatorname { S u p p } M$ . In particular,

text: (We will improve this result in Theorem 26.1.7.)

At this point, it is hard to determine the depth of an A-module M. You can start trying to build an M-regular sequence by successively choosing $\mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , \mathbf { x } _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ but how do you know you have made the right choices to find the longest one? The happy answer is that you can’t go wrong; this is the content of the next result. (We then describe how to find the depth of a module M in practice, in §26.1.5.)

26.1.3. Theorem — Suppose M is a finitely generated module over a Noetherian local ring $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ . Then all maximal M-regular sequences contained in m (M-regular sequences $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ in m that cannot be extended to $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n + 1 } ,$ ) have the same length. Thus the depth of M is the length of any maximal M-regular sequence.

We prove Theorem 26.1.3 by giving a cohomological criterion, Theorem 26.1.4, for a regular sequence to be maximal. (You will then prove Theorem 26.1.3 in Exercise 26.1.D.) We will also use this criterion to give a better bound on the depth in Theorem 26.1.7. Theorem 26.1.3 is the key technical result of this chapter. An important moral is that depth should be understood as a “cohomological” property.
26.1.4. Theorem (cohomological criterion for existence of regular sequences) —
Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring, and M is a nonzero finitely generated A-module. The following are equivalent.

list_items: (i) For every finitely generated A-module N with Supp $\Nu = \{ [ \mathfrak { m } ] \}$ (i.e., N has finite length, Exercise 6.6.X(a)), $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { N } , M ) = 0$ for all ${ \mathfrak { i } } < { \mathfrak { n } }$ .
(ii) $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { m } , M ) = 0$ for all ${ \mathfrak { i } } < { \mathfrak { n } }$ .
(iii) There exists an M-regular sequence in m of length n.

text: This result can be extended in various ways; see, for example, [Mat1, Thm. 28].
Proof. Clearly (i) implies (ii).
26.1.C. EXERCISE. Prove that (ii) implies (i). Hint: Use induction on the length of N, and the long exact sequence for $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \cdot , M )$ .

Proof that (iii) implies (ii). The case $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ is vacuous. We inductively prove the result for all n. Suppose (iii) is satisfied, where $n \geq 1$ , and assume that we know (ii) for “all smaller n.” Choose a regular sequence $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ of length n. Then $x _ { 1 }$ is a non-zerodivisor on M, so we have an exact sequence

Then $M / x _ { 1 } M$ has a regular sequence $x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 }$ of length ${ \mathfrak { n } } - 1 ,$ so by the inductive hypothesis, $\mathrm { E x t _ { A } ^ { i } } ( \mathrm { A } / \mathfrak { m } , M / x _ { 1 } M ) = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } < \mathfrak { n } - 1$ . Taking the Ext long exact sequence for $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathfrak { m } , \cdot )$ for (26.1.4.1), we find that

$$
\operatorname {E x t} _ {A} ^ {i} (A / \mathfrak {m}, M) \xrightarrow {\times x _ {1}} \operatorname {E x t} _ {A} ^ {i} (A / \mathfrak {m}, M)
$$

is an injection for ${ \mathfrak { i } } < { \mathfrak { n } }$ . Now $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { m } , M )$ can be computed by taking an injective resolution of $M ,$ and applying $\mathrm { H o m } _ { \mathrm { A } } ( \mathrm { A } / \mathfrak { m } , \cdot )$ . Hence as $x _ { 1 }$ lies in m (and thus annihilates $A / { \mathfrak { m } }$ ), multiplication by $x _ { 1 }$ is the zero map. Thus (ii) holds for n as well.
Proof that (ii) implies (iii). The case $\mathfrak { n } = 0$ is vacuous.

We deal next with the case ${ \mathfrak { n } } = 1 ,$ by showing the contrapositive. Assume that there are no non-zerodivisors in m on $M _ { ☉ }$ , so by Exercise $2 6 . 1 . \mathrm { A } , \mathfrak { m }$ is an associated prime of M. Thus (§6.6.7) we have an injection $\lambda / { \mathfrak { m } } \hookrightarrow M ,$ , yielding $\mathrm { H o m } _ { \mathrm { A } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathfrak { m } , M ) \neq 0 ,$ , as desired.

We now inductively prove the result for all $\mathsf n > 1$ . Suppose (ii) is satisfied, where ${ \mathfrak { n } } \geq 2 \quad$ , and assume that we know (iii) for “all smaller n.” Then by the case $\mathfrak { n } = 1$ , there exists a non-zerodivisor $x _ { 1 }$ on $M _ { ☉ }$ , so we have a short exact sequence (26.1.4.1). A portion of the Ext long exact sequence for

$\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathfrak { m } , \cdot )$ for (26.1.4.1) is

$$
\operatorname {E x t} _ {A} ^ {i} (A / \mathfrak {m}, M) \longrightarrow \operatorname {E x t} _ {A} ^ {i} (A / \mathfrak {m}, M / x _ {1} M) \longrightarrow \operatorname {E x t} _ {A} ^ {i + 1} (A / \mathfrak {m}, M).
$$

By assumption, $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { m } , M )$ and $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } + 1 } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathfrak { m } , M )$ are 0 for $\mathfrak { i } < \mathfrak { n } - 1$ , so $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathrm { A } / \mathfrak { m } , M / x _ { 1 } M ) = 0$ for $\mathfrak { i } < \mathfrak { n } - 1$ , so by the inductive hypothesis, we have an $\left( M / x _ { 1 } M \right)$ -regular sequence $x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { \mathrm { n } }$ of length n − 1 in m. Adding $_ { x _ { 1 } }$ to the front of this sequence, we are done. □

26.1.D. EXERCISE. Prove Theorem 26.1.3. Hint: By Theorem 26.1.4 (notably, the equivalence of (ii) and (iii)), you have control of how long an M-regular sequence in m can be. Use the criterion, and the long exact sequence used in the proof of Theorem 26.1.4, to show that any M-regular sequence in m can be extended to this length.

26.1.E. EXERCISE. Suppose M is a finitely generated module over a Noetherian local ring $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ If $x$ is a non-zerodivisor in m, show that depth $\left( M / x M \right) =$ depth M − 1. Hint: Theorem 26.1.3.
26.1.5. Finding the depth of a module. We can now compute the depth of M by successively finding non-zerodivisors, as follows. Is there a non-zerodivisor $x$ on $M$ in m?

list_items: (a) If not, then depth $M = 0$ (Exercise 26.1.A).
(b) If so, then choose any such $\mathsf { x } ,$ and (using the previous exercise) repeat the process with $M / x M$

text: The process must terminate by Exercise 26.1.B.

list_items: 26.1.F. IMPORTANT EXERCISE. Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a dimension d regular local ring. Show that depth $\boldsymbol { A } = \mathrm { d }$ . Hint: Exercise 13.2.B.
26.1.G. EXERCISE (CF. EXERCISE 24.3.I). Suppose X = Spec R, where

text:
$$
R = k [ w, x, y, z ] / (w y, w z, x y, x z),
$$

Hint: Show that $w - { \mathfrak { y } }$ is not a zerodivisor, and that $\mathsf { R } / ( w - \mathsf { y } )$ has an embedded point at the origin.
26.1.6. Depth is bounded by the dimension of associated prime ideals.
Theorem 26.1.4 can be used to give an important improvement of the bound (26.1.2.1) on depth by the dimension:
26.1.7. Theorem — The depth of a module M is at most the smallest dim A/p, as p runs over the associated prime ideals of M.
(The example of two planes meeting at a point in Exercise 26.1.G shows that this bound is not sharp.) The key step in the proof of Theorem 26.1.7 is the following result of Ischebeck.

26.1.8. Lemma — Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring, and M and N are nonzero finitely generated A-modules. Then $\mathrm { E x t } _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( \mathsf { N } , M ) = 0$ for $\mathrm { i } <$ depth M − dim Supp N.
Proof. Consider the following statements.
(generalr) Lemma 26.1.8 holds for dim Supp $\mathsf { N } \leq \mathsf { r }$
(primer) Lemma 26.1.8 holds for dim Supp $\mathsf { N } \leq \mathsf { r }$ and $\mathsf { N } { = } \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { p }$ for some prime p.
Note that (general ) (and hence (prime )) is true, as in this case Supp $\mathsf { N } = \{ [ \mathsf { m } ] \} ,$ and the result follows from Theorem 26.1.4 (from “(iii) implies (i)”).

26.1.I. EASY EXERCISE. Prove Theorem 26.1.7. Hint: If ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in \operatorname { A s s } ( M )$ , then $\mathrm { H o m } _ { A } ( \mathsf { A } / \mathsf { p } , \mathsf { M } ) \neq 0$ (§6.6.7).
26.2 Cohen–Macaulay Rings and Schemes

list_items: 26.2.1. Definition. A Noetherian local ring $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is Cohen–Macaulay if depth $\lambda { = } \dim \lambda$ , i.e., if equality holds in (26.1.2.1). (One may define Cohen–Macaulay module similarly, but we won’t need this concept.) A locally Noetherian scheme is Cohen–Macaulay if all of its local rings are Cohen–Macaulay. We won’t use this language, but a ring A is a Cohen–Macaulay ring if all of its localizations $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ at all primes $\mathfrak { p }$ are Cohen–Macaulay, or, equivalently, if Spec A is Cohen–Macaulay. (Fact 26.2.14 below, that Cohen–Maculayness of a local ring is preserved by localization, ensures that there is no contradiction between this definition and the definition of a Cohen–Macaulay local ring.)

26.2.A. EXERCISE. Show that every locally Noetherian scheme of dimension 0 is Cohen– Macaulay. Show that a locally Noetherian scheme of dimension 1 is Cohen–Macaulay if and only if it has no embedded points.
26.2.2. (Counter)example. Let X be the example of Exercise 26.1.G—two planes meeting at a point. By Exercise 26.1.G, X is not Cohen–Macaulay.

26.2.B. EXERCISE. Suppose A is a Cohen–Macaulay Noetherian local ring. Use Theorem 26.1.7 to show that Spec A is pure dimensional, and has no embedded points. (It is not true that Noetherian local rings of pure dimension having no embedded prime ideals are Cohen–Macaulay; see Example 26.2.2.)

26.2.3. Theorem (slicing criterion for Cohen–Macaulayness) — Suppose $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring, and $x \in { \mathfrak { m } }$ is a non-zerodivisor. Then $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is Cohen–Macaulay if and only i $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } \left( \boldsymbol { A } / \boldsymbol { x } , \mathfrak { m } \right)$ is Cohen–Macaulay.
Compare this to the slicing criteria for regularity and flatness (Exercise 13.2.C and Theorem 24.6.6, respectively).

26.2.4. Geometric interpretation of the slicing criterion. Suppose X is a locally Noetherian scheme, and D is an effective Cartier divisor. If X is Cohen–Macaulay, then so is D. If D is Cohen–Macaulay, then X is Cohen–Macaulay at the points of D.

26.2.C. EXERCISE. Prove Theorem 26.2.3, using Theorem 26.1.3, the fact that maximal regular sequences (in m) all have the same length. (Hint: Recall Exercise 12.3.I, that dim $\mathtt { A } / ( \mathsf { f } ) \ge \dim \mathtt { A } - 1$ for any $\mathsf { f } \in \boldsymbol { A } .$ .)

text: Chapter 26 Depth and Cohen–Macaulayness

26.2.D. EXERCISE. Show that if $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is Cohen–Macaulay, then a set of elements $\mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \hdots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { r } } \in \mathfrak { m }$ is a regular sequence (for A) if and only if d $\operatorname { i m } \lambda / ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { \operatorname { r } } ) = \dim \lambda - r$ .

26.2.5. By Exercise 26.1.F, regular local rings (Noetherian by definition) are Cohen–Macaulay. In particular, as smooth schemes over a field k are regular (Theorem 13.2.7(b), proved in §24.8.9), we see that smooth k-schemes are Cohen–Macaulay. (In combination with Exercise 26.2.B, this explains why effective Cartier divisors on smooth varieties have no embedded points, justifying a comment made in Aside 20.2.1.) Combining this with Exercise 26.2.D or §26.2.4, we have the following.
26.2.6. Proposition — Regular embeddings in smooth k-schemes are Cohen–Macaulay.

26.2.7. As a consequence of Proposition 26.2.6 and the fact that Cohen–Macaulay schemes have no embedded points (Exercise 26.2.B), we see that regular embeddings in smooth k-schemes (in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ or $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ , for example) have no embedded points, generalizing Exercise 6.6.F (the hypersurface in $\mathbb { A } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ case). This is not clear without the theory of Cohen–Macaulayness!

26.2.8. Alternate definition of Cohen–Macaulayness. The slicing criterion (Theorem 26.2.3) gives an enlightening alternative inductive definition of Cohen–Macaulayness in terms of effective Cartier divisors, in the spirit of the method of §26.1.5 for computing depth. Suppose as before that $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring.

list_items: (i) If $\dim { \cal A } = 0$ , then A is Cohen–Macaulay (Exercise 26.2.A).
(iia) If $\dim { \cal A } > 0$ , and every element of m is a zerodivisor, then A is not Cohen–Macaulay (by Exercise 26.1.A).

(iib) Otherwise, choose any non-zerodivisor $x$ in m. Then A is Cohen–Macaulay if and only if $\boldsymbol { A } / ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ (necessarily of dimension dim A − 1 by Krull’s Principal Ideal Theorem 12.3.3) is Cohen–Macaulay.

text: The following example could have been stated (but not proved) before we knew any algebraic geometry at all. (We work over $\mathbb { C }$ rather than over an arbitrary field only to ensure that the statement requires as little background as possible.)

26.2.E. FUN EXERCISE (MAX NOETHER’S AF + BG THEOREM). Suppose f, ${ \mathfrak { g } } \in \mathbb { C } [ { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 0 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 1 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 2 } ]$ are two homogeneous polynomials, cutting out two curves in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 2 }$ that meet “transversely,” i.e., at a finite number of reduced points. Suppose $\mathtt { h } \in \mathbb { C } [ x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ]$ is a homogeneous polynomial vanishing at these points. Show that $\hslash \in ( \mathsf { f } , \mathsf { g } )$ . Hint: Show that the intersection of the affine cones $\mathsf { V } ( \mathsf { f } )$ and ${ \mathsf { V } } ( { \mathsf { g } } )$ in $\mathbb { A } ^ { 3 }$ has no embedded points. (This problem is quite nontrivial to do without the theory developed in this chapter! As a sign that this is subtle: you can easily construct three quadratics e $ \sf { \Psi } , \sf { f } , \sf { g } \in \mathbb { C } [ \sf x _ { 0 } , \sf x _ { 1 } , \sf x _ { 2 } ]$ cutting out precisely the two points $[ 1 , 0 , 0 ]$ and $[ 0 , 1 , 0 ] \in \mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 2 } ,$ yet the line $z = 0$ is not in the ideal $( e , \mathsf { f } , \mathsf { g } )$ for degree reasons.)
As additional motivation for Cohen–Macaulayness, the next exercise is seemingly basic, but we need Cohen–Macaulayness to prove it cleanly.

26.2.F. EXERCISE. Suppose $\mathsf { H } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { H } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ are n hypersurfaces in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ of degrees $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { d } _ { n } ,$ respectively, and suppose their scheme-theoretic intersection Z is a finite number of points. Show that the sum (over the points of Z) of the lengths of Z at the points is $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } \cdots \mathrm { d } _ { n }$ . Equivalently: Show that the length of Z is $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } \cdots \mathrm { d } _ { n }$ . Informally: The hypersurfaces intersect at $\mathrm { d } _ { 1 } \cdots \mathrm { d } _ { n }$ points, “counted correctly.” Hint: Bézout’s Theorem, in the form of Exercise 18.6.J.
26.2.9. Miracle flatness.
We conclude with a remarkably simple and useful criterion for flatness, which we shall use in the proof of Serre duality. The main content is the following algebraic result.

26.2.10. Miracle Flatness Theorem (algebraic version) — Suppose $\Phi \colon ( \mathrm { B } , \mathfrak { n } )  ( \mathrm { A } , \mathfrak { m } )$ is a (local) homomorphism of Noetherian local rings, such that A is Cohen–Macaulay, and B is regular, and
26.2 Cohen–Macaulay Rings and Schemes
$A / { \mathfrak { n } } A = A \otimes _ { \mathrm { B } }$ $( \mathtt { B } / { \mathfrak { n } }$ ) (the ring corresponding to the fiber) has pure dimension dim A − dim B. Then ϕ is flat.

Proof. We prove Theorem 26.2.10 by induction on dim B. If dim $\mathrm { B } = 0$ , then B is a field (Exercise 13.2.A), so the result is immediate, as everything is flat over a field (Exercise 24.1.A(a)). Assume next that dim $\mathrm { B } > 0 ,$ and we have proved the result for all $\mathbf { \Omega } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathbf { B }$ of smaller dimension.” Choose $\mathsf { x } \in \mathsf { n } \backslash \mathsf { n } ^ { 2 } .$ , so $\mathrm { B } / x$ is a regular local ring of dimension dim B − 1 (Exercise 12.3.I). Then

By (Exercise 12.3.I), dim $\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { A } / { \mathrm { x } } \mathsf { A } \geq \dim \mathsf { A } - 1 , } \end{array}$ , so we have $\dim { \cal A } / { \mathrm { { x } } } { \cal A } = \dim { \mathrm { B } } / ( { \mathrm { x } } ) + \dim { \cal A } / { \mathrm { n } } { \cal A } =$ dim A − 1. By Exercise 26.2.D, $A / x A$ is a Cohen–Macaulay ring, and $x$ is a non-zerodivisor on A. The inductive hypothesis then applies to $( \mathtt { B } / ( x ) , \mathfrak { n } ) \to ( \mathtt { A } / x \mathtt { A } , \mathfrak { m } ) .$ , so $A / x A$ is flat over $\mathrm { B } / ( x )$ . Then by the local slicing criterion for flatness (on the target, Theorem 24.6.6), $\mathrm { B } \to A$ is flat, as desired.

list_items: 26.2.11. Miracle Flatness Theorem — Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a morphism of pure dimensional finite type k-schemes, where X is Cohen–Macaulay, Y is regular, and the fibers of $\pi$ have dimension dim X − dim Y. Then π is flat.
26.2.G. EXERCISE. Prove the Miracle Flatness Theorem 26.2.11. (Do not forget that schemes usually have non-closed points!)

text: The geometric situation in the Miracle Flatness Theorem 26.2.11 is part of the following pretty package.

26.2.H. EXERCISE (THE MIRACLE FLATNESS PACKAGE). Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is a map of locally →finite type k-schemes, where both X and Y are pure dimensional, and Y is regular. Show that if any two of the following hold, then the third does as well:

list_items: (i) $\pi$ is flat of relative dimension dim X − dim Y.
(ii) $x$ is Cohen–Macaulay.
(iii) Every fiber $X _ { y }$ is Cohen–Macaulay of pure dimension dim X − dim Y.

text: Hint: If $\Phi \colon \mathrm { B }  \mathcal { A }$ is a flat ring map, then $\boldsymbol { \Phi }$ sends non-zerodivisors to non-zerodivisors (Observation 24.1.2).
The statement of Exercise 26.2.H can be improved, at the expense of killing the symmetry. In the implication that (ii) and (iii) imply (i), the Cohen–Macaulay hypotheses in (iii) are not needed.

26.2.12. Example. As an example of Exercise 26.2.H in action, we consider the example of two planes meeting a point, continuing the notation of Exercise 26.1.G. Consider the morphism π: $x $ $\Upsilon \{ = \mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ given by $( w - \mathfrak { y } , \mathfrak { x } - z )$ . Note that Y is regular (§13.3.6). But X is not Cohen–Macaulay (Exercise 26.1.G) and $\pi$ is not flat (Exercise 24.3.I), so by Exercise 26.2.H, you can use either of these to prove the other.
26.2.13. Fancy properties of Cohen–Macaulayness.
We mention a few additional properties of Cohen–Macaulayness without proof. They are worth seeing, but we will not use them.
If A is a Noetherian ring that is the quotient of a Cohen–Macaulay ring, the locus of Cohen– Macaulay points in Spec A is open; see [Mat2, Ex. 24.2]. Related:
26.2.14. Fact (cf. Fact 13.8.5) — Any localization of a Cohen–Macaulay Noetherian local ring at a prime ideal is also Cohen–Macaulay.
(See [Stacks, tag 00NB], [E, Prop. 18.8], [Mat2, Thm. 17.3(iii)], or [Mat1, Thm. 30] for a direct proof.) In particular, for varieties, Cohen–Macaulayness may be checked at closed points
Chapter 26 Depth and Cohen–Macaulayness

(Exercise 5.3.F). The fact that Cohen–Macaulayness is preserved by localization can be used to quickly show that Cohen–Macaulay local rings are catenary (see [E, Cor. 18.10], [Mat1, Thm. 31(ii)], or [Mat2, Thm. 17.9]). A ring A is Cohen–Macaulay if and only if A[x] is Cohen–Macaulay ([E, Prop 18.9], [Mat2, Thm 17.7], [Mat1, Thm. 33]), if and only if A[[x]] is [Mat2, p. 137]. A local ring is Cohen–Macaulay if and only if its completion is Cohen–Macaulay ([E, Prop. 18.8], [Mat2, Thm 17.5]).
For these and many other cheerful facts, and, in general, a thorough introduction to Cohen– Macaulayness, see [BruH].
26.3 Serre’s $\mathsf { R } _ { 1 } + \mathsf { S } _ { 2 }$ Criterion for Normality
The notion of depth yields a useful criterion for normality, due to Serre.

26.3.1. Definition. Suppose A is a Noetherian ring, and $\ v { k } \in \mathbb { Z } ^ { \geq 0 }$ . We say A has property $\mathsf { R } _ { \mathsf { k } }$ (A is regular in codimension $\leq \mathrm { k } ,$ or, more sloppily, A is regular in codimension k) if for every prime ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset A$ of codimension at most $\mathsf { k } , \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ is regular. (We have repeatedly seen the importance of regularity in codimension 1.) Thus a Noetherian ring is regular and only if it has property $\mathtt { R _ { k } }$ for all k.

We say A has property $S _ { \mathrm { k } }$ if for every prime ${ \mathfrak { p } } \subset A ,$ the local ring $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ has depth at least $\operatorname* { m i n } ( \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } , \dim A _ { \mathfrak { p } } )$ )—“the local rings are Cohen–Macaulay up until codimension ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ and have depth at least k thereafter.” Thus a Noetherian ring is Cohen–Macaulay if and only if it has property $S _ { \mathrm { k } }$ for all k.

26.3.A. EASY EXERCISE. Note that a Noetherian ring A trivially always has property $S _ { 0 }$ . Show that a Noetherian ring A has property $\mathtt { R } _ { 0 }$ if Spec A is “generically reduced”: it is reduced at the generic point of each of its irreducible components. Show that a Noetherian ring A has property $\mathsf { S } _ { 1 }$ if and only if Spec A has no embedded points. (Possible hint: Exercise 26.1.A.)

26.3.B. EXERCISE (IMPORTANT ELEMENTARY INTERPRETATION OF $S _ { 2 }$ ). Suppose A is a Noetherian integral domain. Show that A has property $S _ { 2 }$ if and only if for all nonzero ${ \mathfrak { g } } \in { \mathfrak { A } } ,$ A/(g) has no embedded primes.

26.3.C. EXERCISE. Show that a Noetherian ring A has no nilpotents (Spec A is reduced) if and only if it has properties $\mathtt { R } _ { 0 }$ and S1. (Hint: Show that a Noetherian scheme is reduced if and only if each irreducible component is generically reduced, and it has no embedded points.)
Incrementing the subscripts in Exercise 26.3.C yields Serre’s criterion.
26.3.2. Theorem (Serre’s criterion for normality) — A Noetherian ring A is normal if and only if it has properties ${ \sf R } _ { 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 }$ .

(Recall from §5.4.1 that a Noetherian ring A is normal if its localizations $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { p } }$ at all primes $\mathfrak { p }$ are integrally closed domains.) Thus failure of normality can have two possible causes: it can be failure of ${ \sf R } _ { 1 }$ (something we already knew, from the equivalence of (a) and (g) in Theorem 13.5.8), or it can be the more subtle failure of $S _ { 2 }$ . Examples of varieties satisfying R1 but not $S _ { 2 }$ are given in Example 26.3.4 (two planes meeting at a point) and Exercise 26.3.F (the pinched plane).
26.3.D. EASY EXERCISE. Prove Serre’s criterion for normality (Theorem 26.3.2). Hint: Look closely at Proposition 13.5.24 and Proposition 13.5.26.

26.3.3. Applications. First, Serre’s Criterion for normality implies that Cohen–Macaulay schemes are normal if and only if they are regular in codimension 1. Thus to check normality of hypersurfaces (or, more generally, regular embeddings) in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ (or, more generally, in any smooth variety), it suffices to check that their singular locus has codimension greater than 1. (You should think through the details of why these statements are true.) In particular, this gives a new (more complicated) proof of Exercise 5.4.I(b) and (c).
26.3 Serre’s $\mathsf { R } _ { 1 } + \mathsf { S } _ { 2 }$ Criterion for Normality

list_items: 26.3.E. EXERCISE (PRACTICE WITH THE CONCEPT). Show that two-dimensional normal varieties are Cohen–Macaulay.

26.3.4. Example: Two planes meeting at a point. The variety X of Exercise 26.1.G (two planes meeting at a point) is not normal (why?), but it is regular away from the origin. This implies that X does not have property $S _ { 2 }$ (and hence is not Cohen–Macaulay), without the algebraic manipulations of Exercise 26.1.G.

text: We already knew that this example was not Cohen–Macaulay, but the same idea can show that the pinched plane Spec $\mathbf { k } [ x ^ { 3 } , x ^ { 2 } , x \mathbf { \bar { y } } , \ y ]$ (appearing in Exercise 13.5.H) is not Cohen–Macaulay. Because of the “extrinsic” description of the ring, it is difficult to do this in another way.

list_items: 26.3.F. EXERCISE: THE PINCHED PLANE IS NOT COHEN–MACAULAY. Let A be the subring $\operatorname { k } [ x ^ { 3 } , x ^ { 2 } , x y , y ]$ of k[x, y]. Show that A is not Cohen–Macaulay. Hint: Exercise 13.5.H showed that A is not integrally closed.

26.3.5. Regular local rings are integrally closed. Serre’s criterion for normality can be used to show that regular local rings are integrally closed without going through the difficult Fact 13.8.5 (the Auslander–Buchsbaum Theorem) that they are unique factorization domains. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay (§26.2.5), and (by Exercise 26.3.C) regular in codimension 0 as they are integral domains (Theorem 13.2.11), so we need only show that regular local rings are regular in codimension 1. We can invoke a different difficult Fact 13.8.2 that localizations of regular local rings are again regular, but at least we have shown this for localizations of finitely generated algebras over a perfect field (see Theorem 13.8.3 and Exercise 21.6.B).
26.3.6. Caution. As is made clear by the following exercise, the condition $S _ { 2 }$ is a condition on all prime ideals, not just those of codimension at most 2.
26.3.G. EXERCISE. Give an example of a variety satisfying ${ \sf R } _ { 1 }$ , and Cohen–Macaulay at all points of codimension at most 2, which is not normal.

26.3.7.** Projective normality. We will not use the notion of projective normality, but mention it because many readers may come across it, and it is easy to introduce. It is a property of a closed embedding of $\pi { : } X \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \ k } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ . Let $\mathcal { L } = \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ be the line bundle giving the closed embedding (§15.2), and for convenience let $\mathsf { R } ( \mathcal { L } ) _ { \bullet } : = \oplus _ { \mathsf { m } \geq 0 } \Gamma ( X , \mathcal { L } ^ { \otimes \mathsf { m } } )$ be the graded ring of “sections of all powers of $\mathcal { L }$ .” Then we say (Definition 1) $\mathbb { X } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is projectively normal if X is normal and for all $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0$ , $\mathsf { H } ^ { \circ } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { O } ( \mathsf { m } ) ) \to \mathsf { H } ^ { \circ } ( X , \mathcal { O } _ { X } ( \mathsf { m } ) )$ is surjective. In other words, all sections of ${ \mathcal { L } } ^ { \otimes \mathfrak { m } }$ for all $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0$ “come from” polynomials; there are no “new” sections not “arising” from $\mathsf { H } ^ { 0 } ( \mathsf { X } , \mathcal { L } )$ . Equivalently (Definition 2), via $\mathcal { L } = \pi ^ { * } \mathcal { O } ( 1 )$ , $\mathsf { R } ( \mathcal { L } ) _ { \bullet }$ is a quotient of the polynomial ring $\mathbf { k } [ \mathbf { x } _ { 0 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } } ]$ . You will quickly be able to show that this is equivalent to the statement (Definition 3) that $\mathrm { \Delta } X$ is normal and) $\mathsf { H } ^ { 1 } ( \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } } , \mathcal { I } _ { \mathsf { X } } ( \mathsf { m } ) ) = 0$ for all $\mathfrak { m } \geq 0$ , and also to the statement (Definition 4) that X is normal, $\mathsf { R } ( \mathcal { L } )$ . is generated in degree 1, and $\mathbb { X } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is an embedding by a complete linear series (assuming it is nondegenerate).

26.3.H. EXERCISE. Show that complete intersections in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ are projectively normal if and only if they are normal if and only if they are regular in codimension 1.
It is not too hard to show that $\mathbb { X } \hookrightarrow \mathbb { P } _ { \mathsf { k } } ^ { \mathsf { n } }$ is normal if and only if its affine cone is normal, [Ha1, Ex. II.5.14].

text: Chapter 26 Depth and Cohen–Macaulayness
Chapter 27
The Twenty-Seven Lines on a Cubic Surface
Wake an algebraic geometer in the dead of night, whispering: “27”. Chances are, he will respond: “lines on a cubic surface.”
—R. Donagi and R. Smith [DS] (on page 27, of course)

Since the middle of the nineteenth century, geometers have been entranced by the fact that there are 27 lines on every smooth cubic surface, and by the remarkable structure of the configuration of the lines. Their discovery by Cayley and Salmon in 1849 has been called the beginning of modern algebraic geometry, [Do, p. 55].

The reason so many people are bewitched by this fact is because it requires some magic, and this magic connects to many other things, including fundamental ideas we have discussed, other beautiful classical constructions (such as Pascal’s Mystical Hexagon Theorem 19.4.4, the fact that most smooth quartic plane curves have 28 bitangents, exceptional Lie groups, . . . ), and many themes in modern algebraic geometry (deformation theory, intersection theory, enumerative geometry, arithmetic and diophantine questions, . . .). It will be particularly pleasant for us, as it takes advantage of many of the things we have learned.
You are now ready to be initiated into the secret fellowship of the 27 lines.
27.0.1. Theorem — Every smooth cubic surface in $\mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathbf { k } } } } ^ { 3 }$ contains exactly 27 lines.
Theorem 27.0.1 is closely related to the following.
27.0.2. Theorem — Every smooth cubic surface over $\bar { \mathsf k }$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ blown up at six points.

There are many reasons why people consider these facts magical. First, there is the fact that there are always 27 lines. Unlike most questions in enumerative geometry, there are no weasel words such as “a general cubic surface” or “most cubic surfaces” or “counted correctly”—as in, “every monic degree d polynomial has d roots—counted correctly.” And somehow (and we will see how) it is precisely the smoothness of the surface that makes it work.
Second, there is the magic that you always get the blow-up of the plane at six points (§27.3).

Third, there is the magical incidence structure of the 27 lines, which relates to $\mathsf { E } _ { 6 }$ in Lie theory. The Weyl group of $\mathsf { E } _ { 6 }$ is the symmetry group of the incidence structure (see Remark 27.2.5). In a natural way, the 27 lines form a basis of the 27-dimensional fundamental representation of $\mathsf { E } _ { 6 }$ .

27.0.3. On the structure of this chapter. Throughout this chapter, X will be a smooth cubic surface over an algebraically closed field k. In §27.1, we establish some preliminary facts. In §27.2, we prove Theorem 27.0.1, away from characteristic 3. In §27.3, we prove Theorem 27.0.2. We remark here that the only input that §27.3 needs from §27.2 is Exercise 27.2.J. This can be done directly by hand (see, for example, [Rei1, §7], [Be, Thm. IV.13], or $[ \mathrm { S h } ,$ pp. 246–7]), and Theorem 27.0.2 readily implies Theorem 27.0.1, using Exercise 27.3.F. We would thus have another, shorter, proof of Theorem 27.0.1. The reasons for giving the argument of §27.2 are that it is natural given what we have done so far, it gives you some glimpse of some ideas used more broadly in the subject (the key idea is that a map from one moduli space to another is finite and flat), and it may help you further appreciate and digest the tools we have developed.

image_caption: Figure 27.1 The 27 lines on the Clebsch cubic surface (courtesy of Greg Egan)

text: 27.0.4. On the location of this chapter.

This topic is Chapter 27 for purely numerological reasons; it could certainly be read after Chapter 24. This is a feature rather than a bug: you needn’t read the details of the later chapters in order to enjoy this one. In fact, you should probably read this many chapters earlier, taking key facts as “black boxes,” to appreciate the value of seemingly technical definitions and theorems before you learn about them in detail. This topic is a fitting conclusion to an energetic introduction to algebraic geometry, even if you do not earlier cover everything needed to justify all details.
27.1 Preliminary Facts

By Essential Exercise 15.1.C, there is a 20-dimensional vector space of cubic forms in four variables, so the cubic surfaces in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ are parametrized by $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ . Let $\Delta \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ be the locus corresponding to singular cubics. Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2 shows that the locus of smooth cubics $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta$ is dense and open in the space $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ of all cubics.

27.1.A. EXERCISE. Show that any smooth cubic surface X is “anticanonically embedded”—it is embedded by the complete anticanonical linear series $\mathcal { H } _ { X } ^ { \vee }$ . Hint: The adjunction formula, Exercise 21.5.B.

27.1.B. EXERCISE. Suppose $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { P } _ { \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ^ { 3 }$ is a smooth cubic surface. Suppose C is a curve on X. Show that C is a line if and only if C is a $( - 1 )$ -curve (Definition 20.2.8). Hint: The adjunction formula again, perhaps in the guise of Exercise 20.2.B(a); and also Exercise 18.6.K.
It will be useful to find a single cubic surface over a single field with exactly 27 lines:
27.1.C. HANDS-ON EXERCISE. Show that the Fermat cubic surface
Chapter 27 The Twenty-Seven Lines on a Cubic Surface
in $\mathbb { P } _ { \mathbb { C } } ^ { 3 }$ has precisely 27 lines, each of the form

$$
x _ {0} + \omega x _ {\mathrm {i}} = x _ {\mathrm {j}} + \omega^ {\prime} x _ {\mathrm {k}} = 0,
$$

where $\{ 1 , 2 , 3 \} = \{ \mathrm { i } , \mathrm { j } , \mathrm { k } \} , \mathrm { j } < \mathrm { k } ,$ and ω and $\omega ^ { \prime }$ are cube roots of 1 (possibly the same). This will require some brute force. Possible hint: Up to a permutation of coordinates, show that every line in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ can be written ${ \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 0 } = { \mathrm { a x } } _ { 2 } + { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { b x } } } _ { 3 }$ , ${ \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } } _ { 1 } = { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { c } } } { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } } _ { 2 } + { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { d } } } { \boldsymbol { \mathsf { x } } } _ { 3 }$ . Show that this line is on (27.1.0.1) if and only if
Show that if ${ \mathrm { a } } , { \mathrm { b } } , { \mathrm { c } } ,$ and d are all nonzero, then (27.1.0.2) has no solutions.

27.1.1.* The singular cubic surfaces give an irreducible divisor $\Delta$ in the parameter space $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ of all cubic surfaces. This discussion is starred not because it is hard, but because it is not needed in the rest of the chapter. Nonetheless, it is a pretty application of what we have learned, and it foreshadows key parts of the argument in §27.2.

Bertini’s Theorem 13.4.2 shows that the locus of smooth cubics in the $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ of all cubics is dense and open, but the proof suggests more: that the complement $\Delta$ of this locus is of pure codimension 1. We now show that this is the case, and even that this “discriminant hypersurface” $\Delta$ is irreducible.
27.1.D. EXERCISE. (Hint for both: Recall the solution to Exercise 12.2.N.)

(a) Define the incidence correspondence $\mathsf { Y } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ corresponding to the data of a cubic surface $\Sigma ,$ along with a singular point ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ . (This is part of the exercise! We need Y as a scheme, not just as a set.) Let $\mu$ be the projection $\Upsilon \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ .
(b) Show that Y is an irreducible smooth variety of dimension 18, by describing it as a $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 5 }$ -bundle over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ .

27.1.E. EXERCISE. Show that there exists a cubic surface with a single singular point. Feel free to assume $\mathrm { k }$ is your favorite field; the main point is to familiarize yourself with geometric ideas, not the peculiarities of positive characteristic fields. (Hint if char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ : Exercise 13.3.C.)

27.1.F. EXERCISE. Show that $\mu ( \mathsf { Y } ) = \Delta$ is a closed irreducible subset of codimension 1. Hint for the codimension 1 statement: Exercise 12.4.A or Theorem 12.4.1, in combination with Exercise 27.1.E.
Your argument will generalize with essentially no change to deal with degree d hypersurfaces in $\mathbb { P } ^ { \mathrm { n } }$ .
27.2 Every Smooth Cubic Surface (over $\bar { \mathsf { k } }$ ) Contains 27 Lines

We are now ready to prove Theorem 27.0.1. Until Exercise 27.2.K, to avoid distraction, we assume char $\overline { { \mathsf { k } } } = 0$ . However, the following argument carries through without change if char $\overline { { \mathsf { k } } } \neq 3$ . The one required check—and the reason for the restriction on the characteristic—is that Exercise 27.1.C works with $\mathbb { C }$ replaced by any such $\bar { \boldsymbol { \mathrm { k } } }$ .
27.2.A. EXERCISE. (Hint for both: Recall the solution to Exercise 12.2.N.)

(a) Define the incidence correspondence $Z \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \times \mathbb { G } ( 1 , 3 )$ corresponding to the data of a line ℓ in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ contained in a cubic surface X. (As in Exercise 27.1.D, this is part of the exercise.) Let $\pi$
27.2 Every Smooth Cubic Surface (over k) Contains 27 Lines
be the projection $Z \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ .
(b) Show that Z is an irreducible smooth variety of dimension 19, by describing it as a $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 5 }$ -bundle over the Grassmannian $\mathbb { G } ( 1 , 3 )$ .

27.2.B. EXERCISE. Use the fact that there exists a cubic surface with a finite number of lines (Exercise 27.1.C), and the behavior of dimensions of fibers of morphisms (Exercise 12.4.A or Theorem 12.4.1), to show the following.

text: The following fact is the key result in the proof of Theorem 27.0.1, and one of the main miracles of the 27 lines, which ensures that the lines stay distinct on a smooth surface. It states, informally, that two lines can’t come together without damaging the surface. This is really a result in deformation theory: we are explicitly showing that a line in a smooth cubic surface has no first-order deformations.
27.2.1. Theorem — If ℓ is a line in a regular cubic surface X, then $\{ \ell \subset X \}$ is a reduced isolated point of the fiber of π : Z P19. $Z \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$
Before proving Theorem 27.2.1, we use it to prove Theorem 27.0.1.

27.2.2. Proof of Theorem 27.0.1, assuming Theorem 27.2.1. Now $\pi$ is a projective morphism, and by Theorem 27.2.1, over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta , \pi$ has relative dimension 0, and hence has finite fibers. Hence, by Theorem 18.1.6 (projective $^ +$ finite fibers $=$ finite), $\pi$ is finite over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta$ .

Furthermore, Z and $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ are both smooth of dimension 19 (over $\mathbb { Z }$ ), and the fibers of $\pi : Z \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ are (geometrically) reduced points above $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta ,$ again by Theorem 27.2.1. Then by Exercise 24.8.G, $\pi$ is étale over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta$ (and, in particular, flat).

Thus, over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta , \pi$ is a finite flat morphism, and so the fibers of $\pi$ (again, away from $\Delta$ ) always have the same number of points, “counted correctly” (Remark 24.3.8). But by Theorem 27.2.1, above each closed point of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta ,$ each point of the fiber of $\pi$ counts with multiplicity 1 (here using $\mathrm { k } = \bar { \mathrm { k } }$ ). Finally, by Exercise 27.1.C, the Fermat cubic surface gives an example of one regular cubic surface with precisely 27 lines, so (as $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta$ is connected) we are done. □
We have actually shown that, away from ∆, $Z \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 }$ is a finite étale morphism of degree 27.

27.2 Every Smooth Cubic Surface (over k) Contains 27 Lines
27.2.4. The configuration of lines.

By the “configuration of lines” on a cubic surface, we mean the data of which pairs of the 27 lines intersect. We can readily work this out in the special case of the Fermat cubic surface (Exercise 27.1.C). (It can be more enlightening to use the description of X as a blow-up of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ ; see Exercise 27.3.F.) We now show that the configuration is the “same” (interpreted appropriately) for all smooth cubic surfaces.

27.2.5. Remark (cf. Remark 27.3.7): The Weyl group $W ( \mathrm { E } _ { 6 } )$ . The symmetry group of the configuration of lines—i.e., the subgroup of the permutations of the 27 lines preserving the intersection data—magically turns out to be the Weyl group of $\mathsf { E } _ { 6 } ,$ a group of order 51840. (You know enough to at least verify that the size of the group is 51840, using the Fermat cubic surface of Exercise 27.1.C, but this takes some work.) It is no coincidence that the degree of $W ^ { \prime \prime }$ over $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta$ is 51840, and the Galois group of the Galois closure of $\mathsf { K } ( Z ) / \mathsf { K } ( \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 9 } \setminus \Delta )$ is isomorphic to $W ( \mathsf { E } _ { 6 } )$ (see [H1, III.3]).

27.2.6.* Fano varieties of lines, and Hilbert schemes. In Exercises 27.2.A and 27.2.K, you constructed a moduli space of lines contained in a $\Sigma ,$ as a scheme. Your argument can be generalized to any $\mathsf { X } \subset \mathbb { P } ^ { \mathsf { N } }$ . This construction is called the Fano variety of lines of X (not to be confused with the notion of a Fano variety, §21.5.5), and is an example of a Hilbert scheme.

27.3 Every Smooth Cubic Surface (over k) is a Blown-Up Plane

We now prove Theorem 27.0.2. As stated in $\ S 2 7 . 0 . 3 ,$ , this section is remarkably independent from the previous one; all we will need is Exercise 27.2.J, and it is possible to prove that in other ways.

Suppose X is a smooth cubic surface (over $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ ). Suppose ℓ is a line on X, and choose coordinates on the ambient $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ so that ℓ is cut out by $x _ { 0 }$ and $_ { x _ { 1 } }$ . Projection from ℓ gives a rational map $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 } \mathrel { \mathop { \mathrm { ~ \tiny ~ - ~ } } } \to \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ (given by $[ \mathsf { x } _ { 0 } , \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \mathsf { x } _ { 2 } , \mathsf { x } _ { 3 } ] \mapsto [ \mathsf { x } _ { 0 } , \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } ] )$ , which extends to a morphism on X. The reason is that this rational map is resolved by blowing up the closed subscheme ${ \sf V } ( { \sf x } _ { 0 } , { \sf x } _ { 1 } )$ (Exercise 22.4.L). But $\left( \mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 0 } , \mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 } \right)$ cuts out the effective Cartier divisor ℓ on $\Sigma ,$ and blowing up an effective Cartier divisor does not change X (Observation 22.2.1).

Now choose two disjoint lines $\ell$ and $\ell ^ { \prime }$ as in Exercise 27.2.J, and consider the morphism $\rho \colon X  \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } ,$ , where the map to the first $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is the projection from $\ell ,$ and the map to the second $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is the projection from $\ell ^ { \prime }$ . The first $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ can then be identified with $\ell ^ { \prime }$ , and the second with ℓ.

(This idea appeared earlier in the development of the group law on the cubic curve; see Proposition 19.10.3.) Given a general point $x \in X ,$ we obtain a point $( { \mathfrak { p } } , { \mathfrak { q } } ) \in \ell ^ { \prime } \times \ell$ by projecting from $\ell ^ { \prime }$ and ℓ.
In particular, we have shown for the first time that every smooth cubic surface over $\overline { { \mathtt { k } } }$ is rational.

27.3.B. EXERCISE: ρ CONTRACTS PRECISELY $\ell _ { 1 } , \ldots , \ell _ { 5 }$ . Show that ρ is an isomorphism away from the $\ell _ { \mathrm { i } }$ mentioned in Exercise 27.2.J, and that each $\rho \big ( \ell _ { \mathrm { i } } \big )$ is a point $\mathsf { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \ell ^ { \prime } \times \ell$ .
27.3.1. Proposition — The morphism $\rho \colon X  \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is the blow-up of $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ at the five pi.

We give two proofs. The first demonstrates the utility of a number of technical tools we have developed (or will develop in the future—there is one “forward reference” here). The second is a down-to-earth explicit “calculation.” Different people prefer different approaches, but there are advantages in seeing them side by side. The first shows how to apply tools in quite general situations where we don’t have explicit control over the geometry. The second gives us rather complete and direct control of the geometry in question, without needing formal functions.

27.3.2. Proof 1 of Proposition 27.3.1. By Castelnuovo’s Criterion (Theorem 28.6.1), as the lines $\ell _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are $( - 1 )$ -curves (Exercise 27.1.B), they can be contracted. More precisely, there is a morphism $\beta \colon X \to X ^ { \prime }$ that is the blow-up of $X ^ { \prime }$ at five closed points $\mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \mathfrak { p } _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } ,$ such that $\ell _ { \mathrm { i } }$ is the exceptional divisor at $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ . We wish to show that $x ^ { \prime }$ is $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ .

The morphism $\rho \colon X  \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ yields a morphism $\rho ^ { \prime } \colon X ^ { \prime } \setminus \{ \mathsf { p } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots \mathsf { p } _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } \} \longrightarrow \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ . We now show that $\rho ^ { \prime }$ extends over $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ for each ${ \mathrm { i } , }$ sending $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ to ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ . Choose an open neighborhood of $\mathsf { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } \in \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ isomorphic to $\mathbb { A } ^ { 2 }$ , with coordinates $x$ and y. Then both $x$ and $_ { 9 }$ pull back to functions on a punctured open neighborhood of $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ (i.e., there is some open neighborhood U of $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ such that $x$ and y are functions on $\mathrm { { U } \backslash \{ p _ { i } ^ { \prime } \} , }$ ). By Algebraic Hartogs’s Lemma 13.5.19, they extend over $\mathsf { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } ,$ and this extension is unique as $\mathbb { P } ^ { 1 } \times \mathbb { P } ^ { 1 }$ is separated—use the Reduced-to-Separated Theorem 11.3.2 if you really need to. Thus $\rho ^ { \prime }$ extends over $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime }$ . (Do you see why $\rho ^ { \prime } ( \mathsf { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ^ { \prime } ) = \mathsf { p } _ { \mathrm { i } } ?$ )

The process can be reversed: we can blow-up $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ at six points, and embed it in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ . We first explain why we can’t blow up $\mathbb { P } ^ { 2 }$ at just any six points and hope to embed the result in $\mathbb { P } ^ { 3 }$ . Because the cubic surface is embedded anticanonically (Exercise 27.1.A), we see that any curve C in X must satisfy $\deg _ { C } \mathcal { H } _ { X } < 0$ .

Chapter 28
Power Series and the Theorem on Formal Functions
Power series are a central tool in analytic geometry. Their analog in algebraic geometry, completion, is similarly useful. We will only touch on some aspects of the subject.

In §28.1, we deal with some algebraic preliminaries. In $\ S 2 8 . 2 ,$ we use completions to (finally) give a definition of various singularities, such as nodes. (We won’t use these definitions in what follows.) In §28.3, we state the main technical result of the chapter, the Theorem on Formal Functions 28.3.2. The subsequent three sections give applications. In §28.4, we prove Zariski’s Connectedness Lemma 28.4.1 and the Stein Factorization Theorem 28.4.3. In $\ S 2 8 . 5 ,$ we prove a commonly used version of (Grothendieck’s version of) Zariski’s Main Theorem 28.5.1; we rely on $\ S 2 8 . 4$ . In §28.6, we prove Castelnuovo’s Criterion for contracting $( - 1 )$ -curves, which we used in Chapter 27. The proof of Castelnuovo’s Criterion also uses $\ S 2 8 . 4$ . Finally, in §28.7, we prove the Theorem on Formal Functions 28.3.2.

There are deliberately many small sections in this chapter, so you can see that they tend not to be as hard at they look, with the exception of the proof of the Theorem on Formal Functions itself, and possibly Theorem 28.1.6 relating completion to exactness and flatness.
28.1 Algebraic Preliminaries

Suppose A is a ring, and $J _ { 1 } \supset J _ { 2 } \supset \cdots$ is a decreasing sequence of ideals. The limit $\mathrm { l i m } A / \mathrm { J } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ is often denoted by ${ \hat { A } } ,$ and is called the completion of A (for this sequence of ideals); the sequence of ideals is left implicit. The most important case of completion is if $J _ { \mathfrak { n } } = \mathrm { I } ^ { \mathfrak { n } }$ , where I is an ideal of A. The limit lim A/In is called the I-adic completion of A, or the completion of A along I or at I.

28.1.1. Example. We define $\mathbf { k } [ [ x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ] ]$ as the completion of $\mathbf k [ \pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \dots , \pmb { x } _ { \mathrm n } ]$ at the maximal ideal $\left( \mathsf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathsf { x } _ { \mathsf { n } } \right)$ . This is the ring of formal power series in n variables over k (Example 1.3.3).
The p-adic numbers (Example 1.3.4) are another example.

28.1.A. EXERCISE. Suppose that $\mathrm { J } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \supset \mathrm { J } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \supset \cdots$ is a decreasing series of ideals that is cofinal with $\operatorname { J } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ . In other words, for every $\scriptstyle \int _ { \mathrm { \Pi n , \mu } }$ there is some $J _ { \mathsf { N } } ^ { \prime }$ with $\mathrm { J } _ { \mathrm { n } } \supset \mathrm { J } _ { \mathrm { N } } ^ { \prime } ,$ and for every $\mathrm { J } _ { \mathrm { n } } ^ { \prime } ,$ there is some $J _ { \mathsf { N } }$ with $J _ { \textrm { n } } ^ { \prime } \supset J _ { \textrm { N } }$ . Show that there is a canonical isomorphism $\operatorname* { l i m } A / \operatorname { J } _ { \mathfrak { n } } ^ { \prime } \longleftrightarrow \operatorname* { l i m } A / \operatorname { J } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ . Thus what matters is less the specific sequence of ideals than the “cofinal” equivalence class.
28.1.2. Preliminary remarks. We have an obvious morphism $\boldsymbol { A } \to \hat { \boldsymbol { A } }$ .

If we put the discrete topology on $\mathsf { A } / \mathrm { J } _ { \mathrm { n } } ,$ then $\hat { \boldsymbol A }$ naturally has the structure of a topological ring (a ring that is a topological space, where all the ring operations are continuous, or, equivalently, a ring object in the category of topological spaces). We then can have the notion of a topological module M over a topological ring $A ^ { \prime }$ —a module over the underlying ring $A ^ { \prime }$ , with a topology, such that the action of $A ^ { \prime }$ on $M$ is continuous. This is a useful point of view, but we won’t use it.
28.1.B. EXERCISE. Suppose m is a maximal ideal of a ring A. Show that the completion of A at m is canonically isomorphic to the completion of $\mathsf { A } _ { \mathfrak { m } }$ at m.

If $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring, then the natural map $\boldsymbol { A } \to \hat { \boldsymbol { A } }$ is an injection: anything in the kernel must lie in $\cap { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { \mathrm { i } }$ , which is 0 by the Krull Intersection Theorem (Exercise 13.9.A(b)). Thus “no information is lost by completing,” just as analytic functions are (locally) determined by their power series expansion.

(In the case of completion at an ideal I, we say that A is I-adically separated if $A  { \hat { A } }$ is an injection, and complete with respect to I if $A  { \hat { A } }$ is an isomorphism, although we won’t need these phrases. For example, the Krull Intersection Theorem implies that if I is a proper ideal of a Noetherian integral domain or a Noetherian local ring, then A is I-adically separated.)

28.1.C. EXERCISE. Suppose that $( A , { \mathfrak { m } } )$ is a Noetherian local ring containing its residue field k (i.e., it is a k-algebra). Let $\mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , . . . , \mathbf { x } _ { \mathrm { n } }$ be elements of A whose images are a basis for ${ \mathfrak { m } } / { \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ . Show that the map of k-algebras

defined by $\mathrm { t } _ { \mathrm { i } } \mapsto \mathsf { x } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ is a surjection. (First explain why there is such a map!) As usual, for local rings, the completion is assumed to be at the maximal ideal.
Exercise 28.1.C is a special case of the Cohen Structure Theorem. (See [E, §7.4] for more on this topic.)

Suppose $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ is regular, of codimension d (i.e., $\dim { \mathcal { O } } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } } = \mathrm { d }$ ). 28.1.4. Some more geometric motivation.

Exercise 28.1.D may give some motivation for completion: “in the completion, regular schemes look like affine space.” This is often stated in the suggestive language of “formally locally, regular schemes are isomorphic to affine space”; this will be made somewhat more precise in §28.3.1.

We now give a little more geometric motivation for completion, which we will not use later on. Recall from $\ S 1 3 . 6 . 1$ that étale morphisms are designed to look like “local isomorphisms” in differential geometry. But Exercise 13.6.F showed that this metaphor badly fails in one important way. More precisely, let $\mathsf { Y } = \mathsf { S p e c k } [ \mathsf { t } ] .$ , and $X { = } \mathrm { S p e c k } [ { \mathrm { u } } , 1 / { \mathrm { u } } ] ,$ , and let $\mathfrak { p } \in \mathsf { X }$ be given by $\mu = 1$ , and $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ be given by $\mathrm { t } = 1$ . Suppose char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2$ . The morphism $\pi \colon X \to \Upsilon$ induced by $\mathrm { t } \mapsto \mathrm { u } ^ { 2 }$ is étale, and $\pi ( { \mathfrak { p } } ) = { \mathfrak { q } }$ . But there is no open neighborhood of p that $\pi$ maps isomorphically onto an open neighborhood of q. However, the following exercise shows that $\pi$ induces an isomorphism of completions (an isomorphism of “formal neighborhoods”).

Show that $\pi$ induces an isomorphism of completions $\hat { \mathcal { O } } _ { \Upsilon , { \mathfrak { q } } } \to \hat { \mathcal { O } } _ { \mathsf { X } , { \mathfrak { p } } } .$ (If you are not familiar with Hensel’s Lemma, you might rediscover its central idea in the course of solving this exercise.)

Chapter 28 Power Series, Theorem on Formal Functions
These ideas are close to the definition of “formal étaleness” discussed in §24.8.13.

You can interpret these results as the statement that “the implicit function theorem works formally locally,” even though it doesn’t work Zariski-locally. (The étale topology is somewhere between these two; it is partially designed so that the implicit function theorem in this sense always holds, essentially by fiat. But this is not the place to discuss the étale topology.)
Here are two more fun problems secretly about Hensel’s Lemma.

28.1.5. Completion and exactness.
We conclude this section with an interesting and useful statement.

28.1.6. Theorem — Suppose A is a Noetherian ring, and $\mathrm { I } \subset A$ is an ideal. For any A-module M, let $\hat { M } = \operatorname* { l i m } M / \mathrm { I } ^ { \mathrm { j } } M$ be the completion of M with respect to I.

list_items: (a) The completion $\hat { A }$ (of A with respect to I) is flat over A.
(b) If M is finitely generated, then the natural map $\hat { { \cal A } } \otimes _ { { \cal A } } { \cal M }  \hat { { \cal M } }$ is an isomorphism.

(c) If $0 \to M \to \mathsf { N } \to \mathsf { P } \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of finitely generated A-modules, then $0  \hat { { \cal M } } $ $\hat { \mathsf { N } } \to \hat { \mathsf { P } } \to 0$ is exact. (Thus completion preserves exact sequences of finitely generated modules, by Exercise 1.5.F.)

$$
0 \longrightarrow \oplus_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} k [ t ] \xrightarrow {\times (t , t ^ {2} , t ^ {3} , \dots)} \oplus_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} k [ t ] \longrightarrow \oplus_ {n = 1} ^ {\infty} k [ t ] / \left(t ^ {n}\right) \longrightarrow 0.

After completion with respect to the ideal $\big ( \big ( \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { t } , \mathrm { t } , \dots \big ) \big )$ , the sequence is no longer exact in the middle: $( \mathrm { t } ^ { 2 } , \mathrm { t } ^ { 3 } , \mathrm { t } ^ { 4 } , \dots )$ maps to 0, but is not in the image of the completion of the previous term.

28.2 Types of Singularities
Singularities are often most easily defined in terms of completions.

28.2.1. Definition. Suppose X is a dimension 1 variety over ${ \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ,$ and ${ \mathfrak { p } } \in X$ is a closed point. We say that X has a node at p if the completion of $\mathcal { O } _ { { X , \mathfrak { p } } }$ at ${ \mathfrak { m } } _ { \mathsf { X } , \mathsf { p } }$ is isomorphic (as topological rings) to $\overline { { \mathsf { k } } } [ [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] ] / ( \mathsf { x } \mathsf { y } )$ . (How does this compare to the definition of node in §13.3.2?)

The definition of node outside the case of varieties over algebraically closed fields is more problematic, and we give some possible ways forward. For varieties over a non-algebraically closed field ${ \mathrm { k } } ,$ one can always base change to the closure $\bar { \mathsf { k } }$ . As an alternative approach, if p is a k-valued point of a variety over $\mathtt { k }$ (not necessarily algebraically closed), then we could take the same Definition 28.2.1; this might reasonably be called a split node, because the branches (or, more precisely, the tangent directions) are distinguished. Those singularities that are not split nodes, but which become nodes after base change to $\bar { \mathsf { k } }$ (such as the origin in Spec $\mathbb { R } [ x , y ] / ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ,$ , or Spec $\mathbb { Q } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] / ( \mathsf { x } ^ { 2 } - 2 \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } ) )$ , might reasonably be called non-split nodes.

list_items: 28.2.2. Definition. We may define other singularities similarly. To avoid complications, we do so over an algebraically closed field. Suppose X is a variety over ${ \overline { { \mathsf { k } } } } ,$ and p is a closed point of $\Sigma ,$ where char $\mathtt { k } \neq 2 , 3$ . We say that X has a cusp (resp., tacnode, triple point) at p if $\hat { \mathcal { O } } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } }$ is isomorphic to the completion of $\overline { { \mathsf { k } } } [ \mathsf { x } , \mathsf { y } ] / ( \mathsf { y } ^ { 2 } - \mathsf { x } ^ { 3 } )$ (resp., $\overline { { { \sf k } } } [ { \sf x } , { \sf y } ] / ( { \sf y } ^ { 2 } - x ^ { 4 } ) , \overline { { { \sf k } } } [ { \sf x } , { \sf y } ] / ( { \sf y } ^ { 3 } - x ^ { 3 } ) )$ . (See Figure 13.3 for pictures of the first two.) We say that $x$ has an ordinary multiple point of multiplicity ${ \mathfrak { m } } ,$ or ordinary m-fold point, if $\hat { \mathcal { O } } _ { X , \mathfrak { p } }$ is isomorphic to the completion of $\mathbb { k } [ x , y ] / ( \mathsf { f } ( x , y ) ) .$ , where f is a homogeneous polynomial of degree m with no repeated roots. (You can see quickly that an ordinary 2-fold point—or ordinary double point—is precisely a node, and an ordinary 3-fold point is a triple point.)

28.2 Types of Singularities

28.2.F. EXERCISE. We continue the terminology of the previous two exercises. If $x$ is a puredimensional reduced projective curve, show that $\mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { a } } ( X ) = \mathfrak { p } _ { \mathfrak { a } } ( \tilde { X } ) + 1$ .

Thus “resolving a node” of a curve reduces the arithmetic genus of the curve by 1. If you wish, you can readily show that “resolving a cusp” reduces the genus by 1, and “resolving a tacnode” reduces the genus by 2. In general, for each type of curve singularity, the contribution it makes to the genus—the difference between the genus of the curve and that of its “partial normalization at the singular point,” when the curve is projective—is called the δ-invariant. Thus δ for a node or cusp is 1, and δ for a tacnode is 2.

28.2.5. Other definitions. If ${ \overline { { \mathrm { k } } } } = \mathbb { C } ,$ then definitions of this sort agree with the analytic definitions (see [Ar1, §1]). For example, a complex algebraic curve singularity is a node if and only if it is analytically isomorphic to an open neighborhood of $x y = 0$ in $\mathbb { C } ^ { 2 }$ . 28.3 The Theorem on Formal Functions

Suppose π: $\Chi \to \Upsilon$ is proper morphism of locally Noetherian schemes, and $\mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf on $\scriptstyle \mathsf { X } ,$ so $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ is a coherent sheaf (Grothendieck’s Coherence Theorem 18.9.1). Fix a point $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y }$ . We already have a sense that there is an imperfect relationship between the fiber of $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ at q and the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ “on the fiber $\mathrm { X _ { q } , \Omega ^ { \prime \prime } }$ and much of Chapter 25 was devoted to making this precise.

The Theorem on Formal Functions deals with this issue in a different way. Rather than comparing the fiber of $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ with the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ restricted to the fiber, it gives an isomorphism between information on “infinitesimal thickenings” of the fiber. Informally:
Chapter 28 Power Series, Theorem on Formal Functions

28.3.1. We now make this more precise. If Z is a closed subscheme of W cut out by ideal sheaf $\mathcal { I }$ , we say that the closed subscheme cut out by ${ \mathcal { I } } ^ { { \mathrm { n + 1 } } }$ is the nth order formal (or infinitesimal) neighborhood (or thickening) of Z (in W). The phrase “formal neighborhood” without mention of an “order” refers to the information contained in all of these neighborhoods at once, often in the form of a limit of the sort we will soon describe. (This also leads us to the notion of formal schemes, an important notion we will nonetheless not need.)

We turn now to our situation of interest. Rather than the fiber of $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ at ${ \bf q } ,$ we consider its completion: we take the stalk at ${ \sf q } ,$ and complete it at the maximal ideal ${ \mathfrak { m } } _ { \mathbf { q } }$ . Rather than the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ along the fiber, we consider the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ when restricted to the nth formal neighborhood $X _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ of the fiber, and take the limit. The Theorem of Formal Functions says that these are canonically identified.

Before we state the Theorem on Formal Functions, we need to be precise about what these limits are, and how they are defined. To concentrate on the essential, we do this in the special case where q is a closed point of Y, and leave the (mild) extension to the general case to you (Exercise 28.3.A).

We deal first with $\mathbb { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F }$ . It is helpful to note that, by Exercise 28.1.B, we can compute the restriction to the nth formal neighborhood either by pulling back to Spec $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \mathbf { q } }$ (“the germ of Y at ${ \bf q } , \prime \prime$ i.e., tensoring with $\mathcal { O } _ { \Upsilon , \mathfrak { q } }$ ), or in any affine open neighborhood of $\mathsf { q } \in \mathsf { Y } .$ . For convenience, we pick an affine open neighborhood Spec $\textsf { B C Y }$ of q. (Again, by Exercise 28.1.B, it won’t matter which affine open subset we take.) Define $\chi _ { \mathrm { B } } : = \pi ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathsf { S p e c B } \right)$ for convenience. Let ${ \mathfrak { m } } \subset { \mathfrak { B } }$ be the maximal ideal corresponding to q. Then $\big ( \big ( \mathsf { R } ^ { \mathrm { i } } \pi _ { * } \mathcal { F } \big ) _ { \mathrm { q } } \big ) \widehat  \}$ is canonically the completion of the coherent B-module $\mathsf { H } ^ { \mathrm { i } } ( X _ { \mathrm { B } } , \mathcal { F } | _ { X _ { \mathrm { B } } } )$ at m:

$$
\left(\left(R ^ {i} \pi_ {*} \mathcal {F}\right) _ {q}\right) ^ {\hat {}} = \lim  H ^ {i} \left(X _ {B}, \mathcal {F} | _ {X _ {B}}\right) / \mathfrak {m} ^ {n} H ^ {i} \left(X _ {B}, \mathcal {F} | _ {X _ {B}}\right).

We turn next to the cohomology of $\mathcal { F }$ on thickenings of the fiber. We have closed embeddings ${ X _ { \mathfrak { n } } } \hookrightarrow { X _ { \mathfrak { n } + 1 } }$ , and thus maps of cohomology groups
